use: I propose that in feedback-seeking contexts, employing positive and pride-infused language in self-promotion affects the engagement of the target audience (Fredrickson, 2001), resulting in responses that are richer in informational value. I theorize that self-promotion acts as a catalyst for initiative-taking and proactive adjustment by stimulating increased feedback from the environment (Bandura, 1991). These enriched exchanges of information facilitate the pursuit of subgoals within broader goal-striving efforts by sharpening focus and enhancing motivation (Brown et al., 2001; Uy et al., 2024).
text: I posit that in feedback-seeking situations, using positive and prideful language in self-promotion influences the target audience's engagement (Fredrickson, 2001), leading to more information-rich responses. I theorize that self-promotion catalyzes initiative-taking and proactive adaptation by eliciting greater environmental feedback (Bandura, 1991). Enhanced information exchanges support subgoal enactment during goal striving, clarifying focus and motivating efforts (Brown et al., 2001; Uy et al., 2024).

use: Although interest in the expression of gratitude has grown within the literature, important limitations remain. A key gap lies in the limited attention paid to circumstances in which expressions of gratitude may not produce positive outcomes. As enthusiasm for fostering gratitude in organizational settings continues to grow (Fehr et al., 2017; Gallo, 2021; Locklear et al., 2023), it becomes increasingly important to understand the boundary conditions that may shape or constrain its intended benefits.
text: Although the literature on expressions of gratitude is growing, it remains limited in important ways. One notable limitation of existing research is its relative lack of attention to when gratitude expressions are less likely to have positive effects. As calls for bringing more gratitude into organizations have increased (Fehr et al., 2017; Gallo, 2021; Locklear et al., 2023), an understanding of these boundary conditions is essential.

use: This study centers on language—an essential mechanism through which individuals shape how others perceive them and interpret the impressions they receive from others. Language plays a critical role in securing jobs (Kraus et al., 2019), winning arguments (Warner, Colaner, and Park, 2020), obtaining funding (Kanze et al., 2018), and influencing election outcomes (Frimer et al., 2015; Frimer and Skitka, 2018). At its core, human behavior is driven by a fundamental desire for social acceptance (Baumeister and Leary, 1995), and language serves as a powerful tool for pursuing this universal social aim.
text: This study focuses on language, a key tool used to manage others' impressions of us and to derive our impressions of others. Language wins jobs (Kraus et al., 2019), arguments (Warner, Colaner, and Park, 2020), funding (Kanze et al., 2018), and elections (Frimer et al., 2015; Frimer and Skitka, 2018). People are fundamentally motivated to be accepted by others (Baumeister and Leary, 1995), and they use language to meet this social goal.

use: Rankings significantly influence reputation, in part because they provide a numerical foundation for comparing the performance of otherwise diverse organizations—a process known as commensuration (Espeland & Stevens, 1998). Through this process, complex and varied indicators of organizational quality are reduced to a single numerical rank, often at the expense of nuance and important contextual differences (Espeland & Sauder, 2007). For instance, the Corporate Knights Global 100 facilitates comparisons of sustainability performance across a wide array of industries, from finance to technology.
text: Rankings matter for reputation in part because they offer a numerical basis for comparing the performance of seemingly disparate organizations through a process known as commensuration (Espeland and Stevens, 1998). This process condenses diverse pieces of information about organizational quality into a single numerical rank, often overlooking significant heterogeneity and details (Espeland and Sauder, 2007). For example, the Corporate Knights Global 100 allows comparisons of sustainability performance across various industries, ranging from finance to technology.

use: Originally developed by Gaertner and colleagues (1989), recategorization theory extends—but remains distinct from—social categorization theory, which emphasizes how individuals categorize others as out-group members and the negative implications that result (Tajfel & Turner, 1979; Turner, 1987). Recategorization theory offers a framework for mitigating these negative effects by encouraging the cognitive reclassification of out-group members into a more inclusive in-group. When individuals begin to perceive former out-group members as part of a shared in-group, their biases and prejudices toward those individuals are significantly reduced.
text: Developed by Gaertner and colleagues (1989), recategorization theory builds on but is distinct from social categorization theory, which focuses on the occurrence of out-group categorization and its negative consequences (Tajfel and Turner, 1979; Turner, 1987). Recategorization theory explains how these negative consequences can be reduced by recategorizing out-group members as members of a more inclusive in-group. By shifting their cognitive representations of out-group members to see them as in-group members, people reduce their biases toward others.

use: For instance, Ruderman et al. (2002) found that women engaged in multiple nonwork roles developed valuable skills through those experiences, which they then applied in the workplace—contributing both to task performance and interpersonal effectiveness. Natural Language Processing (NLP) offers a powerful means of analyzing such life experiences to inform selection decisions, moving beyond traditional biographical data that focuses on quantifiable elements (e.g., durations, frequencies), as is typical in research not employing NLP. Instead, NLP can analyze the actual language used, enabling researchers and practitioners to tap into the nuanced, detailed insights embedded in narrative data.
text: For example, Ruderman et al. (2002) found that women who occupied multiple nonwork roles brought skills generated through those experiences into work, which translated into task-related and interpersonal skills. NLP can score life experiences to inform selection decisions, and it is not limited to biographical data that can easily be quantified (e.g., amounts, times) as is characteristic of research that does not use NLP. Instead, it can measure the actual words and thus leverage the rich descriptions in narrative data.

use: Reinforcement machine learning (ML) shares the core goal of supervised ML—namely, achieving strong generalization—but is often regarded as a distinct subcategory. Unlike supervised learning, where the system learns from labeled input-output pairs, reinforcement ML involves learning through episodic feedback: the algorithm interacts with its environment, takes actions, and adjusts its behavior based on rewards or penalties it receives. This trial-and-error process is thought to resemble natural human learning, where children are rewarded for following instructions, gradually recognize broader patterns of behavior, and develop new routines—ultimately forming internal frameworks that distinguish socially acceptable from unacceptable conduct (Gollapudi, 2016).
text: Reinforcement ML shares the same objective as supervised ML (i.e., generalization ability) and is sometimes considered a subclass. In reinforcement ML, however, the algorithm learns by receiving episodic feedback; the learning system observes the environment, makes choices, and is rewarded or penalized accordingly. As such, reinforcement learning is believed to mimic natural human learning when children are rewarded for following instructions, begin to discern metathemes of behaviors, and develop new habits—”eventually constructing rubrics for socially acceptable versus unacceptable behavior (Gollapudi, 2016).

use: Rankings also influence an organization's status, particularly through their role in categorization. For example, university rankings classify institutions into hierarchical tiers (Sauder, 2006), while the Fortune 500 designates a specific group of the 500 most prominent U.S. companies (Bermiss, Zajac, & King, 2014). Because audiences associate different categories with varying degrees of prestige (Sharkey, 2014), organizations that enter a high-status list or ascend to a more elite tier often experience a status elevation relative to those excluded or ranked lower (e.g., Merton, 1968; Sauder & Lancaster, 2006; Tian, King, & Smith, 2023).
text: Rankings also affect an organization's status, particularly through categorization. For instance, university rankings sort institutions into tiers (Sauder, 2006), and the Fortune 500 creates a category of the 500 most prominent U.S. firms (Bermiss, Zajac, and King, 2014). As audiences associate categorical labels with varying levels of prestige (Sharkey, 2014), organizations that advance to a prestigious list or a higher tier experience a status boost compared to those that do not (e.g., Merton, 1968; Sauder and Lancaster, 2006; Tian, King, and Smith, 2023).

use: Our study makes three key contributions to the literature. First, extending clinical research on ADHD-related differences in conscientiousness, we test whether contextualizing items—specifically framing them within a work setting—reduces observed mean differences. Second, we examine how work-specific versus general facets of conscientiousness differ by ADHD status, aiming to determine whether overall differences in conscientiousness are primarily driven by facets with varying degrees of job relevance, as identified in cross-occupational meta-analyses. Third, we investigate measurement noninvariance across ADHD and non-ADHD groups for both work-based and general conscientiousness items, shedding light on potential differences in how individuals interpret these items.
text: Our work offers three contributions to the literature. First, we build on clinical findings of ADHD-based differences in conscientiousness by testing whether item contextualization attenuates these mean differences. Second, we compare differences in work-specific and general facets of conscientiousness by ADHD status to understand whether differences in global conscientiousness are driven by facets that vary in job relevance (per cross-occupational meta-analyses). Finally, we assess measurement noninvariance by ADHD status on work and general conscientiousness items, providing insight into potential differences in item interpretation.

use: The foundational question any theory of motivated behavior must address is: How much effort are individuals willing to devote to a given task overall? As previously discussed, discrepancies between goals and performance have been shown to significantly influence the allocation of effort (e.g., Beck & Schmidt, 2012; Vancouver et al., 2008). Greater goal-performance gaps signal the need for increased resource investment to improve performance, while smaller gaps suggest that the task requires less effort and attention, potentially freeing up cognitive and motivational resources for other pursuits.
text: The first question any theory of motivated behavior must answer is how much effort people will be willing to allocate toward a given task as a whole. As discussed above, goal-performance discrepancies have been shown to play an important role in guiding effort intensity (e.g., Beck & Schmidt, 2012; Vancouver et al., 2008). Larger gaps indicate more resources should be allocated toward the task, whereas smaller gaps suggest effective task performance requires less effort and attention, allowing for the redirection of effort toward other objectives.

use: The core of this idea is embodied in the concept of "face time," which denotes the hours employees are visibly present and working (Bailyn, 1993; Elsbach & Cable, 2012; Kossek & Van Dyne, 2008). In many organizational and industry contexts, expectations around face time are deeply embedded in cultural norms and practices (Blair-Loy, 2009; J.W.; O'Neill, 2012). A clear example can be found in the hotel industry, where employees are both expected and explicitly socialized to demonstrate extended hours of "presenteeism" from the early stages of their careers (Cullen & McLaughlin, 2006; Munck, 2001; O'Neill, 2012).
text: The essence of this notion is captured in the concept of "face time," which refers to the hours that employees are visibly working (Bailyn, 1993; Elsbach & Cable, 2012; Kossek & Van Dyne, 2008). In many work settings, expectations of face time are seemingly baked into the organization and industry's culture (Blair-Loy, 2009; J.W.; O'Neill, 2012). For instance, in the hotel industry, workers are expected and socialized to enact long hours of "presenteeism" early on in their careers (Cullen & McLaughlin, 2006; Munck, 2001; O'Neill, 2012).

use: Unsurprisingly, prior research has aimed to identify the factors that promote employee voice (e.g., Detert & Burris, 2007; Tangirala & Ramanujam, 2012). However, much of this work operates under the assumption that employees are able to recognize work-related problems and, therefore, concentrates on encouraging them to raise concerns or suggest improvements—for example, by lowering the perceived risks of speaking up through the cultivation of psychological safety (Edmondson, 2003). We build on this foundation by highlighting an important, yet often overlooked point: employees may also remain silent not because they are unwilling to speak, but because they fail to perceive problems in the first place.
text: Not surprisingly, research has sought to explicate factors that facilitate voice (e.g., Detert & Burris, 2007; Tangirala & Ramanujam, 2012). However, it has mostly assumed that employees can readily identify work problems and, thus, has focused on motivating employees to express concerns about or propose solutions to those problems (e.g., reducing the perceived costs of voice via psychological safety; Edmondson, 2003). We extend such work by noting that employees can also fail to speak up simply because they fail to notice problems in the first place.

use: We evaluate these hypotheses through a series of three experiments. Acknowledging the value of experimental methods in isolating the effects of specific behaviors (e.g., Brown & Lord, 1999; Dirks & Ferrin, 2002), we employ both laboratory and field experiments to ensure a balance between internal and external validity. By highlighting how both the source and content influence the impact of ideological messages, our study helps reconcile previously inconsistent findings and advances a contingency-based understanding of when leaders should personally deliver inspirational communications versus delegate them to beneficiaries.
text: We test these hypotheses across three experiments. Mindful of the importance of using experimental methods to isolate the effects of specific behaviors (e.g., Brown & Lord, 1999; Dirks & Ferrin, 2002), we utilize both laboratory and field experiments, facilitating both internal and external validity. By demonstrating the importance of the source and the content in shaping the effectiveness of ideological messages, our research takes a step toward resolving conflicting results and offers a contingency perspective on when leaders should deliver their own inspirational communications versus outsource them to beneficiaries.

use: We propose that the source of a counterpart’s alternative offer—specifically, whether it originates from a rival—can significantly shape a negotiator’s motives and behavior, ultimately influencing negotiation outcomes. More precisely, we suggest that when a negotiator learns their counterpart is considering an offer from a rival, it triggers a competitive drive to outperform that rival (Kilduff, 2014). This heightened motivation to “win” may lead the negotiator to make less ambitious and more generous offers in an effort to secure the deal and outperform the competing party.
text: We argue that the source of one's counterpart's alternative offer—”whether the offer comes from one's rival or not—”can exert a powerful influence on one's motives and behavior in negotiation, ultimately affecting negotiation outcomes. Specifically, we predict that when a negotiator faces a counterpart who is considering an offer from a rival, it will activate motivation to outperform the rival (Kilduff, 2014). This, in turn, will lead the negotiator to make less aspirational, more favorable offers to the counterpart in an attempt to secure an agreement and thus "beat" the negotiator's rival.

use: One possible explanation for the ongoing challenges faced by the fields of industrial/organizational psychology and organizational behavior is the relatively infrequent use of content validation methods compared to other forms of validation. Literature on scale development emphasizes that various methods are available to support the validity of the inferences drawn from a scale (Hendrick, Fischer, Tobi, & Frewer, 2013; Hinkin, 1995, 1998; MacKenzie, Podsakoff, & Podsakoff, 2011). In addition to content validation, these methods include assessments of reliability, convergent validity, discriminant validity, criterion-related validity, and evaluation of factor structure. use: Firm-stakeholder interactions do not take place in an informational void. Rather, media channels play a pivotal role in mediating these interactions by helping to reduce information asymmetries between firms and their stakeholders (see Graf-Vlachy, Oliver, Banfield, König, & Bundy, 2020, for a review). Accordingly, prior research has demonstrated that media coverage significantly influences corporate reputation (Deephouse, 2000; Rindova & Fombrun, 1999; Zavyalova et al., 2012); shapes firm strategy and performance (Bednar, Boivie, & Prince, 2012; Petkova, Rindova, & Gupta, 2013; Pollock & Rindova, 2003); and affects executives’ public image, tenure, and compensation (Bednar, 2012).
text: Interactions between firms and stakeholders do not occur in an informational vacuum. Instead, information shared though media channels plays a key role in addressing information asymmetries between firms and their stakeholders (for a review, see Graf-Vlachy, Oliver, Banfield, König, & Bundy, 2020). As a result, research has shown that the media shapes corporate reputation (Deephouse, 2000; Rindova & Fombrun, 1999; Zavyalova et al., 2012); firm strategy and performance (Bednar, Boivie, & Prince, 2012; Petkova, Rindova, & Gupta, 2013; Pollock & Rindova, 2003); and the image, tenure, and compensation of executives (Bednar, 2012).

use: A range of theoretical perspectives—including justice theory (Lind & Tyler, 1988), social exchange theory (Blau, 1964), and frustration-aggression theory (Spector, 1997)—propose that workplace deviance often emerges as a response to perceived injustices in the work environment (Bennett et al., 2018). To explore this notion, we examine both organizational injustice—defined as broad perceptions of unfairness within an organization (Ambrose & Schminke, 2009)—and specific forms of interpersonal injustice: abusive supervision, characterized by a supervisor’s hostile behavior (Tepper, 2000), and workplace ostracism, involving experiences of being ignored or excluded (Ferris et al., 2008; K. D. Williams & Zadro, 2001). We consider these dimensions as key factors in distinguishing between different classes of workplace deviance.
text: Various theories (e.g., justice theory, Lind & Tyler, 1988; social exchange theory, Blau, 1964; frustration-aggression, Spector, 1997) suggest that workplace deviance is also a reaction to unjust environmental factors (Bennett et al., 2018). To examine this possibility, we consider organizational injustice (i.e., holistic judgments regarding the unfairness of an organization; Ambrose & Schminke, 2009) and interpersonal injustices of abusive supervision (i.e., hostile behaviors by a supervisor; Tepper, 2000) and ostracism (i.e., being excluded or ignored at work; Ferris et al., 2008; K. D. Williams & Zadro, 2001) as differentiators of the deviance classes.

use: Let us again consider the illustrative Chihuahuas-vs.-muffins example (Figure 2): A reinforcement learning algorithm would begin by randomly classifying the first image in the training set, then proceed to evaluate each subsequent image individually and in sequence. The user provides feedback by indicating whether each classification is correct, allowing the algorithm to continuously update its strategy. Over time, in pursuit of maximizing cumulative rewards, the algorithm learns to make a series of increasingly accurate decisions. This iterative process enables the algorithm to generalize effectively, improving its ability to correctly classify previously unseen images.
text: Let us again leverage the illustrative Chihuahuas-muffins example (Figure 2): A reinforcement learning algorithm would classify the first image in the training dataset at random and each subsequent image individually and sequentially. The user would respond by evaluating whether the classification was correct, and the algorithm would thus continue to update until all of the images in the training dataset were classified. With the objective of maximizing rewards over time, the algorithm would generate a sequence of decisions that would help it achieve the highest reward, thereby achieving generalization ability for novel images.

use: Second, adopting high-level construals of the work context is likely linked to diminished empathy and reduced concern for colleagues. For instance, individuals tend to express less care and empathy toward others they perceive in abstract or psychologically distant terms (e.g., Jones & Rachlin, 2006; Williams & Bargh, 2008). Similarly, high-level construals can obscure the “imageability” of harm—that is, the ease with which harm can be envisioned or mentally represented (Gong & Medin, 2012)—and may impair one's capacity to mentally simulate the consequences of harm to others (e.g., Lammers, 2012). As a result, such abstract construals are likely to promote the dismissal or minimization of potential harm that others might experience in the workplace.
text: Second, high-level work context construals are likely associated with lower levels of empathy and care for colleagues at work. As one example, people care and empathize less for others whom they perceive at a more abstract (high) level (e.g., Jones & Rachlin, 2006; Williams & Bargh, 2008). Relatedly, high-level construals likely mask the “imageability” of harm to others (Gong & Medin, 2012) and hinder the mental simulations of harm to others (e.g., Lammers, 2012). Therefore, high-level work context construals are likely associated with ignoring or downplaying harm that could befall other people at work.

use: Our argument draws on role identity theory (Markus & Wurf, 1987; Riley & Burke, 1995), which posits that the self is constructed through the various social roles an individual enacts (Piliavin & Callero, 1991). Central to our focus is the concept of a creative role identity (Farmer et al., 2003; Jaussi et al., 2007). This refers to the extent to which individuals internalize creativity as a core aspect of their self-concept. In other words, the more strongly someone identifies with the creative role, the more they perceive creativity as an integral and defining feature of who they are (Tierney & Farmer, 2011).
text: We base our arguments on role identity theory (Markus & Wurf, 1987; Riley & Burke, 1995). According to the theory, the self is comprised of the various social roles that an individual occupies (Piliavin & Callero, 1991). Of particular interest here is an individual's creative role identity (Farmer et al., 2003; Jaussi et al., 2007). A creative role identity captures how central the notion of being creative is to a person's view of the self—”the more pronounced the identity, the more individuals see creativity as an essential part of "who they are" (Tierney & Farmer, 2011).

use: Previous research has shown that individuals often struggle to trust others who hold opposing views on deeply held, identity-defining beliefs (Hernández-Lagos & Minor, 2020). This challenge has intensified in an era marked by deepening and increasingly acrimonious partisan polarization (Dimock et al., 2014; Klein, 2020). As a result, people have become increasingly skeptical of information that contradicts their ideological stance—even when such evidence is presented by highly credible and reputable sources (Jurkowitz et al., 2020). These dynamics have important implications for the workplace, where collaboration, shared decision-making, and openness to peer input may be undermined when employees are required to work closely with others who they know hold conflicting beliefs.
text: Prior research has demonstrated that people have a difficult time trusting those with whom they disagree on important, identity-relevant beliefs (Hernández-Lagos & Minor, 2020). Indeed, as society has come to be characterized by more bitter and rancorous partisan polarization (Dimock et al., 2014; Klein, 2020), people have grown weary of evidence for ideologically opposing views, even when the evidence comes from highly reputable sources (Jurkowitz et al., 2020). These dynamics may negatively impact workplace productivity because coworkers often need to collaborate with, take advice from, and make decisions with individuals whom they know hold opposing perspectives.

use: Scholarly attention to homophily has grown substantially since Lazarsfeld and Merton (1954) originally coined the term. A pivotal moment in this trajectory was the publication of McPherson, Smith-Lovin, and Cook’s (2001) influential review on homophily within social network research. This article has since become the most frequently cited piece in the history of the Annual Review of Sociology (Allen, February 5, 2019, most cited article, personal communication with Annual Reviews). Following this milestone, interest in homophily has surged not only within sociology but also across a wide range of disciplines. To illustrate this growth, a Google Scholar search reveals that 2,190 articles published in 2010 referenced homophily—by 2018, that number had nearly tripled to 6,390.
text: Scholars' interest in homophily has increased dramatically since Lazarsfeld and Merton (1954) first introduced the term. McPherson, Smith-Lovin, and Cook's (2001) review of social network research on homophily, which is now the most highly cited article in the Annual Review of Sociology (Allen, February 5, 2019, most cited article, personal communication with Annual Reviews), marked an important inflection point. Since then, homophily research has escalated rapidly in sociology as well as in other disciplines. Of articles published in 2010, a Google Scholar search found 2,190 that referenced homophily; 8 years later, the number nearly tripled to 6,390.

use: Taken together, the viability of a focal category emerges from the combined effects of classification and valuation benefits (see Table 3, last column). However, because classification benefits tend to diminish and valuation benefits tend to grow as heterogeneity increases—both at a decreasing rate—their cumulative impact can produce a variety of possible relationships between heterogeneity and viability. At one end of the spectrum, this relationship may appear as an inverted U-shape, where both low and high levels of heterogeneity undermine category viability. At the opposite end, it may take the form of a U-shape, suggesting that moderate levels of heterogeneity are more detrimental than either low or high levels.
text: Taken together, the viability of a focal category results from the additive effects of classification and valuation benefits (Table 3, last column). However, since classification and valuation benefits respectively decrease and increase with heterogeneity at a decreasing rate, their additive effects yield a range of potential heterogeneity–viability relationships. On one end of this range, the heterogeneity–viability relationship may take an inverted U-shape, where too little or too much heterogeneity harms the viability of a category. On the other end, the relationship may form a U-shape, where a balanced level of heterogeneity is detrimental to a category’s viability.

use: By integrating both dimensions, we identify two distinct cognitive responses to negative events. First, individuals may experience persistent, negatively toned thoughts centered on the event itself—thoughts that are repetitive and focused on past occurrences. We refer to this pattern as backward-oriented negative rumination. Second, negative events can also trigger future-oriented cognitions aimed at reducing the gap between the current, undesirable situation and a preferred outcome. These thoughts involve actively considering ways to address or escape the situation, such as problem-solving or planning corrective action. We term this forward-looking, goal-directed response forward-oriented solution seeking.
text: Combining both dimensions, negative cognitions occurring as a response to a negative event can revolve around the event itself and be highly perseverative and backward-oriented. We call these negatively toned cognitions focusing on negative events backward-oriented negative rumination. Importantly, cognitions emerging from a negative event can also be future-oriented and address possible ways of reducing the discrepancy between the actual and a desired state, for instance, by searching for ways to solve a problem or leaving the situation. We call these cognitions that emerge from a negative event and focus on overcoming the undesired situation forward-oriented solution seeking.

use: Building on these arguments, two meta-analyses have explored the moderating role of autonomy in the relationship between broadly defined extrinsic rewards and either intrinsic motivation (Patall et al., 2008) or creativity (Byron & Khazanchi, 2012). However, these studies do not directly address the core question of the present research: Does task autonomy enhance the positive effect of performance-based pay (PFP) on creative performance? First, Patall et al. (2008) focused on intrinsic motivation as the outcome variable, not creative performance. As previously discussed, findings related to intrinsic motivation cannot be reliably extrapolated to infer effects on creative performance. Consequently, their study offers limited insights into the specific dynamics between PFP and creative performance.
text: Based on these arguments, two meta-analyses attempted to understand the effect of autonomy on the relationship between (broadly defined) extrinsic rewards and intrinsic motivation (Patall et al., 2008) or creativity (Byron & Khazanchi, 2012). Those studies, however, do not address the question of the present study: Does task autonomy make PFP’s effect on creative performance positive? This is because, first, Patall et al. (2008) examined intrinsic motivation, not creative performance, as the dependent variable. As noted, because findings on intrinsic motivation cannot be generalized to predict creative performance, their study has limited implications regarding the PFP–creative performance relationship.

use: The recategorization process likely carries significant implications for the future board positions of directors who differ demographically from incumbents. As previously discussed, directors whose demographic characteristics diverge from those of existing board members often face disadvantages in securing and advancing their roles. Yet, a growing number of these directors have not only completed multiple terms on the boards of major corporations, but have also taken on prominent roles as committee members and chairs in recent years. Recategorization offers a theoretical lens to explain why some demographically different directors are more successful than others in attaining these influential positions and assuming greater responsibility within board structures.
text: The recategorization process also likely has important consequences for demographically different directors' subsequent positions on boards. Although directors who have different demographic characteristics from incumbents are generally disadvantaged in keeping and enhancing their positions on boards, as reviewed above, an increasing number of them have not only served multiple terms on the boards of large corporations but also served as major board committee members and chairs in recent years. Recategorization provides a theoretical explanation for why some demographically different directors are more successful than others in obtaining these positions that allow them to play more important roles on boards.

use: Limited heterogeneity also constrains audiences by reducing their ability to choose among differentiated offerings (Haans, 2019; Navis & Glynn, 2011). It is important to acknowledge that audiences are not monolithic—while some favor typicality, others seek diversity (Goldberg et al., 2016; Pontikes, 2012; Taeuscher et al., 2021). However, even those inclined toward typical products may eventually disengage if faced with overly familiar or repetitive options. In contrast, a more heterogeneous set of offerings can rekindle their interest and engagement (Pontikes & Barnett, 2017; Soublière & Gehman, 2020). As a result, audiences are less likely to remain invested in a category that offers limited perceived value, and are more apt to direct their attention elsewhere.
text: For audiences, too, lower heterogeneity limits their ability to select among alternative products (Haans, 2019; Navis & Glynn, 2011). We recognize that audiences are not created equal; some prefer typicality while others prefer variety (Goldberg et al., 2016; Pontikes, 2012; Taeuscher et al., 2021). Yet, even audiences who value typicality may lose interest in products that resemble what they have seen many times before, whereas a more heterogeneous offering may renew their interest (Pontikes & Barnett, 2017; Soublière & Gehman, 2020). Audiences are thus unlikely to follow a category that affords little valuation benefits, allocating their attention elsewhere instead.

use: The objective of Study 1 was to examine and identify distinct classes of workplace deviance behavior. In particular, we investigated the varying patterns through which employees engage in deviant acts, with the aim of developing both a theoretical and empirical foundation for a person-centered understanding of deviance. To achieve this, we utilized a modified item-level meta-analytic approach (e.g., Carpenter et al., 2016; Klein et al., 2001), extracting item-level data from primary studies based on the 19-item Workplace Deviance Scale (Bennett & Robinson, 2000). We then applied multilevel latent class analysis (MLCA) to uncover the latent classes that emerged across multiple independent samples, encompassing a range of occupational contexts and workforce populations.
text: The purpose of Study 1 was to investigate and establish the nature of workplace deviance behavior classes. Specifically, we explored employees' different patterns of engaging in deviance to develop a theoretical and empirical understanding of person-centered deviance. To do so, we employed a modified item-level meta-analytic approach (e.g., Carpenter et al., 2016; Klein et al., 2001) and collected from the primary studies the item-level data for the 19 items on the workplace deviance scale (Bennett & Robinson, 2000). Using multilevel latent class analysis (MLCA), we explored the classes that emerged across multiple independent samples representing diverse work settings and workers.

use: Much of the information that stakeholders rely on to understand firms does not come directly from official public documents such as press releases or annual reports. Instead, it is filtered, interpreted, and disseminated by the media. The media does not cover all organizations or issues uniformly; rather, it selectively highlights events that align with audiences’ appetite for compelling and dramatic narratives—what has been termed “action news” (Gilliam & Iyengar, 2000). This selective attention influences both what topics the public becomes aware of and how those topics are perceived. Consequently, the media plays a pivotal role in shaping public discourse and policy agendas (McCombs & Shaw, 1972), as well as framing the ways in which audiences interpret the issues presented (Iyengar, 1987).
text: Much of the information that stakeholders have about firms is not sourced directly from public documents (e.g., press releases and annual reports) but is instead curated, interpreted, and distributed by the media. Rather than reporting on all topics and organizations, the media selects events that cater to audiences’ interest in “action news” (Gilliam & Iyengar, 2000), shaping what topics audiences read about and how they think about them. As a result, the media plays a critical role in setting the agenda for public discourse and policy-making (McCombs & Shaw, 1972), and influencing audiences’ interpretation of the topics considered (Iyengar, 1987).

use: In organizational settings, individuals typically carry out their tasks by following established or mutually agreed-upon procedures. Through repeated execution, these tasks become increasingly automatic, as individuals internalize the necessary skills and steps, transforming them into task routines. These routines require minimal cognitive effort, as they are enacted in an automatic, proceduralized, and efficient manner (Kanfer & Ackerman, 1989; Norman & Bobrow, 1975; Ohly et al., 2006, 2017). As a result, task routines serve as cognitive shortcuts, helping individuals conserve mental resources and thereby offering significant benefits for cognitive performance (Ohly et al., 2006, 2017).
text: In organizational contexts, individuals deal with their regular tasks mostly following prescribed or agreed-upon ways. By repeatedly executing a given task in the same way, individuals automatize the skills and steps needed to perform that task, turning it into a task routine. Completing task routines entails low cognitive demands because routines are performed in an automatic, proceduralized, and proficient way (Kanfer & Ackerman, 1989; Norman & Bobrow, 1975; Ohly et al., 2006, 2017). This means that task routines allow individuals to save mental effort and, as such, are very beneficial in terms of cognitive functioning (Ohly et al., 2006, 2017).

use: Recently, Parker and colleagues have made strides toward integrating the strengths of the proactive personality and personal initiative constructs into a unified framework for understanding proactive behavior (Parker et al., 2006). Building upon their work, we define proactive behavior as anticipatory action undertaken by employees to influence themselves and/or their surrounding environment. This definition aligns with established dictionary meanings of proactive behavior as that which "creates or controls a situation by taking the initiative or by anticipating events (as opposed to responding to them)," and of the verb proact as meaning "to take proactive measures; to act in advance, to anticipate" (Oxford English Dictionary, 1989).
text: Recently, Parker and colleagues have taken steps toward combining the favorable features of the proactive personality and personal initiative concepts into an integrated conceptualization of proactive behavior (Parker et al., 2006). Building on their conceptualization, we define proactive behavior as anticipatory action that employees take to impact themselves and/or their environments. This definition is consistent with dictionary definitions of proactive behavior as that which "creates or controls a situation by taking the initiative or by anticipating events (as opposed to responding to them)," and to proact as "to take proactive measures; to act in advance, to anticipate" (Oxford English Dictionary, 1989).

use: In some cases, conducting true field experiments with independent variables is both practical and minimally intrusive. For instance, when studying the effects of training, researchers can implement true experiments by scheduling executive participation in development programs at different times. However, organizational scholars frequently aim to test causal hypotheses under conditions where randomly assigning participants to different experimental treatments is either impractical or ethically problematic. In the following paragraphs, we explain how many large-scale variables, adverse events, and beneficial interventions fall into this category—and how quasi-experimental designs offer a rigorous alternative for such scenarios, even if they often require substantial time, planning, and effort to execute effectively.
text: For some independent variables, true field experiments are quite feasible and noninvasive. For example, in the case of training, researchers may conduct true field experiments by sending executives to development programs at different times. However, organizational researchers often wish to test a causal hypothesis when it is not practical or ethical to randomly assign participants to different conditions with controlled manipulations of independent variables. In the following paragraphs, we describe how many macroscopic variables, negative events, and beneficial treatments can be of this nature and how the quasi-experiment is a viable solution, albeit one that may take considerable time and effort.

use: Second, this study introduces a novel framework for understanding intra-individual cognitive processes, shedding light on how and why structural features of conflict experiences shape an individual’s attentional focus, subsequently influencing task performance in real time. Specifically, the dual-path attentional focus model reveals that the direction of conflict experiences guides the trajectory of self-regulatory processes, while the intensity of these processes is modulated by the boundaries within which the conflict is experienced. By conceptualizing individual team members as intentional agents actively regulating their attention between conflict-related concerns and ongoing task demands, we provide a theoretical explanation for the intra-individual variability observed in the relationship between conflict experiences and task performance.
text: Second, our work offers a new intra-individual cognitive process framework, explaining how and why structural elements of conflict experiences impact individual attentional focus and thereby impact task performance in real time. More specifically, the dual-path attentional focus model explains that the direction of conflict experiences governs the path of self-regulatory processes, while the magnitudes of these paths are altered by the boundary of conflict experiences. By considering individual team members as volitional agents in the regulatory process of attentional focus between the conflict issues and the task engagement, we offer a theoretical rationale underlying the intra-individual variances in conflict–task performance relationships.

use: The potential of machine learning (ML) is so immense that some have predicted it may render theory obsolete (Anderson, 2008; Steadman, 2013). Kitchin (2014: 3) provocatively suggested that “the volume of data, accompanied by techniques that can reveal their inherent truth, enables data to speak for themselves free of theory [emphasis added].” Echoing this sentiment, Wired magazine published a boldly titled article, “The End of Theory: The Data Deluge Makes the Scientific Method Obsolete,” in which then editor-in-chief Chris Anderson argued that in the age of ML, “science can advance even without coherent models, unified theories, or really any mechanistic explanation at all” (Anderson, 2008).
text: So great is the promise of ML that some have predicted its progress will doom theory to obsolescence (Anderson, 2008; Steadman, 2013). Kitchin (2014: 3) made the case that "the volume of data, accompanied by techniques that can reveal their inherent truth, enables data to speak for themselves free of theory [emphasis added]." Similarly, Wired magazine published the provocatively titled piece "End of Theory: Data Deluge Makes the Scientific Method Obsolete," in which its then editor-in-chief argued that in this new era of ML, "science can advance even without coherent models, unified theories, or really any mechanistic explanation at all" (Anderson, 2008).

use: Employees are facing distress at unprecedented levels, with 44% identifying work as a primary source of stress in their lives (Gallup State of the Global Workplace Report, 2023). This workplace distress can stem from a range of factors, including excessive demands (e.g., long hours or constant urgency), insufficient engagement (e.g., reduced hours or looming layoffs), or adverse working environments (e.g., interpersonal tensions, bullying, or abusive supervision). Recent data indicates that 62% of employees experience extreme forms of distress they perceive as beyond their control (American Institute of Stress, n.d.), underscoring that distress is not only pervasive but also an inescapable feature of organizational life (Frost et al., 2000).
text: Employees are experiencing distress at unprecedented rates, with 44% of people reporting work as a major stressor in their lives (Gallup State of the Global Workplace Report, 2023). Distress at work can range from excessive work (e.g., increased hours or urgency) to insufficient work (e.g., reduced hours or threats of layoffs) to unpleasant working conditions (e.g., interpersonal conflicts, bullying, and abusive supervision). Recent reports show that 62% of employees experience extreme levels of distress that they feel are outside of their control (American Institute of Stress, n.d.), demonstrating that distress is a significant and inescapable reality of organizational life (Frost et al., 2000).

use: Another valuable and increasingly relevant perspective comes from system justification theory, a framework that has only recently begun to gain traction in the third-party literature (David et al., 2023; Dodson et al., 2023). System justification theory posits that individuals may rationalize or defend unfavorable conditions, even when doing so conflicts with their own long-term interests (Jost, 2019; Jost & Banaji, 1994). Building on earlier work regarding just world beliefs (Lerner, 1980), the theory suggests that people are driven by a psychological need to perceive the world as structured, predictable, and fair. As a result, observers may downplay negative responses—or even accentuate positive reactions—when confronted with adverse circumstances, motivated by a desire to maintain the legitimacy and stability of the broader social system (Jost, 2019).
text: Another promising complementary lens is system justification theory, which has only recently been applied in the third-party literature (David et al., 2023; Dodson et al., 2023). System justification theory argues that observers may justify unfortunate circumstances even at the expense of their own long-term interests (Jost, 2019; Jost & Banaji, 1994). Integrating earlier arguments about just world beliefs (Lerner, 1980), the theory argues that observers are motivated by a need to believe the world is orderly, predictable, and fair. Observers may therefore exhibit muted negative reactions (or amplified positive reactions) to unfortunate circumstances out of a desire to preserve the larger system (Jost, 2019).

use: We disclose all relevant methodological details, including sample sizes, data exclusions, experimental manipulations, and measures used across our studies. Screen captures of the experimental materials are provided as supplementary online resources via the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/sb7mj/. Full datasets, analysis code, preregistration protocols, and reproducible scripts for all statistical analyses and figures presented in the article are also accessible through the same OSF link. This research received ethical approval from the Harvard University Area Committee on the Use of Human Subjects (Protocol IRB15-1845).
text: We report all sample sizes, data exclusions, all manipulations, and all measures in the studies. Screen captures of the experimental materials are available as additional online material via the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/sb7mj/. The complete data, code to reproduce all statistical analyses and figures in the article, and the preregistration reports are also available via the same link. All our studies were preregistered on AsPredicted. We conducted all our analyses using R version 4.3.3 and the latest version of packages available as of March 2, 2024. The research was approved by the Harvard University Area Committee on the Use of Human Subjects (IRB15-1845).

use: Before turning to the theoretical and practical implications of our findings, it is important to acknowledge several key limitations. While our aim was to explore psychological ownership for the organization across a diverse range of occupations and organizational contexts, our study remains constrained by its specific cultural and geographical setting. Future research should investigate psychological ownership in alternative contexts—particularly in European countries where differing legal frameworks and social welfare systems may shape how employees perceive and experience ownership (Rousseau & Shperling, 2003). Additionally, we encourage studies in non-Western environments, where cultural norms such as collectivism and strong role-based expectations may attenuate the influence of psychological ownership on employee behaviors.
text: Before we discuss the theoretical and practical implications of our research, we note several important limitations. Although our objective was to examine psychological ownership for the organization across a broad range of jobs in a variety of settings, our study is bounded by cultural and geographic factors. Future research should examine psychological ownership in different contexts, such as in European settings where different legal arrangements and social programs may influence employee conceptualizations of ownership (Rousseau & Shperling, 2003). We also recommend research in non-Western settings where cultural values such as collectivism and role obligations may reduce the effects of psychological ownership on employee behavior.

use: To address this issue, we begin by organizing clothing characteristics into psychologically meaningful dimensions. We then draw on attribution theory and the stereotype content model (SCM) to construct a conceptual framework that clarifies the distinct messages these dimensions convey, connects them to outcomes relevant for organizations, and identifies the mechanisms through which these effects are transmitted. We propose that observers form attributions based on the wearer's clothing characteristics, which influence their stereotypical modes of information processing. These attributions, in turn, shape the observer’s behavior toward the wearer (see Figure 1). Furthermore, variables related to the wearer, the observer, and the broader context serve as moderators of the initial links in this mediated process.
text: To address this issue, we first organize characteristics into psychologically meaningful dimensions. Then, we use attribution theory and the stereotype content model (SCM) to develop a conceptual model to explain the different messages conveyed by these dimensions, to link these dimensions to organizationally relevant outcomes, and to identify the mechanisms that transmit effects. We argue that characteristics of the wearer's clothing trigger an observer's attributions by affecting their stereotypical information processing. The attribution process then affects the observer's behavior vis-Ã -vis the wearer (see Figure 1). Factors relating to the wearer, the observer, and the context then moderate the first-stage linkages in this mediation model.

use: We propose that the inability to mentally disengage from work during leisure time influences subsequent emotional states through distinct forms of job-related cognition. These cognitions encompass not only reflections on events from the past workday but also anticipatory thoughts such as worries and planning regarding future work activities (Song et al., 2024). To offer a more nuanced understanding of job-related cognition, we draw on prior conceptualizations of work-related thinking during off-job time (Meier et al., 2016; Querstret & Cropley, 2012) and integrate them with the framework of control theory (Carver & Scheier, 1998). More precisely, our approach is informed by control-theoretical perspectives on repetitive thought and rumination (Martin & Tesser, 1996; Watkins, 2008).
text: We propose that lack of detachment from work during leisure time relates to subsequent affective states via various types of job-related cognitions. These cognitions include reflections about what has happened at work during the past workday as well as worries and plans for the future (Song et al., 2024). To develop a differentiated view on job-related cognitions, we build on earlier conceptualizations of job-related cognitions during leisure time (Meier et al., 2016; Querstret & Cropley, 2012) and align them with a control-theory approach (Carver & Scheier, 1998). Specifically, we build on control-theory perspectives on repetitive thought and rumination (Martin & Tesser, 1996; Watkins, 2008).

use: Motivational energy doesn’t just arise from tight-knit partnerships or dynamic duos—it can be fueled by our connections to larger groups, communities, and organizations. People often strive for a sense of belonging, the pursuit of status, or the desire to make a difference for those they may never meet. Scientists, for instance, developed COVID-19 vaccines at unprecedented speed to help end a global crisis, while climate activists dedicate themselves to securing a better future for generations yet to come. Yet, as anyone who has suffered under a toxic manager or been drained by a relentless critic knows, relationships aren’t always inspiring—they can just as easily sap motivation. One can only imagine how many more summits might have been scaled if not for the demoralizing influence of those who hold others back.
text: Motivating relationships are not limited to dynamic duos. Motivation can be driven by connections to broader groups and organizations, where people aim for belonging, status, or impact on complete strangers. Scientists have produced COVID vaccines in record time to end a global pandemic, and climate activists have worked to create a better world for generations who have not even been born yet. Of course, as anyone who has ever had an abusive boss or a discouraging Dementor can attest, relationships can also be demotivating. We can only wonder how many more peaks might have been reached if not for the deflating effects of other people.

use: We therefore propose that deonance theory should be understood as a valuable lens for interpreting many of the relationships explored in the third-party literature—particularly when expanded to incorporate empathy. However, it should be complemented by other theoretical perspectives that are better suited to understanding nonmoral emotions. Broader appraisal theories of emotion, such as Lazarus’s (1991) framework, may serve as useful counterparts. For instance, in the analysis of schadenfreude, Li et al.’s (2019) adaptation of Lazarus’s theory provides meaningful insights. Within this broader context, deonance theory can be positioned as a specific appraisal theory—especially attuned to the appraisals and action tendencies linked to moral emotions (Folger & Skarlicki, 2005; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger & Glerum, 2015).
text: We therefore argue that deonance theory should be viewed as a lens that explains many of the relationships in the third-party literature—especially if expanded to include empathy—but that it should be paired with other lenses more suited to nonmoral emotions. More general appraisal theories of emotion, like Lazarus’s (1991) theory, could be helpful complements. In the case of schadenfreude, Li et al.’s (2019) adaptation of Lazarus’s (1991) theory offers helpful guidance. Deonance theory would then be viewed as one specific appraisal theory—one uniquely relevant to appraisals and action tendencies associated with moral emotions (Folger & Skarlicki, 2005; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger & Glerum, 2015).

use:
In summary, when a work context is interpreted at a higher (i.e., more abstract) level of construal, several effects may follow: (a) harmful events are likely to be perceived as less immediate or probable, rendering harm-prevention tasks as less urgent or essential in the present moment; (b) prompts or justifications for empathizing with, considering, and protecting others may become less prominent; and (c) attention may shift away from concrete details and situational elements that are critical for ensuring safety and preventing harm. Accordingly, we propose:
Hypothesis 1: Work context construal is negatively related to perceived harm. As individuals construe a work context at a higher (more abstract) level, their perception of potential harm will decrease.
text:
In sum, the more a given work context is construed at a higher (more abstract) level, (a) harmful events may be viewed as less likely and more hypothetical, so harm-preventing tasks could be seen as less critical in the moment; (b) cues and rationales for caring for, thinking of, and protecting others may be less salient; and (c) focus will likely be drawn to specific details and features of situations that are key to safety and harm prevention. Therefore:
Hypothesis 1: Work context construal is negatively associated with perceived harm. As a work context is construed at a higher (more abstract) level, less harm will be perceived.

use: The meaning maintenance model posits that disruptions heighten individuals’ sensitivity to a range of foundational needs, and that the frustration or satisfaction of these needs drives efforts to preserve a sense of meaning (Heine et al., 2006). Research on needs has a rich heritage within organizational behavior and both social and applied psychology (e.g., Deci & Ryan, 2000; Dweck, 2017), though existing frameworks vary in terminology and levels of conceptual abstraction. In line with our broader theoretical approach—and in light of the central importance of both the inherited legacy and the departed legator—we focus on needs situated at the crossroads of the meaning, psychological ownership, and identity literatures.
text: The meaning maintenance model argues that such disruptions make individuals more sensitive to a variety of underlying needs, with the frustration or fulfillment of those needs fueling the attempts to maintain meaning (Heine et al., 2006). There is a long history of examining needs in organizational behavior and social or applied psychology (e.g., Deci & Ryan, 2000; Dweck, 2017), with relevant taxonomies differing in terminology and level of conceptual aggregation. Given our overarching framework, and given the key roles played by the legacy being inherited and the legator who has departed, we examined needs that lie at the intersection of the meaning, psychological ownership, and identity literatures.

use: Second, scholarly perspectives on how the process of forming problem representations unfolds over time differ considerably. Some studies stress the importance of fully developing the problem representation before any solutions are proposed (Baer, Dirks, & Nickerson, 2013; Park & Baer, 2022). In contrast, other research allows for the idea that problem understanding can emerge after initial solutions have been proposed—or even in parallel with solution development (Dorst & Cross, 2001; Harvey & Kou, 2013). This lack of consensus on the temporal sequencing of these activities introduces ambiguity regarding both the timing and progression of the problem representation process, complicating efforts to determine when and how it should be initiated and pursued.
text: Second, conceptualizations of how the process of developing problem representations unfolds over time also vary, with some studies emphasizing the need for the process to occur and conclude prior to the development of any solutions (Baer, Dirks, & Nickerson, 2013; Park & Baer, 2022), while others invite the possibility that an understanding of the problem may be developed after some solutions have been derived, or even concurrently (Dorst & Cross, 2001; Harvey & Kou, 2013). Naturally, ambiguity regarding the temporal sequencing of the activities involved in the development of problem representations makes it difficult to offer insights into when and how this process needs to be initiated and advanced.

use: By consistently demonstrating helping behavior toward fellow unit members, challengers convey a clear and powerful signal that such actions are both normative and valued. This practice can prompt other unit members to reassess their beliefs about what constitutes appropriate behavior within the group (March, 1994) and to recalibrate their expectations regarding how their colleagues are likely to act (Elster, 1989). Through ongoing modeling of prosocial conduct, challengers can undermine prevailing norms of self-interest and legitimize a culture of support. For instance, in a field study conducted at a Fortune 500 retail company, Grant, Dutton, and Rosso (2008) found that employees who observed coworkers assisting others in times of need were more likely to view such behavior as legitimate and engage in it themselves.
text: By consistently modeling helping behavior toward other unit members, challengers send a clear message that this behavior is both common and desirable. This may lead work unit members to revise their views of what constitutes appropriate action (March, 1994) within the unit and to adjust their expectations of how others in the unit will behave (Elster, 1989). Consistently modeling helping behavior can thus break down norms of self-interest by legitimizing helping behavior. For example, in a field study of a Fortune 500 retail company, Grant, Dutton, and Rosso (2008) found that employees who saw coworkers helping colleagues in need felt that it was more legitimate to do so themselves.

use: The body of literature on time use within organizations is extensive and has given rise to numerous previous reviews. Our article seeks to build upon and complement these existing efforts. Some of these earlier reviews adopt a psychological perspective, focusing either on the individual-level factors that influence time management behaviors (e.g., Claessens, Eerde, Rutte, & Roe, 2007) or on how individuals perceive and engage with time (e.g., Shipp & Cole, 2015). Other reviews aim to advance the integration of time-related constructs—such as duration, timing, and entrainment—and methodological tools—such as sequence analysis, time lags, and activity mapping—into the mainstream of management research (e.g., Ancona, Goodman, Lawrence, & Tushman, 2001; Ancona, Okhuysen, & Perlow, 2001; George & Jones, 2000; Roe, 2008; Sonnentag, 2012).
text: The literature on time use in organizations is vast and has inspired a number of prior reviews. Our article complements these efforts. Some previous reviews take a psychological approach, considering either the individual-level factors that shape time management behaviors (see Claessens, Eerde, Rutte, & Roe, 2007) or the ways in which individuals approach and experience time (see Shipp & Cole, 2015). Other reviews suggest strategies for more effectively integrating time-related concepts (e.g., duration, timing, and entrainment) and methodological tools (e.g., sequence analysis, time lags, and activity mapping) into management research (see Ancona, Goodman, Lawrence, & Tushman, 2001; Ancona, Okhuysen, & Perlow, 2001; George & Jones, 2000; Roe, 2008; Sonnentag, 2012).

use: A foundational principle of role theory holds that roles are transmitted from a sender to a receiver through a process of social exchange unfolding during a role episode (Katz & Kahn, 1966). This exchange is inherently a form of dyadic influence, wherein the sender seeks to persuade the receiver to adopt or internalize the proposed role (Graen & Scandura, 1987; Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991). Remarkably, despite its significance, this classic perspective has garnered limited attention in contemporary studies on role definition and role expansion. To fully grasp how roles are shaped and negotiated, it is essential to examine the interpersonal influence dynamics at play within the exchange between sender and receiver.
text: A core tenet of role theory is that roles are communicated from a sender to a receiver through a social exchange process that occurs over the course of a role episode (Katz & Kahn, 1966). This social exchange constitutes an exercise of dyadic influence, as the sender is attempting to persuade the receiver to accept the role (Graen & Scandura, 1987; Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991). Surprisingly, this classic insight from role theory has received little attention in existing research on role definitions and role expansion. In order to understand the interpersonal influence dynamics of role definitions, it is critical to focus on the exchange between the sender and the receiver.

use: Building on these emerging theoretical perspectives and empirical findings, it becomes crucial to understand how individuals’ psychological experiences shape the effectiveness of their initiative. Grounded in self-determination theory (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Ryan & Deci, 2000), we propose that the link between initiative and performance is moderated by the type of motivation individuals experience in the context of the task at hand—specifically, their levels of autonomous and controlled motivation. We argue that when individuals exhibit high autonomous motivation and low controlled motivation, they engage in initiative driven by personal choice rather than by pressure or obligation. This sense of volition equips them with the psychological resources needed to sustain effective and meaningful initiative over time.
text: Building on these emerging theoretical perspectives and empirical findings, it is important to understand how the psychological experiences of individuals influence the effectiveness of their initiative. Drawing on self-determination theory (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Ryan & Deci, 2000), we propose that the relationship between initiative and performance varies as a function of individuals' autonomous and controlled motivations in the task in which initiative is taken. Specifically, we argue that when individuals experience high autonomous motivation and low controlled motivation toward a task, they take initiative based on choice rather than pressure and obligation, which provides them with the psychological resources necessary to engage in effective forms of initiative over time.

use: Cliques serve as the foundational building blocks of communities. Within networks, tightly interconnected firms form cohesive groupings that enhance the flow and exchange of resources among members. Importantly, a single network community may include multiple overlapping cliques, where some firms participate in more than one group. This overlapping structure enables network communities to reflect not only direct social interactions but also indirect ones—those that occur through partners of partners. As a result, network communities encompass extended chains of social ties, providing a richer and more far-reaching view of the social fabric. We propose that it is precisely this indirect yet familiar access to others within the network that underpins the benefits associated with community membership.
text: Cliques are the essential components that make up communities. Densely interacting firms form close-knit groupings that facilitate resource sharing among group members. It's crucial to remember that a single network community can have several cliques with members that overlap. This overlapping structure allows network communities to account for not only direct social interactions but also indirect ones, which occur through connections between one's partners and the partners' partners. Network communities thus contain a chain of social ties and extend beyond immediate cliques, capturing a more comprehensive picture of social interactions. We anticipate that such indirect yet familiar access to community members is at the heart of the advantages of network communities.

use: Not all forms of competition are equally compelling. Rivalries, in particular, elicit heightened motivation, as individuals become especially driven to outperform opponents with whom they share a history (Kilduff et al., 2010). Through a series of insightful archival and experimental studies, researchers have investigated the motivational impact of rivalries. On the one hand, such rivalries have been shown to reliably enhance performance at both the individual and team levels. For instance, in a study of long-distance runners, participants ran faster when competing against a known rival (Kilduff, 2014). Similarly, professional teams in baseball, basketball, football, and hockey were more likely to succeed in the playoffs when one of their major rivals had performed well—especially if that rival had won the championship the previous year (Pike et al., 2018).
text: Not all competitions are equally motivating. In rivalries, individuals are particularly motivated to defeat an opponent with whom they have a history (Kilduff et al., 2010). In a series of clever archival and experimental studies, researchers have explored the motivational consequences of rivalries. On the one hand, rivalries have clear and consistent performance benefits for both individuals and teams. In a study of long-distance runners, people ran faster when their rivals were in a race (Kilduff, 2014). Further, professional baseball, basketball, football, and hockey teams were more successful in the playoffs if their biggest rival had done well the prior year—”particularly if they had won the championship (Pike et al., 2018).

use: Despite its strengths, the experimental method remains underutilized by many organizational scholars (Highhouse, in press). A prevailing criticism is that experiments often occur in laboratory settings, leading to concerns that they produce a limited "science of the sophomore"—research findings that may lack relevance and generalizability to real-world employees and organizational contexts (e.g., Gordon, Slade, & Schmitt, 1986). Additionally, critics argue that laboratory experiments suffer from weak external validity. According to this view, experiments do not so much test the truth of a hypothesis as they assess whether the researcher can skillfully construct artificial conditions that validate what is already intuitively obvious: “not whether a hypothesis is true, but rather whether the experimenter is a sufficiently ingenious stage manager to produce in the laboratory conditions which demonstrate that an obviously true hypothesis is correct” (McGuire, 1973, p. 449).
text: Despite its advantages, however, many organizational scholars shy away from the experimental method (Highhouse, in press). A common critique of experiments is that because they often take place in the laboratory, they create only a "science of the sophomore," lacking generalizability to the employees and organizational phenomena that matter to our research (e.g., Gordon, Slade, & Schmitt, 1986). Researchers have also criticized laboratory experiments for a lack of external validity by arguing that they test "not whether a hypothesis is true, but rather whether the experimenter is a sufficiently ingenious stage manager to produce in the laboratory conditions which demonstrate that an obviously true hypothesis is correct" (McGuire, 1973, p. 449).

use: Employment interviews place substantial cognitive demands on interviewees, requiring them to simultaneously process complex and often unfamiliar questions, retrieve relevant past experiences, and engage in impression management (König et al., 2007). Given this cognitive load, it is not surprising that general mental ability (GMA) is a strong predictor of interview performance (Roth & Huffcutt, 2013). GMA is linked not only to job-relevant knowledge, skills, and abilities but also to the capacity to discern evaluative criteria, enabling interviewees to formulate responses that align with interview expectations (Kleinmann et al., 2011; König et al., 2007). While prior research has explored GMA’s indirect influence on interview ratings, its direct relationship with observable interviewee behavior remains underexplored.
text: Employment interviews require interviewees to simultaneously manage multiple cognitive demands, including responding to (often novel and complex) questions, recalling past experiences, and engaging in impression management (König et al., 2007). Given these cognitive demands, it is unsurprising that GMA strongly predicts interview outcomes (Roth & Huffcutt, 2013). GMA is known to be associated not only with job-relevant knowledge, skills, and abilities but also with the ability to identify the evaluation criteria, which helps interviewees generate appropriate interview answers (Kleinmann et al., 2011; König et al., 2007). Although GMA’s indirect effect on interview ratings has received research attention, the direct relationship between GMA (or cognitive abilities, more broadly) and interviewee behavior is understudied.

use: Supervised learning is commonly applied to two core types of problems: regression and classification (Brink, Richards, & Fetherolf, 2016). Regression focuses on predicting a continuous output value for each instance based on input variables, whereas classification pertains to assigning input data to one of several predefined, discrete categories. In supervised machine learning, researchers typically adopt a low tolerance for unexpected outcomes, instead emphasizing clearly defined outcome variables and pre-established categories suited for deductive, theory-driven analysis. Since the goal is often to enhance predictive accuracy using a limited, predetermined set of variables, supervised learning prioritizes model optimization through structured, iterative refinement and logical deduction.
text: Supervised learning is generally leveraged toward two types of problems: regression problems and classification problems (Brink, Richards, & Fetherolf, 2016). Regression involves predicting the output value on a criteria of interest for each individual based on input data, while classification involves determining the discrete class to which a sample datum belongs. In supervised ML, the researcher generally assumes a low tolerance for surprise results and specifies outcome instructions and pre-established categories appropriate for deductive a priori theory testing. As the researcher is likely leveraging the algorithm to refine predictions between a set of finite and pre-specified variables, a supervised ML search would seek to maximize model fit through disciplined refinement and deduction.

use: Consensus is not a static outcome but a dynamic process that unfolds over time. Drawing on open systems theory (Katz & Kahn, 1978), contextual factors—including external events affecting team members—can shape how consensus develops within teams (Kozlowski & Chao, 2012). Some of these factors may inhibit or disrupt consensus-building, while others may facilitate or accelerate it (Kozlowski, 2015; Kozlowski & Klein, 2000). In light of the abrupt onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, we adopted a phenomenon-driven approach (Hambrick, 2007) to deepen our understanding of how consensus emerges (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000) in virtual teams during a time of external crisis. The COVID-19 pandemic introduced two distinct and evolving contextual features that, though not specific to work itself, significantly influenced the professional lives of many individuals (Caligiuri et al., 2020).
text: Consensus is a process that emerges over time. According to open systems theory (Katz & Kahn, 1978), contextual facets (i.e., events) related to team members can affect the consensus emergence process (Kozlowski & Chao, 2012). While some may weaken or hinder consensus, others accelerate or enhance it (Kozlowski, 2015; Kozlowski & Klein, 2000). Given the sudden occurrence of the COVID-19 pandemic, we used a phenomenon-driven approach (Hambrick, 2007) to expand our understanding of consensus emergence (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000) in virtual teams during an external crisis. The COVID-19 pandemic has featured two dynamic key facets that, while not work-specific, had an impact on the work lives of many individuals (Caligiuri et al., 2020).

use: Conscientiousness is a broad personality trait that encompasses several more specific facets, including self-efficacy, orderliness, dutifulness (or dependability), achievement-striving, self-discipline, and cautiousness (Costa & McCrae, 1992; Goldberg, 1990; Johnson, 2014). Meta-analytic findings indicate that among these facets, dutifulness and achievement-striving are more strongly associated with key work outcomes—namely, higher task performance, reduced counterproductive work behavior, and enhanced overall job performance (Dudley et al., 2006; Ones et al., 2007). Importantly, conscientiousness adds predictive value beyond cognitive ability when assessing job performance. Moreover, unlike cognitive ability, conscientiousness is not associated with substantial racial group differences (Foldes et al., 2008), positioning it as a valuable selection tool that can help minimize adverse impact while effectively forecasting employee performance (Hogan, 2005; Ones et al., 2007).
text: Conscientiousness is a general trait that encompasses the narrower facets of self-efficacy, orderliness, dutifulness or dependability, achievement-striving, self-discipline, and cautiousness (Costa & McCrae, 1992; Goldberg, 1990; Johnson, 2014). Meta-analyses examining associations between conscientiousness facets and work performance suggest that, relative to other facets, dutifulness and achievement-striving have stronger positive associations with task performance, avoidance of counterproductive work behavior, and overall job performance (Dudley et al., 2006; Ones et al., 2007). Conscientiousness shows incremental validity above cognitive ability, yet unlike cognitive ability, conscientiousness does not produce large differences by race (Foldes et al., 2008), making it an attractive way to reduce adverse impact during selection while also predicting performance (Hogan, 2005; Ones et al., 2007).

use: During the 1990s and 2000s, various dimensions of organizational justice were empirically linked to key social exchange cognitions. For instance, Konovsky and Pugh (1994) found that both distributive and procedural justice were positively related to employees' trust in their supervisors in a hospital setting. Similarly, Moorman et al. (1998) demonstrated a connection between procedural justice and perceptions of organizational support, also using a sample of hospital employees.
