In research focusing on layoff survivors within pharmaceutical companies, Kernan and Hanges (2002) showed that procedural justice was associated with organizational commitment, while interpersonal and informational justice served as predictors of trust in management. Lastly, Karriker and Williams (2009), using a sample spanning multiple organizations, found that distributive, procedural, and interpersonal justice were all significantly related to employees’ perceptions of high-quality relationships with both supervisors and the broader organization.
text: The various justice dimensions were linked to several social exchange cognitions in the 1990s and 2000s. For example, Konovsky and Pugh (1994) linked distributive and procedural justice to trust in one's supervisor in a sample of hospital employees. Moorman et al. (1998) linked procedural justice to the sense that one's organization was supportive, also in a sample of hospital employees. In a study of layoff survivors in pharmaceutical firms, Kernan and Hanges (2002) linked procedural justice to organizational commitment, with interpersonal and informational justice predicting trust in management. Finally, in a multi-organization sample, Karriker and Williams (2009) linked distributive, procedural, and interpersonal justice to perceptions that supervisor and organizational relationships were of high quality.

use: While gender scholars often conceptualize agency (also referred to as competence; Fiske et al., 2002) as a singular dimension, it actually encompasses multiple facets, including dominance, status, and ability. Although women are generally stereotyped as deficient in all three, the dimension of dominance—defined as the capacity to influence others through control over resources or the use of sanctions (Emerson, 1962; Magee & Galinsky, 2008; Anderson, Hildreth, & Howland, 2015)—holds particular significance for women in leadership roles. A perception of low dominance poses a distinct disadvantage for leaders. Indeed, dominance inferred from facial features or vocal cues is a strong predictor of electoral success, especially in Western contexts (Todorov et al., 2005; Antonakis & Dalgas, 2009; Klofstad, Anderson, & Peters, 2012). Western audiences, in particular, tend to favor leaders who project dominance.
text: Although gender scholars typically examine agency (also termed competence; Fiske et al., 2002) as a unidimensional construct, the concept comprises several subdimensions, including dominance, status, and ability. Women are stereotyped as lacking in all three, but dominance, defined as influence over others via control of resources or punishment (Emerson, 1962; Magee and Galinsky, 2008; Anderson, Hildreth, and Howland, 2015), is especially relevant to women leaders. Being seen as lacking dominance is a particular burden for leaders. Dominance inferred from a leader's face or voice is a robust predictor of election outcomes, particularly in Western countries (Todorov et al., 2005; Antonakis and Dalgas, 2009; Klofstad, Anderson, and Peters, 2012). Westerners, especially, prefer their leaders dominant.

use: Finally, alternative approaches to evaluating candidate backgrounds have important implications for the interpretive value of narrative data. For instance, meta-analytic evidence indicates that employment interviews often capture a wide range of qualities, including personality traits, social competence, judgment, job knowledge, and cognitive ability (Huffcutt et al., 2001; Salgado & Moscoso, 2002). Similarly, research on accomplishment records suggests that descriptions of candidates’ prior achievements may provide more meaningful insights than purely quantitative measures like years of experience (Hough, 1984). In addition, qualitative accounts often capture more subtle or "elusive" elements of prior work—such as task difficulty and job complexity—which better reflect the "density" and developmental depth of those experiences (Tesluk & Jacobs, 1998, pp. 323, 329).
text: Finally, other methods of assessing candidate backgrounds bear on the meaningfulness of narrative data. For example, meta-analytic evidence shows that personality, social skills, judgment, job knowledge, and mental ability are frequently captured in employment interviews (Huffcutt et al., 2001; Salgado & Moscoso, 2002). Research on accomplishment records similarly illustrates that information on what candidates achieved in past jobs may be more relevant than numeric data such as years of experience (Hough, 1984). Moreover, qualitative components may reflect the more "elusive" aspects of work experience, such as task difficulty and job complexity (Tesluk & Jacobs, 1998, p. 323), which better assess the "density" or developmental intensity of the experiences (Tesluk & Jacobs, 1998, p. 329).

use: To evaluate our theoretical claims, we draw on longitudinal data from syndication networks within the Chinese venture capital (VC) industry. In this context, VC firms typically co-invest with others rather than acting independently, resulting in the formation of interorganizational networks (Hochberg, Ljungqvist, & Lu, 2007; Wang et al., 2022). A key methodological challenge in assessing the influence of community affiliations lies in accurately identifying the boundaries of network communities. We address this challenge by employing recent advances in community-detection algorithms (Palla, Barabási, & Vicsek, 2007; Palla, Derényi, Farkas, & Vicsek, 2005). These techniques allow us to deconstruct the larger network into smaller, analytically tractable components—specifically, overlapping communities—where nodes are densely interconnected within each local network (Vedres & Stark, 2010).
text: To test our arguments, we analyze longitudinal data on syndication networks in the Chinese venture capital (VC) industry. VC firms usually syndicate investments with others rather than investing alone, fostering interorganizational networks (Hochberg, Ljungqvist, & Lu, 2007; Wang et al., 2022). A primary challenge in estimating the effect of community affiliations is to identify the boundaries of network communities. We overcome this challenge by leveraging recent advances in community-detection technology (Palla, Barabási, & Vicsek, 2007; Palla, Derényi, Farkas, & Vicsek, 2005). This technology decomposes a whole network into small, comparable parts in such a way that local networks in each part are composed of densely connected nodes, called overlapping communities (Vedres & Stark, 2010).

use: By taking the interconnectedness of categories seriously, this paper not only explores how coherence within a single category influences its viability, but also investigates how coherence across a network of related categories plays a critical role. We build on emerging scholarship that highlights how “intra-category and inter-category relations shift as actors use and remodel the elements of a broader meaning system” (Lo et al., 2020: 86). We contend that when actors introduce new products and engage their audiences, they often deviate in meaningful ways from previously established practices (Lounsbury & Crumley, 2007; Zhao et al., 2018). These deviations reshape coherence both within the focal category and in relation to adjacent categories, thereby influencing the future viability of the focal category.
text: Taking the relatedness of categories seriously, this paper not only explains how coherence within a category shapes its viability, but additionally examines the effect of coherence across a set of related categories. We build on recent insights that have drawn attention to how “intra-category and inter-category relations shift as actors use and remodel the elements of a broader meaning system” (Lo et al., 2020: 86). We argue that, as actors introduce new products and mobilize their audiences, they introduce meaningful deviations from what others have done before (Lounsbury & Crumley, 2007; Zhao et al., 2018). This shifts coherence within the focal category and across related categories, which affects the subsequent viability of a focal category.

use: Trust plays a vital role in fostering effective working relationships, particularly between supervisors and subordinates (Lewicki & Bunker, 1996; Lewicki et al., 2006; Mayer et al., 1995; S. Wang et al., 2010; Weiss et al., 2021). When subordinates trust their supervisors, they are more likely to exhibit positive behavioral outcomes—such as enhanced performance and organizational citizenship behaviors—as well as favorable attitudinal outcomes, including greater job satisfaction and stronger organizational commitment (Dirks & Ferrin, 2002; Konovsky & Pugh, 1994; Rich, 1997). Conversely, the absence of trust can damage organizational functioning by weakening both interpersonal and group-level relationships. Consistent with previous literature, we define trust as a willingness to accept vulnerability in relation to another party, grounded in positive expectations of that party’s intentions or behavior (Mayer et al., 1995; Rousseau et al., 1998).
text: Trust is essential for building effective working relationships, especially supervisor-subordinate relationships (Lewicki & Bunker, 1996; Lewicki et al., 2006; Mayer et al., 1995; S. Wang et al., 2010; Weiss et al., 2021). Trust in supervisors enables subordinates to achieve positive behavioral (e.g., performance and organizational citizenship behaviors) and attitudinal outcomes (e.g., job satisfaction and organizational commitment; Dirks & Ferrin, 2002; Konovsky & Pugh, 1994; Rich, 1997). In contrast, a lack of trust harms organizations by eroding relationships at the dyadic and group level. In line with prior research, we define trust as the willingness to be vulnerable to a counterpart based on positive expectations of the counterpart (Mayer et al., 1995; Rousseau et al., 1998).

use: Commitment is a central concept in understanding the relationship between individuals and organizations (Mathieu & Zajac, 1990; Mowday & Sutton, 1993). In this study, we concentrate on organizations as the target of employee commitment for both theoretical and practical reasons. Theoretically, our aim is to advance existing knowledge on how organizational programs influence employees’ psychological attachment to their work organizations. Practically, organizational commitment serves as a robust predictor of reduced absenteeism and turnover (for a comprehensive review, see Meyer, Stanley, Herscovitch, & Topolnytsky, 2002). Gaining deeper insight into how organizational commitment is formed can support efforts to improve employee retention—an especially critical issue in sectors such as retail and services, where high turnover continues to pose a significant challenge (Cascio, 2003).
text: Commitment is a key concept for explaining relationships between individuals and organizations (Mathieu & Zajac, 1990; Mowday & Sutton, 1993). We focus on organizations as commitment targets for both theoretical and practical reasons. From a theoretical perspective, we seek to deepen existing knowledge about how organizational programs affect employees' psychological attachments to their work organizations. From a practical perspective, organizational commitment is a strong predictor of decreased absenteeism and turnover (for a review, see Meyer, Stanley, Herscovitch, and Topolnytsky [2002]). Understanding how organizational commitment develops can assist organizations in efforts to increase employee retention, which is important in industries in which organizations struggle to retain employees, such as the retail and service industries (Cascio, 2003).

use: We define AI management as a collection of computational processes that fulfill managerial functions by making executive decisions that directly impact individuals—such as assigning tasks or evaluating performance (Broussard, 2018; Felten et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2015; Logg et al., 2019; Rogers, 1987; Yu et al., 2017). The adoption of AI management systems is becoming increasingly widespread in organizations, assuming roles traditionally filled by humans, including recruitment and performance appraisal (Biswas, 2021; Brynjolfsson & McAfee, 2017; Cheng & Hackett, 2021; Marr, 2018; Raisch & Krakowski, 2021; Simonite, 2015). We propose that the perceived benevolence of AI, in comparison to human managers, will be a key factor influencing trust in and acceptance of these systems. Furthermore, we explore the distinctive role emotions may play in shaping this perception and its consequences.
text: We define AI management as a set of computational procedures that serve a management role and make executive decisions that influence target individuals (e.g., work assignment and performance appraisal; Broussard, 2018; Felten et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2015; Logg et al., 2019; Rogers, 1987; Yu et al., 2017). AI management is increasingly common within organizations, taking on responsibilities such as recruitment and performance evaluation (Biswas, 2021; Brynjolfsson & McAfee, 2017; Cheng & Hackett, 2021; Marr, 2018; Raisch & Krakowski, 2021; Simonite, 2015). We anticipate that the perceived benevolence of AI management, versus human management, will influence trust and acceptance of these systems, and we consider the unique role that emotions will play in this relationship.

use: Furthermore, our structural equation modeling results reinforce the idea that behavioral responses to the perpetrator are driven primarily by emotion, rather than a combination of emotion and cognition. While anger toward the perpetrator was linked to more negative evaluations of them, those evaluations did not mediate the relationship between witnessing mistreatment and engaging in antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator. Instead, this relationship was mediated exclusively by anger. This finding supports the perspective that swift, emotionally charged reactions to mistreatment may serve an adaptive evolutionary function—suggesting that reacting quickly and forcefully (“fighting fire with fire”) may serve to deter future moral transgressions (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015).
text: Moreover, our structural equation modeling results supported the notion that behavioral reactions to the perpetrator are fueled by emotion rather than a mix of emotion and cognition. Although anger toward the perpetrator was associated with more negative evaluations of the perpetrator, those evaluations did not play a mediating role in the connection between observing mistreatment and antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator. Instead, that connection was mediated solely by anger toward the perpetrator. That result lends support to the view that rapid and not thoroughly considered responses to mistreatment can be evolutionarily adaptive—that “fighting fire with fire” quickly can prevent moral violations in the future (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015).

use: Building on the above definition of "vigilance," we conceptualize "vigilant leadership" as a set of leader behaviors aimed at encouraging others to anticipate potential challenges and proactively prepare for unforeseen setbacks. Several elements of this definition are worth highlighting. First, vigilant leadership should not be understood as a fixed trait, an individual skill, or a psychological condition inherent to the leader. Instead, our definition deliberately frames vigilant leadership as an influence process—one that actively redirects followers’ attention toward emerging threats and motivates them to take preventive action. Similar to other forms of change-oriented leadership observed across organizational hierarchies—from senior executives to frontline team leaders (Gerpott et al., 2019)—vigilant leadership can manifest at all levels within an organization.
text: Drawing on the aforementioned definition of "vigilance," we define "vigilant leadership" as leader behaviors that are intended to stimulate others to focus on potential challenges and respond to future unexpected setbacks. A few aspects of this definition are noteworthy. First, vigilant leadership is not a trait, a skill, or a psychological state possessed by a leader. Rather, our definition explicitly focuses on vigilant leadership as an influence process that shifts followers' attention to potential threats and encourages followers to take precautionary steps to avoid them. Like other change-oriented leadership behaviors that can be seen from top executives to team leaders (Gerpott et al., 2019), vigilant leadership can also be found at all levels of an organization.

use: Organizational scholars have increasingly highlighted the influential role that rankings play in shaping both an organization’s reputation and its status. A substantial body of prior research has shown that rankings serve as key mechanisms for transforming reputations—defined as audience perceptions of an organization based on its historical actions or performance—into tangible and visible comparative hierarchies (Rao, 1994; Rindova et al., 2018; Sharkey, Kovács, & Hsu, 2023). In addition to crystallizing reputations, rankings also affect organizational status, which refers to an organization’s position within a broader social hierarchy (see, e.g., Sauder, 2006; Rider & Tan, 2015; Rindova et al., 2018; Accominotti, Lynn, & Sauder, 2022; Sharkey, Kovács, & Hsu, 2023). This expanding stream of research suggests that rankings, by shaping both reputation and status, significantly influence how various audiences—such as consumers, investors, or stakeholders—evaluate organizations, and in turn, affect key organizational outcomes.
text: Organizational scholars have recognized the significant role of rankings in shaping an organization's reputation and status. Much prior research has documented that rankings play a pivotal role in crystallizing reputations—”the perceptions of organizations based on their past performance or behaviors—”into visible comparative orderings (Rao, 1994; Rindova et al., 2018; Sharkey, Kovács, and Hsu, 2023). Rankings can also influence organizational status, defined as an organization's position in a social hierarchy (see, e.g., Sauder, 2006; Rider and Tan, 2015; Rindova et al., 2018; Accominotti, Lynn, and Sauder, 2022; Sharkey, Kovács, and Hsu, 2023). This growing literature suggests that by influencing organizational reputation and status, rankings impact audience (e.g., consumer, investor, or stakeholder) evaluations of organizations and, therefore, organizational outcomes.

use: Daydreaming represents a distinct form of mind wandering—one that holds particular significance for workplace creativity. To understand its role, we begin by situating daydreaming within the wider domain of mind wandering and the expanding body of research that investigates it. In recent years, scholars have increasingly aligned in their understanding of what constitutes mind wandering and how it diverges from other related constructs in the literature. As summarized in Dane’s (2018: 180) comprehensive review, “mind wandering is a psychological state in which one's thoughts have departed from the task at hand as well as the stimulus environment more generally [emphasis in original].” Put differently, during moments of mind wandering, individuals generate thoughts that are decoupled from their immediate context and current activities.
text: Daydreaming is a specific type of mind wandering and one that is especially relevant for creativity in the workplace. We first consider daydreaming in the broader context of mind wandering and the research surrounding it. Recent years have witnessed increasing convergence among researchers in terms of what mind wandering signifies and how it differs from other concepts in the literature. As noted in Dane's (2018: 180) review of work in this area, "mind wandering is a psychological state in which one's thoughts have departed from the task at hand as well as the stimulus environment more generally [emphasis in original]." In other words, when one's mind is wandering, one is generating thoughts disconnected from one's present circumstances.

use: This evidence aligns with predictions from motivated information processing theory, suggesting that intrinsic motivation—by highlighting interest—directs attention primarily toward novelty rather than usefulness. As Amabile (1996: 118) noted, the early phases of the creative process, “where the novelty of the outcome is importantly determined, may require intrinsic motivation.” However, as the process progresses “toward stages where the novelty of the work (though still important) is less crucial,” other forms of motivation “may serve to focus and energize the individual toward getting the job done in an appropriate way.” This perspective points to the possibility that psychological mechanisms emphasizing usefulness could enable individuals to take the novel ideas sparked by intrinsic motivation and translate them into creative, applicable outcomes.
text: This evidence suggests, as predicted by motivated information processing theory, that by drawing attention to interest, intrinsic motivation cultivates a primary focus on novelty but not necessarily on usefulness. Indeed, Amabile (1996: 118) proposed that early stages of the creative process, "where the novelty of the outcome is importantly determined, may require intrinsic motivation," but in advancing toward "stages where the novelty of the work (though still important) is less crucial," other motivators "may serve to focus and energize the individual toward getting the job done in an appropriate way." This proposition raises the possibility that psychological processes that draw attention to usefulness may enable employees to channel the novel ideas prompted by intrinsic motivation toward creativity.

use: Conceptually, verbal ability is highly pertinent to interviewee performance, as it serves as a strong predictor of interview outcomes (König et al., 2007; Melchers et al., 2009). In fact, some interviews are even regarded as verbally administered cognitive ability assessments (Hunter & Hirsch, 1987). Verbal ability also represents a central facet of general mental ability (GMA), as it underpins a broad range of specific cognitive skills (Carroll, 1993). In light of this, the present study explores AVI machine learning (ML) models trained on performance data from multiple-choice tests measuring either GMA or verbal ability. Compared to self-report measures or observer-based ratings, multiple-choice ability tests are often considered more "objective" tools of assessment (Chamorro-Premuzic & Furnham, 2005), as they are less prone to individual subjective biases (Vazire, 2010; Woo et al., 2022).
text: Conceptually, verbal ability is highly relevant to interviewee behavior as it also strongly predicts interview outcomes (König et al., 2007; Melchers et al., 2009), and some interviews are even viewed as verbally administered cognitive ability tests (Hunter & Hirsch, 1987). Additionally, verbal ability is a key component of GMA because it is involved in a variety of specific abilities (Carroll, 1993). Given this, the current article investigates AVI ML models that were trained on either GMA or verbal ability multiple-choice test scores. Multiple-choice ability tests are considered a more “objective” assessment method compared to self-reports or observer ratings (Chamorro-Premuzic & Furnham, 2005) because they are not susceptible to idiosyncratic subjective biases (Vazire, 2010; Woo et al., 2022).

use: Negotiation is broadly understood as an interaction between two or more parties aimed at reaching an agreement on a matter of shared concern (Goldman & Shapiro, 2012; Mellers et al., 2010). In this context, we concentrate on the most fundamental type of negotiation: distributive negotiation, also known as "value claiming." This form involves dividing a fixed pool of value, where one party’s gain is directly tied to another party’s loss (Pruitt, 1981)—for instance, when negotiating a single issue such as price. While more complex negotiations may incorporate integrative—or "value creating"—elements, where mutual benefits can be achieved without disadvantaging the other party (Bazerman et al., 2000; Shell, 2006; Weingart et al., 1996), it is important to note that most negotiations include at least some distributive elements.
text: Negotiation can be broadly defined as an interaction between two or more parties to attempt to reach an agreement on an issue of mutual interest (Goldman & Shapiro, 2012; Mellers et al., 2010). Here, we focus on the most basic form of negotiations: distributive negotiations, or "value claiming," in which negotiators split a fixed amount of value, and one party's gain comes inherently at the other party's loss (Pruitt, 1981), such as a negotiation on the single issue of price. Although more complex negotiations can entail integrative, or "value generating," aspects, in which one side can benefit without hurting the other side (Bazerman et al., 2000; Shell, 2006; Weingart et al., 1996), most negotiations contain some distributive components.

use: Some scholars have anchored the link between third-party perceptions of mistreatment and empathy for victims by first outlining deonance theory, then turning to empirical research that focuses more directly on victim-centered responses (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023). Others have utilized the peripheral references to deonance theory noted earlier, operating under the assumption that the theory’s treatment of the moral emotion of anger could be extended to encompass empathy as well (Hu et al., 2022). Still others have briefly cited deonance theory before ultimately framing their empathy-related predictions within the broader literature on empathy itself (Liang & Park, 2022a). As an empirical illustration, Liang and Park (2022a) found that third-party observers who witnessed coworkers being sexually harassed by customers experienced feelings of empathy toward the affected coworkers.
text: Some scholars have grounded relationships between third-party perceptions of mistreatment and empathy toward the victim by describing deonance theory before citing more victim-focused empirical studies (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023). Other scholars have drawn on the tangential deonance theory passages cited above while assuming the theory’s handling of the moral emotion of anger could also be applied to the moral emotion of empathy (Hu et al., 2022). Still, other scholars have included a brief citation of deonance theory before grounding empathy predictions in arguments rooted in the empathy literature (Liang & Park, 2022a). To describe an empirical example, Liang and Park (2022a) showed that third parties who observed coworkers being sexually harassed by customers felt empathy toward those coworkers.

use: Sexual harassment encompasses three distinct forms: unwanted sexual attention, sexual coercion, and gender harassment. Of these, unwanted sexual attention and sexual coercion—both involving sexual advances aimed at initiating a sexual relationship (Lim & Cortina, 2005)—have historically dominated the scholarly discourse on sexual harassment (Chawla et al., 2019). In contrast, gender harassment has received comparatively less academic attention, despite being widely prevalent (Chawla et al., 2021; Kabat-Farr & Cortina, 2014; Keplinger et al., 2019). Defined by verbal and nonverbal behaviors that belittle or degrade individuals based on their gender or sex (Cortina & Areguin, 2021), gender harassment is qualitatively different from the other forms. Unlike unwanted sexual attention and sexual coercion, gender harassment does not involve efforts to obtain sexual cooperation, but rather reflects hostility and contempt rooted in gender-based bias (Fitzgerald et al., 1995).
text: Sexual harassment subsumes three manifestations: unwanted sexual attention, sexual coercion, and gender harassment. Unwanted sexual attention and sexual coercion involve sexual advances with the intent of establishing a sexual relationship (Lim & Cortina, 2005) and have tended to be the focus of sexual harassment research (Chawla et al., 2019). In contrast, gender harassment has been studied far less, despite its pervasiveness (Chawla et al., 2021; Kabat-Farr & Cortina, 2014; Keplinger et al., 2019). Reflecting verbal and nonverbal behaviors that condescend and demean victims based on their gender or sex (Cortina & Areguin, 2021), gender harassment is distinct from unwanted sexual attention and sexual coercion because it does not involve soliciting acts of sexual cooperation (Fitzgerald et al., 1995).

use: One key reason for the surge of interest in justice research following Greenberg’s (1987) seminal review was the growing awareness that the four dimensions of justice—distributive, procedural, interpersonal, and informational—exert powerful influences on employee responses (Colquitt et al., 2001). These influences included both main effects and interactions among the justice types (e.g., Brockner & Wiesenfeld, 1996; Skarlicki & Folger, 1997). Notably, many of these responses took the form of cognitions—employees’ reflections on their relationships with their supervisors or organizations. Over time, scholars increasingly interpreted these cognitions as indicators of Blau’s (1964) concept of social exchange relationships (Cropanzano & Byrne, 2000). From this vantage point, such cognitions signal a perception that these relationships are grounded in mutual obligation, trust, psychological commitment, and meaningfulness (Colquitt et al., 2014).
text: One reason why interest in justice exploded in the decades after Greenberg's (1987) review was the realization that distributive, procedural, interpersonal, and informational justice had significant effects on employee reactions (Colquitt et al., 2001) —” including both main and interactive effects (e.g., Brockner & Wiesenfeld, 1996; Skarlicki & Folger, 1997). Among those reactions were cognitions reflecting how employees thought about their relationships with their supervisors or organizations. Over time, the bulk of those cognitions became viewed as indicators of Blau's (1964) concept of a social exchange relationship (Cropanzano & Byrne, 2000). From this perspective, the cognitions reflect the sense that relationships with supervisors or organizations are characterized by mutual obligation, trust, commitment, and significance (Colquitt et al., 2014).

use: The cognitively demanding nature of task proactivity is, in turn, likely to lead to mental fatigue (Borragán et al., 2017; Trejo et al., 2015). Mental fatigue refers to a decline in cognitive performance resulting from sustained mental effort (Ishii et al., 2014). It is distinct from the subjective sensation of “feeling fatigued,” although the two can be related, they do not always coincide (Ackerman & Kanfer, 2009; Hockey, 2011). “Feeling fatigued” is more closely linked to motivational states and may occur even in the absence of observable cognitive decline (Ackerman & Kanfer, 2009), whereas mental fatigue corresponds to measurable reductions in cognitive performance. As such, the effects of the cognitive demands inherent in task proactivity are best conceptualized in terms of mental fatigue.
text: The cognitively effortful process of task proactivity is, in turn, likely to result in mental fatigue (Borragán et al., 2017; Trejo et al., 2015). Mental fatigue is defined as a deterioration in cognitive performance resulting from cognitive demands (Ishii et al., 2014). Mental fatigue is different from the subjective experience of "feeling fatigued," even though they are sometimes, but not always, related to each other (Ackerman & Kanfer, 2009; Hockey, 2011). Whereas feeling fatigued involves motivational factors that may manifest in the absence of cognitive performance deterioration (Ackerman & Kanfer, 2009), mental fatigue reflects actual cognitive performance decrements. Therefore, the consequences of the cognitive processes involved in task proactivity are most appropriately described in terms of mental fatigue.

use: Second, we seek to engage with and respond to growing concerns in the leadership literature regarding the use of overly broad or "lumped" theoretical frameworks (Carton, 2022). Such theories—exemplified by transformational leadership—tend to group together a variety of distinct behaviors under a single overarching theme. This approach fails to adequately account for the individual dimensions within the construct, obscuring how each behavior is differently shaped and uniquely impacts outcomes (van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013; Yukl, 1999). In light of these critiques, recent scholarship has advocated for "a wholesale break from the past," urging researchers to focus on leader behaviors that are best conceptualized as unified, coherent constructs (Carton, 2022). In response, we center our investigation on a "split conceptualization of leading" by examining vigilant leadership as a distinct, unidimensional construct.
text: Second, we aim to address concerns that recent scholars have expressed over existing leadership scholarship, which has been criticized for using lumped theories (Carton, 2022). Lumped theories of leadership, such as transformational leadership, assemble a bundle of distinct behaviors that pertain to a common theme and fail to capture how each dimension is shaped and exerts a unique influence on outcomes (van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013; Yukl, 1999). Thus, scholars have recently called for "a wholesale break from the past" to study leader behaviors that can be best understood as a single theoretical construct (Carton, 2022). We answer this call by focusing on a "split conceptualization of leading" and examining vigilant leadership as a different and unidimensional construct.

use: The aim of this article is to review the organizational justice literature, with particular emphasis on the insights and contributions found in articles published in Personnel Psychology. We begin by outlining the foundational research that explored employee perceptions of justice and demonstrated how such perceptions reliably predict key organizational outcomes. We then highlight several significant extensions that have broadened the scope of the field. These include viewing justice as a component of organizational climate, investigating how third parties respond to justice-related events, and examining the motivations behind supervisors' adherence to justice principles. Recognizing the critical role of cultural context in shaping justice experiences—as well as the importance of justice for fostering diversity and inclusion—we conclude our thematic review with focused attention on these increasingly relevant areas.
text: The purpose of this article is to review the organizational justice literature, with special attention paid to the contributions offered by articles published in Personnel Psychology. We begin by describing how foundational work on employee perceptions of justice arose and how it was used to predict important reactions. We then turn to important extensions that helped expand the literature beyond those foundations. Those include conceptualizing justice as an aspect of an organization's climate, examining reactions to justice among third parties, and exploring why supervisors adhere to justice rules. Given the importance of cross-cultural issues when experiencing justice—”and the role that justice plays in diversity and inclusion—”we close the specific sections of our review with additional coverage of those areas.

use: Finally, this study embraces the intricate dynamics of task interdependencies within collectives and deepens our understanding of conflict across team boundaries in multiteam systems (MTSs)—that is, systems in which multiple teams work interdependently toward shared goals (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). In many real-world contexts, cross-team interactions are often carried out by individual representatives rather than through full-team engagement (Davison, Hollenbeck, Barnes, Sleesman, & Ilgen, 2012). Given that team boundaries constitute one of the key structural dimensions shaping conflict experiences, our framework offers new insights into how conflict operates within MTSs. In particular, this study contributes to the MTS literature by illuminating how individual members perceive and navigate differences between intra-team and inter-team interactions.
text: Finally, this study embraces the complex dynamics of task relations in collectives and advances our understanding of conflict across team boundaries in multiteam systems (“MTSs”) (i.e., teams working interdependently with other teams; Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). In many real-world settings, interactions across team boundaries are conducted by individual representatives of different teams rather than entire teams interacting with one another (Davison, Hollenbeck, Barnes, Sleesman, & Ilgen, 2012). As team boundaries are identified as one of the two structural elements of conflict experiences, our framework provides fresh perspectives on the impacts of conflict in MTSs. Specifically, this study enriches the MTS literature by shedding light on the distinctions among within and between team interactions as experienced by individual members.

use: Multiple studies have demonstrated that efforts to uphold cultural worldviews and maintain personal significance play a key role in buffering individuals against the existential anxiety brought on by thoughts of mortality. For instance, research shows that when individuals are made aware of their own death, receiving positive feedback can significantly reduce both self-reported death anxiety and physiological indicators of distress (see Pyszczynski, Greenberg, Solomon, Arndt, & Schimel, 2004, for a review). Notably, the psychological effects triggered by mortality awareness appear to be distinct. Similar reductions in anxiety are not observed in response to other common stressors—such as concerns about future plans or achievements, fear of public speaking, worries about extreme physical pain or academic failure, or even actual poor performance on intelligence assessments (Pyszczynski et al., 1999).
text: Several studies have further shown that these efforts to defend cultural worldviews and personal worth serve the anxiety-buffering function of protecting people against fears of their own mortality. For example, researchers have found that, after inducing death awareness, giving individuals positive feedback reduces self-reported death anxiety and objective measures of physiological arousal (for a review see Pyszczynski, Greenberg, Solomon, Arndt, & Schimel, 2004). Perhaps most importantly, research suggests that these effects of death awareness may be unique; they do not occur in response to other forms of anxiety, such as worries about future plans and success, fears of public speaking, concerns about intense physical pain or failing a test, and actual poor performance on intelligence tests (Pyszczynski et al., 1999).

use: Research on face time expectations consistently reveals that individuals who work longer hours are generally perceived as more committed to their jobs and organizations (e.g., Perlow, 1997). For example, Elsbach et al. (2010) found that managers who were present in the office outside typical business hours—a pattern described as “extracurricular face time”—were frequently viewed by coworkers as “dedicated” and “committed,” despite little awareness of their actual tasks or the quality of their work. Similarly, in a study of an accounting firm, Ruiz Castro (2012) observed that employees who were physically present during nonstandard hours were regarded by managers as more committed—even though putting in long hours was not necessary to complete their work.
text: Scholarship that highlights face time requirements overwhelmingly finds that people who work longer hours are perceived by others to be more dedicated to their jobs and organizations (e.g., Perlow, 1997). For instance, Elsbach et al. (2010) found that managers who were observed in the office outside of normal business hours—”termed "extracurricular face time"—”were described by coworkers as "committed" and "dedicated," without much insight into what those managers were actually doing or the quality of their work. In a study of an accounting firm, Ruiz Castro (2012) similarly noted that employees who were physically present in the office at nonstandard times were deemed to be more committed by managers, even though long hours were not required to get the work done.

use: Natural Language Processing (NLP) broadly refers to "a set of methods for making human language accessible to computers" (Eisenstein, 2019, p. 1). Situated at the intersection of artificial intelligence and linguistics, NLP closely aligns with traditional text analysis techniques in organizational psychology and management, such as content analysis (Hsieh & Shannon, 2005) and grounded theory (Corbin & Strauss, 1990). NLP commonly incorporates both closed-dictionary methods (e.g., Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count; Pennebaker et al., 2015) and open-dictionary methods (e.g., latent Dirichlet allocation [LDA]; Blei et al., 2003). Leveraging machine learning algorithms, NLP offers a data-driven means of extracting and summarizing themes and other dimensions within textual data (corpora). At its core, NLP involves transforming words and phrases into structured categories (variables) in order to represent core ideas and generate meaning from large volumes of text.
text: NLP broadly refers to "a set of methods for making human language accessible to computers" (Eisenstein, 2019, p. 1). It exists at the intersection of artificial intelligence and linguistics, and it relates closely to traditional text analysis methods in organizational psychology and management (e.g., content analysis, Hsieh & Shannon, 2005; grounded theory; Corbin & Strauss, 1990). NLP typically relies on closed-dictionary approaches (e.g., Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count; Pennebaker et al., 2015) and open-dictionary approaches (e.g., latent Dirichlet allocation [LDA]; Blei et al., 2003). NLP utilizes machine learning algorithms in a data-driven approach to summarize themes and other dimensions of text (corpus). It involves reducing words or phrases to logical categories (variables) to represent ideas (generate meaning) from the text.

use: Identifying the distinct mediating roles of two mechanisms allows us to chart a critical path toward resolving the puzzling findings presented by Colquitt et al. (2013). Their meta-analysis revealed that exchange-based mechanisms had surprisingly limited mediating power in explaining the effects of justice on counterproductive work behavior (CWB), leading them to emphasize affective explanations over relational ones. This appeared to challenge the foundational role of the relational perspective in understanding justice. Our findings not only replicate their results regarding the minimal influence of exchange-based mechanisms but also highlight the significant explanatory power of identity-based mechanisms—particularly interdependent identity—in clarifying how justice mitigates CWB. In doing so, our study reaffirms and strengthens the theoretical importance of the relational approach in justice research.
text: Distinguishing the unique mediating roles of two mechanisms specifically enables us to navigate a critical path to addressing the puzzling findings in Colquitt et al.'s (2013) work. In showing that exchange-based mechanisms had unexpectedly little power in mediating justice effects on CWB, their review seemed to undercut the essential role of the relational approach as they instead invoked an affective lens to account for justice effects on CWB. Our results not only replicate their findings on the nonsignificant mediating effects of exchange-based mechanisms but also suggest that identity-based mechanisms, especially interdependent identity, play a powerful role in explicating how justice reduces CWB. In doing so, our research reclaims the core theoretical value of the relational approach in the justice literature.

use: Research has shown that as the frequency of negative behaviors increases, audience evaluations tend to shift, with repeated incidents reducing the impact or visibility of subsequent ones. This dynamic enables later offenders to obscure their wrongdoing amid the growing number of transgressions—essentially benefiting from a “safety in numbers” effect (Ahmadjian & Robinson, 2001; Zavyalova et al., 2012). Since individuals are more likely to focus their attention on events that appear novel or stand out from a broader pattern (Jones & McGillis, 1976), isolated misconduct by a single firm garners significant attention. However, as instances of similar misconduct multiply, each becomes less distinctive, resulting in decreased salience, diminished newsworthiness, and ultimately, reduced scrutiny (Zavyalova et al., 2012). Reflecting this phenomenon, a Wall Street Journal article observed that a “string of major data breach disclosures … may leave consumers numb to future cyberattacks” (Janofsky, 2017).
text: Scholars have found that increasing prevalence changes audiences’ evaluations of negative behaviors, where repeated occurrences decrease the salience of later instances, allowing later transgressors to hide behind the accumulating misconduct—that is, providing “safety in numbers” (Ahmadjian & Robinson, 2001; Zavyalova et al., 2012). Because people tend to allocate their attention to subjects that seem novel or unusual relative to others (Jones & McGillis, 1976), a single firm’s misconduct attracts attention by sticking out, but the misconduct’s proliferation diminishes its salience and newsworthiness, which decreases the attention paid to each firm’s misconduct (Zavyalova et al., 2012). Indeed, a Wall Street Journal article suggested that a “string of major data breach disclosures … may leave consumers numb to future cyberattacks” (Janofsky, 2017).

use: Although intersectionality has become a highly politicized concept in recent years (Coaston, 2019), the term was originally introduced decades ago by legal scholar Kimberlé Crenshaw (1989, 1991). Crenshaw used the concept to illuminate legal cases in which Black women were compelled to frame their experiences as either racial or gender-based discrimination, but not both simultaneously. Since its inception, intersectionality has evolved into what some scholars regard as the most significant contribution to feminist theory in the twenty-first century (McCall, 2005). As contemporary feminist theorists increasingly challenge the enduring tendency to treat gender as a singular, homogeneous category—and question the foundational assumptions of traditional gender analysis—there is growing recognition that “the individual’s social location as reflected in intersecting identities must be at the forefront of any investigation of gender” (Shields, 2008: 301).
text: Although intersectionality has become highly politicized in recent years (Coaston, 2019), the term was coined decades ago by legal scholar Kimberlé Crenshaw (1989, 1991), who highlighted legal cases in which Black women were forced to choose between bringing a claim of racism or sexism to court. Since then, intersectionality has become what some scholars call the most important contribution to feminist thinking of the twenty-first century (McCall, 2005). As modern feminist theorists have begun to challenge the persistent homogenization of gender and question the assumptions underlying conventional approaches to the study of gender, there is increased understanding that "the individual's social location as reflected in intersecting identities must be at the forefront of any investigation of gender" (Shields, 2008: 301).

use: The third question explores the relative influence of emotion and cognition in shaping third-party responses to observed mistreatment. Deonance theory highlights the role of emotions—particularly anger—as the primary catalyst for third-party behavioral reactions (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008). However, broader conceptualizations of emotion reveal that emotions can influence behavior both directly—through affect-driven responses—and indirectly—by shaping cognitive judgments that, in turn, guide behavior (Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996). Moreover, some variations of deonance theory do acknowledge a more cognitively mediated process for forming behavioral responses (Folger & Skarlicki, 2008; Folger & Stein, 2017). This raises a key question: When third parties respond behaviorally after witnessing mistreatment, to what extent are their actions driven purely by emotional reactions, or by an interplay between emotion and cognition?
text: The third question centers on the relative importance of emotion and cognition in shaping third-party behaviors after observing mistreatment. Deonance theory emphasizes emotions—most notably anger—as the drivers of third-party behavioral reactions (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008). But conceptual discussions of emotions illustrate that they can shape behavior both directly (i.e., affect-driven behaviors) and indirectly through cognition (i.e., judgment-driven behaviors; Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996). And the various formulations of deonance theory do occasionally describe a more cognitive process for determining behavioral reactions (Folger & Skarlicki, 2008; Folger & Stein, 2017). When third parties react to observing mistreatment behaviorally, to what degree are those reactions explained by emotion or by a mix of emotion and cognition?

use: We argue that leader leniency likely initiates a sensemaking process among observers, prompting them to consider contextual factors that might mitigate perceptions of unfairness (D. A. Jones & Skarlicki, 2012). Sensemaking is a socially constructed process that emerges when events—such as lenient responses by leaders—deviate from established expectations or normative standards (Maitlis et al., 2013). As such, when leaders behave in ways that appear inconsistent with organizational norms, observers are likely motivated to understand and interpret the rationale behind these actions. Given leaders’ responsibility to safeguard both employees and the organization from harm, observers may gravitate toward explanations that align with prevailing expectations of leaders as ethical stewards (Schilling et al., 2023). In doing so, they may interpret the leader's leniency as stemming from humanitarian values or empathetic concern toward the offender.
text: We contend that leader leniency likely triggers sensemaking among observers to consider factors that may reduce the sense of unfairness (D. A. Jones & Skarlicki, 2012). Sensemaking is a social construction process that occurs naturally when events, like leader leniency, are inconsistent with expectations and norms (Maitlis et al., 2013). Hence, leaders’ leniency should motivate observers to try to “make sense” of why the leader acted inconsistently with standards. Given the leaders’ role to protect employees and the organization from harm, observers may consider salient explanations that align with norms and expectations of the leader as an ethical steward (Schilling et al., 2023). Observers may search for information explaining the leader as acting with humanitarianism or with empathy toward the offender.

use:
Moderating Effect of Task Autonomy  
To address ongoing theoretical debates, I examine how task autonomy moderates the relationship between pay-for-performance (PFP) and employee performance. Autonomy is defined as “the degree to which the job provides substantial freedom, independence, and discretion to the individual” (Hackman & Oldham, 1976, p. 258). Emerging insights from both creativity theory and self-determination theory (SDT) underscore the critical role of autonomy in shaping how PFP influences creative performance. Notably, Amabile and colleagues have argued that extrinsic motivators like PFP can enhance intrinsic motivation—and thereby creativity—when rewards are implemented in contexts that allow for high autonomy, enabling individuals to feel empowered and retain control over their tasks (Amabile & Pratt, 2016; Hennessey & Amabile, 2010; Liu et al., 2011).
text:
Moderating Effect of Task Autonomy
To resolve ongoing theoretical debates, I investigate the moderating effect of autonomy on the PFP–performance relationship. Autonomy is defined as “the degree to which the job provides substantial freedom, independence, and discretion to the individual” (Hackman & Oldham, 1976, p. 258). Evolving perspectives from both creativity theory and SDT acknowledge the importance of autonomy in understanding the effects of PFP on creative performance. As noted, Amabile and colleagues suggested that extrinsic motivators such as PFP can bolster intrinsic motivation and thus synergistically enhance creativity when extrinsic rewards are administered in autonomous work settings where workers have a sense of empowerment and control over their work (Amabile & Pratt, 2016; Hennessey & Amabile, 2010; Liu et al., 2011).

use: Job impact on beneficiaries refers to the extent to which a job offers employees opportunities to positively influence the lives of others. I propose that these opportunities are motivating primarily at the level of the overall job—how employees perceive their full set of responsibilities—rather than at the level of individual tasks (Wong & Campion, 1991). This perspective is grounded in the understanding that tasks within a job can differ in terms of their beneficiary impact. Because of this variation, the broader, more holistic characteristics of the job are likely to have a greater influence on employees’ experiences. This is supported by psychological, statistical, and practical considerations, which suggest that synthesizing the impact of numerous discrete tasks into a coherent understanding of the entire job is inherently challenging (Taber & Alliger, 1995).
text: Job impact on beneficiaries is the degree to which a job provides opportunities for employees to affect the lives of beneficiaries. I assume that opportunities for impact are motivating at the level of the job—”how employees experience their collections of tasks—”rather than at the level of the single task (Wong & Campion, 1991). The rationale behind this assumption is that, within a job, tasks can vary in their impact on beneficiaries. The global properties of the job appear to be substantially more influential than single tasks in shaping employees' experiences, given that it is psychologically, statistically, and practically difficult to aggregate the large number of single tasks that employees carry out into the experience of a whole job (Taber & Alliger, 1995).

use: Over the past decade, a growing body of research has highlighted the potential for variation in how individual team members experience conflict—referred to as inter-individual variance—and emphasized the importance of these individual experiences in understanding the effects of intra-team conflict on team members. Despite this growing attention, empirical findings regarding the impact of inter-individual conflict experiences on personal outcomes remain mixed. On one side, studies have shown that individuals who perceive higher levels of conflict compared to their teammates tend to report lower job satisfaction, diminished performance (Jehn, Rispens, & Thatcher, 2010; Jen, 2013), and increased absenteeism (Jehn, Rupert, & Nauta, 2006). On the other side, more recent research suggests that elevated experiences of conflict at the individual level can also have positive outcomes, such as enhancing creativity (Yong, Sauer, & Mannix, 2014) and boosting personal effectiveness (Wang, Homan, & Jehn, 2020).
text: Over the past decade, amassing evidence suggests the potential for variance among individual team members’ conflict experiences (i.e., inter-individual variance), and noted the significance of individual conflict experiences in understanding the effect of intra-team conflict on individual members. Interestingly, however, empirical findings of inter-individual conflict on individual outcomes have been largely inconclusive. On the one hand, scholars have found that individuals who experience relatively more conflict than other team members showed lower satisfaction, poorer performance (Jehn, Rispens, & Thatcher, 2010; Jen, 2013), and higher absenteeism (Jehn, Rupert, & Nauta, 2006). On the other hand, more recent work has revealed that relatively high individual conflict experiences may contribute to one’s creativity (Yong, Sauer, & Mannix, 2014) and effectiveness (Wang, Homan, & Jehn, 2020).

use: To evaluate these hypotheses, I conducted three field experiments featuring varied manipulations of task significance and distinct measures of job performance. The first and third experiments were conducted with fundraising callers who solicited alumni donations for a university, while the second centered on lifeguards responsible for ensuring swimmer safety at a community recreation center. I operationalized task significance through the use of stories—narratives capable of conveying vivid, concrete, and personalized information (Jenni & Loewenstein, 1997). Stories serve as powerful sources of inspiration (Piccolo & Colquitt, 2006), offering relatable examples that resonate emotionally with employees and act as impactful tools for learning and comprehension. They create lasting impressions and facilitate meaning-making by translating abstract concepts into tangible and emotionally engaging experiences (Bandura, 1978; Heath, Bell, & Sternberg, 2001; Martin, Feldman, Hatch, & Sitkin, 1983; Weick, 1995).
text: To test these hypotheses, I conducted three field experiments with different task significance manipulations and different job performance measures. The first and third experiments focused on fundraising callers soliciting alumni donations to a university, and the second experiment focused on lifeguards protecting swimmers at a community recreation center. I operationalized task significance in the form of stories, which have the capacity to provide rich information that is vivid, concrete, and personalized (Jenni & Loewenstein, 1997). Stories can function as inspirational devices (Piccolo & Colquitt, 2006) that provide tangible exemplars, registering with employees on a deep, emotional level and serving as memorable occasions for learning and understanding experiences (Bandura, 1978; Heath, Bell, & Sternberg, 2001; Martin, Feldman, Hatch, & Sitkin, 1983; Weick, 1995).

use: Our research contributes to resolving the ongoing debate regarding the relationship between intrinsic motivation and creativity. Specifically, we theorized—and found empirical support for—the idea that this link is contingent upon other-focused psychological processes: intrinsic motivation is most likely to foster higher creativity when employees are also prosocially motivated and inclined to adopt others’ perspectives. Previous studies have pointed to contextual moderators of the intrinsic motivation–creativity relationship, such as task complexity (Gagné & Deci, 2005) and the quality of leader-member exchange (Tierney et al., 1999), both of which are believed to enhance the creative output of intrinsically motivated individuals by providing stimulating challenges and minimizing external constraints. However, there has been little exploration into how internal psychological tendencies might moderate this relationship, leaving a critical gap in our understanding.
text: Our research takes a step toward resolving the controversy about the link between intrinsic motivation and creativity. We proposed and found that this relationship is contingent on other-focused psychological processes: intrinsic motivation is most likely to be associated with higher levels of creativity when employees are also prosocially motivated to take the perspectives of others. Studies have begun to identify contextual moderators of the relationship between intrinsic motivation and creativity, such as task complexity (Gagné & Deci, 2005) and leader-member exchange relationships (Tierney et al., 1999), both of which are thought to stimulate the creativity of intrinsically motivated employees by providing them with challenges and freedom from constraints. However, little research has addressed the possibility that other psychological processes may moderate this relationship.

use: Research on the effects of rankings has yielded mixed findings. On one hand, several studies suggest that rankings can meaningfully influence various organizational outcomes by shaping how external audiences perceive and evaluate an organization (e.g., Sauder & Lancaster, 2006; Pope, 2009; Bowers & Prato, 2018). On the other hand, a growing body of research reports weak or negligible effects of rankings on audience perceptions and organizational performance (e.g., McDonough et al., 1998; Thornbury & Brooks, 2010; Jones, 2016; Ursu, 2018). This inconsistency in the literature implies that the impact of rankings may not be uniform, but rather contingent on specific organizational or contextual factors. Consequently, instead of asking whether rankings matter universally, this study adopts a contingency perspective to explore when rankings matter—specifically, under what conditions an organization’s ranked position has a more or less pronounced effect on its outcomes.
text: Yet, research on the effects of rankings has produced mixed results. Several studies indicate that rankings can significantly impact various organizational outcomes by shaping how audiences assess an organization (e.g., Sauder and Lancaster, 2006; Pope, 2009; Bowers and Prato, 2018). In contrast, other research finds that rankings have weak or no effects on audience assessments and outcomes (e.g., McDonough et al., 1998; Thornbury and Brooks, 2010; Jones, 2016; Ursu, 2018). This mixed evidence suggests that the extent to which rankings matter may vary across organizations or situations. Thus, rather than asking whether rankings matter, this study adopts a contingency approach and investigates when rankings matter: under what conditions does an organization's ranking position have a stronger or weaker effect on the organization's outcomes?

use: The studies referenced above have predominantly concentrated on the "legator"—the individual who leaves behind a legacy, as defined by Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017). This emphasis is understandable, given the frequent interest in the motivations and intentions that drive someone to create a legacy. However, within organizations, there are typically more "legatees"—the recipients or beneficiaries of what the legator leaves behind. It is these legatees who become the custodians of the legator’s contributions within the work unit. As the example of Hamilton illustrates, it is often those connected to the legator who actively sustain and advance the legacy. Therefore, expanding theoretical attention to include legatees could yield valuable insights into the actions and dynamics that determine why certain contributions endure over time while others are forgotten.
text: The studies cited above have focused primarily on the "legator"—”a term used by Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017) to reference the person leaving the legacy. That choice is understandable, given that the focus has often been on the motives and concerns that cause someone to leave a legacy. Nevertheless, organizations contain more "legatees"—”the beneficiaries of what the legator has left behind—”and it is those legatees who are entrusted with what the legator has contributed to the work unit. As the Hamilton example illustrates, often it is those connected to a legator who wind up cultivating and promoting a legacy. Thus, extending theorizing on legacy to the legatees could provide insights into the processes and behaviors that cause some contributions to last and others to fade.

use: Rankings have become a pervasive feature of contemporary society. Their prevalence has expanded significantly in recent decades, driven by the emergence of an evaluative culture in which quantification methods and metrics are increasingly used to assess and govern both organizations and individuals (Espeland & Stevens, 2008; Lamont, 2012; Porter, 2020; Sharkey, Kovács, & Hsu, 2023). Today, virtually every kind of organization is subject to rankings: corporations are ranked by revenue (e.g., Fortune 500), hospitals by quality of patient care (e.g., U.S. News Best Hospitals), universities by research output and teaching capability (e.g., Times Higher Education Rankings), and schools by student achievement (e.g., U.S. News Best K-12 Schools). In response, organizations frequently invest significant time, effort, and resources to secure and sustain favorable positions in these rankings (Espeland & Sauder, 2007; Sauder & Espeland, 2009; Askin & Bothner, 2016).
text: Rankings are ubiquitous in modern society. Their use has proliferated in recent decades with the rise of an evaluative culture in which methods and principles of quantification are applied to the governance of organizations and people (Espeland and Stevens, 2008; Lamont, 2012; Porter, 2020; Sharkey, Kovács, and Hsu, 2023). Today, virtually all types of organizations are ranked: firms are ranked on sales (e.g., Fortune 500), hospitals on the treatment of patients (e.g., U.S. News Best Hospitals), universities on research and teaching (e.g., Times Higher Education Rankings), and schools on student performance (e.g., U.S. News Best K-12 Schools). Accordingly, many organizations devote considerable time and resources to maintaining a favorable position in rankings (Espeland and Sauder, 2007; Sauder and Espeland, 2009; Askin and Bothner, 2016).

use: Research indicates that employees engage in a variety of practices to signal their availability and active participation in virtual work settings, thereby projecting commitment and dedication to their organization. For example, in the aforementioned study of a footwear company, lawyers described logging on and responding to messages each evening “just to show their dedication” (Mazmanian, 2013: 1236). Similarly, another study found that teleworkers frequently made deliberate efforts to appear as if they were working as many hours as their in-office counterparts (Stewart, 2000). These findings suggest that, even outside the physical boundaries of a traditional workplace—and perhaps even more so in virtual environments—individuals remain acutely aware that how they allocate their time functions as a signal of commitment. As a result, they develop creative strategies across different media to visibly demonstrate their engagement with work.
text: Research suggests that employees enact a number of practices to convey that they are available and working virtually, and in so doing project commitment and devotion to the workplace. In the footwear company study mentioned above, the lawyers reported staying online and responding to messages every night "just to show their dedication" (Mazmanian, 2013: 1236). Similarly, in another study, telework employees often went out of their way to appear to be working as many hours as their conventional office worker counterparts (Stewart, 2000). This research suggests that even outside the confines of a brick-and-mortar office (and perhaps even more so in virtual work environments), people recognize that time-use signals dedication and find creative ways to demonstrate their dedication to their work through different media.

use: We focus our theoretical and empirical analysis on these four proactive behaviors for three central reasons. First, each behavior exemplifies proactivity by reflecting employees’ self-initiated efforts to act ahead of time in order to influence outcomes and drive change (Crant, 2000; Frese & Fay, 2001; Parker et al., 2006). Second, given that prior research has shown proactive behaviors can be directed toward various targets, we selected these behaviors to assess whether our overarching hypotheses remain consistent across different proactive targets. Traditionally, voice involves speaking up within workgroups, issue-selling targets upward influence toward superiors, taking charge aims to improve organizational work methods, and helping is directed at benefiting coworkers (Grant & Ashford, 2008).
text: We direct our theoretical and empirical attention to these four proactive behaviors for three key reasons. First, these behaviors all reflect proactivity in that they represent self-starting efforts on the part of employees to act in advance to have an impact and effect change (Crant, 2000; Frese & Fay, 2001; Parker et al., 2006). Second, because researchers have observed that proactive behaviors can focus on multiple targets, we selected these behaviors in order to examine whether our general hypotheses hold across different targets of proactive behavior. As traditionally conceptualized, voice focuses on speaking up in workgroups, issue-selling focuses on influencing superiors, taking charge focuses on improving work methods for the organization, and helping focuses on taking actions to benefit coworkers (Grant & Ashford, 2008).

use: Subsequent developments have underscored the inherently interdisciplinary nature of job design, drawing attention to the trade-offs and integrative possibilities that arise when motivational approaches from organizational psychology are considered alongside mechanistic perspectives from industrial engineering, perceptual-motor models from cognitive psychology, and biological viewpoints from the fields of biology and medicine (Campion & McClelland, 1993; Campion & Thayer, 1985; Morgeson & Campion, 2002; for an overview, see Campion, Mumford, Morgeson, & Nahrgang, 2005). It is from this interdisciplinary crossroads that we launch our examination of recent advances in work design theory and research. These advancements have been driven by a growing conceptual shift—from a traditionally narrow focus on individual jobs and prescribed tasks to a more expansive view of work and its broader organizational context (Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991).
text: Other developments highlighted the interdisciplinary nature of job design, emphasizing the potential trade-offs and solutions that emerge when motivational approaches to job design from organizational psychology are considered in juxtaposition with mechanistic approaches from industrial engineering, perceptual-motor approaches from cognitive psychology, and biological perspectives from biology and medicine (Campion & McClelland, 1993; Campion & Thayer, 1985; Morgeson & Campion, 2002; for a review, see Campion, Mumford, Morgeson, & Nahrgang, 2005). It is at this point of departure that we begin our analysis of new advances in work design theory and research. These advances have been stimulated by an increasingly broad focus on work and how it is organized, as opposed to the historically narrower emphasis on jobs and assigned tasks (Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991).

use: Why does disagreement so often undermine trust? Research on the concept of "naive realism" (Ross, 2018; Ross & Ward, 1995, 1996; Schwalbe et al., 2020) suggests that individuals tend to overestimate the objectivity and accuracy of their own beliefs. People generally presume that their perspectives on complex and contentious issues are rational, balanced, and untainted by the cognitive biases and distortions they readily attribute to others. Believing their views to be fundamentally correct, they often regard opposing viewpoints with suspicion—assuming that disagreement stems from ignorance, flawed reasoning, or insincerity on the part of others. Consequently, this leads to growing distrust not only of the differing perspective, but also of the individuals who hold it (Griffin & Ross, 1991; Vallone et al., 1985).
text: Why does disagreement so consistently jeopardize trust? Prior research on the phenomenon of "naive realism" (Ross, 2018; Ross & Ward, 1995, 1996; Schwalbe et al., 2020) argues that people habitually place undue faith in the accuracy and objectivity of their own views. They believe their perceptions of complex and controversial issues are reasonable and free from a variety of biasing distortions and errors that plague others' thinking. Having concluded that their views are fundamentally sound, people are prone to be skeptical of opposing views, attributing disagreement to a lack of knowledge, ability, or sincerity on the part of the other side. As a result, they come to distrust the information itself as well as the source thereof (Griffin & Ross, 1991; Vallone et al., 1985).

use: Our study makes two key contributions to the understanding of cooperation within teams. First, we move beyond the conventional question of whether team members can cooperate in the absence of a central authority, and instead focus on the forms of cooperative behavior they are inclined to adopt. Through a game-theoretical framework, we analyze various scenarios that reflect different balances between efforts to jointly create value and strategies to individually capture value. Second, we investigate which forms of cooperation are sustainable over time, emphasizing the role of repeated interactions, mutual monitoring, and reciprocity. Our model accommodates multiple potential deviations from cooperation, each triggering distinct forms of retaliation. We highlight how team size influences individual behavior and cooperation incentives, while also introducing additional conceptual dimensions that are elaborated in the sections that follow.
text: Our investigation advances the study of cooperation in teams in two major ways. First, we shift the focus from whether team members cooperate without the aid of a central authority to what type of cooperative behavior they are willing to adopt. Using a game-theoretical model, we examine different scenarios depending on the extent to which members cooperate to create value and compete to appropriate value. Second, we explore which form of cooperation can be sustained through repeated interaction, mutual monitoring, and reciprocity, allowing for different deviations from cooperation, which are then accompanied by different retaliations. We point out that member behavior and the incentives to cooperate are related to the size of the team, but we also introduce several conceptual elements that are explained below.

use: Information about a firm is not always accessible through the media, and when it is, it is frequently unbalanced and often unreliable. In many countries, media content is subject to heavy censorship or strict government control, and in some cases, it is compromised by corruption within media organizations or manipulated through misinformation campaigns (e.g., “fake news”), which diminishes its overall credibility. These variations in the quality and accessibility of media coverage suggest that the media does not consistently help reduce information asymmetries between firms and their stakeholders. Nevertheless, with a few notable exceptions (Fang & Peress, 2009; Pollock et al., 2008), academic research examining how media influences stakeholder perceptions of firms has largely overlooked important factors such as the availability, reliability, and topical focus—particularly on contentious issues—of the media content examined.
text: information about a firm is not always available through the media, and the information presented is not always balanced and often is not reliable. In a growing number of countries, the media is heavily censored or tightly controlled by the government, or tainted by corruption among media organizations or by misinformation campaigns (i.e., “fake news”), making its content less reliable overall. Variations across these dimensions of the informational environment imply that the media does not always help reduce information asymmetries between firms and their stakeholders. Yet, with notable exceptions (Fang & Peress, 2009; Pollock et al., 2008), research on the effect of the media on stakeholder evaluations of firms has paid little attention to whether media information is available, reliable, or focused on controversial topics.

use: O'Reilly and Chatman (1986) proposed that organizational commitment stems from multiple foundational sources. Drawing on Kelman’s (1958) framework, they distinguished among three distinct forms of social influence. Compliance arises when individuals align their attitudes and behaviors to gain rewards or avoid punishments. Identification occurs when individuals adopt certain attitudes and behaviors to foster a meaningful, self-defining connection with a person or group. Internalization, on the other hand, takes place when individuals embrace attitudes and behaviors because they are consistent with their own value systems. O'Reilly and Chatman's (1986) study demonstrated that these three bases of commitment—compliance, identification, and internalization—relate differently to prosocial organizational behaviors—those intended to benefit the organization or its members—as well as to employee turnover and intentions to remain with the organization.
text: O'Reilly and Chatman (1986) argued that commitment has multiple bases. According to Kelman (1958), compliance occurs when people adopt attitudes and behaviors in order to obtain specific rewards or to avoid specific punishments. Identification occurs when people adopt attitudes and behaviors in order to be associated with a satisfying, self-defining relationship with another person or group. Finally, internalization occurs when people adopt attitudes and behaviors because their content is congruent with the individuals' value systems. O'Reilly and Chatman (1986) found that compliance, identification, and internalization, viewed as bases of commitment, were differentially related to prosocial organizational behaviors (behaviors that are intended to promote the welfare of the organization or individuals and groups within the organization) and to turnover and intent to stay with an organization.

use: The findings carry significant theoretical implications for research on intrateam conflict. The field has been shifting its focus from merely examining the types and intensity of conflict to exploring group-level characteristics that can enable teams to harness conflict productively (e.g., Behfar et al., 2008; Shaw et al., 2011). In line with this shift, some scholars have proposed contingency models of task conflict (Jehn & Bendersky, 2003; Korsgaard et al., 2008), though empirical validation of these models has been relatively limited. Our study provides direct empirical support for the theoretical claim that psychological safety moderates the relationship between task conflict and team performance (De Dreu, 2008; De Dreu & Weingart, 2003). These findings bolster the case for developing and testing contingency-based frameworks of task conflict and underscore the need for further research in this promising direction.
text: The results have important theoretical implications for intrateam conflict research. The field is advancing toward the exploration of group characteristics, as opposed to investigating only types and levels of conflict, to find ways teams can benefit from internal conflict (e.g., Behfar et al., 2008; Shaw et al., 2011). Others have advocated contingency models of task conflict (Jehn & Bendersky, 2003; Korsgaard et al., 2008), although empirical evidence has been slow to accumulate.
