We directly tested and found evidence for the theoretical proposition that psychological safety will function as a moderator of the association between task conflict and team performance (De Dreu, 2008; De Dreu & Weingart, 2003). The results support the pursuit of contingency models of task conflict in teams and encourage more research along these lines.

use: To ensure organizational success, work products and processes must remain aligned with evolving task and market demands, as misalignments can lead to significant failures (Audia et al., 2000). Given that these demands are dynamic and new challenges continually arise, it is essential for organizations to stay receptive to internal improvements (Christensen & Overdorf, 2000). One key mechanism that supports such adaptability is employee voice—the expression of ideas, suggestions, or concerns about work-related matters. Voice plays a pivotal role in driving organizational change by enabling the identification and correction of errors, enhancing productivity (Detert et al., 2013; Lam & Mayer, 2014; Sherf et al., 2018), and allowing work systems to be adjusted in response to emerging demands (Li & Tangirala, 2022). Crucially, voice represents the attentiveness of frontline employees, who are often best positioned to detect flaws in work processes and products—flaws that might otherwise escape managerial attention (Li et al., 2017).
text: Work products and processes must align with task- and market demands, as misalignments can result in organizational failures (Audia et al., 2000). As those demands vary dynamically and new threats emerge over time, it is crucial that organizations remain open to internal improvements (Christensen & Overdorf, 2000). Voice, or employees' expression of work ideas or concerns, can facilitate such change. Voice enables organizations to reduce errors and increase productivity (Detert et al., 2013; Lam & Mayer, 2014; Sherf et al., 2018) by helping them adapt work products and processes to emerging work demands (Li & Tangirala, 2022). Importantly, voice reflects the vigilance of employees who, being on the front lines, can identify problems in work processes and products that may otherwise go unnoticed (Li et al., 2017).

use: Although research on racial dissimilarity is well established, organizational scholars have only more recently turned their attention to political dissimilarity as a significant dimension of self- and other-categorization. Roth et al. (2017) catalyzed this emerging area through their political affiliation model, which has since supported a growing body of work, particularly on perceptions of hireability (Münke et al., 2024; Roth et al., 2020, 2024). Much of this research emphasizes basic ingroup/outgroup distinctions and evaluative judgments of job candidates. I contribute to this field by shifting the focus toward trust dynamics among politically or racially dissimilar coworkers within inherently pluralistic organizational environments—settings where, despite tendencies toward in-group hiring, individuals inevitably engage and build relationships across lines of difference. In particular, I compare the effects of political and racial dissimilarity, analyzing how liberal and conservative coworkers asymmetrically respond to various forms of interpersonal difference.
text: While studies of racial dissimilarity are not at all new, organizational scholars have more recently identified political dissimilarity as another important basis for self- and other-categorization. Roth et al. (2017) ignited research on this topic with their political affiliation model, which has been especially useful in undergirding studies related to hireability (Münke et al., 2024; Roth et al., 2020, 2024). Such research largely focuses on basic ingroup/outgroup dynamics and perceptions of job candidates. I add to this arena by examining trust dynamics that play out between dissimilar coworkers in, inevitably, pluralistic organizations (despite biased in-group hiring)—”those who, in contrast to job candidates, naturally interact and form relationships. Specifically, I juxtapose political and racial dissimilarity and examine liberal and conservative coworkers' asymmetric responses to different types of interpersonal difference.

use: To address this question, we challenge the implicit assumption of uniformity and propose that decision-makers’ beliefs about the effectiveness of potential responses in enhancing firm performance can vary significantly—and that these beliefs are instrumental in shaping the direction of problemistic search. Acknowledging that consensus among a firm’s decision-makers is not guaranteed, we contend that the selection of a particular response depends heavily on the existence of a dominant, shared belief in its effectiveness. More specifically, when compelling evidence persuades previously skeptical decision-makers, this dominant belief is reinforced, thereby increasing the likelihood that the organization will act on it. For example, clear and consistent correlations between prior R&D investments and improved firm performance may serve as persuasive evidence supporting R&D as an effective means to address performance deficiencies.
text: To address this question, we relax the implicit assumption and argue that decision-makers’ beliefs regarding the effectiveness of responses in improving firm performance may vary, and that such beliefs play an important role in directing problemistic search. Recognizing that a firm’s decision-makers may not all agree on the effectiveness of a given response, we further argue that a dominant shared belief in the effectiveness of a specific response is critical when a firm chooses that response. In particular, when supportive evidence convinces non-supporters of a dominant shared belief that the response is indeed effective, the effect of that belief will be strengthened. For instance, strong correlations between a firm’s prior R&D investments and firm performance may provide strong evidence justifying the effectiveness of R&D in addressing performance shortfalls

use: Research has traditionally gravitated toward one of two dominant perspectives. One influential stream, grounded in the life cycle model, emphasizes the progression of categories through distinct developmental stages. This line of inquiry adopts a two-phase model of category evolution—emergence and maturation (for reviews, see Cattani et al., 2017; Durand & Khaire, 2017)—and typically treats categories as either nascent or established (Hsu & Grodal, 2015; Navis & Glynn, 2010). In the early stages, when newly forming categories are difficult to evaluate, actors work to construct a coherent and shared understanding of the category’s meaning and boundaries (Kennedy, 2008; Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010). Once a category gains legitimacy and its viability becomes more stable, attention shifts to strategic positioning within it, as actors aim to differentiate themselves from peers (Deephouse, 1999; Navis & Glynn, 2010, 2011). This process continues unless unexpected disruptions threaten the category’s stability and call for redefinition or adaptation (Glynn & Marquis, 2004).
text: Research has typically favored one of two perspectives. Drawing on a life cycle model, one research stream has focused on how categories move from one developmental stage to the next. Research in this vein has adopted a two-step model of category evolution (for reviews, see Cattani et al., 2017; Durand & Khaire, 2017), viewing categories as either emerging or mature (Hsu & Grodal, 2015; Navis & Glynn, 2010). Early on, since emerging categories are harder to assess, actors strive to create a coherent understanding of the category (Kennedy, 2008; Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010). Once established, the viability of a category is considered stable, and actors then focus on claiming their differentiated position (Deephouse, 1999; Navis & Glynn, 2010, 2011), unless unforeseen jolts destabilize the category (Glynn & Marquis, 2004).

use: Drawing on research that bridges work context and construal level theory, we define work context construal as the mental representation of the specific attributes that characterize a workplace. Like other forms of construal, this representation exists along a continuum from concrete to abstract. As individuals construe a work context at a higher (more abstract) level, their attention shifts toward (a) holistic or gestalt aspects of the workplace rather than specific details, (b) the overarching reasons for upholding organizational values rather than the specific tactics for doing so, and (c) broad goals, values, or social groups rather than the unique circumstances or individuals involved (Liberman & Trope, 2014; Trope & Liberman, 2010). Accordingly, we propose that work context construal is variable and shaped by how individuals subjectively perceive and interpret a particular work environment.
text: Integrating research on work context with construal level theory, we define work context construal as a mental representation of the discrete features of a given workplace. As with other construals, work context construal varies on a continuum ranging from concrete to abstract. This means that as the construal of a work context becomes more high-level (abstract), the focus will be more on (a) gestalt features (vs. specific details) of the workplace, (b) why actions to uphold organizational values are important (vs. how they will do so), and (c) abstract goals, values, or groups (vs. unique demands or people of a situation; Liberman & Trope, 2014; Trope & Liberman, 2010). Thus, we propose that work context construal varies and is relative to how an individual perceives a given work environment.

use: While the method appears straightforward, empirical research evaluating its effectiveness remains limited. A comparable approach was implemented by the Educational Testing Service (ETS) in the 1980s as part of a legal settlement following the case Golden Rule Insurance Co. et al. v. Washburn et al. (1984). Known as the “Golden Rule Settlement,” the agreement mandated that test items be categorized by both difficulty level and subgroup performance disparities during item development. Items answered correctly by fewer than 40% of test-takers were classified as difficult, while those showing a performance gap greater than 15% between groups were flagged for potential bias. The procedure prioritized selecting items from the item pool that were both relatively easy and exhibited minimal group performance differences. However, this strategy failed to increase the proportion of Black applicants passing the test (Anrig, 1987). Moreover, scholars have noted that such a procedure compromises the reliability and validity of the assessments (Linn & Drasgow, 1987).
text: Although this method is straightforward, research testing such a strategy is limited. The Educational Testing Service adopted a somewhat similar strategy in the 1980s in response to a lawsuit (Golden Rule Insurance Co. et al. v. Washburn et al., 1984). The "Golden Rule Settlement" required categorizing test items by difficulty and subgroup differences during test development. For difficulty, items with less than 40% correct were viewed as difficult; for group differences, items differing by more than 15% in difficulty were flagged. Easy items and items with low group differences were to be chosen first from an item bank. This procedure did nothing to improve the proportion of Black applicants who passed the test (Anrig, 1987). Additionally, the procedure reduces the reliability and validity of tests (Linn & Drasgow, 1987).

use: Observers closely monitor whether leaders take action to punish misconduct, recognizing that such responses are central to upholding ethical standards within organizations. Leaders serve as moral stewards, tasked with preserving ethical norms and social order. It is their responsibility to sanction offenders in order to deter future transgressions, safeguard employee well-being, and promote ethical and productive behavior among staff (Podsakoff et al., 2010; van Prooijen, 2018). When leaders exhibit leniency, they fall short of fulfilling this responsibility, as they fail to adequately address the wrongdoing and allow its detrimental effects to persist. In this sense, leniency carries a “moralizing effect” for observers (van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2021), often leading to perceptions of unfairness. Indeed, Zhu et al. (2021) describe leniency as a “justice failure,” as it suggests that wrongdoers are able to escape appropriate consequences for their actions.
text: Observers pay close attention to whether or not leaders punish offenders for their misconduct. Leaders are moral stewards in organizations who maintain ethics and social order. Leaders are responsible for dispensing punishment against offenders to curb further misconduct, protect employees’ well-being, and ensure employees maintain ethical and productive behavior (Podsakoff et al., 2010; van Prooijen, 2018). Thus, leaders’ leniency constitutes negligence in their responsibility because it does not appropriately address the misconduct and leaves its harmful consequences unattended. In this way, leniency has a “moralizing effect” on observers (van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2021) and thus is likely to be judged as unfair. To be sure, Zhu et al. (2021) termed leniency a “justice failure” because it conveys that offenders are allowed to evade punishment for their misconduct.

use: Shifts in the employment landscape have eroded employees’ physical, administrative, and temporal ties to organizations (Cascio, 2003; Pfeffer & Baron, 1988). Today, employees are more mobile, exercise greater autonomy, and rely less on their organizations for long-term employment security than in the past. In response, organizations are increasingly focused on reinforcing employees’ psychological bonds by fostering affective commitment—defined as an emotional attachment and identification with the organization (Mathieu & Zajac, 1990; Meyer & Allen, 1991). A robust body of research links affective commitment to a range of positive behavioral outcomes, including reduced absenteeism and turnover, as well as enhanced job performance (Cooper-Hakim & Viswesvaran, 2005; Riketta, 2002; Somers, 1995). As a result, both scholars and practitioners remain deeply invested in exploring how affective commitment emerges and develops over time. This remains a core concern within organizational scholarship (Mowday & Sutton, 1993).
text: Changing employment landscapes have weakened employees' physical, administrative, and temporal attachments to organizations (Cascio, 2003; Pfeffer & Baron, 1988). Employees are more mobile, more autonomous, and less dependent on their organizations for employment than ever before. To address these challenges, organizations are increasingly seeking to strengthen employees' psychological attachments by cultivating affective commitment—”an attitude of emotional dedication—”to organizations (Mathieu & Zajac, 1990; Meyer & Allen, 1991). Extensive research has demonstrated that affective commitment to organizations is linked to important behavioral outcomes ranging from decreased absenteeism and turnover to increased job performance (Cooper-Hakim & Viswesvaran, 2005; Riketta, 2002; Somers, 1995). Accordingly, scholars and practitioners continue to share a deep interest in understanding how affective commitment to organizations develops. This pursuit is a foundational task for organizational scholarship (Mowday & Sutton, 1993).

use: Long ago, in a scholarly era now distant, values occupied a central place in institutional theory and research (Clark, 1956; Selznick, 1949, 1951, 1957; Zald & Denton, 1963; see also Perrow, 1986, for a review). Rooted deeply in the sociological tradition, institutional theory emerged during a period when values held a prominent position within the broader discipline. It is therefore unsurprising that early institutional scholars treated "value infusion" and institutionalization as largely overlapping processes. Observing society, these thinkers perceived a landscape saturated with values, to which institutions were expected to adapt. Internally, too, values were seen as pervasive—aspirational ideals that guided organizational behavior, unified and motivated members, captivated leaders’ attention, and shaped the institution’s very identity.
text: A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away, values were at the very center of institutional theory and research (Clark, 1956; Selznick, 1949, 1951, 1957; Zald & Denton, 1963; see also Perrow, 1986, for a review). The institutional tradition has deep sociological roots and first emerged during an era when values were preeminent in the larger discipline. As such, it is perhaps unsurprising that early institutionalists viewed the processes of "value infusion" and institutionalization as mostly synonymous. When these scholars looked out at society, they saw a value-rich landscape to which the individual institution needed to adapt itself. When they looked inside this social entity, values were also ubiquitous. These aspirational ideals shaped its actions, integrated and inspired its members, preoccupied its leaders, and defined its very identity.

use:
Positive cognitions that arise in response to favorable events often manifest as experiences of savoring (Bryant, 2021) and basking (Martin & Tesser, 1996). These mental processes involve reflecting on positive outcomes, such as successfully closing the gap between a current and a desired state or surpassing a previously set goal (Carver, 2004). We refer to these retrospective, positively charged thoughts as backward-oriented positive rumination, as they center on what has already occurred. 

Conversely, positive cognitions triggered by uplifting events can also adopt a future-oriented stance. Rather than simply being content with a positive outcome, such cognitions may spark new aspirations by establishing fresh discrepancies or goals to pursue (Phillips et al., 1996; Scherbaum & Vancouver, 2010). We describe these forward-looking, motivation-generating thoughts as forward-oriented goal generation, as they represent a shift from reflecting on past achievements to envisioning and striving toward new possibilities.
text: Positive cognitions that occur as a response to positive events refer to experiences of savoring (Bryant, 2021) and basking (Martin & Tesser, 1996). Thereby, cognitions focus on positive things that have happened, for instance, after reducing the discrepancy between an actual and a desired state or after exceeding a previously set goal (Carver, 2004). We call these positively toned cognitions with a focus on what has happened backward-oriented positive rumination. Cognitions originating from positive events can also take a future-oriented perspective. Such cognitions may not be complacent with having experienced a positive event but may go beyond the status quo by creating a new discrepancy (Phillips et al., 1996; Scherbaum & Vancouver, 2010). We call these cognitions emerging from positive events and focusing on prospective goals and activities forward-oriented goal generation.

use: We contribute to ongoing discussions in proactivity research, particularly those recognizing that judgments of proactive behavior can be subjective (e.g., Grant & Ashford, 2008). Voice is frequently categorized as a form of proactive behavior (Parker & Collins, 2010) due to its future-oriented and change-driven nature (Liang et al., 2012; Morrison, 2014). While voice may exhibit these qualities, we emphasize that managers’ perceptions of its proactivity can differ depending on how actively they solicit input from employees. These perceptual differences carry meaningful consequences for employees, particularly concerning the recognition and rewards they receive for speaking up. Our research underscores the importance of examining when and to what extent managers actually attribute proactivity to employee behaviors—an issue that deserves greater attention in the proactivity literature.
text: We add to conversations in the research on proactivity that have noted the possibility that inferences of proactivity can be subjective (e.g., Grant & Ashford, 2008). Voice is often classified as a proactive behavior (Parker & Collins, 2010) because it is both forward-looking and change-oriented (Liang et al., 2012; Morrison, 2014). We point out that, although voice might have these characteristics, the proactivity that managers perceive in the behavior can vary based on their own voice solicitation levels, and that such perceptions have important implications for the employees in terms of the rewards they are able to garner for their voice. Through our research, we thus highlight that it is important for the proactivity literature to consider and examine when and to what extent managers actually attribute proactivity to their employees.

use: While a direct relationship between expressions of gratitude and perceptions of prosocial identity is plausible, we argue that this effect is likely to depend significantly on the context in which the gratitude is expressed. Drawing on the stereotype content model (Fiske, 2018), we center our analysis on perceptions of a leader’s warmth and competence. As highlighted by Fiske et al. (2002), these two dimensions explain much of the variation in how individuals are perceived across diverse social situations and thus serve as key interpretive lenses for social interactions. Competence reflects a leader’s ability to successfully execute tasks and achieve goals (House & Kerr, 1973; Price & Garland, 1981), while warmth pertains to qualities such as trustworthiness and friendliness, revealing a leader’s underlying intentions and motives (Fiske, 2018).
text: Although a direct link between gratitude and prosocial identity perceptions can be expected, we argue that this main effect is likely to hinge on the context in which an expression of gratitude is given. Specifically, we draw from the stereotype content model (Fiske, 2018) and focus on perceptions of leaders’ warmth and competence. As noted by Fiske et al. (2002), these dimensions account for much of the variance in how people are perceived across a range of contexts, and as such are likely to act as an important lens through which social interactions are viewed and interpreted. Competence refers to a leader’s capacity to engage in effective task completion (House & Kerr, 1973; Price & Garland, 1981). Warmth references a leader’s trustworthiness and friendliness, capturing their intentions and motives (Fiske, 2018).

use: Efforts to structure group interactions in order to minimize the process losses that often accompany collaborative work have a well-established tradition in the management sciences. One early and influential example can be found in the work of Van de Ven and Delbecq (1971), who developed a formalized group process aimed at fostering creative solutions to strategic challenges. Central to their approach is the use of the nominal group technique. Nominal groups are composed of members who contribute equally, but initially do so in isolation—without direct interaction. In essence, nominal groups temporarily suspend the social dynamics typically associated with group work. After this independent phase, individual contributions are brought together, often using a round-robin method, where members sequentially share their ideas until all have been surfaced.
text: The use of processes to orchestrate group interactions in an effort to rid them of the process losses that invariably accompany such interactions has a longstanding history in the management sciences. For instance, in the context of groups charged with identifying and developing creative solutions to strategic problems, Van de Ven and Delbecq (1971) developed such a group process. At the heart of this model is the nominal group technique. Nominal groups refer to those groups in which the same number of members contribute their efforts, but they do so without interacting at first. In other words, nominal groups, at least temporarily, eliminate the social aspect of group life. After working individually, member output is often aggregated using a round-robin format with members sharing their ideas until all ideas have been unearthed.

use: Across field and laboratory studies, we investigate whether employee proactivity counteracts the influence of extraverted leadership on group performance. In our first study, we analyze field data to explore whether the typically positive association between extraverted leadership and profitability—in this case, among pizza delivery franchises—is reversed when employees exhibit high levels of proactivity. In our second study, a controlled laboratory experiment allows us to test whether teams led by highly extraverted leaders perform better when employees are less proactive, whereas teams with less extraverted leaders achieve superior performance when employees are more proactive. By integrating findings from both real-world and experimental settings, we strengthen the internal and external validity of our conclusions. The aligned results across these two contexts—natural variations in leadership and proactivity in the field, and deliberate manipulations in the lab—consistently show that the interaction between leader extraversion and employee proactivity significantly affects two objective performance metrics: profits and group output.
text: In field and laboratory studies, we examine whether employee proactivity reverses the effect of extraverted leadership on group performance. In our first study, we use field data to examine whether a positive relationship between extraverted leadership and the profits of pizza delivery franchises is reversed when employees are proactive. In our second study, we use a laboratory experiment to test whether groups led in a highly extraverted manner perform better when employees are not proactive, while groups led in a less extraverted manner perform better when employees are proactive. The combination of field and laboratory data supports both the external and internal validity of our hypotheses, and we find parallel results across naturally occurring and experimentally manipulated variations in leaders' extraversion and employees' proactivity for predicting two objective performance outcomes: profits and output.

use: Modern organizations are increasingly integrating AI into managerial functions, replacing roles traditionally held by human managers. This shift has spurred a growing body of research emphasizing the need to understand human–algorithm interactions, particularly in relation to trust within organizational contexts (Choung et al., 2023; Glikson & Woolley, 2020; Langer & Landers, 2021). For instance, W. Wang and Benbasat (2007) demonstrated that transparency—providing explanations for how and why a system generated its recommendations—enhanced users’ perceptions of an automated recommendation agent’s competence and benevolence, thereby increasing their willingness to follow its advice. Building on this work, Bedur and Fritzsche (2022) conducted qualitative interviews with twelve AI experts—including engineers, managers, information systems professionals, and researchers—who indicated that perceptions of AI’s ability, benevolence, and integrity are crucial for evaluating both its potential risks and benefits.
text: Modern organizations are shifting more toward AI management and implementing AI in roles traditionally occupied by human managers, and a burgeoning literature highlights the importance of understanding human-to-algorithm interactions for understanding trust within organizations (Choung et al., 2023; Glikson & Woolley, 2020; Langer & Landers, 2021). For example, W. Wang and Benbasat (2007) found that transparency (explanations of how and why a system made a recommendation) could elevate the extent to which an automated recommendation agent was perceived as competent and benevolent, and increase participants' willingness to use the recommendations. More recently, Bedur and Fritzsche (2022) conducted qualitative interviews with 12 experts in the field of AI (including engineers, managers, information systems specialists, and researchers) who thought perceptions of AI's ability, benevolence, and integrity would be helpful for understanding its risks and benefits.

use: Next, we examine the potential moderating role of creative personality (Gough, 1979) in shaping the relationship between rewards and both the quantity and creativity of employees’ ideas. While prior research has not directly investigated how creative personality interacts with the specific types of rewards analyzed in this study, existing work suggests that creative personality can moderate the influence of contextual factors on creative outcomes (Madjar et al., 2002; Zhou, 2003). Individuals with a creative personality are typically nonconformist, open to complexity, and possess wide-ranging interests (Barron & Harrington, 1981; Martindale, 1989). They also tend to engage in divergent thinking (McCrae, 1987), a trait often linked to a stronger inclination toward prosocial behaviors such as charitable giving (Xu & Mehta, 2015). As a result, employees with a highly creative personality may find other-oriented rewards particularly compelling and may react more favorably to them than to self-focused incentives.
text: Next, we explore the possibility that creative personality (Gough, 1979) moderates the effects of the rewards on the number and creativity level of employees' ideas. Although no prior study has examined the interaction of creative personality and the rewards included in our study, research has shown that individuals' creative personality interacts with contextual conditions to influence their creative responses (Madjar et al., 2002; Zhou, 2003). Individuals with a creative personality are unconventional, attracted to complexity, and have broad interests (Barron & Harrington, 1981; Martindale, 1989). They tend to engage in divergent thinking (McCrae, 1987), which has been associated with a preference for exhibiting prosocial behaviors, such as donating money (Xu & Mehta, 2015). Thus, those with a creative personality may find other rewards especially attractive and respond more positively to them than to self-rewards.

use: The second question focuses on observer reactions that appear to contradict both the principles and intentions of deonance theory. A wave of recent studies has revealed that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are often accompanied by feelings of pleasure—or schadenfreude—in response to the observed harm (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). While these emotional responses were initially theorized to depend on characteristics related to both the observer and the victim, empirical findings have instead demonstrated robust main effects of mistreatment on schadenfreude across varying conditions (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). This emergence of schadenfreude challenges traditional assumptions and introduces a contrasting perspective in the literature that deserves deeper examination. To what extent does schadenfreude reliably arise from witnessing mistreatment—and what do these findings suggest about the validity and scope of deonance theory?
text: The second question centers on observer reactions that seem inconsistent with both the letter and spirit of deonance theory. A flurry of recent articles have shown that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are associated with pleasure—or schadenfreude—from the event (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). Although such relationships were theorized to depend on observer- and victim-focused variables, results indicated the main effects of observing mistreatment on schadenfreude when collapsing across such variables (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). Schadenfreude in reaction to observed mistreatment represents a divergent perspective in the literature that warrants further attention. How reliably does schadenfreude emerge as a consequence of observing mistreatment—and what implications do such findings have for deonance theory?

use: Previous research highlights the challenges that arise from insufficient specificity in analyzing temporal events—especially given that “transitions are central to research within organizations” (Bliese et al., 2017, p. 264). As Ballinger and Schoorman (2007) observed, a leader transition is not a singular event but rather a sequence comprising multiple stages: recognition of an impending leadership change, the departure of the outgoing leader, the arrival of the successor, and eventual stabilization of group dynamics. Across these phases, leader transitions can provoke a range of collective responses that evolve over time. For instance, the initial departure may disrupt established routines and destabilize role relationships within a unit. However, as the new leader assumes their role and the team gradually reestablishes role clarity and interaction patterns, the resulting influence on unit outcomes is likely to shift—highlighting the need for a dynamic, time-sensitive approach to studying leadership transitions.
text: Prior work suggests that a lack of specificity in examining temporal events may be problematic, as "transitions are central to research within organizations" (Bliese et al., 2017, p. 264). As Ballinger and Schoorman (2007) noted, a leader departure episode consists of a series of events: the discovery of a leadership change, exit of the departing leader, entrance of the incoming leader, and the subsequent stabilization of the group process. Thus, a leader transition can elicit collective reactions that vary across events and time. For example, the departure of a leader may disrupt unit routines and the stability of role relationships within the unit; however, as the incoming leader enters the unit and employee roles and member interactions stabilize, the impact on unit outcomes likely changes, illuminating the importance of examining leader departure over time.

use: Readers of top-tier journals in the organizational sciences often accept published findings at face value. That is, they typically presume that the reported results stem from rigorous scientific methods and offer meaningful theoretical or practical contributions to both fundamental and applied research. However, our review challenges this assumption on two key grounds. First, the distribution of p-values for published tests of moderation effects deviates significantly from what would be expected if those tests were highly powered, free from publication bias, and not influenced by questionable research practices. Second, the reviewed articles—all published after 2018—almost entirely lacked two foundational methodological practices now widely regarded as essential for robust research: a priori power analyses and the preregistration of hypotheses.
text: Readers of leading journals in the organizational sciences are likely to take what is published at face value. That is, they probably assume that the results reported in those journals are based on sound scientific practices and that the results offer meaningful theoretical or practical insights for their own work, both basic and applied. Our review suggests that this assumption is unwarranted for two reasons. First, the distribution of p-values for published tests of moderation effects does not match the distribution that one would expect for highly powered tests of real effects conducted without publication bias or engaging in questionable research practices. Second, the coded articles—”all published after 2018—”were almost entirely devoid of two basic practices that are now accepted as necessary for sound research methods: a priori power analyses and preregistration of hypotheses.

use: Applied to the present context, our model proposes that when leaders are perceived as deserving of gratitude, observers are more likely to interpret expressions of gratitude toward them as stemming from genuine prosocial motives. Conversely, when leaders are viewed as undeserving of gratitude, third-party witnesses are prone to attribute less prosocial intent to the expresser, instead interpreting the gratitude as motivated by self-interest—such as an attempt to manage impressions or curry favor with the leader (Yun et al., 2007). These propositions are consistent with a broad body of research showing that affiliative behaviors like expressing gratitude can be motivated by a range of intentions (Rioux & Penner, 2001), and that third-party observers often draw inferences about these underlying motives—particularly in the context of leader–employee interactions (Bowler et al., 2019). As depicted in Figure 1, we therefore advance the following proposition:
text: Applied to the current context, our model suggests that when leaders are seen as deserving of gratitude, expressers’ gratitude will be seen as driven by prosocial inclinations. In contrast, when leaders are seen as undeserving of gratitude, third-party witnesses are likely to perceive expressers of gratitude as less prosocial, and instead adopt more selfish attributions for the gratitude behavior, such as a desire to improve the leader’s impression of the expresser (Yun et al., 2007). Indeed, these arguments are aligned with extensive research demonstrating that affiliative behaviors such as gratitude expression can be driven by an array of motives (Rioux & Penner, 2001), and that third-party witnesses readily make inferences about these motives, especially when witnessing behavior within a leader–employee dyad (Bowler et al., 2019). As illustrated in Figure 1, we thus propose the following:

use: Cultivating a dual-growth mindset is likely to support more significant agentic change than fostering either a self-growth or job-growth mindset in isolation. Meaningful changes to one's job often require corresponding developments in the self, and likewise, personal growth may depend on taking on new or different job responsibilities. For instance, a telephone salesperson aiming to pitch at trade shows may need to enhance their public speaking skills to be effective in this new role—while those intent on improving their public speaking may, in turn, need the opportunity to present at trade shows to gain real-world practice. A dual-growth mindset can motivate employees to undertake transformative changes that involve mutually reinforcing shifts in both self and job. In the absence of this simultaneous focus, individuals may find it difficult to recognize or pursue substantial opportunities for agentic change (Bandura, 2006, 2018).
text: Fostering dual-growth mindset should enable more substantial agentic changes than fostering either self- or job-growth mindset alone. Desirable changes to the job may require complementary changes to the self, and vice versa. For example, telephone salespeople who want to take on the new task of pitching at trade shows may need to improve their public speaking ability to make this change happen—and those who want to improve their public speaking ability may need to take on the task of pitching at trade shows in order to gain practice. Developing dual-growth mindset may encourage employees to pursue relatively substantial changes of this sort, in which both the self and job are changed in complementary ways. Without the self and job at play simultaneously, employees may struggle to see opportunities for substantial agentic change (Bandura, 2006, 2018).

use: The notion of legacy has taken on growing significance in contemporary culture. For instance, Amazon’s “Inspiration and Motivation” section features more than 3,000 books that address themes related to legacy. At its core, the concept speaks to a deeply human desire—to be part of something greater, to have made a meaningful impact, and to leave behind traces that shape the future (Galford & Maruca, 2006; Kotre, 1999). In today’s digital age, individuals engage with legacy in increasingly accessible ways: through software like Legacy Family Tree to trace their ancestry, services like Legacybox to digitize cherished photographs and home videos, or platforms such as Legacy.com to share and read memorials of loved ones. The cultural prominence of legacy is further reflected in the enduring popularity of Hamilton—a Broadway musical that spotlights the lasting contributions of Alexander Hamilton and the efforts of his wife, Eliza, to preserve and promote his legacy following his death at the hands of Aaron Burr.
text: The concept of legacy has become quite salient in contemporary life. The "inspiration and motivation" section of Amazon's bookstore lists over 3,000 titles relevant to legacy. After all, it is a human instinct to want to feel part of something larger, to have done something that "counts," and to have actions that somehow shape the future (Galford & Maruca, 2006; Kotre, 1999). Today, individuals can learn about their ancestors through Legacy Family Tree software, digitize their photos and home movies using the Legacybox service, and upload or read obituaries of loved ones at Legacy.com. The salience of legacy can even be seen in the attention devoted to Hamilton—”a musical focused on the contributions of Alexander Hamilton and the promotion of those contributions by his wife, Eliza, after his death at the hands of Aaron Burr.

use: During a conflict experience, conflict-related issues impose a regulatory strain on individuals' attentional focus, prompting a process of self-regulation. This self-regulation involves two opposing forces that influence where attention is directed (Beal et al., 2005). The first force is on-task attentional pull, which stems from an individual’s perceived sense of control—defined as “the belief that one has at one’s disposal a response that can influence the aversiveness of an event” (Thompson, 1981: 89). When individuals perceive themselves as capable of managing the conflict, they are better able to alleviate the cognitive effort required to maintain task focus. The second force consists of off-task attentional demands, which represent competing needs or concerns that can disrupt one’s current concentration on the task. In essence, the stronger these demands, the more likely individuals are to feel compelled to shift their attention away from their primary task (Beal et al., 2005).
text: In a conflict experience, conflict issues are a regulatory burden on individuals’ attentional focus that triggers a self-regulation process. There are two competing forces in the self-regulation process of attentional focus (Beal et al., 2005). First, on-task attentional pull comes from a sense of control, which is defined as “the belief that one has at one’s disposal a response that can influence the aversiveness of an event” (Thompson, 1981: 89). The more individuals believe that they can control conflict situations, the more they can relieve the regulatory burden of staying on task. Second, off-task attentional demands are any competing needs that can disrupt one’s current attentional focus on task. In other words, the higher these demands are, the more individuals feel pressured to reallocate their attentional focus away from their ongoing task (Beal et al., 2005).

use: When employees embrace an ideological message without suspicion, they are more likely to experience a sense of personal meaning in their work and perceive it as socially significant. This increases their willingness to invest additional time and effort into their tasks (Grant, 2008; Purvanova, Bono, & Dzieweczynski, 2006; Thompson & Bunderson, 2003), thereby enhancing their effectiveness in achieving organizational objectives. In this way, ideological messages from beneficiaries serve as cues that employee efforts contribute to valued outcomes, boosting motivation to strive toward those ends (Vroom, 1964). Accordingly, we propose that ideological messages are more likely to improve employee performance when communicated by beneficiaries rather than by organizational leaders, with the effect being mediated by the presence or absence of suspicion. As Gandhi aptly noted, "The moment there is suspicion about a person's motives, everything he does becomes tainted."
text: When employees accept an ideological message without suspicion, they will be more likely to feel that their work is personally meaningful and socially valued, and more willing to dedicate additional time and energy to their work (Grant, 2008; Purvanova, Bono, & Dzieweczynski, 2006; Thompson & Bunderson, 2003). This, in turn, will increase their effectiveness in accomplishing organizational goals. In effect, ideological messages from beneficiaries will signal to employees that their work can advance valued outcomes, motivating them to work harder in order to achieve these outcomes (Vroom, 1964). Thus, we propose that ideological messages will be more likely to enhance employees' performance when they are delivered by beneficiaries than by leaders, and that this effect will be mediated by suspicion. As Gandhi stated, "The moment there is suspicion about a person's motives, everything he does becomes tainted."

use: Our study addresses three central questions by conducting a meta-analysis of existing research on third-party perceptions of mistreatment. Specifically, we develop an integrative model that incorporates a range of emotional responses (such as anger directed at the perpetrator, empathy for the victim, and schadenfreude regarding the incident), cognitive evaluations (of both the perpetrator and the victim), and behavioral outcomes (including both antisocial and prosocial actions toward each party). This comprehensive framework is illustrated in Figure 1. In the figure, varying shades indicate the degree of alignment between each emotion and the principles of deonance theory: black represents emotions that are fully consistent in both letter and spirit, dark gray denotes consistency in spirit but not letter, and light gray indicates a lack of consistency altogether. We assess our model using a matrix of meta-analytic correlations, a method that offers greater statistical power and broader generalizability than any single primary study could provide.
text: Our study seeks answers to those three key questions by performing a meta-analysis of the literature on third-party perceptions of mistreatment. More specifically, we construct an integrative model that includes multiple emotions (anger toward the perpetrator, empathy toward the victim, schadenfreude from the event), multiple cognitions (evaluation of the perpetrator, evaluation of the victim), and multiple behaviors (antisocial and prosocial behaviors toward the perpetrator and victim). This model is presented in Figure 1. The figure uses shading to reflect the extent to which each emotion is consistent with deonance theory: black for consistent in letter and spirit, dark gray for consistent in spirit but not letter, and light gray for inconsistent altogether. We then test our model using a matrix of meta-analytic correlations—affording more statistical power and generalizability than would be provided by any one primary study.

use:
Employee creativity is a critical driver of sustained organizational success. This holds true in both the sense of “big creativity”—where organizations depend on novel ideas to gain competitive advantage and fuel innovation and strategic change—and in the sense of “small creativity,” which supports incremental improvements to everyday processes, practices, and problem-solving (Shalley, Hitt, & Zhou, 2015; cf. March, 1991). In this way, creativity is a universal concern for organizations, central to both breakthrough innovation and continuous adaptation.  

Importantly, the need for creativity extends beyond roles traditionally associated with innovation, such as those in research and development. It applies broadly across the workforce, encompassing any employee confronted with nonroutine, complex, or unexpected challenges in their work (Hirst, van Knippenberg, Chen, & Sacramento, 2011). Creativity, therefore, is not the domain of a privileged few but a key asset across roles, departments, and functions.
text: Employee creativity is key to sustained business success. This is both true in a "big creativity" sense in that organizations rely on creativity to achieve competitive advantage and for the innovation and change needed for long-term viability, and in a "small creativity" sense in that organizations rely on creativity for incremental improvements to processes and procedures as well as to tackle day-to-day job challenges (e.g., to solve problems, to deal with unexpected circumstances; Shalley, Hitt, & Zhou, 2015; cf. March, 1991). Creativity thus is on the agenda for all organizations. Moreover, the demands for and value of creativity are not limited to a select group of employees in jobs that explicitly mandate creativity, such as research and development personnel, but more broadly involve any employee grappling with nonroutine job challenges (e.g., Hirst, van Knippenberg, Chen, & Sacramento, 2011).

use: Our analysis reveals a consistent theoretical orientation across scholarly work on homophily. Nearly all definitions incorporate some form of the three foundational elements originally identified by Lazarsfeld and Merton (1954): the tendency to interact, the nature or context of association, and similarity in attributes. The following examples, with key elements italicized for emphasis, illustrate how these components are embedded in a range of contexts. In organizational settings, homophily is defined as "the degree to which pairs of individuals who interact are similar in identity or organizational group affiliations" (Ibarra, 1993: 61). Among members of the New York State Assembly, it is described as "the degree to which pairs of individuals who interact are similar in certain attributes" (Ferber & Pugliese, 2000: 402). Even in studies of animal behavior, homophily is noted as "the tendency for individuals to interact with similar individuals" in research on raccoons (Hirsch, Prange, Hauver, & Gehrt, 2013: 463). While these definitions vary in scope and specificity, they share a unified theoretical core.
text: We find that scholars present a consistent theoretical approach. Almost all include some version of Lazarsfeld and Merton's (1954) three original homophily components: the tendency to interact, the type of association, and attribute similarity. The following examples add italics to highlight them. Within an organization, homophily is "the degree to which pairs of individuals who interact are similar in identity or organizational group affiliations" (Ibarra, 1993: 61). With regard to members of the New York State Assembly, homophily is "the degree to which pairs of individuals who interact are similar in certain attributes" (Ferber & Pugliese, 2000: 402). Even with reference to the social behavior of raccoons, homophily is "the tendency for individuals to interact with similar individuals" (Hirsch, Prange, Hauver, & Gehrt, 2013: 463). Although these definitions differ in their specificity, the basic theoretical intent is common.

use: When we conducted latent class analyses (LCAs) on each individual dataset, the model enumeration process produced solutions ranging from two to five classes (M = 3.6, SD = 1.05). These analyses yielded a variety of class structures—for instance, some identified uniformly high or low engagement across all deviance behaviors, others indicated a preference for either interpersonal deviance (ID) or organizational deviance (OD), and a few revealed patterns that diverged significantly from our initial expectations. While these individual LCAs highlighted both consistent patterns and notable variations in deviance classifications across studies, our next step was to assess employee classifications at a broader level by analyzing the aggregated dataset that combined all primary studies (see also Kabins et al., 2016). In contrast to the individual LCAs, which were based on samples with a mean size of N = 310.90 (SD = 209.59), the multilevel LCA utilized the full sample of 6,218 employees, while also controlling for study-level differences.
text: When we conducted individual LCAs on each data set, the enumeration process indicated model solutions ranging from two to five classes (M = 3.6, SD = 1.05). Several different classes emerged, such as ones that illuminated a high- or low-level of engagement on all deviance behaviors, some that showed a preference for ID or OD behaviors, and others that were very different from our expectations. Although these individual LCAs revealed important similarities and differences in deviance classes, our next step was to evaluate employee classifications using the larger total sample comprising all of the individual primary studies (see also Kabins et al., 2016). Whereas the individual LCAs focused on classifications in samples with an average N = 310.90 (SD = 209.59), the multilevel LCA reveals individual classifications using the complete sample of 6,218 employees while accounting for study-level effects.

use: Lin, Kain, and Fritz (2013) demonstrate that intrusions deplete valuable resources such as time and increase workload demands, contributing to experiences of time famine (Perlow, 1999) and time pressure (Leroy & Glomb, 2018; Mark, Gudith, & Klocke, 2008). These interruptions often leave individuals with less time than anticipated, forcing them to revise or reorganize their schedules (e.g., Mitchell, Harman, Lee, & Lee, 2008). As a result, work is more likely to be perceived as overwhelming (Kirmeyer, 1988) and mentally taxing (Mark et al., 2008). Intrusions have been consistently associated with elevated stress levels (Fletcher, Pooter, & Telford, 2018; Lin et al., 2013; Mark et al., 2008; Perlow, 1999). This stress is intensified when the interruptions stem from tasks unrelated to the focal activity (Weigl, Beck, Wehler, & Schneider, 2017). Even when controlling for workload volume, intrusions are found to heighten anxiety and, over time, contribute to emotional exhaustion (Lin et al., 2013).
text: Lin, Kain, and Fritz (2013) find that intrusions threaten valued resources such as time and add work to one's schedule, leading to time famine (Perlow, 1999) and time pressure (Leroy & Glomb, 2018; Mark, Gudith, & Klocke, 2008) as people find themselves with less time than expected and must rework their schedule (e.g., Mitchell, Harman, Lee, & Lee, 2008). Intrusions cause work to be appraised as more overloading (Kirmeyer, 1988) and mentally effortful (Mark et al., 2008), and have been linked to stress (Fletcher, Pooter, & Telford, 2018; Lin et al., 2013; Mark et al., 2008; Perlow, 1999). Stress is exacerbated when intrusions are caused by tasks unrelated to the current one (Weigl, Beck, Wehler, & Schneider, 2017), and controlling for workload level, intrusions tend to increase anxiety and over time lead to emotional exhaustion (Lin et al., 2013).

use: The relationship between performance shortfalls and search behavior is a central concern in the behavioral theory of the firm (BTOF) (Cyert & March, 1963) and has been extensively explored in broader organizational theory and strategic management research (e.g., Billinger, Srikanth, Stieglitz, & Schumacher, 2021; Joseph, Klingebiel, & Wilson, 2016). Scholars have investigated a range of firm responses to underperformance, including research and development (R&D) efforts (Miller & Chen, 2004; Yu, Minniti, & Nason, 2019), strategic moves such as acquisitions and divestitures (Iyer & Miller, 2008; Kuusela, Keil, & Maula, 2017), and even more extreme reactions like illegal or unethical actions (Desai, 2014; Mishina, Dykes, Block, & Pollock, 2010). The diversity of these responses naturally raises a key question: What specific types of actions do firms undertake when faced with underperformance? This question continues to garner significant scholarly interest (e.g., Hu, Gu, & Xia, 2022; Kuusela et al., 2017; Posen, Keil, Kim, & Meissner, 2018).
text: The influence of performance shortfalls on search behavior represents an important question in the behavioral theory of the firm (BTOF) (Cyert & March, 1963) and related literatures in organizational theory and strategic management (e.g., Billinger, Srikanth, Stieglitz, & Schumacher, 2021; Joseph, Klingebiel, & Wilson, 2016). The existing literature has examined various types of search or organizational responses, such as research and development (R&D) (Miller & Chen, 2004; Yu, Minniti, & Nason, 2019), acquisition and divestiture (Iyer & Miller, 2008; Kuusela, Keil, & Maula, 2017), and illegal or unethical behavior (Desai, 2014; Mishina, Dykes, Block, & Pollock, 2010). Such a wide variety of responses inevitably invites a question: Which specific responses do firms adopt when addressing performance shortfalls? This question has attracted much scholarly attention (e.g., Hu, Gu, & Xia, 2022; Kuusela et al., 2017; Posen, Keil, Kim, & Meissner, 2018).

use:
The benefits of closed networks have been prominently emphasized through the clique perspective. A clique is defined as a group of three or more firms in which every member is directly connected to each other. This highly interconnected configuration fosters network cohesion (Krackhardt, 1998), which helps eliminate barriers to collaboration (Kim et al., 2021) and promotes the flow of knowledge across group boundaries (Tortoriello & Krackhardt, 2010; Tortoriello et al., 2012). Moreover, the presence of multiple parties in joint interactions functions not only as a platform for collaboration but also as a means of mutual monitoring, which aids in conflict resolution and supports the collective achievement of shared goals (Davis, 2016). 

However, the focus on direct ties within a clique can obscure the strategic value of indirect connections in a network (Ahuja, 2000b). Firms are capable of accessing valuable resources not only from direct collaborators but also through their collaborators’ partners. These second-degree connections allow firms to extend their network reach in a cost-effective manner, leveraging broad access without the need for numerous direct ties (Burt, 1992).
text: The promise of closed networks has been highlighted by the clique perspective. A clique refers to a group of at least three collaborating firms in which every pair of actors is connected. Such an interconnected structure stimulates network cohesion (Krackhardt, 1998), which enables the removal of collaboration barriers (Kim et al., 2021) and facilitates cross-group knowledge transfer (Tortoriello & Krackhardt, 2010; Tortoriello et al., 2012). Furthermore, multiparty interactions can serve as both a collaboration and a monitoring mechanism, effectively resolving conflicts and facilitating the pursuit of common objectives as a group (Davis, 2016). Nevertheless, a clique of direct contacts tends to understate the abundant indirect access in one's network (Ahuja, 2000b). Firms can obtain valuable resources from both immediate partners and partners' partners. Such indirect connections enable focal firms to create a large network in a cost-efficient way (Burt, 1992).

use: Both scholars and managers recognize the importance of workers carefully attending to and accurately processing signals in their environment to support sound decision-making and avoid costly mistakes (Bazerman, 2014; Chabris & Simons, 2010). Unsurprisingly, much of the research on mind wandering has centered on its potential risks and negative impacts on performance (Randall, Oswald, & Beier, 2014). Studies have explored the harmful consequences of mind wandering across multiple domains, including medical practice (Smallwood, Mrazek, & Schooler, 2011), education (Smallwood, Fishman, & Schooler, 2007), reading comprehension (Smallwood, McSpadden, & Schooler, 2008), and daily functioning (Robertson, Manly, Andrade, Baddeley, & Yiend, 1997). Beyond performance outcomes, researchers have also highlighted the affective toll of a wandering mind: the more individuals engage in mind wandering, the lower their reported levels of psychological well-being (Killingsworth & Gilbert, 2010; though for a differing view, see Mason, Brown, Mar, & Smallwood, 2013).
text: As scholars and managers are well aware, it is important for workers to attend to and process signals carefully in their surroundings in order to make informed decisions and avoid costly errors (Bazerman, 2014; Chabris & Simons, 2010). Perhaps unsurprisingly, then, research on mind wandering has focused largely on its performance-related risks and costs (Randall, Oswald, & Beier, 2014). Such research has examined the deleterious outcomes of mind wandering in areas including medical practice (Smallwood, Mrazek, & Schooler, 2011), education (Smallwood, Fishman, & Schooler, 2007), reading (Smallwood, McSpadden, & Schooler, 2008), and everyday life (Robertson, Manly, Andrade, Baddeley, & Yiend, 1997). In addition, researchers have documented the hedonic costs of a wandering mind. As it appears, the more one's mind wanders, the lower their psychological well-being (Killingsworth & Gilbert, 2010; for a counterpoint, see Mason, Brown, Mar, & Smallwood, 2013).

use: Building on a substantial body of research demonstrating that individuals are highly attuned to cues signaling others’ prosocial or antisocial dispositions (Goodwin, 2015), we argue that expressions of gratitude directed toward leaders tend to enhance perceptions of the expresser’s prosocial identity. Put simply, acts of gratitude function as prominent indicators of the expresser’s prosocial orientation (McCullough et al., 2001; Waters et al., 2022), thereby shaping how observers perceive the expresser—as kind, generous, and morally inclined. These are precisely the qualities that define a prosocial identity (Gneezy et al., 2012; Grant et al., 2008). This line of reasoning aligns closely with attribution theory and, in particular, the correspondence principle, which posits that when a person’s behavior aligns with a specific trait, observers are likely to infer that the individual possesses that trait (Jones & Davis, 1965; Park et al., 2022).
text: Following extensive research showing that individuals are highly attentive to cues indicating others’ prosocial or antisocial tendencies (Goodwin, 2015), we propose that leader-directed expressions of gratitude will tend to increase perceptions of the expresser’s prosocial identity. Simply put, expressions of gratitude act as salient signals of expressers’ prosociality (McCullough et al., 2001; Waters et al., 2022), in turn influencing the extent to which a witness is likely to view the expresser as a kind and generous person—precisely the sorts of attributes that are central to the notion of prosocial identity (Gneezy et al., 2012; Grant et al., 2008). Indeed, these ideas are highly consistent with the attributions literature and the correspondence principle, which states that when an actor’s behavior corresponds with a particular trait, the actor is generally seen as possessing that trait (Jones & Davis, 1965; Park et al., 2022).

use: We propose that cognitively demanding work is connected to daydreaming in two key ways. First, this type of work entails tackling complex and challenging problems—an endeavor that can be both energizing and frustrating. For individuals engaged in such work, unresolved problems may continue to occupy their thoughts, resurfacing as task-related reflections or mental imagery even while their attention is directed elsewhere (see Gable et al., 2019). In other words, the intellectually stimulating nature of cognitively demanding tasks draws workers back into mental engagement with those problems, often before they formally return to address them. This idea is consistent with broader findings that complex and challenging tasks tend to capture attention and intensify cognitive effort (Christian, Garza, & Slaughter, 2011). Put simply, cognitively demanding work functions like a stimulant—it sparks daydreams that orbit the very challenges it presents.
text: We theorize that cognitively demanding work relates to daydreaming in two ways. First, such work involves solving challenging problems—”a quest that can prove both invigorating and vexing. For one who is engaged in cognitively demanding work, problems that have proved resistant to solutions may prompt problem-related thoughts and images, even when one is engaged in other tasks (see Gable et al., 2019). In other words, the stimulating quality of problems stemming from cognitively demanding work entices workers to mentally reengage with problems before returning to them in a more formal capacity. This possibility aligns with the broader observation that challenging and complex work tasks tend to engage people and drive their cognitive activity (Christian, Garza, & Slaughter, 2011). In short, cognitively demanding work has the quality of a stimulant—”it serves to spark daydreams around the very tasks and problems it involves.

use: Since the influential work of March and Simon (1958), a substantial body of research has consistently demonstrated that employee attitudes—such as job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and job embeddedness—serve as proximal predictors of voluntary turnover (e.g., Boswell et al., 2005; Hom & Griffeth, 1991; Lee & Mitchell, 1994; Maertz & Griffeth, 2004; Mobley, 1977; Price & Mueller, 1981; Reina et al., 2018; Steers & Mowday, 1981). Broadly speaking, empirical findings support the notion that lower levels of satisfaction at work are linked to a higher likelihood of quitting. Furthermore, negative work attitudes often mediate the relationship between characteristics or experiences within the work environment and subsequent employee turnover (Hom et al., 2012; Reina et al., 2018; Rubenstein et al., 2015). Research also suggests that deteriorating attitudes toward work are commonly accompanied by cognitive withdrawal (e.g., contemplating resignation) as well as behavioral forms of detachment, such as increased absenteeism (Hom & Griffeth, 1991; Mitra et al., 1992; Rosse, 1988).
text: Beginning with the seminal work of March and Simon (1958), studies have found consistent support for the notion that attitudes (satisfaction, commitment, embeddedness, etc.) are proximal predictors of voluntary turnover (e.g., Boswell et al., 2005; Hom & Griffeth, 1991; Lee & Mitchell, 1994; Maertz & Griffeth, 2004; Mobley, 1977; Price & Mueller, 1981; Reina et al., 2018; Steers & Mowday, 1981). In general, research supports the proposition that the less satisfied one is at work, the more likely one will quit. Attitudes, such as dissatisfaction, mediate the association between the work environment (characteristics, experiences) and turnover (Hom et al., 2012; Reina et al., 2018; Rubenstein et al., 2015). Research shows that declines in work attitudes are often accompanied by cognitive (thoughts of quitting) and behavioral (absenteeism) withdrawals from the work environment (Hom & Griffeth, 1991; Mitra et al., 1992; Rosse, 1988).

use: We conceptualize coherence within a product category as the collectively constructed response to the question, “What kind of thing is this?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). Put differently, intra-category coherence reflects the extent of heterogeneity present among products within a given category (Haans, 2019; Lo et al., 2020). For example, a category displays low heterogeneity when actors frame their offerings using similar vocabularies, claims, identities, or other cultural resources. Conversely, a high degree of heterogeneity is evident when actors draw on divergent cultural resources to frame their products. Importantly, our interest lies in the breadth of variation in the cultural materials mobilized by actors—independent of whether those variations are viewed as legitimate or considered nonconforming (Goldberg et al., 2016).
text: We view coherence within a product category as the collectively held answer to the question “What kind of thing is it?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). Stated differently, intra-category coherence refers to the degree of heterogeneity that a set of products exhibits within a focal category (Haans, 2019; Lo et al., 2020). For instance, we would consider a category to exhibit a low degree of heterogeneity when a collection of actors frame their products by mobilizing similar sets of vocabularies, claims, identities, or other cultural resources, and to exhibit a high degree of heterogeneity when actors frame their products by mobilizing dissimilar sets of cultural resources. To be clear, our focus is on the range of variation in terms of the cultural resources that actors mobilize, regardless of whether these variations are commonly accepted or perceived as atypical (Goldberg et al., 2016).

use: Consider, for example, a university professor delivering a lecture to students. When driven by intrinsic motivation, the professor's effort stems from a genuine enjoyment of teaching itself—finding joy and satisfaction in the act of lecturing. In contrast, when motivated prosocially, the professor is fueled by the desire to benefit students through their learning—deriving meaning and fulfillment from the anticipated positive impact on others. In essence, intrinsic and prosocial motivations differ across at least three key dimensions. Intrinsic motivation is characterized by autonomous self-regulation and a present-focused engagement with the task. Prosocial motivation, on the other hand, involves self-regulation that is aligned with internalized or value-based goals and an orientation toward future outcomes. These distinctions imply that the two forms of motivation are relatively independent. Consequently, they can coexist and potentially interact—an interaction that lies at the heart of the present study.
text: To illustrate, consider the case of a university professor presenting a lecture to students. When intrinsically motivated, the teacher's effort is based on enjoyment of the task of lecturing, which provides joy and pleasure in the process of performing. When prosocially motivated, the teacher's effort is based on a desire to educate students, which provides meaning and fulfillment in the outcome of student learning. In summary, prosocial and intrinsic motivations differ along at least three dimensions. Intrinsic motivation involves autonomous self-regulation and a focus on a process in the present. Prosocial motivation involves self-regulation introjected or identified with values and a focus on an outcome in the future. These distinctions suggest that the two motivations can be understood as relatively independent. As such, it is possible for the two motivations to interact, a possibility that is central to the present research.

use: When applied to emotional responses, empirical tests of system justification theory have consistently linked system-justifying cognitions with heightened positive affect and reduced negative affect (Jost & Hunyady, 2005; Jost, 2019). For instance, in a study by Jost and colleagues (2008), participants engaged in a game where status advantages were randomly assigned and then maintained across several rounds. After the game, participants who rated the system as more justifiable and legitimate reported greater happiness and less anger. Notably, this increase in happiness was observed among both high-status and low-status participants (Jost et al., 2008). These findings illustrate what the authors refer to as the “palliative function” of system-justifying beliefs—namely, their capacity to soothe negative emotions and promote emotional well-being (Jost & Hunyady, 2005; Jost, 2019). In contexts where individuals observe mistreatment as third parties, this palliative function could manifest as feelings of schadenfreude—especially when the mistreatment is perceived as legitimate within an accepted power hierarchy.
text: When applied to affective reactions, tests of system justification theory have linked system-justifying cognitions to increased positive affect and decreased negative affect (Jost & Hunyady, 2005; Jost, 2019). For example, Jost et al. (2008) had participants play a game where status advantages were arbitrarily assigned and then perpetuated over the course of multiple rounds. After playing the game, ratings of how justifiable and legitimate the game was were associated with more happiness and less anger—with the happiness effects emerging for both advantaged and disadvantaged participants (Jost et al., 2008). Such effects are described as the “palliative function” of system-justifying cognitions (Jost & Hunyady, 2005; Jost, 2019). In the context of third-party observations of mistreatment, that palliative function could manifest in schadenfreude from the event—especially in the presence of cognitions that justify and legitimize the power structure within which the mistreatment occurred.

use: We define the use of technology for non-work-related purposes during work hours as a behavioral phenomenon composed of three interconnected elements. Specifically, such use (a) is facilitated by technology, (b) centers on activities unrelated to core job tasks, and (c) takes place during designated work time. These three characteristics form the foundational and consistent criteria shared across the various scholarly communities investigating this phenomenon. We adopt “use” as a broad, inclusive term intended to encompass all behaviors meeting these criteria. Our aim is not to encourage future research to study “use” in overly generalized terms; rather—as elaborated in the five thematic sections of our review—we advocate for research that explores more specific, clearly defined use behaviors, distinguished by meaningful and concrete categories. Nevertheless, we begin with this general definition to delineate the conceptual boundaries of the phenomenon, specifying what is included under our scope and what lies beyond it.
text: We broadly define technology use for non-work-related purposes at work as a behavioral phenomenon consisting of three interrelated components. Use behaviors (a) are technology-facilitated, (b) primarily focus on non-task-related activity, and (c) occur during work time. These are three core, shared characteristics that are consistent across the three communities studying use. "Use" is intended as an umbrella term to capture all forms of behaviors that satisfy these three criteria. We neither expect nor encourage future research to study use per se, but—”as we explain throughout the five themes discussed in our review below—”advocate for the study of more discrete use behaviors that can be categorized according to more concrete distinctions. Nonetheless, we begin by addressing the overall phenomenon to establish a broad boundary that separates the types of behaviors that our definition encompasses from those that are beyond our definitional scope.

use: We define the use of technology for non-work-related purposes during work hours as a behavioral phenomenon composed of three interconnected elements. Specifically, such behaviors (a) are enabled by technology, (b) are primarily directed toward activities unrelated to work tasks, and (c) take place during official work time. These three shared characteristics are consistently recognized across the scholarly communities that examine this type of behavior. The term "use" serves as an inclusive label that encompasses all behaviors meeting these criteria. We do not anticipate—nor do we advocate for—future research to focus on "use" as a generalized concept. Rather, as we elaborate across the five themes explored in our review, we encourage the investigation of more specific, well-defined use behaviors that can be meaningfully categorized through clearer conceptual distinctions. Nevertheless, we begin with this broader conceptualization in order to establish the definitional boundaries of the phenomenon, clarifying what kinds of behaviors are included within, and excluded from, our scope of analysis.
text: We broadly define technology use for non-work-related purposes at work as a behavioral phenomenon consisting of three interrelated components. Use behaviors (a) are technology-facilitated, (b) primarily focus on non-task-related activity, and (c) occur during work time. These are three core, shared characteristics that are consistent across the three communities studying use. "Use" is intended as an umbrella term to capture all forms of behaviors that satisfy these three criteria. We neither expect nor encourage future research to study use per se, but—”as we explain throughout the five themes discussed in our review below—”advocate for the study of more discrete use behaviors that can be categorized according to more concrete distinctions. Nonetheless, we begin by addressing the overall phenomenon to establish a broad boundary that separates the types of behaviors that our definition encompasses from those that are beyond our definitional scope.

use: In this paper, we draw on Attention Restoration Theory (ART) to develop a theoretical model that explains how exposure to nature in the workplace influences employee creativity. Specifically, we propose that contact with nature enhances creative output by broadening cognitive processing, with this effect moderated by individuals’ openness to experience (see Figure 1). To test our model, we adopt a “full-cycle research approach” (Chatman & Flynn, 2005: 774), which involves investigating the phenomenon in both field and experimental contexts to strengthen the internal and external validity of our findings. We carried out five complementary studies utilizing diverse methodologies—including both field and laboratory designs, and encompassing within-person and between-person analyses. Our participants span a variety of occupations and industries across multiple cultural and national contexts, including the United States, Taiwan, Indonesia, and Canada. Through the development and empirical testing of our model, this research makes notable contributions to understanding how nature contact influences individuals, delineates the boundaries of these effects, and sheds light on the contextual and psychological drivers of creativity within organizational settings.
text: In this paper, we leverage ART to develop a model explaining the effects of contact with nature at work on employee creativity via broadened cognitive processing and shaped by openness to experience (see Figure 1). We then test this model using a "full cycle research approach" (Chatman & Flynn, 2005: 774)—”examining a phenomenon in field and experimental settings to enhance the internal and external validity of the findings. Specifically, we conducted five studies that employ different research methodologies (i.e., field and experimental studies across both within- and between-person levels), using participants from different jobs and industries across multiple countries and cultures (i.e., United States, Taiwan, Indonesia, and Canada). In developing and testing our theoretical model, our research makes important contributions to our understanding of how contact with nature affects individuals, the boundaries of these effects, and the sources of creativity within organizations.
