use: The constraints on meaningful work are theoretically more pronounced for female workers who bear household responsibilities—responsibilities that, in emerging economies like India, often extend to caring for members of the extended family (Venkatesh et al., 2017). Operating within a family–work context, these women frequently shoulder obligations such as unpaid caregiving (Ahl & Nelson, 2015) and are disproportionately affected by time poverty (Us-Saqib & Arif, 2012). Empirical evidence from Pakistan indicates that women experience greater "time poverty" than men, with working women facing even more severe time constraints than their nonworking counterparts due to the gendered division of labor (Us-Saqib & Arif, 2012). These additional demands on time and energy can significantly hinder women in poverty from participating in formal paid employment (Goodman & Kaplan, 2019; Sturges, 2012) and may, by extension, limit their ability to pursue or experience meaningful work—a concern this study seeks to explore.
text: The constraints on meaningful work are, theoretically, more intense for female workers with household responsibilities that, in emerging economies such as India, often include taking care of an extended family (Venkatesh et al., 2017). Female workers typically operate in a family–work context with obligations such as unpaid caring work (Ahl & Nelson, 2015), and experience time poverty (Us-Saqib & Arif, 2012). Evidence from Pakistan shows that women are more “time poor” than men and working women are more “time poor” than nonworking women (Us-Saqib & Arif, 2012) due to the gendered division of labor. The additional demands on their time and effort faced by working women in poverty may severely reduce their capacity to engage in formal paid work (Goodman & Kaplan, 2019; Sturges, 2012) and perhaps, as a result, their capacity to engage in meaningful work, as we explore in this research.

use: Cultural anthropologist Ernest Becker laid the groundwork for empirical inquiry into how individuals cope with the awareness of death through a trilogy of influential books on the denial of death, one of which earned the Pulitzer Prize (Becker, 1973). Becker argued that the awareness of mortality is a distinctively human trait—and a profound burden. He emphasized the function of cultural worldviews in alleviating existential anxiety arising from the inevitability of death (for a review, see Liechty, 2002). In the 1980s, three social psychologists encountered Becker’s work and began designing experiments to test and build upon his theories. Today, more than two decades later, terror management theory has become one of the most influential and expansive frameworks in social psychology. Researchers have conducted over 250 studies exploring and validating its predictions about how individuals psychologically manage the awareness of their mortality (Greenberg, Koole, & Pyszczynski, 2004; Pyszczynski et al., 2003).
text: Cultural anthropologist Ernest Becker set the stage for empirical attention to how individuals respond to death awareness with three books on the denial of death, one of which won the Pulitzer Prize (Becker, 1973). Becker argued that awareness of death is a uniquely human capability and curse, and he focused on the role of cultural belief systems in buffering against existential anxiety about impending death (for a review see Liechty, 2002). In the 1980s three social psychologists discovered Becker's work and began to design experiments to test and elaborate on his theories. Now, two decades later, terror management theory is among the most generative perspectives in social psychology. Researchers have conducted well over 250 studies to test and extend terror management theory predictions about how individuals deal with the cognizance of their own mortality (Greenberg, Koole, & Pyszczynski, 2004; Pyszczynski et al., 2003).

use: We were also interested in exploring potential effects associated with the “working with things” job characteristic. This dimension captures the degree to which a role involves manual, or physical, labor. One possible expectation is that the influence of general cognitive ability (g) on job performance may be weaker in roles that are high in manual labor demands (e.g., infantry roles compared to air traffic control). If manual labor tasks can increasingly be performed through automaticity with experience, then greater job experience may reduce the performance advantages conferred by g more significantly in such roles than in others. This hypothesis would be supported by evidence of an underadditive interaction between g and time in service. We had no a priori hypotheses regarding the remaining job characteristics—namely, unpleasant working conditions and fine motor control—so any observed effects of those component scores were examined on a purely exploratory basis.
text: We were also interested in possible effects of the working with things characteristic. Working with things reflects the extent to which jobs require manual (i.e., physical) labor. One possibility is that effects of g on job performance tend to be smaller for jobs high on working with things (e.g., infantry vs. air traffic controller). If jobs that primarily involve manual labor can be performed more automatically, then increasing job experience might attenuate the effect of g on job performance in manual labor jobs to a greater degree than in other types of jobs. This speculation would be supported by finding an underadditive g x time in service interaction. We had no specific hypotheses concerning the other job characteristics (unpleasant working conditions and fine motor control), so we tested for effects of the component scores reflecting those job characteristics on a purely exploratory basis.

use: The primary objective of our research is to examine individual patterns of workplace deviance and to evaluate whether the widely used interpersonal-organizational deviance (ID-OD) framework accurately reflects how employees actually engage in deviant behaviors. To date, most studies have relied almost exclusively on variable-centered approaches, which focus on relationships between dimensions such as ID, OD, and overall deviance. These methods rest on the assumption that individuals exhibit uniform patterns of deviance, aiming to reveal how deviant behavior correlates with other workplace constructs (M. Wang & Hanges, 2011). In contrast, we investigate the potential of a person-centered approach—one that emphasizes the behavior combinations shared among groups of employees (M. Wang et al., 2012)—to offer deeper insight into workplace deviance. The existence of distinct subpopulations, or “deviance classes,” is a plausible premise, supported by the diversity of theories and perspectives on how individuals enact deviant behavior (Bennett et al., 2018).
text: The core aims of our research are to investigate individual patterns of workplace deviance and assess whether the dominant ID-OD deviance framework may, in fact, be disconnected from the way employees actually engage in deviance. Indeed, deviance has been studied almost exclusively using variable-centered approaches, which focus on relationships among variables or dimensions such as ID, OD, and overall deviance. This approach assumes homogeneity in patterns of individual deviance engagement in order to understand how deviance relates to other workplace phenomena (M. Wang & Hanges, 2011). We explore whether a person-centered approach—”a focus on the combinations of behaviors that a group of employees have in common (M. Wang et al., 2012)—”adds to our understanding of workplace deviance. Such subpopulations of deviance, or deviance classes, are reasonable to expect given the myriad of theories and perspectives describing how individuals enact deviance (Bennett et al., 2018).

use: Examinations of numerous high-profile organizational failures—such as the Texas City refinery explosion, NASA’s Columbia Space Shuttle disaster, the Chernobyl nuclear meltdown, and BP’s Deepwater Horizon oil spill—consistently highlight a recurring theme: key stakeholders neglected their ethical responsibility to appropriately balance the often competing imperatives of safety and profitability. In the case of Deepwater Horizon, for example, decision-makers prioritized profit, undertaking safety-compromising risks that ultimately led to disaster (Report to the President, 2011). A similar dynamic contributed to the Camp Fire that devastated Paradise, California, where Pacific Gas and Electric gradually deprioritized safety investments in favor of other operational concerns (Blunt, 2023). Likewise, in a government hearing investigating the Boeing 737 MAX tragedies, a member of Congress starkly summarized the root issue: “this is a story about a company cutting corners, taking short-cuts, sacrificing safety to achieve maximum profits” (House Hearing & 116 Congress, 2019; Robison, 2022, p. 236).
text: Investigations of many organizational failures—such as the Texas City refinery explosion, the National Aeronautics and Space Administration’s Columbia Space Shuttle disaster, the Chernobyl nuclear catastrophe, and British Petroleum (BP)’s Deepwater Horizon oil spill—consistently reveal that key stakeholders failed to uphold their ethical obligations to appropriately balance competing demands of safety and profitability. For instance, the Deepwater Horizon failure involved decision makers taking profit-driven risks that jeopardized safety (Report to the President, 2011). Similarly, the Camp Fire that destroyed Paradise, California, partly stemmed from a gradual lack of investment in safety by Pacific Gas and Electric, in favor of other priorities (Blunt, 2023). Finally, during a government hearing on the Boeing 737 MAX failure, a Congressperson concluded that “this is a story about a company cutting corners, taking short-cuts, sacrificing safety to achieve maximum profits” (House Hearing & 116 Congress, 2019; Robison, 2022, p. 236).

use: In the entrepreneurial domain, I explore how self-promotion influences experimentation and the adaptation of business models. Experimentation refers to the process of investigating novel ideas through iterative, small-scale trials (Bingham & Davis, 2012; Levinthal, 2017). This may involve testing product features, exploring market segments, or experimenting with organizational structures and customer engagement strategies (Thomke, 2020). Business models (BMs) define how entrepreneurs create, deliver, and capture value (Snihur & Markman, 2023; Teece, 2010). Adapting these models—often referred to as pivoting—entails redefining core elements such as value propositions or restructuring value chains. The entrepreneurial process can be understood through a social-cognitive lens (McMullen & Dimov, 2013), which posits that individuals regulate their behavior and decision-making via feedback loops, including mechanisms like goal-setting, discrepancy detection, and performance monitoring (Bandura, 1991). Entrepreneurs articulate their goals through BMs and modify them through ongoing experimentation (Frese & Gielnik, 2023; Sarasvathy, 2001). Applying this theoretical perspective to the practice of self-promotion may uncover underexplored advantages of this strategy within entrepreneurial activity.
text: In the entrepreneurial domain, I examine how self-promotion impacts experimentation and business model adaptation. Experimentation involves exploring novel ideas through small-scale testing (Bingham & Davis, 2012; Levinthal, 2017). This includes testing product features, probing markets, or experimenting with organizational strategies and customer management tactics (Thomke, 2020). Business models (BMs) represent how entrepreneurs create, deliver, and capture value (Snihur & Markman, 2023; Teece, 2010). Proactively adapting BMs, or pivoting, involves redefining these mechanisms, such as changing value propositions or reorganizing the value chain. The entrepreneurial journey aligns with the social-cognitive perspective (McMullen & Dimov, 2013), which suggests individuals control their thoughts and actions through feedback systems, including goal discrepancy reduction and production (Bandura, 1991). Entrepreneurs define goals through their BMs and adapt them via experimentation (Frese & Gielnik, 2023; Sarasvathy, 2001). Applying this perspective to entrepreneurial self-promotion may reveal previously understudied benefits of this tactic.

use: To promote learning and support behavioral change, the intervention draws on participants’ prior experiences with job crafting (Kolb et al., 2000). It is structured around the four key stages of the experiential learning cycle (Kolb et al., 2000), encouraging employees to engage proactively with job crafting practices (see Table 1). The learning process begins with concrete experiences—direct engagement with the behavior—followed by a reflective phase during which individuals examine and evaluate these experiences (Sumsion & Fleet, 1996). In the third stage, reflection gives rise to abstract conceptualization, where individuals develop new understandings of the behavior and recognize its potential value (Kolb et al., 2000). In this phase, highlighting the relevance and benefits of the behavior plays a crucial role in motivating individuals to invest the necessary time and energy for implementation (Nielsen, Randall, Brenner, & Albertsen, 2009). Finally, in the fourth stage, individuals move to active experimentation, applying the behavior in practice and generating new concrete experiences that can further reinforce learning (Kolb et al., 2000).
text: To stimulate learning and facilitate behavior change, the intervention builds on participants' past experiences with job crafting (Kolb et al., 2000). The intervention incorporates the four stages that are important in the learning process (Kolb et al., 2000) to encourage employees to actively apply job crafting (see Table 1). Learning starts with concrete experiences with the behavior, which are followed by reflecting upon this behavior (Sumsion & Fleet, 1996). After reflection, individuals reach the third stage of learning, where they have abstract ideas about the new behavior and acknowledge that implementation can be beneficial (Kolb et al., 2000). During this stage, underscoring the value of the behavior to increase individuals' willingness to invest energy and time in implementing it is important (Nielsen, Randall, Brenner, & Albertsen, 2009). During the last stage, individuals actively test the behavior to create new experiences (Kolb et al., 2000).

use: We propose that employees form two interconnected perceptions of workplace impunity: perceived personal impunity and perceived contextual impunity. This distinction builds on insights from broader workplace misconduct research, which has consistently shown that employees' engagement in their own misconduct is related to—but meaningfully distinct from—the misconduct they observe in others (Andreoli & Lefkowitz, 2009; Otten & Gordijn, 2014; Robinson & O'Leary-Kelly, 1998). For example, Robinson and O'Leary-Kelly (1998) found that an individual's misconduct is influenced by the behavior of their work group. However, they also highlighted that this influence is not uniformly strong and can vary depending on factors such as how similar the observed behaviors are. In a similar vein, Andreoli and Lefkowitz (2009) identified different antecedents for engaging in one's own misconduct versus witnessing misconduct carried out by others in the organization.
text: We propose that employees hold two related perceptions of workplace impunity, namely perceived personal impunity and perceived contextual impunity. We draw on distinctions in the broader workplace misconduct research, which has portrayed employees' own misconduct as related to, but distinct from, the misconduct they observe in others (Andreoli & Lefkowitz, 2009; Otten & Gordijn, 2014; Robinson & O'Leary-Kelly, 1998). For instance, in distinguishing the two, Robinson and O'Leary-Kelly (1998) demonstrated that an employee's engagement in misconduct is influenced by the misconduct that occurs in their work group; however, the authors also demonstrated that such a relationship is not uniformly strong and can vary based on different factors, such as the similarity of behaviors in the group. Similarly, Andreoli and Lefkowitz (2009) distinguished between factors that contribute to employees' own misconduct and those that contribute to the likelihood of observing others' misconduct in their organization.

use: A central principle of deonance theory is the concept of the deontic response—namely, that witnessing mistreatment automatically provokes anger, which in turn drives an impulsive desire to punish the wrongdoer (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). This sequence of reactions has drawn the most scholarly attention in the third-party literature, and our findings provide strong empirical support for deonance theory’s core assertions. Specifically, observing mistreatment was powerfully associated with anger directed at the perpetrator—an effect size that compares favorably with meta-analytic findings linking personal experiences of mistreatment with feelings of negative affect (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Willness et al., 2007). This result reinforces the idea that witnessing mistreatment constitutes a breach of moral norms, one that evokes an elevated emotional response in the observer (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).
text: A key tenet of deonance theory is the deontic response—that observing mistreatment should automatically trigger anger, with that anger fueling impulsive retribution against the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). The relationships contained in that tenet have received by far the most attention from third-party scholars, with our results offering robust support for deonance theory’s predictions. Observing mistreatment was strongly related to feelings of anger toward the perpetrator—an effect size that compares favorably to meta-analytic connections between experiencing mistreatment and feelings of negative affect (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Willness et al., 2007). That result lends support to the notion that observed mistreatment represents a violation of moral norms, triggering a heightened state of arousal for the observer (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).

use: Knowledge forms the foundation of emotion regulation skills and capabilities (Mayer et al., 2008). Emotion regulation knowledge is defined as “awareness of the most effective strategies to modify and nurture emotions in particular situations” (Côté et al., 2011: 1074). Research shows that individual differences in this form of knowledge emerge as early as age three (Eisenberg, 2000). Longitudinal studies further reveal that children who effectively regulate their emotions at ages three and four tend to exhibit greater social competence by the time they reach kindergarten (Denham et al., 2003). Among adolescents, strong emotion regulation knowledge is associated with higher peer acceptance and more frequent friendship nominations (Mestre et al., 2006). Similarly, college students with higher emotion regulation knowledge not only enjoy more positive and respectful interactions but are also more likely to be perceived as socially adept by their peers (Lopes et al., 2004; Lopes et al., 2005).
text: Knowledge lies at the heart of emotion regulation skills and abilities (Mayer et al., 2008). Emotion regulation knowledge refers to "awareness of the most effective strategies to modify and nurture emotions in particular situations" (CÃ´té et al., 2011: 1074). Individual differences in emotion regulation knowledge are visible as early as three years of age (Eisenberg, 2000), and longitudinal studies have shown that children who regulate emotions effectively at ages three and four are more socially competent in kindergarten (Denham, Blair, DeMulder, Levitas, Sawyer, & Auerbach-Major, 2003). Adolescents with strong emotion regulation knowledge are more likely to be listed as friends by their peers (Mestre, Guil, Lopes, Salovey, & Gil-Olarte, 2006), and undergraduates with strong emotion regulation knowledge have more enjoyable and respectful interactions and are nominated by peers as more socially skilled (Lopes, Brackett, Nezlek, Schütz, Sellin, & Salovey, 2004; Lopes, Salovey, CÃ´té, & Beers, 2005).

use: We recruited 806 participants via Amazon Mechanical Turk (55% female; M_age = 38.93), all of whom passed a basic comprehension check before being randomly assigned to experimental conditions (for participant characteristics by condition, see Table S4 in the online supplementary material). This sample size provides sufficient statistical power (90%) to detect a small effect size (Cohen’s d = 0.13) at the conventional 0.05 significance level. At the outset, participants rated their agreement with five statements concerning employment in the United States using 7-point Likert scales ranging from "strongly disagree" to "strongly agree." One key item stated: “The federal government should increase the minimum wage to $15 an hour.” Based on their response to this statement, participants were shown a message that opposed their stated position—either in favor of or against increasing the minimum wage. The other four statements served as distractors.
text: We recruited 806 participants from Amazon Mechanical Turk (55% female, M_age = 38.93) who passed a simple comprehension check prior to assignment to experimental conditions (see additional online material Table S4 for the characteristics of participants across conditions). This sample size is sufficient to detect an effect size of Cohen's d = 0.13 with 90% power at the standard significance level of 0.05. Participants began by reporting their attitudes on five statements related to employment in the United States (from strongly disagree to strongly agree on 7-point Likert scales). Specifically, one statement read: "The federal government should increase the minimum wage to $15 an hour." A participant's response on this item determined whether they would see a message favoring or opposing an increase in the minimum wage, with all participants receiving a message contrary to the position they had endorsed. The remaining four statements were distractors.

use: In a laboratory experiment, Weiss et al. (1999) manipulated whether undergraduate participants won or lost a competition, as well as whether the procedures were portrayed by confederates as fair, biased in their favor, or biased against them. The findings revealed that anger was significantly intensified when participants experienced a loss due to procedures perceived as biased against them. In a separate experience sampling study, Judge et al. (2006) explored daily fluctuations in perceptions of interpersonal justice across multiple organizations. Their analysis showed that day-to-day variations in perceived interpersonal justice were significantly associated with corresponding fluctuations in anger, even after accounting for individuals' baseline affectivity. In a sentiment-focused investigation, Baer et al. (2018) studied the impact of "unfairness talk"—that is, conversations about perceived injustice with colleagues—among a sample of bus drivers. Their findings demonstrated that engaging in unfairness talk was negatively related to feelings of hopefulness toward supervisors and positively related to anger directed at them.
text: In an example of a laboratory study, Weiss et al. (1999) varied whether undergraduates won or lost a competition and also whether confederates painted procedures as just, biased for them, or biased against them. The results showed that anger was significantly higher when a loss was the result of a procedure biased against them. In an example of an experience sampling study, Judge et al. (2006) examined daily variation in interpersonal justice in a multi-organization sample. Their results showed that within-person variation in interpersonal justice was associated with within-person variation in anger, even when controlling for dispositional affectivity. In an example of a study focused on sentiments, Baer et al. (2018) examined the effects of "unfairness talk"—”talking about unfairness with colleagues—”in a sample of bus drivers. Their results showed that unfairness talk was negatively related to hopefulness toward one's supervisor and positively related to anger toward one's supervisor.

use: Bennett and Robinson’s (2000) influential framework categorized workplace deviance based on the target of the behavior—either directed at individuals (interpersonal deviance, or ID) or the organization as a whole (organizational deviance, or OD). This ID-OD typology, along with their accompanying measurement scale, has become the dominant approach for conceptualizing and operationalizing workplace deviance in the literature (Berry et al., 2007; Mackey et al., 2021). Bennett and Robinson (2000) originally posited that “if an individual engages in one behavior from a family [of deviance behaviors], he or she is more likely to engage in another behavior from that family than to engage in a behavior within another family” (pp. 349–350). This proposition rests on two key assumptions. First, it implies that employees tend to engage in either ID or OD behaviors, but not both, typically in reaction to different situational triggers (Bennett et al., 2018). However, this assumption is called into question by consistently strong meta-analytic correlations between ID and OD, suggesting that the boundaries between the two forms of deviance may be more porous than originally theorized (Berry et al., 2007; Mackey et al., 2021).
text: Bennett and Robinson's (2000) seminal framework classified workplace deviance behaviors based on their targeting of other workers (ID) or the organization as a whole (OD). The ID-OD framework, along with Bennett and Robinson's (2000) measure, represents the most common way researchers have conceptualized and operationalized workplace deviance to date (Berry et al., 2007; Mackey et al., 2021). Bennett and Robinson (2000) originally theorized that "if an individual engages in one behavior from a family [of deviance behaviors], he or she is more likely to engage in another behavior from that family than to engage in a behavior within another family" (pp. 349-350), indicating two noteworthy assumptions. First, it suggests that employees will engage in either ID or OD, often in response to different environmental prompts (Bennett et al., 2018). Interestingly, strong ID-OD meta-analytic correlations undermine the veracity of this assumption (Berry et al., 2007; Mackey et al., 2021).

use: The expectations others place upon us are deeply intertwined with—and carry far-reaching implications for—status. In recent years, status has become a central concern in work motivation research, driven by the growing recognition that individuals are motivated not only by achievement but also by the desire to secure and enhance their position within social hierarchies. For instance, in a study of telemarketing sales representatives at a financial services firm, status strivings were found to predict job performance above and beyond achievement strivings (Barrick et al., 2002). Status hierarchies can be based on various attributes, including performance and intelligence. The level of status earned through prior performance can either motivate or hinder future outcomes. Research on NASCAR drivers and PGA golfers (Bothner et al., 2012) revealed a curvilinear relationship between past and future performance: individuals with moderate status tended to perform better, while those with very high status often underperformed. One possible explanation is that holding a top position—such as being the favorite—may lead to complacency.
text: The expectations others hold of us have deep roots in—”and broad implications for—”status. Status has increasingly emerged as a fundamental concern in work motivation research, following the recognition that individuals are motivated to maintain and improve their standing in social hierarchies. In a study of telemarketing sales representatives in a financial services company, status strivings predicted performance even after controlling for achievement strivings (Barrick et al., 2002). Status hierarchies can exist across a range of dimensions, from performance to intelligence. The level of status attained based on past performance can motivate or undermine future performance. In studies of NASCAR drivers and PGA golfers (Bothner et al., 2012), there was a curvilinear relationship between past performance and future performance, such that moderate status in the field predicted higher performance, but very high status was associated with lower performance. It may be that high status—”e.g., being a favorite—”can make individuals complacent.

use: In the context of this study, incumbents are likely to downplay the demographic differences of a newly appointed director once they recategorize that individual as part of their in-group based on perceived similarities. They tend to highlight points of commonality and may even perceive the new director as more similar to themselves than is objectively the case (Simon, Greenberg, & Brehm, 1995). This emphasis on perceived similarity can also inhibit the activation of demographic faultlines—hypothetical divisions within a group that emerge from the alignment of salient attributes and serve to split the group into subgroups (Lau & Murnighan, 1998). Since activated demographic faultlines are associated with heightened conflict among group members, recategorization has the potential to mitigate tensions between incumbents and the new director. By focusing on similarities rather than differences, this process can help foster smoother integration and strengthen interpersonal relationships (Jehn & Bezrukova, 2010).
text: In the context of the present study, incumbents are likely to trivialize the demographic differences of a new director once they recategorize him or her as an in-group member based on his or her similarities. They may emphasize the new director's similarities to them and perceive the new director to be more similar to them than he or she actually is (Simon, Greenberg, and Brehm, 1995). Incumbents' focus on a new director's similarities to them can also prevent the activation of demographic faultlines, defined as hypothetical lines that divide a group into multiple subgroups based on the alignment of salient attributes (Lau and Murnighan, 1998). Given that the activation of demographic faultlines increases conflicts among group members, recategorization can reduce a new director's conflicts with incumbents and improve their relationship by directing incumbents' attention to the new director's similarities rather than his or her differences (Jehn and Bezrukova, 2010).

use: The entrepreneurs featured in this study were selected from a pool of over 130 companies operating in the United Kingdom, obtained through the British-Bulgarian Chamber of Commerce following initial contact with the Embassy of the Republic of Bulgaria. This initial list was refined using a survey designed to distinguish transnational entrepreneurs (TEs)—those who serve as bridges between nations and cultures—from ethnic entrepreneurs, who cater primarily to a specific ethnic community. The survey also helped ensure that selected cases met a set of predefined selection criteria. These were: (1) alignment with the definition of TEs provided by Drori et al. (2009), as outlined in the introduction; (2) classification as small or medium-sized enterprises (SMEs); (3) location within the Greater London area; and (4) operation in the consulting services sector, as specified in their company profiles. The focus on consulting firms was deliberate, as the study is particularly concerned with the role of knowledge-based resources.
text: The entrepreneurs studied were identified from a list of more than 130 companies operating in the United Kingdom, obtained from the British-Bulgarian Chamber of Commerce after an initial contact with the Embassy of the Republic of Bulgaria. The group was further filtered by means of a survey that had the purpose of separating TEs, who act as bridges between nations and cultures, from ethnic entrepreneurs (i.e., persons serving only a particular ethnic community), as well as ensuring that the selected cases fulfill other selection criteria. These were that companies (1) fall under the Drori et al. (2009) definition of the TE cited in the introduction, (2) be considered SMEs, (3) be located in the Greater London area, and (4) engage in consulting services (as declared in their company profile). The reason for the decision to focus on consulting service providers was that we are particularly interested in knowledge resources.

use: This research indicates that hiring professionals should consider incorporating more job-relevant conscientiousness self-report measures into selection processes—particularly those that enhance item contextualization and focus on the achievement-striving and dutifulness facets. Our findings suggest that such tailored approaches may help reduce group differences in conscientiousness and its components between individuals with and without ADHD. Additionally, prior research has demonstrated that this strategy can also bolster the predictive validity of assessments (Bing et al., 2004; Lievens et al., 2008; Ones et al., 2007; Schmit et al., 1995). It is important to clarify, however, that the use of self-reported conscientiousness measures is legally permissible. In general, personality assessments have not been found to violate the Americans with Disabilities Act, as they are not designed to diagnose psychological conditions (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019; Wu & LeBreton, 2011). Rather, our findings suggest that when used as part of a comprehensive evaluation strategy, conscientiousness assessments can be meaningfully improved through context-specific design and facet-level precision—offering particular advantages for applicants with ADHD.
text: The present research suggests that hiring professionals should strive to use more job-relevant conscientiousness self-report measures in selection procedures, both in terms of item contextualization and the use of achievement-striving and dutifulness facets. Our findings suggest this approach may attenuate group differences in conscientiousness and facets by ADHD status, and other research suggests it can increase validity as well (Bing et al., 2004; Lievens et al., 2008; Ones et al., 2007; Schmit et al., 1995). Yet, to be clear, self-reported conscientiousness assessments are not unlawful, and overall, personality assessments have not been found to violate the Americans with Disabilities Act because they are not intended to diagnose respondents (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019; Wu & LeBreton, 2011). Instead, the implication of our findings is that conscientiousness assessments, as one part of a holistic approach to evaluation, can be improved through contextualization and facet-specificity, with particular benefits to those with ADHD.

use: Our study deepens identity-based justice research by illuminating the nuanced differences between two components of self-concept. While both positive self-evaluations and interdependent identity are shaped by experiences of group justice, their distinct psychological foundations suggest they influence cooperative behavior in different ways (Gecas, 1982; Markus & Wurf, 1987). Prior research has largely conflated these constructs, treating them as equivalent in their capacity to motivate voluntary cooperation (Blader & Tyler, 2009). By bringing greater specificity to this area of inquiry, we demonstrate that evaluative aspects of self-concept—positive self-evaluations—empower employees with a sense of personal agency that drives them to act for the group’s benefit, making them stronger predictors of organizational citizenship behavior (OCB). In contrast, the cognitive dimension—interdependent identity—encourages individuals to internalize group values and norms, making it more predictive of cooperative work behavior (CWB). This distinction offers a more refined understanding of how identity-based mechanisms contribute to different forms of voluntary group-oriented behavior, advancing theoretical insights into the psychological underpinnings of cooperation.
text: Our work further advances identity-based justice research by shedding light on the subtle differences in the roles of two self-concept components. Although positive self-evaluations and interdependent identity arise from group justice experiences, their distinct psychological bases would lead to differing behavioral approaches to group welfare (Gecas, 1982; Markus & Wurf, 1987). Extant research has treated them identically regarding their role in motivating voluntary cooperation (Blader & Tyler, 2009). Moving this line of work to a greater level of granularity, we largely document that the evaluative-based self-concept equips employees with personal agency for group benefits, making positive self-evaluations more powerful in predicting OCB, whereas the cognitive-based self-concept motivates them to internalize and act on group values and norms, leading to greater predictability of interdependent identity for CWB. This is thought-provoking because we introduce a more fine-grained approach to understanding why and how identity-based mechanisms engender different types of voluntary cooperation.

use: To achieve this, we utilized two samples of mock video interviews, human-generated transcripts of interviewee responses, and four sets of automated speech recognition (ASR) transcripts to explore two key areas: (1) disparities in ASR accuracy across various demographic subgroups—namely, English as a Second Language (ESL) speakers vs. non-ESL speakers; people of color (including Black, Asian, Latine, Indigenous, multiracial, and other racial/ethnic groups) vs. White interviewees; Black vs. White interviewees; and male vs. female interviewees—in the context of job interviews; and (2) whether, and to what extent, these subgroup differences in ASR accuracy introduce bias into machine learning (ML)-generated interview scores. These biases are assessed in terms of (a) differential validity, indicated by systematically weaker convergent correlations for one group compared to another (a key aspect of test validity), and (b) differential mean scores, reflected in one group receiving consistently lower scores than another (Tay et al., 2022), using ML scores derived from human transcripts as a benchmark. More specifically, our study investigates the following hypotheses and research questions:
text: To do so, we used two samples of mock video interviews, human transcriptions of interviewee responses, and four sets of ASR transcriptions to investigate (1) differences in ASR accuracy across demographic subgroups (specifically, English as a second language [ESL] speakers vs. non-ESL speakers; people of color, i.e., Black, Asian, Latine, Indigenous, more than one race, and other groups, vs. White interviewees; Black vs. White interviewees; and male vs. female interviewees) in the interview context and (2) whether and to what extent subgroup differences in ASR accuracy bias the ML interview scores, operationalized as (a) differential validity (i.e., systematically lower convergent correlations for one group compared to another; one aspect of validity) and (b) differential mean scores (i.e., systematically lower scores for one group compared to another; Tay et al., 2022), as compared to ML scores generated from human transcriptions. Specifically, our study examines the following hypotheses and research questions:

use: With our nuanced conceptualization of job-related cognitions during leisure time, we contribute to the broader literature on job-related cognitions and, more specifically, on job-related rumination (Jimenez et al., 2022; Weigelt et al., 2019). Previous research on job-related rumination has often relied on a unidimensional approach (Pindek et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2013), addressed only selective aspects of negative versus positive rumination (Fritz & Sonnentag, 2006; Frone, 2015), or employed confounded and relatively imprecise measures to capture different rumination modes (Cropley et al., 2012; see Table S1 for further detail). Our study addresses these limitations by offering a comprehensive, integrative perspective grounded in control theory. This framework incorporates both the valence (positive vs. negative) and the temporal direction (past vs. future) dimensions of rumination, thereby advancing theoretical insight into the mechanisms linking insufficient psychological detachment and affective outcomes. Through this lens, we illustrate the value of applying control theory to recovery research, enhancing understanding of job-related rumination and its implications.
text: With our differentiated conceptualization of job-related cognitions during leisure time, we advance the literature on job-related cognitions in general and on job-related rumination in particular (Jimenez et al., 2022; Weigelt et al., 2019). Until now, research on job-related rumination has either adopted a unidimensional view on rumination (Pindek et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2013), captured just some aspects of negative versus positive rumination (Fritz & Sonnentag, 2006; Frone, 2015), or used confounded and relatively unspecific measures for various rumination modes (Cropley et al., 2012; see Table S1 for more details). In our research, we overcome these limitations and provide an integrative control-theory perspective. This perspective combines the valence dimension with a temporal-direction dimension, moving the rumination literature forward and helping to improve the theoretical understanding of why lack of detachment relates to subsequent affect. With this approach, we demonstrate how control theory can be fruitfully applied within recovery research.

use: Although stereotype threat often operates beneath conscious awareness, leaders tend to respond to it in systematic and recognizable ways. Prior research indicates that individuals strategically use language—which is inherently dynamic, adaptable, and influential—to defy negative stereotypes about their own group, particularly in interactions with outgroup members. For instance, White liberals have been shown to use fewer dominance-related words when speaking to predominantly Black as opposed to predominantly White audiences (Dupree & Fiske, 2019), thereby countering stereotypes that portray White Americans as inherently dominant. Conversely, Black and Latina/o conservatives tend to use more dominance-related language than their liberal counterparts in mostly White environments (Dupree, 2021), challenging stereotypes that frame racial minorities as lacking power. While it is plausible that women in leadership roles similarly adopt counter-stereotypical language in outgroup contexts, empirical evidence on this behavior remains limited. Drawing from research on gender stereotypes, social identity threat, and self-presentation, I propose that women operating in male-dominated leadership arenas engage in counter-stereotypical self-presentation by employing dominant language that directly challenges prevailing stereotypes of submissiveness.
text: Although stereotype threat is relatively unconscious, leaders act to counter these stereotypes in predictable ways. Prior research suggests that people use language (which is dynamic, flexible, and impactful) that violates negative ingroup stereotypes when interacting with outgroup members. White liberals use fewer words related to dominance when addressing mostly Black (versus mostly White) audiences (Dupree and Fiske, 2019), thus violating stereotypes that depict White Americans as more dominant, and Black and Latina/o conservatives use more words related to dominance than liberals do in mostly White settings (Dupree, 2021), thus violating stereotypes that depict racial minorities as powerless. Women leaders may also use counter-stereotypical language in outgroup settings, but little research has tested this possibility. Grounding these ideas in the literatures on gender stereotypes, social identity threat, and self-presentation, I predict that women navigating leadership positions (a majority-men domain) engage in counter-stereotypical behavior by using dominant language that opposes submissiveness stereotypes.

use: Although scholars increasingly acknowledge the value of intersectional perspectives, their integration into social and behavioral research has progressed more slowly. In fact, some have characterized intersectionality as “a perspective in search of a method” (Shields, 2008: 306), highlighting the theoretical and methodological challenges it presents—especially in quantitative studies. There is a tendency among researchers to interpret intersectionality in narrowly statistical terms, often reducing it to a 2 x 2 interaction between identity-based variables. This allows for empirical testing of how the effect of one variable (e.g., gender) may depend on or be shaped by another (e.g., sexual orientation). However, such analyses are insufficient when isolated from the necessary theoretical and contextual grounding. Robust intersectional research must begin with a theoretical articulation of the stereotypes—embedded in social, cultural, and historical contexts—that justify the expectation of interaction effects. This article aims to carry out both the theoretical and empirical components of such work.
text: While scholars have increasingly noted the importance of intersectional perspectives, the application of such perspectives to social and behavioral scholarship has been slower to materialize. Indeed, some scholars have called intersectionality "a perspective in search of a method" (Shields, 2008: 306), possibly due to the theoretical and empirical complexities of conducting such analyses, particularly in quantitative work. Scholars may be tempted to view intersectionality in purely statistical terms, a 2 x 2 analysis of two identity-based variables. Through statistical analyses, one can empirically test how the effect of one variable (e.g., gender) is impacted by the effects of another variable (e.g., sexual orientation). Yet, such analysis is incomplete without accompanying theoretical and contextual work. Intersectional analyses of any identity-based phenomenon must first involve a theoretical conceptualization of the underlying stereotypes—”stereotypes rooted in social, cultural, and historical contexts—”that drive a rationale for proposed interaction effects. This article takes both these steps.

use: We propose that the inconsistencies across prior findings may stem from a mismatch between the level at which the phenomenon occurs (intra-individual) and the level at which it is typically theorized (inter-individual). At its essence, conflict is experienced at the intra-individual level—that is, within the person (Ilies et al., 2011; Rispens & Demerouti, 2016). However, most existing studies conceptualize and measure conflict as individuals’ general assessments of its overall frequency or intensity, effectively collapsing multiple conflict episodes—each potentially varying widely in form and in interpersonal context—into a single aggregated judgment (Park et al., 2020). This broad inter-individual framing obscures the rich within-person variability in how conflict is actually experienced, limiting our understanding of its nuanced effects on individuals. For example, an individual might, at one moment, actively express conflict toward a teammate (i.e., sending conflict) and, at another, be on the receiving end of conflict from another group member (i.e., receiving conflict). Each of these experiences carries different psychological and behavioral implications that are concealed when conflict is treated as a uniform, person-level construct.
text: We suggest that these disparate findings may emanate from a misalignment between the levels of the phenomenon (intra-individual) and theory (inter-individual). First, at its core, the experience of conflict is an intra-individual phenomenon (Ilies et al., 2011; Rispens & Demerouti, 2016). Nonetheless, in most studies, individual conflict experiences have been conceptualized and operationalized as one’s summary judgments of conflict amount or frequency, which may represent an amalgamation of conflict manifestations that have occurred in different ways and with different others (Park et al., 2020). This broad way of theorizing inter-individual conflict limits our understanding of the conflict effects on individuals because it masks the within-person variance in conflict experiences. For instance, at one point, an individual may be involved in a conflict incident wherein they express conflict with another member (i.e., sending conflict), and, at another point, they may be targeted by conflict expressed by other team members (i.e., receiving conflict).

use: The opportunity structure explanation of homophilous behavior proposes that individuals’ likelihood of associating with similar others is shaped by how people are distributed across categories within a given social setting. For instance, some scholars estimate association probabilities based on the distribution of managers across functional areas. Yet empirical research frequently finds that actual social connections deviate from these statistical expectations. A likely explanation is that, over time, social norms develop that imbue certain job functions with value or prestige. In some organizations, for example, engineering roles may be highly esteemed, while in others, they may be seen as less prestigious. These status hierarchies become part of the organization’s shared understanding, internalized by members through socialization, and subsequently shape patterns of affiliation. Without accounting for status norms, it is difficult to determine whether social ties arise from structural opportunities, normative values, or an interplay of both. Overlooking the influence of such norms in analyses of homophily disregards the meanings individuals attach to their associations and the conditions under which these ties become socially significant.
text: The opportunity structure explanation of homophilous behavior suggests that individuals' distributions across categories within a social context define the probability they choose similar others. For example, some scholars use the distribution of managers across job functions to define their probabilities of association. Research shows, however, that actual associations often do not follow these expected probabilities. One likely reason is that over time, social norms emerge and attach value to individuals' job functions. In some organizations, engineering functions hold high status; whereas in others, they hold low status. An organization's status system becomes common knowledge among those socialized within it, which influences individuals' tendency to associate with one another. Without measuring status norms, it is unclear whether such associations result from the opportunity structure, the norms, or both. Failing to consider the role norms play in homophily omits why and under what conditions such associations are meaningful to those who experience them.

use: Sleep and work behavior are closely interconnected. Most theoretical frameworks in this area have emphasized the role of biophysiological processes occurring during sleep and how these processes influence cognitive functioning upon waking (e.g., Barnes, Lucianetti, Bhave, & Christian, 2015; Barnes, Schaubroeck, Huth, & Ghumman, 2011; Christian & Ellis, 2011). This cognitive functioning, in turn, shapes employee behavior (Litwiller, Snyder, Taylor, & Steele, 2017). As research in this space has largely coalesced around a biophysiological model, it has attracted considerable interest from both management scholars and practitioners (Barnes, 2012). However, the prevailing emphasis on biophysiological explanations may be premature and overly narrow. In addition to physical processes, individuals also undergo psychological experiences during sleep—namely, dreams. These experiences are frequently remembered (e.g., Hobson, Pace-Schott, & Stickgold, 2000; Nielsen, 2000) and often become the subject of waking reflection and interpretation, making dreams a potential influence on subsequent behavior (Morewedge & Norton, 2009). As such, dreams may represent a meaningful yet underexplored source of variability in employee behavior.
text: Sleep and work behavior are linked. Most theorizing in this domain has attributed this link to how biophysiological processes occurring during sleep impact waking cognitive functioning (e.g., Barnes, Lucianetti, Bhave, & Christian, 2015; Barnes, Schaubroeck, Huth, & Ghumman, 2011; Christian & Ellis, 2011), which in turn affects employee behavior (Litwiller, Snyder, Taylor, & Steele, 2017). As this body of work has converged around a biophysiological model, it has generated substantial interest among management scholars and practitioners alike (Barnes, 2012). Yet the consensus around biophysiological explanations of sleep and work behavior may be premature. Beyond objective changes in physiology, people also navigate psychological experiences during sleep, known as dreams. These experiences are often recalled (e.g., Hobson, Pace-Schott, & Stickgold, 2000; Nielsen, 2000) and subject to waking sensemaking processes, lending dreams the capacity to alter waking behavior (Morewedge & Norton, 2009). Dreams may therefore be an important, overlooked source of variance in employee behavior.

use: Emerging theory on group flow remains underdeveloped, lacking a comprehensive framework that clearly defines what group flow entails and elucidates the mechanisms through which it emerges and dissipates (Pels, Kleinert, & Mennigen, 2018). The aim of this study is to advance a more refined conceptualization of group flow as an emergent, dynamic state, and to offer a theoretical explanation of how it develops and fades over time. This approach sheds light on the fluid and moment-to-moment interaction patterns that unfold as group members come together to prepare for and engage in a variety of tasks at the peak of their capabilities. In doing so, we emphasize the significance of group momentum and the evolving interplay among cognitive, emotional, and behavioral processes over time. Furthermore, we detail the diverse benefits that group flow generates—not only for individual members but also for the collective functioning and performance of the group as a whole.
text: Nascent theory on group flow remains underspecified and has yet to provide a comprehensive theoretical account of what group flow is, as well as the processes through which it arises and dissipates (Pels, Kleinert, & Mennigen, 2018). The goal of the present research is to provide a more detailed conceptualization of group flow as an emergent state and a theoretical account of how group flow arises and dissipates over time, thereby providing a window into the momentary and changing patterns of interactions that unfold as members come together to prepare and perform a wide range of tasks to the best of their abilities. In doing so, we highlight the importance of group momentum and the dynamic interplay between the cognitive, affective, and behavioral changes that unfold over time. In addition, we outline the wide range of benefits that group flow has for its members, as well as the group as a whole.

use: Creativity is widely defined as the generation of ideas, solutions, or products that are both novel (original) and appropriate (feasible, potentially useful) (e.g., Amabile, 1983; Paulus & Nijstad, 2003; Sternberg & Lubart, 1999). Among these criteria, originality is often regarded as the defining feature of creativity—without it, creativity cannot exist. However, an idea or product that is original but lacks appropriateness is typically dismissed as eccentric or irrelevant. A key challenge lies in the fact that what is deemed inappropriate or impractical at one moment may later be recognized as highly appropriate—and thus highly creative—or the reverse may occur. Vincent van Gogh's legacy exemplifies this phenomenon: while he died in poverty and obscurity, his artwork is now celebrated and fetches millions. This temporal dimension highlights the difficulty of objectively assessing creativity. As a result, many scholars propose that creativity is best evaluated through social consensus—specifically, when qualified experts agree that an idea or product is creative (e.g., Amabile, 1982; Csikszentmihalyi, 1999).
text: Creativity is commonly defined as the production of ideas, problem solutions, and products that are both novel (original) and appropriate (feasible, potentially useful) (e.g., Amabile, 1983; Paulus & Nijstad, 2003; Sternberg & Lubart, 1999). Originality is considered the hallmark of creativity, and indeed one cannot be creative without being original. However, ideas or products that are original but not appropriate would be considered crazy and irrelevant. One problem is that original ideas that are considered inappropriate at some point in time may be seen as appropriate (and therefore highly creative) later on (or vice versa). The work of Vincent van Gogh is a good illustration: Van Gogh died a poor man, but his work now sells for millions. Creativity therefore cannot readily be established in an objective way, and several researchers have suggested that a product or idea is creative when relevant experts agree that it is creative (e.g., Amabile, 1982; Csikszentmihalyi, 1999).

use: Job crafting theory posits that individuals can actively reshape their work behavior by taking an agentic role in modifying their job design (Berg et al., 2010; Grant et al., 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). These modifications may involve changes to various job boundaries: task boundaries (e.g., altering the scope, type, or number of tasks performed), relational boundaries (e.g., changing with whom one interacts or the quality of those interactions), cognitive boundaries (e.g., reframing one’s perception of the job and its significance), and skill boundaries (e.g., seeking skill development opportunities or simplifying aspects of the job) (Berg, Wrzesniewski, Grant, Kurkoski, & Welle, 2022; Bindl, Unsworth, Gibson, & Stride, 2019; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). More recent studies have further distinguished between two broad orientations of these practices: promotion-oriented job crafting, which seeks to expand or enrich aspects of the job, and prevention-oriented job crafting, which aims to reduce or minimize aspects of the job to avoid potential negative outcomes (Bindl et al., 2019).
text: Job crafting theory suggests that people indeed can change their work behavior by being agentic actors in their workplace to modify their jobs (Berg et al., 2010; Grant et al., 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Such modifications can relate to task boundaries (i.e., altering the type or number of job tasks), relational boundaries (i.e., altering with whom one interacts at work, or the nature of interactions), cognitive boundaries (i.e., altering the view one has of their job and the meaning assigned to it), and skill boundaries (i.e., seeking opportunities to develop new skills or simplifying the job) (Berg, Wrzesniewski, Grant, Kurkoski, & Welle, 2022; Bindl, Unsworth, Gibson, & Stride, 2019; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). More recent research has also demonstrated that those practices can be categorized as either promotion-oriented (aimed at expanding job aspects) or prevention-oriented (aimed at decreasing job aspects to prevent negative outcomes from occurring) job crafting (Bindl et al., 2019).

use: Our aim is to explore the contingencies that shape how negative evaluations of one’s tasks and self influence emotional exhaustion. Emotional exhaustion refers to a state of affective depletion, marked by low energy and a psychologically aversive experience (see Halbesleben & Buckley, 2004; Maslach et al., 2001, for reviews). To examine task- and self-evaluations, we concentrate on two key constructs: intrinsic motivation and core self-evaluations. Intrinsic motivation plays a pivotal role in how employees perceive and assess their work tasks. When employees are intrinsically motivated, they are driven by genuine interest and enjoyment in the tasks themselves, which leads to more favorable task evaluations. In contrast, a lack of intrinsic motivation often results in more negative task appraisals (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Ryan & Deci, 2000). For self-evaluations, we draw on the concept of core self-evaluations—a widely used framework for understanding how individuals perceive themselves. This construct reflects the degree to which employees view their overall self-worth and capabilities in a positive versus negative manner (Judge, Erez, Bono, & Thoreson, 2002).
text: Our focus is on understanding contingencies that influence the impact of negative task and self-evaluations on emotional exhaustion. Emotional exhaustion is a state of affective fatigue that is characterized by low levels of energy and is experienced as psychologically aversive (for reviews, see Halbesleben & Buckley, 2004; Maslach et al., 2001). To capture task and self-evaluations, we focus on intrinsic motivation and core self-evaluations. Intrinsic motivation is a central concept for understanding how employees evaluate their tasks. When employees are intrinsically motivated, they experience a desire to expend effort based on inherent enjoyment and interest in work tasks themselves, and when they are not intrinsically motivated, they evaluate their tasks more negatively (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Ryan & Deci, 2000). Core self-evaluations, a dominant concept for understanding how employees evaluate themselves, refers to the extent to which employees see their global self-concepts in a positive or negative light (Judge, Erez, Bono, & Thoreson, 2002).

use: Due to the inherent challenges of directly observing boardroom behavior, researchers have largely relied on quantitative studies—frequently using secondary data—and grounded their analyses in established theoretical frameworks to infer the mechanisms by which board gender diversity influences firm outcomes. For instance, theories from cognitive psychology (Fiske & Taylor, 1984), upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984), and gender socialization theory (Bandura, 1977; Bussey & Bandura, 1999) suggest that women, shaped by distinct socialization experiences, bring different cognitive perspectives compared to men. Additionally, concepts such as gender stereotypes (Schein, 1973; 1975), tokenism (Kanter, 1977a; 1977b), social identity theory (Ashforth & Mael, 1989), and social network theory (Feld, 1981) have been used to explain how gender diversity may alter the dynamics of board decision-making. However, despite the expanding body of research on board gender diversity, our understanding of the actual influence of women directors remains incomplete without a direct examination of the interpersonal behaviors and interactions that unfold within the boardroom itself.
text: Given the difficulty of examining boardroom behavior, scholars have conducted quantitative studies, predominantly with secondary data, and have used established theories to infer the mechanisms underlying board gender diversity's influence on firm outcomes. For example, concepts from cognitive psychology (Fiske & Taylor, 1984), upper echelon theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984), and gender socialization theory (Bandura, 1977; Bussey & Bandura, 1999) have been used to suggest that, due to their background and gender socialization, women have cognitive perspectives and experiences that diverge from those of men. Moreover, researchers have used theories on gender stereotypes (Schein, 1973; 1975), tokenism (Kanter, 1977a; 1977b), social identity (Ashforth & Mael, 1989), and social networks (Feld, 1981) to explain how board gender diversity may impact board decision-making. Yet, despite the growing volume of research on board gender diversity, we cannot fully understand the influence of women directors on the board without examining the behaviors that actually occur in the boardroom.

use: The second stream of research has directly examined how practices from different AMO (Ability, Motivation, Opportunity) domains interact with one another. Most studies in this area have focused on two-way interactions between selected HR practices, but their findings have been mixed. For instance, some studies identified synergistic effects between practices such as selection (A) and participation (O; Wright et al., 1999), training (A) and teamwork (O; Birdi et al., 2008), and performance appraisal (M) and internal career opportunities (O; Darwish et al., 2013). Conversely, other studies found no evidence of such synergies—for example, between internal staffing (A) and team autonomy (O; Arthur, 2011) or performance-based pay (M) and teamwork (O; Frick et al., 2013). Our review of the literature shows that only one study has attempted to examine potential interrelations among HR practices spanning all three AMO domains. However, even this effort was limited, as it considered only three practices: staffing (A), performance-based pay (M), and participation (O; J. Delery & Gupta, 2016).
text: The second body of work has directly tested the interactions among practices that belong to different AMO domains. Most research along this line has explored two-way interactions between select HR practices, and the results have been inconsistent. For example, some studies found support for synergistic interactions between practices, such as selection (A) and participation (O; Wright et al., 1999), training (A) and teamwork (O; Birdi et al., 2008), and performance appraisal (M) and internal career opportunity (O; Darwish et al., 2013). In contrast, other studies failed to support synergistic interactions, for example, between internal staffing (A) and team autonomy (O; Arthur, 2011) and performance pay (M) and teamwork (O; Frick et al., 2013). Our literature review revealed that only one study explored the possible interrelations between HR practices across all AMO domains, but it did so by using only three practices: staffing (A), performance-based pay (M), and participation (O; J. Delery & Gupta, 2016).

use: Intersectional analysis is essential to this—and, indeed, any—exploration of gender inequality. Gender stereotypes are inherently racialized, meaning that interpretations of gendered behavior, such as stereotype-relevant communication by leaders, are shaped by the leader’s race. For example, Black women are often perceived as less feminine and more dominant than their White counterparts (Hall, Galinsky, & Phillips, 2015; Rosette et al., 2016). In addition, they are burdened by the “angry Black woman” stereotype—an ingrained belief that Black women are more angry or hostile than others (Harris-Perry, 2011; Durik et al., 2006). Given that individuals tend to attend more to and exaggerate behavior that aligns with stereotypes (Trope & Thompson, 1997; Biernat, 2012), expressions of dominance by Black women may be disproportionately interpreted as unwarranted anger. This dynamic can lead to discrediting them in professional or leadership contexts. Consequently, Black women leaders may face heightened risks of backlash, which may in turn shape how they choose to express themselves and how their language is publicly received. Understanding these dynamics requires an intersectional approach—one that recognizes how the convergence of race and gender informs both communication strategies and their consequences for women leaders.
text: Intersectional analyses are crucial for this (and, indeed, any) examination of gender inequality. Gender stereotypes are racialized. As such, theorized gender differences in leaders' stereotype-relevant communication likely depend on leaders' race. For instance, Black women are seen as less feminine and more dominant than White women (Hall, Galinsky, and Phillips, 2015; Rosette et al., 2016). Moreover, Black women contend with the "angry Black woman" stereotype (Harris-Perry, 2011), the notion that Black women are angrier than other women (Durik et al., 2006). Because people attend more closely to and exaggerate stereotype-consistent behavior (Trope and Thompson, 1997; Biernat, 2012), Black women's expressions of dominance may be amplified and perceived as unjustified anger, leaving them discredited. Black women leaders therefore contend with an additional possibility of backlash, which potentially influences their use of dominant language and the public's reactions to such language. Intersectional analyses are therefore necessary to fully understand women leaders' stereotype-relevant communication and its implications.

use: Conflict behaviors—defined as expressions of opposing viewpoints between individuals (Weingart, Behfar, Bendersky, Todorova, & Jehn, 2015)—frequently emerge as people collaborate within and across team boundaries in organizational settings (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018; Mathieu, Hollenbeck, van Knippenberg, & Ilgen, 2017). Individual experiences of conflict involve patterns of interpersonal interaction that unfold as individuals confront disputes while performing their tasks. These experiences are inherently directional, encompassing both (1) sending—outward expressions of disagreement—and (2) receiving—being the recipient of such expressions (Tsai & Bendersky, 2016; Weingart et al., 2015). Moreover, conflict is not limited to intra-team interactions; it often traverses team boundaries, meaning that individuals may encounter conflict with both teammates and members of other teams at varying points in time (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018; Mortensen & Haas, 2018). We propose that these conflict experiences—shaped by their directionality and boundary location—differentially impact individuals’ ongoing task performance.
text: Conflict behaviors, through which individuals express oppositional standpoints (Weingart, Behfar, Bendersky, Todorova, & Jehn, 2015), commonly occur when individuals are working together within and between team boundaries in an organizational system (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018; Mathieu, Hollenbeck, van Knippenberg, & Ilgen, 2017). Individual conflict experiences comprise behavioral interactions among individuals as they engage in conflict while completing their tasks. Every conflict experience has two directions: (1) sending (i.e., outward direction), expressing the conflict issues, and (2) receiving (i.e., inward direction), being the target of the expression (Tsai & Bendersky, 2016; Weingart et al., 2015). Furthermore, conflict often crosses team boundaries, meaning that team members may experience conflict with members of their own team and members of other teams at different times (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018; Mortensen & Haas, 2018). We argue that individuals’ ongoing task performance will be differently affected by their conflict experiences that are characterized by their directions and boundaries

use: Crucially, our structural equation modeling results demonstrated that schadenfreude had meaningful behavioral implications. Specifically, experiencing schadenfreude in response to the event was positively linked to antisocial behavior toward the victim, indicating that it functioned as a mediating mechanism between witnessing mistreatment and engaging in such harmful actions. Additionally, because schadenfreude was negatively associated with evaluations of the victim, its mediating role was embedded within a more complex pathway: observing mistreatment led to schadenfreude, which in turn resulted in more negative perceptions of the victim, ultimately predicting antisocial behavior toward them. A similarly troubling pattern emerged concerning the perpetrator: observing mistreatment elicited schadenfreude, which was then associated with more favorable evaluations of the perpetrator, and those evaluations in turn predicted prosocial behavior toward the perpetrator. These findings underscore that schadenfreude is not merely a benign emotional experience—it can shape evaluative judgments and drive consequential behavior toward both victims and perpetrators.
text: Importantly, our structural equation modeling results revealed that those schadenfreude effects were consequential behaviorally. Because schadenfreude from the event was positively related to antisocial behavior toward the victim, it played a mediating role in the connection between observing mistreatment and such antisocial behaviors. Because it was also negatively related to the evaluation of the victim, that mediating role was supplemented by a more complex pattern where observing mistreatment was associated with schadenfreude, schadenfreude was associated with more negative victim evaluations, and those victim evaluations were associated with antisocial behavior toward the victim. A similarly dark sequence played out on the perpetrator side of the ledger, where observing mistreatment was associated with schadenfreude, schadenfreude was associated with more positive perpetrator evaluations, and those perpetrator evaluations were associated with prosocial behavior toward the perpetrator. Such results suggest that schadenfreude is not merely a harmless affective indulgence—it can wind up having behavioral consequences for the parties involved.

use: Our study offers two key contributions. First, we shed light on the challenges individuals face in sustaining creative output over time. Among 224 first-time authors in the U.K., only around half go on to publish a second book. Similarly, in two experimental studies, just 21% and 34% of first-time creators chose to pursue a second idea rather than continue developing their initial one. Strikingly, even among those who achieve early success—such as winning an award for their novel work—the likelihood of producing subsequent novel work declines significantly. In response to these findings, we contribute both theoretically and empirically by advancing a model of creative productivity over time within the same domain. Through this framework, we pinpoint key factors that influence sustained creative engagement and offer practical insights into how the barriers to ongoing idea generation might be reduced.
text: Our study makes two primary contributions. First, our work highlights the difficulties that people have in sustaining creativity over time. Among the 224 first-time authors in the U.K., only about 50% publish a second book. In our two experimental studies, the percentage of first-time producers who decided to develop a second idea as opposed to exploiting the first idea was 21% and 34%, respectively. Indeed, among producers of novel work, winning an award for it significantly reduces the likelihood of producing follow-up novel work. We contribute to the literature by building theory and by providing empirical evidence for a model of creative productivity over time and in the same domain. In doing so, we identify some of the key factors that regulate the extent to which people remain creatively productive and offer some practical guidance for how to ameliorate the forces that deter people from continually engaging in the process of creating new ideas.

use: Recent developments in personality research within organizational contexts reflect a notable shift toward a more dynamic understanding of personality. Rather than focusing solely on how personality traits predict work-related outcomes, increasing attention is being paid to the reciprocal influence of work experiences on personality development (Sosnowska et al., 2021). Growing empirical evidence underscores the idea that personality continues to evolve throughout adulthood (Denissen et al., 2019), and that this evolution is meaningfully shaped by an individual’s professional experiences and career trajectory. Influential factors include job attitudes (e.g., Wille, Hofmans, et al., 2014), work characteristics (e.g., Wu et al., 2020), vocational interests (e.g., Wille & De Fruyt, 2014), and markers of career success (e.g., Hirschi et al., 2021), such as the achievement of leadership roles (e.g., Li et al., 2021; Nie & Zacher, 2015). Conceptualizing personality as a continuously developing construct has significantly enriched and broadened the scope of personality research in organizational settings (Tasselli et al., 2018).
text: Importantly, personality research in organizational contexts has recently evolved toward a more dynamic approach to personality, wherein the focus lies not only on understanding how personality predicts work-related phenomena but also on the reversed effects from work to personality change (Sosnowska et al., 2021). Recent evidence supports the idea that personality continues to develop throughout adulthood (Denissen et al., 2019), and that individual developmental trajectories can be shaped by one's work and career, including but not limited to job attitudes (e.g., Wille, Hofmans, et al., 2014), work characteristics (e.g., Wu et al., 2020), vocational interests (e.g., Wille & De Fruyt, 2014), and career success indicators (e.g., Hirschi et al., 2021), such as the attainment of leadership positions (e.g., Li et al., 2021; Nie & Zacher, 2015). Indeed, this view of personality as a construct that is in constant development has deepened and expanded research on personality in the work context (Tasselli et al., 2018).

use: Latent psychological constructs, such as personality traits, are often expressed through context-specific behaviors—verbal behavior being a prime example. To serve as inputs in machine learning (ML) models designed to assess these psychological constructs, such behaviors must first be transformed into quantitative data (Tay et al., 2020). This typically involves transcribing the interviewee’s spoken words and applying natural language processing (NLP) techniques to convert the raw text into structured, analyzable data. These data are then used by ML models to infer psychological attributes (Hickman et al., 2022). However, the documented differences in automatic speech recognition (ASR) accuracy across demographic subgroups raise concerns. Specifically, the process of transcribing speech via ASR may introduce disproportionately higher error rates—effectively a form of bias—for certain groups. The ML measurement bias framework (Tay et al., 2022) posits that such bias in the input data (termed data bias) can propagate through the modeling pipeline, resulting in biased predictions. Consequently, individuals belonging to demographic subgroups with lower ASR accuracy may be unfairly disadvantaged by the ML-based scoring of psychological characteristics.
text: Latent psychological constructs, such as personality traits, manifest as context-specific behaviors (e.g., verbal behavior). These behaviors must be converted into quantitative data before they can be used as predictors in ML models to score these latent psychological constructs (Tay et al., 2020).
