Toward this goal, words spoken by the interviewee are first transcribed and then converted into structured, quantitative data using natural language processing (NLP) before being input into ML models that score interviewees' psychological attributes (Hickman et al., 2022). Accordingly, the evidence that ASR transcription exhibits subgroup accuracy differences raises concerns that converting spoken words into transcribed speech using ASR will introduce more errors—”a form of bias—”for certain subgroups. The ML measurement bias framework (Tay et al., 2022) suggests that biases in ASR input data (referred to as data bias) could bias downstream ML models. If so, this could result in bias disadvantaging members of demographic subgroups for whom the ASR transcriptions were less accurate.

use: Once a witness forms a perception of a leader’s deservingness of gratitude, attribution theory offers important insights into how the expresser of gratitude will subsequently be viewed. As outlined by Heider (1958), attributional processes occur across multiple stages. The first is causal attribution, where an observer attempts to discern whether an outcome resulted from personal agency or situational factors. The second is intention attribution, where the observer evaluates whether the action in question was deliberate or accidental. The third involves motive attribution, where the observer seeks to understand the underlying reasons behind a person's actions. Prior research underscores that employees are particularly attuned to contextual cues when forming motive attributions about leaders, and that these attributions significantly influence their responses. For instance, Matta et al. (2020) demonstrated that employees’ motive attributions directly affect their trust in leaders, while Qin et al. (2020) showed that such attributions—especially those concerning perceived humility—can shape employee deviance.
text: Once a witness’s perceptions of a leader’s deservingness of gratitude have been formed, the attributions literature suggests direct implications for how the expresser will be perceived. According to Heider (1958), attributional processes occur at several levels. First are causal attributions, wherein an individual seeks to determine if an outcome was driven by the person or the situation. Next are intention attributions, wherein an individual seeks to determine if an action was intentional or accidental. Third are motive attributions, wherein an individual seeks to determine why another person acted in a certain way. Past research has shown that employees are highly sensitive to features of the situation relevant to the motive attributions ascribed to leaders, and that these attributions impact observer behavior in important ways. For example, Matta et al. (2020) found that motive attributions shape employees’ trust in leaders, while Qin et al. (2020) found that motive attributions surrounding leaders’ acts of humility shape employee deviance.

use: Enhancing fairness in self-reported selection measures may lead to greater inclusion of neurodivergent individuals in the workplace; however, this alone does not ensure that employees with ADHD will receive the working conditions and accommodations they require to succeed. A consensus statement by medical professionals (Adamou et al., 2013) outlined several strategies for supporting employees with ADHD. These include aligning individuals’ strengths with job demands, providing administrative tools and structures to aid in time management and organization, designing quiet workspaces to reduce distractions, and delivering performance feedback with sensitivity to emotional lability and heightened sensitivity to rejection (Adamou et al., 2013). Complementing this, Lyhne et al.’s (2021) qualitative research on workplace support for individuals with ADHD revealed that key supports include clear role expectations, assistance in developing routines and self-confidence, and access to a trusted “lifeline” — someone who offers ongoing mentorship, feedback, and support. It is essential that future research prioritizes the creation of environments where neurodivergent employees, like all workers, are given the necessary conditions to reach their full potential.
text: In addition, increasing fairness in self-reported selection measures may bring more neurodivergent employees into the workplace, but this does not guarantee that people with ADHD will have the working conditions and accommodations they need to thrive. A consensus statement by clinicians (Adamou et al., 2013) suggested strategies to support success among employees with ADHD, including maximizing fit between individuals’ characteristics and job demands, using administrative supports and tools to help with time management and organization, creating quiet workspaces to minimize distractions, and being conscious of sensitivity to rejection and emotional lability when communicating performance appraisals (Adamou et al., 2013). Lyhne et al.’s (2021) qualitative study on workplace support for people with ADHD found that people with ADHD benefited from clear expectations of job roles, support for building self-confidence and routine, and having a “lifeline” who provides support, feedback, and mentorship. Future research must ensure that neurodivergent workers, like all workers, have the conditions necessary to thrive.

use: The primary aim of quasi-experimentation is to enhance causal inference while preserving both internal and external validity, all without disrupting "real life" through intrusive intervention (Campbell & Stanley, 1966; Cook & Campbell, 1979; Webb, Campbell, Schwartz, & Sechrest, 1966). A quasi-experiment is conducted in a naturalistic setting and involves a shift in a key independent variable of interest, but it relaxes one or both core conditions of traditional laboratory and field experiments: random assignment to treatment groups and controlled manipulation of the independent variable. As such, quasi-experiments encompass both experimenter- and manager-initiated interventions where randomization is not feasible, such as instances where treatments are applied to intact or preexisting groups. They also include instances where changes to the independent variable occur naturally rather than as a result of direct manipulation. As Evans (1976) observed, “Often this type of research is an opportunistic process. One finds out about an organizational experiment and ‘parachutes in’ to find out about its impact” (p. 99).
text: The objective of quasi-experimentation is to strengthen causal inference while maintaining internal and external validity without interrupting "real life" through intrusive intervention (Campbell & Stanley, 1966; Cook & Campbell, 1979; Webb, Campbell, Schwartz, & Sechrest, 1966). A quasi-experiment is a study that takes place in a field setting and involves a change in a key independent variable of interest but relaxes one or both of the defining criteria of laboratory and field experiments: random assignment to treatment conditions and controlled manipulation of the independent variable. Quasi-experiments thus include experimenter-controlled and manager-controlled interventions in which random assignment is not achieved, such as when treatments are assigned to intact or preexisting groups. Quasi-experiments also include changes to an independent variable that are naturally occurring rather than manipulated. As Evans (1976) explained, "Often this type of research is an opportunistic process. One finds out about an organizational experiment and 'parachutes in' to find out about its impact" (p. 99).

use: Bias refers to an unjustified response—be it evaluative, emotional, cognitive, or behavioral—that either advantages or disadvantages an individual or the group they belong to in relation to others (Dovidio & Gaertner, 2010). A widely recognized framework for distinguishing types of bias or attitudinal preferences is the tripartite model of attitudes (Rosenberg & Hovland, 1960). In its contemporary interpretation, this model conceptualizes attitudes as evaluations of a target that operate along three interconnected dimensions: cognitive (thoughts, beliefs, or ideas), affective (positive or negative emotions), and behavioral (observable reactions and responses; Fabrigar et al., 2005; Kaiser & Wilson, 2019). When applied to intergroup contexts, these dimensions correspond to distinct phenomena: stereotypes represent the cognitive component, encompassing culturally shared mental representations of a particular group; prejudice reflects the affective component, marked by generalized emotional responses of favor or disfavor; and discrimination captures the behavioral component, where attitudinal biases are expressed through decisions or actions (Esses & Dovidio, 2002; Fiske, 1998).
text: Bias is an unfair response (evaluative, emotional, cognitive, or behavioral) that favors or disfavors an individual or the group to which they belong over others (Dovidio & Gaertner, 2010). One way bias, or attitudinal preferences, is broadly distinguished in the literature is through the tripartite model of attitudes (Rosenberg & Hovland, 1960). According to the contemporary view of this theoretical model, attitudes reflect an appraisal of a target associated with three dimensions: cognitive (thoughts, ideas, or beliefs), affective (negative or positive emotions), and behavioral (overt actions and responses; Fabrigar et al., 2005; Kaiser & Wilson, 2019). When applying this model to intergroup attitudes, these manifestations relate to the following concepts: stereotypes constitute the cognitive dimension, representing socially learned mental schemas of a given group; prejudice is the affective dimension characterized by an overall positive or negative valence; and discrimination embodies the behavioral dimension, capturing attitudes manifested in actions and decisions (Esses & Dovidio, 2002; Fiske, 1998).

use: Many scholars consider the deep intertwining of self and work to be the defining feature of experiencing work as a calling (Cardador, Dane, & Pratt, 2011; Dobrow & Tosti-Kharas, 2011; Hirschi, 2011). They have proposed various pathways through which this connection may emerge, such as the alignment of core life beliefs—like moral obligations or prosocial values—with one's professional activities (Bellah et al., 2007; Wrzesniewski, 2012); the pursuit or realization of a life purpose through work (Duffy & Dik, 2013); the belief in being uniquely created, destined, or inherently “wired” for a specific type of work (Baumeister, 1991; Bunderson & Thompson, 2009); and the experience of work as an all-consuming, deeply meaningful passion (Dobrow & Tosti-Kharas, 2011; Dobrow, 2013). Given that callings represent some of the most profound self-work connections individuals can experience, it becomes crucial to examine how professionals who feel called construct identities that enable this integration of self and work—and what types of self-work relationships make such integration possible.
text: Many scholars regard an intimate connection between self and work to be the essence of the experience of work as a calling (Cardador, Dane, and Pratt, 2011; Dobrow and Tosti-Kharas, 2011; Hirschi, 2011). They have posited a variety of ways this connection may arise, including a blending of core life beliefs (moral duties, prosocial values) and work (Bellah et al., 2007; Wrzesniewski, 2012); a fulfillment of one's life purpose in and through one's work (Duffy and Dik, 2013); an experience of being uniquely created, destined, or specially "hardwired" for a particular kind of work (Baumeister, 1991; Bunderson and Thompson, 2009); and an experience of one's work as a consuming, meaningful passion (Dobrow and Tosti-Kharas, 2011; Dobrow, 2013). If callings are the most intimate connections people can have with their work, it is important to understand how called professionals construct identities that achieve this integration of self and work and what forms of self-work connection underlie this integration.

use: Second, our research centers on the behaviors of responders—specifically, the quality of the responses they provide to those who are suffering. This approach contrasts with much of the existing literature, which predominantly examines how past experiences of distress influence internal, non-behavioral outcomes such as emotions and attitudes (e.g., willingness to help, empathy, or sympathy; Clark et al., 2019). Moreover, when prior studies have examined behavioral expressions of compassion, they have typically done so through quantitative measures—for example, the amount of charitable donations (Lim & DeSteno, 2016) or the amount of time devoted to meeting with recipients of bad news (Molinsky et al., 2012). In contrast, our study adopts a relational perspective on compassion, emphasizing the quality of compassionate behavior as perceived by the sufferer (i.e., Compassionate Action Quality or CAQ), along with the responder’s belief in the effectiveness of their compassion (i.e., Self-Efficacy or SE). By incorporating both the sufferer’s experience and the responder’s internal assessment, our approach underscores the fundamentally relational character of compassion.
text: Second, our research focuses on responders' behaviors—”specifically, the quality of the responses they offer to sufferers. This is in contrast to the majority of the prior research, which explores the impact of previously endured distress on non-behavioral outcomes such as affect and attitudes (e.g., intention to help, felt empathy or sympathy; Clark et al., 2019). Moreover, the studies that do utilize behavioral indicators tend to operationalize compassionate behaviors solely in terms of quantity, such as the size of charitable giving (e.g., Lim & DeSteno, 2016) or the amount of time spent meeting with recipients of bad news (e.g., Molinsky et al., 2012). Instead, our research, utilizing the relational perspective of compassion, focuses on the quality of compassionate actions as experienced by the sufferer (i.e., CAQ), as well as the responder's belief in the efficacy of their compassionate action (i.e., SE). Accounting for both the sufferer's experience and the responder's perspective reflects the inherently relational nature of compassion.

use: With these foundational elements in place, we propose that it is the dynamic interplay between an individual’s motivationally linked trait and the degree to which a contextual variable signals either opportunities for creativity or expectations surrounding creativity that shapes Person–Situation interactions in creative behavior. Within this framework, the motivational lens model offers a nuanced account of various Person–Situation mechanisms: trait activation occurs when the situation provides opportunities aligned with traits tied to intrinsic motivation, or when it imposes expectations aligned with traits linked to extrinsic motivation; trait inhibition results when such opportunities are absent for traits associated with intrinsic motivation. The model also explains trait substitution, where individuals with lower levels of a given trait exhibit heightened sensitivity—those with intrinsically motivated traits respond more to expectations, while those with extrinsically motivated traits respond more to available opportunities. Trait channeling involves the influence of situational expectations, which may signal both the desirability and undesirability of creativity—this is particularly relevant for traits anchored in extrinsic motivation. Finally, the model accounts for curvilinear effects, suggesting that the impact of opportunities or expectations for creativity may follow a nonlinear trajectory in relation to relevant situational factors.
text: With these building blocks in place, we outline how it is the interplay of the motivation associated with a trait and the extent to which the situation variable of interest reflects opportunities for creativity or expectations for creativity that shape Person-Situation interactions on creativity. Through these considerations, the motivational lens model explains trait activation (opportunities in the situation for traits associated with intrinsic motivation; expectations in the situation for traits associated with extrinsic motivation), trait inhibition (the absence of opportunities for traits associated with intrinsic motivation), trait substitution (for traits associated with intrinsic motivation, greater sensitivity to expectations at lower levels of the trait; for traits associated with extrinsic motivation, greater sensitivity to opportunities at lower levels of the trait), trait channeling (expectations that range from conveying the desirability to conveying the undesirability of creativity for traits associated with extrinsic motivation), and curvilinear effects (opportunities or expectations for creativity that are curvilinearly related to the situation variable).

use: A significant portion of value creation within and across organizations stems from team production—a process characterized by the interdependence of members’ efforts and outcomes (Alchian & Demsetz, 1972; Holmstrom, 1982). Since the effectiveness of team performance hinges on individual choices that generate both positive and negative externalities, a core challenge in teamwork lies in resolving the cooperation dilemma (Dawes, 1980; Fehr & Gintis, 2007; Holmstrom, 1982). As in broader societal and communal contexts, team members benefit most when all parties cooperate. However, each individual may lack sufficient incentive to contribute, raising the classic problem of the prisoner's dilemma. Nevertheless, under specific conditions, members can develop interaction patterns that enable varying degrees of socially beneficial cooperation, thereby enhancing collective value (see Ostrom, 1990). Research has shown that such cooperation is more likely when interactions occur under the “shadow of the future” or through credible threats of retaliation (see Malcomson, 2013), whereas forming cooperative norms becomes increasingly difficult as team size grows (see Fudenberg & Tirole, 1991).
text: Much of the value creation within and between organizations results from team production, a process in which members' productive activities and outcomes are interdependent (Alchian & Demsetz, 1972; Holmstrom, 1982). Because team performance depends on choices that produce positive and negative externalities, a central issue in teamwork is the cooperation dilemma (Dawes, 1980; Fehr & Gintis, 2007; Holmstrom, 1982). As for the social life of communities and societies, team members are better off when everyone cooperates, but they may lack incentives to do so individually. Therefore, they face a prisoner's dilemma. Yet members may also interact under certain conditions to achieve some degree of socially beneficial cooperation (see Ostrom, 1990), and thus generate more value. For example, it is well understood that cooperation to create value can be maintained under the shadow of the future and the threat of retaliation (see Malcomson, 2013), and that cooperation is harder to establish in larger teams (see Fudenberg & Tirole, 1991).

use: My research centers on how transformational leadership and beneficiary contact influence followers' job performance. Job performance refers to the effectiveness of employees’ actions in furthering organizational objectives (Campbell, 1990). Transformational leadership is commonly understood as encompassing four key dimensions of leader behavior: inspirational motivation, idealized influence, intellectual stimulation, and individualized consideration (Bass, 1985; Burns, 1978). Inspirational motivation involves articulating a compelling and optimistic vision of the future. Idealized influence reflects the leader’s ability to inspire admiration and earn respect by exemplifying core values, expressing a shared sense of purpose, and emphasizing collective over individual interests. Intellectual stimulation encourages followers to challenge prevailing assumptions and to embrace innovative thinking. Individualized consideration focuses on tailoring support to each follower through personalized mentoring, coaching, and empathy. Through these behaviors, transformational leaders aim to inspire followers to transcend their personal interests and engage in purposeful efforts that support a collective and meaningful vision (e.g., Shamir, Zakay, Breinin, & Popper, 1998; Thompson & Bunderson, 2003).
text: My focus is on the effects of transformational leadership and beneficiary contact on followers' job performance. Performance is the effectiveness of followers' behaviors in advancing organizational goals (Campbell, 1990). Transformational leadership is typically conceptualized as a collection of four dimensions of leader behavior: inspirational motivation, idealized influence, intellectual stimulation, and individualized consideration (Bass, 1985; Burns, 1978). Inspirational motivation involves articulating a compelling vision of the future. Idealized influence involves engaging in charismatic actions that earn respect and cultivate pride, such as discussing important values and beliefs, communicating a sense of purpose, and encouraging a focus on collective interests. Intellectual stimulation involves challenging followers to question their assumptions and think differently. Individualized consideration involves personalizing interactions with followers by providing relevant mentoring, coaching, and understanding. By engaging in these transformational behaviors, leaders seek to motivate employees to look beyond their immediate self-interest to contribute to a broader vision (e.g., Shamir, Zakay, Breinin, & Popper, 1998; Thompson & Bunderson, 2003).

use: Another important boundary condition to consider is whether the motivational lens model can be generalized to personal attributes beyond personality. Amabile (1988) identified three key personal attributes that drive creativity: intrinsic motivation, domain-relevant expertise, and creativity-relevant skills. Our analysis is grounded in findings from person-in-situation creativity research, which primarily focuses on personality traits rather than expertise or ability. As such, extending the model to include expertise and creative ability, in the absence of direct empirical support, remains largely speculative. Nevertheless, it is plausible to suggest that the ability to act on one’s expertise or capabilities depends on situational opportunities that allow such action. Similarly, it stands to reason that individuals are more likely to leverage their abilities or expertise when the situation explicitly signals that such actions are expected. In this way, higher levels of ability and expertise may increase the likelihood that individuals express their intrinsic motivation—when prompted by opportunities—or their extrinsic motivation—when prompted by situational expectations.
text: Another issue to consider in respect to boundary conditions is whether the motivational lens model would generalize to personal attributes other than personality. Amabile (1988) identified intrinsic motivation, domain-relevant expertise, and creativity-relevant ability as the three personal attributes driving creativity. Our analysis is anchored on the evidence in person-in-situation creativity research, which concerns personality traits and not expertise or ability. Thus, absent an evidence base, considering if the model may be extended to include expertise and creative ability inevitably is more speculative. That said, it seems reasonable to propose that to act on one's ability or expertise, one needs opportunities in the situation to do so. It also seems reasonable to argue that one is more likely to act on one's ability or expertise the more the situation conveys expectations that one does so. Higher ability and expertise may thus make it more likely that individuals act on their intrinsic motivation—”stimulated by opportunities—”or one's extrinsic motivation—”stimulated by expectations.

use: Finally, the specific characteristics of a particular workplace or situation—frequently identified in the safety literature as critical sources of risk—are inherently obscured when viewed through high-level construals. For instance, many potential hazards are addressed through highly specific and concrete actions (e.g., verifying that equipment meets safety standards, ensuring crews wear protective gear, completing safety checklists; see D. A. Hofmann & Stetzer, 1996; D. A. Hofmann et al., 2003) or through tangible reminders (e.g., “Safety First” signage), all of which may be overlooked when the work environment is conceptualized abstractly. Increasing the level of construal, or “moving from a concrete representation of an object to a more abstract representation,” entails preserving what are considered central features while omitting details labeled as incidental through the process of abstraction (Trope & Liberman, 2010, p. 441). As a result, high-level construals of work contexts often exclude the “small” elements of a workplace—even though these very elements are frequently the most immediate and influential sources of harm (e.g., Reid et al., 2014).
text: Finally, specific details of a given workplace or situation—which are often identified by the safety literature as key sources of harm—are by definition lost via high-level construals. For example, many sources of harm are often mitigated by very concrete behaviors (e.g., checking equipment is up to code; ensuring crew are wearing safety gear; completing safety checklists; D. A. Hofmann & Stetzer, 1996; D. A. Hofmann et al., 2003) and reminders (e.g., “Safety First” reminder signs) that can be lost when conceptualizing the work context at a high level. Increasing work context construal level or “moving from a concrete representation of an object to a more abstract representation involves retaining central features and omitting features that by the very act of abstraction are deemed incidental” (Trope & Liberman, 2010, p. 441). Thus, high-level work context construals involve ignoring the “small” details or features of workplaces, yet those features are often proximal sources of harm (e.g., Reid et al., 2014).

use: As discussed earlier, competence refers to a leader’s capacity to effectively act on their intentions and motivations. Even when leaders and employees share aligned interests, a lack of competence can hinder employees’ career advancement, making it challenging for them to stand out or receive the promotions, raises, and recognition they believe they have earned (Erickson et al., 2007). In contrast, a competent leader is able to translate intentions into effective action, thereby enhancing team performance and increasing the team’s visibility within the organization. This supportive dynamic can foster employee growth and career mobility. Given that gratitude often involves a recognition of another’s strengths, we propose that gratitude toward a competent leader will be perceived as warranted, whereas similar expressions toward an incompetent leader may be viewed as unearned. Supporting this notion, prior work suggests that gratitude tends to be anchored in competence-relevant domains, such as skill development and professional growth (Cheng et al., 2015).
text: As previously discussed, competence refers to a leader’s ability to successfully act on their intentions and motivations. Even when leaders’ and employees’ interests align, incompetence can thwart employees’ career progress, making it difficult for them to stand out and receive the raises, promotions, and other benefits they might feel they otherwise would have earned (Erickson et al., 2007). In contrast, a competent leader successfully follows through on their intentions and motivations, and in this way may be able to support the team’s performance and raise the team’s visibility to upper management, enabling employees to thrive and careers to progress. To the extent that gratitude entails an acknowledgment of a leader’s strengths, we theorize that such gratitude will be seen as deserved when a leader is competent, and undeserved when a leader is less competent. Consistent with this idea, past research has shown that gratitude is often rooted in competence-related domains, such as skill development (Cheng et al., 2015).

use: Despite its importance, punishment is not always enforced consistently—leaders may overlook misconduct or deliberately reduce penalties. Indeed, research has shown that disciplinary decisions are often applied inconsistently and driven by personal, rather than principled, considerations (Cole, 2008). When leaders partially or fully withdraw the prescribed consequences for wrongdoing, they engage in an act of leniency (Zipay et al., 2021). Leniency involves three key actors: the leaders who grant it, the offenders who committed the misconduct, and the observing employees who attempt to interpret and make sense of the decision. Each of these actors experiences distinct outcomes. For leaders, leniency shapes their self-perception and informs future actions (Chiang et al., 2024; Zipay et al., 2021). For offenders, leniency may confer immediate benefits, such as lighter punishment, but can also carry long-term costs, including reduced learning and potential for repeated violations (see Mooijman & Graham, 2018; Podsakoff et al., 2010, for reviews). For observers, leniency introduces social uncertainty and prompts moral reflection, potentially altering perceptions of fairness and group norms (Dang et al., 2017a; van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2012, 2021).
text: Despite the significance of punishment, leaders sometimes turn a blind eye to or lessen penalties for misconduct. To be sure, research has shown that leaders often dispense discipline inconsistently and for idiosyncratic reasons (Cole, 2008). Leaders who reduce or remove in full the prescribed punishment for misconduct engage in leniency (Zipay et al., 2021). Leniency involves three primary actors: leaders (who grant leniency), offenders (who engaged in the misconduct), and observing employees (who try to make sense of the leniency). For each actor, there are consequences. For leaders, leniency influences their sense of self and subsequent behavior (Chiang et al., 2024; Zipay et al., 2021). For offenders, leniency provides short-term benefits (i.e., lesser penalty) and long-term consequences (e.g., lack of learning, repeated misconduct; see Mooijman & Graham, 2018; Podsakoff et al., 2010, for reviews). And for observers, leniency creates instability in social dynamics and raises moral implications (Dang et al., 2017a; van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2012, 2021).

use: Why do some creators struggle to replicate their initial creative success, while others continue to generate new and inventive work? Although research has explored creativity—commonly defined as the production of something novel and potentially useful (Amabile, 1996; Oldham & Cummings, 1996)—across time, it primarily shows that creative output often becomes increasingly incremental over the course of a career (e.g., Audia & Goncalo, 2007; Conti et al., 2014; Mannucci & Yong, 2018; Miron-Spektor et al., 2022). However, what remains less understood is what drives some producers to continue engaging in creative work, while others disengage entirely. At the heart of our inquiry lies a central paradox: The very factors typically associated with the success of a creative production—such as the novelty of the idea or the acclaim it receives through awards and recognition—may, under certain conditions, actually diminish a creator’s motivation to pursue further work in the same creative domain.
text: What causes some producers to struggle to repeat their initial creative production while others go on to continually produce creative works? Some research has examined creativity—”the production of something novel and potentially useful (Amabile, 1996; Oldham & Cummings, 1996)—”over time. This work has shown that producers often create increasingly incremental work as time goes on (e.g., Audia & Goncalo, 2007; Conti et al., 2014; Mannucci & Yong, 2018; Miron-Spektor et al., 2022). Yet, what determines whether or not producers continue the process of creative production at all, rather than abandoning it, is not well understood. The basic premise underlying our arguments is the notion that some of the factors that have been known to enhance, at least to some degree, the success of a creative production—”the novelty of the underlying idea and the acclaim that is bestowed upon it via an award or recognition—”can, paradoxically, serve to constrain a producer's appetite to produce follow-up work in that same domain.

use: To our knowledge, this article presents the first empirical investigation into the practical considerations of using conscientiousness self-report measures with individuals diagnosed with ADHD. While our findings provide valuable insights, several limitations point to opportunities for further research. First, our conclusions are specific to the particular measure we employed. We selected the Johnson (2014) scale due to its comprehensive content coverage, strong psychometric qualities, demonstrated correlations with other conscientiousness assessments, and public accessibility. However, future studies could explore whether these findings extend to other personality instruments, such as the Big Five Inventory-2 (Soto & John, 2017) or compound and circumplex personality models (Woods & Anderson, 2016). Relatedly, it is important to note that no single assessment tool is typically used in isolation for personnel decisions. Nonetheless, the presence of adverse impact in any single component of a multi-step selection process remains a legal and ethical concern, as emphasized in Connecticut v. Teal (1982). Therefore, additional research is warranted to investigate whether alternative methods for assessing conscientiousness—such as situational judgment tests, structured interviews, work samples, or forced-choice self-report formats—might yield different outcomes in the context of ADHD-related differences in conscientiousness.
text: Our article serves as the first empirical investigation, to our knowledge, of potential practical considerations when using conscientiousness self-report measures with those with ADHD. This research carries some limitations that highlight opportunities for future research. First, our results are limited to the measures we used. We chose our measure (Johnson, 2014) because of its comprehensiveness, psychometric properties, correlations with other conscientiousness measures, and public accessibility, but future research might examine the generalizability of other personality measures (i.e., the Big Five Inventory-2, Soto & John, 2017; or compound, circumplex models of personality, Woods & Anderson, 2016). Relatedly, no single assessment is used to make personnel decisions. However, even if just one part of a multipart selection system shows adverse impact, that is viewed as problematic by the courts (Connecticut v. Teal, 1982). Nonetheless, further research is needed to understand whether alternative conscientiousness assessment methods (e.g., situational judgment tests, structured interviews, work samples, and/or forced-choice self-reports) might impact ADHD-based differences in conscientiousness.

use: Given that dispositional regulatory focus reflects individuals’ preferred goals and coping strategies, we draw on regulatory focus theory (Higgins, 1997, 1998) to explore how these preferences may account for variations in employee performance under conditions of high versus low stress. Dispositional regulatory focus—defined as a tendency toward either promotion-focused or prevention-focused self-regulation—has been shown to significantly influence performance outcomes (e.g., Wallace & Chen, 2006; Wallace, Chen, & Kanfer, 2005; Wallace, Little, & Shull, 2008). We propose that such performance differences become more pronounced when employees face elevated stress levels, either from demanding and motivating challenges (i.e., challenge stress) or from obstructive and discouraging obstacles (i.e., hindrance stress). Using a sample of 160 medical device salespersons, we investigate how challenge stress and hindrance stress moderate the relationship between employees' dispositional regulatory orientations and their job performance, as measured by both manager-rated assessments and the percentage of sales quota achieved.
text: Because dispositional regulatory focus involves preferences in terms of goals and coping strategies, we turn to regulatory focus theory (Higgins, 1997, 1998) to understand how employees' preferred goals and coping strategies may explain performance differences when employees experience high levels of stress (as compared to low levels of stress). Dispositional regulatory focus—”employees' tendencies toward promotion-focused and prevention-focused self-regulatory orientations—”has been shown to have important performance implications (e.g., Wallace & Chen, 2006; Wallace, Chen, & Kanfer, 2005; Wallace, Little, & Shull, 2008). We argue that when employees experience high degrees of stress due to challenges at work (i.e., challenge stress) or stress due to looming obstacles (i.e., hindrance stress), performance differences associated with regulatory focus will be more evident. Specifically, with a sample of 160 medical device salespersons, we examine whether challenge stress and hindrance stress moderate the relationship between employees' dispositional self-regulatory tendencies and their subsequent job performance (in terms of manager-rated performance and percentage of their sales quota met).

use: Publishing news is an inherently demanding endeavor. As McQuail (1985) observed, journalists operate under constant pressure to produce timely articles about newsworthy events, often within extremely tight deadlines. To meet these expectations, they adopt strategies that streamline and accelerate the processes of identifying newsworthiness and crafting stories (Pollock et al., 2008). Central to these strategies are media routines—“established practices shared across members of the journalistic profession that shape how an individual journalist creates coverage” (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020: 57). A key set of these routines pertains to how journalists gather information and produce content. To facilitate efficiency and depth, news organizations frequently assign journalists to “beats,” specific realms such as government agencies, organizations, or industries, which they cover consistently over time (Davies, 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013). This repetition enables journalists to develop subject-matter expertise and cultivate a reliable network of sources (Westphal & Deephouse, 2011). Additionally, beat reporting allows them to build on previous reporting, create cohesive narrative arcs, and minimize the need for entirely new material in every article (Hirsch, 1977; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013).
text: Publishing news is a demanding job. McQuail (1985) noted that journalists face pressures to consistently produce articles about newsworthy events on very tight deadlines; thus, they engage in behaviors that simplify and speed up the process of establishing newsworthiness and producing news articles (Pollock et al., 2008). Media routines are “established practices shared across members of the journalistic profession that shape how an individual journalist creates coverage” (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020: 57). One set of routines has to do with sourcing and producing news. To expedite this process, journalists are often assigned to “beats,” where they routinely cover the same government agencies, organizations, or industries (Davies, 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013). This allows them to gain industry expertise and cultivate sources whom they use routinely (Westphal & Deephouse, 2011). It also allows them to build on their prior stories, develop narrative arcs, and reduce the amount of new content they need for each article (Hirsch, 1977; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013).

use: Our conceptualization builds most directly on the definition proposed by Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017). We define legacy as “an enduring contribution to a work unit that is ascribed to a legator, that has implications for meaning and identity, and that may influence unit functioning after the legator’s membership in the unit has ended.” The contributions attributed to the legator may stem from intentional efforts, driven by an explicit legacy motive (Bang et al., 2017; Fox et al., 2010; Fox & Wade-Benzoni, 2017), or from a conscious concern with leaving a legacy (Kang, 2016; Matta & Beamish, 2008; Wade-Benzoni et al., 2010). Alternatively, these contributions may be more inadvertent, becoming meaningful only in hindsight through retrospective sensemaking about the legator’s time in the unit. Importantly, aligning with Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017), our definition centers on positive contributions, as both academic literature and common usage of the term "legacy" typically associate it with favorable impacts—a point we revisit in our Discussion.
text: Our theorizing draws most closely on Fox and Wade-Benzoni's (2017) definition. Specifically, we define legacy as "an enduring contribution to a work unit that is ascribed to a legator, that has implications for meaning and identity, and that may influence unit functioning after the legator's membership in the unit has ended." The contributions ascribed to the legator could have been created intentionally, as a result of an explicit legacy motive (Bang et al., 2017; Fox et al., 2010; Fox & Wade-Benzoni, 2017) or a conscious legacy concern (Kang, 2016; Matta & Beamish, 2008; Wade-Benzoni et al., 2010). Alternatively, the contributions ascribed to the legator could have been more unintentional, only becoming salient via retrospective sensemaking about time spent in the unit. Note that, following Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017), our definition focuses specifically on positive contributions, as both scholarly and lay uses of the legacy term tend to imbue it with a positive valence (though we revisit this issue in our Discussion).

use: Creative Self-Efficacy (CSE) refers to employees’ beliefs about their ability to generate creative ideas within a given context (Tierney & Farmer, 2002). Rooted in Social Cognitive Theory, self-efficacy reflects individuals’ judgments about their competence and perceived control in relation to specific tasks or domains. These beliefs are shaped by contextual factors, and once formed at high levels, they act as critical psychological mechanisms that enable individuals to exercise agency in their future behavior (Bandura, 2012). Contextual signals—through their symbolic meanings and informational value—can shape CSE (e.g., Schunk, 1984). Among these, the allocation of rewards stands out as one of the most significant organizational decisions, typically managed by supervisors and leaders (Milkovich & Newman, 2008). In situations where both choice and no-choice conditions exist, employees who are allowed to select their own reward for engaging in creative behavior are likely to interpret this opportunity as a signal of managerial trust in their creative abilities, reinforcing their belief that they are capable of being creative.
text: CSE refers to employees' beliefs about whether they can generate creative ideas in the context that they are in (Tierney & Farmer, 2002). According to Social Cognitive Theory, self-efficacy beliefs represent individuals' assessments of their capabilities and control over a specific type of task or domain. Such beliefs are influenced by the context, and once high levels of such beliefs are formed, they will serve as the psychological mechanism that facilitates individuals' exercising their agency in subsequent behaviors (Bandura, 2012). Contextual cues can influence CSE through their symbolic meanings and informational effects (e.g., Schunk, 1984). Reward allocation is one of the most important decisions for organizations, and hence, it has typically been controlled by managers (Milkovich & Newman, 2008). When choice and no-choice conditions are present, employees who have the opportunity to choose a reward for generating creative ideas are likely to assess this contextual cue as they are entrusted with this important decision because they have the capabilities to be creative.

use: Actors can benefit from receiving coworker work-family support that is both timely and tailored to their specific needs. They may then reinvest these workplace resources to enhance their family lives by offering similar support to their partners (Hirschi et al., 2019). Drawing on the Work–Family Facilitation (W-FF) model (Wayne et al., 2007), we propose that coworker work-family support functions as an environmental resource within the workplace, contributing to the actor’s developmental gains—such as improved skills and capacities to effectively manage and integrate work and home responsibilities (Carlson et al., 2006). These gains, in turn, facilitate the provision of work-family support within their personal lives. Supporting this work-to-family facilitation pathway, previous research has found that general coworker social support at work is positively associated with higher levels of work-family enrichment (see Lapierre et al., 2018, for a meta-analytic review). In the same vein, both qualitative (McMullan et al., 2018) and quantitative (Lu et al., 2009) studies underscore the important role coworkers play in promoting employees' work-life balance, demonstrating a positive link between domain-specific coworker work-family support and work-to-family facilitation. Based on this reasoning, we propose the following:
text: Actors can take advantage of receiving more targeted and timely coworker work-family support and invest the resources obtained at work to improve their home lives by providing work-family support to their partners (Hirschi et al., 2019). Drawing from the W-FF model (Wayne et al., 2007), we propose that coworker work-family support represents an environmental resource at work that leads to increases in developmental gains of actors (e.g., increased skills and abilities to effectively balance work and home commitments; Carlson et al., 2006), thereby facilitating work-family support provision at home. In support of our work-to-family facilitation proposition, previous research linked generalized coworker social support at work to greater work-family enrichment (see Lapierre et al., 2018 for meta-analytic evidence). Similarly, in recognition of the integral role that coworkers play in enhancing employees' work-life balance, qualitative (McMullan et al., 2018), and quantitative (Lu et al., 2009) research highlights the positive association of domain-specific coworker work-family support with work-to-family facilitation. Based on this rationale, we predict:

use: Although prior research has extensively explored how stakeholders evaluate firms, there remains significant potential to advance this body of work by investigating how the informational environment either enables or constrains firms' efforts to shape stakeholder perceptions. To deepen our understanding of these dynamics, we conceptualize and theorize three key dimensions of the informational environment: information availability—the extent of stakeholders' knowledge about a firm; information content—the specific nature of what stakeholders know about the firm; and information reliability—the degree to which stakeholders trust the sources that inform them. Building on scholarship in firm–stakeholder dialogue—understood as a structured, interactive, and proactive two-way exchange of information between a firm and a stakeholder (Burchell & Cook, 2013; O’Riordan & Fairbrass, 2008; Rasche & Esser, 2006)—we examine how a firm’s approach, whether proactive or reactive, shapes and is shaped by its interactions with specific stakeholders. We propose that the effectiveness of this dialogue is highly contingent upon the broader informational environment in which the firm–stakeholder relationship—and the dialogue itself—is situated. Accordingly, we develop hypotheses that reflect this contextual sensitivity, highlighting the interplay between firm communication strategies and the informational conditions under which they occur.
text: Despite extensive prior research on stakeholders’ evaluations of firms, there is ample opportunity to advance this research by examining how the informational environment enhances or constrains firms’ ability to shape their stakeholders’ evaluations. To deepen insights on these questions, we define and theorize three dimensions of the informational environment: information availability, or how much stakeholders know about a firm; information content, or what stakeholders know about a firm; and information reliability, or how much stakeholders trust the media that informs them. We also build on work on firm–stakeholder dialogue—a structured, interactive, and proactive two-way information-exchange process between a firm and a stakeholder (Burchell & Cook, 2013; O’Riordan & Fairbrass, 2008; Rasche & Esser, 2006)—to understand whether a firm assumes a proactive or a reactive stance toward a specific stakeholder and to develop hypotheses proposing that the impact of firm–stakeholder dialogue is highly contingent on the informational environment in which the relationship between the firm and the stakeholder—and the firm–stakeholder dialogue itself—is embedded.

use: While competence can serve as a key indicator of whether someone is deserving of gratitude, it is unlikely to be the sole criterion. A competent individual may still fail to produce a positive outcome—particularly if their intentions are not benevolent. Conversely, someone with lower levels of competence may nonetheless have a meaningful and favorable impact. Therefore, we consider warmth as an additional dimension of deservingness. As Cuddy et al. (2011, p. 76) explain, “Warmth indicates whether others are likely to have positive or negative intentions toward us [while] competence answers the question of whether the other is capable of carrying out those intentions.” Warmth reflects attributes such as trustworthiness, friendliness, and social openness. It conveys the perception that an individual has good intentions, and it has been linked to numerous positive outcomes for employees. In contrast, individuals perceived as lacking warmth are generally seen as less worthy of assistance or prosocial behavior, as they tend to be viewed as self-interested and less trustworthy (Fiske, 2018).
text: Although competence can be conceptualized as an important marker of deservingness of gratitude, it is unlikely to be the only marker. Competent people can still fail to have a positive impact (e.g., when they lack good intentions), and individuals who are less competent can still have a positive impact. We thus examine warmth as an additional marker of deservingness. As noted by Cuddy et al. (2011, p. 76) “Warmth indicates whether others are likely to have positive or negative intentions toward us [while] competence answers the question of whether the other is capable of carrying out those intentions.” Warmth signals an individual’s trustworthiness, friendliness, and sociability. It is generally associated with the belief that an individual means well, and as such is positively associated with a wide range of positive outcomes for employees. In contrast, less warm individuals are generally seen as less deserving of help and other prosocial actions, as these individuals are seen as more self-focused and less trustworthy (Fiske, 2018).

use: While differing in approach, prior studies have converged on the idea that a category’s viability primarily hinges on its internal coherence—namely, the extent to which actors and audiences share a collective understanding and consistent use of that category (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Wry, Lounsbury, & Glynn, 2011). However, by largely examining categories in isolation, this body of work has implicitly assumed that a category’s viability can be assessed independently of others. In reality, categories are embedded within broader classification systems and do not evolve in a vacuum (Lo et al., 2020). Although scholars acknowledge this embeddedness (Boghossian & David, 2021; Cudennec & Durand, 2023; Gehman & Grimes, 2017), current theory lacks clarity on how entrepreneurial activity within adjacent or related categories can influence the viability of a focal category—and vice versa. This gap is significant, as the way actors and audiences interpret and employ one category can reshape how they perceive and utilize others (Hsu & Grodal, 2021). If a category’s viability depends on what occurs within it, then developments in related categories must also be seen as consequential.
text: Despite their different perspectives, these studies have commonly viewed the viability of a category as an outcome of its internal coherence—that is, how actors and audiences collectively understand and use the category (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Wry, Lounsbury, & Glynn, 2011). However, by focusing on one category at a time, extant research has implicitly assumed that the viability of categories can be understood independently from one another. Yet, categories do not evolve in isolation (Lo et al., 2020). Although scholars agree that categories are nested in a broader classification system (Boghossian & David, 2021; Cudennec & Durand, 2023; Gehman & Grimes, 2017), how entrepreneurial action in related categories affects the viability of a focal category (and vice versa) remains undertheorized. This is an important lacuna because how actors and audiences use one category can alter how they use another (Hsu & Grodal, 2021). If the viability of a category is shaped by what happens within it, what happens across other related categories matters too.

use: In practice, research within the organizational justice literature has employed two primary approaches to measure responses to such questions. The first approach involves evaluating perceptions of the specific rules that are believed to generate fairness (Blader & Tyler, 2003; Colquitt, 2001; Moorman, 1991; Rupp & Cropanzano, 2002)—rules that typically fall into four established dimensions. Distributive justice concerns perceptions about whether decisions adhere to appropriate allocation norms, most commonly based on equity (Adams, 1965; Homans, 1961). Procedural justice pertains to perceptions of whether the decision-making procedures are consistent, accurate, unbiased, correctable, and open to input or voice (Leventhal, 1980; Thibaut & Walker, 1975). Interpersonal justice relates to whether individuals feel that the communications surrounding decision-making are respectful and dignified (Bies & Moag, 1986; Greenberg, 1993), while informational justice focuses on whether those communications are perceived as truthful, clear, and adequately explanatory (Bies & Moag, 1986; Greenberg, 1993). The second approach assesses an overall or holistic perception of fairness, commonly referred to as global justice perceptions. This method does not explicitly reference any specific fairness rules or dimensions but instead captures a general sense of how fair individuals feel they have been treated (Ambrose & Schminke, 2009; Colquitt et al., 2015; Kim & Leung, 2007).
text: In practice, research in the organizational justice literature has used two distinct approaches to measure the answers to such questions. One approach is to assess perceptions of the rules that can create fairness (Blader & Tyler, 2003; Colquitt, 2001; Moorman, 1991; Rupp & Cropanzano, 2002)—”rules that tend to fall into four dimensions. Distributive justice reflects perceptions of whether a decision follows appropriate allocation norms—”most often equity (Adams, 1965; Homans, 1961). Procedural justice reflects perceptions of whether a process is consistent, accurate, unbiased, correctable, and receptive to voice (Leventhal, 1980; Thibaut & Walker, 1975). Interpersonal justice reflects perceptions of whether communications about a decision or process are respectful and proper (Bies & Moag, 1986; Greenberg, 1993), with informational justice reflecting perceptions of whether they are truthful and explanatory (Bies & Moag, 1986; Greenberg, 1993). The other approach is to assess a global sense of fairness that does not explicitly evoke any particular rules (Ambrose & Schminke, 2009; Colquitt et al., 2015; Kim & Leung, 2007).

use: Elaboration reflects a form of cognitive flexibility that enables individuals to engage in deeper and more expansive exploration of ideas. It is widely regarded as a core component of creativity due to its role in reshaping—defined as the ability to organize information into multiple conceptual categories. While reshaping has been consistently linked to creative thinking and outcomes, elaboration has long been recognized as a foundational element of creativity (Guilford, 1967), alongside originality and fluency. Contemporary definitions continue to build on this foundation, describing elaboration as the capacity to navigate diverse and expansive mental spaces (Althuizen & Chen, 2022). A growing body of research supports the idea that elaboration significantly contributes to creativity (Bledow et al., 2013), such as in contexts aiming to enhance university teaching through cognitive and emotional exploration. Importantly, studies have shown that motivational and affective factors influence the extent of elaboration, which in turn affects creative performance (De Dreu et al., 2008; Friedman, Förster, & Denzler, 2007; Ong & Leung, 2013). The following sections examine these antecedents in greater detail and explore their impact on the dynamics of creative collaboration.
text: Elaboration reflects cognitive flexibility, allowing for deeper and broader exploration of ideas, and is considered essential for creativity due to its influence on reshaping—”the aptitude to categorize information into multiple conceptual groups. Although reshaping is consistently related to creative thinking and outcomes, elaboration is a foundational dimension of creativity (Guilford, 1967), with theorists recognizing that originality, fluency, and elaboration constitute the basic elements of creativity. More recent definitions follow this conceptualization of elaboration as involving navigation of various and expansive mental spaces (Althuizen & Chen, 2022). Across multiple studies, the results support that elaboration is important to creativity (Bledow et al., 2013), such as when aiming to improving university teaching through emotional and cognitive explorations. Importantly, research have found that motivational and affective antecedents shape the degree of elaboration which impact creativity (De Dreu et al., 2008; Friedman, Förster, & Denzler, 2007; Ong & Leung, 2013). The sections that follow will detail these antecedents and provide insight on how they shape creative collaboration dynamics.

use: Where do quality, compassionate responses originate? On the one hand, common intuitions suggest that individuals who have previously experienced distress may be more inclined to respond with greater compassion toward others undergoing similar hardship. Supporting this notion, prior studies have shown that individuals with firsthand experience of distress often exhibit stronger empathy toward those currently suffering comparable events (Barnett et al., 1986; Batson et al., 1996; Eklund et al., 2009), a deeper understanding of the impact of social pain (Nordgren et al., 2011), an increased sense of moral responsibility to support others facing similar challenges (Christy & Voigt, 1994; Lim & DeSteno, 2016; Staub & Vollhardt, 2008; Vollhardt & Staub, 2011), and a greater likelihood of intervening to assist those in similar distress (Christy & Voigt, 1994). Collectively, this body of research suggests that prior personal distress may enhance the responder’s perspective, particularly by strengthening their motivation to offer compassionate assistance.
text: Where might quality compassionate responses come from? On the one hand, lay beliefs may lead us to believe that those who have previously endured distress will be more likely to demonstrate quality compassion toward those who are experiencing similar distress. For instance, prior research has found that people who previously endured distress show increased feelings of empathy for sufferers who are experiencing a similar event (Barnett et al., 1986; Batson et al., 1996; Eklund et al., 2009), heightened appreciation of the effects of social pain (Nordgren et al., 2011), increased sense of responsibility for others dealing with such distress (Christy & Voigt, 1994; Lim & DeSteno, 2016; Staub & Vollhardt, 2008; Vollhardt & Staub, 2011), and greater likelihood of intervening on behalf of sufferers who are dealing with the same kind of distress (Christy & Voigt, 1994). This body of research suggests that previously endured distress is perceived to be beneficial from the responder's perspective, particularly as it pertains to their motivation to help.

use: Entrepreneurship offers a rich and distinctive context for exploring self-promotion within feedback-seeking environments. Entrepreneurs must actively advocate for both themselves and their ventures while navigating significant uncertainty (McMullen & Shepherd, 2006). Unlike many other professional roles, they operate without clear-cut guidelines, supervisory support to help prioritize tasks, or reliable peer networks for feedback and encouragement (Drencheva et al., 2024). As a result, entrepreneurs often rely on feedback from those around them—frequently from unfamiliar individuals (van Werven et al., 2023)—which can elevate the perceived image risks and thus intensify the need for self-promotion (Pai et al., 2023). Their venture is deeply entwined with their personal identity (Gielnik et al., 2017; Grimes, 2018), making the act of self-promotion inextricably linked to promoting the venture itself. Drawing from Stevens and Kristof (1995), I define entrepreneurial self-promotion as positive assertions made by entrepreneurs about themselves, their ventures, their future aspirations, or past achievements—comparable to how job applicants showcase their competencies (Godfrey et al., 1986; Roulin et al., 2015). However, the entrepreneurial context places a greater emphasis on individual agency, suggesting that the strategic use of self-promotion may offer unique advantages that remain underexplored.
text: Entrepreneurship provides a compelling context for studying self-promotion in feedback-seeking environments. Entrepreneurs must advocate for themselves and their ventures while navigating uncertainty (McMullen & Shepherd, 2006). Unlike other professions, they lack clear guidelines, supervisors to help prioritize tasks, and reliable peers for support (Drencheva et al., 2024). This forces entrepreneurs to seek feedback from those around them, often interacting with unfamiliar individuals (van Werven et al., 2023), which can heighten perceived image costs and thus increase the demand for self-promotion (Pai et al., 2023). Their venture is a key part of their identity (Gielnik et al., 2017; Grimes, 2018), so self-promotion naturally connects to their venture. Following Stevens and Kristof (1995), I define entrepreneurial self-promotion as positive statements entrepreneurs make about themselves, their ventures, future plans, or past accomplishments, similar to job applicants highlighting their skills (Godfrey et al., 1986; Roulin et al., 2015). The entrepreneurial context, however, is more susceptible to individual agency, suggesting potential benefits from self-promotion that are not yet fully understood.

use: Work in today’s economy is increasingly demanding, placing significant cognitive and emotional burdens on employees. As a result, many individuals report experiencing heightened levels of stress and mental strain (American Psychological Association, 2021; Health and Safety Executive, 2022). Extensive job demands have been consistently linked to diminished well-being (Gonzalez-Mulé et al., 2021; Guthier et al., 2020), and in extreme cases, they may even contribute to premature mortality (Gonzalez-Mulé & Cockburn, 2017). To mitigate these adverse effects, it is essential that employees recover adequately during their leisure time (Bennett et al., 2018; Zijlstra et al., 2014). One key factor in effective recovery is psychological detachment from work during non-work hours (Sonnentag & Fritz, 2015). Psychological detachment refers to the “sense of being away from the work situation” (Etzion et al., 1998, p. 579), and involves mentally disconnecting from work tasks and responsibilities. When employees are unable to detach, research shows they face increased emotional strain and experience declines in overall well-being (Bennett et al., 2018; Steed et al., 2021).
text: Work in today's economy is highly demanding. Due to high cognitive and emotional demands, many employees experience tension and stress (American Psychological Association, 2021; Health and Safety Executive, 2022). High job demands are related to poor well-being (Gonzalez-Mulé et al., 2021; Guthier et al., 2020) and even may result in premature death (Gonzalez-Mulé & Cockburn, 2017). To counteract the negative consequences of highly demanding work, employees need to recover during leisure time (Bennett et al., 2018; Zijlstra et al., 2014). Research has identified leisure-time psychological detachment from work as a crucial aspect of a successful recovery process (Sonnentag & Fritz, 2015). Psychological detachment from work refers to an employee's "sense of being away from the work situation" (Etzion et al., 1998, p. 579). It implies mentally distancing oneself from one's work and temporarily forgetting about work during leisure time. Meta-analyses showed that lack of psychological detachment from work is associated with unfavorable affective outcomes and impaired well-being (Bennett et al., 2018; Steed et al., 2021).

use: We build on conceptual foundations to explore how different deviance classes may be distinguished based on antecedents of perceived organizational unfairness. Specifically, we propose that employees assigned to deviance classes characterized by a higher propensity for interpersonal harm—such as “making fun of someone” or “acting rudely” (e.g., Slacking Jerks, Thieving Antagonists)—are more likely to have experienced abusive supervision or workplace ostracism, as such experiences can trigger retaliatory or mirroring behaviors (Mitchell & Ambrose, 2007; Priesemuth et al., 2022). In a similar vein, membership in classes marked by disengagement behaviors—such as “coming in late,” “daydreaming,” or “putting little effort into work” (e.g., Stagnant Workers, Slacking Jerks)—may be more prevalent among individuals reporting high levels of injustice, abusive supervision, or ostracism. This aligns with existing research linking these forms of perceived unfairness to various manifestations of employee withdrawal (Chi & Liang, 2013; Cole et al., 2010; Ferris et al., 2008; Huang et al., 2020). Research Question 3: Is deviance class membership systematically shaped by perceptions of organizational unfairness, including injustice, abusive supervision, and ostracism?
text: We draw on conceptual assumptions to speculate on how the remaining deviance classes might be differentiated based on these organizational unfairness antecedents. We anticipate that belonging to classes with a high likelihood of engaging in harmful behaviors toward other individuals, such as "making fun of someone" or "acting rudely" (e.g., Slacking Jerks, Thieving Antagonists), is more likely among employees who have experienced abusive supervision or ostracism, as employees might respond with similar behaviors (Mitchell & Ambrose, 2007; Priesemuth et al., 2022). Similarly, belonging to classes reflective of behaviors such as "coming in late," "daydreaming," and "putting little effort into work" (e.g., Stagnant Workers, Slacking Jerks) might be more likely among employees who report high injustice, abusive supervision, and ostracism, given the positive links employee withdrawal has with these unfair experiences (Chi & Liang, 2013; Cole et al., 2010; Ferris et al., 2008; Huang et al., 2020). Research Question 3: Is deviance class membership differentiated by organizational unfairness variables, such as injustice, abusive supervision, or ostracism?

use: While more conventional ideas can certainly hold value, it is concerning that selection bodies often overlook ideas that are either objectively more novel or viewed as more valuable by relevant stakeholders (Harvey & Mueller, 2021). Groups tend to exhibit a similar aversion to bold, unconventional ideas as they do to those that merely reinforce the status quo—despite the latter being demonstrably less useful (Boudreau et al., 2016). Moreover, the persistent drift toward ideas of moderate creativity reflects a significant misallocation of human time and energy. The resources spent deliberating and debating ideas—along with the interpersonal conflicts such discussions may generate—might instead be conserved through alternative selection mechanisms. For example, randomized selection processes, crowd-based evaluation (shown to be relatively effective in practice; Mollick & Nanda, 2016), or AI-driven novelty detection systems (Just et al., 2023) could offer more efficient and potentially more effective means of surfacing breakthrough ideas.
text: Although ideas that are more mundane may have value, the tendency for selection bodies to disregard ideas that are either objectively more novel or are considered of greater value by relevant constituents is worrisome (Harvey & Mueller, 2021). Groups seem to have the same distaste for the more daring ideas that they have for ideas that are completely redundant to the status quo, despite such ideas undoubtedly being less valuable (Boudreau et al., 2016). Furthermore, the fact that groups experience a predictable gravitational pull towards ideas of average creativity also constitutes a considerable waste of human time and effort. The time and effort it takes to deliberate the various ideas and the potential conflict that may erupt as a result of such deliberations could be saved, for instance, by using a lottery, by outsourcing idea selection to the crowd - an approach that has been found to be reasonably effective (Mollick & Nanda, 2016) - or by using AI-based novelty detection (Just et al., 2023).

use: Task transitions refer to shifts in attention from one activity to another—for example, when a professor moves from working on a journal manuscript to preparing an upcoming lecture. These transitions can vary in form and experience: they may be triggered by external interruptions, initiated deliberately to manage progress across various projects, or even prompted by a desire to avoid monotony. In line with much of the existing literature, our primary focus is on task switches caused by interruptions—that is, when an individual is diverted to a new task before completing the original one. We do not examine transitions that occur after the completion of a task, even though these can also impose cognitive demands (e.g., Leroy, 2009). Such transitions are typically less disruptive, as they are often accompanied by the psychological satisfaction of task completion (e.g., Lewin, 1926, 1935). For the purposes of this review, we restrict our analysis to task transitions at the individual level that take place within the bounds of a single workday.
text: Task transitions refer to shifts in focus from one activity to another, such as professors switching their focus from writing a journal manuscript to preparing for an upcoming lecture. These task transitions can have different qualities and characteristics, and may include someone else interrupting one's workflow, one switching tasks to manage progress across multiple projects, or one transitioning to avoid boredom. Consistent with much of the existing research, we primarily focus on task switching due to interruptions; that is, a task occurring when the initial task is incomplete. We do not cover task transitions that occur when one completes work on one task and as a result turns attention to another. Although these transitions can also be cognitively difficult (e.g., Leroy, 2009), they tend to be easier to manage because of the satisfaction of one's need for completion (e.g., Lewin, 1926, 1935). For the purpose of this review, we limit our focus to transitions that occur at the individual level and within the course of a workday.

use: Heider (1958) advanced similar claims, proposing that humanitarian impulses and compassion are often triggered by recognizing that another person has a personal need—typically when they are perceived as being in distress or suffering (Dutton et al., 2014). Such needs can arise from deeply personal circumstances, including crises, pain, financial hardship, personal disrepair, or tragedy (Kahn, 2019). The perception of someone in distress evokes humanitarian concern, heightening an observer’s sense of moral obligation and eliciting a stronger inclination toward decency and kindness (Haidt, 2003). In this sense, the recognition of personal need becomes a powerful motivator, prompting observers to shield the suffering individual from cruelty and harm (Glover, 1999; Heider, 1958). These responses are likely to influence perceptions of fairness and how individuals react to a leader’s behavior. Empirical studies affirm that employees take others’ needs into account when assessing incidents of unfair leadership (Cobb, Folger, & Wooten, 1995; Cobb, Wooten, & Folger, 1995). Acknowledging need not only elicits supportive responses (Lamm & Schwinger, 1980; Mannix et al., 1995), but also guides observers’ moral evaluations in ways that drive efforts to alleviate others’ suffering (Brown et al., 2023).
text: Indeed, Heider (1958) made similar arguments. He argued that humanitarianism and compassion can be evoked by knowing another person has a personal need—that they are perceived to be in distress or suffering (Dutton et al., 2014). A personal need can derive from several intimate circumstances, such as crisis, pain, financial troubles, disrepair, or tragedy in one’s personal life (Kahn, 2019). Perceiving another who has a personal need and is in distress raises humanitarianism, amplifying a desire for decency and kindness toward the individual (Haidt, 2003). Personal need, then, should motivate observers to protect the person in distress from cruelty (Glover, 1999; Heider, 1958), and such considerations would thereby shape fairness perceptions and reactions to the leader. Certainly, research has shown that employees weigh needs when leaders act unfairly (Cobb, Folger, & Wooten, 1995; Cobb, Wooten, & Folger, 1995). A consideration of needs inspires supportive reactions (Lamm & Schwinger, 1980; Mannix et al., 1995) and influences observers’ judgments that motivate efforts to reduce distress (Brown et al., 2023).

use: When does conflict enhance team performance? Over the past two decades, a substantial body of research has sought to address this question by distinguishing between relationship conflict and task conflict (Amason, 1996; Jehn, 1995, 1997; Jehn & Bendersky, 2003). The foundational argument suggests that relationship conflict generally undermines team performance, while task conflict can, under certain conditions, contribute positively. While empirical studies have largely affirmed the detrimental effects of relationship conflict, evidence supporting the beneficial impact of task conflict—and the specific contextual conditions under which it occurs—has proven more elusive (Korsgaard, Jeong, Mahony, & Pitariu, 2008). For instance, in a meta-analysis conducted by De Dreu and Weingart (2003), task conflict was found to be negatively related to team performance (r = –.23), yet the considerable variation across studies pointed to the existence of potential moderating factors. Despite such findings and multiple calls for further research into these moderators (e.g., De Dreu, 2008; Jehn & Bendersky, 2003), we still know surprisingly little about the specific conditions that enable task conflict to have a positive effect on team performance.
text: When does conflict improve team performance? Considerable research over the last two decades has sought to answer this question by emphasizing the distinction between relationship- and task-based conflict (Amason, 1996; Jehn, 1995, 1997; Jehn & Bendersky, 2003). The original logic argued that relationship conflict would damage team performance, whereas task conflict may improve it under certain conditions. Subsequent empirical tests generally support this conclusion for relationship conflict, although the positive link between task conflict and performance, and the contextual factors that bring it about, have been much harder to find (Korsgaard, Jeong, Mahony, & Pitariu, 2008). For example, in their meta-analysis, De Dreu and Weingart (2003) found a negative association between task conflict and team performance (âˆ’.23), but they also found substantial variance in the relationship, indicating possible moderators of the effect. Despite this evidence and other calls for future research on moderators (e.g., De Dreu, 2008; Jehn & Bendersky, 2003), surprisingly little is known about the circumstances under which task conflict associates positively with team performance.

use: The nature of work has evolved significantly over time. Workflows have grown increasingly unpredictable, and advances in technology have introduced a wider array of ways to structure and coordinate work. Consequently, task interdependence is often not fully known in advance. These changes carry two important implications. First, it is now virtually impossible to design an entire organization in advance of actual work being carried out; in practice, new and unforeseen elements continually emerge during the course of work. Second, job design can no longer be approached as a deterministic process; contemporary job descriptions tend to be broad, outlining general responsibilities rather than specific tasks. In the absence of clearly predefined tasks to anchor organizational and job design, task interdependence loses its centrality as both a guiding principle and as a tight link to goal and knowledge interdependence. In this context, workers gain more autonomy to pursue overarching goals and apply their expertise in ways that reflect their individual preferences rather than rigid specifications. As a result, goal and knowledge interdependence emerge as distinct and critical dimensions in shaping how both work and organizations are designed.
text: However, the nature of work has shifted over time. Workflows have become more unpredictable, and technology now enables a greater variety of ways to organize work. As a result, task interdependence is often not fully known ex ante. These developments have two notable implications. First, it is now virtually impossible to design an entire organization before agents actually conduct the work; indeed, novel elements arise constantly while work is being performed. Second, job design can no longer be treated as deterministic; modern-day job descriptions are broad and contain high-level responsibilities.
