In the absence of clearly defined tasks to guide organization and job design, task interdependence becomes both a less prominent signpost for organizing and less tightly coupled with goal and knowledge interdependence. Here, agents gain the freedom to follow broader goals and apply their knowledge in ways that reflect their own preferences rather than predefined requirements. As a result, goal and knowledge interdependence become salient in their own right as indispensable components of organization and job design.

use: The Comprehensive Inventory of Thriving (CIT; Hausler et al., 2017; Su et al., 2014) was employed to assess overall well-being and includes a total of 54 items. This instrument captures seven key dimensions: (1) subjective well-being, (2) relationships, (3) engagement, (4) meaning in life, (5) accomplishment, (6) autonomy, and (7) optimism. Responses are rated on a five-point Likert scale ranging from "I strongly disagree" (1) to "I strongly agree" (5) (Su et al., 2014). These seven dimensions can be grouped into two overarching categories of well-being: subjective well-being and psychological well-being. Example items include: “My life is going well” (subjective well-being), and “There are people I can depend on to help me” (relationships / psychological well-being). The German version of the CIT has demonstrated excellent internal consistency, with a Cronbach’s alpha of .96 in previous research (Hausler et al., 2017); in the current study, the internal consistency was also strong (α = .89). Given the high intercorrelation between subjective and psychological well-being (r = .91; Hausler et al., 2017, p. 224) and the paper’s primary focus on work-related characteristics, we followed the authors’ recommendation and utilized the aggregated total score of general well-being.
text: The Comprehensive Inventory of Thriving (CIT; Hausler et al. 2017; Su et al. 2014) was used to measure general well-being and comprises 54 items. The measure consists of seven different components: (1) subjective well-being, (2) relationships, (3) engagement, (4) meaning in life, (5) accomplishment, (6) autonomy, and (7) optimism, which are measured on a five-point scale from "I strongly disagree" (=1) to "I strongly agree" (=5) (Su et al. 2014). The seven components can be further categorized into the two main components of well-being: subjective and psychological well-being. Item examples are: "My life is going well" (subjective well-being); "There are people I can depend on to help me" (relationships / psychological well-being). The German version showed a very good Cronbach's alpha of .96 (Hausler et al. 2017); in this study, it was .89. As the latter highly intercorrelate (r = .91; Hausler et al. 2017, p. 224) and the focus of this paper lies on the work characteristics, we used the equally recommended total score of general well-being.

use: When employees cultivate a dual-growth mindset, they begin to perceive both themselves and their jobs—that is, the self and the situation—as adaptable and capable of growth. This expanded view of flexibility is expected to enhance employees’ ability to initiate agentic changes in their work environments (Bandura, 2006, 2018). However, not all agentic changes are equally effective in sustainably increasing happiness. According to Lyubomirsky et al. (2005), changes are more likely to foster lasting happiness when they generate experiences that are volitional, self-concordant (aligned with one’s intrinsic interests and motivations), effortful, and novel (meaningfully distinct from established routines). Fostering a dual-growth mindset is more likely to encourage agentic change that fulfills all four of these criteria. In contrast, promoting either a self- or job-growth mindset in isolation may lead primarily to changes that are volitional and self-concordant, but not necessarily effortful or novel. The latter two elements—effort and novelty—are particularly important because they help keep experiences engaging and dynamic, counteracting the psychological adaptation that contributes to the hedonic treadmill effect. Minor or superficial changes typically fall short in these respects and are unlikely to produce lasting increases in happiness. To make a meaningful and enduring impact, agentic changes must be relatively substantial in scope and character.
text: When employees develop a dual-growth mindset, they come to see both the self and job—that is, the person and situation—as relatively flexible. This should increase employees’ capacity to make agentic changes in their work (Bandura, 2006, 2018). However, not all agentic changes are likely to durably heighten happiness. According to Lyubomirsky et al. (2005), changes are more likely to increase happiness when they produce experiences that are volitional, self-concordant (matching one’s interests and motivations), effortful, and novel (substantially different from one’s prior routines). Fostering dual-growth mindset should encourage agentic changes that meet these four criteria, while the agentic changes from fostering either self- or job-growth mindset may only meet the first two criteria (volitional and self-concordant). The latter two criteria—effortful and novel—are important because they help keep the experiences “fresh,” mitigating the adaptation and habituation processes that drive the hedonic treadmill. Small changes are unlikely to be effortful or novel enough to durably boost happiness. To have a meaningful impact on happiness, agentic changes need to be relatively substantial.

use: Reviews in industrial/organizational psychology and organizational behavior repeatedly highlight instances where published measurement scales fall short in adequately capturing the content of the constructs they are intended to assess. For instance, Stone-Romero (1994) identified numerous scales related to affect, strain, and job attitudes in which items either failed to include relevant content or included content that was tangential or irrelevant. He concluded that, as a result, "tremendous amounts of time, effort, and other resources have been expended on research that has poor conceptual and methodological underpinnings" (p. 175). More recently, Aguinis and Vandenberg (2014) emphasized that insufficient attention to content validation is among several issues that lead “organizational science researchers [to] begin their data analysis journey with a losing hand” (p. 590). Likewise, Aguinis and Edwards (2014) noted that “there is no shortage of studies in which the correspondence between constructs and measures is tenuous” (p. 149). Collectively, these critiques underscore persistent shortcomings in content validation—defined as the methodological process of assessing the extent to which items on a scale effectively sample the full range of content associated with a construct (Cronbach, 1990; Nunnally, 1978).
text: Reviews in industrial/organizational psychology and organizational behavior continue to point to cases where published scales do not adequately sample the content associated with a construct. For example, Stone-Romero (1994) described scales in the affect, strain, and job attitude realms where items either failed to sample relevant content or instead sampled irrelevant content. He concluded, "As a result, tremendous amounts of time, effort, and other resources have been expended on research that has poor conceptual and methodological underpinnings" (p. 175). More recently, Aguinis and Vandenberg (2014) argued that inadequate attention to content validation was one of a number of ways that "organizational science researchers begin their data analysis journey with a losing hand" (p. 590). Similarly, Aguinis and Edwards (2014) observed, "there is no shortage of studies in which the correspondence between constructs and measures is tenuous" (p. 149). Such criticisms point to problems with content validation—”the methodological process of gauging the degree to which scale items adequately sample the universe of content associated with a construct (Cronbach, 1990; Nunnally, 1978).

use: To understand why competence and warmth may moderate the impact of gratitude expressions, we turn to the concept of perceived deservingness. Feather (2008) defines deservingness as "judgments that relate to outcomes that are earned or achieved as products of a person’s action” (p. 1231). While such judgments can arise from specific behaviors, they may also be inferred indirectly based on a person’s traits or qualities (Feather, 2008, p. 1231). At the core of deservingness lies the perceived alignment between an individual’s actions, motivations, and intentions, and the outcomes they receive. Positive actions, intentions, and motivations are generally seen as warranting positive outcomes, while negative behaviors or intentions are viewed as meriting negative consequences. When this alignment is disrupted—such as when someone benefits despite acting inappropriately—evaluators often view the situation as unjust and respond negatively. For instance, research shows that people react unfavorably when advantageous outcomes are given to disliked outgroup members (Stephan & Finlay, 1999), or to individuals who have violated group norms (Smith, 2000).
text: In considering why competence and warmth should moderate gratitude expressions’ effects, we focus on perceptions of deservingness. Feather (2008) defined deservingness as “judgments that relate to outcomes that are earned or achieved as products of a person’s action” (p. 1231). These judgments can be made in relation to a specific action, but can also be “indirectly inferred on the basis of information about a person’s qualities” (Feather, 2008, p. 1231). Central to the concept of deservingness is the alignment between a person’s actions, motivations, and intentions and the benefits they receive in return. Good actions, motivations, and intentions are seen as deserving of good outcomes, while bad actions, motivations, and intentions are seen as deserving of bad outcomes. Evaluators tend to react negatively when individuals receive undeserved outcomes, as such misalignment is generally perceived as unjust. For example, evaluators tend to react negatively when good outcomes befall outgroup members who are disliked (Stephan & Finlay, 1999), and when good outcomes befall people who violate group norms and expectations (Smith, 2000).

use: This article contributes to the multi-team system (MTS) literature by advancing theoretical understanding of conflict within MTSs and bringing conflict to the forefront of mainstream MTS discussions—a space traditionally centered on leadership and coordination, with limited attention to individual-level dynamics (Mathieu, Luciano, & DeChurch, 2018). In many real-world MTS contexts, interactions between teams are facilitated by select representatives rather than the entire teams engaging directly (e.g., through boundary spanners; Davison et al., 2012; Marrone, 2010; or handoff processes; McElroy et al., 2015). Hence, it is critical for MTS research to account for the distinct experiences of individuals embedded in these systems, as well as the implications of those experiences for overall performance. Additionally, as effective functioning in MTSs requires alignment within and across team boundaries, understanding opposing or countervailing forces becomes central to optimizing system performance (DeChurch & Zaccaro, 2013; Luciano, Fenters, Park, Bartels, & Tannenbaum, 2021). Our findings—demonstrating the detrimental effects of intra-team conflict and the potentially beneficial outcomes of inter-team conflict—provide preliminary evidence that conflict can serve as a countervailing force within MTSs, potentially rooted in how individuals experience conflict across different team boundaries.
text: Turning to the MTS literature, this article expands theory on conflict in MTSs and elevates conflict as important in the mainstream MTS conversation—which has historically been dominated by leadership and coordination and has yet to consider individual-level relationships (Mathieu, Luciano, & DeChurch, 2018). In many real-world settings, between-team interactions are conducted by representatives of different teams rather than the entire team interacting with the entire other team (e.g., boundary spanners; Davison et al., 2012; Marrone, 2010; handoffs; McElroy et al., 2015). Thus, it is essential for research to consider the different experiences of individuals operating in MTSs and their performance consequences. Furthermore, as MTSs are required to work effectively within and between teams, identifying countervailing effects is crucial to MTS success (DeChurch & Zaccaro, 2013; Luciano, Fenters, Park, Bartels, & Tannenbaum, 2021). The negative effects of intra-team conflict and the positive effects of inter-team conflict in our study offer initial evidence that conflict is a countervailing force in MTSs that may have some roots in differences between intra-individual conflict experiences.

use: The aim of this paper is to develop a theoretical model explaining how legatees respond to the departure of a legator. We begin by highlighting key challenges in conceptualizing legacies within lower organizational tiers and those inherited by individuals in supporting roles. Notably, contributions commonly attributed to managers, informal leaders, or organizational “stars” often result from the efforts of multiple legatees who played critical, though sometimes unrecognized, roles. When the legator exits, these contributions may become perceived as “contested territory”—zones of ambiguity and dispute over future ownership and direction (Brown, Lawrence, & Robinson, 2005). To explore these dynamics, we employ the metaphor of a "footprint" and draw upon insights from the literature on psychological ownership and territoriality (Brown et al., 2005; Pierce, Kostova, & Dirks, 2001).  Building on these foundations, we develop a theory of legatee responses in the wake of a legator’s departure. We conceptualize the legator’s exit as a disruptive event—a shock—that prompts legatees to reexamine questions of meaning (Heine, Proulx, & Vohs, 2006) and identity (Vignoles, 2011; Vignoles et al., 2006) while simultaneously confronting their evolving sense of psychological ownership (Pierce et al., 2001).
text: The purpose of this paper is to build a model of how legatees react to the departure of a legator. We begin by describing some issues involved in theorizing about legacies in the lower echelons and legacies inherited by legatees. For example, contributions that are ascribed to managers, informal leaders, and stars often represent contributions for which multiple legatees played a pivotal role. Once the legator departs, those legatees may view the contributions as "contested territory"—”marked by disagreements about who owns them moving forward (Brown, Lawrence, & Robinson, 2005). To navigate such issues, we draw on the metaphor of a footprint while applying concepts from psychological ownership and territoriality (Brown et al., 2005; Pierce, Kostova, & Dirks, 2001). We then build theory about legatee reactions after the legator departs. We cast that departure as a shock that causes legatees to reconsider issues of meaning (Heine, Proulx, & Vohs, 2006) and identity (Vignoles, 2011; Vignoles, Regalia, Manzi, Golledge, & Scabini, 2006) as they grapple with feelings of ownership (Pierce et al., 2001).

use: From an empirical standpoint, this study presents the first meta-analytic synthesis of the third-party mistreatment literature. Meta-analysis serves a critical function in the development of academic fields—it consolidates existing findings, identifies gaps where additional research is needed, tests for moderating variables, and generates insights that can shape future inquiry (Carlson & Ji, 2011). A meta-analysis focusing on third-party reactions to observed mistreatment offers a unique contribution by enabling direct comparisons with findings from studies where individuals personally experience mistreatment ("first-party" studies). Prior meta-analyses in related domains—such as abusive supervision, perceived injustice, workplace incivility, and sexual harassment—have consistently revealed moderate positive associations with negative affect and antisocial behavior, and moderate negative associations with positive affect and prosocial behavior (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Mackey et al., 2017; Willness et al., 2007). Given that third-party observers are not personally targeted by the mistreatment, one might reasonably expect smaller effect sizes in our analysis. However, if the effects are comparable, this underscores the significance of considering the third-party perspective—especially in light of evidence that mistreatment is more frequently observed than personally experienced (Dhanani et al., 2021).
text: Empirically speaking, we provide the first meta-analytic synthesis of the third-party mistreatment literature. Meta-analysis plays an important role in the life cycle of a literature—consolidating findings, revealing areas where more studies are needed, testing potential moderating variables, and providing insights that spur new directions (Carlson & Ji, 2011). A meta-analysis of third-party reactions to observing mistreatment provides an additional benefit: informing comparisons to the results of “first-party” studies on experiencing mistreatment. Meta-analyses of the abusive supervision, injustice, incivility, and sexual harassment literatures have yielded moderate positive correlations with negative affect and antisocial behavior and moderate negative correlations with positive affect and prosocial behavior (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Mackey et al., 2017; Willness et al., 2007). One might expect the effect sizes in our meta-analysis to be weaker because the mistreatment did not actually happen to the third parties. If that is not the case, however, the importance of the third-party perspective becomes more evident—especially given the higher prevalence of observing versus experiencing mistreatment (Dhanani et al., 2021).

use:
Our empirical findings on women's behavior in the boardroom challenge dominant assumptions about how women are expected to act in such settings. Contrary to gender stereotypes—which portray women directors as deferential and primarily concerned with “others’ well-being” and “emotional sensitivity” (Heilman, 2012: 115)—we find that women often display rationality and independence. This is evident in their tendency to ask probing questions, engage in critical discussion, and seek out diverse viewpoints. Notably, the women in our study do not conform to traditional gender role expectations.

At the same time, our findings highlight that male directors frequently act in ways that reinforce gender stereotypes and biases. Men often overlook the expertise of women directors, dismiss or sideline their questions and contributions, and treat their input as marginal. As a result, despite the women’s extensive professional experience and achievements, they are not fully acknowledged or respected by their male peers. In sum, although gender diversity has made notable progress—particularly across European boardrooms—women directors continue to encounter structural and interpersonal barriers to being recognized for their competence.
text: Our data-based insights on women's behavior in the boardroom also challenge prevailing theories regarding how women are likely to behave. While gender stereotypes would suggest that women directors would be deferential and reflect "concern for others" and "emotional sensitivity" (Heilman, 2012: 115), we observe that by asking in-depth questions and being willing to debate issues and get multiple opinions on the table, women exhibit rationality and autonomy. Moreover, the women in our study do not demonstrate that they conform to gender role expectations. However, our study also reveals that men directors behave in ways that are consistent with gender stereotypes and bias, in that they fail to recognize women directors for their breadth of knowledge, and tend to ignore their questions or comments or treat these as irrelevant. Thus, despite their experiences and career accomplishments, women are not fully acknowledged or respected by men in the boardroom. Overall, then, while board gender diversity has improved significantly, especially in European companies, women directors still face difficulties in being recognized for their competence.

use: Second, cognitively demanding work can, at times, become overwhelming. When mental strain reaches a certain threshold, individuals may resort to daydreaming as a means of psychological escape. These mental excursions can take the form of playfully imagining alternative solutions to other work-related issues, or they may drift into more surreal and fantastical realms. In particular, bizarre daydreams—characterized by their implausible, often otherworldly content—offer individuals a powerful form of mental reprieve from the pressures of their work and other life stressors. By momentarily transporting the mind away from immediate concerns, responsibilities, or stressors, such daydreams provide a temporary, albeit fleeting, release from real-world demands—echoing findings that individuals seek mental withdrawal as a coping mechanism for stress (Taris, Schreurs, & Van Iersel-Van Silfhout, 2001). In light of these dynamics, we anticipate a positive relationship between the cognitive demands of work and the frequency of both problem-focused and bizarre daydreaming.
text: Second, in some cases, cognitively demanding work can become too much to bear. In such cases, people's minds may turn to daydreaming as a form of mental escape. Doing so may involve playfully generating possibilities related to work-related problems different from the one at hand, or it may involve engaging in more bizarre forms of daydreaming. Indeed, because they involve departures into highly implausible and, at times, fantastic realms, bizarre daydreams provide individuals maximal psychological escape from the intensity of their work and related sources of stress in their lives. That is, such daydreams enable individuals to distance themselves temporarily from whatever problems, concerns, duties, or demands are causing them stress—”a possibility in tune with the observation that people seek release from stresses and demands through specific forms of psychological withdrawal (Taris, Schreurs, & Van Iersel-Van Silfhout, 2001). Given these various possibilities, we expect to see positive associations between the extent to which work is cognitively demanding and the occurrence of daydreams of both the problem-oriented kind and the bizarre kind.

use: In line with my hypotheses, shifts in a company’s position on the Fortune ranking significantly impacted stock returns, but only under specific conditions that likely enhance the salience and informational value of such changes. These conditions include: (a) when the company experienced a drop rather than a rise in ranking; (b) when alternative information sources—such as media coverage or financial analyst attention—were limited; (c) when the company had relatively low institutional ownership and a greater proportion of individual shareholders; and (d) when the company held a lower ranking in the previous year. These findings underscore the conditional nature of ranking effects, as upward movements in ranking generally had little to no impact on stock performance. Furthermore, in contexts where rankings are expected to carry limited informational value—characterized by broad media and analyst coverage, significant institutional ownership, and already high rankings—neither upward nor downward changes in ranking had a meaningful influence on stock returns. Additional exploratory analysis reveals that a decline in ranking begins to significantly affect stock performance only once it exceeds a threshold of a 15-place drop.
text: Consistent with my hypotheses, changes in a company's position in the Fortune ranking significantly influenced stock returns under specific circumstances that plausibly increase the salience and information value of a change: notably, (a) when the company experienced a fall rather than a rise in ranking; (b) when alternative sources of information about the company, such as media outlets or financial analysts, were scarce; (c) when a lesser portion of the company was held by institutional investors compared to individual shareholders; and (d) when the company had a relatively low ranking in the preceding year. Highlighting the conditional impact of ranking shifts, rises in ranking typically had a minimal effect on stock returns. Moreover, in situations in which rankings are expected to have low information value—”extensive media and analyst coverage, substantial institutional ownership, and previously high rankings—”neither falls nor rises in ranking significantly affected stock returns. Further exploratory analysis indicates that falls in the Fortune list start to significantly affect stock returns only when they surpass a critical threshold of a 15-place drop.

use:
Find-remind-bind theory has historically centered on dyadic interactions, highlighting how expressions of gratitude influence the relationship between the person expressing thanks and the recipient (Algoe et al., 2010). More recently, however, Algoe and colleagues (2020) have proposed a broader conceptualization of gratitude’s binding function. They emphasized the need to account for third-party observers and posited that gratitude can also foster social bonds between witnesses and both the expresser and recipient, thereby shaping group-level social dynamics.

Building on this evolving line of research, our study shifts focus to how witnesses perceive the prosocial identities of expressers. Identities, in general, refer to “the set of meanings that define who one is” (Burke & Stets, 2009, p. 3). A prosocial identity, more specifically, represents a self-conception oriented around helping others and is closely tied to prosocial behaviors (Gneezy et al., 2012; Ramarajan et al., 2017). Beyond influencing helping behavior, organizational research has associated prosocial identity with a range of important outcomes, including increased affective commitment to the organization (Grant et al., 2008) and stronger organizational identification (Cha et al., 2014).
text:
Find-remind-bind theory has traditionally focused on dyads, emphasizing how expressions of thanks shape the relationship between expresser and recipient (Algoe et al., 2010). More recently, Algoe et al. (2020) argued for a more expansive perspective on the binding impact of gratitude. Specifically, they argued for the importance of considering third-party witnesses and hypothesized that gratitude can bind third-party witnesses to the expresser and recipient of gratitude, thus impacting social dynamics at the level of the group.

Our research builds on this literature by focusing on witnesses’ perceptions of expressers’ prosocial identities. Identities can be broadly defined as “the set of meanings that define who one is” (Burke & Stets, 2009, p. 3). Prosocial identity in turn refers to a conceptualization of the self that is centered around being helpful and is closely linked to prosocial behavior (Gneezy et al., 2012; Ramarajan et al., 2017). Beyond its effects on prosocial behavior, organizational scholars have linked prosocial identity to outcomes such as employees’ affective commitment (Grant et al., 2008) and organizational identification (Cha et al., 2014).

use: Latack (1986) proposed that the "turning-to-work" strategy manifests as immersion in work—characterized by extended work hours and heightened effort. While his study focused on this strategy as a response to job-related stress, it underscored a broader and longstanding notion: individuals may cope with stressors by engaging more deeply in their work. Yet, this earlier research offers only a narrow view of how such coping unfolds, particularly when it comes to stressors unrelated to the job. Although previous studies have noted that individuals might respond by working longer or harder (Latack, 1986; Lazarus & Folkman, 1984), they provide little insight into what people are actually doing during these extended hours, especially in the context of coping with grief. As a result, our understanding remains underdeveloped when it comes to the specific activities employees engage in while immersed in work, the underlying mechanisms that drive these behaviors, and how such changes influence both individuals and their work experiences.
text: Latack (1986) suggested that the turning-to-work strategy takes the form of immersion in work, which implies spending longer hours at work and putting more effort into it. Although Latack (1986) elaborated on the immersion in work as a strategy for coping with job stress, his study illustrated that the idea that people can turn to work to cope with stressors is not novel. However, the aforementioned research leaves us with a very limited understanding of how such coping happens, especially in regard to coping with non-job stress. In particular, while it has implied that people may work longer hours and put more effort into their jobs (Latack, 1986; Lazarus & Folkman, 1984), it has been silent about what employees are doing as they turn to work as a way to cope with grief. Thus, an understanding of the processes they undertake during their longer hours, the mechanisms that give rise to those processes, as well as the effect that such changes in work behavior can have on people and their work, remains limited.

use: Historically, research on technology adoption within organizations has centered on perceived usefulness and ease of use, as articulated in the Technology Acceptance Model (Davis, 1989). However, as artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly assumes roles traditionally performed by humans, recent scholarship has adopted a sociotechnical perspective—applying frameworks typically used to study human-to-human interactions to better understand human-AI dynamics (de Visser et al., 2016; Kong et al., 2023; Lazar & Nelson, 2023). For instance, Langer et al. (2023) observed that the deployment of AI assistants in ethically sensitive contexts, such as hiring decisions, has shifted technology adoption considerations from conventional performance metrics to concerns about the fairness and moral quality of AI-driven decisions. In this evolving context, trust has emerged as a vital lens for understanding how individuals perceive and engage with AI, playing a key role in determining adoption (Glikson & Woolley, 2020). Moreover, studies such as Choung et al. (2023) have shown that trust in AI assistants is positively associated with favorable attitudes—such as viewing the technology as approachable or “pleasant”—highlighting the affective dimensions of human-AI trust formation.
text: Research on the adoption of technology within organizations has historically focused on its perceived usefulness and ease of use (technology acceptance model; F. D. Davis, 1989). As AI has begun to occupy organizational positions traditionally filled by humans, recent research has shifted toward a sociotechnical perspective that uses paradigms traditionally reserved for human-to-human interactions to understand human-to-AI interactions (de Visser et al., 2016; Kong et al., 2023; Lazar & Nelson, 2023). For example, Langer et al. (2023) argued that as AI assistants are increasingly used to support decisions with ethical implications such as personnel recruitment, decisions on whether to adopt technology have moved beyond traditional performance measures to questions regarding the morality and fairness of the system's decisions. Trust has been found to provide a powerful paradigm for capturing perceptions toward AI and assessing whether individuals will adopt it (Glikson & Woolley, 2020), and scholars such as Choung et al. (2023) have found that positive attitudes toward AI assistants (e.g., viewing the technology as "pleasant") are positively correlated with trust in the assistant.

use: Leaders play a critical role in shaping the work environment and significantly influence the innovative behaviors of their followers through various mechanisms. They offer essential support during times of difficulty (Unsworth, Wall, & Carter, 2005) and help shape followers’ emotional responses in ways that can facilitate creativity (Zhou & George, 2003). Leaders also often control key resources needed for innovation and can serve as valuable assets by offering technical expertise or access to social networks (Mumford & Licuanan, 2004). Furthermore, they shape how employees perceive their job characteristics—a factor closely linked to creativity and innovation, as noted earlier (Purvanova, Bono, & Dzieweczynski, 2006). The overall pattern of findings underscores the leader’s influence on employees’ creative and innovative performance. In particular, leadership—especially when assessed through Leader-Member Exchange (LMX) quality—was consistently found to be positively associated with followers’ creative and innovative behaviors. While the strength of this relationship varies slightly across different leadership constructs such as supervisory support, LMX, and transformational leadership, the general trend indicates a positive link between leadership and creativity. Collectively, these findings emphasize the critical role of leadership and contextual factors in fostering workplace innovation.
text: Leaders are important shapers of the work environment and influence their followers' innovative behaviors in a number of ways. For example, leaders provide meaningful support during challenging times (Unsworth, Wall, & Carter, 2005), including shaping the emotional responses of followers (Zhou & George, 2003). They often have control over resources necessary for the innovation process and can be resources for their followers in terms of technical knowledge or social networking (Mumford & Licuanan, 2004). Additionally, they influence their followers' perceptions of job characteristics (Purvanova, Bono, & Dzieweczynski, 2006), which is an important correlate of innovation as discussed above. The pattern of results provides support for the leader's role in the employees' creative and innovative performance. Leadership, especially as measured through LMX quality, was positively related to the creative and innovative behavior of followers. Although the magnitude of the relationship varied somewhat among measures of supervisory support, LMX, and transformational leadership, in general, leadership was positively related to creativity. Taken together, these results highlight the importance of contextual and leadership influences in the creativity process.

In the control-theory framework, two key dimensions characterize ruminative cognitions: their valence and their temporal orientation. The first dimension—valence—distinguishes between negative and positive cognitions. Negative cognitions typically arise in response to adverse events, such as failing to achieve a valued goal or recognizing a gap between the current state and a desired one (Martin & Tesser, 1996). This perceived discrepancy may also pertain to emotional states—for example, feeling a misalignment between one's current and desired affective condition (Larsen, 2000; Watkins, 2008). In contrast, positive cognitions emerge in response to favorable outcomes, such as making unexpected progress toward a meaningful goal.

The second dimension—temporal direction—refers to the time focus of ruminative thoughts. According to control theory, such cognitions may center on the present moment, reflecting on negative events that signal a discrepancy, or on positive events indicating goal attainment. Alternatively, they may be future-oriented, involving thoughts about how to resolve present discrepancies or how to leverage positive developments to create new aspirations (Watkins, 2008).
text: Within this control-theory approach, two dimensions of ruminative cognitions are important. The first dimension refers to the valence of the cognition, and the second to its temporal direction. In terms of valence, negative cognitions occur as a response to negative events, for instance, when failing to reach an important goal or when noticing a discrepancy between the present and a desired situation (Martin & Tesser, 1996). This discrepancy can even refer to a mismatch between an actual and a desired affective state (Larsen, 2000; Watkins, 2008). Positive cognitions occur as a response to positive events, for instance, when making unforeseen progress toward a goal. In terms of temporal direction, the control-theory perspective implies that ruminative cognitions can either focus on the present situation (i.e., negative events indicating a discrepancy between the actual and a desired state and positive events indicating the attainment of a desired state) or on future steps to overcome negative events (i.e., reduce an actual discrepancy) or to take advantage of positive events (i.e., create a new discrepancy; cf. Watkins, 2008).

use: Directing attention toward the perspectives of others helps employees establish a benchmark for discerning which ideas are worth adopting and which should be set aside. Grounded in motivated information processing theory, research suggests that when employees consider viewpoints beyond their own, they are more inclined to engage in integrative thinking—bringing together and aligning multiple perspectives into cohesive insights (De Dreu et al., 2000). As they engage with a wider range of diverse perspectives, employees develop a more nuanced understanding of which ideas are likely to be viewed as valuable by various beneficiaries, constituents, or stakeholders (Amabile, 1996). For example, Mohrman et al. (2001) demonstrated that academic researchers produced work seen as more relevant and useful when they actively considered the perspectives of practitioners. Additionally, adopting others’ viewpoints can help employees uncover practical applications for novel ideas that might otherwise appear unfeasible. Research on product development teams, for instance, has shown that when team members take into account the perspectives of both coworkers and customers, they are better equipped to transform creative concepts into viable products (Dougherty, 1992; Purser, Pasmore, & Tenkasi, 1992).
text: Focusing attention on the perspectives of others will provide employees with a standard for determining which ideas should be selected as useful versus discarded as less useful. According to motivated information processing theory and research, when employees take the perspectives of others, they are more likely to think in an integrative fashion to consolidate and align these perspectives (De Dreu et al., 2000). As employees consider more numerous and diverse perspectives, they gain a deeper understanding of which ideas different groups of beneficiaries, constituents, or stakeholders are most likely to consensually consider useful (Amabile, 1996). Indeed, Mohrman et al. (2001) found that when academic researchers took the perspectives of practitioners, they succeeded in conducting research that practitioners judged as more useful. Furthermore, taking others' perspectives can enable employees to identify useful applications of novel but otherwise impractical ideas. Studies of product development teams have shown that when employees adopt the perspectives of coworkers and customers, they are more capable of translating their novel ideas into useful products (Dougherty, 1992; Purser, Pasmore, & Tenkasi, 1992).

use: Workplace deviance—defined as “voluntary behavior that violates significant organizational norms and threatens the well-being of the organization, its members, or both” (Robinson & Bennett, 1995, p. 556)—is a pervasive phenomenon in organizational life (Marasi, 2014; Marasi et al., 2018) and remains one of the most widely examined constructs in organizational science. Researchers have long acknowledged the complexity of deviance, offering numerous alternative conceptualizations, dimensions, and typologies to better understand how employees engage in such behaviors (e.g., Carpenter & Berry, 2017; Evans et al., 2023; Griffin & Lopez, 2005; Gruys & Sackett, 2003; Marcus et al., 2016; Spector et al., 2006). Despite this diversity, most research continues to categorize deviant behaviors based on their primary targets—either other individuals (interpersonal deviance; ID) or the organization as a whole (organizational deviance; OD; Bennett & Robinson, 2000). This ID–OD distinction has become a central organizing framework in the literature, shaping a substantial portion of our current understanding of workplace deviance (Berry et al., 2007; Ellen et al., 2021). Indeed, by providing conceptual clarity and empirical tractability, this framework has galvanized scholarly attention and facilitated the accumulation of a rich body of knowledge on deviance in organizational contexts (Bennett et al., 2018; Mackey et al., 2021).
text: Workplace deviance—”defined as "voluntary behavior that violates significant organizational norms and threatens the well-being of the organization, its members, or both" (Robinson & Bennett, 1995, p. 556)—”is ubiquitous in organizations (Marasi, 2014; Marasi et al., 2018) and one of the most popular constructs studied in organizational science. Scholars have noted the complex nature of deviance and have proposed various alternative conceptualizations, dimensions, or facets of how employees engage in deviance (e.g., Carpenter & Berry, 2017; Evans et al., 2023; Griffin & Lopez, 2005; Gruys & Sackett, 2003; Marcus et al., 2016; Spector et al., 2006). However, research typically distinguishes deviance based on whether the behaviors target other individuals (interpersonal deviance; ID) or the organization itself (organizational deviance; OD; Bennett & Robinson, 2000), and much of our knowledge surrounding the phenomenon of deviance has been built around this ID-OD distinction (Berry et al., 2007; Ellen et al., 2021). Indeed, this framework helped propel interest in the topic, generating a vast amount of knowledge of deviance in organizations (Bennett et al., 2018; Mackey et al., 2021).

Building on the arguments outlined above, we propose that receiving conflict within a team can be detrimental for the receiver, primarily because it increases the likelihood that they will feel compelled to direct their attention toward the conflict. This is particularly plausible given the ongoing interaction and shared priorities between the sender and receiver (Firth et al., 2015). When the receiver becomes newly aware of a conflict issue through the sender, this awareness introduces off-task attentional demands that divert cognitive resources away from the primary task at hand. In essence, this increased awareness is likely to correlate positively with off-task attentional demands, which subsequently undermine task engagement.

Furthermore, the perceived legitimacy and priority of conflict arising within the team—when compared to conflict that occurs between teams—intensifies its attentional draw for the receiver. This dynamic further disrupts task engagement, as the receiver may perceive within-team conflict as more relevant or urgent. As a result, conflict received within team boundaries may impair individual task performance by heightening off-task attentional demands. Formally stated:
text: Consistent with the arguments advanced above, we suggest that receiving within-team conflict can be detrimental to receivers as they are more likely to feel compelled to allocate their attentional focus to the conflict issues. This follows from the likelihood that the sender and receiver will continue interacting with each other and share priorities (Firth et al., 2015). As the conflict receiver has just been informed of the conflict issue by the sender, we speculate that this newly gained awareness creates off-task attentional demands, diverting their attention and efforts away from the focal task. In other words, this heightened awareness is likely to be positively related to off-task attentional demands, which, in turn, negatively impacts task engagement. In the meantime, the level of legitimacy and priority attributed to a within-team conflict experience, when compared to a between-team conflict experience, strengthens the off-task attentional pull for the receiver, thereby negatively affecting their task engagement. Hence, receiving conflict within the team boundaries may negatively impact the individual’s task performance by increasing their off-task attentional demands. More formally:

use: Furthermore, although Matta et al. (2017) shed light on the consequences of employees’ direct experiences of justice variability from their immediate supervisors, research has yet to explore whether vicarious experiences of justice variability—observing how others are treated—have similarly significant effects. As organizations increasingly shift toward flatter hierarchical structures (Lord et al., 2017), many employees now operate within dual-leader systems, where they witness firsthand how their own supervisors are treated by higher-level managers—those two levels above them. In response, our study examines how justice variability directed at supervisors by upper-level managers influences employees’ psychological states and behaviors. Drawing on uncertainty management theory and the literature on supervisors’ linking-pin role (Likert, 1961), we propose that when higher-level managers mistreat supervisors, employees may perceive this as a threat to the supervisor’s capacity to fulfill key linking-pin responsibilities—such as securing resources or special access for their team (Graen et al., 1977). Moreover, when this same manager also exhibits ethical leadership toward the supervisor, the juxtaposition of conflicting signals intensifies the effect. This contradiction heightens employees’ uncertainty, amplifying the harmful consequences of abusive supervision and contributing to greater emotional exhaustion.
text: Further, while Matta et al. (2017) offered insights into the consequences of employees' direct experiences of justice variability from their supervisors, research has not addressed whether vicarious experiences of justice variability are also consequential. With organizations increasingly adopting flatter hierarchies (Lord et al., 2017), many employees work in a dual-leader system where they become aware of how their direct supervisors interact with and are treated by higher-level managers (the leaders are two levels above them). We thus also study how justice variability directed at employees' supervisors by higher-level managers impacts employees psychologically and behaviorally. Integrating uncertainty management theory with research on supervisors' linking-pin role (Likert, 1961), we suggest that when managers abuse a supervisor, employees experience uncertainty about their supervisor's ability to perform linking-pin role responsibilities (e.g., obtain valuable resources or privileges from the manager for the group; Graen et al., 1977). When that manager also displays ethical leadership toward the supervisor, the conflicting cues have a violating effect, exacerbating the employee's uncertainty and increasing the detrimental impact of abusive supervision on employee exhaustion.

use: Although scholarly perspectives differ on whether work serves as a burden or a refuge during difficult times, they share a common assumption: that employees confronting personal hardship experience work in one of these two ways. Consequently, research has largely focused on offering guidance to organizations, managers, and colleagues about how to ease the employee’s return to work (e.g., Petriglieri & Maitlis, 2019; Wilson et al., 2020), often overlooking the active role employees themselves might play in this process. In contrast, the job crafting literature has emphasized that individuals are capable of shaping their own work experiences (Berg, Grant, & Johnson, 2010; Grant, Fried, Parker & Frese, 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Yet, this body of work has limited relevance for grieving employees, as it typically frames job crafting as a response to work-related situational triggers (Park & Park, 2021; Zhang & Parker, 2019), thereby neglecting how personal life events might serve as catalysts for job crafting behavior.
text: While the role of work has been seen by the scholarship from those opposite viewpoints, what remains in common between them is the assumption that an employee who goes through challenging times finds either a burden or a refuge in their work. As a result, the literature has predominantly focused on exploring recommendations for organizations, managers, and colleagues on how to alleviate the employee's experience upon returning to work (e.g., Petriglieri & Maitlis, 2019; Wilson et al., 2020) while setting aside the role the employee can play in this process. Yet, the literature on job crafting has revealed that people are agentic when it comes to shaping their work experiences (Berg, Grant, & Johnson, 2010; Grant, Fried, Parker & Frese, 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). However, the current state of the job crafting literature has limited applicability to a grieving employee because the theory suggests that job crafting can be triggered by work-related situational antecedents (Park & Park, 2021; Zhang & Parker, 2019), thus failing to connect personal life events with job crafting behavior.

use: As third-party literature has expanded its focus on empathy toward victims, it has simultaneously accumulated a growing body of research on schadenfreude in response to observed mistreatment. Notably, several studies in our review examined both emotions within the same investigation (Chen et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023; Feng & Savani, 2023). Our findings indicate that witnessing mistreatment is positively associated with feelings of schadenfreude related to the event. Although the effect size was smaller than those found for anger and empathy, we believe many scholars in the third-party literature may still be struck by its strength (rc = .19). A closer look at the 19 correlations in this category shows that 15 of them were positive, with only one demonstrating a substantially negative value (Feng & Savani, 2023; rc = −.60). This consistency is particularly striking given that many of the studies we coded hypothesized that schadenfreude would primarily be triggered by specific observer- or victim-related moderators (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020).
text: As the third-party literature has amassed more studies on empathy toward the victim, it has also amassed more studies on schadenfreude from the event. Indeed, some of the studies included in our review included both emotions in their investigations (Chen et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023; Feng & Savani, 2023). Our results showed that observing mistreatment was positively related to feelings of schadenfreude from the event. Although the effect size was weaker than the anger and empathy results, we suspect many third-party scholars would still be surprised by its magnitude (rc = .19). Indeed, an examination of the 19 correlations in that cell of our synthesis reveals that 15 of them had a positive sign—with only one being sizably negative (Feng & Savani, 2023, where rc = −.60). The consistency of the relationship is surprising given that many of the studies we coded theorized that schadenfreude would mostly emerge at particular levels of observer- and victim-focused moderators (Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020).

use: Failures often serve as a catalyst for organizational attention, prompting firms to improve their routines and operational processes (Cyert & March, 1963; Darr, Argote, & Epple, 1995; Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; Levitt & March, 1988; Say & Vasudeva, 2020). Consequently, a significant body of research within organizational learning theory has explored the conditions under which failures—whether directly experienced or vicariously observed—can lead to meaningful knowledge acquisition and transfer (Baumard & Starbuck, 2005; Bennett & Snyder, 2017; KC, Staats, & Gino, 2013; Kim & Miner, 2007; Knott & Posen, 2005; Park, Lehman, & Ramanujam, 2022; Sitkin, 1992). However, this body of theory also emphasizes that knowledge gained through experience is rarely retained perfectly over time. Instead, organizations are prone to losing portions of what they have learned as time passes—a phenomenon known as "knowledge depreciation" (Argote, 1996; Argote & Miron-Spektor, 2011; Arthur & Huntley, 2005; Darr et al., 1995; David & Brachet, 2011; Martignoni & Keil, 2021; Musaji, Schulze, & De Castro, 2020). In essence, knowledge depreciation is the counterpart to knowledge retention, reflecting the gradual erosion of organizational knowledge over time.
text: Failures can capture organizational attention and motivate firms to improve their routines and processes (Cyert & March, 1963; Darr, Argote, & Epple, 1995; Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; Levitt & March, 1988; Say & Vasudeva, 2020). As a result, much research in organizational learning theory has sought to identify whether and when failures might yield new knowledge, whether through direct experience or through vicarious observation and knowledge transfer (Baumard & Starbuck, 2005; Bennett & Snyder, 2017; KC, Staats, & Gino, 2013; Kim & Miner, 2007; Knott & Posen, 2005; Park, Lehman, & Ramanujam, 2022; Sitkin, 1992). However, organizational learning theory holds that the retention of knowledge learned through experience over time is usually imperfect, such that some organizational knowledge is lost as time progresses—”a process referred to as "knowledge depreciation" (Argote, 1996; Argote & Miron-Spektor, 2011; Arthur & Huntley, 2005; Darr et al., 1995; David & Brachet, 2011; Martignoni & Keil, 2021; Musaji, Schulze, & De Castro, 2020). Such knowledge depreciation is the inverse of knowledge retention; it represents the loss of organizational knowledge over time.

use: Supporting this connection, prior research shows that perceived benevolence fosters high-quality relationships between followers and their current leaders (Dirks & Ferrin, 2002; McAllister, 1995; Yang et al., 2009). More pertinent to our focus on emergent leadership, cooperative individuals who sacrificed greater personal resources for the benefit of the group were granted more influence and elevated social status, as they were perceived to be acting in the group’s best interests (Hardy & Van Vugt, 2006; Willer, 2009). Additionally, individuals who violated norms garnered increased leadership support—but only when their actions served others (Van Kleef et al., 2012). Collectively, these findings align with the social engagement hypothesis (Keltner et al., 2008), which suggests that people willingly assign higher social rank to those they perceive as genuinely concerned with advancing the welfare of their ingroup. Applying this to our context—and given our earlier theorizing that autonomy-helpers are generally viewed by recipients as even more benevolent than dependency-helpers—we build on these prior findings and the social engagement hypothesis to propose:
text: In support of this link, previous research indicates that perceived benevolence is conducive to high-quality relationships between followers and current leaders (Dirks & Ferrin, 2002; McAllister, 1995; Yang et al., 2009). More relevant to our focus on supporting prospective leadership, cooperative group members who sacrificed more of their own resources for the group were granted greater influence and conferred higher social rank because they were seen as motivated to promote group interests (Hardy & Van Vugt, 2006; Willer, 2009). Moreover, norm violators gained greater support for their leadership, but only when breaking rules to benefit others (Van Kleef et al., 2012). Together, the above findings are consistent with the social engagement hypothesis (Keltner et al., 2008) positing that people volitionally confer higher social ranks to those perceived as caring more about, and advancing the interests of, their ingroup members. use: In line with this reasoning, the DEEL model posits that the magnitude of information-knowledge gaps is directly proportional to the overall intensity of on-task effort. When these gaps are relatively small (as depicted toward the right side of Figure 1), the model suggests that learners will invest less total effort in the task (represented by the combined gray areas in Figure 1). This reduction occurs because learners tend to reallocate their limited cognitive and motivational resources toward other goals or conserve them for future tasks. Such decreases in effort when gaps are minimal serve a regulatory function, preventing excessive investments in learning once a satisfactory level of competence has been attained. In contrast, larger information-knowledge gaps (shown on the left side of Figure 1) trigger feelings of curiosity and a sense of knowledge deprivation, which motivate learners to engage more deeply and improve their competence in navigating the task environment (Litman, 2008; Litman & Jimerson, 2004; White, 1959). Accordingly, larger gaps are expected to prompt stronger overall investments in task-related effort. Based on this logic, we hypothesize that total on-task effort will be positively correlated with the perceived size of information-knowledge gaps in learning scenarios that involve performance-based goals.
text: Along these lines, the DEEL model proposes that the size of information-knowledge gaps should be directly related to the overall intensity of on-task effort. When information-knowledge gaps are smaller (toward the right side of Figure 1), DEEL proposes that learners will invest less overall effort in the task (the combination of the gray areas shown in Figure 1) because they are reallocating their limited resources toward other goals or preserving them for future use. Reductions in effort when information-knowledge gaps are small help prevent overinvestments in learning once an acceptable level of competence is reached. Conversely, larger information-knowledge gaps (shown on the left side of Figure 1) elicit feelings of interest and knowledge deprivation that drive learners to enhance their effectiveness in navigating the task environment (Litman, 2008; Litman & Jimerson, 2004; White, 1959). As such, larger information-knowledge gaps should stimulate greater overall investments of on-task effort. Based on this rationale, we predict that total investments of on-task effort will be positively related to the magnitude of perceived information-knowledge gaps when learning in performance contexts.

use: Problem-oriented daydreams refer to thoughts and mental images that are loosely sparked by, or ultimately linked to, the challenges individuals encounter in their daily work or personal lives (Singer & Antrobus, 1972). We propose that the primary theoretical mechanism through which these daydreams foster creativity lies in their capacity to produce novel and useful ideas (Giambra, 1974; Singer, 1975). Similar to the previous example, such daydreams often follow a period of focused effort on a difficult problem in the quest for a solution. While the thoughts and imagery that arise during problem-oriented daydreaming may not directly stem from the specific problem at hand—just as the image of a lightning bolt in Einstein’s account was unlikely to be a direct outcome of his mathematical work—they can nonetheless stimulate a more playful interaction with the issue. Through this process, individuals may uncover previously unconsidered possibilities, which encourage further exploration or refinement, ultimately giving rise to ideas that are not only original but also capable of addressing the problem in meaningful ways.
text: Problem-oriented daydreams encompass those thoughts and images that are loosely inspired by or can be ultimately connected to the problems individuals face in their day-to-day activities or in their non-work lives (Singer & Antrobus, 1972). We suggest that the primary theoretical mechanism by which such daydreams enhance creativity is directly via the generation of novel and useful ideas (Giambra, 1974; Singer, 1975). Much like in the previous example, daydreams may follow a period of deep engagement with a challenging problem in the search for a solution. Although the thoughts and images that come to mind during problem-oriented daydreaming may not directly stem from or relate to the unresolved problem—”the image of the bolt of lightning in Einstein's example is unlikely to have been the direct consequence of his mathematical exercise—”they may nevertheless inspire playful engagement with the problem. In this way, new possibilities may emerge that cause individuals to further explore or refine these new possibilities to result in ideas that are not only novel but also have the potential to ultimately address the problem.

use: An alternative psychological tendency suggests that people are inclined to seek patterns in recurring cues (Rindova et al., 2010; Whitson & Galinsky, 2008), with these perceived patterns themselves becoming newsworthy. In this view, repeated instances of misconduct can lead observers to identify a broader pattern, prompting them to scrutinize subsequent occurrences more closely (Desai, 2011; Han et al., 2023; Naumovska et al., 2021). For instance, when Capital One revealed on July 19, 2019, that its customers’ personal data had been compromised, it triggered hundreds of media reports. A striking feature of these reports was the frequent reference to earlier data breaches, which framed the Capital One incident not as an isolated case but as part of an ongoing trend—highlighting systemic vulnerabilities and warning the public of future breaches (Andriole, 2019; Kiesnoski, 2019; Youn, 2019). While multiple factors likely contributed to the media's strong response, the "safety-in-numbers" concept would suggest that journalists, aware of the many prior breaches, would downplay this event. Instead, past breaches were invoked to underscore the significance of the Capital One incident, amplifying rather than diminishing its perceived importance.
text: An alternative psychological tendency also exists, where people look for patterns in repeated cues (Rindova et al., 2010; Whitson & Galinsky, 2008), and the patterns become newsworthy. Observers could thus perceive a pattern of misconduct based on repeated instances and consequently scrutinize current instances more intensely (Desai, 2011; Han et al., 2023; Naumovska et al., 2021). For example, when Capital One disclosed on July 19, 2019, that its customers’ personal data had been exposed, the media wrote hundreds of articles about it. Noticeable in the articles publicizing this incident were the numerous mentions of prior data breaches, warning the public that the Capital One breach was no exception, and that they should expect many more breaches (Andriole, 2019; Kiesnoski, 2019; Youn, 2019). Although different factors may have been at play, the safety-in-numbers effect suggests that the Capital One breach should not have received such extensive media coverage, given journalists’ awareness of the many prior data breaches, yet these breaches were used as evidence of the Capital One breach’s importance, rather than a reason to ignore it.

use: The use of technology for nonwork-related purposes during work hours—such as internet browsing (Blau, Yang, & Ward-Cook, 2006), social media engagement (Moqbel, Nevo, & Kock, 2013), messaging with family and friends (Yeh, Ma, Pan, Chuang, & Jhuang, 2020), or online gambling (Hadlington & Parsons, 2017)—is widely recognized as a “pervasive behavior observed in the daily work environment” (Lee, Lee, & Kim, 2007: 75). Research suggests that employees may spend up to two hours per workday on such activities, contributing to an estimated $85 billion annual loss in productivity for organizations (Andel, Kessler, Pindek, Kleinman, & Spector, 2019). However, these figures may overstate the true cost, as they often do not account for potential productivity rebounds or savings associated with reduced worker burnout and improved well-being (Syrek, Kühnel, Vahle-Hinz, & De Bloom, 2018). As the lines between work and personal life continue to blur—and given the competing implications of such behaviors—management scholars have increasingly focused on technology use at work for nonwork purposes (Ciolfi & Lockley, 2018; Farivar & Richardson, 2021; Russo, Ollier-Malaterre, Kossek, & Ohana, 2018).
text: Technology use for nonwork-related purposes at work, such as internet browsing (Blau, Yang, & Ward-Cook, 2006), social media use (Moqbel, Nevo, & Kock, 2013), communicating with family and friends via messenger applications (Yeh, Ma, Pan, Chuang, & Jhuang, 2020), and online gambling (Hadlington & Parsons, 2017), is a "pervasive behavior observed in the daily work environment" (Lee, Lee, & Kim, 2007: 75). Estimates suggest employees spend up to two hours per workday engaging in such behaviors, costing organizations an annual $85 billion in lost productivity (Andel, Kessler, Pindek, Kleinman, & Spector, 2019). At the same time, these statistics on the costs of lost productivity may be overstated or misleading if they fail to capture subsequent productivity gains or cost savings from reduced burnout (Syrek, Kühnel, Vahle-Hinz, & De Bloom, 2018). With the increased blurring of work and nonwork boundaries and these potential costs and benefits, management scholars have devoted significant attention to the study of technology use for nonwork-related purposes at work (Ciolfi & Lockley, 2018; Farivar & Richardson, 2021; Russo, Ollier-Malaterre, Kossek, & Ohana, 2018).

use: The current moment presents a timely and compelling opportunity for a comprehensive review of existing research on social context and time use. First, evolving patterns of work make the influence of social context increasingly essential to understanding how time is allocated and experienced. A growing number of jobs—such as those held by professionals, entrepreneurs, contract workers, and gig workers—differ markedly from the traditional Industrial Era model of wage-based, time-clocked labor. Rather than being governed by a clear, time-bound labor agreement, these contemporary roles are often "task-oriented" (Thompson, 1967), with individuals paid a fixed amount (e.g., salary or contract fee) to complete specific tasks, regardless of the time required (Zerubavel, 1979b, 1981). This task-based structure often blurs the boundaries of the standard workday, leaving workers with the persistent sense of being "always on" (e.g., Perlow, 1999; Shih, 2004). At the same time, traditional hourly-wage roles persist, but with a notable shift: many of these positions have become increasingly precarious, characterized by irregular schedules, unpredictable hours, and economic instability—traits that distinguish them starkly from the more stable, factory-based jobs of the past (Gerstel & Clawson, 2018; Kalleberg, 2009; Spreitzer, Cameron, & Garrett, 2017; Standing, 2016).
text: The moment is especially ripe for a comprehensive review of prior research on social context and time use. First, the nature of work is changing in ways that make social context more critical to understanding time use. Many jobs—”such as those of professionals, entrepreneurs, contract workers, and gig workers—”do not resemble Industrial Era, wage-based time/labor employment. Instead of revolving around a clear, time-based labor contract, contemporary jobs often have a "task orientation" (Thompson, 1967); individuals are paid a set amount (e.g., salary or contract fee) to complete certain tasks, regardless of how long it takes to accomplish them (Zerubavel, 1979b, 1981). These jobs have a tendency to encroach beyond the boundaries of a "standard" workday, such that workers can be left feeling "always on" (e.g., Perlow, 1999; Shih, 2004). Other contemporary jobs continue to compensate people based on an hourly wage, similar to older factory employment, but are significantly more "precarious" than traditional waged work because of unpredictable work schedules and inconsistent hours (Gerstel & Clawson, 2018; Kalleberg, 2009; Spreitzer, Cameron, & Garrett, 2017; Standing, 2016).

use: Organizational citizenship behavior remains a critical area of interest for both researchers and practitioners. It refers to employees’ voluntary efforts to go beyond their formal job requirements to contribute positively to their teams and organizations (C. A. Smith, Organ, & Near, 1983). More than forty years ago, scholars first suggested that such behaviors—helping colleagues, safeguarding organizational interests, offering constructive feedback, investing in self-development, and fostering a positive workplace climate—can enhance effectiveness at the individual, group, and organizational levels (Katz, 1964; Katz & Kahn, 1966). In the years since, a substantial body of research has validated this foundational idea. At the group and organizational levels, consistent engagement in citizenship behaviors has been linked to better performance outcomes—both in terms of quality and quantity—as well as increased customer satisfaction (Ehrhart & Naumann, 2004; Podsakoff, MacKenzie, Paine, & Bachrach, 2000). On the individual level, employees who demonstrate initiative and contribute beyond their formal roles tend to receive more favorable performance evaluations from supervisors (e.g., MacKenzie, Podsakoff, & Fetter, 1991, 1993; Motowidlo & Van Scotter, 1994), and are more frequently recognized with awards and promotions (e.g., Van Scotter, Motowidlo, & Cross, 2000).
text: Organizational citizenship behavior is a critical concern for both scholars and practitioners. Citizenship describes efforts by employees to take initiative to contribute in ways that are not formally required by the organization (C. A. Smith, Organ, & Near, 1983). More than four decades have passed since researchers proposed that employees can advance group, organizational, and personal effectiveness by engaging in citizenship behaviors such as helping coworkers, protecting the organization, making constructive suggestions, developing oneself, and spreading goodwill (Katz, 1964; Katz & Kahn, 1966). In recent years, extensive research has supported this core proposition. At collective levels, groups and organizations with employees who engage in high levels of citizenship achieve higher performance quality, performance quantity, and customer satisfaction (Ehrhart & Naumann, 2004; Podsakoff, MacKenzie, Paine, & Bachrach, 2000). At the individual level, employees who engage in citizenship by taking initiative to contribute to other people and the organization earn higher supervisor performance evaluations (e.g., MacKenzie, Podsakoff, & Fetter, 1991, 1993; Motowidlo & Van Scotter, 1994) and more awards and promotions (e.g., Van Scotter, Motowidlo, & Cross, 2000).

use: The use of technology for nonwork-related activities during work hours—such as internet browsing (Blau, Yang, & Ward-Cook, 2006), engaging with social media (Moqbel, Nevo, & Kock, 2013), messaging with family or friends (Yeh, Ma, Pan, Chuang, & Jhuang, 2020), and even online gambling (Hadlington & Parsons, 2017)—has become a common and pervasive behavior in contemporary workplaces (Lee, Lee, & Kim, 2007: 75). Some estimates suggest that employees may dedicate up to two hours of each workday to these activities, potentially resulting in productivity losses amounting to $85 billion annually (Andel, Kessler, Pindek, Kleinman, & Spector, 2019). However, such figures may exaggerate the true impact if they do not account for potential offsetting benefits, such as increased productivity following short breaks or cost savings associated with lower rates of burnout (Syrek, Kühnel, Vahle-Hinz, & De Bloom, 2018). As the boundaries between work and personal life grow increasingly blurred—and given the nuanced implications for both productivity and employee well-being—the phenomenon of nonwork-related technology use at work has attracted growing interest from management scholars (Ciolfi & Lockley, 2018; Farivar & Richardson, 2021; Russo, Ollier-Malaterre, Kossek, & Ohana, 2018).
text: Technology use for nonwork-related purposes at work, such as internet browsing (Blau, Yang, & Ward-Cook, 2006), social media use (Moqbel, Nevo, & Kock, 2013), communicating with family and friends via messenger applications (Yeh, Ma, Pan, Chuang, & Jhuang, 2020), and online gambling (Hadlington & Parsons, 2017), is a "pervasive behavior observed in the daily work environment" (Lee, Lee, & Kim, 2007: 75). Estimates suggest employees spend up to two hours per workday engaging in such behaviors, costing organizations an annual $85 billion in lost productivity (Andel, Kessler, Pindek, Kleinman, & Spector, 2019). At the same time, these statistics on the costs of lost productivity may be overstated or misleading if they fail to capture subsequent productivity gains or cost savings from reduced burnout (Syrek, Kühnel, Vahle-Hinz, & De Bloom, 2018). With the increased blurring of work and nonwork boundaries and these potential costs and benefits, management scholars have devoted significant attention to the study of technology use for nonwork-related purposes at work (Ciolfi & Lockley, 2018; Farivar & Richardson, 2021; Russo, Ollier-Malaterre, Kossek, & Ohana, 2018).

use: In this research, we explore whether AI management is perceived as less benevolent than human management, how this perception may undermine employee trust, and whether the negative impact is amplified in emotionally charged contexts—situations where the absence of emotional capacity in AI becomes more salient to employees. Through this investigation, we offer several contributions to the literatures on trust, emotion, algorithmic authority, and AI adoption. First, we extend interpersonal trust theory (Mayer & Davis, 1999; Mayer et al., 1995) to the emerging dynamic between machine managers and human subordinates, specifically by examining how employees experience and interpret AI management. Second, we apply the Emotions as Social Information (EASI) model (Van Kleef, 2008; van Kleef & Côté, 2022; see also Elfenbein, 2023; Scherer & Moors, 2019) to illuminate the critical role of perceived benevolence in shaping trust in AI leaders. Third, we advance the conversation on AI adoption in organizational settings by emphasizing how emotional context influences employee openness to AI-based management systems.
text: In this research, we consider if AI management is viewed as less benevolent than human management, the detrimental effect this might have on trust, and if this potential negative relationship is exacerbated in contexts that demand empathy, where employees will be more sensitive to AI's incapability to experience emotions. In doing so, we make several contributions to the literature on trust, emotions, algorithms, and AI adoption. First, we extend interpersonal trust theory (Mayer & Davis, 1999; Mayer et al., 1995) to explain the emerging leader-subordinate relationship between machine and human by considering the perspective of employees who are the subjects of AI management. Second, we draw insights from the emotions as social information (EASI) model (Van Kleef, 2008; van Kleef & CÃ´té, 2022; see also Elfenbein, 2023; Scherer & Moors, 2019) to shed light on the important role that perceived benevolence plays in employee trust of AI management. Third, we contribute to the growing research on AI adoption in the workplace by highlighting the important role that emotional context plays in the acceptance of AI management.

use: Relative Power. We propose that the power dynamic between senders and receivers significantly influences how senders craft their messages. Specifically, when senders possess greater power than receivers, they are more likely to emphasize their own values. Conversely, when senders hold less power, they tend to be more attuned to the need for aligning their messages with the values of the receivers. Social psychological research suggests that power increases self-focus and reduces the perceived need to consider the perspectives of others, as it reduces dependence and imposes cognitive demands that limit perspective-taking (Fiske, 1993; Galinsky, Magee, Inesi, & Gruenfeld, 2006). As such, powerful senders are more inclined to foreground their own values in their communications. For instance, imagine a senior consultant inviting a junior colleague to join a pro bono project for a nonprofit organization. If the senior consultant is genuinely motivated by social impact, she is likely to highlight the chance to “make a difference,” even if the junior consultant is more interested in the reputational advantages of the assignment.
text: Relative power. First, we propose that the relative power between senders and receivers will shape which approach senders take in their presentations. When senders have higher power than receivers, they will be more likely to focus inward on their own values; when senders have lower power than receivers, they will be more sensitive to the importance of tailoring their presentations to receivers' values. Social psychological research shows that possessing power tends to increase individuals' attention to their own values, as power implies less dependence on others and imposes demands that limit attentional resources for taking others' perspectives (Fiske, 1993; Galinsky, Magee, Inesi, & Gruenfeld, 2006). Consequently, when senders are more powerful than receivers, we expect that they will tend to focus on their own values. For example, consider a senior consultant inviting a junior consultant to join a pro bono consulting project for a nonprofit organization. If the senior consultant genuinely cares about the nonprofit, she is likely to emphasize the opportunities to make a difference, even if the junior consultant is more concerned about reputational benefits.

use: Luther’s concept of calling redefined the nature of work by elevating it from a burdensome necessity to a sacred duty. He recast labor as a divine offering, imbuing everyday work with spiritual significance. Building on this foundation, John Calvin and others refined the idea further, emphasizing the uniquely personal dimension of one's calling. Unlike Luther, who associated calling primarily with one’s social station (Weber, 1930: 85), Calvin argued that a true calling stems from one’s distinct, God-given talents and abilities (Hardy, 1990: 66). A calling, in this view, is discovered at the intersection of individual gifts and service to humanity. Each person, therefore, bears a profound responsibility to identify and commit to their particular calling: “For as God bestows any ability or gift upon any of us, he binds us to such as have need of us and as we are able to help” (Calvin, 1574: 307). As Weber (1930: 106) summarized, “For everyone without exception God's Providence has prepared a calling, which he should profess and in which he should labour. And this calling is . . . God's commandment to the individual to work for the divine glory.”
text: Luther's concept of calling elevated work by transforming it from a necessary evil into a divine offering.
