While exploring the behavioral dimensions of conflict is undoubtedly valuable, we also encourage future research to address a key limitation of our study: the need to directly compare the behavioral content of conflict with individuals’ perceptions of it, both within and across task episodes. Might it be that people’s overall evaluations of conflict are shaped more by emotional intensity or unresolved tensions than by the actual frequency or nature of conflict behaviors? A systematic set of studies investigating the relationship between observed conflict behaviors and perceived conflict would meaningfully bridge the gap between behavioral insights and the predominantly perception-oriented approaches in conflict research (Pondy, 1967). Such work could shed light on the origins of perceptual asymmetries (Jehn et al., 2010) and deepen our understanding of how conflicts emerge and escalate (Shah et al., 2021).

Additionally, future investigations may benefit from broader sampling across individuals and team configurations, which would enable researchers to explore a wider range of individual and group-level influences. For instance, individual traits such as agreeableness may impact the likelihood of expressing conflict, while traits like conscientiousness may be associated with acting upon perceived conflict from others. At the team level, established conflict norms may shape how individuals perceive and interpret their conflict experiences (Jehn & Mannix, 2001). Exploring these individual and collective dynamics could offer a more comprehensive understanding of conflict in teams.
text: Although studying the behavioral elements of conflict is a fruitful endeavor, we also call for future research to address a limitation of our study by directly comparing the behavioral content and perception of each conflict, both separately and across task episodes. Could it be that individuals’ summary judgments of conflict are more heavily influenced by emotional or unresolved conflict as opposed to the actual occurrence of conflict? A robust series of studies linking conflict behaviors and perceptions would provide an important link between conflict behaviors and the dominant perception-based approach (Pondy, 1967). We speculate these studies may help reveal the underlying bases of perceptual asymmetries (Jehn et al., 2010) and conflict origins (Shah et al., 2021). Future research investigations may also consider sampling a greater number of individuals and team arrangements to enable incorporation of other individual- or team-level factors. For example, perhaps individual differences are linked to the propensity to engage in sending conflict experiences (e.g., agreeableness) or the propensity to take action based on receiving conflict experiences (e.g., conscientiousness). Alternatively, perhaps team conflict norms may influence an individual’s perceptions of their experiences (Jehn & Mannix, 2001).

use: Psychological ownership refers to a mental state in which an individual perceives a particular object—whether tangible or intangible—as “mine” (Pierce et al., 2001; see also Pierce, Kostova, & Dirks, 2003). This sense of ownership holds important implications for how individuals derive meaning from their experiences (Pierce et al., 2001, 2003) and provides a useful lens for understanding how legatees are affected by a legacy. In our theorizing, we explore circumstances under which a legatee comes to perceive the legacy as their own—claiming it as “mine.” At the same time, we consider an alternative possibility, wherein a legatee explicitly and intentionally regards the legacy as “not mine.” Scholars have termed this contrasting mindset psychological disownership—a condition in which an individual deliberately distances themselves from or disavows a particular object (Brown et al., 2005). Psychological ownership and psychological disownership exemplify what researchers have called bivariate—rather than bipolar—constructs. This distinction reflects the fact that the two are only moderately negatively related and possess distinct underlying mechanisms and correlates (Cacioppo, Gardner, & Berntson, 1997). Similar examples of bivariate constructs can be found in the relationships between positive and negative affect (Watson & Tellegen, 1985), individualism and collectivism (Triandis, 1996), engagement and burnout (Schaufeli et al., 2002), and voice and silence (Sherf, Parke, & Isaakyan, 2021).
text: Psychological ownership reflects a state of mind in which an individual feels that some target of ownership—”whether material or immaterial—”is "mine" (Pierce et al., 2001; see also Pierce, Kostova, & Dirks, 2003). Psychological ownership has significant implications for meaning (Pierce et al., 2001, 2003) and will help us understand how legatees are impacted by the legacy itself. Our theorizing will describe a circumstance wherein a legatee comes to view the legacy as "mine." However, we will also describe what would occur when a legatee views the legacy—”actively and explicitly—”as "not mine." Scholars have described this phenomenon as psychological disownership—”a state of mind in which an individual actively disavows some target (Brown et al., 2005). Psychological ownership and disownership are examples of constructs that are more accurately described as "bivariate" rather than "bipolar," because their negative association is more moderate and their nomological networks more distinct (see Cacioppo, Gardner, & Berntson, 1997). Other examples of bivariate constructs include positive and negative affect (Watson & Tellegen, 1985), individualism and collectivism (Triandis, 1996), engagement and burnout (Schaufeli, Salanova, González-Romá, & Bakker, 2002), and voice and silence (Sherf, Parke, & Isaakyan, 2021).

Drawing on the intrinsic motivation perspective, it becomes evident that merely having creative role models in the workplace does not automatically lead employees to demonstrate creativity. Their exposure to creative peers does not ensure they will actually apply the observed strategies or actively participate in creative tasks. For employees to exhibit high levels of creativity, they must also possess strong intrinsic motivation (Amabile, 1996; Fox, 1983). This perspective further emphasizes that supervisory behaviors play a critical role: controlling behaviors tend to suppress, whereas informational behaviors tend to enhance both intrinsic motivation and creative output (George & Zhou, 2001; Oldham & Cummings, 1996). 

Building on this, the current study integrates social cognitive theory with the intrinsic motivation perspective to propose that employees’ creativity is jointly shaped by the presence of creative coworkers—who provide opportunities for observational learning—and supervisors’ behaviors, which influence intrinsic motivation. Specifically, the effectiveness of creative coworkers in fostering creativity depends on the nature of supervisory behavior. In particular, for coworker creativity to positively influence an employee’s own creativity, supervisors should either refrain from excessive monitoring or offer developmental feedback. Put differently, either of these two supervisory approaches moderates the relationship between exposure to creative coworkers and individual creative performance.
text: On the basis of the intrinsic motivation perspective, it becomes clear that the presence of creative models may not always lead employees to exhibit creativity because it does not guarantee that the employees will actually use the strategies they have acquired and engage in creative activities. For them to actually exhibit high levels of creativity, they also need to have high levels of intrinsic motivation (Amabile, 1996; Fox, 1983). Moreover, this perspective suggests that supervisor behaviors that are controlling inhibit, whereas those that are informational facilitate intrinsic motivation and creativity (George & Zhou, 2001; Oldham & Cummings, 1996). Thus, the present study integrates social cognitive theory and the intrinsic motivation perspective in arguing that the presence of creative coworkers (which provides employees with the opportunity to observe and learn creativity skills and strategies) and supervisor behaviors (which affect intrinsic motivation) jointly influence employees' creativity. More specifically, for the presence of creative coworkers to have a positive relation with creativity, supervisors should either not exert close monitoring or provide developmental feedback. In other words, each of these two supervisor behaviors interacts with the presence of creative coworkers to affect creativity.

We contend that, while deonance theory does not explicitly address whether third parties should respond to observed mistreatment with empathy toward the victim, such a response aligns with the underlying spirit of the theory. Deonance is characterized as an emotionally charged psychological state, activated by a sense of moral obligation, norms, and ethical considerations (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008). Prior discussions of deonance in the context of personal moral transgressions suggest it can give rise to various moral emotions beyond anger, including guilt and shame (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010). Extending this line of reasoning, the emergence of empathy as a manifestation of deonance is a natural progression—especially considering that empathy itself constitutes a heightened affective state (Batson et al., 2002). Furthermore, empathic responses are compatible with deonance theory’s focus on evolutionary adaptiveness (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Empathy plays an evolutionarily adaptive role by fostering care for offspring, family members, and others critical to the family’s well-being (Batson et al., 2002). Accordingly, we propose the following:

Hypothesis 3: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively associated with empathy toward the victim.
text:
We argue that, although the letter of deonance theory is silent on whether third parties should react to observing mistreatment with empathy toward the victims, this prediction is consistent with the spirit of the theory. The psychological state of deonance is described as an emotionally charged state of arousal that is activated by moral norms, directives, and considerations (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008). Discussions of deonance in the context of one’s own moral violations argue that it can manifest in moral emotions other than anger, such as guilt and shame (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010). The notion that deonance can also manifest as empathy is a straightforward extension of such arguments—particularly because empathy is itself a heightened state of arousal (Batson et al., 2002). Moreover, empathic reactions can be reconciled with deonance theory’s emphasis on evolutionary adaptiveness (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Empathy is itself adaptive, as it encourages caring for offspring, family members, and those upon whom the family relies (Batson et al., 2002). We therefore predict:
Hypothesis 3: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively related to empathy toward the victim.

use: Anderson and Gerbing (1991) illustrated their method using five personality constructs: energetic, impulse purchaser, thrill-seeking, avoids complexity, and unreflective—each represented by seven measurement items. They utilized two independent groups of judges, with each group comprising 20 undergraduate students. Initially, judges were provided with a sheet listing the definitions of the five constructs in random order. For instance, one definition read: “Thrill Seeking. The thrill-seeking person enjoys stimulating and exciting activities, even if they involve some danger” (p. 736, emphasis in original). Separately, judges were given a second sheet containing all 35 items, also arranged randomly. An example item stated, “______ I take dares just for fun” (p. 735). Judges were instructed to fill in the blank with the construct—energetic, impulse purchaser, thrill-seeking, avoids complexity, or unreflective—that they felt best represented the meaning of the item. While Anderson and Gerbing (1991) did not report complete results for the 35 items or the five resulting scales, they did provide evidence that PSA and CSV values significantly predicted item factor loadings in a subsequent confirmatory factor analysis.
text: Anderson and Gerbing (1991) demonstrated their approach using five personality constructs: energetic, impulse purchaser, thrill-seeking, avoids complexity, and unreflective, each of which was measured with seven items. Two samples of judges were employed, with each sample consisting of 20 undergraduates. The judges were shown the definitions of the five constructs on a sheet of paper, presented in random order. For example, one definition was presented as "Thrill Seeking. The thrill-seeking person enjoys stimulating and exciting activities, even if they involve some danger" (p. 736, emphasis in original). The judges were then shown the 35 items on a separate sheet of paper, again presented in random order. For example, one item was presented as "______ I take dares just for fun" (p. 735). Judges were then asked to fill in the blank with either energetic, impulse purchaser, thrill-seeking, avoids complexity, or unreflective, depending on which construct they believed was represented by the item. Anderson and Gerbing (1991) did not present full results for either the 35 items or the five scales, though they did show that PSA and CSV values predicted items' factor loadings in a subsequent confirmatory factor analysis.

use: A notable gap in impression management research lies in understanding how self-promotion affects the behavior of the self-promoter—particularly in contexts marked by high uncertainty and autonomy. In such environments, many workers find it increasingly difficult to anticipate outcomes due to limited information or a lack of clear situational awareness, yet they are still expected to produce results based on their own decisions and actions (Ashford et al., 2018; Cropanzano et al., 2023). To thrive under these conditions, individuals are encouraged to actively seek feedback, defined as “a subset of information available to individuals in their work environment” (Ashford & Cummings, 1983, p. 372), while simultaneously managing the impressions they convey to others (e.g., Bourgoin & Harvey, 2018; Petriglieri et al., 2019). Given that feedback loops play a central role in image-sensitive settings, research must move beyond an exclusive focus on how tactics like self-promotion are perceived by observers. Social interactions are increasingly recognized as rich sources of information (Wheatley et al., 2024), and examining the reciprocal dynamics of impression management can uncover how self-promotion shapes not only external perceptions but the behavior and sensemaking of the self-promoter. Such a perspective is essential for explaining why individuals take on the inherent social risks of self-promotion—and what this reveals about the complex interrelation between image management and information exchange in organizational life.
text: A gap in impression management research is the effect of self-promotion on the self-promoter's behavior, especially in environments characterized by high uncertainty and high autonomy. An increasing number of workers struggle to predict work outcomes due to insufficient information or a lack of situational understanding, but they are required to achieve results based on their own actions and choices (Ashford et al., 2018; Cropanzano et al., 2023). To succeed in such settings, they are expected to actively seek out feedback, defined as "a subset of information available to individuals in their work environment" (Ashford & Cummings, 1983, p. 372), yet must also carefully manage their image (e.g., Bourgoin & Harvey, 2018; Petriglieri et al., 2019). Since feedback loops are crucial in image-sensitive situations, we need to go beyond the one-dimensional focus on others' perceptions of tactics such as self-promotion. Given the information-rich nature of social interactions (Wheatley et al., 2024), examining the reciprocal effects of impression management can reveal key influences between actors and targets. This approach is vital for understanding why individuals engage in socially risky behaviors like self-promotion and its broader implications for information exchange in business settings.

use: Reputation and status play critical roles in shaping how audiences evaluate organizations. Although both concepts capture societal judgments about what is deemed valuable (Goode, 1978; Weber, 1978; Wilson, 1985), they differ in their sources and in the mechanisms through which they influence audience perceptions (Washington & Zajac, 2005; Jensen & Roy, 2008; Chandler et al., 2013; Sorenson, 2014). Reputation is typically understood as an external audience’s perception of how well an organization’s past actions or performance align with specific role expectations (Rindova et al., 2005; Lange, Lee, & Dai, 2011; Jensen, Kim, & Kim, 2012; Sharkey, Pontikes, & Hsu, 2022). As such, reputation serves as a signal that enables audiences to anticipate an organization’s future behavior or results in particular contexts (Jensen & Roy, 2008; see also Wilson, 1985). In contrast, status refers to the prestige an organization possesses by virtue of its position within a social hierarchy (Jensen, 2006; Sauder, Lynn, & Podolny, 2012; Piazza & Castellucci, 2014). It is often derived from affiliation with high-status categories, rather than from demonstrated performance or outcomes (Malter, 2014; Sharkey, 2014; Sorenson, 2014). Consequently, whereas reputation informs evaluations in domain-specific ways, status tends to exert a more generalized influence on how audiences assess organizations (Jensen, Kim, & Kim, 2011; Chandler et al., 2013).
text: Reputation and status both shape how audiences evaluate organizations. Although both constructs reflect societal views of what is considered valuable (Goode, 1978; Weber, 1978; Wilson, 1985), they differ in how organizations acquire them and how they influence audience assessments (Washington and Zajac, 2005; Jensen and Roy, 2008; Chandler et al., 2013; Sorenson, 2014). Reputation is often defined as the perception of an organization based on how well its past performance or behaviors have met specific role expectations of external audiences (Rindova et al., 2005; Lange, Lee, and Dai, 2011; Jensen, Kim, and Kim, 2012; Sharkey, Pontikes, and Hsu, 2022). Accordingly, reputation helps audiences to predict an organization's future performance or behavior within certain domains (Jensen and Roy, 2008; see also Wilson, 1985). Status, in contrast, is a form of prestige that accumulates from an organization's standing in a social hierarchy (Jensen, 2006; Sauder, Lynn, and Podolny, 2012; Piazza and Castellucci, 2014). It is often gained through membership in esteemed categories, without necessarily reflecting past performance or behaviors (Malter, 2014; Sharkey, 2014; Sorenson, 2014). Thus, unlike reputation, status shapes audience evaluations more broadly (Jensen, Kim, and Kim, 2011; Chandler et al., 2013).

use: Machine learning (ML) operates through algorithmic pattern recognition and inference, often with minimal explicit instruction or adherence to traditional scientific frameworks (Bishop, 2006; Samuel, 1959). Its strengths primarily lie in the capacity to rapidly iterate through models, testing numerous combinations of linear and nonlinear predictors simultaneously. This allows ML to uncover associations in data that may be overlooked by researchers working solely with a priori, theory-driven—sometimes rigidly entrenched—hypotheses. The appeal of ML lies in its superior predictive power and its capacity for impartial, data-driven analysis, often outperforming human-devised, theory-bound approaches (Tshitoyan et al., 2019). In essence, ML has the potential to predict outcomes more effectively than current theory-based science. Additionally, it opens the door to unexpected, serendipitous discoveries (Tshitoyan et al., 2019). Importantly, we encourage readers to reflect on the level of tolerance for surprising or unanticipated results that may be appropriate for their specific research questions as we outline each broad category of ML. Generally, the likelihood that an ML model will yield surprising insights depends significantly on the algorithm selected—particularly whether key predictors or classifications are imposed by the researcher or emerge organically through bottom-up methods applied to complex, high-dimensional data.
text: ML relies on algorithmic patterns and inference, often with little explicit instruction or allegiance to scientific doctrine (Bishop, 2006; Samuel, 1959). The strengths of ML are largely a function of its ability to quickly iterate models, comparing multiple combinations of linear and nonlinear predictors in tandem to identify associations in the data that could be missed by scientists relying exclusively on a priori, theoretically informed—”and at times dogmatically calcified—”hypotheses. The promises of ML analytics are thus that they surpass human-generated approaches in terms of predictive accuracy and dispassionate strategy (Tshitoyan et al., 2019). In short, ML can outpredict what theory-driven science is currently capable of. Moreover, ML can also offer unprecedented possibilities for serendipitous discovery (Tshitoyan et al., 2019). Notably, we suggest that the reader consider the appropriate level of tolerance for surprise results for a given research question as we describe each general category of ML. In general, the actual likelihood of an ML algorithm returning surprise results is impacted by the choice of algorithm deployed (e.g., whether the predictors or categories within a model are predetermined by the researcher, or emerge through bottom-up approaches to structuring complex, multidimensional data).

use: Research on the impact of expressing gratitude in the workplace has expanded significantly in recent years. One line of inquiry has demonstrated that employees benefit from receiving gratitude. For example, Lee et al. (2019) found that employees who are thanked by colleagues tend to be more engaged at work the following day. Other studies have linked the receipt of gratitude to greater energy among leaders (Sheridan & Ambrose, 2022), higher job satisfaction (Ritzenhöfer et al., 2017), and an enhanced sense of prosocial impact (Ni et al., 2022) among employees. Shifting focus to the other side of the gratitude exchange, research has also uncovered positive effects for those who express gratitude. Outside of organizational settings, this work shows that expressing gratitude enhances the expresser’s perception of their relationship quality with the recipient (Lambert et al., 2010) and strengthens their ability to navigate interpersonal conflict constructively (Lambert & Fincham, 2011). Within the realm of organizational research—although still relatively limited—evidence suggests that expressing gratitude can influence how the expresser is perceived by others. For instance, gratitude expressions have been associated with increased perceptions of job candidates’ friendliness (Percival & Pulford, 2020) and leaders’ trustworthiness (Ritzenhöfer et al., 2017).
text: Research on the impact of gratitude expression in the workplace has grown dramatically in recent years. In one stream of research, scholars have shown that employees benefit when they receive gratitude. For instance, Lee et al. (2019) found that employees who receive gratitude from their colleagues tend to be more engaged at work the next day. Other research has linked received gratitude to increased energy in a sample of leaders (Sheridan & Ambrose, 2022) as well as job satisfaction (Ritzenhöfer et al., 2017) and perceived prosocial impact (Ni et al., 2022) in samples of employees. Turning to the other side of the expresser–recipient relationship, scholars have also shown benefits for the expresser. Outside the workplace, this literature has shown that expressions of gratitude improve the expresser’s perceptions of their relationship with the recipient (Lambert et al., 2010) and the expresser’s capacity for constructive conflict resolution (Lambert & Fincham, 2011). Organizational research, while more limited, has shown that expressions of gratitude influence how the expresser is perceived. For example, expressions of gratitude have been linked to perceptions of job candidates’ friendliness (Percival & Pulford, 2020) and leaders’ trustworthiness (Ritzenhöfer et al., 2017).

use: The second assumption is that behaviors within a given dimension are substitutable—that is, they are viewed as interchangeable expressions of the same underlying construct. However, research challenges this notion by highlighting substantial conceptual and empirical variation in both the visibility (Carpenter et al., 2017) and severity (e.g., Robinson & Bennett, 1995) of behaviors associated with interpersonal deviance (ID) and organizational deviance (OD). For instance, OD behaviors span a broad spectrum, ranging from relatively minor acts like daydreaming or withholding effort to more serious violations such as theft or the use of alcohol and illegal drugs in the workplace (Bennett & Robinson, 2000; Evans et al., 2023). As a result, two employees may receive identical OD scores despite engaging in qualitatively different behaviors—one frequently arriving late, and another frequently stealing company property. A similar gradient is present in ID behaviors, which range from less severe actions like teasing a colleague to more serious offenses such as making derogatory comments about someone’s race, religion, or ethnicity. These examples underscore the potential for ambiguity in interpreting deviance scores, since they can obscure significant differences in the nature and implications of deviant acts. When such variability is overlooked, researchers and practitioners risk missing important theoretical insights and practical nuances that are crucial for understanding and addressing workplace deviance effectively.
text: The second assumption is that behaviors within a dimension are substitutable, such that they are equivalent forms of the same underlying construct. Research also gives reasons to question this assumption by showing that behaviors representing ID and OD show considerable conceptual and empirical variability in observability (Carpenter et al., 2017) and severity (e.g., Robinson & Bennett, 1995). For example, OD behaviors range from minor actions like daydreaming and withholding effort to extreme violations like taking property and using alcohol or illegal drugs at work (Bennett & Robinson, 2000; Evans et al., 2023). Thus, two individuals could have the same OD score even if one employee rates frequent engagement in "coming in late to work" and the other rates frequent engagement in "taking property from work." A similar continuum exists for ID, which includes minor infractions like "making fun of someone" to more severe offenses such as "making derogatory ethnic, religious, or racial remarks," resulting in the same ambiguous interpretation of observed ID scores. Given that workers may engage in such disparate forms of deviance, ignoring this variability is unlikely to help either scholars or organizations understand the complete theoretical or practical picture.

Given these conflicting theoretical perspectives, I propose that the predictions offered by motivation theories, Cognitive Evaluation Theory (CET)/Self-Determination Theory (SDT), and creativity theory will be more strongly challenged as the intensity of pay-for-performance (PFP) increases. Traditional motivation theories—most notably expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964)—support Gerhart and Fang’s (2014) earlier assertion that PFP must be sufficiently intense to trigger stronger extrinsic motivation, which can then be combined with intrinsic interest in creative tasks to enhance creative performance. In line with this reasoning, Gneezy and Rustichini (2000) found that performance peaked under moderate to high levels of PFP intensity, leading them to conclude, “Pay enough or don’t pay at all.” However, it is important to note that their findings were based on non-intrinsically rewarding tasks (e.g., GMAT or IQ tests), for which most theories consistently predict a positive effect of PFP incentives.

In contrast, SDT and traditional creativity theory suggest that increased PFP intensity is likely to have a negative impact on performance, especially for creative tasks. Stronger PFP schemes can intensify perceptions of external control, which in turn diminishes intrinsic motivation—a vital driver of creativity—and ultimately reduces creative performance. Along these lines, Ariely et al. (2009) argued that high levels of incentive can shift cognitive functioning “from ‘automatic’ to ‘controlled’” processes (p. 452), further disrupting the spontaneity and openness often required for creative work.
text: In light of these conflicting theoretical perspectives, I suggest that the predictions of motivation theories, CET/SDT, and creativity theory regarding creative performance will be more sharply contradicted when PFP intensity is higher. Traditional motivation theories, especially expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964), align with Gerhart and Fang’s (2014) former concern that PFP intensity should be strong enough to prompt stronger extrinsic motivation that can be additively combined with intrinsic interests in creative tasks, resulting in higher creative performance. Consistent with this, Gneezy and Rustichini (2000) found that performance was the highest under mid- and high-level PFP intensity, prompting them to state, “Pay enough or don’t pay at all.” However, their findings were for noninteresting tasks (e.g., GMAT/IQ exam), for which all theories agree there is a positive effect of PFP. By contrast, SDT and traditional creativity theory predict that higher PFP intensity is more detrimental to performance, especially in creative tasks, because a stronger PFP scheme likely arouses a stronger perception of being controlled, which will more severely undermine one’s intrinsic motivation and thus creative performance. Similarly, Ariely et al. (2009) argued that higher incentives drive one’s “mental processes from ‘automatic’ to ‘controlled’” (p. 452).

use: More formally, we account for the inverted U-shaped relationship by examining how lower levels of distinctiveness affect both classification and valuation benefits (see Table 3, dashed line). As previously theorized, classification benefits—referring to a category’s ability to function as a meaningful common denominator—tend to decline as heterogeneity increases. However, when a focal category shares elements with adjacent or well-known categories, it becomes easier for both actors and audiences to grasp its meaning (Lo et al., 2020; Taeuscher et al., 2022). Prior research shows that entrepreneurs often turn to familiar categories to enhance comprehensibility and comparability (Hargadon & Douglas, 2001; Kennedy, 2008; Navis & Glynn, 2010). For instance, the emergence of the “e-cigarettes” category benefited from its association with traditional “cigarettes,” making it more readily intelligible—even though its distinct products targeted different consumer groups and social contexts (Hsu & Grodal, 2021). A similar dynamic played out with hot yoga: its significant overlap with Bikram yoga enabled practitioners to quickly interpret the category, despite the broader variety of practices it encompassed. Consequently, when a category is less distinctive, it can accommodate greater internal heterogeneity before its classification benefits begin to erode—specifically, before it starts to be seen as ambiguous or incoherent. This dynamic shifts the heterogeneity–classification benefits curve further to the right.
text: More formally, we explain this inverted U-shaped relationship by considering how lower degrees of distinctiveness influence both classification and valuation benefits (see Table 3, dashed line). Recall that we theorized that classification benefits—that is, how well a category establishes a useful common denominator—decrease with heterogeneity. However, when a focal category shares elements with other related categories, actors and audiences understand what the category is about with greater ease (Lo et al., 2020; Taeuscher et al., 2022). As prior studies have shown, entrepreneurial actors often leverage familiar categories to establish comparability and comprehensibility (Hargadon & Douglas, 2001; Kennedy, 2008; Navis & Glynn, 2010). For example, leveraging the familiarity of “cigarettes” made the “e-cigarettes” category easier to understand, even if its unique products catered to different audiences with distinct social practices (Hsu & Grodal, 2021). In the case of hot yoga, because of its substantial overlap with Bikram yoga, practitioners readily made sense of the new category despite its more diverse practices. Therefore, a less distinctive category tolerates more heterogeneity before classification benefits begin to drop—that is, when the category becomes perceived as ambiguous or fragmented. This pushes the heterogeneity–classification benefits curve to the right.

use: With the footprint metaphor in mind, two key complexities arise when theorizing about legacies in the lower echelons and those inherited by legatees. The first concerns the attribution of contributions to a specific legator. Similar to other socially evaluative constructs, such attributions emerge through a process of social construction (Graffin, Pfarrer, & Hill, 2012; Lovelace, Bundy, Hambrick, & Pollock, 2018). This process tends to be more deliberately curated in the upper echelons, where the contributions of founders and CEOs are often amplified through internal communications, press releases, and media narratives that help craft a lasting image (Hayward, Rindova, & Pollock, 2004; Lovelace et al., 2018). Such narratives can obscure the reality that organizational contributions frequently stem from complex, collaborative team processes involving multiple actors (Gilson & Shalley, 2004; Kurtzberg & Amabile, 2001), or that successful implementation required the joint efforts of many individuals (Lu, Bartol, Venkataramani, Zheng, & Liu, 2019). In contrast, within the lower echelons—where less effort is made to manage such narratives—legatees may more readily challenge the attribution of credit, perceiving themselves as deserving partial recognition for the footprint credited to the legator.
text: With this footprint metaphor in mind, there are two complexities when theorizing about legacies in the lower echelons and legacies inherited by legatees. The first complexity centers on the ascription of contributions to a particular legator. As seen with other constructs of a social-evaluative nature, such ascriptions are the product of a social construction process (Graffin, Pfarrer, & Hill, 2012; Lovelace, Bundy, Hambrick, & Pollock, 2018). That process is likely to be more actively managed in the upper echelons, as the contributions offered by founders and CEOs are accompanied by internal messaging, press releases, and media coverage that create a lasting image (Hayward, Rindova, & Pollock, 2004; Lovelace et al., 2018). Such messaging may gloss over the reality that contributions are often the product of a complex team process in which multiple individuals had a hand (Gilson & Shalley, 2004; Kurtzberg & Amabile, 2001) or where multiple people were needed to achieve implementation (Lu, Bartol, Venkataramani, Zheng, & Liu, 2019). In the lower echelons, where that social construction process is less managed, legatees may be more willing to argue that they deserve some of the credit for the footprint ascribed to the legator.

use: The dominant theoretical framework for understanding how employee support programs foster affective organizational commitment draws from a core principle of social exchange theory: individuals tend to reciprocate the benefits they receive (Blau, 1964; Emerson, 1976; Homans, 1958). Scholars investigating perceived organizational support have applied this perspective to explain how affective commitment develops, suggesting that employees return organizational support with emotional dedication (Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). When organizations provide assistance through employee support programs, employees perceive this as an indication that the organization is committed to fulfilling both their material and socioemotional needs (Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). Such offerings are interpreted as expressions of organizational care and value toward employees’ well-being. In line with the reciprocity norm, this perception fuels employees’ motivation to reciprocate by strengthening their emotional attachment to the organization. Supporting this theory, cross-lagged longitudinal studies show that earlier perceptions of organizational support predict subsequent increases in affective commitment, but not the reverse (see Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). These findings suggest that when employee support programs successfully communicate organizational support, they are likely to generate enhanced affective commitment among employees.
text: The prevailing theoretical perspective for explaining how employee support programs cultivate affective organizational commitment is based on the central tenet of social exchange theory: individuals reciprocate what they receive (Blau, 1964; Emerson, 1976; Homans, 1958). Researchers studying perceived organizational support have drawn on this theory to explain the development of affective commitment to organizations, proposing that in exchange for receiving support from organizations, employees reciprocate with emotional dedication (Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). When organizations offer help and assistance through employee support programs, employees are assured that the organization is willing to meet their material and socioemotional needs (Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). Employees interpret this support as a signal that the organization values and cares about their well-being, and following the norm of reciprocity, they are motivated to reciprocate by developing a stronger emotional bond with the organization. Corroborating this hypothesis, cross-lagged longitudinal research indicates that earlier perceptions of support from an organization predicted increases in affective commitment to the organization, but not vice versa (see Rhoades & Eisenberger, 2002). This evidence suggests that when support programs enable employees to receive support, they are likely to respond with increased affective commitment to their organization.

use: We attribute this disappearance to the combined influence of three interrelated forces. First, early empirical studies found that the social characteristics of jobs—such as opportunities for interpersonal interaction and forming friendships—were relatively weak predictors of motivational outcomes (Hackman & Lawler, 1971; Hackman & Oldham, 1976, 1980). These underwhelming results led researchers to conclude—arguably too hastily—that social aspects were less significant than task-related features. Second, theorists from the social information processing perspective challenged this view, arguing that job perceptions and attitudes are shaped primarily by social cues from others, rather than being objectively determined by the structural features of work (Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978). This critique steered scholarly attention toward conceptually disentangling task characteristics from social influences. Researchers saw such separation as essential not only for demonstrating the objective nature of job design independent of social context, but also for adjudicating how job characteristics and social cues may separately and jointly influence employee perceptions, attitudes, motivation, and performance (Griffin, 1983, 1987). Third, the rise of the cognitive revolution in organizational research shifted the scholarly focus inward—toward understanding the mental processes of individual employees—thereby directing attention away from the social structures that underlie interpersonal connections at work (Locke & Latham, 2002).
text: We attribute this vanishing act to the interplay of three forces. First, scholars returned initial empirical results that social characteristics of jobs—”such as dealing with others and friendship opportunities—”were weak predictors of motivational outcomes (Hackman & Lawler, 1971; Hackman & Oldham, 1976, 1980). These disappointing findings led researchers to conclude, perhaps prematurely, that social characteristics were less important than task characteristics. Second, social information processing theorists launched a critique that job perceptions and attitudes are socially constructed by cues from other people, not objectively determined by structural characteristics of work (Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978). This critique motivated scholars to focus their conceptual attention on cleanly separating tasks from social cues. Scholars deemed this pure separation critical to proving that jobs have objective structural characteristics that exist independent of interpersonal relationships, and critical to adjudicating the debate by showing that job characteristics and social cues can independently and interactively influence perceptions, attitudes, motivation, and performance (Griffin, 1983, 1987). Third, the cognitive revolution had begun to dominate organizational scholarship, leaving work design researchers more interested in studying the psychological processes inside employees' heads than the social structures that created bonds between them (Locke & Latham, 2002).

Interests and values are not the only factors driving occupational stratification in personality traits. Personality traits are closely linked to a range of social, emotional, and behavioral skills (Soto et al., 2022), which different occupations demand to varying extents. As a result, individuals who possess higher levels of specific traits—and the skills they entail—are more likely to be selected for and retained in roles that align with those requirements. For instance, while many may aspire to leadership or managerial positions, only those with the necessary interpersonal and organizational competencies typically associated with such roles are likely to attain them. In fact, personality assessments are increasingly used as explicit tools in recruitment and selection processes. This practice has evolved into a rapidly expanding, multibillion-dollar industry and represents one of the most tangible commercial applications of personality research.

In addition, certain traits consistently correlate with higher job performance across a wide range of occupations. Traits such as conscientiousness and extraversion—and, to a lesser extent, emotional stability, openness, and agreeableness—are positively associated with workplace effectiveness (Judge et al., 2013; Wilmot & Ones, 2021). More prestigious or competitive occupations, which typically attract a larger applicant pool, often have the luxury of being more selective about such performance-related traits. This selectivity can further reinforce the stratification of personality traits across occupational categories.
text:
Interests and values may not be the only mechanisms that lead to occupational stratification in personality traits. Personality traits also come with different social, emotional, and behavioral skills (Soto et al., 2022) that occupations may require to different degrees. Therefore, people with higher levels of certain skills and associated traits are more likely to be selected for and retained at the jobs requiring these skills. For example, many of those interested in managerial roles may not end up in these roles due to lacking any number of skills typically expected of leaders. Often, personality trait assessments are explicitly used in the candidate selection processes. In fact, this is a growing multibillion dollar business field, offering one of the most direct commercial applications of personality research.
Moreover, certain personality traits tend to go with higher performance in most jobs, especially those in the conscientiousness or extraversion domains but also the domains of emotional stability, openness, and agreeableness (Judge et al., 2013; Wilmot & Ones, 2021). Because more prestigious jobs may often attract more applicants and can therefore afford to be more selective regarding performance-related traits, this may contribute to typical personality trait levels varying across jobs.

Temporary leaders bring with them a range of distinctive characteristics that can significantly influence unit functioning. First, their appointment is often formally announced by the organization (Mooney et al., 2017; Wiersema & Zhang, 2011), yet employees may perceive this announcement as an implicit signal that the organization was unable to adequately develop or select a suitable permanent successor (Ballinger & Marcel, 2010). This perception can foster a leadership vacuum, accompanied by uncertainty about who holds authority and ambiguity surrounding the unit’s future strategic direction. Additionally, while previous research indicates that temporary leaders generally possess formal decision-making authority, they often have less latitude in influencing major strategic initiatives compared to their permanent counterparts (Farquhar, 1995), and they are typically less inclined to implement substantial strategic changes (Mooney et al., 2013). 

Moreover, temporary leaders may be motivated to showcase their leadership potential in hopes of securing a permanent position (Alley, 2005). As a result, their efforts may be geared more toward sustaining immediate performance and minimizing disruption associated with the leadership transition (Ballinger & Marcel, 2010). These distinct qualities of temporary leaders are likely to prompt different responses from workgroups than those seen during transitions to permanent leadership.
text: Temporary leaders possess several novel characteristics relevant to unit functioning. To begin with, the appointment of a temporary leader is often communicated by the organization (Mooney et al., 2017; Wiersema & Zhang, 2011); however, a temporary leader may be interpreted by employees as a signal suggesting that the organization was not able to ensure the development and selection of a replacement leader (Ballinger & Marcel, 2010). As a result, there may be a leadership vacuum, with uncertainty regarding who is in charge, as well as the future strategic directions of the unit. In addition, while prior work suggests temporary leaders do have formal decision-making authority, they may have less managerial discretion over major strategic decisions than permanent leaders (Farquhar, 1995) and may be less likely to induce large strategic changes (Mooney et al., 2013). Furthermore, temporary leaders may look for opportunities to demonstrate capabilities associated with obtaining a permanent leadership position (Alley, 2005). Therefore, temporary leaders may focus more on minimizing the disruption of the turnover event in order to maximize immediate performance (Ballinger & Marcel, 2010). The unique characteristics of temporary leaders are likely to elicit different workgroup responses compared to permanent leader replacements.

use: Some psychologists have developed cognitive ability tests that yield smaller differences between demographic groups. For instance, the creators of the Siena Reasoning Test report reduced group differences in test scores, attributing this to minimized reliance on prior knowledge, limited language demands, and an increased use of graphical stimuli (Yusko et al., 2010). This suggests that varying the types of items used in a test may be an effective strategy for decreasing subgroup disparities. Beyond this, McDaniel (2018) identified three additional strategies: incorporating easier items, lowering the reliability of the test, and decreasing its cognitive saturation. However, when researchers present findings of reduced subgroup differences, it is often unclear which of these mechanisms contributed to the outcome. This ambiguity stems from the lack of systematic comparisons between tests that differ based on only one specific factor. As previously mentioned, one explanation could be that certain item types inherently reduce subgroup differences. Alternatively, researchers may—either deliberately or inadvertently—include items that are lower in difficulty or associated with reduced subgroup d, relative to the full range of potential test items. The goal of this article is to examine how removing items with high difficulty levels or high subgroup d values affects both the overall subgroup d of a test and its criterion-related validity.
text: Some psychologists report cognitive ability tests that produce scores with smaller group differences. For example, the authors of the Siena Reasoning Test report that their test results in reduced group differences because of reduced reliance on prior knowledge, reduced use of language, and increased use of graphical stimuli (Yusko et al., 2010). Thus, the use of different item types is one proposed strategy for reducing subgroup differences. McDaniel (2018) suggested three additional strategies: use easy items, make the test less reliable, and reduce the cognitive saturation of the test. Our sense is that when researchers report smaller subgroup differences for a test than are typically observed, it is unclear which mechanism produced the result, as systematic comparisons of tests differing only in one mechanism are not generally conducted. As noted above, one possibility is that certain item types produce smaller differences. Another possibility is that the researchers, intentionally or unintentionally, end up using items lower in difficulty or subgroup d than in a full range of possible items. Our goal in this article was to explore the effects of removing high-difficulty or high subgroup d items on a test's overall score subgroup d and criterion-related validity.

Research on stakeholder evaluations has largely operated under the implicit assumption that the broader environment in which firms and stakeholders interact provides them with reliable access to information about one another, as well as about other market and nonmarket actors. While it is well established that informational asymmetries persist—despite firms' efforts to disclose information either voluntarily or through regulatory mandates (Christmann & Taylor, 2006; Kim & Lyon, 2011)—prior studies have tended to emphasize the role of media as a crucial mechanism for bridging these asymmetries and shaping stakeholder assessments of firms (Pfarrer, Pollock, & Rindova, 2010). However, this emphasis typically overlooks a critical consideration: whether media information is, in fact, consistently available or trustworthy. In reality, media coverage of corporate entities varies significantly, particularly for smaller firms (Fang & Peress, 2009; Pollock, Rindova, & Maggitti, 2008), and its reliability fluctuates depending on contextual factors such as media censorship, political bias, and the prevalence of misinformation or “fake news” within a country (Enikolopov & Petrova, 2015; King, Pan, & Roberts, 2013).

Despite these challenges, extant research has yet to systematically examine whether and how the informational environment—defined as the availability, reliability, and substance of information about corporate organizations within a given society—influences the effects of firm–stakeholder interactions, impression management strategies, and reputation-building efforts. This raises an important and overlooked question: To what extent does the informational environment shape the role of firm–stakeholder interactions as alternative channels of information exchange, and how does it affect their impact on stakeholder evaluations?
text: At the same time, research on stakeholder evaluations has largely assumed that the environment in which firms and stakeholders interact facilitates their access to reliable information about each other, and about other market and nonmarket actors. Acknowledging that informational asymmetries (Stiglitz, 2000) persist despite mandated or voluntary information disclosure by firms (Christmann & Taylor, 2006; Kim & Lyon, 2011), prior research has emphasized that media information plays a critical role in reducing these information asymmetries and in shaping stakeholders’ evaluations of firms (Pfarrer, Pollock, & Rindova, 2010) without questioning whether media information is available or reliable. Yet, media coverage of corporate organizations varies, especially among smaller organizations (Fang & Peress, 2009; Pollock, Rindova, & Maggitti, 2008), and the reliability of media information varies with the level of media censorship, bias, and “fake news” in a country (Enikolopov & Petrova, 2015; King, Pan, & Roberts, 2013). However, research has not yet investigated whether the informational environment—the availability, reliability, and content of information about a corporate organization in society—affects the impact of firm–stakeholder interactions, impression management, and reputation-building efforts. Does the informational environment affect the importance of firm–stakeholder interactions as alternative channels of information exchange and their effects?

Mistreatment—used here as an umbrella term for harmful interpersonal interactions in the workplace (Hershcovis et al., 2020)—is both a pervasive and impactful feature of organizational life. A recent meta-analytic review reported that 34% of employees have personally experienced some form of mistreatment at work (Dhanani et al., 2021). Based on estimates linking such experiences to increases in absenteeism and declines in productivity, the review also calculated the financial cost of workplace mistreatment to fall between $691.70 billion and $1.97 trillion annually—a staggering burden for organizations.

Importantly, the same review found that 44% of employees reported witnessing mistreatment at work (Dhanani et al., 2021). Given that observing mistreatment is even more common than experiencing it directly, it is significant that a growing body of research has begun to focus on third-party perceptions of mistreatment—defined as observers’ evaluations of inappropriate behavior directed toward others (O’Reilly & Aquino, 2011). These behaviors may include abusive supervision (e.g., Mitchell et al., 2015), organizational injustice (e.g., O’Reilly et al., 2016), workplace incivility (e.g., Reich & Hershcovis, 2015), sexual harassment (e.g., Pierce et al., 2004), among other forms of misconduct. Despite the seemingly detached “bird’s-eye view” held by third parties, studies have shown that witnessing mistreatment meaningfully influences observers’ emotions, thought processes, and subsequent actions (e.g., Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Pierce et al., 2004; Reich & Hershcovis, 2015).
text:
Mistreatment—an umbrella term for harmful interpersonal interactions at work (Hershcovis et al., 2020)—is a common and consequential aspect of organizational life. A recent review examined the prevalence of mistreatment in the workplace, finding that 34% of employees had experienced it (Dhanani et al., 2021). Drawing on estimates linking mistreatment to absenteeism and lost productivity, the review suggested that mistreatment costs organizations somewhere between $691.70 billion and $1.97 trillion annually.

Notably, that same review suggested that 44% of employees had observed mistreatment at work (Dhanani et al., 2021). Given the higher prevalence of observing versus experiencing mistreatment, it is fortunate that a distinct literature has arisen on third-party perceptions of mistreatment—defined as observer assessments of inappropriate conduct toward another (O’Reilly & Aquino, 2011). Such conduct may take the form of abusive supervision (e.g., Mitchell et al., 2015), injustice (e.g., O’Reilly et al., 2016), incivility (e.g., Reich & Hershcovis, 2015), sexual harassment (e.g., Pierce et al., 2004), and other inappropriate behaviors. Despite the “bird’s-eye view” afforded to third parties, research has shown that observing mistreatment can shape third-party emotions, cognitions, and behaviors (e.g., Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Pierce et al., 2004; Reich & Hershcovis, 2015).

use: Accordingly, for employees with low core self-evaluations, perceived prosocial impact offers a positive experience that counteracts their negative self-perceptions, thereby mitigating emotional exhaustion. Since emotional exhaustion often stems from sustained attention to negative self-views (Best et al., 2005), perceived prosocial impact can serve as a protective buffer by redirecting focus away from these distressing internal narratives and adverse work conditions. It does so by heightening awareness of one’s favorable circumstances (Bartel, 2001) and reinforcing a sense of meaningful contribution to others (Midlarsky, 1991). As such, perceived prosocial impact may be especially instrumental in shielding employees with low core self-evaluations from emotional exhaustion. By fostering positive emotions and shifting attention toward one’s value to others, it reduces the effort needed for self-regulation—effort that would otherwise be taxing for individuals low in intrinsic motivation and self-regard. Conversely, for employees with high intrinsic motivation and strong core self-evaluations, the benefits of perceived prosocial impact are less pronounced, as they are already oriented toward the positive features of their work and identity, and typically experience self-regulation as less demanding. Therefore, we propose that perceived prosocial impact attenuates the negative influence of low intrinsic motivation and low core self-evaluations on emotional exhaustion.
text: Thus, for employees with low core self-evaluations, perceived prosocial impact will provide a positive experience that counters their negative self-concepts, reducing emotional exhaustion. Emotional exhaustion is often the result of excessive focus on negative self-views (Best et al., 2005), and perceived prosocial impact can buffer against emotional exhaustion by shifting attention away from these distressing self-views and work circumstances, increasing awareness of one's fortunate circumstances (Bartel, 2001) and positive contributions to others (Midlarsky, 1991). Perceived prosocial impact may thereby play a particularly important role in protecting against emotional exhaustion for employees with low core self-evaluations. By cultivating positive emotions and focusing attention on one's contributions to others, perceived prosocial impact makes self-regulation less effortful, preventing emotional exhaustion for employees with low intrinsic motivation and core self-evaluations, who would typically find self-regulation exhausting. On the other hand, because employees with high intrinsic motivation and core self-evaluations are already focused on positive aspects of their tasks and identities, and because they already find self-regulation less effortful, perceived prosocial impact has less relevance to influencing their experiences of emotional exhaustion. We therefore predict that perceived prosocial impact weakens the negative relationships of low intrinsic motivation and core self-evaluations with emotional exhaustion.

use: As the nature of work grows increasingly complex and fast-paced, individuals are now more frequently required to coordinate their efforts both within and across team boundaries (Davison et al., 2012; Zaccaro, Marks, & DeChurch, 2012). Excluding between-team interactions from analyses, therefore, limits our understanding of individuals' lived experiences in contemporary organizational settings. In today’s multifaceted business environment, teams often manage multiple, interdependent tasks, with different components being executed by different team members at varying times (Mathieu et al., 2017; Mathieu, Maynard, Rapp, & Gilson, 2008). Despite this reality, most existing conflict research implicitly assumes that teams operate on a single, unified task—such as collective decision-making—or that members jointly perform one or more activities (O’Neill, Allen, & Hastings, 2013). Such assumptions overlook the task and role differentiation that typically exist in multiteam systems. To more accurately capture the effects of conflict on individuals embedded in complex team arrangements—and to align theoretical explanations with the multilevel nature of the phenomenon—it is essential to consider the unique contours of conflict. These include the directionality of conflict (sending vs. receiving) and the scope or boundary across which it occurs (within-team vs. between-team), as individuals carry out distinct tasks in interdependent team contexts. Accordingly, we examine (a) the structural characteristics of individuals’ conflict experiences and (b) the conditions under which those experiences affect their task performance in real time.
text: as the nature of work becomes increasingly complex and dynamic, individuals are increasingly required to coordinate their efforts within and between teams (Davison et al., 2012; Zaccaro, Marks, & DeChurch, 2012). Omitting between-team interactions, thus, is limiting our understanding of the lived experience of individuals. Furthermore, in today’s complex business environment, teams often undertake multiple tasks, different components of which are performed by different team members at different points in time (Mathieu et al., 2017; Mathieu, Maynard, Rapp, & Gilson, 2008). However, much of the published conflict research has implicitly considered teams as working on a single task (e.g., a decision-making team) or as collectively completing one or more task activities (O’Neill, Allen, & Hastings, 2013). Therefore, to understand the different effects of conflict on individuals performing in more complex collectives and to align the levels of the phenomenon and theory, it is critical to account for unique conflict experiences, characterized by the directions (sending vs. receiving) and boundaries (within team vs. between team), as individuals perform various tasks in a multiteam context. Consequently, we consider (a) the structural elements of individual conflict experiences and (b) when and why those experiences shape their task performance in real time.

use: Employee voluntary turnover has long captured the attention of both practitioners and scholars, with sustained interest spanning over a century (Hom et al., 2017). In recent years, the focus of turnover research has expanded beyond the individual level (Griffeth et al., 2000; Hom et al., 2012; Rubenstein et al., 2018) to encompass broader levels of analysis, including teams, work units, and entire organizations (Hausknecht & Trevor, 2011; Nyberg & Ployhart, 2013; Shaw, 2011; van der Vegt et al., 2010). Of particular note is the growing scholarly attention to group turnover—defined as “employee departures that occur within groups,” such as “teams, work groups, or departments, which are often nested within a single organization and/or location” (Hausknecht & Trevor, 2011, p. 353, emphasis ours). This shift reflects increasing recognition of phenomena like "spin-out" teams forming new ventures, coordinated "lift-outs" moving to competing firms, and employees transitioning across organizations in "molecular" or "pack" formations (e.g., Agarwal et al., 2016; Groysberg & Abrahams, 2006; Marx & Timmermans, 2017; Munyon et al., 2011). Yet, despite this growing interest, research on group turnover and its developmental trajectory remains fragmented. This fragmentation hampers a systematic understanding of groups as a meaningful unit of analysis in turnover studies and limits our ability to identify and explain the antecedents and processes underlying collective departures (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000).
text: Employee voluntary turnover has intrigued practitioners and researchers for over a century (Hom et al., 2017). Recently, there has been a notable shift from a primarily individual-level focus (Griffeth et al., 2000; Hom et al., 2012; Rubenstein et al., 2018) to higher analytical levels, such as groups, units, and organizations (Hausknecht & Trevor, 2011; Nyberg & Ployhart, 2013; Shaw, 2011; van der Vegt et al., 2010). The surge of interest in group turnover, defined as "employee departures that occur within groups," including "teams, work groups, or departments, which are often nested within a single organization and/or location" (Hausknecht & Trevor, 2011, p. 353, emphasis ours), is particularly noteworthy, as burgeoning studies have shed light on "spin-out" teams leaving to start new ventures, collective "lift-outs" joining competing firms, and employees migrating in "molecules" or "packs" across organizations (e.g., Agarwal et al., 2016; Groysberg & Abrahams, 2006; Marx & Timmermans, 2017; Munyon et al., 2011). However, research on group turnover as well as its developmental process remains fragmented, which not only impairs studying groups as a distinct unit of reference with regard to turnover but also hinders our ability to explore its antecedents and developmental process (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000).

use: In this study, we propose that sharing a SRPN (Self-Revealing Personal Narrative) can interrupt the well-established association between disagreement and distrust. We define SRPNs as autobiographical narratives in which the speaker discloses an action or event that humbles them—often involving a past or present struggle that could be experienced as embarrassing. As such, SRPNs generate vulnerability by making the speaker susceptible to negative evaluation from others (Derlega et al., 1993; Kelly & McKillop, 1996; Laurenceau et al., 1998; Moon, 2000). We theorize that this overt willingness to face potential social cost signals a commitment to honesty, prompting listeners to perceive the speaker as more trustworthy. Specifically, we hypothesize that individuals who support their arguments by drawing on a personal, humbling experience will be viewed as more honest—and their information as more credible—than those who rely on impersonal forms of evidence, such as statistics or third-party anecdotes, which do not entail the same degree of personal risk. This proposition is supported by previous research showing that perceptions of honesty often align with perceptions of principled character (Sitkin & Roth, 1993), a trait fundamental to the development of trust (Butler, 1991; Mayer et al., 1995).
text: In the present work, we theorize that sharing a SRPN can disrupt the previously documented link between disagreement and distrust. We define SRPNs as stories that divulge an action or event that humbles the protagonist storyteller, for example, by revealing a current or past struggle that has the potential to cause embarrassment. Thus, SRPNs create vulnerability by exposing the speaker to others' negative judgment (Derlega et al., 1993; Kelly & McKillop, 1996; Laurenceau et al., 1998; Moon, 2000). We theorize that such a willingness to incur a potential interpersonal cost may lead listeners to infer that the speaker values honesty. We therefore hypothesize that people who support an argument by sharing a humbling experience from their own life will be seen as more honest, and the information they share as more accurate, than people who draw on impersonal evidence such as statistical data or stories about others (which do not expose them to the same cost). This theorizing is supported by prior work demonstrating that judging someone to be honest is associated with judging them to be highly principled (Sitkin & Roth, 1993), a characteristic that is an important antecedent to trust (Butler, 1991; Mayer et al., 1995).

use: Dating back to Darwin (1872), scholars have theorized that cognitive ability shapes verbal behavior (what people say), paraverbal behavior (how they say it), and nonverbal behavior (what people do, such as facial expressions; Borkenau & Liebler, 1995; Pennebaker & King, 1999). The influence strength across these behavioral channels may differ—for instance, cognitive ability might have a greater effect on verbal behavior than on paraverbal or nonverbal behavior, given that verbal expressions are generally considered to be more consciously regulated (Lakin, 2006). Beyond this theoretical distinction lies an important practical consideration: if cognitive ability also manifests in paraverbal and nonverbal behaviors, then machine learning (ML) models relying solely on verbal cues might overlook key information. Conversely, if cognitive ability is not conveyed through these other channels, then including paraverbal and nonverbal behaviors in ML models may introduce noise or bias. This issue is especially relevant in discussions of algorithmic interview (AVI) fairness, as facial recognition tools used to assess nonverbal behavior have been shown to yield lower accuracy for individuals with darker skin tones—particularly for Black and African American candidates (Harris et al., 2018). While we are not aware of research specifically investigating how cognitive ability correlates with verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal cues in employment interviews, such relationships have been explored in other contexts.
text: Stretching back to Darwin (1872), theories have posited that cognitive ability affects verbal (i.e., what people say), paraverbal (i.e., the delivery of their speech), and nonverbal behaviors (i.e., what people do, such as facial expressions; Borkenau & Liebler, 1995; Pennebaker & King, 1999). The relative strength of these paths may vary—for example, cognitive ability may influence verbal behavior more than paraverbal or nonverbal behavior because verbal behaviors are thought to be under more conscious control than other modes of behavior (Lakin, 2006). Beyond the conceptual question, this is an important practical question because if cognitive ability manifests as paraverbal and nonverbal behaviors, ML models using only verbal behaviors could be considered deficient. In contrast, if it does not, then ML models that include paraverbal and nonverbal behaviors could be considered contaminated. Further, many concerns about AVI fairness center on findings that facial recognition software for measuring nonverbal behaviors tends to be less accurate for people with darker skin tones and, specifically, Black and African American interviewees (Harris et al., 2018). Although we are unaware of studies examining how cognitive ability relates to verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors in employment interviews, these relationships have been examined in other settings.

use: The hostile emotions linked to psychological reactance are often accompanied by “energizing properties that drive individuals to engage in freedom-restoration” (Miron & Brehm, 2006, p. 4; see also Ashforth & Saks, 2000; S. S. Brehm & Brehm, 2013; Rosenberg & Siegel, 2018). Scholars have argued—and empirical work has shown—that one common way employees seek to maintain, reclaim, or assert control is by demonstrating greater initiative (Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Frese et al., 1996). This initiative typically comprises two key elements: individuals strive to direct their own actions and influence operational decisions that affect them, whether directly or indirectly (Ashforth, 1989; Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Frese, 1989). Personal initiative is closely tied to the desire for autonomy; it enables employees to step off the sidelines and adopt an active role, ensuring they are “taking control to make things happen rather than watching things happen” (Parker et al., 2010, p. 828). Consistent with this idea, studies find that a strong desire for control correlates with higher levels of initiative (Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Binnewies et al., 2009; Ouyang et al., 2019; Salanova & Schaufeli, 2008). Building on this line of research, we suggest that increased autonomy associated with side hustles—and the corresponding amplification of psychological reactivity—fosters proactive initiative within individuals’ primary full-time employment.
text: The feelings of hostility associated with psychological reactance are often accompanied by "energizing properties that drive individuals to engage in freedom-restoration" (Miron & Brehm, 2006, p. 4; see also Ashforth & Saks, 2000; S. S. Brehm & Brehm, 2013; Rosenberg & Siegel, 2018). Scholars have argued and demonstrated that one way employees attempt to maintain, regain, or seize control is by exercising greater initiative (Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Frese et al., 1996). This initiative generally has two components, wherein employees strive to be the captain of their own actions and to influence operating decisions that impact them directly or indirectly (Ashforth, 1989; Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Frese, 1989). Personal initiative is inextricably connected to a desire for autonomy; it removes the employee from the sidelines and ensures they are "taking control to make things happen rather than watching things happen" (Parker et al., 2010, p. 828). Supporting this proposal, research indicates that a desire for control is associated with higher initiative (Ashforth & Saks, 2000; Binnewies et al., 2009; Ouyang et al., 2019; Salanova & Schaufeli, 2008). In line with this prior research, we propose that elevated side-hustle autonomy—”and the corresponding increase in psychological reactivity—”promotes initiative in full-time work.

use: A substantial body of research highlights the diverse strategies individuals employ to signal their dedication to work. A central theme in this literature is how employees actively make their long working hours visible to colleagues and supervisors. These temporal displays of commitment are often positively reinforced, reflecting organizational norms that equate time investment with employee loyalty. For example, Perlow (1999) found that software engineers deliberately chose to work late at the office—rather than from home—to ensure their managers noticed their extended hours. Similarly, Anderson-Gough, Grey, and Robson (2001) showed that accounting trainees completed their timesheets with the intent of maximizing the number of reported hours. They often allocated time to client projects that still had budgetary allowance rather than to projects where the budget had been exhausted. This allowed them to create a formal, trackable record of their work effort, which they perceived as critical to cultivating favorable impressions of their commitment. These behaviors are consistent with Trefalt’s (2013) findings, which revealed that lawyers selectively concealed non-work obligations from certain colleagues, aiming to avoid casting doubt on their professional dedication.
text: A large body of research reveals the various practices and activities that individuals adopt to demonstrate dedication to their work. Most notably, this work details how employees find numerous ways to make long work hours visible to coworkers and managers. Such temporal projections of dedication are often rewarded in the workplace, reflecting organizational beliefs that time allocation reflects employee commitment. Perlow (1999) found that software engineers "chose" to work in the middle of the night in the office (rather than at home) to ensure that their managers knew they were working long hours. Anderson-Gough, Grey, and Robson (2001) revealed that accounting trainees filled out their time sheets with the goal of maximizing recorded hours. For instance, they assigned their work hours to client projects that still had budgeted money remaining, rather than to those that no longer had client funding. This created an official, visible record of the hours they had worked, which they believed was important in terms of creating favorable impressions of their commitment. These tactics echo Trefalt's (2013) findings that lawyers strategically withheld information about their non-work commitments from certain colleagues in hopes of preventing those coworkers from developing negative impressions about their dedication to the workplace.

use: True field experiments offer a compelling response to concerns about external validity by preserving the authenticity of both employees' experiences and the organizational phenomena under investigation. Nonetheless, these experiments frequently entail trade-offs between internal and external validity. Enhancing internal validity typically requires researchers to implement random assignment and deliberately manipulate variables—strategies that can inadvertently alter the core nature of the phenomenon and thereby compromise external validity (Argyris, 1975). In practice, achieving genuine random assignment in field settings proves challenging (Evans, 1976), and even when it is successfully executed, it may diminish the realism of the independent variable (Argyris, 1975). For instance, randomly assigning individuals to teams can result in artificial social groupings that lack the natural complementarity of skills, interpersonal chemistry, and self-selection commonly found in organically formed teams. Additionally, participants often become aware of the differential treatments being applied to others, which can shape their psychological and behavioral reactions (Cook & Campbell, 1979). The presence of experimenters may also introduce expectancy effects that influence participant behavior independently of the manipulated variable (Eden, 1984, 2003; Orne, 1962; Rosenthal, 1994). In capturing the essence of these limitations, Lawler (1977, p. 577) aptly observed that “the methodological requirements of traditional experiments fail to mesh with the realities of life in organizations.”
text: True field experiments transcend these concerns about external validity by maintaining fidelity to the employees and organizational phenomena of interest. However, true field experiments often involve trade-offs between internal and external validity. To maximize internal validity, researchers carry out random assignment and the deliberate manipulation of variables, which may change the nature of the phenomenon and thereby undermine external validity (Argyris, 1975). It is difficult to randomly assign participants to treatment groups in the field (Evans, 1976), and even when this is accomplished, it may affect the authenticity of the independent variable (Argyris, 1975). For example, randomly allocating people to teams may produce an artificial social situation that does not provide the complementary skills, interpersonal compatibility, and elements of self-selection that are present in natural team formation. Moreover, participants often become aware of the treatments given to participants in other groups, which can influence their psychological and behavioral responses (Cook & Campbell, 1979). Experimenters also introduce expectancies that can bias participants' responses apart from the variables being manipulated (Eden, 1984, 2003; Orne, 1962; Rosenthal, 1994). Summarizing these concerns, Lawler (1977, p. 577) noted that "the methodological requirements of traditional experiments fail to mesh with the realities of life in organizations."
