use: The Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) model (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007) differentiates between job demands and job resources. According to this framework, demands are aspects of work that incur physiological and/or psychological costs (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007, p. 312) and are typically viewed as potential sources of stress. In contrast, resources are elements that enhance motivation—such as promoting work engagement—because they aid in achieving work-related goals and foster personal growth, learning, and development (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007, p. 312). Importantly, job resources can contribute to improved health by mitigating the negative effects of health-impairing job demands. Among the most widely recognized job resources are social support and autonomy/job control (Semmer & Udris, 2004). Both of these promote, or inherently include, decision latitude—offering employees the opportunity to engage in self-directed behavior, such as utilizing personal strengths. When workers are granted the freedom to implement their own ideas or determine which tasks to prioritize (i.e., autonomy), the likelihood of applying personal preferences and individual strengths at work substantially increases. A similar dynamic can be expected when employees experience psychological safety within a supportive, non-judgmental work environment (i.e., social support), empowering them to express personal strengths and preferences. Therefore, autonomy and social support are well-aligned with the concept of thriving work characteristics, as both foster environments where employees are encouraged and enabled to flourish.
text: The Job Demands-Resources model (JD-R; Bakker and Demerouti 2007) distinguishes between demands and resources at work. Demands, as they describe them, are associated with certain physiological and/or psychological costs (Bakker and Demerouti 2007, p. 312) and are conceptualized as potential stressors. By contrast, resources are able to increase motivation (e.g., work engagement) as they are functional in achieving work goals, stimulate personal growth, learning, and development (Bakker and Demerouti 2007, p. 312). Subsequently, resources may lead to better health as they can alleviate the costs of health-impairing demands. Social support and autonomy/job control have become known as two central job resources (Semmer and Udris 2004). Both include and/or promote (decision) latitudes, which enable possibilities for individually preferred behavior (like applying individual character strengths). When employees have the possibility to use their own ideas in performing the work tasks or to decide for themselves what tasks to pursue (autonomy), the probability of applying individual preferences and strengths at work strongly increases. A similar effect is to be expected when employees feel safe to unfold personal preferences and strengths due to a supportive, non-condemning work environment (social support). Thus, the resources autonomy and social support comply with the concept of thriving work characteristics.

use: Anderson and Gerbing (1991) proposed their content validation method to address a practical challenge in scale development. They observed a recurring inefficiency in research where scholars would collect scale data, only to find—during confirmatory factor analysis—that items loaded onto unintended constructs. To mitigate this risk, they introduced a pretesting technique designed to anticipate how well items would perform in such analyses. In this method, judges are presented with multiple construct definitions alongside a set of items. Their task is to read each item and assign it to the construct definition they believe it best represents. Two indices are then computed to assess item validity: the proportion of substantive agreement (psa) and the substantive-validity coefficient (csv). These are calculated as follows: N is the total number of judges, nc is the number of judges who correctly sorted the item, and no is the maximum number of times the item was sorted into any incorrect construct. The psa ranges from 0 to 1, with 1 indicating unanimous correct classification by all judges. The csv ranges from -1 to 1, reaching -1 when all judges sort the item incorrectly and 1 when all sort it correctly, with none selecting an incorrect construct.
text: Anderson and Gerbing (1991) introduced their content validation approach for a practical reason. They noted the inefficiency created when scholars collected scale data, only to see the items load on unintended constructs in a confirmatory factor analysis. They therefore proposed a pretesting methodology that could be used to foreshadow how well items would perform in such analyses. Judges would be given a set of multiple construct definitions and a set of items. They would then be asked to read the items and sort them into the appropriate construct definition. Two indices—”the proportion of substantive agreement (psa) and the substantive-validity coefficient (csv)—”would then be calculated as shown below, where N is the total number of judges, nc is the number who sorted the item correctly, and no is the maximum number of times an item was sorted into any other construct in the set. The psa statistic ranges from 0 to 1, achieving the latter value when all judges classify an item correctly. The csv statistic ranges from -1 to 1, achieving the former value when no judges classify an item correctly and all do so incorrectly, and the latter value when all judges classify an item correctly and none do so incorrectly.

use: When engaging in reciprocal actions aimed at achieving fair treatment, employees tend to contribute work resources that strike an optimal balance—restoring the perceived fairness of exchanges with the group and/or specific members without undermining their own self-interest (Flynn & Brockner, 2003). Among the spectrum of possible contributions, individual task performance—defined as role-prescribed efforts that result in goods and services essential for group success—stands out as one of the most immediate and observable forms of reciprocation (Van Scotter et al., 2000). As a universally recognized resource with clearly defined evaluation criteria, exceptional task performance not only generates direct benefits for the group, its leaders, and fellow members (Colquitt et al., 2013; Foa & Foa, 1980), but also signals a substantial reciprocal investment by employees in the group’s welfare. In this way, task performance plays a critical role in restoring perceived balance in social exchanges, regardless of who the benefitting party is. Crucially, high levels of performance are also closely tied to personal gains, such as increased rewards and recognition. In fact, Flynn (2003) observed that employees strategically adjusted their productivity to offset perceived imbalances in group exchanges. Furthermore, justice research has repeatedly found that perceptions of fairness and the resulting high-quality exchanges within groups or with individual members are positively associated with enhanced task performance (Masterson et al., 2000; Wayne et al., 2002).
text: When performing reciprocal actions for fair treatment, employees seek to contribute work resources that are relatively optimal for them to restore exchange balance with the group and/or members without compromising personal interests (Flynn & Brockner, 2003). Among various work resources, individual task performance—”role-prescribed efforts that produce goods and services pivotal to group success—”is one of the most proximal indicators of individual reciprocation because it defines employees' in-role responsibilities for the interest of the group (Van Scotter et al., 2000). As a form of universal resource with clear evaluation criteria, exemplary task performance not only directly benefits the group and its authority figures and members (Colquitt et al., 2013; Foa & Foa, 1980) but also manifests a high level of reciprocal contributions that employees have made for the interest of these parties, thereby helping balance social exchange regardless of the targeted parties. More importantly, producing high performance is directly linked to employees' work outputs that could boost personal interests related to rewards and recognition. Indeed, Flynn (2003) found that employees varied productivity to offset imbalanced exchanges with groups. Justice research has shown that justice-triggered high exchange quality with the group or members increases individual performance (Masterson et al., 2000; Wayne et al., 2002).

use: Much of the existing research on workplace racial discrimination has predominantly centered on comparisons between Black and White employees, with significantly less scholarly attention devoted to other racial-minority groups, such as Asian Americans (Colella et al., 2017; Ruggs et al., 2013). Underscoring the need for a more nuanced understanding of this relatively understudied population, a meta-analysis found that Asian Americans report experiencing significantly higher levels of workplace mistreatment—both broadly, encompassing abusive supervision, bullying, discrimination, harassment, incivility, ostracism, interpersonal conflict, physical and verbal aggression, and specifically with regard to racial discrimination—compared to other racial-minority groups relative to Whites (e.g., Black vs. White, Hispanic vs. White; McCord et al., 2018). While the analysis did not explore the underlying causes of this striking disparity, theoretical perspectives suggest that the meanings individuals attach to their racial identity are shaped by how their group is represented in broader society (Ashforth & Mael, 1989). In other words, racial categories carry distinct, socially constructed meanings informed by prevalent stereotypes. As such, Asian Americans are subject to unique stereotyping, which likely influences how they experience and respond to identity-related challenges in the workplace.
text: The preponderance of research on workplace racial discrimination has focused mostly on the experiences of Black versus White employees, with considerably less attention paid to other racial-minority groups, such as Asian Americans (Colella et al., 2017; Ruggs et al., 2013). Highlighting the necessity for a deeper understanding of this relatively understudied group, a meta-analysis revealed that Asian Americans perceive a significantly higher degree of workplace mistreatment—”both in a broad sense (i.e., abusive supervision, bullying, discrimination, harassment, incivility, ostracism, interpersonal conflict, physical aggression, and verbal aggression) and specifically racial discrimination at work—”than other minority groups relative to Whites (i.e., Black vs. White, Hispanic vs. White; McCord et al., 2018). Although it was beyond the scope of that article to provide a rationale for this stark difference, theory suggests that it may be attributable to how individuals attach specific meanings to the racial group to which they belong based on how society depicts that racial group (Ashforth & Mael, 1989). That is, different racial groups carry with them qualitatively different meanings, shaped by differences in stereotypes within a social context. In that sense, Asian Americans are stereotyped in unique ways and thus likely react to group identity-related struggles at work in their own unique ways.

use: To gain deeper insight into the essence and defining features of profoundly meaningful work, a growing, albeit still limited, group of management scholars has turned to the concept of work as a personal calling (Bellah et al., 1985; Wrzesniewski et al., 1997; Dobrow, 2004; Hall & Chandler, 2005; Dik & Duffy, 2009). These researchers begin with the premise that work pursued purely for financial gain or career progression is unlikely to evoke a strong sense of purpose, significance, or transcendent meaning. In contrast, when work is perceived as a calling, it acquires both deep personal value and broader social relevance (Pratt & Ashforth, 2003). Scholars in this domain argue that a calling may represent the “strongest” (Bellah et al., 1985: 66), most “extreme” (Dobrow, 2004: B1), or “deepest” (Hall & Chandler, 2005: 160) pathway to meaningful work (see also Ciulla, 2000: 52; Pratt & Ashforth, 2003: 320; Wrzesniewski, 2003: 302). Research aligned with this premise has found that individuals who view their work through the lens of a calling report higher levels of job and career satisfaction (Wrzesniewski et al., 1997; Dobrow, 2006; Duffy & Sedlacek, 2007), greater overall life satisfaction (Wrzesniewski et al., 1997), and lower levels of stress, depression, and work-life conflict (Treadgold, 1999; Oates, Hall, & Anderson, 2005).
text: To better understand the nature and characteristics of deeply meaningful work, a small but growing number of management scholars have looked to the notion of work as a personal calling (Bellah et al., 1985; Wrzesniewski et al., 1997; Dobrow, 2004; Hall and Chandler, 2005; Dik and Duffy, 2009). These scholars began with the assumption that work done solely for economic or career advancement reasons is unlikely to inspire a sense of significance, purpose, or transcendent meaning. When viewed as one's calling, however, work assumes both personal and social significance (Pratt and Ashforth, 2003). Scholars have suggested that a sense of calling may offer the "strongest" (Bellah et al., 1985: 66), most "extreme" (Dobrow, 2004: B1), or "deepest" (Hall and Chandler, 2005: 160) route to truly meaningful work (see also Ciulla, 2000: 52; Pratt and Ashforth, 2003: 320; Wrzesniewski, 2003: 302). Consistent with this assumption, research has suggested that individuals who view their work as a calling are more satisfied with their work and career (Wrzesniewski et al., 1997; Dobrow, 2006; Duffy and Sedlacek, 2007), experience greater life satisfaction (Wrzesniewski et al., 1997), and are less likely to suffer from stress, depression, and conflict between work and nonwork (Treadgold, 1999; Oates, Hall, and Anderson, 2005).

use: Research has shown that actions which are beneficial but deviate from established norms—such as leniency—can prompt prosocial sensemaking among observers (Grant et al., 2008). This process encourages employees to focus on cues that interpret the leader's behavior as prosocial. When observers encounter information that highlights a need for humanitarian concern, they may be more inclined to view the leader’s leniency as justified. In this light, leniency may initiate a search for contextual cues suggesting the leader’s actions reflect compassion toward the offender. Such cues can foster the belief that offering moral consideration to the offender is appropriate, thereby mitigating perceptions of unfairness and softening observers’ punitive responses toward the leader. In a related vein, insights from moral deontology (Kant, 1785/2012) indicate that observers' moral regard for offenders may also shape their responses to leniency (Potter, 2009). For example, Brubacher (2019) found that moral concern for the offender could temper judgments about the inappropriateness of leniency. He contended that “when people think about individual offenders, the role of group-level moral concerns is reduced while the role of individual-level moral concerns is increased” (p. 142), thereby diminishing observers’ punitive reactions. These findings imply that when observers consider the emotional or moral plight of the offender, they may place greater weight on individual-level concerns, reducing the perceived severity of the leniency.
text:
Research has demonstrated that acts that are beneficial yet inconsistent with norms (such as leniency) may trigger prosocial sensemaking from observers (Grant et al., 2008). This particular process focuses employees’ attention toward cues about the leader’s behavior that show the leader is acting prosocially. Information that supports the need for humanitarianism, then, would suggest the leader’s prosocial actions were warranted. Accordingly, leader leniency may trigger a search for information that may support the notion that the leader’s leniency was an act of mercy to the offender. Such information, then, would suggest that moral consideration toward the offender is reasonable, which would lessen the unfairness of the leniency and observers’ punitive reactions to the leader.

Similarly, moral deontology research (Kant, 1785/2012) also suggests that moral regard for offenders may influence observers’ reactions to leniency (Potter, 2009). For instance, Brubacher (2019) found that observers’ evaluations about the wrongness of leniency were countered by moral concern for the offender. He argued that “when people think about individual offenders, the role of group-level moral concerns is reduced while the role of individual-level moral concerns is increased” (p. 142), thereby reducing observers’ punitive reactions. Thus, Brubacher’s findings suggest that observers weigh the offender’s distress in their reactions to the leniency.

use: The directions in which conflict is experienced—sending versus receiving—reflect a self-regulatory process of allocating attentional focus. Sending conflict stems from a deliberate communicative intent. Whether the conflict is asserted or requested, it represents the sender’s underlying subgoals and momentary attentional focus (Levelt, 1993). Through sending conflict, individuals actively attempt to restore their task engagement by reinforcing on-task attentional pull. For instance, expressing the conflict to a relevant party (e.g., through debate or challenge) or even disengaging from the situation altogether (e.g., walking away) can help individuals reestablish a sense of control and refocus on their core tasks. In contrast, receiving conflict places greater demands on off-task attention. Being on the receiving end of such communication imposes an attentional burden, as conflict-related issues divert focus away from the ongoing task. As a result, receivers are compelled to assess the legitimacy of the conflict demand to determine whether it warrants their attention and response (Herrmann, 2012). In doing so, they strive to regain task engagement by reducing these disruptive attentional intrusions. For example, instead of addressing the conflict directly, a receiver might decide to sidestep the issue and redirect their focus back to the primary task at hand.
text:
The directions of conflict experience (sending vs. receiving) capture the self-regulatory process of attentional focus allocation. Sending conflict is the consequence of elaborative communicative intention. Either asserted or requested, the conflict itself reflects the senders’ subgoals and their attentional focus at the given moment (Levelt, 1993). By sending conflict, individuals attempt to restore their task engagement by increasing their on-task attentional pull. For example, senders may gain a sense of control (i.e., increase the on-task attentional pull) by expressing the conflict issue to the relevant party (e.g., debating, doubting) or even simply removing themselves from the situation (e.g., walking away).

Meanwhile, receiving conflict increases off-task attentional demands. Notably, being a target of this communication comes at a cost for receivers because the conflict issues now impose off-task attentional demands that are competing against the attentional demands associated with their ongoing task. Accordingly, receivers are likely to assess the legitimacy of a demand to gauge the extent to which they are obligated to pay attention to and address the conflict issues (Herrmann, 2012). During this process, receivers attempt to regain their task engagement by minimizing off-task attentional demands. For example, receivers may opt to bypass addressing the conflict issue and simply return to their primary taskwork.

use: The attraction–selection–attrition (ASA) model similarly addresses the alignment between individuals and their work environments (B. Schneider, 1987). According to this framework, people are drawn to organizations whose prevailing values mirror their own; organizations, in turn, tend to hire individuals who resemble current members, while those who do not fit are more likely (or encouraged) to leave. Given the established connections between values and personality traits (Parks-Leduc et al., 2015), the ASA model has broader implications for personality. Although it primarily conceptualizes fit at the organizational level, distinctions between organizations and occupations often overlap, as certain jobs are more prevalent in specific organizational types. Therefore, the ASA model also suggests that personality trait differences may emerge across occupational domains. In a similar vein, trait activation theory (Tett & Burnett, 2003) explores the dynamic interplay between personality and work environments. It proposes that the expression of personality traits is context-dependent, varying across situations based on how well the traits align with specific job demands and individuals' perceptions of that alignment. When people operate in contexts that match their typical trait patterns, they are likely to recognize and benefit from the situational advantages those traits afford (Tett et al., 2021). Consequently, differences in job type—through the presence of distinct trait-relevant cues—may elicit variation in how personality traits are expressed across occupational settings.
text:
Likewise, the attraction–selection–attrition (ASA) model addresses the fit between individuals and their work environments (B. Schneider, 1987). According to this model, individuals are attracted to organizations with prevalent values similar to theirs, organizations select workers who are similar to the incumbents, and those who do not fit in with the organization tend (to be made) to leave. Given the links between values and personality traits (Parks-Leduc et al., 2015), the ASA model also pertains to personality more broadly, and while it conceptualizes fit at the organizational level, organizational and occupational differences are bound to overlap because different jobs are common in different organizations. Hence, the ASA model also implies personality trait differences across occupations.
Similarly, the trait activation theory (Tett & Burnett, 2003) addresses interactions between personality and work environments. It posits that personality traits are expected to manifest differently across specific contexts and situations, with traits leading to varying outcomes based on their fit with job demands and perceptions of that fit. Individuals in contexts/situations that align with their typical trait levels are likely to recognize the advantages of their traits in those contexts/situations (Tett et al., 2021). As a result, individuals’ personality traits may differ across job types due to varying trait-relevant situations.

use: Organizational Citizenship Behaviors (OCBs) refer to discretionary actions that go beyond formal job requirements, yet play a vital role in enhancing organizational effectiveness. Common examples include assisting coworkers, participating in non-mandatory meetings, and volunteering for additional job-related tasks. Williams and Anderson (1991) categorize OCBs based on their target: OCBs directed toward individuals or coworkers are referred to as OCB-I, while those directed toward the organization as a whole are termed OCB-O. These behaviors are distinct from in-role behaviors (IRBs), which are the core duties necessary to fulfill one’s job responsibilities (Williams & Anderson, 1991). According to a meta-analysis by Podsakoff, Whiting, Podsakoff, and Blume (2009), OCBs are significantly associated with critical organizational outcomes, including productivity and operational efficiency. While many studies have emphasized the benefits of OCBs (e.g., Borman & Motowidlo, 1993; Podsakoff & MacKenzie, 1997), some have raised cautionary perspectives (e.g., Bergeron, 2007; Nielsen et al., 2012). Consequently, a substantial body of research has sought to identify the antecedents of helping behaviors in the workplace (Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). Nonetheless, the precursors of OCBs remain only partially understood (Chiaburu et al., 2011). The present study focuses on examining the role of Dark Triad personality traits in predicting the likelihood and nature of citizenship behaviors.
text: OCBs are actions that are beyond explicit role requirements, but essential for organizational effectiveness. Typical examples of OCBs are helping colleagues, attending non-required work meetings, and volunteering for extra job activities. Williams and Anderson (1991) suggest that OCBs can be defined by their target. Citizenship behaviors aimed at individuals/colleagues (OCB-I) are different from citizenship behaviors directed towards the organization (OCB-O). Citizenship behaviors can also be differentiated from in-role behaviors (IRB). IRBs are the necessary behaviors for task completion (Williams & Anderson, 1991). According to Podsakoff, Whiting, Podsakoff, and Blume's (2009) meta-analysis, there are a number of meaningful associations between OCBs and a variety of important organizational-level outcomes, including organizational productivity and efficiency. There has been a plethora of studies on the positive effects of OCBs (Borman & Motowidlo, 1993; Podsakoff & MacKenzie, 1997, but see Begeron, 2007; Nielsen, Bachrach, Sundstrom, & Halfhill, 2012 for a counterargument). Not surprisingly, a considerable amount of research has been devoted to identifying the antecedents of helping behavior in the workplace (Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). However, the antecedents of OCBs are far from being fully understood (Chiaburu et al., 2011). The focus of the present research is the importance of the dark triad personality traits on predicting citizenship behaviors.

use: AI agents exhibit exceptional capabilities across numerous domains—ranging from outperforming humans in chess, Go, and poker—yet they are widely regarded as lacking the capacity for genuine emotional experience (Bigman & Gray, 2018; H. M. Gray et al., 2007; K. Gray & Wegner, 2012; Huebner, 2010; Kramer et al., 2018; Searle, 1980). While AI systems are increasingly able to recognize and replicate human emotional expressions—using methods such as convolutional neural networks for facial recognition (Kajale et al., 2023; Ng et al., 2023)—their emotional outputs rely on pattern detection and statistical inference rather than authentic, felt experience. As Sajjad et al. (2023, p. 820) note, this lack of sentience undermines any claim to true emotional comprehension. AI may convincingly simulate an understanding of emotion, but it fundamentally lacks the capacity to feel emotions in the way humans do—a fact that people intuitively perceive. Given that genuine emotional experience is considered critical for human empathy and for prosocial behaviors such as altruistic helping (Gaertig et al., 2019; Hawk et al., 2011; Keltner et al., 2006; R. B. Lount, 2010), we argue that AI’s inability to empathize will diminish perceptions of its benevolence, particularly when compared to a human manager.
text: AI agents demonstrate high capability across many domains (e.g., beating humans in chess, Go, and poker), but they are perceived as deficient in their capacity to experience emotions (Bigman & Gray, 2018; H. M. Gray et al., 2007; K. Gray & Wegner, 2012; Huebner, 2010; Kramer et al., 2018; Searle, 1980). AI can recognize and mimic human emotions using techniques such as convolutional neural networks for facial expression analysis (Kajale et al., 2023; Ng et al., 2023). However, AI's identification and expression of emotions is based on pattern recognition and data analysis rather than genuine emotional comprehension through "the experience of a sentient being" (Sajjad et al., 2023, p. 820). While AI can simulate an understanding of emotions, it lacks the capacity to experience emotions in the way that humans do, and humans recognize this limitation. Given that the capacity to experience emotions is seen as essential for humans to empathize with others and engage in benevolent behaviors, such as altruistic helping (Gaertig et al., 2019; Hawk et al., 2011; Keltner et al., 2006; R. B. Lount, 2010), we anticipate that the incapacity of AI to empathize with others will harm the extent to which it is viewed as benevolent compared to a human manager.

use: I begin by arguing that the psychological experiences arising from perceptions of different types of dissimilarity can undermine coworker trust in distinct ways. Racial dissimilarity represents a surface-level difference grounded in a visible physical attribute—one that is typically immediate and easily observable (Harrison et al., 1998, 2002). Political dissimilarity, by contrast, constitutes a deep-level difference, rooted—especially in the U.S.—in one’s ingroup identification as liberal or conservative in values, and/or as affiliated with the Democratic or Republican parties. Unlike race, political affiliation is generally not visibly apparent in workplace settings, where employees seldom display overt political symbols such as campaign apparel. For some individuals, political identity may carry only symbolic meaning, such as signaling tribal allegiance (Swigart et al., 2020, p. 1067). Nevertheless, research consistently shows that partisan identification is widely perceived to reflect core "deep-level values, attitudes, and beliefs" (Roth et al., 2020, pp. 472–473; Roth et al., 2022). Consequently, discerning a coworker’s political identity often requires abstract inferences about their values, beliefs, and attitudes—typically extending beyond observable workplace behavior—to integrate both affiliation and value orientation as part of the self-concept (Swigart et al., 2020). Drawing on the well-established distinction between surface- and deep-level differences (Mannix & Neale, 2005), I adopt a construal-level perspective to examine how perceived racial and political dissimilarities differentially erode trust. Specifically, I explore how these dissimilarities influence judgments of trustworthiness and risk-taking behavior, following the integrative trust framework by Mayer, Davis, and Schoorman (1995).
text: I first contend that the psychological experiences associated with perceiving different types of dissimilarity should undermine coworker trust in different ways. Racial dissimilarity is a surface-level difference based on a physical attribute that, typically, one can concretely and immediately observe (Harrison et al., 1998, 2002). In contrast, political dissimilarity is a deep-level difference based, in the United States, on one's ingroup identification as liberal or conservative in values (and/or Democratic or Republican in affiliation) that, usually, cannot be directly observed (especially in the workplace, where employees rarely wear campaign buttons or t-shirts). For some people, political identities "may only hold symbolic meanings (e.g., represent tribal memberships)" (Swigart et al., 2020, p. 1067). However, partisan affiliation is widely understood to reflect "deep-level values, attitudes, and beliefs" (Roth et al., 2020, pp. 472-473; Roth et al., 2022). As such, perceiving one's political identity—”which incorporates both values and affiliation into one's self-concept (Swigart et al., 2020)—”often requires thinking abstractly about a coworker's values, attitudes, and beliefs beyond the workplace. Building on the well-established distinction between surface- and deep-level differences (see Mannix & Neale, 2005), I take a construal-level approach to studying how perceived racial and political dissimilarity differentially generate distrust (via trustworthiness and risk-taking behavior; Mayer et al., 1995).

use: First, footprints vary in surface area—some are small and easy to overlook, while others are large and visible from a distance. Similarly, legacies differ in magnitude—they may influence only a few members of a unit or extend their impact to many. On one hand, legacies associated with lower hierarchical levels may be smaller than those created at higher levels, such as those left by founders, CEOs, or top management teams, due to the broader influence of their decisions. On the other hand, managers, informal leaders, and standout employees can exert substantial influence within their teams—and in some cases, help spark innovations that ripple beyond their immediate units (Morgeson et al., 2015). This dynamic is illustrated in Bednar’s (2013) qualitative study of legacy in two book printing companies. He interviewed employees, inviting them to recount the defining events and key figures in their company’s history. While founders and CEOs were frequently named, employees in lower-level roles also featured prominently in these narratives. For instance, a quality manager overseeing the data processing center was cited by 25% of respondents for making contributions that had a far-reaching effect on colleagues across the organization.
text: First, footprints have a surface area—”they are either smaller prints that are hard to notice or larger prints that can be seen from farther away. In a similar way, legacies have a magnitude—”they either impact a smaller number of unit members or a larger number of unit members. On the one hand, legacies in the lower echelons may be smaller than those in the upper echelons, given the reach and impact involved in founder, CEO, and top management team decisions. On the other hand, managers, informal leaders, and stars can have a sizable impact on the other members of their work units—”and can trigger innovations that reverberate beyond those units (Morgeson et al., 2015). Such impact can be seen in a qualitative study of legacy in two book printing companies. Bednar (2013) asked employees to tell him the story of their companies, focusing on the defining moments and key characters in their corporate histories. Although the founders and CEOs were mentioned as key characters, so too were employees who occupied positions at lower rungs of the hierarchy. For example, the quality manager who ran the data processing center was referenced by a quarter of the respondents for making contributions that affected a wide number of employees.

use: Some research on groups has specifically explored how attention, shared emotions, and interpersonal interactions shape group performance (Collins, 1990, 2005; Lepisto, 2022; Metiu & Rothbard, 2013). Building on this foundation—and drawing on key characteristics commonly linked to the phenomenon of group flow (Duncan & West, 2018; Pels et al., 2018; van den Hout et al., 2018)—we conceptualize group flow as an emergent, group-level state defined by three interrelated features: (a) members exhibiting full but effortless attention toward one another and their contributions; (b) the collective experience of shared, positively charged emotional arousal; and (c) a pattern of interactions in which individual contributions rapidly and seamlessly build on previous ones, leading to synchronized group action. While existing descriptive accounts of group flow have identified a range of static conditions thought to enable its emergence—as well as traits associated with it, such as loss of self-consciousness, deep concentration, and a strong sense of unity (Duncan & West, 2018; Quinn, 2005; Sawyer, 2007; van den Hout et al., 2018)—prior theories fall short in explaining the micro-level, moment-to-moment behaviors that group members enact to initiate and maintain this optimal collaborative state. Understanding these dynamic processes is the central aim of our study.
text: Some research on groups has focused specifically on the role that attention, shared emotions, and interactions play in shaping group performance (Collins, 1990, 2005; Lepisto, 2022; Metiu & Rothbard, 2013). Building on this research, and guided by the characteristics typically associated with group flow (Duncan & West, 2018; Pels et al., 2018; van den Hout et al., 2018), we define group flow as a group-level, emergent state that is characterized by (a) members paying full, yet seemingly effortless attention (to others and their contributions); (b) shared, positive emotional arousal; both of which are elicited and sustained by (c) interactions that are characterized by member contributions that add on to prior contributions in a swift manner resulting in action synchrony. Although existing descriptive accounts of group flow have identified a set of static conditions that may give rise to group flow and specified characteristics that signify group flow, such as a loss of self-consciousness, intense concentration, and a sense of unity (Duncan & West, 2018; Quinn, 2005; Sawyer, 2007; van den Hout et al., 2018), existing theory does not explain what group members do in the moment and over time that gives rise to and sustains this optimal group state, which is the focus of our work.

use: Organizational scholars have increasingly turned their attention to systematically exploring the factors that influence the breadth of employees’ role definitions. Recent studies have highlighted both contextual and individual determinants of how roles are understood and enacted. From a contextual standpoint, broader role definitions have been linked to job characteristics such as high autonomy, enhanced control, and manageable demands (Morgeson et al., 2005; Parker, 1998; Turner, Chmiel, & Walls, 2005). Other research points to the enabling role of supportive workplace environments, including high-quality relationships with supervisors and organizational climates marked by support and procedural justice (Hofmann et al., 2003; Kamdar, McAllister, & Turban, 2006; Tepper et al., 2001). On the individual level, broader role definitions have been associated with traits such as a proactive personality (Parker & Sprigg, 1999), prosocial value orientations (Nauta, De Dreu, & van der Vaart, 2002), and the possession of relevant knowledge and skills (Morgeson et al., 2005). Collectively, these findings shed important light on how role definitions are shaped by elements of job design, organizational culture and climate, and individual differences in personality, values, and capabilities. Consequently, researchers are beginning to map out the job characteristics, interpersonal dynamics, and cultural conditions that tend to promote role expansion, alongside the personal attributes that make certain employees more or less predisposed to broaden their roles.
text: Accordingly, organizational researchers have begun to systematically investigate the factors that affect the breadth of employees' role definitions. Recent research has identified both contextual and individual factors that affect role definitions. From a contextual perspective, researchers have examined how broader role definitions are facilitated by job characteristics such as high autonomy, high control, and low demands (Morgeson et al., 2005; Parker, 1998; Turner, Chmiel, & Walls, 2005), as well as by high-quality supervisor relationships and organizational climates that provide support and procedural justice (Hofmann et al., 2003; Kamdar, McAllister, & Turban, 2006; Tepper et al., 2001). From an individual perspective, researchers have examined how broader role definitions are predicted by higher levels of proactive personality (Parker & Sprigg, 1999), prosocial value orientations (Nauta, De Dreu, & van der Vaart, 2002), and knowledge and skill (Morgeson et al., 2005). Together, these studies have provided valuable insights into how role definitions are affected by job designs, organizational cultures and climates, and individual differences in personalities, values, and abilities. As a result, researchers are beginning to understand the job, relationship, and organizational conditions that are generally likely to motivate role expansion, as well as the types of employees that are more and less likely to expand their role definitions.

use: While early research often characterized initiative as an extra-role behavior, more recent studies have acknowledged that initiative can also manifest within one's formal role, particularly when individuals exert high levels of effort in carrying out their core responsibilities and assigned tasks (Frese & Fay, 2001; Grant & Ashford, 2008). As noted by Podsakoff et al. (2000, p. 524), initiative is considered "extra-role only in the sense that it involves engaging in task-related behaviors at a level" that exceeds the "minimally required or generally expected levels." They further clarify that initiative encompasses behaviors such as exerting extra effort, demonstrating persistence, and volunteering for additional responsibilities. For instance, initiative is demonstrated when a job seeker, after facing repeated rejections, proactively turns to their network for guidance, researches companies ahead of interviews, or seeks opportunities to shadow professionals in unfamiliar roles. Likewise, a salesperson exhibits initiative by identifying prospective clients in a new market, devising novel persuasive strategies, or tailoring a unique advertising package to retain a dissatisfied customer. In each case, initiative is evident through self-directed, sustained efforts—often undertaken in the face of difficulties—that go beyond routine expectations.
text: Although some researchers originally treated initiative as an extra-role behavior, more recent scholarship has recognized that initiative can be undertaken in-role by investing high levels of effort in core tasks and assigned responsibilities (Frese & Fay, 2001; Grant & Ashford, 2008). As Podsakoff et al. (2000, p. 524) explained, initiative "is extra-role only in the sense that it involves engaging in task-related behaviors at a level" that is "beyond minimally required or generally expected levels." They go on to explain that initiative can include behaviors such as task effort and persistence, as well as volunteering for additional duties. For example, initiative is occurring when a job applicant reaches out to her network for advice after numerous rejections, does research on a company before a job interview, or requests the opportunity to shadow an employee to learn more about a new career path. Initiative is also at play when a salesperson identifies and contacts prospects in a new industry, develops a different strategy for convincing customers to buy a product or service, and designs a custom advertising package in an effort to please a client who is ready to walk away. These examples reflect initiative in that they involve self-starting, persistent actions, sometimes in the face of barriers.

use: Although the configurational perspective and the AMO (Ability, Motivation, Opportunity) model are widely accepted and utilized within strategic HR literature, several critical theoretical and empirical gaps remain. From a theoretical standpoint, the configurational perspective posits that synergies among HR practices generate a system-level effect that exceeds the combined impact of individual practices (Chadwick, 2010). Yet, researchers seldom delve into the underlying mechanisms—why and how specific practices within a system should interact to produce these synergies. Concepts such as configurations, internal fit, complementarity, interrelations, and synergy are frequently cited in HRM research, but often in a metaphorical or rhetorical sense, serving more as a general rationale for clustering HR practices than as rigorously theorized constructs (Chadwick, 2010, p. 86). Similarly, while the AMO model is regularly employed as an overarching framework, existing research rarely investigates—or clearly articulates—how high-performance HR practices targeting the AMO dimensions interact to affect critical outcomes like organizational performance (Bos-Nehles et al., 2023). Moreover, the AMO framework primarily anticipates interactions across its three domains but overlooks the potential interconnections within each domain (e.g., the interaction between staffing and training). This omission is noteworthy, given that High-Performance Work Systems (HPWS) are hierarchically organized: individual HR practices are embedded within AMO domains, and these domains, in turn, are integrated into the larger HPWS. Addressing these overlooked intra-domain dynamics could significantly enrich our understanding of how HR systems function as cohesive, performance-enhancing configurations.
text: Despite the widespread acceptance and use of the configurational perspective and AMO model in the strategic HR literature, several important theoretical and empirical issues have been overlooked. Theoretically, the configurational perspective proposes that synergies between HR practices create a systems effect that is greater than the sum of the individual practice effects (Chadwick, 2010). However, studies rarely theorize why and how practices within the same system should relate to one another to create such synergies. Indeed, terms such as configurations, internal fit, complementarity, interrelations, and synergy are “often invoked metaphorically in HRM research as a loose rationale for working with aggregations of HRM practices” (Chadwick, 2010, p. 86). Similarly, although researchers often use the AMO model as a guiding framework, rarely have they considered (or at least fully articulated) whether and how high-performance HR practices that target the AMO domains interact to influence outcomes such as firm performance (Bos-Nehles et al., 2023). In addition, although the AMO model predicts interactions among the three HR domains, it does not consider interrelations that exist within each domain (e.g., Staffing × Training). This is a significant omission because an HPWS is hierarchically structured such that individual practices are nested within AMO domains, which, in turn, are nested within the HPWS.

use: Recognizing that existing models centered on job attitudes and perceived alternatives left a significant portion of voluntary turnover unexplained, Lee and Mitchell (1994) introduced the unfolding model. This model proposes that many voluntary resignations occur abruptly and in the absence of prior dissatisfaction. Instead, these departures are often triggered by “shocks” — sudden, distinct, and disruptive events that compel individuals to reflect on whether they should remain in or leave their jobs (Lee & Mitchell, 1994). Drawing from traditional turnover models as well as Beach’s (1990) image theory, Lee and Mitchell identified multiple “paths” through which turnover unfolds. According to image theory, individuals are constantly exposed to new information, yet most of it is disregarded, allowing the status quo — staying in one’s job — to remain intact. However, certain shocks are so impactful that they disrupt this stability, prompting serious contemplation about quitting. This decision-making process involves comparing the current job situation with one’s internal “images,” which encompass personal values in both work and non-work domains, long-term career and life goals, and the strategies or tactics employed to achieve those goals. When potential options conflict significantly with these internal images, they may be dismissed with minimal conscious deliberation. Consequently, if one’s job becomes deeply misaligned with their images, a decision to quit can occur relatively swiftly and without extensive analysis.
text: Finding that models based on attitudes and alternatives left substantial variance in voluntary turnover unexplained, Lee and Mitchell (1994) developed their unfolding model. The unfolding model posits that many voluntary quits occur suddenly and without previous dissatisfaction. These quits are precipitated by "shocks" - sudden, identifiable, and jarring events that prompt the individual to deliberate on whether they want to stay or quit (Lee & Mitchell, 1994). Building on traditional turnover approaches and Beach's (1990) image theory, Lee and Mitchell identified multiple "paths" to turnover. According to image theory, people are constantly bombarded with information - but most of this information is ignored, and the status quo persists (i.e., one stays in one's current job). However, on some occasions, employees experience shocks that are so jarring that they prompt serious deliberations about quitting. These deliberations involve comparisons between the current job situation and one's images, which include one's values for work and non-work, one's trajectory or goals for work and non-work, and one's strategic image or tactics for attaining one's goals in work and non-work. Options that are highly discrepant with one's images may be rejected with relatively little conscious deliberation, such that when one's job situation becomes highly incompatible with one's images, one may quit relatively spontaneously.

use: Although there is a growing body of research examining the conditions under which knowledge is generated and transferred from organizational failures (e.g., Baumard & Starbuck, 2005; Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; KC et al., 2013; Kim & Miner, 2007; Park et al., 2022), far less attention has been paid to how such knowledge deteriorates over time—and why some lessons from failure endure within organizational structures, routines, and memories, while others fade more quickly. As Argote, Lee, and Park (2021: 16) recognize, "further research is needed to advance our understanding of whether different types of experience depreciate at different rates." This gap in theoretical understanding is particularly significant, as organizations that rapidly forget the lessons of past failures are more likely to repeat similar errors, often with serious organizational and societal repercussions (Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; Shrivastava, 1994). A stark example of this can be found in Massey Energy’s history: in 2007, a fire in one of its coal mines resulted in the death of two miners. Although the company implemented some corrective actions, the knowledge and lessons from that incident did not endure. Just three years later, in 2010, similar hazardous conditions led to a catastrophic explosion at the Upper Big Branch Mine, claiming the lives of 29 miners—the deadliest U.S. mining accident in decades.
text: While the circumstances surrounding knowledge creation and the transfer of knowledge from failures are increasingly well examined (e.g., Baumard & Starbuck, 2005; Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; KC et al., 2013; Kim & Miner, 2007; Park et al., 2022), less is known about the depreciation of such knowledge and why the knowledge from some failures persists longer in organizational structures, routines, and memories than the knowledge from others. Indeed, as Argote, Lee, and Park (2021: 16) noted, "further research is needed to advance our understanding of whether different types of experience depreciate at different rates." The lack of theory in this area is especially salient since organizations that forget failures quickly are often doomed to repeat similar mistakes more frequently over time, with negative organizational and societal consequences (Haunschild & Sullivan, 2002; Shrivastava, 1994). For example, in 2007, a coal mine owned by Massey Energy experienced a fire that killed two miners. Although the firm made changes in response to the accident, lessons learned from the incident did not persist for long. In 2010, similar conditions to those precipitating the 2007 fire led to an explosion at Massey Energy's Upper Big Branch Mine, resulting in the deaths of 29 miners—”the deadliest mining accident in the United States in several decades.

use: Citizenship behaviors refer to voluntary, discretionary actions that are not formally rewarded by organizations but nonetheless contribute to their overall effectiveness (Organ, 1988). Scholars have identified two distinct types of citizenship behaviors: affiliative and challenging (Van Dyne, Cummings, & McLean Parks, 1995). Affiliative citizenship behaviors are aimed at preserving the status quo by reinforcing and supporting existing work processes and interpersonal relationships. These behaviors typically include acts such as assisting colleagues, demonstrating politeness and respect, and willingly working additional hours (Van Dyne et al., 1995). In contrast, challenging citizenship behaviors are directed toward transforming the status quo by questioning current practices and introducing improvements to workflows and relationships (Van Dyne et al., 1995). Examples of such behaviors include raising concerns (LePine & Van Dyne, 1998), advocating for important issues (Ashford, Rothbard, Piderit, & Dutton, 1998), and proactively taking initiative to implement constructive changes (Bettencourt, 2004; Choi, 2007; Frese & Fay, 2001; Morrison & Phelps, 1999). Empirical research indicates that both affiliative and challenging citizenship behaviors can significantly enhance organizational performance and effectiveness (e.g., Podsakoff et al., 2000; Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). In the sections that follow, we examine how prosocial motives and impression management motives may differentially influence the enactment of both affiliative and challenging forms of citizenship behavior.
text: Citizenship behaviors are discretionary contributory actions that are not explicitly rewarded by organizations but nonetheless enhance their effectiveness (Organ, 1988). Researchers have argued that citizenship behaviors can be classified into two different categories: affiliative versus challenging (Van Dyne, Cummings, & McLean Parks, 1995). Affiliative citizenship behaviors are actions directed toward maintaining the status quo by promoting and supporting existing work processes and relationships (Van Dyne et al., 1995). Affiliative citizenship behaviors include helping colleagues, showing courtesy toward others, and taking the initiative to work additional hours (Van Dyne et al., 1995). Challenging citizenship behaviors are actions directed toward changing the status quo by questioning and improving upon existing work processes and relationships (Van Dyne et al., 1995). Challenging citizenship behaviors include voicing problems (LePine & Van Dyne, 1998), selling issues (Ashford, Rothbard, Piderit, & Dutton, 1998), and taking charge to implement constructive changes to work methods (Bettencourt, 2004; Choi, 2007; Frese & Fay, 2001; Morrison & Phelps, 1999). Research has shown that both affiliative and challenging forms of citizenship behaviors can contribute to effective outcomes in organizations (e.g., Podsakoff et al., 2000; Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). In the following sections, we consider how prosocial and impression management motives are likely to relate to both affiliative and challenging citizenship behaviors.

use: To advance theory, research, and practice on leader characteristics, it is essential to understand how these traits influence group performance (Kaiser et al., 2008). In this article, we aim to explore the circumstances under which extraverted leadership enhances—or hinders—group effectiveness. Specifically, we propose that extraverted leadership is beneficial when employees show low levels of proactivity, leading to improved group performance. However, when employees are highly proactive, this relationship reverses, and extraverted leadership may actually undermine performance. This suggests that in today’s rapidly evolving business environment, less extraverted leaders may possess underappreciated strengths. As organizations face increasing dynamism, uncertainty, and unpredictability, leaders can no longer rely solely on delivering top-down visions to drive success (Griffin, Neal, & Parker, 2007). Now more than ever, leaders must rely on employees to initiate bottom-up change through constructive voice (Van Dyne & LePine, 1998), proactive efforts to improve workflows (Morrison & Phelps, 1999), and upward influence (Dutton, Ashford, O'Neill, & Lawrence, 2001). Yet, research indicates that many leaders perceive such proactive behaviors as disruptive or threatening, preventing them from leveraging employees’ contributions effectively (Detert & Burris, 2007; Grant, Parker, & Collins, 2009; Parker, Williams, & Turner, 2006; Seibert, Kraimer, & Crant, 2001).
text: To advance theory, research, and practice on leader characteristics, it is critical to understand how they affect group performance (Kaiser et al., 2008). Our goal in this article is to examine the conditions under which extraverted leadership contributes to versus detracts from group performance. We propose that when employees are not proactive, extraverted leadership contributes to higher group performance, but when employees are proactive, this relationship reverses to a negative one. There is good reason to believe that in a changing business world, less extraverted leaders bring important strengths to the table. As organizational life becomes more dynamic, uncertain, and unpredictable, it has become increasingly difficult for leaders to succeed by merely developing and presenting their visions top-down to employees (Griffin, Neal, & Parker, 2007). More than ever before, leaders depend on employees to proactively advance bottom-up change by voicing constructive ideas (Van Dyne & LePine, 1998), taking charge to improve work methods (Morrison & Phelps, 1999), and engaging in upward influence (Dutton, Ashford, O'Neill, & Lawrence, 2001). However, research suggests that many leaders see these proactive behaviors as threats or distractions, and they thus fail to benefit from employees' contributions (Detert & Burris, 2007; Grant, Parker, & Collins, 2009; Parker, Williams, & Turner, 2006; Seibert, Kraimer, & Crant, 2001).

use: Given that quasi-experimentation was originally developed by scholars with psychological orientations (Campbell & Stanley, 1966; Cook & Campbell, 1979), it stands to reason that such researchers may be more likely to publish studies using this method. To explore this possibility, we examined the frequency of quasi-experiments published over a 25-year span in six leading journals focused on applied psychology and micro-organizational behavior: Journal of Applied Psychology (JAP), Personnel Psychology (PPsych), Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes (OBHDP), Journal of Organizational Behavior (JOB), Journal of Management (JoM), and Journal of Applied Behavioral Science (JABS). Our review identified 30 quasi-experiments in JAP, 18 in PPsych, 2 in OBHDP, 5 in JOB, 1 in JoM, and 7 in JABS. While JAP and PPsych appear to account for a disproportionate share of these studies, it's important to contextualize this pattern. Specifically, JAP published more than twice as many total articles as any of the other journals over the same period, and PPsych featured a special issue on quasi-experimentation in 2002, which contributed 5 of its 18 studies. Overall, these findings indicate that quasi-experiments are exceedingly rare in applied psychology and micro-organizational behavior journals, comprising less than 1% of total publications in these outlets during the 25-year period analyzed.
text: Because quasi-experiments were developed by researchers with psychological orientations (Campbell & Stanley, 1966; Cook & Campbell, 1979), it is plausible that they are published more often by such researchers. To investigate this possibility, we counted the number of quasi-experiments published in the same 25-year period in six journals that specialize in publishing organizational research in the areas of applied psychology and micro-organizational behavior: Journal of Applied Psychology (JAP), Personnel Psychology (PPsych), Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes (OBHDP), Journal of Organizational Behavior (JOB), Journal of Management (JoM), and Journal of Applied Behavioral Science (JABS). We found a total of 30 quasi-experiments in JAP, 18 in PPsych, 2 in OBHDP, 5 in JOB, 1 in JoM, and 7 in JABS. Although it may appear that quasi-experiments were published more frequently in JAP and PPsych than in the other journals, it is worth noting that JAP published more than twice as many total articles during this period than any of the other journals that we examined, and that PPsych published a special issue on quasi-experimentation in 2002 that attracted 5 of its 18 quasi-experiments. Thus, our analysis of six applied psychology and micro-organizational behavior journals suggested that quasi-experiments are extremely rare, appearing in less than 1% of all articles published in these journals.

use: Scholarly interest in role expansion has its roots in early theoretical work on role innovation (Katz & Kahn, 1966; van Maanen & Schein, 1979). While foundational role theory often conceptualized roles as fixed structures assigned by supervisors and passively accepted by employees, emerging perspectives on role innovation challenged this static view. These newer perspectives recognized the agency and initiative of role incumbents, who frequently take proactive steps to redefine and expand their roles (Grant & Ashford, 2008; Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991; Morrison, 1994; Nicholson, 1984; Parker et al., 1997; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). From this standpoint, individuals often retain discretion over whether to accept or reject role-expansion requests. Building on this insight, our aim is to investigate how interpersonal influence processes shape role incumbents' decisions to embrace or decline such requests. Our analysis is specifically focused on contexts in which role expansion is not mandated or imposed. Instead, we examine cases where individuals have the autonomy to choose whether or not to expand their roles. Moreover, we concentrate on situations marked by uncertainty—where role incumbents are undecided—since these are the conditions under which persuasion and influence tactics are most impactful.
text: Scholarly interest in role expansion can be traced back to theoretical perspectives on role innovation (Katz & Kahn, 1966; van Maanen & Schein, 1979). Whereas early work on role theory assumed that roles were static objects assigned by supervisors and accepted by incumbents, these perspectives on role innovation emerged when scholars recognized that role incumbents often exercise agency and proactivity by taking the initiative to modify and enlarge their own role definitions (Grant & Ashford, 2008; Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991; Morrison, 1994; Nicholson, 1984; Parker et al., 1997; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). From this viewpoint, role incumbents often have discretion about whether or not to accept a request for role expansion. Building on this observation, our goal is to explain how interpersonal influence processes affect role incumbents' decisions about whether or not to expand a role. Our analysis does not apply to situations in which role expansion is required or coerced; our analysis is bounded to situations in which role incumbents have discretion or freedom of choice about whether or not to accept a role-expansion request. Our analysis is also circumscribed to situations in which role incumbents are uncertain about whether or not to expand their roles, which is where persuasion and influence techniques are likely to have the greatest leverage.

use: In this study, we examine employees’ chronic regulatory focus—that is, their enduring disposition to self-regulate in alignment with their personal standards and goals (Higgins, 1997, 1998). Regulatory Focus Theory identifies two distinct and independent modes of self-regulation, each associated with different preferences for goal pursuit and coping strategies: (a) promotion focus and (b) prevention focus (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Scholer & Higgins, 2010). Individuals with a promotion focus tend to pursue goals oriented toward growth, advancement, and aspiration. They are motivated by the potential for gains and the avoidance of missed opportunities (nongains), and they favor eager strategies such as being enthusiastic, taking risks, and exploring multiple options (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Molden, Lee, & Higgins, 2008). In contrast, individuals with a prevention focus concentrate on goals related to security, responsibility, and maintaining the status quo. They are driven by a desire to prevent losses and ensure the absence of negative outcomes, and they tend to rely on vigilant strategies such as exercising caution, attending to details, and engaging in thorough planning (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Molden et al., 2008). Importantly, promotion and prevention focus are not opposite ends of a single spectrum but are independent regulatory orientations. Thus, an individual may exhibit high levels of one focus, both, or neither (Higgins et al., 2001; Wallace & Chen, 2006).
text: In the present study, we are concerned with employees' chronic regulatory focus—”their enduring tendency to self-regulate and align themselves with their standards and goals (Higgins, 1997, 1998). As relevant to the present study, regulatory focus theory proposes two independent modes of self-regulation with distinct preferences for goal attainment and strategically different ways of coping: (a) a promotion focus and (b) a prevention focus (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Scholer & Higgins, 2010). Promotion-focused individuals are concerned with goals related to accomplishment and aspirations, are motivated to ensure gains and to ensure against nongains, and prefer eager approach strategies, such as being enthusiastic, taking risks, and considering multiple alternatives (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Molden, Lee, & Higgins, 2008). Prevention-focused individuals are concerned with goals related to maintenance and keeping the status quo, are motivated to ensure nonlosses and to ensure against losses, and prefer vigilant avoidance strategies, such as being cautious, and focusing on details and careful planning (Crowe & Higgins, 1997; Molden et al., 2008). It is important to note that promotion focus and prevention focus are not two ends of a continuum but are rather independent dimensions. One person could have high levels in one dimension, both, or neither (Higgins, Friedman, Harlow, Idson, Ayduk, & Taylor, 2001; Wallace & Chen, 2006).

use: Recent research indicates that job crafting strategies—such as seeking resources, seeking challenges, and reducing demands—play a critical role in facilitating the development of new work roles that help employees maintain their well-being (i.e., high levels of work engagement and low levels of exhaustion), both during cutback-related changes and more routine organizational transformations (Petrou, Demerouti, & Xanthopoulou, 2017). Furthermore, two studies have demonstrated that interventions designed to promote job crafting behavior positively impact change-related outcomes, including openness to change, adaptive performance (Demerouti et al., 2017), and both task and contextual performance, as well as proactive change behaviors (Gordon et al., 2018). Generating robust empirical support for the effectiveness of job crafting interventions in enhancing employees' attitudes toward change and their adaptive behavior is essential, given that attitudes significantly influence behavior (Fishbein & Ajzen, 2012). This is particularly relevant as employees must increasingly demonstrate adaptability and resilience to navigate uncertainty and perform effectively and safely in dynamic work environments (Pulakos, Arad, Donovan, & Plamondon, 2000). However, empirical evidence in this area remains limited. In addition, efforts to train employees in the specific job crafting strategy of reducing demands have been associated with less favorable change outcomes (Demerouti et al., 2017). Finally, it remains unclear whether job crafting interventions are equally effective in blue-collar contexts—such as the retail sector—since existing studies have exclusively targeted white-collar populations (e.g., public sector workers and healthcare professionals).
text: Recent evidence suggests that job crafting strategies (i.e., seeking resources, seeking challenges, and reducing demands) facilitate the emergence of new work roles that help employees retain their well-being (i.e., high work engagement and low exhaustion) during cutback-related or regular organizational changes (Petrou, Demerouti, & Xanthopoulou, 2017). Moreover, two studies showed that an intervention that stimulates job crafting behavior has been effective in increasing change-related outcomes (i.e., openness to change, adaptive performance; Demerouti et al., 2017), task and contextual performance, as well as change behavior (Gordon et al., 2018). Empirical evidence on the effectiveness of the job crafting intervention in improving attitude towards change and adaptive behavior is essential, since attitudes are the precursors of behavior (Fishbein & Ajzen, 2012) and workers need to be increasingly adaptable to uncertainty in order to operate effectively and safely in changing environments (Pulakos, Arad, Donovan, & Plamondon, 2000). However, such evidence has been limited so far. Also, training employees to apply the job crafting strategy of reducing demands was found to result in unfavorable change outcomes (Demerouti et al., 2017). Finally, it is unclear whether the job crafting intervention can also be effective for blue-collar work (e.g., in the retail sector), since all such interventions so far concerned white-collar employees (e.g., public sector employees, nurses).

use: Gagné and Deci (2005) offered a more refined interpretation of Self-Determination Theory (SDT), identifying conditions under which extrinsic rewards do not necessarily undermine intrinsic motivation. Central to their argument is the role of autonomy: extrinsic rewards are less likely to negatively impact—and may even enhance—intrinsic motivation when offered within autonomy-supportive work environments. At the core of SDT lies the distinction between autonomous and controlled forms of motivation, with intrinsic motivation representing the most autonomous and highest-quality form (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Howard et al., 2017; Ryan & Deci, 2000, 2017). As Howard et al. (2017) emphasized, the quality of motivation—specifically, the presence of autonomous reasons—matters more than its quantity, defined as the total sum of both autonomous and controlled motivations. Moreover, since “autonomy involves acting with a sense of volition and having the experience of choice” (Gagné & Deci, 2005, p. 333), an autonomy-supportive context facilitates the internalization and eventual integration of extrinsic motives into more intrinsic forms (see Figure 1 in Gagné & Deci, 2005; Figure 1 in Howard et al., 2017). Accordingly, autonomy emerges as a pivotal factor in shaping the influence of pay-for-performance (PFP) systems on intrinsic motivation. Overall, the theoretical consensus predicts that PFP can positively affect creative performance, especially within autonomy-enhancing environments.
text: Gagné and Deci (2005) provided a more nuanced interpretation of SDT, suggesting several conditions under which extrinsic rewards do not necessarily harm intrinsic motivation. Specifically, they proposed the importance of autonomy, stating that extrinsic rewards are less likely to be detrimental to intrinsic motivation and might even enhance intrinsic motivation if they are implemented in autonomous work environments. Indeed, the foundational essence of SDT is the distinction between autonomous motivation and controlled motivation, where intrinsic motivation is inherently autonomous and of the highest quality (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Howard et al., 2017; Ryan & Deci, 2000, 2017). Howard et al. (2017) noted that the quality of motivation (i.e., the autonomous reasons) is more important than the quantity of motivation (i.e., the sum of autonomous and controlled reasons). Further, because “autonomy involves acting with a sense of volition and having the experience of choice” (Gagné & Deci, 2005, p. 333), an autonomy-supportive climate facilitates the internalization and ultimately integration of extrinsic motivation toward intrinsic motivation (see Figure 1 in Gagné & Deci, 2005, and Figure 1 in Howard et al., 2017). Thus, autonomy becomes central to understanding the effects of PFP on intrinsic motivation. In essence, theoretical perspectives converge to anticipate a positive effect of PFP on creative performance in autonomy-supportive work settings.

use:
Crucially, the deontic response is characterized not only by the experience of anger but also by the behavioral impulses that this anger provokes toward the perpetrator. These impulses are described in the literature as “retribution as an end in itself” (Folger et al., 2005, p. 225) and metaphorically as “fighting fire with fire” (Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, p. 94). Such responses fall within the broader category of antisocial behaviors—actions undertaken with the intent to harm others (Robinson & O’Leary-Kelly, 1998). These retaliatory actions are thought to embody the adaptive function of anger, serving as deterrents against future misconduct by signaling that inappropriate treatment will not go unchallenged (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). This perspective is supported by research that positions anger as a mediating mechanism linking third-party perceptions of mistreatment to antisocial behaviors against the perceived wrongdoer (e.g., Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). For instance, Mitchell et al. (2015) found that third parties who witnessed abusive supervision directed at a coworker experienced anger toward the supervisor, and that this anger, in turn, motivated deviant behavior against the supervisor. Based on this reasoning, we proposed the following:
Hypothesis 2: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment have a positive indirect effect on antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator through anger directed at the perpetrator.
text:
Importantly, the deontic response is not only described as feelings of anger. Rather, it is also described as feelings of anger that trigger behaviors against the perpetrator. Such behaviors are described as “retribution as an end in itself” (Folger et al., 2005, p. 225) and as “fighting fire with fire” (Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, p. 94). Such reactions fall under the broad heading of antisocial behaviors, defined as actions intended to harm others (Robinson & O’Leary-Kelly, 1998). It is those behaviors that represent the adaptive value of anger, as they should dissuade perpetrators from engaging in inappropriate treatment in the future (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). In support of such logic, studies have cast anger in a mediating role when linking third-party perceptions of mistreatment to antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator (e.g., Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). For example, Mitchell et al. (2015) showed that third parties who observed a coworker receiving abusive supervision felt anger toward the supervisor, with that anger fueling deviance against the supervisor. We therefore predicted:
Hypothesis 2: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment have a positive indirect effect on antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator through anger toward the perpetrator.

use: Because creativity is widely regarded as a rare and valuable trait (Amabile, 1996), individuals who strongly identify with a creative role may come to see their abilities as both distinctive and exceptional (Grimes, 2018; Vincent & Kouchaki, 2016). This perception is especially likely to emerge when the uniqueness of their abilities is made salient to themselves and to those around them. Receiving a prestigious award in recognition of one's creative contributions—an honor typically reserved for a select few—heightens awareness of the rarity and exceptional nature of the creative identity. Although recognizing one’s creative gifts as uncommon can offer motivational benefits, it also brings into focus the possibility that such a prized identity may be lost. Given the inherent scarcity of genuinely novel ideas, creative individuals are acutely aware of the challenge involved in producing follow-up work that matches the originality or impact of their earlier accomplishments. The threat of seeing one’s creative identity diminish is a psychologically aversive and fear-inducing prospect that people are motivated to avoid (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979; Pettit et al., 2010; Thaler, 1980). In this light, the act of creating a subsequent piece of work—especially if it falls short of prior success—is likely to be experienced as a significant threat, potentially resulting in both public and private deterioration of one’s sense of self as a creative contributor (Jensen & Kim, 2015).
text: As creativity is considered a quality that is rare and valuable (Amabile, 1996), individuals with a creative role identity may come to view their abilities as unique and special (Grimes, 2018; Vincent & Kouchaki, 2016). This is particularly likely to be the case when these abilities and their uniqueness are made salient to the self and others. Naturally, receiving an award for one's novel contributions—”an honor that is typically only bestowed upon a select few—”makes salient the rarity and special nature of a creative role identity. While the realization that one's creative gifts are rare and special may be associated with some benefits, it also highlights the potential that this unique and special identity can be lost. Given the rarity of novel ideas, individuals are aware of the difficulty of producing follow-up work that can rival their initial creative production. The prospect of seeing one's creative identity erode is a generally aversive and threatening experience that people try to avoid (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979; Pettit et al., 2010; Thaler, 1980). Indeed, producing follow-up work that potentially pales compared to one's initial production is likely to be perceived as particularly threatening, as it would lead to a public and private erosion of one's identity as a producer of creative work (Jensen & Kim, 2015).

use: Collaborative behavior represents a specific form of teamwork behavior centered on achieving collective task goals. It primarily takes place during the execution phase of teamwork, where team members put planned activities into action (Rousseau et al., 2006). This execution phase is critical for converting team inputs—such as members’ characteristics, expertise, or skills—into tangible outputs like performance outcomes (Marks et al., 2001). Building on the integrated framework proposed by Rousseau et al. (2006), we distinguish three key dimensions of collaborative behavior in teams: coordination, communication, and cooperation. Coordination refers to the behavioral processes that align and integrate individual efforts to accomplish shared tasks, particularly under time constraints. This dimension relies heavily on intrateam interactions and can be shaped by both internal dynamics and external influences (Fisher, 2014; Marks et al., 2001). Communication involves the exchange of task-relevant information among team members, encompassing both the content and frequency of interactions. Well-functioning communication enhances team effectiveness by ensuring that critical information is accessible and widely shared across the team (Marks et al., 2001; Salas et al., 2005). Finally, cooperation reflects the willingness of team members to invest effort toward collective goals.
