It is demonstrated through individuals’ active participation in completing tasks that exceed the capabilities of any single member, highlighting the interdependence essential for effective teamwork (Wagner, 1995).
text: Collaborative behavior is a form of teamwork behavior that focuses on task accomplishment. It mainly occurs in the execution of teamwork, where team members implement planned activities (Rousseau et al., 2006). This process aims to transform team input (e.g., team member characteristics or skills) into team output (e.g., performance; Marks et al., 2001). Drawing on the integrated framework of Rousseau et al. (2006), we distinguish collaborative behavior in teams in three dimensions: coordination, communication, and cooperation. Coordination is a behavioral process that ensures the integration and completion of team members' activities to accomplish an assigned task under time constraints. It is based on intrateam interactions and can be affected by internal and external phenomena (Fisher, 2014; Marks et al., 2001). Communication (i.e., information sharing) is based on the exchange of task-related information among the team members, including the level of communication. A high level of communication increases the team's effectiveness by ensuring the availability of information and its dissemination among team members (e.g., Marks et al., 2001; Salas et al., 2005). Lastly, cooperation is the willingness to contribute to accomplishing team goals, expressed in the individual effort of team members in completing tasks. Cooperative behavior involves team members working together to achieve the goals that an individual could not achieve alone (Wagner, 1995).

use: I adopt an intersectional perspective to explore gender differences in leaders' language use, focusing on how communication varies in relation to social context. Research increasingly shows that individuals often employ counter-stereotypical language when interacting in outgroup settings. For example, White liberals tend to use fewer dominance-related words (such as "powerful") when addressing predominantly Black as opposed to predominantly White audiences (Dupree & Fiske, 2019), unintentionally reinforcing stereotypes through a dynamic that may be well-intentioned but also patronizing. Similarly, women leaders working in male-dominated environments may adopt language patterns that challenge traditional stereotypes of women as submissive (Heilman, 2001; Ridgeway, 2001; Eagly, 2007). I argue that women in prominent leadership roles are more likely than their male counterparts to use dominant language—utilizing words like "powerful," "competitive," and "assertive"—when speaking to colleagues or the public. Furthermore, I propose that women leaders’ use of such dominant language may carry meaningful consequences for how they are portrayed in the media and perceived by the public. Specifically, the more frequently women leaders employ dominant language, the more likely they may be penalized for violating gender norms. While much attention has been given to public responses to women's communication styles, there remains limited empirical evidence regarding whether, how, and to what effect women leaders use counter-stereotypical language in their professional roles. This article seeks to fill that gap.
text: I take an intersectional approach to examine gender differences in leaders' communication via language. A growing body of research finds that, in outgroup settings, people often use counter-stereotypical language. For instance, White liberals use fewer words related to dominance (e.g., "powerful") when addressing mostly Black (versus mostly White) audiences (Dupree and Fiske, 2019), violating stereotypes that depict White Americans as dominant, in a likely well-meaning (but patronizing) phenomenon. Women leaders navigating workplaces composed of mostly men may also use counter-stereotypical language in such workplaces—”language that violates stereotypes depicting women as submissive (Heilman, 2001; Ridgeway, 2001; Eagly, 2007). I propose that women in high-profile leadership positions use dominant language more often than men do, referencing more words related to dominance (e.g., "powerful," "competitive," and "assertive") when addressing peers or the public. Moreover, I suggest that women leaders' use of dominant language has direct implications for media portrayals and public support, such that the more often women leaders use dominant language, the more they will be penalized for it. Although much research has explored public reactions to women's communication, there is little direct evidence of whether and how women leaders use counter-stereotypical language at work and how such use impacts how the media portray and whether the public supports women. This article addresses these issues.

use: To investigate the impact of previously endured distress on Compassionate Action Quality (CAQ), we integrate insights from two key research domains: comforting relationships and the hot-cold empathy gap. First, we draw upon the comforting relationships literature (Burleson, 2009; Miller & Stiver, 1997) to identify the types of behaviors that are most effective in demonstrating high CAQ by alleviating another’s distress. This body of work examines the conditions and interpersonal processes that promote emotional support and relief from suffering (Burleson, 2009; Fletcher, 1998; Miller & Stiver, 1997). It emphasizes that comfort is most effectively conveyed through responses that are sufferer-centric—placing the primary focus on the individual in distress rather than the responder—and validating, meaning they affirm the legitimacy and reasonableness of the sufferer’s experience (Edmond & Keefe, 2015; Fletcher, 1998; Jones & Wirtz, 2006; Miller & Stiver, 1997). Second, to uncover the psychological mechanisms that influence how responders attempt to express compassion, we draw on hot-cold empathy gap theory (Loewenstein, 1996). This framework helps explain how individuals who have (versus have not) previously experienced distress relate to others currently undergoing suffering. Specifically, the theory identifies three key mediating factors through which past personal distress influences CAQ: the extent to which responders focus on themselves versus the sufferer, the degree to which they validate the sufferer’s emotional state, and their level of self-efficacy in offering effective support.
text: To examine the effect of previously endured distress on CAQ, we bring together two lines of research: comforting relationships and the hot-cold empathy gap. First, we draw on the comforting relationship literature (Burleson, 2009; Miller & Stiver, 1997) to illuminate what kind of behaviors are likely to demonstrate high CAQ by alleviating sufferers' distress. The comforting relationships literature focuses on the conditions and processes that facilitate emotional support or relief from distress (Burleson, 2009; Fletcher, 1998; Miller & Stiver, 1997). This literature suggests that comfort is experienced when responses are sufferer-centric (i.e., responses wherein the sufferer is the focus of attention rather than the responder's own experience) and validating (i.e., responses that recognize the sufferer's experience as reasonable and legitimate; Edmond & Keefe, 2015; Fletcher, 1998; Jones & Wirtz, 2006; Miller & Stiver, 1997). Second, to uncover the mechanisms that shape the responders' compassion attempts, we build on hot-cold empathy gap theory (Loewenstein, 1996), which illuminates how responders who have (vs. have not) previously endured distress relate to the distress of sufferers who are currently enduring distress. More specifically, this literature points to three mediators that shape the effect of previously endured distress on CAQ: the responder's self-focus (vs. focusing on the sufferer), validation of the sufferer's distress, and self-efficacy around their ability to help.

use: While prior studies have demonstrated the value of the legacy concept in management research, two key conceptual gaps highlight the need for further theoretical development. First, existing empirical work has predominantly focused on legacy creation within upper echelons—examining the roles of CEOs (Kang, 2016; Matta & Beamish, 2008), founders (Aronoff, 2004; Baker & Wiseman, 1998; Hammond et al., 2016; Jaffe & Lane, 2004; Jaskiewicz et al., 2015), and senior decision-makers who exert influence over firm-level strategies and policies (Bang et al., 2017; Wade-Benzoni et al., 2010, 2012). However, legacy is not confined to the top of the organizational hierarchy. We contend that individuals occupying lower-echelon roles—those not part of the top management team—can also leave meaningful and lasting legacies. Specifically, capable middle managers, informal leaders, and high-performing employees (see Aguinis & O'Boyle, 2014 for a discussion of star performers) can each exert outsized influence. At the very least, these individuals may leave legacies that shape the thoughts, emotions, and behaviors of their colleagues at the group or unit level. At most, their actions can transform aspects of the broader work environment, producing bottom-up effects that resonate throughout the organization (Morgeson, Mitchell, & Liu, 2015). Expanding the concept of legacy to encompass lower-echelon employees thus holds promise for increasing its relevance to core questions in organizational behavior and human resource management.
text: Although these studies have illustrated the value of the legacy concept within management, two conceptual gaps create a need for further theorizing. First, empirical studies have largely centered on the upper echelons—”on CEOs (Kang, 2016; Matta & Beamish, 2008), founders (Aronoff, 2004; Baker & Wiseman, 1998; Hammond et al., 2016; Jaffe & Lane, 2004; Jaskiewicz et al., 2015), and organizational decision-makers with influence over firm-level policies (Bang et al., 2017; Wade-Benzoni et al., 2010; 2012). We believe legacies can also be left in the lower echelons—”by individuals who are not on the top management team, instead occupying positions at lower rungs of the hierarchy. More specifically, legacies can be left by effective managers, capable informal leaders, and star performers who have a disproportionate impact on productivity (see Aguinis & O'Boyle, 2014, for a discussion of the latter). At a minimum, such individuals can leave legacies that continue to shape the cognitions, emotions, and behaviors of the coworkers left behind in their work units. At a maximum, such individuals may alter features of the work environment that impact other corners of the organization in a bottom-up fashion (Morgeson, Mitchell, & Liu, 2015). Extending theorizing on legacy to lower-echelon employees would therefore make the construct more relevant to research questions in organizational behavior and human resource management.

use: Although racial group differences in conscientiousness appear minimal, notable mean differences may emerge based on respondents’ psychiatric disability status (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). This is particularly evident in the case of ADHD, a condition characterized by attention dysregulation (e.g., inattention and hyperfocus), hyperactivity, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, and frequent mind-wandering (Kooij et al., 2019). An ADHD diagnosis does not imply an inability to focus altogether, but rather that individuals with ADHD often require greater motivation than neurotypical individuals to concentrate on relevant stimuli or to disregard irrelevant ones (e.g., Sonuga-Barke, 2005). Clinical studies consistently demonstrate that individuals with ADHD tend to score lower on conscientiousness compared to those without the diagnosis (Gomez & Corr, 2014; Nigg et al., 2002). Stanton and Watson (2016), examining correlations between ADHD symptom severity and conscientiousness facets, found that inattention was strongly negatively correlated with global conscientiousness (r = −.76) as well as with all six facets: self-discipline (r = −.73), self-efficacy (r = −.61), orderliness (r = −.58), cautiousness (r = −.56), dutifulness (r = −.50), and achievement-striving (r = −.44). While extensive research has explored the relationship between ADHD symptoms and global personality traits, studies examining these associations at the facet level remain limited. This gap is important to address, as it may reveal that mean differences in global conscientiousness associated with ADHD are primarily driven by specific facets that are less closely tied to job performance outcomes.
text: Despite a lack of mean differences by race, conscientiousness may show mean differences based on respondent psychiatric disability status (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). This is especially the case for ADHD, which is defined by attention dysregulation (i.e., inattention and hyperfocus), hyperactivity, impulsivity, emotion dysregulation, and excessive mind-wandering (Kooij et al., 2019). An ADHD diagnosis does not mean that a person is unable to pay attention but rather that they may require greater motivation than average neurotypical people to attend to important stimuli or not attend to unimportant stimuli (e.g., Sonuga-Barke, 2005). Clinical research shows that people with (vs. without) ADHD tend to score lower on conscientiousness (Gomez & Corr, 2014; Nigg et al., 2002). Stanton and Watson (2016) examined correlations between ADHD symptom severity and facet scores, finding that inattention is negatively correlated with global conscientiousness (r = −.76) and all six facets: self-discipline (r = −.73), self-efficacy (r = −.61), orderliness (r = −.58), cautiousness (r = .56), dutifulness (r = −.50), and achievement-striving (r = −.44). Compared to the literature on associations between ADHD symptoms and global personality traits, facet-level personality research remains relatively sparse. Such research is important, as it may reveal that ADHD-based differences on global conscientiousness are driven primarily by differences on facets that are less predictive of job performance.

use: We propose that these seemingly inconsistent findings may stem from a mismatch between the level at which conflict is experienced (intra-individual) and the level at which it is often theorized (inter-individual). First, conflict is fundamentally experienced within the individual (Ilies, Johnson, Judge, & Keeney, 2011; Rispens & Demerouti, 2016). However, prevailing conceptualizations of individual conflict often overlook this by conflating the direction of conflict—whether an individual is the source or recipient—or by focusing solely on aggregated evaluations of conflict amount or frequency. Such summaries may blend together distinct manifestations of conflict that vary in form and social target (Park, Mathieu, & Grosser, 2020). This broad, aggregated approach obscures the variability of conflict experiences within individuals, thereby limiting our understanding of how conflict impacts them. Second, the contextual boundary of the conflict—whether it occurs within or across teams—plays a pivotal role, shaping how individuals interpret and respond to these experiences. These boundaries act as psychological frames (Maloney, Bresman, Zellmer-Bruhn, & Beaver, 2016), creating a sense of separation between teams (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018) and, in turn, influencing the subjective nature of the conflict experience (e.g., de Vries, Hollenbeck, Davison, Walter, & van der Vegt, 2016; Porck et al., 2019). Collectively, advancing our understanding of how individual conflict influences broader collectives requires disentangling these two structural dimensions of conflict—direction and boundary—as individuals continuously shift between tasks and social contexts over time.
text: We suggest that these disparate findings may emanate from a misalignment between the levels of the phenomenon (intra-individual) and theory (inter-individual). First, at its core, the experience of conflict is an intra-individual phenomenon (Ilies, Johnson, Judge, & Keeney, 2011; Rispens & Demerouti, 2016). Yet, traditional conceptualizations of individual conflict lump together the directions (sending vs. receiving) of conflict experiences, or focus only on the summary judgments of conflict amount or frequency that may be an amalgamation of conflict manifestations that have occurred in different ways and with different others (Park, Mathieu, & Grosser, 2020). This broad way of theorizing inter-individual conflict limits our understanding of conflict effects on individuals because it masks the within-person variance in conflict experiences. Second, the boundaries (within- vs. between-team) of individual conflict experiences have important implications, as they serve as a contextual lens (Maloney, Bresman, Zellmer-Bruhn, & Beaver, 2016), which creates psychological separation between teams (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018), and, thus, alters the nature of individual experiences (e.g., de Vries, Hollenbeck, Davison, Walter, & van der Vegt, 2016; Porck, Matta, Hollenbeck, Oh, Lanaj, & Lee, 2019). Taken together, advancing our understanding of individual conflict effects in collectives requires unpacking these two structural elements of individual conflict experience (i.e., directions and boundaries) as individuals engage in different tasks over time.

use: Appraisal-tendency perspectives (Beal, 2014; Lerner & Keltner, 2000; Lerner et al., 2015) propose that experiencing awe upon awakening establishes a lasting cognitive frame of reference—or appraisal tendency—that shapes how employees interpret subsequent work-related stressors. Specifically, awe encourages employees to evaluate daily challenges in light of their broader significance, perceiving them through the lens of "the grand scheme of things." This shift in perspective enhances employee resilience, defined as the capacity to form adaptive interpretations of workplace stressors (Fletcher & Sarkar, 2013). Central to appraisal-tendency theory is the idea that emotions generate "a cognitive predisposition to appraise future events in line with the central appraisal dimensions that triggered the emotion" (Lerner & Keltner, 2000: 477). Awe, in particular, gives rise to a "small-self" appraisal tendency—a diminished focus on personal concerns and ego (e.g., Piff, Dietze, Feinberg, Stancato, & Keltner, 2015; Shiota et al., 2007). This cognitive stance continues to shape perception and judgment even after the emotion itself has subsided (Beal, 2014). As a result, employees are likely to perceive subsequent stressors as less threatening and more surmountable than they would otherwise, thereby bolstering their resilience. Moreover, since greater resilience fosters more effective coping behaviors—strategies to manage external demands (Fletcher & Sarkar, 2013)—experiencing awe is also expected to enhance employees’ progress toward their daily work goals. Accordingly, we hypothesize that experiencing awe upon awakening enhances employee resilience, which in turn promotes greater progress on daily work goals.
text: Appraisal-tendency perspectives (Beal, 2014; Lerner & Keltner, 2000; Lerner et al., 2015) suggest that experiencing awe upon awakening will create a lasting frame of reference, or appraisal tendency, that encourages employees to appraise subsequent work stressors relative to their importance "in the grand scheme of things." This will increase employee resilience, or the formation of adaptive appraisals of stressful situations at work (Fletcher & Sarkar, 2013). A core tenet of appraisal-tendency theory is that emotions engender "a cognitive predisposition to appraise future events in line with the central appraisal dimensions that triggered the emotion" (Lerner & Keltner, 2000: 477). Awe engenders a small self appraisal tendency characterized by a reduced focus on oneself and one's concerns (e.g., Piff, Dietze, Feinberg, Stancato, & Keltner, 2015; Shiota et al., 2007). This small-self appraisal tendency acts as a cognitive lens that colors the interpretation of events, even after the affective experience of awe has dissipated (Beal, 2014). Thus, employees will view subsequent work stressors as less threatening and more manageable than they otherwise would, increasing their resilience. Further, because resilience promotes effective coping behaviors, or efforts to manage external demands (Fletcher & Sarkar, 2013), awe will also increase employee work goal progress. We therefore hypothesize that experiencing awe upon awakening increases employee resilience, and ultimately daily work goal progress.

use: When examined at the group level, theories of contagious turnover from sociology and social psychology emphasize how negative job attitudes can become collectively shared and amplified through psychosocial mechanisms such as social comparison, informational influence, emotional contagion, and group sensemaking (Bartunek et al., 2008; Felps et al., 2009). One common trajectory involves a dissatisfied group member voicing their discontent, intentions to quit, or progress in seeking alternative employment—such as sharing signals of labor market desirability and ease of movement—before actually resigning (Bartunek et al., 2008; March & Simon, 1958; Porter & Rigby, 2021). According to social comparison and social information processing theories (Festinger, 1954; Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978), individuals depend heavily on similar others—like members of their own group—to interpret organizational realities. Consequently, one member’s decision to resign can prompt others to focus more closely on the negative features of their own work environment or to legitimize their existing dissatisfaction. It may also lead them to reevaluate their marketability and confidence in securing alternative employment. These shifts in thought, emotion, and behavior surrounding turnover can increase the probability that fellow group members will also depart, potentially creating a “snowball” effect in which exits multiply contagiously (David et al., 2015; Krackhardt & Porter, 1985, 1986; Porter & Rigby, 2021; Wang, Yuan, et al., 2016).
text: Specifically, when aggregated to groups, theorizing on contagious turnover in sociology and social psychology highlights how negative job attitudes may often become shared across a group and strengthened via psychosocial processes such as social comparison, informational influence, emotional contagion, and group sensemaking (Bartunek et al., 2008; Felps et al., 2009). One form this path can take is that a dissatisfied group member expresses to other group members their disparaging opinions, turnover intentions, and/or alternative job search efforts and results (i.e., desirability and ease-of-movement cues; Bartunek et al., 2008; March & Simon, 1958; Porter & Rigby, 2021) before actually quitting. As social comparison and social information processing theories (Festinger, 1954; Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978) argue that individuals rely heavily on similar others, such as group members, to construct their own organizational realities, one group member's turnover decision can affect other members, leading them to attend more to negative aspects of their own work environment (or validating their preexisting negative perceptions) and to reconsider their own labor market competitiveness and potential job search efficacy. All of these turnover-related thoughts, feelings, and/or behaviors should increase the likelihood of other members' turnover behavior, swelling into a contagious "snowball" of group member exits (David et al., 2015; Krackhardt & Porter, 1985, 1986; Porter & Rigby, 2021; Wang, Yuan, et al., 2016).

use: Our findings replicate and extend prior research by examining differences in self-reported conscientiousness between individuals with and without ADHD. Specifically, we explored variations at both the trait and facet levels, assessed measurement equivalence, and evaluated the potential of item contextualization to mitigate group differences. This work is critical for ensuring that widely used self-report selection tools (a) accurately capture job-relevant behaviors and (b) permit valid score comparisons across diverse groups. We found that specifying items as referring to workplace behavior—rather than behavior in general—reduced ADHD-related differences in conscientiousness and its facets. Moreover, the largest disparities appeared in those facets that are relatively weak predictors of job performance (Ones et al., 2007), suggesting that overall group differences may be driven more by variation in less job-relevant facets. Additionally, we found that measures of conscientiousness may lack psychometric equivalence across ADHD and non-ADHD groups, casting doubt on the appropriateness of direct score comparisons. Taken together, these findings challenge the common view that conscientiousness-based assessments are free from diversity-related concerns associated with cognitive ability measures (Foldes et al., 2008). Instead, our results reveal meaningful differences in how items are interpreted and highlight the importance of facet-level analyses. As definitions of workplace diversity increasingly include neurodiversity, both researchers and practitioners must critically assess whether selection instruments designed for neurotypical populations effectively capture the job-relevant capabilities of neurodivergent individuals.
text: Our findings replicated and extended previous research by investigating ADHD-based differences in self-report measures of conscientiousness, its facets, and its measurement properties, as well as the potential mitigating impact of item contextualization. This is crucial for ensuring that commonly used self-report selection measures (a) reflect job-relevant behavior and (b) allow valid comparisons of respondents’ scores. We found that clarifying that items refer to behavior at work rather than in general reduced ADHD-based differences in conscientiousness and its facets. Further, the facets with the largest group differences were those that are relatively less predictive of job performance (Ones et al., 2007), indicating that large overall group differences on conscientiousness might reflect differences on less job-relevant facets. We also found that conscientiousness measures may not be psychometrically equivalent across those with and without ADHD. This calls into question whether it is appropriate to compare conscientiousness scores across these two groups. Overall, our findings challenge the general assumption that conscientiousness addresses diversity-related concerns raised by other cognitive measures (Foldes et al., 2008) by highlighting differences in item interpretation and facet-level variation in the magnitude of group differences. As definitions of workplace diversity evolve to include neurodiversity, both scholars and practitioners must consider whether selection tools developed for neurotypical people are designed to reflect the job-relevant capabilities of neurodivergent respondents.

use: First, when motivation is fully autonomous, initiative is likely to be positively associated with performance. In other words, individuals driven by autonomous motivation—without the influence of controlled motivation—are most likely to engage in initiative in ways that are effective and productive. As previously discussed, autonomously motivated individuals tend to consistently display sustained attention, intrinsic interest, energy, and enthusiasm, all of which contribute to high-quality effort (Ryan & Deci, 2000). Second, when motivation is entirely controlled, initiative is less likely to enhance performance. This is because controlled motivation typically leads to mere compliance rather than genuine engagement, making it more probable that individuals will take initiative in a perfunctory or minimal-effort manner, fulfilling obligations without exceeding them (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Kelman, 1958; Sussmann & Vecchio, 1982). Third, when individuals lack both autonomous and controlled motivation, initiative is again unlikely to yield performance benefits. The absence of both motivational sources often results in apathy (Ryan & Deci, 2000), leading to behavior that lacks intentionality, direction, and focus. As a result, any initiative taken under these conditions is likely to be weak, unfocused, or minimal in effort. Taken together, these points highlight why high levels of autonomous motivation combined with low levels of controlled motivation are most conducive to effective initiative, while low autonomous motivation—regardless of controlled motivation levels—tends to produce less impactful efforts.
text: First, when motivation is purely autonomous, initiative will be positively related to performance. In other words, individuals experiencing autonomous but not controlled motivation will engage in the most effective forms of initiative. As noted above, individuals guided purely by autonomous motivation are likely to maintain attention, interest, energy, and enthusiasm, facilitating effective effort (Ryan & Deci, 2000). Second, when motivation is purely controlled, initiative will be less likely to contribute to higher performance. This is because purely controlled motivation tends to foster mere compliance, which makes it more likely that individuals who take initiative will do so in a lackluster manner or with the minimum levels of effort necessary to fulfill perceived obligations (Gagné & Deci, 2005; Kelman, 1958; Sussmann & Vecchio, 1982). Third, when motivation is neither autonomous nor controlled, initiative will also be unlikely to contribute to higher performance. When these two motivations are lacking, individuals tend to experience apathy (Ryan & Deci, 2000). Thus, when they do take initiative, they are likely to do so with a lack of direction, intentionality, and focus - or again with lackluster effort. Together, these arguments explain why the combination of high autonomous and low controlled motivation will be associated with more effective initiative than when low autonomous motivation is coupled with either high or low controlled motivation.

use: To understand when and why conflict experiences influence individuals, we center our analysis on the allocation of cognitive resources—specifically, the self-regulatory process of attentional focus (Beal et al., 2005). “Attentional focus,” defined as the degree to which an individual channels cognitive resources toward a task (Kahn, 1990; Rothbard, 2001), is essential for effective task execution (Beal et al., 2005; Kahneman, 1973). Throughout task engagement, individuals often encounter a range of conflict experiences. A key concern is that cognitive capacity is inherently limited and becomes taxed when individuals are simultaneously engaged in competing mental demands (Kanfer & Ackerman, 1989). Conflict-related concerns represent such off-task demands, drawing attention away from focal tasks and requiring individuals to make decisions about where to direct their limited attentional resources (Hirst & Kalmar, 1987; Kahneman, 1973). We argue that this decision-making process constitutes a form of self-regulation, as it involves “effortful attempts to control and alter naturally occurring behaviors or mental states” (Beal et al., 2005: 1058). Depending on how individuals manage this self-regulatory challenge, they may choose to reallocate attention toward off-task conflict concerns—or resist doing so—which directly affects their cognitive engagement with the task at hand. Therefore, the influence of conflict experiences on task performance can be more fully understood by examining the cognitive mechanisms involved in individuals’ self-regulatory management of attentional focus.
text: To explore when and why conflict experiences affect individuals, we focus on the allocation of cognitive resources, and, more specifically, the self-regulatory process of attentional focus (Beal et al., 2005). “Attentional focus,” or the amount of cognitive resources a person is allocating to the task (Kahn, 1990; Rothbard, 2001), is critical for task accomplishment (Beal et al., 2005; Kahneman, 1973). Individual members can be subject to various conflict experiences throughout their task engagement. At issue is that individuals possess limited cognitive capacity, which is strained by the concurrent cognitive tasks they are involved in (Kanfer & Ackerman, 1989). “Conflict issues” constitute off-task concerns that require individuals to determine how to allocate their attentional foci (Hirst & Kalmar, 1987; Kahneman, 1973). We suggest that the decision regarding the allocation of attentional focus is, to some extent, a form of self-regulation, as it entails “effortful attempts to control and alter naturally occurring behaviors or mental states” (Beal et al., 2005: 1058). Depending on this self-regulatory decision, individuals may or may not reallocate attentional focus to off-task concerns (e.g., conflict issues), which, in turn, determines the attentional focus toward their currently engaged task. In sum, the impact of conflict experiences on individual task performance can be better understood by integrating cognitive mechanisms related to individuals’ self-regulatory processes of attentional focus.

use: Voluntary turnover imposes significant costs and disruptions on organizations, prompting scholars to investigate how and why high-performing employees choose to leave, and how such departures might be prevented. Much of the existing research has concentrated on identifying antecedents to voluntary turnover, with Lee and Mitchell’s (1994) unfolding model offering a foundational framework. This model outlines five distinct paths employees may follow in reaching the decision to quit, and subsequent research has highlighted the wide variety of experiences that can underlie voluntary departures. However, less attention has been paid to the more affective, visceral aspects of these experiences—particularly how they may differ across the model’s various paths and how they might influence what employees encounter after they leave (e.g., Shipp et al., 2014). Although anecdotal accounts suggest that some turnover experiences are not only dissatisfying but also emotionally painful, the concept of social pain—defined as “the unpleasant experience that is associated with actual or potential damage to one's sense of social connection or social value” (Eisenberger, 2012, p. 421)—has yet to be substantially integrated into turnover scholarship. This study seeks to advance turnover theory by examining the following question: How does the experience of social pain vary among individuals departing via different paths in the unfolding model, and how might such painful experiences influence the criteria and choices involved in selecting their next job with a new employer?
text: Voluntary turnover creates substantial costs and disruptions for organizations, so scholars seek to better understand and prevent the departure of high-performing employees. A great deal of research has focused on precursors to voluntary turnover, and Lee and Mitchell's (1994) unfolding model identified five paths that employees may travel toward the ultimate decision to quit. This model, and the research it has inspired, demonstrates that voluntary turnover can include a diverse array of experiences. However, little research has explored how the more visceral elements of voluntary turnover may differ among paths or how those elements could impact employee experiences after they quit (e.g., Shipp et al., 2014). Despite anecdotal evidence that some experiences of voluntary turnover are associated with not only dissatisfaction but also pain, the role of social pain in turnover and subsequent job selection has not yet received much scholarly attention. Social pain is "the unpleasant experience that is associated with actual or potential damage to one's sense of social connection or social value" (Eisenberger, 2012, p. 421). This inquiry aims to expand turnover theory by addressing the question: How might the experience of social pain differ for individuals who quit according to different paths of the unfolding model, and how might these painful experiences relate to the selection of a new job with a new employer?

use: A third unresolved issue concerns how so-called professionals form a deep, personal connection to their work—something inherently individual—while simultaneously attaining legitimacy as professionals—a status granted by others. Prior research has shown that a key aspect of identity work in professional contexts involves aligning one’s personal sense of self with the role identity expected by the profession (Ashforth & Schinoff, 2016). For instance, Pratt, Rockmann, and Kaufmann (2006) studied medical residents as they navigated the early stages of their careers. Faced with discrepancies between their self-perceptions and the realities of their daily tasks, these residents engaged in “identity customization,” revising their understanding of who they were to bring their evolving identities in line with the demands and experiences of their profession. Similarly, Ibarra’s (1999) research on junior consultants transitioning into senior roles found that professionals undertook “adaptation tasks”—such as observing role models and experimenting with provisional selves—in order to build coherent and socially appropriate work identities. Chreim, Williams, and Hinings (2007) further demonstrated that when professional role identities shift, institutional and organizational forces often require individuals to reshape their work identities to align with those new expectations. Collectively, these studies suggest that being recognized as a legitimate professional necessitates the continual adaptation of one’s work-related identity to conform with evolving professional standards and roles.
text: A third unresolved issue centers on how called professionals achieve an intimate connection between self and work (something uniquely personal) while also becoming legitimate professionals (something conferred by others). Existing studies have established that a fundamental aspect of identity work for a professional is constructing an identity that aligns with the role identity of their profession (Ashforth and Schinoff, 2016). Pratt, Rockmann, and Kaufmann (2006), for example, studied residents as they sought to construct professional identities while entering the medical profession. In response to initial inconsistencies between the ways they saw themselves and the types of work they did, residents engaged in identity "customization," changing their understanding of who they were so that their identities were consistent with their situated experiences. In her study of junior consultants transitioning to more senior roles, Ibarra (1999) found that professionals engaged in "adaptation tasks" such as observing role models and experimenting with provisional selves, which helped them construct stable and positive identities that were appropriate for their new jobs. Chreim, Williams, and Hinings (2007) found that when professional role identities change, institutional and organizational pressures compel individual role occupants to adapt their work identity to fit the new role identity. These studies show that to be a legitimate professional requires tailoring one's work-related identity so that it fits the professional role.

use:
Introducing a work-specific frame of reference (FOR) to conscientiousness assessments may prove advantageous for both individuals with ADHD and employers, as this context enhances the predictive validity of conscientiousness for job-related outcomes (Bing et al., 2004; Fisher et al., 2017; Holtz et al., 2005; Hunthausen et al., 2003; Lievens et al., 2008; Schmit et al., 1995). Contextualizing personality assessments is a methodological consideration that shapes how individuals respond, grounded in the understanding that trait expression—reflected in attitudes, behaviors, and cognitions—varies across different settings (Lievens & Sackett, 2017; Tett & Burnett, 2003; Wright & Mischel, 1987). Given that research on employees with ADHD indicates their conscientious behavior is context-sensitive (e.g., Lasky et al., 2016), we propose the following hypothesis:  
Hypothesis 1: Applying a work-specific FOR to conscientiousness items will reduce the mean differences in conscientiousness and its individual facets—typically observed on decontextualized measures—between individuals with and without ADHD.

Furthermore, as a supplementary exploration, we seek to determine whether individuals with and without ADHD interpret conscientiousness measures in comparable ways. If the psychometric properties of conscientiousness self-reports differ by ADHD status—indicating measurement noninvariance—this would raise concerns about whether observed score differences genuinely reflect variations in conscientiousness levels or instead stem from disparate interpretations of the items, unrelated to the intended underlying construct.
text:
Imposing a work FOR on conscientiousness measures could be beneficial both to respondents with ADHD and to employers due to its work focus, which strengthens the predictive validity of conscientiousness for work outcomes (Bing et al., 2004; Fisher et al., 2017; Holtz et al., 2005; Hunthausen et al., 2003; Lievens et al., 2008; Schmit et al., 1995). Contextualizing personality assessments is a methodological factor that influences how information is elicited from respondents, relying on the logic that people’s trait expressions (i.e., the stability of attitudes, behaviors, and cognitions) differ across contexts (Lievens & Sackett, 2017; Tett & Burnett, 2003; Wright & Mischel, 1987). Therefore, given that research on workers with ADHD suggests that their conscientious behavior depends on contextual factors (e.g., Lasky et al., 2016), we hypothesize:
Hypothesis 1: Imposing a work-specific FOR on conscientiousness items decreases mean differences in conscientiousness and its facets, based on ADHD status, that are typically observed on decontextualized measures.

Additionally, in an exploratory manner, we are interested in understanding whether conscientiousness measures are interpreted similarly by people with and without ADHD. If conscientiousness self-report measures have different psychometric properties across groups (i.e., there is measurement noninvariance), this calls into question whether observed conscientiousness scores reflect differences in actual levels of conscientiousness or differences in how items are interpreted, independent from the latent construct being assessed.

use: The third scholarly community adopts a descriptive perspective, examining specific behaviors as constructs such as cross-domain communication (Wan, Shaffer, Lau, & Cheung, 2019), setting-inconsistent mobile communication (Lutz, Schneider, & Vorderer, 2020), social media use (Charoensukmongkol, 2014; Lu, Zhong, Sun, & Qin, 2023), personal web usage (Anandarajan, Simmers, & D'Ovidio, 2011; Mahatanankoon, Anandarajan, & Igbaria, 2004), online news consumption (Andel, Arvan, & Shen, 2021), personal blogging (Bizzi, 2020), ICT use for personal purposes at work (Chesley, 2014), personal smartphone use in the workplace (Derks, Bakker, & Gorgievski, 2021; Lekkas, Price, & Jacobson, 2022; Patterer, Yanagida, Kühnel, & Korunka, 2021), and nonwork-related interruptions on the job (Horvath, Gueulette, & Gray, 2021; Russo et al., 2018). This descriptive stream spans management, cyberpsychology, and MIS literatures—similar to the counterproductive work behavior (CWB) community in disciplinary reach—and shares with the recovery literature a common focus on use-related outcomes. However, the descriptive community diverges notably from both the CWB and recovery traditions by refraining from making normative judgments at the outset about whether a behavior is inherently positive or negative. For instance, behaviors are examined under neutral labels like "personal web usage" rather than terms charged with evaluative assumptions such as "cyberslacking" or "microbreak." These studies tend to adopt a neutral stance, acknowledging that the behavior in question may yield both benefits and drawbacks, and they typically assess outcomes post hoc, based on whether empirical evidence supports or challenges the hypotheses and the theoretical frameworks from which those hypotheses are derived.
text: The third community takes a descriptive approach, studying specific behaviors as constructs like cross-domain communication (Wan, Shaffer, Lau, & Cheung, 2019), setting-inconsistent mobile communication (Lutz, Schneider, & Vorderer, 2020), social media use (Charoensukmongkol, 2014; Lu, Zhong, Sun, & Qin, 2023), personal web usage (Anandarajan, Simmers, & D'Ovidio, 2011; Mahatanankoon, Anandarajan, & Igbaria, 2004), online news consumption (Andel, Arvan, & Shen, 2021), personal blogging (Bizzi, 2020), ICT use for personal purposes at work (Chesley, 2014), personal smartphone use at work (Derks, Bakker, & Gorgievski, 2021; Lekkas, Price, & Jacobson, 2022; Patterer, Yanagida, Kühnel, & Korunka, 2021), and nonwork-related interruptions at work (Horvath, Gueulette, & Gray, 2021; Russo et al., 2018). The descriptive community encompasses management, cyberpsychology, and MIS literatures (like the CWB community) and primarily focuses on use-related outcomes (like the recovery community). However, a critical departure from both the CWB and recovery communities is that the descriptive community avoids a priori framing about whether the construct or behavior is "good" or "bad" (e.g., personal web usage is studied as "personal web usage" rather than "cyberslacking" or a "microbreak"). Instead, these studies have often treated the behavior as somewhat neutral (recognizing that it can involve both pros and cons) and made post hoc evaluations based on whether hypotheses and the underlying theories on which the hypotheses were based are confirmed or rejected.

use: What do researchers focus on when they study various forms of proactive behavior, and how do they measure it? If we approached proactivity as a general phenomenon, what aspects would we seek to understand? In this section, our aim is not to provide a bibliographic overview of the diverse literatures on proactivity, but rather to uncover the key dimensions along which proactive behavior—viewed as a broad category of action—differs. Our review of the literature reveals that proactive behavior can be distinguished along at least five dimensions: form, intended target of impact, frequency, timing, and tactics. We selected these dimensions based on two guiding criteria. First, they emerge across multiple streams of research on proactivity. Second, drawing on the idea that folk schemas can be valuable tools for developing conceptual frameworks (e.g., Adler & Kwon, 2002), we utilized a journalistic schema that suggests behavior can be comprehensively described by answering the questions “who, what, when, where, how, and why” (e.g., Stovall, 2005). In our model, the "who" is represented both by the employee engaging in the proactive behavior and by the intended target of its impact; the "what" is reflected in the behavior's form and frequency; the "when" and "where" are encapsulated by timing; and the "how" is illustrated through the tactics employed. We address the question of "why"—the motivations and underlying mechanisms—in the following section on antecedents.
text: When researchers study different proactive behaviors, what do they care about, and what do they measure? If we were to study proactivity as a general phenomenon, what would we want to know? In this section, our goal is not to portray the various literatures on proactivity bibliographically, but rather to gain insights into the different dimensions along which proactive behavior, as a general category of action, varies. Our review of proactivity research indicates that proactive behavior varies along at least five dimensions: form, intended target of impact, frequency, timing, and tactics. We chose these dimensions on the basis of two criteria. First, the dimensions appeared in multiple literatures on proactive behavior. Second, based on suggestions that folk schemas have value for constructing conceptual frameworks (e.g., Adler & Kwon, 2002), we drew on one such folk schema from journalism suggesting that behavior is adequately described by answering the questions "who, what, when, where, how, and why" (e.g., Stovall, 2005). In our dimensions, the "who" is captured in part by the employee enacting the proactive behavior and in part by the intended target of impact; the "what" is captured by form and frequency; the "when" and "where" are captured by timing; and the "how" is captured by the tactics used. We reserve discussion of the "why" for a subsequent section on antecedents and mechanisms.

use:
Management scholars have shown growing interest in how social organizations can generate social impact by tackling grand societal challenges and advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs; George, Howard-Grenville, Joshi, & Tihanyi, 2016), with recent scholarship urging more active engagement (Howard-Grenville, Davis, Dyllick, Thau, & Tsui, 2019). On the frontlines of these efforts are fieldworkers—including community health workers, educators, and microfinance agents—who possess intimate knowledge of and engagement with local communities (Leana, Appelbaum, & Shevchuk, 2009). These individuals serve as vital links between organizations and the communities they aim to support, playing an essential role in delivering meaningful social impact (Stephan, Patterson, Kelly, & Mair, 2016). Despite their significance, we still know surprisingly little about how fieldworkers contribute to the achievement of social impact (Stephan et al., 2016).

This paper adopts a novel theoretical lens to explore the contributions of fieldworkers in advancing the SDGs: the job-crafting perspective. Job crafting refers to the proactive efforts individuals make to reshape their roles, including redefining work boundaries, to enhance the meaning and value of their work (Bruning & Campion, 2018; De Bloom, Vaziri, Tay, & Kujanpää, 2020; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Foundational work by Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) suggested that professionals such as nurses and teachers enrich their roles by serving beneficiaries in ways that go beyond formal job descriptions, adding personal significance to their work. Subsequent research on lower-tier employees (e.g., Berg, Wrzesniewski, & Dutton, 2010; Leana et al., 2009) has confirmed that job crafting is a widespread phenomenon, demonstrating how workers at all levels actively shape their tasks to create meaning and enhance impact.
text:
Management scholars are increasingly interested in understanding how social organizations may achieve social impact by addressing grand challenges in society and attaining sustainable development goals (SDGs; George, Howard-Grenville, Joshi, & Tihanyi, 2016), with recent calls to action (Howard-Grenville, Davis, Dyllick, Thau, & Tsui, 2019). Fieldworkers, such as community health workers, teachers, and microfinance agents, who understand and act upon local circumstances (Leana, Appelbaum, & Shevchuk, 2009), connect these organizations to target groups in local communities, and are crucial for attaining social impact (Stephan, Patterson, Kelly, & Mair, 2016). Yet, at present, we know very little about the role these workers play in attaining social impact (Stephan et al., 2016).

In this paper, we apply a fresh theoretical lens to understand fieldworkers’ role in addressing SDGs; namely, a job-crafting perspective. This perspective implies that individuals proactively change their roles, including the boundaries of their work, in ways that are meaningful to them (Bruning & Campion, 2018; De Bloom, Vaziri, Tay, & Kujanpää, 2020; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Initial theorists (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001) argued that workers such as nurses and teachers serve beneficiaries in additional ways as part of shaping their roles meaningfully. Later evidence for lower-ranked employees (Berg, Wrzesniewski, & Dutton, 2010; Leana et al., 2009) demonstrates that these employees do indeed proactively shape what they do in ways that are meaningful for them.

use: Building on social identity theory, identity-based research—including work grounded in the group-value model (Lind & Tyler, 1988), the relational model of authority (Tyler & Lind, 1992), and the group engagement model (Tyler & Blader, 2003)—proposes that fair treatment serves as a critical social signal for self-concept formation and development, which in turn fosters voluntary cooperative behavior. When individuals perceive justice, it affirms their self-worth and standing within the group, thereby strengthening the evaluative dimension of the self-concept, such as group-based self-esteem and perceived social status (i.e., positive self-assessments; Blader & Tyler, 2009). Additionally, justice conveys that affiliating with the fair party is both safe and beneficial, encouraging individuals to align their self-concept with that of the group as a whole or with specific members (Holmvall & Bobocel, 2008). This interdependent form of identity—reflecting the cognitive aspect of the self-concept—drives individuals to prioritize the group's needs and goals as central to their work behavior (Tyler & Blader, 2003). What unites both the evaluative and cognitive facets of self-concept development is a sense of “oneness”: the perception that the individual and the group are psychologically intertwined, which leads to intrinsic motivation to advance the group’s success (Tajfel & Turner, 1979). While both exchange-based and identity-based mechanisms offer explanations for justice effects, the differing core motivations they activate—enhancing self-interest versus advancing collective welfare—may result in varying levels of influence on key organizational outcomes.
text: Drawing from social identity theory, identity-based research (e.g., studies involving the group-value model, Lind & Tyler, 1988; the relational model of authority, Tyler & Lind, 1992; and the group engagement model, Tyler & Blader, 2003) argues that fair treatment conveys important social information for self-concept development, which elicits voluntary cooperative behavior. Justice affirms employees' self-worth and social standing within the group, boosting the evaluative component of the self-concept, such as group-based self-esteem or felt status (i.e., positive self-evaluations; Blader & Tyler, 2009). Justice also indicates that it is safe and beneficial to merge one's self-concept with the fair party, fostering identity with the group as a whole and/or with specific members (Holmvall & Bobocel, 2008). Such interdependent identity, which captures a cognitive component of the self-concept, then propels employees to place the needs and interests of the group as the driving force of their work behavior (Tyler & Blader, 2003). The common thread underlying both evaluative- and cognitive-based self-concept development is the sense of "oneness"—”that is, the self and the group are integrated as overlapping psychological entities—”which intrinsically motivate employees to enhance the success of the group (Tajfel & Turner, 1979). Although both exchange- and identity-based mechanisms explain justice effects, their distinct underlying motivations to enhance self- versus group interests and welfare can lead to variations in their mediating power across key outcomes.

use: Our study offers three key contributions. First, we identify new antecedents of safety behavior—work context construal, perceived harm, and ethical framing—which help illuminate why employees may fail to recognize the consequences of their decisions. This extends the safety literature (e.g., D. A. Hofmann et al., 2017; Neal & Griffin, 2006) by addressing an underexplored dimension: the role of ethics in decision-making related to safety. Second, we reveal a potentially overlooked downside of abstract construals—that individuals may lose sight of potential harm, thereby diminishing their awareness of the ethical implications of their actions. This advances construal level theory by highlighting an unintended consequence of abstract thinking (i.e., a reduced perception of harm), and responds to recent calls for more nuanced understanding of whether the relationship between construal level and ethical behavior is inherently positive or negative (e.g., Wiesenfeld et al., 2017). Third, we provide a cautionary perspective on emerging trends in safety-oriented work design, particularly remote work arrangements. As work design increasingly separates operators from their work contexts—for example, through remote monitoring or drone inspections (Grubic, 2018)—the perception of harm may diminish even as physical risks are mitigated. If this perceptual shift occurs, the full safety benefits of such technological innovations may be compromised.
text: Our work makes three key contributions. First, we identify novel antecedents of safety behavior—work context construal, perceived harm, and ethical framing—which may help to explain how employees may not be able to “see” the consequences of their decisions. This adds to the safety literature (e.g., D. A. Hofmann et al., 2017; Neal & Griffin, 2006), where the ethics of decision making has not received much attention. Second, we document a potential downside of abstract construals which has yet to be fully investigated: individuals losing sight of the potential harm and, therefore, being less aware of the ethical implication of their decisions. This contributes to construal level theory by suggesting a possible unintended consequence of abstract construals (i.e., reduced perception of harm) and heeding calls to better understand whether the relationship between construal level and ethical behavior is positive or negative (e.g., Wiesenfeld et al., 2017). Third, we offer a cautionary note to recent trends in safety-related work design by highlighting a potential downside of certain remote work arrangements. Increasingly, work design changes are distancing the operator and the work context (e.g., remote monitoring; drone inspections; Grubic, 2018). Although these innovations can reduce risks in many ways (e.g., injury to the employee doing the inspection), if the perception of harm is concurrently reduced, then the full benefits of these technologies may not be realized.

use: We examine three key dimensions of the informational environment: information availability, a firm-level construct capturing the volume of information that exists about a firm; information reliability, a country-level construct reflecting the degree to which media information is perceived as trustworthy; and thematic content, a dynamic, time-sensitive construct that indicates the extent to which media coverage emphasizes ESG-related topics. Prior theoretical work highlights both the sheer volume of media coverage (i.e., information availability) and the nature of the topics raised in that coverage (i.e., thematic content) as critical attributes shaping how firms are represented in the public domain (Carroll & Deephouse, 2014; Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020). To complement these perspectives, we introduce a third and often overlooked dimension—information reliability—that varies widely across countries and fluctuates over time within individual countries. Although scholars have called for greater focus on the broader informational contexts in which corporate information is disseminated (Connelly, Certo, Ireland, & Reutzel, 2011), and adjacent fields have increasingly engaged with the changing dynamics of media trust and reliability (Allcott & Gentzkow, 2017; Lazer et al., 2018; Osmundsen, Bor, Vahlstrup, Bechmann, & Petersen, 2021; Pennycook & Rand, 2021), such considerations have yet to be incorporated into mainstream management research on media effects. Consequently, diverging from much of the existing literature that assumes stakeholders treat media content as inherently reliable, we emphasize that perceptions of media reliability vary substantially—shaping how stakeholders interpret and respond to the information they encounter.
text: We consider three dimensions of the informational environment: information availability, a firm-level construct describing the amount of information that exists about a firm; information reliability, a country-level construct defining the extent to which media information can be trusted; and thematic content, a time-varying construct that reflects the extent to which information available about a firm focuses on ESG issues. Existing theoretical work has suggested that the volume of coverage (i.e., information availability) and the topics included in this coverage (i.e., thematic content) are two important attributes of media coverage of firms (Carroll & Deephouse, 2014; Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020).1 We also consider a third dimension—information reliability—that varies significantly across countries and within countries across time. Despite calls for more research on the environment within which information is exchanged (Connelly, Certo, Ireland, & Reutzel, 2011) and growing attention to shifts in the informational environment in related disciplines (Allcott & Gentzkow, 2017; Lazer et al., 2018; Osmundsen, Bor, Vahlstrup, Bechmann, & Petersen, 2021; Pennycook & Rand, 2021), this has not been studied in prior management research on the media’s impact on corporate organizations (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020). Thus, in contrast to prior organizational research that has largely taken for granted that stakeholders perceive media information as reliable, we stress that the reliability of media information varies, thus shaping how stakeholders perceive the information they receive.

use: We propose that impression management motives prompt employees to express their prosocial inclinations through affiliative forms of citizenship behavior—such as helping, demonstrating courtesy, and showing initiative. Impression management theory suggests that individuals with strong motives to manage how they are perceived are particularly mindful of avoiding actions that might damage their image in the eyes of others (Jones & Pittman, 1982; Leary & Kowalski, 1990). Therefore, employees who are driven by both prosocial and impression management motives are expected to favor affiliative behaviors that support others and the organization, while safeguarding their own reputations. Supporting this perspective, research in lifespan developmental psychology by McAdams and de St. Aubin (1992) found that individuals can concurrently satisfy prosocial and self-enhancing goals by engaging in contributions that assist others and enhance their own image. Accordingly, employees with high levels of both motives are more likely to engage in citizenship behaviors that allow them to both “do good” and “look good”—actions that help others while also enhancing their personal standing (Grant, 2008; Sheldon, Arndt, & Houser-Marko, 2003). Since affiliative citizenship behaviors—like helping, courtesy, and taking initiative—generally support the existing organizational order, they provide a safe way for employees to contribute meaningfully without putting their personal image at risk. Based on this reasoning, we hypothesize that impression management motives will amplify the positive relationship between prosocial motives and affiliative citizenship behaviors.
text: However, we propose that impression management motives will encourage employees to express their prosocial motives in affiliative forms of citizenship, such as helping, courtesy, and initiative. Impression management theory and research suggest that when employees have strong impression management motives, they are careful to avoid creating a negative image in the eyes of others (Jones & Pittman, 1982; Leary & Kowalski, 1990). As such, we expect that employees with strong prosocial and impression management motives will tend to engage in affiliative citizenship behaviors that benefit other people and the organization without jeopardizing their own reputations. Indeed, in lifespan developmental psychology research, McAdams and de St. Aubin (1992) proposed and found that individuals are able to simultaneously fulfill prosocial and impression management motives by contributing in ways that both help others and strengthen their own reputations. Thus, employees with strong prosocial and impression management motives are likely to gravitate toward citizenship behaviors that simultaneously "do good" and "look good"—”actions that are beneficial to others and themselves (Grant, 2008; Sheldon, Arndt, & Houser-Marko, 2003). Because affiliative citizenship behaviors such as helping, courtesy, and initiative contribute to maintaining the status quo, they are likely to benefit other people and the organization without threatening employees' images. For these reasons, we hypothesize that impression management motives will strengthen the positive association between prosocial motives and affiliative citizenship behaviors.

The substantial emphasis on third-party research examining anger and antisocial behavior directed at perpetrators reflects the influential role deonance theory has played in shaping this body of work. However, the literature has also grown to include a significant number of studies exploring empathy and prosocial responses toward victims. This development points to a noticeable disconnect between deonance theory and the broader trajectory of third-party research, as the theory has remained largely silent on the topic of victim-directed reactions (Folger, 2012; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Shukla, 2019; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005). We contend that while deonance theory, in its formal articulation, emphasizes anger and antisocial reactions, its underlying ethos or spirit is also capable of accounting for empathetic and prosocial responses.

The findings of our meta-analysis provide encouraging support for a broader conceptualization of the deontic response. Specifically, witnessing mistreatment was found to be moderately positively associated with empathy toward the victim—a result with no evident counterpart in meta-analyses focusing on first-party experiences of mistreatment, thereby underscoring the distinctiveness of the third-party perspective. Deonance theory, when interpreted in terms of its foundational moral and emotional core, can readily incorporate this finding by recognizing that the morally charged arousal it describes can take the form of emotions beyond anger. Furthermore, the theory’s grounding in evolutionary logic can be expanded to propose that empathy, like anger, serves a key adaptive function in human social life (Batson et al., 2002).
text:
That so many third-party studies have focused on anger and antisocial behaviors toward the perpetrator illustrates the dominant role that deonance theory has played in shaping the literature. And yet, the literature has amassed a substantial number of studies that have examined empathy and prosocial behaviors toward the victim. Such activity reveals a disconnect between deonance theory and the evolution of the third-party literature because the lens is so silent on reactions to the victims (Folger, 2012; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Shukla, 2019; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005). We argued that, although the letter of deonance theory emphasizes anger and antisocial responses, its spirit is capable of also explaining empathy and prosocial responses.

The results of our meta-analysis offer promising support for what would be an expanded version of the deontic response. Observing mistreatment was moderately positively related to feelings of empathy toward the victim. That result has no clear analog in “first-party” meta-analyses of experiencing mistreatment, illustrating the uniqueness of the third-party context. The spirit of deonance theory can accommodate that finding by acknowledging that the emotionally charged arousal that characterizes deonance can manifest in moral emotions other than anger. Moreover, the theory’s emphasis on evolutionary adaptiveness can be extended to acknowledge that empathy offers survival value of its own (Batson et al., 2002).

Many of the barriers to happiness at work may, surprisingly, be of our own creation. Scholars have long emphasized that psychological well-being is closely linked to agentic change—the capacity to intentionally shape and influence one’s actions and environment (Bandura, 2000, 2018; Hirst et al., 2020). In fact, a growing body of research demonstrates that employees actively engage in agentic behaviors to enhance their work experiences, ultimately boosting their own happiness on the job (e.g., Cangiano et al., 2019; Plomp et al., 2016; Taris & Wielenga-Meijer, 2010; Tims et al., 2013).

However, many employees feel more like passive objects molded by their circumstances than active agents crafting their own well-being (Bell & Staw, 1989). Too often, individuals “seize and freeze” (Kruglanski & Webster, 1996) on rigid beliefs about personal attributes, overlooking opportunities for meaningful change (Dweck, 1999; Heslin et al., 2005, 2006; Seitz & Owens, 2020). Psychological research has long shown that beliefs about the self exist along a continuum from fixed to growth mindsets (Dweck, 1986, 2006). Those with a fixed mindset tend to view personal traits as static, whereas individuals with a growth mindset see the potential for developing abilities and qualities over time (Madan et al., 2019). Taken together, this literature suggests that adopting a growth mindset increases one's likelihood of exercising agency to improve aspects of the self in meaningful ways (Dweck, 2006; Sue-Chan et al., 2012).
text:
At work, a surprising number of the limits on our happiness may be of our own making. Scholars have long recognized how psychological experiences depend on agentic change—exercising the capacity to purposefully shape and alter one’s actions and circumstances (Bandura, 2000, 2018; Hirst et al., 2020). Indeed, a growing body of research suggests that employees can and do make agentic changes to their experiences in organizations, thereby improving their own happiness on the job (e.g., Cangiano et al., 2019; Plomp et al., 2016; Taris & Wielenga-Meijer, 2010; Tims et al., 2013).

Yet, many employees feel more like sculptures in undesirable circumstances than sculptors of their own happiness (Bell & Staw, 1989). All too often, employees “seize and freeze” (Kruglanski & Webster, 1996) on a fixed view of human qualities, missing opportunities for agentic change (Dweck, 1999; Heslin et al., 2005, 2006; Seitz & Owens, 2020). Psychologists have long demonstrated that mindsets about the self vary along a spectrum from fixed to growth (Dweck, 1986, 2006). Individuals who hold a fixed mindset believe people are largely unchangeable, while those with growth mindsets believe people can change their abilities and traits (Madan et al., 2019). Broadly, this literature suggests that when people hold growth mindsets, they are more inclined to exercise agency to change key aspects of themselves in desirable ways (Dweck, 2006; Sue-Chan et al., 2012).

use: When understood as an adverb, proactivity refers to a specific mode of action (Crant, 2000), one that can take place either within the formal boundaries of an employee’s assigned role or outside of them. This interpretation stands in contrast to earlier conceptualizations in the organizational citizenship behavior literature, where proactive behavior has often been regarded solely as an extra-role phenomenon (Parker et al., 2006; Van Dyne, Cummings, & McLean Parks, 1995). From this standpoint, proactivity is inherently extra-role because behaviors defined as "in-role" are typically assigned by others and, as such, are presumed to lack agency or forward-thinking intent (O'Reilly & Chatman, 1986; Organ & Konovsky, 1989). However, a growing body of scholarship has reached a consensus that proactivity should be understood as a process that can apply to both in-role and extra-role tasks. First, employees can enact in-role responsibilities proactively (e.g., Crant, 2000; Frese & Fay, 2001)—for instance, by completing work ahead of deadlines or mobilizing additional resources to deliver superior results. Second, the boundary between in-role and extra-role behavior is often ambiguous (e.g., Frese & Fay, 2001; Morrison, 1994), as many actions can be interpreted as either, depending on the perceptions of the employee and observers. Employees also differ in how expansively they define their roles (Morrison, 1994), with dispositionally proactive individuals more likely to adopt broader role definitions (e.g., Parker, 2000; Parker et al., 1997).
text: Understood as an adverb, proactivity describes a particular process of action (Crant, 2000) that can occur either within or beyond the boundaries of employees' roles. It is worth noting that this conceptualization contrasts with perspectives in the citizenship behavior literature, where scholars have often treated proactive behavior as exclusively extra-role (Parker et al., 2006; Van Dyne, Cummings, & McLean Parks, 1995). According to these views, proactive behaviors are necessarily extra-role by definition (e.g., O'Reilly & Chatman, 1986; Organ & Konovsky, 1989), because in-role behaviors are assigned by others and therefore may neither be agentic nor anticipatory. However, scholars have recently converged around the view that proactivity is a process that can be applied to either in-role or extra-role activities. First, employees can perform in-role tasks in a proactive manner (e.g., Crant, 2000; Frese & Fay, 2001). For example, employees can complete tasks ahead of schedule or marshal additional resources to carry out these tasks. Second, the distinctions between in-role and extra-role behavior are often unclear (e.g., Frese & Fay, 2001; Morrison, 1994), given that many behaviors can be considered either in-role or extra-role depending on how they are construed by the employee and by observers. For example, employees vary in how broadly they define their roles (Morrison, 1994), with dispositionally proactive employees defining their roles more broadly (e.g., Parker, 2000; Parker et al., 1997).
