The concept of gratitude and its expression has attracted scholarly interest since ancient times. Much of this attention has emphasized the moral importance of gratitude, portraying it as both a virtue and a means of fostering strong, meaningful relationships (Leithart, 2014). For instance, Cicero famously described gratitude as “the parent of all other virtues,” while religious traditions around the world have long encouraged the cultivation of gratitude as a spiritual and ethical practice (Emmons & Kneezel, 2005; Tsang & Martin, 2016). In modern scholarship, particularly within the fields of positive psychology and positive organizational scholarship, gratitude continues to be celebrated—now with a focus on the psychological and relational benefits of nurturing grateful emotions and developing habits of gratitude expression (DeSteno et al., 2014; Lopez & Snyder, 2009).

This view of gratitude as a virtue is grounded in its strong connection to prosocial behavior. Algoe’s (2012) find-remind-bind theory articulates gratitude’s social role in identifying and reinforcing cooperative relationships. Gratitude helps individuals identify potential prosocial partners, reminds them of the value of existing ones, and strengthens bonds with them by motivating affiliative behavior. In this way, expressions of gratitude initiate and sustain virtuous cycles of prosociality—deepening relationships over time and fostering mutual support and collaboration (Algoe, 2012; Kong & Belkin, 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Locklear et al., 2023).
text:
Notions of gratitude and its expression have received scholarly attention since antiquity. Much of this attention has focused on gratitude’s moral significance, whereby gratitude and its expression have been understood as virtues and mechanisms for maintaining high-quality connections (Leithart, 2014). Cicero, for example, praised gratitude as “the parent of all other virtues” while religions across the globe have encouraged practitioners to cultivate gratitude for centuries (Emmons & Kneezel, 2005; Tsang & Martin, 2016). In the modern era, scholars associated with the positive psychology and positive organizational scholarship movements have likewise highlighted the virtues of gratitude, emphasizing the benefits of cultivating grateful emotions and a habit of gratitude expression (DeSteno et al., 2014; Lopez & Snyder, 2009).

The notion of gratitude as virtuous is rooted in its link to prosociality. According to Algoe’s (2012) find-remind-bind theory, gratitude’s social function is to orient people toward good social partners. Gratitude helps people find new partners that are likely to act prosocially toward them, reminds them of the prosocial partners they currently have, and binds them to prosocial partners by compelling them to act in affiliative ways. Thus, expressions of gratitude and related affiliative behavior create virtuous cycles of prosociality, strengthening relationships, and encouraging both parties to help each other over extended periods of time (Algoe, 2012; Kong & Belkin, 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Locklear et al., 2023).

use: Job crafting is a bottom-up approach to job redesign in which employees proactively modify their roles to better align with their personal preferences and to derive greater meaning from their work. Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) identify three key ways individuals engage in this process: by altering the boundaries of their tasks, reshaping workplace relationships, and reframing the way they perceive their work. In this study, we examine job crafting through the lens of the Job Demands-Resources (JD-R) theory (Bakker & Demerouti, 2017; Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli, 2001). Within this framework, job crafting refers to the self-initiated changes employees make to optimize the alignment between job demands and resources and their own abilities and preferences, thereby enhancing or restoring person-job fit (Tims & Bakker, 2010). Job demands are aspects of a role that require sustained effort and are consequently linked to psychological or physiological strain, whereas job resources are elements that support goal achievement, mitigate demands, and promote personal development (Demerouti et al., 2001). Employees engage in job crafting by increasing job resources (e.g., seeking feedback, enhancing autonomy) to better cope with demands and pursue objectives; by introducing challenging demands (e.g., taking on new responsibilities or additional tasks) to maintain motivation and prevent stagnation; by reducing hindering demands (e.g., avoiding certain tasks, simplifying complex responsibilities) to safeguard well-being; and by optimizing demands to improve efficiency and work flow (e.g., streamlining procedures; Demerouti & Peeters, 2018; Petrou, Demerouti, Peeters, Schaufeli & Hetland, 2012).
text: Job crafting represents a bottom-up approach to job redesign where employees alter their jobs to make them fit their preferences and find meaning. Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) suggest that individuals craft their jobs by altering their task boundaries, the relationships at work, and how they think about their work. In this study, we conceptualize job crafting from the perspective of job demands-resources (JD-R) theory (Bakker & Demerouti, 2017; Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli, 2001). Accordingly, job crafting concerns changes employees make to balance their job demands and job resources with their personal abilities and needs to create or restore their person-job fit (Tims & Bakker, 2010). Job demands refer to aspects of the job that require effort and, therefore, are associated with psychophysiological costs, whereas job resources refer to job aspects that facilitate dealing with job demands, goal accomplishment, and growth (Demerouti et al., 2001). Specifically, individuals craft their job by seeking job resources (e.g., seeking feedback, maximizing autonomy) to deal with job demands and to achieve goals, by seeking challenging demands (e.g., asking for more responsibilities, seeking more tasks) to maintain motivation and avoid boredom, by reducing hindering demands (e.g., task avoidance, reducing task complexity) to protect their health and energy, and by optimizing demands to surpass inefficient working methods (e.g., simplify work processes; Demerouti & Peeters, 2018; Petrou, Demerouti, Peeters, Schaufeli & Hetland, 2012).

use: The location of conflict—whether it emerges within a team or between teams—acts as a key contextual cue that shapes individuals’ attentional priorities. This cue influences the dynamic balance between two competing forces: the drive to maintain focus on the immediate task at hand (i.e., on-task attentional pull) and the distractions posed by conflict-related demands (i.e., off-task attentional demands). Team boundaries foster a psychological separation between groups, limiting the visibility into each other’s tasks, including their significance and consequences. As a result, attention is redirected inward, prompting individuals to allocate their cognitive resources predominantly toward their own team’s activities (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). Scholars have long noted the difficulty of balancing intra-team focus with the need for inter-team coordination in multiteam systems. This challenge is amplified by individuals’ tendency to prioritize the needs of their own team (Choi, 2002; Kanfer & Kerry, 2012; Shuffler & Carter, 2018). In other words, inter-team interactions are often perceived as a potential drain on “critical resources away from [their focal] team-level performance,” thereby “diverting one’s attention away from immediate task performance” (Kanfer & Kerry, 2012: 93). Although successful functioning in multiteam systems depends on collaboration across team boundaries, these very boundaries tend to channel members’ attention and resources inward. Consequently, conflict that arises within team boundaries is often seen as more legitimate and urgent than conflicts occurring across teams (Herrmann, 2012).
text: The boundaries of conflict experience (within team vs. between team) serve as a contextual cue that determines the priority of attentional focus. Consequently, it alters the balance between two competing forces: the inclination to stay focused on the current task (i.e., on-task attentional pull) and the distractions stemming from conflict-related demands (i.e., off-task attentional demands). Team boundaries create psychological division between teams, obscuring the understanding of each other’s tasks, such as relative importance and implications. This redirection of attentional focus compels individuals to concentrate their resources toward their own team’s activities (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). Indeed, scholars have noted that managing the balance between focusing on within-team tasks and coordinating between teams poses a challenge in multi-team arrangements. This is because individuals tend to prioritize allocating resources within their own (Choi, 2002; Kanfer & Kerry, 2012; Shuffler & Carter, 2018). In other words, individuals may view interactions between teams as potentially diverting “critical resources away from [their focal] team-level performance,” which has the potential for “diverting one’s attention away from immediate task performance” (Kanfer & Kerry, 2012: 93). Thus, although teams operating in a multiteam context must work together, the team boundaries often direct members’ resources more toward their focal team. As a result, individuals perceive greater legitimacy and prioritize conflict experiences that occur within their team boundaries over those occurring between team boundaries (Herrmann, 2012).

use: These studies present several important limitations that offer valuable directions for future research. First, while our focus was on happiness (with job crafting intentions examined as a plausible mediator), a dual-growth mindset may have broader implications. Future studies could investigate its relationship with additional outcomes such as job performance or organizational citizenship behavior (Organ, 1988), as well as alternative mediators like personal initiative (Frese & Fay, 2001), self-efficacy (Bandura, 1982), or curiosity (Harrison, 2011). Although we did not observe sustained improvements in job performance (see Footnote 4), it is possible that a dual-growth mindset could enhance performance if the intervention is more directly tailored to that objective. Second, we assessed job crafting intentions rather than job crafting behaviors. This methodological choice helped minimize potential biases associated with repeated measurement, where prompting participants to report on behavioral changes over time might inadvertently influence those behaviors and obscure the true effects of the initial intervention. However, intentions do not always align with actual behavior (cf. Ajzen, 1991), and participants’ actions may have diverged from their stated intentions. Third, while our measure of job crafting intentions was effective for contrasting the dual and job conditions, the structure of the intervention prevented participants in the self condition from expressing job crafting intentions. Consequently, any job crafting that may have occurred in the self condition could have gone undetected. Future research would benefit from incorporating more granular behavioral assessments of job crafting and exploring additional mediators to gain a deeper understanding of dual-growth mindset effects.
text: These studies have key limitations that can be addressed in future research. First, we focused on happiness (and job crafting intentions as one likely mediator), but dual-growth mindset may have many additional important consequences. Future research could explore the link between dual-growth mindset and other potential outcomes, such as job performance or organizational citizenship behavior (Organ, 1988), and other potential mediators, such as personal initiative (Frese & Fay, 2001), self-efficacy (Bandura, 1982), or curiosity (Harrison, 2011). Although we did not find sustained effects for job performance (see Footnote 4), dual-growth mindset may boost performance when the intervention is aimed more specifically at this goal. Second, we measured job crafting intentions, as opposed to job crafting behaviors. This approach avoided potential biases from surveying participants over time about the changes they have made, which could influence their behavior and thus undermine inferences about the effects of the initial intervention. Nonetheless, participants’ actual behavior may have differed from their intentions (cf. Ajzen, 1991). Third, our measure of job crafting intentions was relatively effective at comparing the dual and job conditions, but by design, the intervention did not allow participants in the self condition to convey job crafting intentions. As a result, our measure may have missed job crafting that occurred in the self condition. Future research could use more granular behavioral measures of job crafting and other potential mediators.

use: By analyzing this model, we offer several conceptual contributions. First, the prevailing perspective on the threat-voice relationship generally assumes that threats naturally provoke voice (e.g., Lebel, 2016). We challenge this assumption by emphasizing that many threats are inherently ambiguous and may not clearly prompt employees to speak up. In fact, we argue that ambiguous threats often fail to elicit voice precisely when such input is most valuable for addressing uncertainty. Second, voice research has largely rested on the premise that employees occupy advantageous positions for detecting workplace issues, suggesting that the main organizational challenge lies in encouraging voice through reducing psychological costs (e.g., by fostering psychological safety) or enhancing perceived benefits (e.g., increasing perceived influence or impact) (Detert & Burris, 2007; Tangirala & Ramanujam, 2012). We expand this literature by offering an alternative, attention-based perspective: employees may remain silent not due to fear or lack of motivation, but simply because they do not perceive or interpret certain issues as problems requiring input. Finally, we examine an ironic twist to a widely accepted enabler of voice—manager trustworthiness (Holtz et al., 2020). We show how this attribute, while typically considered beneficial, can inadvertently suppress voice by reducing employee vigilance. Thus, we contribute to a growing body of research suggesting that manager trustworthiness can have unintended consequences, such as lowering employees’ likelihood of noticing and voicing concerns about managerial oversights or errors (Gargiulo & Ertug, 2006).
text: By examining this model, we make several conceptual contributions. First, the current discourse on the threat-voice relationship assumes that threats spur voice (e.g., Lebel, 2016). We point out that this is not necessarily the case, especially as many threats are inherently ambiguous. By suggesting that ambiguous threats do not adequately trigger voice, we highlight that employees may not speak up precisely when their input is desired to deal with the uncertainties surrounding ambiguous threats. Second, the voice literature has typically assumed that employees are in vantage positions to recognize work problems and, thus, the primary challenge for organizations is to motivate voice by decreasing the psychological costs (enhancing psychological safety) or increasing the perceived benefits (providing a sense of instrumentality or impact) of speaking up (Detert & Burris, 2007; Tangirala & Ramanujam, 2012). We extend this literature by presenting an alternative attention-based view that employees may often fail to speak up, not because they lack psychological safety or instrumentality, but simply because they do not recognize problems or areas for improvement. Finally, we highlight how, paradoxically, a factor traditionally seen as a facilitator of voice—”manager trustworthiness (Holtz et al., 2020)—”can unintentionally impede it. Thereby, we add to emerging work suggesting that manager trustworthiness can have a dark side, as it inadvertently lowers vigilance that enables employees to catch omissions or errors of the manager (Gargiulo & Ertug, 2006).

Alternatively, when individuals encounter conflict originating from members of another team, the pressure to redirect their attentional focus tends to be lower. As noted earlier, individuals prioritize tasks based on team boundaries, typically giving precedence to the goals and activities of their own team (Kanfer & Kerry, 2012; Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). Accordingly, we propose that individuals assess whether to shift their attention in relation to these boundaries. Specifically, when confronted with between-team conflict, individuals are more inclined to maintain their focus on their primary task, rather than divert attention toward the conflict itself.

Importantly, beyond merely preserving attentional focus, between-team conflict can present a distinctive opportunity to enhance task performance. Prior research has shown that information originating outside the team’s boundaries can offer valuable perspectives and insights into the broader context in which the team operates (Marrone, 2010; Somech & Khalaili, 2014). As such, the content embedded within a conflict may help individuals reevaluate their current situations and gain a clearer understanding of their work (Lê & Jarzabkowski, 2015). In these instances, individuals may choose to utilize the information received through the conflict to improve task outcomes without experiencing pressure to shift attention away from focal responsibilities. Thus, receiving conflict from outside team boundaries may support task performance by sustaining focus and minimizing cognitive load related to off-task distractions. Formally stated:

Hypothesis 2b. Experiences of receiving between-team conflict will be positively associated with task performance.
text:
Alternatively, when individuals receive conflict from members of another team, the pressure for redirecting attentional focus is lower. As discussed above, individuals evaluate priorities based on their team boundaries and favor their focal team’s activities (Kanfer & Kerry, 2012; Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018). Therefore, we argue that receivers will determine whether to switch their attentional focus based on team boundaries. In short, when receiving between-team conflict, individuals are more likely to keep their attentional focus on their focal task rather than redirect it to conflict issues.

However, beyond simply keeping their attentional focus, receiving between-team conflict presents a unique opportunity for receivers’ task performance. Studies have demonstrated that information situated outside of the team boundaries can provide unique knowledge and deeper understanding of the general environment in which a team is embedded (Marrone, 2010; Somech & Khalaili, 2014). Hence, specific information conveyed within a conflict expression can be useful for receivers to reassess situations and understand their tasks better (Lê & Jarzabkowski, 2015). In such case, receivers may choose to leverage this information to enhance their task performance without feeling pressured to shift their attentional focus. Therefore, when receiving conflict between team boundaries, it may have a positive impact on an individual’s task performance by maintaining their attentional focus while reducing off-task attentional demands. More formally:

Hypothesis 2b. Receiving between-team conflict experiences will be positively related to task performance.

use: Building on dominance complementarity theory, we argue that when employees engage in proactive behaviors, extraverted leadership may hinder rather than enhance group performance. Dominance complementarity theory (Carson, 1969; Kiesler, 1983) posits that effective interpersonal interactions arise when one individual displays dominant, assertive behavior while the other responds with submissive, accommodating behavior. A foundational principle of the theory is the human tendency to seek equilibrium in social exchanges (Leary, 1957): when one person assumes a dominant role, the counterpart is expected to adopt a more yielding stance, allowing for smoother coordination and mutual understanding. As Kiesler (1983: 198) observed, complementarity occurs when the person socially acknowledged as superior assumes the "one-up" or primary position, while the other, perceived as subordinate, occupies the "one-down" or secondary position. This dynamic signifies an accepted status hierarchy (Tiedens & Fragale, 2003), wherein both parties are able to affirm their self-identities—dominants gaining affirmation of their power and submissives experiencing reassurance and psychological safety. The clarity of roles that this interaction fosters reduces ambiguity about authority, mitigates conflict and rivalry, and enhances goal alignment (Bendersky & Hays, in press; Tiedens, Chow, & Unzueta, 2007; Wiltermuth, 2009). Conversely, when dominance complementarity is absent, individuals may compete for influence or status, generating tension and diverting attention and resources away from task achievement (Smelser, 1961).
text: Drawing on dominance complementarity theory, we propose that when employees engage in proactive behaviors, extraverted leadership is negatively rather than positively associated with group performance. According to dominance complementarity theory (Carson, 1969; Kiesler, 1983), effective interactions are achieved when dominant, assertive behavior from one party is matched by submissive, passive behavior from another. A core tenet of dominance complementarity is that people seek balance in interpersonal interactions (Leary, 1957): when one acts dominant, the other is expected to act submissive, and this pairing allows them to coordinate their actions and interactions effectively. Kiesler (1983: 198) proposed that complementarity exists when the individual "contextually recognized as being superior is in the 'one-up' or primary position, whereas the person recognized as being inferior is in the 'one-down' or secondary position." Dominance complementarity represents the existence of a status hierarchy (Tiedens & Fragale, 2003) in which both members are able to "confirm... self-definitions" (Kiesler, 1983: 198). The dominant parties gain validation of their power and status, and the submissive parties gain support and security. For both parties, uncertainty about authority roles is reduced, preventing conflict and competition and facilitating the pursuit of common goals (Bendersky & Hays, in press; Tiedens, Chow, & Unzueta, 2007; Wiltermuth, 2009). When complementarity is lacking, individuals often focus on jockeying for position, which can create anxiety and distract attention and energy away from task completion (Smelser, 1961).

use: Additionally, previous research on Leader-Member Exchange (LMX) underscores the pivotal role leaders play in shaping employee experiences (Ballinger et al., 2010), highlighting the leader-employee relationship as a key determinant in employees’ career-related decisions (Nishii & Mayer, 2009). Employees often engage in collective sensemaking (e.g., Bartunek et al., 2008; Langfield-Smith, 1992; Maitlis, 2005; Porter & Rigby, 2021), and the shared anticipation of disruption stemming from a leadership change can prompt coordinated turnover among unit members following a leader’s exit (Ballinger & Schoorman, 2007; Pitcher et al., 2000). Because leaders serve as salient and influential points of reference within a unit, their departure can act as a psychological “shock,” prompting employees to re-evaluate their individual alignment with the organization (Lee & Mitchell, 1994; Staw, 1980). In particular, this disruption may trigger a wave of reflection centered on employees' loyalty to the outgoing leader, prompting those with the strongest loyalty to leave soon after. Accordingly, we propose that the relationship between leader departure and collective unit-level turnover is most pronounced during the exit phase as conceptualized in Ballinger and Schoorman’s (2007) model—immediately following the leader’s departure. In this phase, those remaining may experience an instinctive and emotional response to the disruption, opting to leave swiftly and with minimal evaluation of alternatives, resulting in a sharp, initial spike in collective turnover.
text: Additionally, prior research on LMX highlights how critical leaders are to their employees (Ballinger et al., 2010) and that the leader-employee relationship is an important factor when employees make employment decisions (Nishii & Mayer, 2009). Employees may engage in collective sensemaking (e.g., Bartunek et al., 2008; Langfield-Smith, 1992; Maitlis, 2005; Porter & Rigby, 2021), whereby the shared anticipation of potential disruption caused by a new leader may drive members of the unit to turnover following a leader's departure (Ballinger & Schoorman, 2007; Pitcher et al., 2000). As leaders are highly visible referents within the unit, a leader's departure may function as a shock that leads workers within the unit to reconsider their own fit within the organization (Lee & Mitchell, 1994; Staw, 1980). Specifically, the shock of a leader's departure may inspire unit employees to collectively reflect on their loyalty to the departing leader, resulting in the immediate departure of those who are most loyal to the leader. Thus, we hypothesize that the association of leader departure with unit-level collective turnover should be strongest during the exit phase of Ballinger and Schoorman's (2007) model, immediately following the leader's departure. In other words, those left behind may enact an immediate, visceral reaction to such a shock, choosing to depart quickly and without the consideration of alternatives, leading to an initial increase in collective turnover directly following a leader departure event.

use: The effectiveness of work units often hinges on employees’ helping behaviors (Katz & Kahn, 1966; Organ, 1988). Helping encompasses prosocial, promotive, and collaborative actions aimed at benefiting others—behaviors that enhance interpersonal harmony, support task execution, and strengthen relationships (Brief & Motowidlo, 1986; George & Brief, 1992; Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). As a facet of the altruism dimension within organizational citizenship behavior (Bateman & Organ, 1983; Organ, 1997), workplace helping includes supporting colleagues with job-related responsibilities (Anderson & Williams, 1996), offering emotional support for personal hardships (Kahn, 1998), doing favors (Flynn & Brockner, 2003), showing compassion in the face of distress (Dutton, Worline, Frost, & Lilius, 2006), and cooperating with team members (Dukerich, Golden, & Shortell, 2002). These helping behaviors serve as essential components of organizing—the collective process by which individuals align their efforts to accomplish shared objectives (Weick, 1979). Empirical research has demonstrated that helping behaviors are positively linked to performance outcomes across a wide range of contexts, including increased sales in pharmaceutical teams (Podsakoff, MacKenzie, Paine, & Bachrach, 2000); enhanced effectiveness in intelligence and military units (Hackman & Wageman, 2007; Ehrhart, Bliese, & Thomas, 2006); greater collective creativity in consulting firms (Hargadon & Bechky, 2006); improved productivity in paper mill crews (Podsakoff, Ahearne, & MacKenzie, 1997); higher sales performance and employee retention in retail environments (George & Bettenhausen, 1990); as well as better revenue performance, operational efficiency, customer satisfaction, and service quality in restaurants (Koys, 2001; Walz & Niehoff, 2000).
text: The success of work units often depends on helping behaviors from employees (Katz & Kahn, 1966; Organ, 1988). Helping involves prosocial, promotive, and cooperative behaviors intended to benefit others—”actions that foster interpersonal harmony, facilitate task completion, and build and maintain relationships (Brief & Motowidlo, 1986; George & Brief, 1992; Van Dyne & LePine, 1998). As part of the altruism dimension of organizational citizenship (Bateman & Organ, 1983; Organ, 1997), helping in the workplace includes assisting others with work-related tasks (Anderson & Williams, 1996), offering care and support to coworkers with personal problems (Kahn, 1998), performing favors (Flynn & Brockner, 2003), expressing compassion in response to pain and suffering (Dutton, Worline, Frost, & Lilius, 2006), and cooperating with peers (Dukerich, Golden, & Shortell, 2002). Helping behaviors are fundamental building blocks of organizing, the process through which individual employees coordinate their efforts to achieve collective goals (Weick, 1979). Evidence indicates that helping behaviors are positively associated with sales in pharmaceutical sales units (Podsakoff, MacKenzie, Paine, & Bachrach, 2000); performance in intelligence units (Hackman & Wageman, 2007) and military units (Ehrhart, Bliese, & Thomas, 2006); collective creativity in consulting teams (Hargadon & Bechky, 2006); productivity in paper mill crews (Podsakoff, Ahearne, & MacKenzie, 1997); sales performance and voluntary retention in retail stores (George & Bettenhausen, 1990); and revenue, operating efficiency, customer satisfaction, and performance quality in restaurants (Koys, 2001; Walz & Niehoff, 2000).

use: Our study offers several key contributions. First, by analyzing both intra-category and inter-category dynamics, we build upon—and diverge from—the prevailing view that a category’s viability is primarily determined by its internal coherence (Grodal, 2018; Wry et al., 2011). Drawing on recent theoretical developments (Lee et al., 2018; Lo et al., 2020), we theorize and empirically demonstrate how entrepreneurial actions—such as launching new products and engaging with prospective audiences—can shape not only the internal heterogeneity of a category but also its distinctiveness relative to adjacent categories. Second, while previous research has typically addressed the viability of individual categories in isolation (Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2010), we respond to calls for a more holistic approach that considers multiple categories concurrently (Haans, 2019). By examining how categories are situated within a broader classification system, we are able to address not only the essential question, “What kind of thing is it?” but also the equally important, “What kind of thing is it not?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). Finally, our research underscores the potential of crowdfunding environments as rich empirical contexts for studying category dynamics. While prior studies have predominantly focused on factors driving the success of individual crowdfunding campaigns (see Short et al., 2017 for a review), they have largely neglected the collective and temporal dimensions of crowdfunding ecosystems (with the notable exception of Soublière & Gehman, 2020). To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to investigate the viability of entire categories within crowdfunding contexts.
text: Our study makes several contributions. First, by considering both intra- and inter-category dynamics, we extend and depart from the insight that the viability of a category is primarily driven by its internal coherence (Grodal, 2018; Wry et al., 2011). Building on theoretical advances (Lee et al., 2018; Lo et al., 2020), we theorize and show how entrepreneurial action—in the form of introducing new products and communicating with potential audiences—dynamically shifts not only the degree of heterogeneity that a category exhibits internally, but also its distinctiveness in relation to related categories. Second, whereas prior studies have analyzed the viability of one category at a time (Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2010), we answer calls to examine multiple categories simultaneously (Haans, 2019). Examining how categories are embedded in the broader classification system enables us to not only consider the fundamental question “What kind of thing is it?” but also “What kind of thing is it not?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). Finally, our study highlights the value of crowdfunding as fertile ground to study category dynamics. Prior studies have explained the success factors of individual campaigns (for a review, see Short, Ketchen, McKenny, Allison, & Ireland, 2017), but have largely overlooked the collective and temporal dynamics of crowdfunding (but see Soublière & Gehman, 2020). To our knowledge, our study is the first to consider the viability of entire categories in crowdfunding settings.

use:
Unlike supervised or reinforcement machine learning (ML), which depend on clearly defined input-output pairs, unsupervised ML operates solely on input data and is primarily used to uncover latent structures within complex or high-dimensional datasets. For example, while supervised or reinforcement models might aim to forecast voter turnout in a binary national election, unsupervised learning could explore the data to reveal previously obscured subgroups within the electorate—potentially uncovering a dozen or more distinct ideological segments beyond the simple two-party classification. Without a predefined target or external guidance, unsupervised algorithms analyze unlabeled data independently, identifying and constructing hidden patterns, groupings, or relationships.

In practice, unsupervised learning is especially valuable when well-labeled datasets are unavailable, but there exists a wealth of unstructured, multidimensional, and unlabeled data, such as narrative responses to news articles, social media posts, images, or customer reviews (Alashwal, El Halaby, Crouse, Abdalla, & Moustafa, 2019). To return to the popular "Chihuahua or muffin" challenge, the user would provide a diverse collection of unlabeled images as input to the algorithm. In the absence of label-based supervision, the algorithm’s task is to identify shared features across the data and cluster similar images together. While it might organize the images into two main clusters—one for canines and one for baked goods—it could also detect subtler distinctions, assembling more than two groups based on nuanced visual similarities.
text: In contrast to supervised or reinforcement ML, which rely on paired input and output data, unsupervised ML relies only on input data and is most often used to autonomously decipher underlying structures in complex or multidimensional data. For instance, while a supervised or reinforcement model might be leveraged to predict voter turnout in a two-party national election, unsupervised learning might be used to identify the actual underlying structure of voter categories hidden within that two-party system (potentially revealing a dozen or more distinct ideological orientation blocks). In the absence of a specified objective and support from the user, the algorithm independently explores unlabeled data, extracting and constructing hidden patterns and structures. In practice, unsupervised ML is especially useful when well-labeled data are not available for analysis, but significant multidimensional, unstructured, and unlabeled data are available—”such as narrative comment responses to news stories, social media posts, images, or customer reviews on e-commerce websites (Alashwal, El Halaby, Crouse, Abdalla, & Moustafa, 2019). Returning once more to the Chihuahua-muffin task, the user would provide a full and inclusive set of unlabeled images as input to the unsupervised algorithm. In the absence of labels, the algorithm would seek to learn the hidden associations between various images and form clusters that have similar features. While an unsupervised algorithm could conceivably generate two clusters corresponding to the canine-baked goods schema, a solution involving additional clusters might also emerge.

Roberts (2006) expanded the Attraction-Selection-Attrition (ASA) model to account for the possibility of personality change, reviewing available evidence at the time that suggested occupational characteristics could influence trait development through socialization. However, the accumulated body of research since then indicates that specific life experiences—regardless of their nature—tend to have only modest and often inconsistent effects on personality traits across individuals (Bühler et al., 2023). This makes it unlikely that work experiences play a large, systematic role in driving broad personality change. That said, personality traits do commonly change over time (Mõttus, 2022), and job-related experiences, which differ significantly across occupations, may still play a contributory—if limited—role in this process (Holman & Hughes, 2021; Wu, 2016; Zheng et al., 2023).

A particularly plausible explanation is that occupational experiences may reinforce or intensify the very traits that initially predisposed individuals to pursue those roles in the first place. This idea aligns with the corresponsive principle of personality development (Le et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2003), which suggests that the traits guiding people into particular roles are the same traits most likely to be shaped and reinforced by those roles. For instance, working in leadership, sales, or caregiving positions may strengthen traits crucial to those professions—a process sometimes deliberately supported through coaching or professional development (McCormick & Burch, 2008). This concept is also supported by the demands–affordances transactional model (Woods et al., 2019) and the TESSERA framework—Triggering Situations, Expectancy, States/State Expressions, and Reactions (Wrzus & Roberts, 2017)—both of which describe personality change as the result of ongoing interactions between personal traits and persistent situational contexts. Thus, both selection into particular roles and socialization within them may jointly contribute to the observed average trait differences across occupational groups.
text:
Roberts (2006) extended the ASA model to accommodate personality change, reviewing evidence to support trait changes in response to occupational characteristics (socialization) available at that time. The totality of evidence accumulated since then suggests that specific life experiences of any kind usually have only modest effects on personality traits, at least in ways that are similar across people (Bühler et al., 2023), making work experiences’ large and systematic roles in personality change unlikely. Yet, personality trait change is common (Mõttus, 2022), and job-related experiences that vary across occupations may play some role in it (Holman & Hughes, 2021; Wu, 2016; Zheng et al., 2023).
Perhaps most plausibly, job-related experience may accentuate the traits that contributed to people ending up in these jobs in the first place, consistently with the corresponsive principle of personality development (Le et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2003). For example, being in leadership, sales, or care positions may amplify the traits typically required to choose and be chosen for these positions; sometimes, this is explicitly intended with coaching support (Mccormick & Burch, 2008). This idea is also consistent with the demands-affordances transactional model (Woods et al., 2019) and the Triggering Situations, Expectancy, States/State Expressions, and Reactions framework (Wrzus & Roberts, 2017), which both describe trait changes resulting from transactions between personality traits and persistent situations. Hence, both the selection and socialization effects may similarly contribute to average trait differences between occupations.

use: Empirical research has produced inconsistent and, at times, perplexing findings regarding the link between intrinsic motivation and creativity (see reviews by Amabile & Mueller, 2007; George, 2007; Shalley et al., 2004). Much of this evidence comes from laboratory studies involving children and college students creating artistic products—settings in which researchers can manipulate intrinsic motivation and obtain objective, expert evaluations of creative output. Several of these experiments demonstrate that participants exposed to heightened intrinsic motivation tend to produce work judged as more creative (e.g., Amabile, 1979; Koestner, Ryan, Bernieri, & Holt, 1984). However, other experimental studies report minimal, inconsistent, or even negligible effects of intrinsic motivation on creativity (e.g., Amabile, 1985; Amabile, Hennessey, & Grossman, 1986; Eisenberger & Aselage, 2009; Shalley & Perry-Smith, 2001). For instance, Amabile et al. (1986: 21) noted that several lab studies “do not allow definitive conclusions...only some of the various intrinsic interest measures showed correlations with creativity.” They also cautioned that these studies may be susceptible to reverse causality, suggesting that self-reports of intrinsic motivation might stem from, rather than lead to, the enjoyment and satisfaction derived from engaging in creative expression (Amabile et al., 1986: 21). Table 1 provides an overview of the key laboratory studies, indicating that intrinsic motivation appears more reliably associated with creativity in artistic and writing tasks than in idea generation or problem-solving activities related to business contexts.
text: However, empirical research has yielded mixed and often confusing results about whether intrinsic motivation enhances creativity (for reviews, see Amabile and Mueller [2007], George [2007], and Shalley et al. [2004]). Most of this research has involved laboratory experiments with children and college students developing artistic products, in which it is possible to manipulate intrinsic motivation and obtain independent expert ratings of creative outcomes. A number of laboratory experiments have shown that when participants are induced to experience high levels of intrinsic motivation, their products are rated as more creative (e.g., Amabile, 1979; Koestner, Ryan, Bernieri, & Holt, 1984). However, other laboratory experiments have shown weak, mixed, or no benefits of intrinsic motivation for creativity (e.g., Amabile, 1985; Amabile, Hennessey, & Grossman, 1986; Eisenberger & Aselage, 2009; Shalley & Perry-Smith, 2001). For example, Amabile et al. (1986: 21) stated that several laboratory studies "do not allow definitive conclusions...only some of the various intrinsic interest measures showed correlations with creativity." They further noted these studies are vulnerable to the possibility of reverse causality. Rather than causing creativity, self-reports of intrinsic motivation "might result from a greater enjoyment and satisfaction experienced" in expressing creativity (Amabile et al., 1986: 21). Table 1 summarizes key details of the pertinent laboratory studies, showing that intrinsic motivation has been more consistently linked to creativity in artwork and writing tasks than to creativity in producing ideas and solutions relevant to business problems.

use: Moreover, micro-level research across organizational behavior, psychology, and cognitive science has explored the cognitive and emotional challenges—as well as the potential benefits—associated with frequent task switching, investigating how these dynamics influence outcomes at the individual level. However, findings in this domain have been somewhat inconsistent, and perspectives across disciplines remain largely fragmented. As our review demonstrates, studies emphasizing task performance, accuracy, error rates, and information-processing quality consistently report the detrimental effects of interruptions, underscoring the mental strain involved in shifting attention from one task to another (e.g., Altmann & Trafton, 2007; Cellier & Eyrolle, 1992; Leroy, 2009; Leroy & Glomb, 2018; Leroy & Schmidt, 2016; Oulasvirta & Saariluoma, 2006; Speier, Vessey, & Valacich, 2003; Zijlstra, Roe, Leonora, & Krediet, 1999). In contrast, research focused on decision quality and satisfaction (e.g., Dijksterhuis & Meurs, 2006), as well as on creativity (Lu, Akinola, & Mason, 2017; Madjar & Oldham, 2006; Madjar & Shalley, 2008), reveals positive outcomes from task switching, highlighting mechanisms such as incubation (Sio & Ormerod, 2009), reduced cognitive fixation (Lu et al., 2017), and psychological recovery (Hunter & Wu, 2016; Kim, Park, & Headrick, 2018; Trougakos & Hideg, 2009). While each stream offers valuable insights, these conflicting findings limit their broader application, impeding the development of coherent guidance for research areas such as job design, task sequencing, and task engagement. From a practical perspective, the implications remain ambiguous: Should managers minimize or promote task switching in roles that demand both high-level information processing and creative thinking?
text: Furthermore, microlevel research in organizational behavior, psychology, and cognitive sciences has investigated the cognitive and emotional challenges and benefits of frequent task switches and their impacts on individual-level outcomes. However, their results have been somewhat inconsistent, and their perspectives are rarely integrated across areas of interest. For example, as revealed by our review, studies focused on task performance, accuracy, errors, and quality of information processing have consistently documented the harmful effects of interruptions, highlighting the cognitive difficulty of switching the focus of attention (e.g., Altmann & Trafton, 2007; Cellier & Eyrolle, 1992; Leroy, 2009; Leroy & Glomb, 2018; Leroy & Schmidt, 2016; Oulasvirta & Saariluoma, 2006; Speier, Vessey, & Valacich, 2003; Zijlstra, Roe, Leonora, & Krediet, 1999). By contrast, work focused on choice quality and satisfaction (e.g., Dijksterhuis & Meurs, 2006) and on creativity (Lu, Akinola, & Mason, 2017; Madjar & Oldham, 2006; Madjar & Shalley, 2008) has pointed to the positive effects of task switching, highlighting instead opportunities for incubation (Sio & Ormerod, 2009), reduction in cognitive fixation (Lu et al., 2017), and recovery (Hunter & Wu, 2016; Kim, Park, & Headrick, 2018; Trougakos & Hideg, 2009). Although these findings provide useful insights, their inconsistencies have limited the ability to inform research on job design, task sequencing, task engagement, etc. From a practical standpoint, recommendations are unclear: Should managers encourage more or less task switching if the work requires both the processing of complex information and creativity?

The finding that schadenfreude plays a role in behavioral mediation—a blend of emotional and cognitive processes—challenges the largely affective framing of deonance theory (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). In contrast to schadenfreude, the mediating roles of anger and empathy aligned more closely with the expectations of an expanded deonance perspective, as they did not involve cognitive processing. Our findings, however, suggest that witnessing mistreatment may, at times, evoke more deliberative responses. Emotions such as schadenfreude can trigger the retrieval of mood-congruent memories or supplement existing information used to form evaluative judgments (Ochsner & Schacter, 2000; Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996). These emotionally charged evaluations may, in turn, guide reasoned behaviors—actions that are cognitively grounded yet still infused with affect.

That said, this observation about mediation arguably “buries the lede” when it comes to understanding the implications of schadenfreude for deonance theory. While recent scholarship on empathy might be integrated into deonance theory by treating it as another morally relevant emotion, integrating schadenfreude presents a more fundamental challenge. Haidt’s (2003) conception of moral emotions offers little justification for classifying schadenfreude in this category. Although in some specific contexts the enjoyment of another’s misfortune might reinforce a perceived moral order, schadenfreude can also arise in situations that lack any such moral justification (Haidt, 2003). Furthermore, the action tendencies stemming from schadenfreude are generally not aligned with the maintenance of moral norms in the way that anger and empathy are (Haidt, 2003).
text:
That schadenfreude played a role in behavioral mediation that was a mix of emotion and cognition goes against the purely emotional tenor of deonance theory (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). The mediating roles for anger and empathy did not involve cognition, as an (expanded) application of deonance theory would have expected. However, our schadenfreude results illustrate that the behavioral consequences of observing mistreatment may sometimes be more thoroughly considered. Emotions may activate memories that are consistent with the current feeling state or “fill in the gaps” in the data used to generate evaluations (Ochsner & Schacter, 2000; Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996). Reasoned behaviors would then be consistent with those evaluations, albeit in a way that remains emotion infused.

Of course, that mediation observation “buries the lede” a bit when reflecting on the implications of schadenfreude for deonance theory. Whereas emerging work on empathy could be reconciled with deonance theory by extending its spirit to include an additional moral emotion, such reconciliation seems less possible with schadenfreude. Haidt’s (2003) conceptualization of moral emotions voiced little support for placing schadenfreude in that category. Although there may be particular circumstances where pleasure from another’s misfortune somehow reaffirms the moral order, schadenfreude can be experienced in events where that is not the case (Haidt, 2003). Moreover, the action tendencies associated with schadenfreude do not preserve moral norms in the way anger and empathy can (Haidt, 2003).

Research on the internal fit of a High-Performance Work System (HPWS) is primarily rooted in the configurational perspective (A. D. Meyer et al., 1993; Miller, 1986). From this viewpoint, an organization’s strategy, structure, and practices form an interrelated and cohesive configuration—one in which individual elements cohere and mutually reinforce one another in pursuit of shared objectives, such as innovation or cost efficiency (A. D. Meyer et al., 1993; Miller, 1986). Applied to HR systems, this means that specific practices can collectively form an HPWS when they align around a common goal—most notably, enhancing workforce performance (Combs et al., 2006; Gerhart, 2012).

Within this context, the Ability–Motivation–Opportunity (AMO) model has emerged as the dominant framework for structuring HR practices to build an HPWS (Gerhart, 2007). The model stems from foundational theories of job performance, which posited that the impact of an employee’s ability on performance hinges on their level of motivation, and vice versa (Locke et al., 1978; Maier, 1955; Vroom, 1964). Building on this logic, Blumberg and Pringle (1982) introduced the concept of opportunity to perform, proposing that job performance arises from a combination of ability, motivation, and opportunity. This triadic framework was later extended to HRM, giving rise to the widely endorsed AMO model, which emphasizes the synergistic effects of HR practices aimed at enhancing employee ability, motivation, and opportunity to contribute (Boxall & Purcell, 2003; Gerhart, 2007; Jiang, Lepak, Han, et al., 2012; Lepak et al., 2006).
text:
As discussed, research on the internal fit of an HPWS is grounded in the configurational perspective (A. D. Meyer et al., 1993; Miller, 1986). According to this perspective, an organization’s attributes, such as strategy, structure, and practices, form a configuration of attributes that occur together. This configuration most often emerges when fit exists among elements that are mutually reinforcing in pursuit of common goals, such as innovation or cost reduction (A. D. Meyer et al., 1993; Miller, 1986). In the context of HR systems, individual practices may combine to form an HPWS given their common focus on increasing workforce performance (Combs et al., 2006; Gerhart, 2012).

The AMO model has served as the predominant framework to configure HR practices toward an HPWS (Gerhart, 2007). This model originated from earlier theoretical work on job performance that proposed that the effect of workers’ ability on performance depends on their motivation and vice versa (Locke et al., 1978; Maier, 1955; Vroom, 1964). By further discussing the role of “opportunity to perform,” Blumberg and Pringle (1982) proposed a theory of work performance suggesting that performance is a function of capacity (ability), willingness (motivation), and opportunity. Extending these early theories to the context of managing HR, scholars developed the widely accepted AMO model of HRM, which suggests synergistic interactions among HR practices designed to affect workforce AMO (e.g., Boxall & Purcell, 2003; Gerhart, 2007; Jiang, Lepak, Han, et al., 2012; Lepak et al., 2006).

use: Research on pacing has examined how group members time their contributions throughout the course of collective work in order to align with predetermined deadlines or task durations (e.g., Gersick, 1988, 1989; Metiu & Rothbard, 2013; Okhuysen & Eisenhardt, 2002; Waller, Zellmer-Bruhn, & Giambatista, 2002). However, this body of work often aggregates individual member activity, thereby obscuring who contributed what, and how those contributions connect to or build upon one another. For example, Gersick (1989) analyzed the timing and thematic content of group comments, but did not differentiate among speakers or specify how comments were linked in interaction. Her findings revealed significant variation in group pacing patterns, the importance of time-related discussion for shaping those patterns, and a tendency for groups to undergo a transition around the project’s midpoint. Extending this foundation, Ford and Sullivan (2004) theorized that novel contributions are more advantageous when introduced earlier in a group's process, while later-stage contributions, especially those made after the midpoint, may disrupt progress as groups typically pivot toward implementation at that stage. More recently, Riedl and Woolley (2017) showed that teams with more “bursty” patterns—marked by brief intervals between bursts of activity—tend to achieve better outcomes when engaged in crowdsourcing tasks. Although this stream of research has significantly enriched our understanding of how various types of contributions are distributed over time at the group level, it falls short in capturing the fluid and evolving nature of real-time interactional dynamics among individual group members.
text: Research on pacing has explored when members contribute during the course of group work in order to fit work into the allotted time (e.g., Gersick, 1988, 1989; Metiu & Rothbard, 2013; Okhuysen & Eisenhardt, 2002; Waller, Zellmer-Bruhn, & Giambatista, 2002). Yet, in this research, the contributions of group members are often aggregated, obscuring who contributed what and in relation to whom. For instance, Gersick (1989) coded the timing and content of comments within a group. However, her work remained agnostic as to who was offering a particular comment and in response to whom. She found that groups varied in how pacing occurred, that timing of time-related comments related to this pacing, and that the midpoint was a common time to transition. Building on this work, Ford and Sullivan (2004) theorized that (novel) contributions are more beneficial earlier in the group's work, and that they are more likely to disrupt group work when they occur after the midpoint, at a time when groups tend to shift their focus toward implementation. More recently, Riedl and Woolley (2017) found that teams that were more "bursty" (i.e., had shorter wait times between team activities) performed better when crowdsourcing. Although this research on pacing has been invaluable in deepening our understanding of the role of the distribution of different types of contributions at the group level of analysis, it falls short in illuminating the changing pattern of momentary interactions between individual members of the group.

use:
When employees perceive fairness and, as a result, identify with their group and its members, they tend to internalize group values, behavioral codes, norms, and expectations. This identification motivates them to avoid engaging in counterproductive work behavior (CWB)—intentional actions that violate the rules or legitimate interests of the group (Dalal, 2005)—because such restraint supports group functioning and, in turn, reinforces employees’ positive self-concepts and identity development (Holmvall & Bobocel, 2008; R. E. Johnson & Lord, 2010). However, employees may not view avoiding CWB primarily as a form of reciprocation for fair treatment, since adherence to group norms is generally regarded as a baseline expectation and a core role obligation. As a result, simply reducing CWB is unlikely to be interpreted as a meaningful or sufficient reciprocal response (Gouldner, 1960).

In contexts of negative reciprocity, where perceived injustice gives rise to CWB, such behavior tends to persist only to the extent that employees feel they have balanced the scales of exchange (Greco et al., 2019). This is partly because engaging in CWB demands significant personal resources. Research indicates that behaviors violating group norms often leave employees expending time and energy to justify or counterbalance their actions in order to preserve their self-image (Merritt et al., 2010). However, when employees disassociate their self-concept from the group due to perceived injustice, they are more likely to engage in persistent CWB aimed solely at advancing personal interests—with little regard for limits (R. E. Johnson et al., 2010).
text: When fairness prompts employees to tie their self-concept to the group and its members, employees internalize and adhere to group values, codes of behavior, norms, and expectations. They are motivated to refrain from displaying CWB-intentional acts that violate the rules and legitimate interests of the group (Dalal, 2005) because doing so would enhance group functioning and further circle back to reinforce positive self-evaluations and identity construction (Holmvall & Bobocel, 2008; R. E. Johnson & Lord, 2010). However, employees may not reciprocate fair treatment by mainly relying on restraining CWB because conformity with group rules and norms is thought to be their code of conduct and fundamental role responsibility. Thus, reducing CWB does not yield direct benefits for the group or members and is less likely to be considered an effective reciprocal action (Gouldner, 1960). In situations of negative reciprocity, to the extent that injustice triggers CWB, it would cease at the level when employees perceive that they make negative exchanges even (Greco et al., 2019). This is because engaging in CWB consumes substantial personal resources, as shown by studies suggesting that norm-violation behavior often makes employees spend much time and energy either justifying or compensating for this negative behavior to reduce its negative impact on self-perceptions (Merritt et al., 2010). However, when employees reject attaching the self to the group due to injustice, they constantly engage in CWB to serve self-interest without an upper limit (R. E. Johnson et al., 2010).

To explore the relationship between task proactivity and subsequent cognitive performance, we conducted two daily diary studies that measured daily task proactivity alongside end-of-day performance on a cognitive task. Study 1 was designed to establish a preliminary link between task proactivity and cognitive performance. Study 2 aimed to replicate these findings and strengthen confidence in the temporal sequence of the variables. To minimize self-reporting bias, we employed the "n-back task"—an objective cognitive task widely used by researchers in laboratory settings to assess cognitive performance (Jaeggi, Buschkuehl, et al., 2010; Jaeggi, Studer-Luethi, et al., 2010). Performance on tasks such as the n-back is a well-established proxy for underlying cognitive functioning (Hockey, 1997; Kahneman, 1973). We integrated this task into an ambulatory assessment, enabling measurement of cognitive performance within participants' everyday environments (Bolger & Laurenceau, 2013; Ohly et al., 2010). By incorporating this objective cognitive measure into a daily diary framework, we are able to examine how task proactivity relates to cognitive functioning under ecologically valid conditions.

In Study 3, we conducted a preregistered experiment in which task proactivity was systematically manipulated to assess its causal effect on two hypothesized mechanisms: elevated mental effort and decreased routineness. In Study 4, we directly compare these mechanisms to a leading alternative explanation—namely, that task proactivity imposes greater self-control demands, leading to ego depletion.
text: To investigate the relationship between task proactivity and subsequent cognitive performance, we conducted two daily diary studies assessing daily task proactivity as well as end-of-day performance in a cognitive task. Study 1 aims to establish the link between task proactivity and cognitive performance. Study 2 aims to replicate the findings of Study 1 and enhance confidence in the temporal ordering of the variables. To reduce individual biases related to self-reporting, we administer the "n-back task," an objective cognitive task extensively used by researchers in lab settings to examine cognitive performance (Jaeggi, Buschkuehl, et al., 2010; Jaeggi, Studer-Luethi, et al., 2010). Performance in cognitive tasks such as the n-back task has been consistently used as an indicator of the underlying cognitive functioning level of individuals (Hockey, 1997; Kahneman, 1973). We administer this cognitive task as part of our ambulatory assessment. This enables us to assess cognitive performance while participants remain embedded in their natural life contexts (Bolger & Laurenceau, 2013; Ohly et al., 2010). Studying cognitive performance in this novel way, including an objective measure in a daily diary study, allows us to examine the relationship of task proactivity with individuals' cognitive functioning in realistic conditions. In Study 3, a preregistered experiment, we then manipulate task proactivity and examine its effect on the proposed mechanisms of increased mental effort and reduced routineness. In Study 4, we contrast these proposed mechanisms with a key competing theoretical mechanism: increased self-control demands and resulting ego depletion.

Although citizenship behaviors can emerge as spontaneous expressions of emotion (George & Brief, 1992), justice scholars have been less inclined to explore emotions as mediators of the relationship between justice and citizenship, instead favoring social exchange mechanisms. More commonly, emotions have been examined to explain how perceptions of injustice lead to counterproductive work behaviors—defined as “discretionary behaviors that represent an attempt to harm the organization” (Sackett & DeVore, 2001). Spurred in part by Greenberg’s (1990) influential study linking organizational justice to outcomes like theft and turnover, researchers have since connected various justice dimensions to a wide range of counterproductive behaviors. These range from more passive forms such as cyberloafing (Lim, 2002) to more overt behaviors like sabotage (Ambrose et al., 2002) and interpersonal aggression (Greenberg & Barling, 1999).

Can emotions help explain the link between justice and counterproductive behaviors? Several studies that examined the relationship between justice perceptions and emotional responses also included counterproductive behaviors as outcome variables. Much like research on social exchange and citizenship behavior, these studies demonstrated—through mediation analyses—that emotions serve as a key conduit in the justice–counterproductivity link. For example, Judge et al. (2006) employed an experience sampling method to show that daily fluctuations in perceived interpersonal injustice predicted daily levels of counterproductive behavior, with anger mediating the relationship. Similarly, Fox et al. (2001) identified a mediating role for a composite of negative emotions including anger, sadness, fear, and boredom. In the domain of Personnel Psychology, Yang and Diefendorff (2009) extended these findings by showing that within-person variation in interpersonal injustice corresponded with within-person variation in counterproductive behaviors due to shifts in negative emotional states.
text:
Although citizenship behavior can occur as a spontaneous expression of affect (George & Brief, 1992), justice scholars have cast emotions as mediators of citizenship effects more rarely than social exchange indicators. Instead, emotions have tended to be used to explain justice effects on counterproductive behaviors—”discretionary behaviors that represent an attempt to harm the organization (Sackett & DeVore, 2001). Fueled in part by Greenberg's (1990) study that used theft and turnover as outcomes, scholars began linking justice dimensions to a number of counterproductive behaviors. Those included more passive examples like cyberloafing (Lim, 2002) and more active examples like sabotage (Ambrose et al., 2002) and interpersonal aggression (Greenberg & Barling, 1999). 

Can the connections between justice and counterproductive behaviors be explained by emotions? Some of the studies that linked justice to emotions also included counterproductive behavior as an outcome variable. As with the studies on social exchange and citizenship, those studies were able to show—”in a mediation sense—”that the justice-counterproductive connection was affect-based. Judge et al.'s (2006) experience sampling study showed that the relationship between daily variation in interpersonal injustice and daily variation in counterproductive behavior was mediated by anger. Similar results were obtained by Fox et al. (2001) using an amalgam of anger, sadness, fear, and boredom. In terms of Personnel Psychology, Yang and Diefendorff's (2009) study used the same amalgam to show that within-person variation in interpersonal injustice was associated with within-person variation in counterproductive behavior because of its effects on negative emotions.

use: The third premise addresses our perspective on institutional theory and situates our paper within this expansive and continually evolving intellectual tradition. Two interconnected points must be made in this regard. First, we do not conceive of institutional theory as a singular, unified perspective—nor do we believe it ought to be. Rather, we see it as a collection of distinctive analytical tools, loosely—and at times, uncomfortably—linked by a shared concern with a particular domain of social life: institutions. This view aligns with the stance that Scott (2014) appears to advocate, and which is embodied in his three-pillar framework. As he aptly put it, “The beginning of wisdom in approaching institutional theory is to recognize at the outset that there is not one but several variants” (Scott, 1987: 493). The second, related point is that our paper should not be interpreted as an attempt to critique or reform the version of institutional theory that crystallized in the late 1970s and early 1980s—nor is it an effort to reinsert values into that modern formulation. Rather, it aims to rearticulate and reinvigorate a distinct and independent line of thought, one that can—and indeed must—stand autonomously. While we openly embrace the notions of bridge-building and reconciliation, we choose those words intentionally. Bridges connect enduring divides, and reconciliation entails, among other things, discovering insight and truth in ideas once seen as irreconcilable or mutually exclusive.
text: The third premise concerns our understanding of institutional theory and our paper's envisioned place within this large and ever-growing intellectual enterprise. There are two related things that must be said here. The first is that we do not view institutional theory as a single perspective that can or should be fully unified. Instead, we view it as a set of distinctive analytical tools that are (loosely and often uneasily) connected by their shared interest in a particular part of the empirical world (i.e., institutions). This is the same basic orientation that Scott (2014) seems to recommend (and which his three-pillar framework embodies). In his words, "The beginning of wisdom in approaching institutional theory is to recognize at the outset that there is not one but several variants" (Scott, 1987: 493). The related point is that our paper should not be understood as an attempt to critique or reform the version of institutionalism that first emerged in the late 1970s and early 1980s (or as an attempt to "bring values back into" this modern approach). It is, instead, an effort to re-articulate and revitalize a distinct and autonomous perspective that can (and actually must) stand on its own legs. Although we have declared our enthusiasm for bridge building and reconciliation, these words are carefully chosen. Bridges span persistent chasms, and reconciliation is, among other things, a process that allows one to see the truth in ideas that were previously viewed as oppositional and mutually exclusive.

use: Prosocial values refer to the importance individuals place on safeguarding and enhancing the well-being of others as a central guiding principle in their lives (e.g., Schwartz, 1992; Schwartz & Bardi, 2001). I argue that task significance is more likely to lead to improved job performance among employees who strongly endorse prosocial values, compared to those who do not. This proposition is grounded in needs-supplies fit theory, which suggests that job performance is enhanced when there is alignment between employees' values and the attributes of their work environments (Edwards et al., 2006; Kristof, 1996). For employees with strong prosocial values, doing work that positively impacts others is highly meaningful. Task significance signals to these individuals that their roles offer opportunities to express and fulfill their prosocial motives (Cable & Edwards, 2004; Clary et al., 1998; De Dreu, 2006; Meglino & Korsgaard, 2004; Rioux & Penner, 2001). Accordingly, task significance is likely to motivate increased discretionary effort among such employees, allowing them to live out their values through their work. Conversely, employees who place less emphasis on prosocial values are less invested in the social impact of their work. For them, task significance holds less personal meaning and thus is less likely to enhance their performance. Based on this reasoning, I hypothesize that task significance more strongly boosts job performance among employees with strong prosocial values.
text: Prosocial values describe the extent to which individuals regard protecting and promoting the welfare of others as important guiding principles in life (e.g., Schwartz, 1992; Schwartz & Bardi, 2001). I propose that task significance is more likely to increase job performance for employees with strong prosocial values than for employees with weak prosocial values. The rationale for this hypothesis derives from theory and research on needs-supplies fit, which suggests that when employees' jobs match their values, they are more willing to invest time and energy in performing effectively (Edwards, Cable, Williamson, Lambert, & Shipp, 2006; Kristof, 1996). Employees with strong prosocial values care about doing work that has a positive impact on others. Task significance communicates to employees with strong prosocial values that their jobs provide the opportunity to express and fulfill their values of benefiting others (Cable & Edwards, 2004; Clary et al., 1998; De Dreu, 2006; Meglino & Korsgaard, 2004; Rioux & Penner, 2001). As a result, employees with strong prosocial values are likely to display enhanced effort in response to task significance to express and fulfill their values of benefiting others. In contrast, employees with weak prosocial values are less concerned about the positive impact of their work on others. As such, task significance has less relevance to their value expression and fulfillment and is thereby less likely to influence their performance. Therefore, it is hypothesized that task significance is more likely to increase job performance for employees with strong prosocial values.

Following established practices that adapt the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines for conducting integrative reviews (Sarkis-Onofre, Catalá-López, Aromataris, & Lockwood, 2021; e.g., Chen, Mehra, Tasselli, & Borgatti, 2022; see also Cronin & George, 2023), we employed a four-step process—illustrated in Figure 1—to identify peer-reviewed articles published in academic journals. In Step 1, we conducted a comprehensive search using the PsycINFO database, structuring our inquiry around four distinct search fields. The first field contained terms related to technology use for nonwork purposes, such as “cyberslacking” and “cyberloafing,” commonly used within the counterproductive work behavior (CWB) literature. The second field included terms denoting the work context, such as “work,” “job,” and “employee.” These first two fields were combined using an “AND” operator to ensure the search captured studies addressing nonwork-related technology use within workplace settings. The third field drew from the recovery literature and included terms like “microbreaks” and “workday breaks.” In the fourth field, we added descriptors from the descriptive literature, such as “setting-inconsistent mobile communication,” “context incongruent smartphone use,” and “family-interrupting work behaviors.” 

Importantly, we applied no disciplinary or journal-based restrictions, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of our integrative review. Furthermore, we chose not to limit results by journal quality metrics (e.g., impact factor or CiteScore), as such filters can inadvertently bias the evidence base by excluding potentially valuable studies (Daniels, 2019). Our initial search in Step 1 yielded 1,409 peer-reviewed articles for preliminary inclusion. A complete list of the search terms and parameters is provided in Table 1.
text: Consistent with approaches that adapt preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses (PRISMA) guidelines for conducting integrative reviews (Sarkis-Onofre, Catalá-López, Aromataris, & Lockwood, 2021; e.g., Chen, Mehra, Tasselli, & Borgatti, 2022; see also Cronin & George, 2023), we used a four-step process displayed in Figure 1 to search for peer-reviewed articles published in academic journals. In Step 1, we conducted a search for articles using the PsycINFO database with four separate search fields. The first two fields included (a) terminology associated with technology use for nonwork-related purposes (including "cyberslacking" and "cyberloafing" from the CWB community); and (b) work-related terms including "work," "job," and "employee." These two fields were combined with an "AND" function to appropriately capture studies that examined use (first field) in a work context (second field). Additionally, we entered specific terminology from the recovery community—including "microbreaks" and "workday breaks"—in the third field, while in the fourth field we entered terminology from the descriptive community, including "setting-inconsistent mobile communication," "context incongruent smartphone use," and "family-interrupting work behaviors." Importantly, we did not place any limits based on discipline or journal since our integrative review is interdisciplinary in scope. We also did not place any limits attempting to filter based on journal quality metrics (e.g., impact factor or CiteScore) since this can unintentionally introduce bias regarding the type of evidence included or excluded for review (Daniels, 2019). Step 1 returned 1,409 academic articles for initial inclusion. Table 1 provides the full list of our search terms and parameters.

These two fields were combined using the "AND" Boolean operator to ensure that selected studies addressed nonwork technology use specifically within the workplace context. 

In addition, the third search field incorporated terms from the recovery literature—such as "microbreaks" and "workday breaks"—while the fourth field included terminology rooted in the descriptive literature, such as "setting-inconsistent mobile communication," "context incongruent smartphone use," and "family-interrupting work behaviors." Similarly, we avoided filtering by journal quality metrics (e.g., impact factor or CiteScore) to minimize bias that could influence which types of evidence were included or omitted (Daniels, 2019). This first step yielded 1,409 academic articles for initial inclusion. A comprehensive list of search terms and inclusion parameters is presented in Table 1.
