Although much of the existing research on construal level theory connects high-level construals with stronger ethical and moral commitments (e.g., Tenbrunsel et al., 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017), we propose that this association may not hold in contexts where safety and risk mitigation are critical. In such environments, abstract interpretations of the work context—i.e., high-level construals—may actually undermine ethical awareness. Specifically, we argue that high-level construals of the work environment lead to diminished perceptions of harm, or a reduced sense of the extent to which one's actions might directly or indirectly cause damage to stakeholders (e.g., Schein & Gray, 2018). This occurs because high-level construals often involve abstract and generalized thinking that blurs or distorts recognition of potential harm. We identify three features of high-level construals that contribute to this muted perception of harm.

First, high-level construals are linked to lower estimations of both the likelihood and severity of harmful workplace events. For instance, individuals operating under high-level construals are more likely to view harmful occurrences as less probable (Wakslak & Trope, 2009), making such risks feel less immediate or pressing (Amit & Greene, 2012). Furthermore, high-level construals tend to frame events as more abstract or hypothetical, reducing their perceived immediacy and relevance (Liberman & Trope, 2014; Trope et al., 2007), which can, in turn, lower perceived risk (e.g., Brügger et al., 2016). Additionally, high-level construals promote greater psychological distance from others—for example, perceiving others as less similar (Stephan et al., 2010)—which can weaken the perceived reality or emotional salience of their potential suffering. Taken together, these tendencies suggest that high-level construals foster a perception of the workplace in which harmful events—and harm itself—appear less probable or concerning.
text:
Although most research on construal level has linked high-level construals with valuing ethics and morality (e.g., Tenbrunsel et al., 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017), we hypothesize that in contexts where safety and risk mitigation is paramount, high-level construals of the work environment may actually be suboptimal with respect to ethics. Specifically, we argue that high-level work context construals result in diminished perceived harm—the degree to which individuals anticipate damage will directly or indirectly befall stakeholders (e.g., Schein & Gray, 2018). This is because high-level work context construals are associated with perceptions of the work context that obfuscate potential sources of harm. There are three specific aspects of high-level construals that speak to this potential for reducing perceptions of harm.

First, high-level work context construals are associated with lower perceived potential of occurrence and impact of harmful events at work. For example, high-level construals are associated with perceiving harmful events as less likely (Wakslak & Trope, 2009), meaning the potential for harm is less salient (Amit & Greene, 2012). Relatedly, high-level construals are associated with perceiving events as more hypothetical and less “real” (Liberman & Trope, 2014; Trope et al., 2007) which can lead to lowered perceptions of risks (e.g., Brügger et al., 2016). Also, high-level construals are associated with perceiving others as more psychologically distant (e.g., more dissimilar; Stephan et al., 2010) which could weaken the “realness” of potential injuries. That is, high-level work context construals likely carry the perception that harmful events—and therefore harm itself—are less likely to occur at work.

To explore the connection between coworker work-family support and a partner’s work-related creativity, we draw on and integrate two theoretical frameworks: the Work-Family Facilitation (W-FF) model (Wayne et al., 2007) and the Integrative Model of Employee and Household Enrichment (IMHE; Quinn et al., 2012). The W-FF model adopts a resource-gain-development perspective, positing that individuals proactively acquire resources in one domain (e.g., work) to enhance functioning in another (e.g., family). While both work-family facilitation and enrichment (Greenhaus & Powell, 2006) emphasize the transfer of resources across the work-home boundary, facilitation is distinct in its broader systems-based lens (Wayne et al., 2007). A key feature of the W-FF model is its capacity to account for how resources gained by one partner in the workplace can positively influence both family and work outcomes of the other partner through bidirectional facilitation processes—namely, work-to-family and family-to-work.

The W-FF model’s emphasis on resource dynamics is grounded in the Conservation of Resources (COR) theory (Hobfoll, 1989, 2002), which suggests individuals are motivated to acquire, retain, and safeguard valued resources. These resources can include tangible assets (e.g., a home), personal traits (e.g., self-efficacy), life circumstances (e.g., marital status), or intangible energies (e.g., emotional or cognitive energy), all of which individuals value or use to pursue additional desired assets, attributes, or conditions. According to both the W-FF model and COR theory, people leverage their existing resources to generate additional ones, creating cycles of accumulation commonly referred to as gain spirals (Hobfoll, 2002).
text: To understand the relationship between coworker work-family support and a partner's work-related creativity, our theorizing integrates the W-FF model (Wayne et al., 2007) and the IMHE (Quinn et al., 2012). The W-FF model advances a resource-gain-development perspective according to which individuals proactively seek to obtain resources in one domain (e.g., work) to facilitate improved system functioning in another domain (e.g., family). Although the concepts of work-family facilitation and work-family enrichment (see Greenhaus & Powell, 2006) share similarities, such as a focus on how resources travel across the work-home interface, facilitation is distinct in that it adopts a wider systems perspective (Wayne et al., 2007). Importantly, the inherent systems view of the W-FF allows for predictions concerning how resources gained by one member of a couple at work may improve family and work outcomes of the other member via bidirectional facilitation processes (i.e., work-to-family and family-to-work). Given its focus on resources, the W-FF model draws from insights of conservation of resources (COR) theory (Hobfoll, 1989, 2002), which proposes that individuals strive to obtain, retain, and protect resources (Hobfoll, 2002). Resources are described as objects (e.g., a house), personal characteristics (e.g., self-efficacy), conditions (e.g., marital status), or energies (e.g., cognitive energy) that persons value or that serve as a means for the attainment of further objects, personal characteristics, conditions, or energies. According to both the W-FF model and COR theory, individuals may utilize their existing resources to gain further resources, resulting in the accumulation of personal resources also referred to as gain spirals (Hobfoll, 2002).

Holland (1959, 1997) proposed a typology comprising six interest-based categories—Realistic, Investigative, Social, Conventional, Enterprising, and Artistic—commonly referred to by the acronym RIASEC. This framework was designed to describe both individuals and occupations, positing that alignment between the two (i.e., person–job fit) promotes more effective and satisfying career choices. According to Holland, individuals employed in roles congruent with their personal attributes are more likely to enjoy their work, demonstrate higher levels of performance and motivation, and show greater success and commitment compared to those in mismatched jobs (Holland, 1997). Empirical support for this theory is extensive: research consistently links person–job fit to a range of positive outcomes, including increased job satisfaction, greater persistence, and enhanced performance (e.g., Ghetta et al., 2020; Hoff et al., 2020; Kristof-Brown et al., 2005; Nye et al., 2017; Su, 2020; Van Iddekinge et al., 2011).

In addition, Holland’s theory emphasizes the role of personality traits in shaping career interests and choices (Holland, 1997). Supporting this notion, a recent meta-analysis found small to moderate correlations (r = −.08 to .36) between RIASEC interest types and the Big Five personality traits (Hurtado Rúa et al., 2019). Notably, the strongest associations emerged between artistic interests and openness to experience (r = .36), and enterprising interests and extraversion (r = .27). However, the majority of studies examining these relationships have relied on self-report measures for both interest types and personality traits, which raises concerns about common method bias. While a few researchers have used actual job titles to assess occupational fit (e.g., Judge et al., 1999; Woods & Hampson, 2010), attempts to map these job titles onto the RIASEC framework and link them to personality dimensions have thus far been limited in scope, often covering only a narrow spectrum of occupations (Deng et al., 2007).
text:
Holland (1959, 1997) proposed six interest-based categories (realistic, investigative, social, conventional, enterprising, and artistic, referred to by the acronym RIASEC) to characterize both individuals and occupations, suggesting that congruence between them (person–job fit) contributes to a successful career choice. He proposed that people working in person-congruent occupations often enjoy their work more than those at jobs with characteristics that do not match theirs, being better-performing and more motivated, successful, and committed (Holland, 1997). Supporting this hypothesis, person–job fit is often associated with higher satisfaction, more persistence, and better performance, among other positive outcomes (Ghetta et al., 2020; Hoff et al., 2020; Kristof-Brown et al., 2005; Nye et al., 2017; Su, 2020; Van Iddekinge et al., 2011).
Holland’s theory also postulates personality traits as important factors involved in occupational interests and, thereby, career choices (Holland, 1997). Indeed, a recent meta-analysis reported small to moderate (r = −.08 to .36) correlations between the RIASEC job interests and the Big Five personality domains (Hurtado Rúa et al., 2019), with the highest correlations between the openness and extraversion personality domains and artistic and enterprising interests, respectively, r = .36 and .27. However, most studies have assessed both the RIASEC interests and personality traits using self-report questionnaires rather than people’s actual job titles, introducing possible shared method biases. Although a few studies have had access to actual job titles (e.g., Judge et al., 1999; Woods & Hampson, 2010), mapping these into the RIASEC framework and then relating the results with personality traits has only covered a limited range of occupations yet (Deng et al., 2007).

Work motivation refers to the set of psychological forces that direct, energize, and sustain effort toward goals in jobs, projects, and tasks. In the early days of research on work motivation, scholars acknowledged the vital role of relationships. The classic Hawthorne studies famously indicated that increased productivity stemmed not from alterations in physical conditions like lighting, but rather from the perception of being observed and valued by managers—though contemporary analyses suggest these effects may have been more modest than initially believed (Levitt & List, 2011). As the field advanced, psychologists increasingly centered their theories on intrapsychic mechanisms—what goes on inside employees’ minds—as well as the structural elements of tasks and incentive systems. In doing so, social influences were largely relegated to the periphery, acknowledged only intermittently in discussions of equity concerns, power and affiliation motives, collectivistic goals, managerial roles in shaping—or eroding—intrinsic motivation, and job designs that support social contact, camaraderie, and meaningful impact on others.

Meanwhile, organizational scholars exploring leadership, teams, justice, power, status, diversity, and emotions delved deeply into relational dynamics but seldom examined them through the lens of motivation. More recently, however, these parallel lines of inquiry have begun to intersect. Researchers in organizational psychology and behavior have made significant progress in understanding how social contexts and relationships drive motivation at work. This convergence is especially timely given that work has become more inherently and pervasively social: We operate in broader networks, engage in more service-oriented roles, navigate team-based structures, collaborate across functions, and frequently find ourselves in a flurry of meetings. At the same time, work is becoming socially different. Organizations are gradually becoming more diverse, and our interactions are increasingly taking place in virtual environments.
text: Work motivation is the set of psychological forces that guide, energize, and sustain effort in jobs, projects, and tasks. At the dawn of work motivation research, scholars recognized the critical role of relationships. The classic Hawthorne experiments suggested that productivity was motivated not by changes in lighting and other working conditions but by attention from managers (although recent analyses suggest the effects are subtle; see Levitt & List, 2011). As psychologists developed theories of work motivation, though, they tended to focus on the processes inside employees' heads as well as the nature of their tasks and incentives. Social factors were largely painted into the background, with occasional nods to equity preferences, power and affiliation motives, collectivistic goals, the roles of managers in setting goals and undermining intrinsic motivation, and the design of jobs that create opportunities for social interaction, friendship, and a positive impact on other people. Meanwhile, organizational scholars interested in leadership, teams, justice, power, status, diversity, and emotions went on to study relationships in depth, but they rarely examined them through the lens of motivation. In recent years, these two streams have begun to converge, and scholars of organizational psychology and organizational behavior have made meaningful strides toward understanding the social forces behind work motivation. This progress has proven timely, as the nature of work has become increasingly social: We have wider networks, more service jobs, more teams, more cross-functional collaborations, and (gasp) more meetings. At the same time, work has become differently social: Our organizations are slowly growing more diverse, and our interactions have rapidly gone virtual.

Automated video interviews (AVIs) rely on interviewee behaviors as inputs for supervised machine learning (ML) models that evaluate candidates' knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs; Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022). Organizations are increasingly implementing AVIs early in their hiring processes due to several potential advantages: AVIs offer high scalability and 24/7 accessibility, enhancing convenience and access for applicants; candidates may respond more favorably to the interview format compared to traditional personality or cognitive ability tests, which can reduce the risk of legal challenges (Hausknecht et al., 2004); they also significantly reduce labor and financial costs relative to manual screening techniques (Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022); and according to vendor claims, AVIs improve hiring quality and shorten time-to-hire (Oswald et al., 2020). Early findings indicate that AVIs may be a viable method for evaluating candidates (Nguyen et al., 2014). However, existing psychometric evaluations of AVIs have primarily focused on noncognitive traits, such as personality (e.g., Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022; Jayaratne & Jayatilleke, 2020), leaving gaps in our understanding of their use in other domains.

Against this backdrop, emerging discussions around artificial intelligence (AI) tools for personnel selection have proposed the potential of AVIs to assess cognitive ability (Dattner et al., 2019; Mondragon, 2021). Practical considerations further drive this interest: the utility of AVIs can be enhanced if they are capable of assessing both cognitive and noncognitive attributes, recognizing the connection between cognitive abilities and maximum performance, and between noncognitive traits and typical performance (Marcus et al., 2007). This growing attention from organizations calls for empirical research that develops and validates AVI-based cognitive ability assessments, examining their reliability and validity. Such work could meaningfully advance our understanding of how human attributes are expressed through interview behavior.
text:
Automated video interviews (AVIs) use interviewee behaviors as inputs to supervised machine learning (ML) models that score interviewee knowledge, skills, abilities, and/or other characteristics (KSAOs; Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022). Organizations are increasingly adopting AVIs early in the hiring process for several potential benefits: AVIs are highly scalable and available on-demand, increasing convenience and access for applicants; applicants may react more positively to interviews than conventional personality and cognitive ability tests, reducing the likelihood of litigation (Hausknecht et al., 2004); AVIs provide considerable person-hour and cost savings compared to manual screening methods (Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022); and vendors claim that AVIs improve the quality of new hires and reduce time to hire (Oswald et al., 2020). Initial research suggests AVIs are potentially viable for assessing interviewees (Nguyen et al., 2014). However, existing psychometric studies on AVIs are limited to noncognitive constructs, such as personality traits (e.g., Hickman, Bosch, et al., 2022; Jayaratne & Jayatilleke, 2020).

In light of this, recent forward-thinking discussions of artificial intelligence (AI) tools for selection have pointed to the possibility of using AVIs to assess cognitive ability (Dattner et al., 2019; Mondragon, 2021). Moreover, vendors and organizations may be motivated for practical reasons: The utility of AVIs can be increased if they simultaneously assess cognitive and noncognitive constructs, as they uniquely relate to maximum and typical performance, respectively (Marcus et al., 2007). This manifest interest from organizations needs answering by empirical research that develops AVI cognitive ability assessments and examines their psychometric properties. Such research can extend knowledge of how human attributes relate to interviewee behavior.

use: Fourth, our study offers a valuable contribution by connecting individual-level conflict to broader collective dynamics across multiple levels of analysis. Specifically, in Study 1, we observed that intra-team conflict was negatively associated with task performance, while inter-team conflict showed a positive association. These patterns held consistent both when the analysis was conducted using a team-level temporal unit and when conflict was reclassified by type (see Appendix B in Additional Materials). This highlights the need for more robust theorizing on team boundaries—particularly how and why they shape team dynamics—which has meaningful implications for both the conflict and multiteam system (MTS) literatures. Within the conflict literature, prior empirical findings on intergroup conflict have predominantly emerged from resource-constrained environments where independent teams compete for limited resources. In such contexts, intergroup conflict has been shown to yield both beneficial outcomes (e.g., enhancing intragroup cohesion; Hinsz & Betts, 2012) and detrimental ones (e.g., spillover effects; Bornstein, Budescu, & Zamir, 1997), often influenced by the underlying power dynamics within teams (Van Bunderen, Greer, & Van Knippenberg, 2018). Our study advances this conversation by examining conflict within MTSs—settings where teams are interdependent rather than independent and where goals are hierarchically aligned. We found that the positive effects of inter-team conflict persisted, but we propose a different underlying mechanism. Specifically, team boundaries confer a sense of entitativity and provide teams with the autonomy to resist disruptions from external forces (Blanchard, Caudill, & Walker, 2020). In doing so, our study shifts the discourse around team boundaries, offering new explanations for their influence on collective dynamics.
text: Fourth, our study enables linking individual conflict to the larger collective across levels of analysis. Notably, in Study 1, the covariates of intra-team conflict and inter-team conflict exhibited negative and positive relationships with task performance, respectively—and these results were consistent when the analyses were conducted at the team-level temporal unit and when conflict was recoded based on types (see Appendix B in Additional Materials). Stronger theorizing on team boundaries, including how and why team boundaries influence team dynamics, offers important implications for both the broader conflict literature and the MTS literature. Starting with the conflict literature, to date, empirical findings on intergroup conflicts have been grounded in resource-finite contexts where two or more independent teams compete against one another for necessary resources to perform. In such contexts, intergroup conflicts have been found to have both positive (e.g., intragroup cohesion; Hinsz & Betts, 2012) and negative (e.g., spillover effect; Bornstein, Budescu, & Zamir, 1997) effects on intragroup functioning, and these effects are largely dependent on the intra-team power structure (Van Bunderen, Greer, & Van Knippenberg, 2018). Our study of conflict in MTSs expands this line of research to a different organizational context (i.e., interdependent teams as opposed to independent teams), where teams had a well-aligned goal hierarchy. Our findings revealed that the positive effects of between-team conflict still manifested—but we theorize it occurs for a different reason. Team boundaries grant the teams entitativity, including the autonomy to resist forces attempting to disrupt them (Blanchard, Caudill, & Walker, 2020). Thus, our study changes the conversation about why team boundaries influence team dynamics.

We investigated these dynamics through two complementary studies. In Study 1, we analyzed a rich dataset comprising 3,036 temporal units collected from continuous audio and video recordings of six live-actor simulated mass-casualty exercises. This allowed us to examine the nuanced effects of intra-individual conflict experiences on task performance. Specifically, we found that sending within-team conflict and receiving between-team conflict were positively associated with individual task performance. In contrast, receiving within-team conflict was negatively associated with performance. In Study 2, we conducted a scenario-based vignette experiment designed to uncover the theoretical mechanisms behind these differing effects. The results from this mixed-design experiment support the notion that the cognitive consequences of conflict—such as on-task attentional focus versus off-task attentional disruption—depend on both the direction of conflict involvement (sending vs. receiving) and the locus of conflict (within vs. between team boundaries).

This article makes several key contributions. Most centrally, it advances the conflict literature by revealing the microfoundations of conflict experiences within collectives—delving into the detailed and moment-to-moment nature of such experiences (Barney & Felin, 2013). By introducing two structural dimensions of individual conflict—direction (sending vs. receiving) and team boundary context (within-team vs. between-team)—we offer a framework for understanding when and why conflict can either enhance or impair individual task performance. Our findings shift the focus in conflict research from aggregate effects of individual conflict to a finer-grained analysis of how individuals experience conflict over time. In doing so, we challenge the literature to account not only for inter-individual variation but also for intra-individual variation—recognizing that individuals may encounter qualitatively different conflict episodes over time, with distinct theoretical and practical implications.
text:
We examined these relationships in two studies. In Study 1, using rich behavioral data (3,036 temporal units) extracted from continuous audio and video streams of six live-actor simulated mass-casualty exercises, we analyzed the mixed effects of intra-individual conflict experiences. More specifically, both sending within-team conflict and receiving between-team conflict experiences have a positive relationship with task performance, whereas the relationship is negative for receiving within-team conflict experiences. In Study 2, we utilized a scenario-based vignette experiment to proffer evidence for the underlying theoretical mechanisms behind these different effects. The evidence from the mixed-design experiment affirms that the two distinct mechanisms behind senders and receivers’ conflict experiences (i.e., on-task attentional pull, off-task attentional demands) are altered by whether they occur within versus between team boundaries.

This article offers several important contributions. Our core contribution is to the conflict literature, as we unpack the microfoundations (i.e., exploring a phenomenon at greater levels of fidelity; Barney & Felin, 2013) of conflict experiences in collectives. Notably, the introduction of two structural elements of individual conflict experiences—directions (sending vs. receiving) and team boundaries (within team vs. between team)—enable explanation of when and why some individual conflict experiences benefit, while others hurt, individual task performance. Thus, our study changes the conversation in the conflict literature from talking about overall effects of individual conflict to unpacking individuals’ unique experiences over time. This challenges the conflict literature to not only consider the potential for inter-individual differences but also intra-individual differences. Individuals can have unique conflict experiences over time and the differences between those experiences are of theoretical and practical importance.

To theorize how individuals’ experiences of conflict affect their task performance in real time, we define “conflict” as behaviors through which individuals express opposing viewpoints (Weingart et al., 2015). A “conflict experience” comprises one or more of these expressions, where a sender communicates a problem or issue to a receiver through verbal and/or non-verbal means (Tsai & Bendersky, 2016; Weingart et al., 2015). The characteristics of different conflict experiences can be analyzed using the structural features of “communication”—defined as the process by which information and shared meaning are conveyed from one person to another (Keyton, 2011).

The first structural feature of a conflict experience is its direction: whether the individual is sending or receiving a conflict message. Every communication event includes both a sender and a receiver (Lunenberg, 2010). In the context of conflict, the sender is the individual who initiates the communication, aiming to convey the significance of a particular issue or disagreement to others. The receiver, often referred to as the "target," is the individual toward whom the conflict expression is directed. The second structural feature is the boundary of the conflict experience—whether it occurs within the team or between teams. Gerbner’s (1956) general communication model emphasizes that communicative acts are not perceived objectively but are shaped by context—for example, whether the conflict occurs within or across team boundaries (Maloney et al., 2016). Research on team boundary dynamics suggests that such boundaries can fundamentally alter how various constructs interact and relate (e.g., de Vries et al., 2016; Porck et al., 2019).
text:
To theorize the effect of individuals’ conflict experiences on their task performance as they occur, we define “conflict” as behaviors through which individuals express oppositional standpoints (Weingart et al., 2015). “Conflict experiences” are composed of one or more expressions, wherein senders verbally and/or non-verbally communicate an issue(s) to receivers (Tsai & Bendersky, 2016; Weingart et al., 2015).1 Characteristics of various conflict experiences can be understood with the structural elements of “communication,” the process of transmitting information and common understanding from one person to another (Keyton, 2011).

The first structural element of conflict experience is the direction of conflict experiences (sending vs. receiving conflict). Every communication exchange involves a sender and a receiver (Lunenberg, 2010). Applying this to conflict expressions, the sender initiates the communication of a conflict issue(s) and has a need or desire to convey the significance of the problem to others. The receiver of the conflict expression is the “target,” to whom the conflict issue is directed. The second structural element of conflict experience is the boundary of conflict experiences (within team vs. between team). Gerbner’s (1956) general model of communication emphasizes that communication agents (i.e., the sender or receiver of a message) cannot objectively grasp an event. Instead, individuals perceive communicative events through the lens of the context; that is, the circumstances under which an event occurs, such as their team membership, within or between team boundaries (Maloney et al., 2016). Research on the dynamics of team boundaries supports that team boundaries can fundamentally alter the nature of relationships between various constructs (e.g., de Vries et al., 2016; Porck et al., 2019).

According to social identity theory, individuals instinctively and swiftly categorize others into "us" and "them" (Tajfel & Turner, 1986). This group categorization fosters intergroup bias—defined as a tendency to favor one’s ingroup over outgroups (Richeson & Sommers, 2016; Tajfel & Turner, 1986). Research has consistently demonstrated the pervasiveness of intergroup bias across both naturalistic and controlled settings, and across a wide array of outcomes, including behaviors and judgments (Brewer, 1979; Pettigrew, 1979). Importantly, intergroup bias is not only a common phenomenon; people also frequently expect others to act in biased ways—presuming they will privilege their ingroup over outgroups (Jin & Baillargeon, 2017; Platow et al., 1997; Wenzel & Mummendey, 1996). Studies on metaperceptions show that observers often overestimate the extent to which people discriminate in favor of their ingroup (Kteily et al., 2016; Lees & Cikara, 2020; Vorauer & Kumhyr, 2001). These expectations emerge early in development: infants anticipate helping behavior from ingroup—but not outgroup—members (Jin & Baillargeon, 2017), and preschoolers expect harm to be more frequently directed toward outgroup members than ingroup members (Rhodes, 2012).

In light of this, when a manager engages in selective silence—choosing to speak up about an issue concerning their ingroup but remaining silent on a comparable issue involving an outgroup—it reinforces existing expectations of intergroup bias. In doing so, the manager effectively exhibits partiality by offering vocal support to the ingroup while withholding similar support from the outgroup, thereby affirming assumptions that individuals act preferentially toward their own group.
text:
According to social identity theory, people rapidly and automatically sort others into “us versus them” (Tajfel & Turner, 1986). This classification into group categories engenders intergroup bias, defined as a preference for ingroup over outgroup members (Richeson & Sommers, 2016; Tajfel & Turner, 1986). Intergroup biases have been shown in a variety of situations (e.g., naturalistic, artificial) and found in a wide range of outcomes (e.g., behaviors, judgments; Brewer, 1979; Pettigrew, 1979). Not only is intergroup bias prevalent, but observers frequently presume that others will act in a biased manner, conferring advantage to their ingroup over their outgroup (Jin & Baillargeon, 2017; Platow et al., 1997; Wenzel & Mummendey, 1996). For example, research on metaperceptions indicate that observers tend to assume people will discriminate in favor of the ingroup over the outgroup more than they actually do (Kteily et al., 2016; Lees & Cikara, 2020; Vorauer & Kumhyr, 2001). This expectation of intergroup bias appears to arise even among young children. Infants expect help to be provided by ingroup (but not outgroup) members (Jin & Baillargeon, 2017), and preschoolers predict harm to be directed more to outgroup compared to ingroup members (Rhodes, 2012).

Thus, when a manager engages in selective silence by previously speaking up on an ingroup reference issue but is then silent on an outgroup focal issue, the manager confirms this expectation of intergroup bias (people behave in ways to support their ingroup and not an outgroup). The manager is, in fact, demonstrating disproportionate support for their ingroup compared to an outgroup by speaking up for the ingroup and remaining silent on an outgroup.

Our structural equation modeling results revealed an intriguing pattern: prosocial behavior toward the victim was driven primarily by emotion, not by a combination of emotion and cognition. While empathy for the victim was linked to more favorable evaluations of the victim, these evaluations did not mediate the relationship between witnessing mistreatment and engaging in prosocial behavior. Rather—similar to the role played by anger—it was empathy alone that mediated this connection. This suggests that an expanded conceptualization of deonance theory incorporating empathy would still support its core proposition: behavioral responses to observed mistreatment tend to be affective, immediate, and instinctive rather than carefully deliberated (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). We hope these findings encourage scholars to revisit and broaden deonance theory, and to pursue further investigation of victim-focused responses.

The importance of such research becomes especially clear in light of the counterintuitive findings from our meta-analytic synthesis. Although observing mistreatment was positively associated with empathic feelings toward the victim, it was negatively associated with evaluating the victim favorably. Surprisingly, it was also linked to increased antisocial behavior toward the victim. And although mistreatment observation did correlate positively with prosocial behavior toward the victim, the effect size was quite small (rc = .11). Collectively, these results demonstrate that third parties, on average, respond to mistreatment by forming more negative impressions of the victim, at times engaging in behavior that may further harm the victim, and offering less support than one might expect. These patterns appear at odds with even an expanded version of deonance theory, setting the stage for a deeper exploration of our findings on schadenfreude.
text:
Interestingly, our structural equation modeling results showed that prosocial behavior toward the victim was fueled by emotion rather than a mix of emotion and cognition. Although empathy toward the victim was associated with more positive evaluations of the victim, those evaluations did not play a mediating role in the connection between observing mistreatment and prosocial behavior toward the victim. Instead—just as with anger—that connection was mediated solely by empathy toward the victim. Thus, an expanded version of the deontic response that includes empathy would share deonance theory’s argument that behavioral reactions to observing mistreatment are rapid and not thoroughly considered (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). We hope these results encourage expanded formulations of deonance theory—as well as additional studies of victim-focused reactions.

The need for more studies of victim-focused reactions is apparent from the counterintuitive findings from our meta-analytic synthesis. Although observing mistreatment was positively related to feelings of empathy toward the victim, it was negatively related to the evaluation of the victim. Moreover, observing mistreatment was positively related to antisocial behavior toward the victim. In addition, although observing mistreatment was positively related to prosocial behavior toward the victim, the size of that effect (rc = .11) is strikingly small. Those results illustrate that, in general, third parties react to mistreatment events by thinking less of the victim, engaging in actions that may harm the victim, and failing to help the victim to the degree that might be expected. Such results seem inconsistent with even an expanded conceptualization of deonance theory, bringing us to the results observed for schadenfreude.

Research consistently shows that individuals are particularly attracted to the misconduct of high-status actors, largely because status amplifies the visibility and perceived significance of their transgressions (e.g., Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin, Bundy, Porac, Wade, & Quinn, 2013). Beyond the attention these individuals naturally command (Sauder, Lynn, & Podolny, 2012), audiences often hold high-status actors to elevated moral and behavioral standards, expecting them to embody exemplary values (Pollock, Lashley, Rindova, & Han, 2019). When these expectations are violated, the misconduct appears especially deviant relative to that of lower-status individuals, thereby attracting greater media interest and public scrutiny (Graffin et al., 2013). Such violations frequently give rise to scandals—defined as “publicized transgressions that run counter to established norms” (Piazza & Jourdan, 2018: 165).

Despite the well-established influence of transgressors’ status, the broader conditions that lead to scandalization remain poorly understood, having received limited scholarly attention (exceptions include Nyhan, 2014, 2017). Much of the existing research takes an event’s classification as a scandal for granted (e.g., Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013; Jonsson, Greve, & Fujiwara-Greve, 2009; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018), focusing instead on how status shapes the consequences of scandals. This approach is problematic because misconduct initially occurs in private and is often tolerated as long as it remains out of public view (Adut, 2005). Indeed, Adut (2008: 19) defines scandalizable misconduct as “anything that will bring about shame or that will embarrass or provoke when made public.” Critically, misconduct becomes a scandal only when it is broadly publicized by a mediating institution, such as the media (Thompson, 2000). While the nature of the wrongdoing is clearly important, we still lack a deeper understanding of why the media chooses to expose—and thereby scandalize—some instances of misconduct while ignoring others.
text:
Research has consistently found that individuals are drawn to high-status actors’ wrongdoing because status imparts salience to their misconduct (e.g., Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin, Bundy, Porac, Wade, & Quinn, 2013). Apart from the attention high-status actors’ behaviors receive (Sauder, Lynn, & Podolny, 2012), audiences also expect high-status actors to represent the highest values (Pollock, Lashley, Rindova, & Han, 2019). Violating these expectations thus makes high-status actors’ misconduct seem more deviant than lower-status actors’ misconduct—and therefore more newsworthy (Graffin et al., 2013)—giving birth to scandals, or “publicized transgressions that run counter to established norms” (Piazza & Jourdan, 2018: 165).

Aside from the transgressors’ status, however, scandalization’s antecedents are poorly understood as they have received remarkably little research attention (for exceptions, see Nyhan, 2014, 2017). Rather, most research on the scandalizing effect of status has asserted that an event is a scandal (e.g., Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013; Jonsson, Greve, & Fujiwara-Greve, 2009; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018) and then looked at how differences in status influence the way the scandal affects different actors. This is problematic because scandals originate from misconduct that occurs in private, and that may be tolerated if it remains private (Adut, 2005). Indeed, Adut (2008: 19) defined scandalizable misconduct as “anything that will bring about shame or that will embarrass or provoke when made public.” Misconduct is scandalized only if it is widely publicized by a mediating institution, such as the press (Thompson, 2000). Thus, while the misconduct’s nature is a critical component of scandalization, we know less about why the media publicizes, and thus scandalizes, some instances of misconduct but not others.

This salience-enhancing effect, however, intensifies the scandalizing impact of a high-status firm’s misconduct when both the current and previous transgressors operate within the same industry. Status hierarchies are typically bounded by horizontal categories like industries, meaning a firm’s market identity emerges from the intersection of its vertical status ranking and its horizontal industry affiliation (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Han & Pollock, 2021; Jensen, Kim, & Kim, 2011; Rao et al., 2005). High-status firms are often viewed as standard-bearers of industry norms and practices (Han & Pollock, 2021; Pollock et al., 2019). Consequently, when multiple high-status firms within the same industry are implicated in misconduct, the perceived pattern captures journalists’ attention, prompting scrutiny not just of the firm but of the broader industry. This framing suggests possible systemic issues (Han et al., 2023; Paruchuri & Misangyi, 2015), enhancing the newsworthiness of current transgressions by embedding them within a wider narrative (Bednar, Boivie, & Prince, 2013; Desai, 2011). For example, journalists are able to construct more compelling stories when they define a high-status firm as a leading software company, leveraging not only its prestige but also its role within the software industry, as opposed to portraying it as merely a high-status organization in general.

By contrast, when misconduct is prevalent outside the transgressor’s industry, journalists are less inclined to spotlight the current wrongdoing—regardless of the firm’s status—since it lacks alignment with a coherent, industry-based narrative. For instance, prior data breaches involving high-status hospitality firms like Hilton and Hyatt can elevate the news value of a similar breach at Marriott (Kelleher, 2019), reinforcing an industry-level trend. However, the same pattern would not increase the perceived relevance of a breach at Walmart and might even diminish its newsworthiness, particularly if journalists define the narrative in terms of a hospitality-sector problem.
text:
This salience-enhancing effect, however, amplifies the scandalizing effect of the focal firm’s high status when the past and current transgressors operate in the same industry. Status hierarchies are often bounded by horizontal categories, such as industries; thus, a firm’s market identity is defined at the intersection of its vertical status position and horizontal industry membership (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Han & Pollock, 2021; Jensen, Kim, & Kim, 2011; Rao et al., 2005). Because high-status firms are considered exemplars of their industry’s norms and practices (Han & Pollock, 2021; Pollock et al., 2019), the perceived commonalities among the past and current high-status transgressors within an industry draw journalists’ attention to the industry and its potentially systemic wrongdoing (Han et al., 2023; Paruchuri & Misangyi, 2015), placing the current misconduct within a larger, newsworthy context (Bednar, Boivie, & Prince, 2013; Desai, 2011). For instance, journalists can develop richer storylines when they perceive a firm’s market identity as a high-status software company by tapping into the firm’s stature in the software industry, compared to viewing the firm as just a high-status company.

When there is a high level of misconduct outside the transgressor firm’s industry, however, journalists are more likely to ignore the current misconduct, even by a high-status firm, as it does not fit a simple and compelling industry-based narrative. Prior data breaches involving Hilton and Hyatt may cause a data breach by Marriott to seem more newsworthy (Kelleher, 2019), but they would not make a data breach by Walmart more newsworthy, and could even reduce the likelihood it would be covered if journalists were to perceive a hospitality industry-based pattern.

use: Our study offers several meaningful contributions to the literature. From an empirical standpoint, it represents one of the few genuinely longitudinal investigations in organizational science that examines trait narcissism across multiple time points, thereby advancing our understanding of how this personality trait evolves throughout individuals’ work lives. In doing so, our research complements and extends previous work on narcissism development in notable ways. First, it focuses on a clearly defined and highly consequential period: the initial six years of individuals’ professional careers following their transition from college to the workforce. This early period of career establishment has been consistently recognized as a critical window for adult personality development, as individuals must decide which roles to inhabit and consciously grow their involvement in selected roles (e.g., Golle et al., 2019; Reitz et al., 2020; Wille et al., 2012). Additionally, unlike Wille et al. (2019), who assessed changes in trait narcissism across three distant time points spanning 22 years, our study adopts a more fine-grained approach by incorporating four measurement waves over a six-year span. Given the potentially unstable and dynamic nature of occupational settings during this formative career stage (e.g., Biemann et al., 2012), it is crucial to examine changes within these environments in detail while avoiding the limitations associated with retrospective reporting (e.g., Wetzel et al., 2020). By distributing assessment points evenly across this six-year timeframe, our design offers a robust basis for capturing changes in both work environments and personality traits (Ployhart & Vandenberg, 2010).
text: Our study adds to the literature in several important ways. In terms of empirical contributions, this is one of the very few true longitudinal studies in the organizational sciences that investigates trait narcissism at multiple time points, thus documenting the developmental properties of this personality trait during people's work lives. Our study complements earlier work on narcissism development and extends it in several ways. First, the present study documents the development of narcissism during a clearly delineated and particularly relevant time period, namely the first 6 years of the professional career following the college-to-work transition. The first years of early career establishment have been proven to be of critical importance to adult personality development because, in this particular time frame, people need to select and selectively expand the number and types of roles that they hold (e.g., Golle et al., 2019; Reitz et al., 2020; Wille et al., 2012). In addition, and in contrast to the work by Wille et al. (2019), which examined changes in trait narcissism between three time points across a 22-year interval, the present study takes a more fine-grained approach by considering four measurement points across 6 years. Given the potentially volatile nature of people's occupational environments during this stage of the career (e.g., Biemann et al., 2012), it is important to track changes in these occupational characteristics in sufficient detail while also avoiding flawed retrospective assessments of these occupations (e.g., Wetzel et al., 2020). Four assessment points distributed equally over the course of the 6-year period provide sufficient information to capture the presumed changes in both work environments and personality (Ployhart & Vandenberg, 2010).

use: To this end, we focus on the intra-individual experience of conflict to offer a conceptual account of why the structural characteristics of such experiences influence the relationship between conflict and task performance. We draw on theories of cognitive resource allocation—specifically, the self-regulatory process of attentional focus (Beal, Weiss, Barros, & MacDermid, 2005). Within conflict situations, individuals act as deliberative agents, deciding whether to redirect their attention from the task at hand to the issues associated with the conflict. Prior research has consistently shown that cognitive task engagement fluctuates over time and is closely tied to how individuals manage competing attentional demands (Hirst & Kalmar, 1987; Kahneman, 1973). Conflict introduces off-task attentional demands via its content, forcing individuals to engage in a self-regulatory process where they determine how to allocate their attention. We propose that the structural features of the conflict experience significantly shape this allocation process. When individuals initiate (i.e., send) conflict, they are generally attempting to sustain or regain attentional focus on the primary task—an “on-task attentional pull.” Conversely, when individuals are the recipients of conflict, they often face increased attentional disruption due to the off-task nature of processing the conflict issues—what we refer to as “off-task demands.” Here, the structural boundaries of the conflict (e.g., within-team vs. between-team) function as social cues. Conflict senders are more likely to preserve their task focus when engaging with team members (within-team) as opposed to members outside their team (between-team). In contrast, conflict receivers perceive greater off-task demands when the conflict originates from within their team, as opposed to outside it.
text: To this end, we zoom in on the intra-individual conflict experiences to provide a conceptual explanation for why these structural elements of individual conflict experiences alter the conflict experience–task performance relationship. To do so, we incorporate theory regarding cognitive resource allocation; in particular, the self-regulatory process of attentional focus (Beal, Weiss, Barros, & MacDermid, 2005). In conflict experiences, individual members are volitional agents who regulate whether to redirect their attentional focus to the conflict issues from their ongoing task at hand. It is well established in the literature that the extent to which a person cognitively engages in tasks is variant across time, and that cognitive task engagement is closely related to how individuals allocate their attentional focus between competing attentional demands (Hirst & Kalmar, 1987; Kahneman, 1973). When experiencing conflict, the content of the conflict—or conflict issue—creates off-task attentional demands, thus requiring individuals to decide how they will allocate their attentional focus. In this self-regulatory process, we propose that the structural elements of individual conflict experiences play a critical role. By sending conflict, individuals attempt to maximize their attentional focus on the task. In contrast, when receiving conflict, individuals try to minimize the off-task attentional demands. In this process, the boundaries of a conflict experience serve as a social cue to individuals. Senders feel better able to maintain their attentional focus on the task (i.e., “on-task attentional pull”) when they express the conflict issues to a member of the same team rather than a different team. Alternatively, receivers feel higher demands of conflict issues (i.e., “off-task demands”) when they receive the conflict within the team boundaries as opposed to between the team boundaries.

We understand coherence across categories as a collectively constructed answer to the question, “What kind of thing is it not?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). By inter-category coherence, we refer to the degree of similarity between a focal category and its related categories, which influences its perceived distinctiveness—either making it stand out more or blend in (Lo et al., 2020; Taeuscher, Zhao, & Lounsbury, 2022). While some cultural elements are unique to a focal category, others are shared among categories that are culturally adjacent. In the realm of television, for example, comedies and documentaries are typically distinct, each with its own defining conventions. However, hybrid examples blur these lines: some documentaries adopt humor to portray reality (e.g., Tiger King), while some comedies mimic the documentary format to frame fictional narratives (e.g., The Office). In such cases, a focal category becomes less distinctive when its participants draw on cultural elements that are also used in neighboring categories, and more distinctive when they rely on resources largely exclusive to their own category.

Table 1 offers a visual schema that maps the relationship between category heterogeneity and distinctiveness. Although these are conceptually different—heterogeneity relates to variation within a category, while distinctiveness concerns differentiation between categories—they are empirically intertwined. A shift in internal heterogeneity can influence external distinctiveness, though not always in a predictable way. For example, when a focal category becomes more internally varied, it may reduce distinctiveness by incorporating cultural resources common in related categories. Alternatively, it may enhance distinctiveness by integrating novel elements not found elsewhere. Likewise, changes in related categories can impact how distinctive the focal category appears, regardless of whether anything changes within it.
text:
We view coherence across categories as a collectively held answer to the question “What kind of thing is it not?” (Glynn & Navis, 2013). By inter-category coherence, we refer to the similarity between a focal category and its related categories, making it either more or less distinctive (Lo et al., 2020; Taeuscher, Zhao, & Lounsbury, 2022).1 While some cultural resources are unique to a focal category, others are shared across related categories. In television, for instance, comedies and documentaries are distinct categories with unique defining features. Yet, some documentaries depict reality with a humorous tone (e.g., Tiger King), and some comedies present fictional events as a documentary (e.g., The Office). Thus, a focal category is less distinctive when actors mobilize cultural resources that other actors in related categories mobilize too, and is more distinctive when actors mobilize cultural resources that mainly belong in their focal category but not in others.

Table 1 provides a schematic representation of category heterogeneity and distinctiveness, and their relations. The two notions are conceptually distinct; heterogeneity pertains to intra-category relations, and distinctiveness to inter-category relations. Yet, both are empirically related. While a shift in heterogeneity can lead to a shift in distinctiveness, they do not necessarily change in the same direction. For instance, when a focal category welcomes a broader range of variations, it may become less distinctive if actors borrow cultural resources that are commonly shared in other related categories, or more distinctive if actors enrich their own categories with cultural resources that are not shared with related categories. Similarly, what occurs in related categories may alter the distinctiveness of a focal category, regardless of what actors do within it.

use: This category of interventions primarily targets the cognitive aspects of attitude formation and seeks to disrupt the typical processing of stereotypes. These interventions work by redirecting attention away from stereotypical cues or by reshaping the choice architecture—that is, altering the way options are presented to decision-makers. In doing so, they influence either how target-related information is perceived or where the evaluator’s attention is directed during evaluation. For instance, Reynolds et al. (2021) prompted participants to adopt a calculative mindset, while Uhlmann and Cohen (2005) required participants to pre-commit to specific evaluation criteria. Singletary and Hebl (2009) provided individuating information to encourage more personalized assessments, and Feng et al. (2020) structured the decision environment by introducing partition dependence in candidate presentation. Within this broader intervention type, there is a distinct subcategory aimed at entirely preventing the activation of stereotypes. These approaches are designed to block stereotypical associations from being triggered in the first place, thereby removing them from the evaluative process altogether. A well-known example is the study by Goldin and Rouse (2000), who examined the impact of anonymized auditions in orchestra hiring, using screens to conceal candidates’ identities. Such anonymization represents a form of choice architecture intervention that eliminates demographic cues, compelling evaluators to base judgments solely on merit-related or human capital criteria. Collectively, these interventions function by directing attention toward non-stereotypical signals or redesigning the evaluative context to dilute or override automatic stereotype processing. Importantly, these strategies do not aim to change the underlying stereotypes or schemas themselves; rather, they seek to disrupt their use in the decision-making process.
text: This class of interventions mainly operates on the cognitive dimension of attitudes and is designed to interfere with the typical processing of stereotypes. This can be accomplished by shifting attentional focus away from stereotypical information or by managing choice architecture (i.e., how alternatives are presented to a decision-maker). As a result, these interventions function by changing how information about a target is perceived or by influencing to which information the evaluator directs their attention. For example, Reynolds et al. (2021) induced participants to adopt a calculative mindset, while Uhlmann and Cohen (2005) asked participants to commit to important criteria prior to evaluations. Singletary and Hebl (2009) provided individuating information about targets, while Feng et al. (2020) manipulated the decision environment by creating partition dependence in the presentation of candidates. A subcategory of this class of interventions aims to prevent stereotype activation completely. In this approach, the strategy employed precludes stereotypes from being triggered altogether during target evaluation, so they do not factor into the decision-making process. A prominent example of this intervention is Goldin and Rouse's (2000) study examining orchestra hiring over time, which concealed candidate identities with a screen during auditions. The anonymization of candidate information is a type of choice architecture intervention aimed at eliminating the influence of demographic information in the evaluation process, forcing the decision-maker to concentrate their appraisal solely on human capital factors instead. In their varied ways, this class of interventions diverts focus to other cues or re-arranges the decision environment to compete with or overwhelm stereotype processing. Thus, in this category, the mental schemas or stereotypes about the target are largely unaffected; rather, the objective is to interfere with their regular processing.

use: In examining the mechanisms behind the recategorization process, Gaertner and colleagues (Gaertner et al., 1989; Dovidio et al., 1997; Gaertner & Dovidio, 2000) observed that categorizing others as out-group members based on noticeable attribute differences tends to occur automatically and instantaneously—an idea supported by extensive empirical evidence (see reviews by Gawronski & Payne, 2010; Mendoza, Gollwitzer, & Amodio, 2010). For example, neuroscience-based studies have demonstrated that individuals form initial impressions based on salient demographic cues within milliseconds (Ito & Urland, 2003; Kunda & Spencer, 2003). Further, social categorization research has shown that when a group evaluates an individual, features that set the person apart are especially likely to guide initial categorization (see Bodenhausen, Kang, & Peery, 2012). This is because the salience of any given attribute often stems from how much it distinguishes the individual from the rest of the group (Biernat & Vescio, 1994; Nelson & Miller, 1995; Fiske & Taylor, 2008). As a result, group members tend to automatically classify someone as an out-group member when they differ on a salient demographic line—even if they share similarities across other key dimensions. For instance, a group of men may instantly categorize a woman as belonging to an out-group, given that gender serves as a highly salient and visually obvious differentiating factor. According to recategorization theory, however, these initial, automatic categorizations can be revised if individuals actively engage with additional information and recognize similarities that connect the target person with the in-group (see Pettigrew, 1998; Gaertner & Dovidio, 2000).
text: In explaining the mechanisms underlying the recategorization process, Gaertner and colleagues (Gaertner et al., 1989; Dovidio et al., 1997; Gaertner and Dovidio, 2000) noted that out-group categorization based on differences in salient attributes tends to be automatic and instant, and there is considerable evidence to support that idea (see recent reviews by Gawronski and Payne, 2010; Mendoza, Gollwitzer, and Amodio, 2010). For instance, research based on neuroscience methods has found that people form initial impressions of others based on salient demographic attributes in a fraction of a second (Ito and Urland, 2003; Kunda and Spencer, 2003). In addition, social categorization research has shown that when a group of individuals evaluates a person, the person's dissimilar attributes are especially likely to become salient bases of their initial categorization of the individual (see review by Bodenhausen, Kang, and Peery, 2012) because the salience of a given attribute is often determined by the degree to which it makes the target person distinct from existing members of the group (Biernat and Vescio, 1994; Nelson and Miller, 1995; Fiske and Taylor, 2008). Thus existing group members tend to instantly categorize a person as an out-group member when he or she is different along a salient demographic dimension even though this person may be similar to them along other major demographic dimensions. For instance, a group of men tends to automatically categorize a woman as an out-group member because gender automatically makes the woman appear different from them. Recategorization theory posits that people can subsequently change their cognitive representations of the person's category membership, however, if they consciously process additional information about the individual's similarities to them (see reviews by Pettigrew, 1998; Gaertner and Dovidio, 2000).

We investigated these dynamics by examining data breaches involving publicly traded U.S. firms between 2015 and 2018. Data breaches—instances involving the unauthorized disclosure of customers’ private information—not only threaten consumer trust in firms (Culnan & Armstrong, 1999; Martin, Borah, & Palmatier, 2017) but also represent a growing societal concern (Accenture, 2019) that ranks among top priorities for executives (Petersen, 2020). Our findings indicate that the status and industry affiliation of prior transgressors shape the conditions under which high-status firms are more or less likely to be scandalized. Specifically, the likelihood that a high-status focal firm would be scandalized increased when past breaches involved high-status insiders, but declined when similar breaches involved high-status outsiders.

This study contributes to the literature on status and organizational misconduct by emphasizing the contextual features that shape how audiences respond to a focal firm’s wrongdoing (Johns, 2006). In particular, we identify the status of prior transgressors as a key boundary condition in the well-documented relationship between organizational status and scandalization (Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin et al., 2013). Johns (2006, 2017) has long argued that contextual neglect can obscure causal mechanisms and lead to variable findings across research. Echoing this, Graf-Vlachy and colleagues (2020: 57) have called for more studies that bring "a cross-context perspective that explicitly contrasts the antecedents of media coverage in different contexts." Our work addresses this gap by showing that both the status and categorical proximity of prior transgressors are consequential yet underexplored factors that influence whether a focal firm’s misconduct attracts public scandal—thereby shedding light on the conditions under which there is either safety or scrutiny in numbers (Desai, 2011; Naumovska et al., 2021; Rindova et al., 2010). Furthermore, our theory and findings advance understanding of how evaluators interpret combinations of status and categorical cues (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Han & Pollock, 2021; Jensen et al., 2011).
text:
We explored these dynamics using data breaches during 2015–2018 involving publicly traded U.S. firms. Data breaches, which result in the unauthorized exposure of customers’ private information, can damage customers’ trust in firms (Culnan & Armstrong, 1999; Martin, Borah, & Palmatier, 2017) and are a serious social issue (Accenture, 2019), topping executives’ concerns (Petersen, 2020). Our findings suggest that past transgressors’ status and industry membership create important boundary conditions for status’s effect on scandalization. Specifically, the positive effect of a focal transgressor’s high status on the likelihood of scandalization was amplified by high-status insiders’ past transgressions, but was weakened by high-status outsiders’ past transgressions.

We contribute to the literatures on status and organizational misconduct by highlighting the importance of the context surrounding a focal firm’s transgression (Johns, 2006), identifying past transgressors’ status as a key boundary condition for the well-established effect of status on scandalization (Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin et al., 2013). Johns (2006, 2017) has argued that most theories give contextual factors too little consideration, which can lead to misunderstanding the mechanisms at work and inconsistent results across studies. Graf-Vlachy and colleagues (2020: 57) also called for more research taking “a cross-context perspective that explicitly contrasts the antecedents of media coverage in different contexts.” We address these limitations by suggesting that past transgressors’ status and categorical proximity to the focal transgressor are important yet overlooked contextual factors that influence whether the transgressor’s misconduct is scandalized, explaining when there is safety or scrutiny in numbers (Desai, 2011; Naumovska et al., 2021; Rindova et al., 2010). Further, our theory and findings offer new insights into how audiences combine status and categories in their evaluations (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Han & Pollock, 2021; Jensen et al., 2011).

Deonance theory’s central prediction is that witnessing mistreatment elicits anger directed at the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). Anger, as a negative emotion, arises in response to a perceived unjust offense against oneself or others (Haidt, 2003). It is considered a moral emotion—one that reflects concern for the rights, welfare, and dignity of others (Haidt, 2003). Within the framework of deonance theory, mistreatment constitutes a violation of moral standards, prompting an elevated, emotionally charged reaction known as the state of deonance (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).

In this state, the hallmark deontic response is anger toward the wrongdoer (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). This reaction is thought to be evolutionarily advantageous, as anger can motivate behaviors that restore or defend the moral order. As such, the experience of anger in response to moral transgressions is described as automatic, fast-acting, and non-rational (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Empirical studies grounded in deonance theory support this portrayal by demonstrating that third-party observers of mistreatment commonly experience anger toward the perpetrator (e.g., Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Spencer & Rupp, 2009; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). For instance, Spencer and Rupp (2009) found that third-party observers felt anger toward customers who treated coworkers unfairly. Based on this logic and prior findings, we proposed the following:

Hypothesis 1: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively associated with anger directed at the perpetrator.
text:
Deonance theory’s signature prediction is that observing mistreatment should result in feelings of anger toward the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). Anger is a negative emotion that is experienced in response to an unjustified offense against oneself or others (Haidt, 2003). It is classified as a moral emotion—as a feeling experienced in response to the interests and welfare of others (Haidt, 2003). According to the logic of deonance theory, mistreatment represents a violation of moral norms, triggering the heightened and emotionally charged arousal that characterizes the state of deonance (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).

In the theory’s depiction of the deontic response, the state of deonance manifests as feelings of anger toward the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). A tendency to respond to moral violations with anger is evolutionarily adaptive because anger can trigger behaviors that reaffirm the moral order. For this reason, the anger response is described as automatic, rapid, and irrational (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Studies drawing on deonance theory have supported that depiction by showing that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are associated with anger toward the perpetrator (e.g., Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Spencer & Rupp, 2009; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). For example, Spencer and Rupp (2009) showed that third parties who observed coworkers being treated unfairly by customers felt anger toward those customers. We therefore predicted:
Hypothesis 1: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively related to anger toward the perpetrator.

The AMO (Ability–Motivation–Opportunity) model of Human Resource Management (HRM) is grounded in several core assumptions. First, it posits that individual HR practices are designed with specific aims that correspond directly to one of the three AMO components (Appelbaum et al., 2000; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012). Accordingly, each practice is primarily intended to enhance a particular domain: ability (e.g., training programs), motivation (e.g. incentive systems), or opportunity (e.g., employee empowerment initiatives). Second, the model underscores the potential for synergistic interactions among HR practices across these domains (Gerhart, 2007). This is rooted in the original AMO-based performance theory (Blumberg & Pringle, 1982), which conceptualized performance as a multiplicative function of A × M × O. When applied to an HRM context, this suggests that the combined effect of practices across all three domains can significantly amplify performance outcomes. Third, the AMO framework emphasizes the "system effects" of HR practices—the idea that configurations of practices addressing all three dimensions collectively exert a greater influence on performance than any single practice alone (Appelbaum et al., 2000, p. 142).

Empirical research grounded in the AMO model has advanced along two primary trajectories. The first examines which HR practices best represent each AMO dimension (Jiang, Lepak, Han, et al., 2012). In this line of work, scholars frequently operationalize each domain by calculating composite scores from relevant HR practices (Boon et al., 2019). These aggregate scores are then used to explore the relationships between AMO dimensions and the broader effects of High-Performance Work Systems (HPWS) on organizational outcomes (Gardner et al., 2011; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012). A series of meta-analyses has demonstrated that bundled AMO practices (Subramony, 2009) and HPWS configurations (Combs et al., 2006) are more strongly associated with enhanced firm performance than isolated practices, supporting the notion of HR system effects.
text:
The AMO model of HRM is based on several key assumptions. First, it suggests that HR practices have distinct goals closely aligned with one of the AMO domains (Appelbaum et al., 2000; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012). Thus, each practice is conceptualized primarily to address one of the three domains: ability (e.g., training), motivation (e.g., rewards), and opportunity (e.g., empowerment). Second, the AMO model emphasizes the potential synergistic effects of HR practices across AMO domains (Gerhart, 2007). This is because the original AMO-based theory of performance (Blumberg & Pringle, 1982) suggests that performance is a multiplicative function of ability, motivation, and opportunity (i.e., performance = f [A × M × O]), which was later extended to the HRM context. Third, the AMO model highlights the system effects of HR practices. Given the synergistic nature of HR practices, a set of practices that target all aspects of AMO is believed to “have a stronger effect on performance than do the individual practices” (Appelbaum et al., 2000, p. 142).

Empirical research on the AMO model has progressed in two ways. The first body of work used the model to identify HR practices that represent each AMO domain (Jiang, Lepak, Han, et al., 2012). In doing so, scholars typically operationalized the AMO domains by averaging scores of the individual HR practices that reflect each domain (Boon et al., 2019). The combined scores were then used to investigate how AMO domains and HPWS influence various workplace outcomes (Gardner et al., 2011; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012). The results of several meta-analyses showed that AMO bundles (Subramony, 2009) and HPWS (Combs et al., 2006) have more positive effects on firm performance compared to individual practices, suggesting potential HR system effects.
