Our work makes several key contributions. First, we advance emerging research on leniency and punishment by deepening understanding of the sensemaking processes that guide observer reactions. Prior studies have shown that moral evaluations of both leaders (e.g., Dang et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2018) and observers themselves (e.g., Zhu et al., 2021) shape responses to leniency. However, scholars have noted that the social construction of misconduct and leniency is nuanced and multifaceted (Bies et al., 2021; D. A. Jones & Skarlicki, 2012). While moral regard for leaders and the self plays a significant role, we highlight that moral regard for the offender—particularly in terms of perceived personal need and distress—can also drive observer reactions (Brubacher, 2019). To capture this complexity, we draw from scholarship on punishment, compassion, and moral virtue trade-offs to explain how observers’ perceptions of an offender’s suffering shape their response to leniency.

Second, we contribute to the compassion literature by addressing calls for a clearer understanding of its antecedents and consequences (Dutton et al., 2014). We offer a theory of how divergent perceptions of offender need and distress can activate (or inhibit) a compassion process that mediates observer reactions to leniency. Our analysis shows that some perceptions of offender personal need—particularly those aligned with broader humanitarian values—can cultivate compassionate responses that mitigate perceived unfairness. In contrast, other forms of personal need or distress may fall short of eliciting compassion. Incorporating the concept of moral virtue trade-offs, we further contend that compassion can function as a moral judgment that helps reconcile tensions between leniency and justice.

Third, we broaden the literature on observer responses to moral violations by leaders, which has largely emphasized the negative consequences of leader mistreatment toward employees (e.g., Skarlicki & Kulik, 2005). We shift this discourse by focusing on leniency—an act that may benefit an offender—as a potential moral breach from the observer’s perspective. Our work reveals how leniency can trigger perceptions of injustice, and we show that compassion for the offender offers a pathway for observers to reframe this perceived violation in more morally acceptable terms.
text:
Our work offers several contributions. First, we extend emerging research on leniency and punishment related to observers’ sensemaking processes. Research has explained how moral evaluations of the leader (e.g., Dang et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2018) and of observers themselves (e.g., Zhu et al., 2021) influence reactions to leniency. Yet, scholars have highlighted that the social construction of misconduct and leniency is complex (Bies et al., 2021; D. A. Jones & Skarlicki, 2012). Although moral regard for leaders and the self is motivating, moral regard for the offender may also influence reactions (Brubacher, 2019). We integrate principles from punishment, compassion, and moral virtue trade-offs to explain how moral regard for offenders (viz., offenders’ personal need and distress) influences observer reactions to leniency.

Second, we extend the compassion literature by addressing calls to identify antecedents and consequences of compassion (Dutton et al., 2014). We explicate how differing perceptions about offenders’ need and distress elicit (or do not) a compassion process that influences observer reactions to leniency. We also build theory to explain how certain perceptions of offender personal need align with humanitarianism to elicit compassion needed to allay the unfairness of leniency, whereas other types of needs and distress limit compassion reactions. By incorporating principles of moral virtue trade-offs, we also extend compassion theory to explain how compassion represents a moral appraisal that counters justice violations.

Third, we extend the literature on observer reactions to leader moral violations, which has traditionally focused on how leader mistreatment of employees provokes negative observer reactions (see Skarlicki & Kulik, 2005). Our work shifts the theoretical conversation toward understanding how leniency—beneficial behavior for an offender—can also represent a moral violation by the leader, which raises unfairness perceptions that can be attenuated by compassion.

Organizations are increasingly acknowledging the value of workplace neurodiversity—a term that captures the broad range of neurological and cognitive variations among individuals (LeFevre-Levy et al., 2023). An estimated 15% to 20% of the population is neurodivergent, encompassing conditions such as attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism, and dyslexia (Doyle, 2020). Despite representing a substantial segment of the workforce, neurodivergent individuals remain significantly underrepresented in organizational research. Their inclusion brings numerous benefits, both to the individuals and the organizations that employ them (LeFevre-Levy et al., 2023; Silver et al., 2023). However, many current selection tools—particularly self-report personality assessments designed for neurotypical populations—may inadvertently disadvantage neurodivergent candidates. Such tools may emphasize irrelevant traits or fail to accurately capture the strengths and potential of these applicants (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). In this study, we explore these concerns by examining average group differences and divergent interpretations of conscientiousness assessments between neurotypical individuals and those with ADHD—a group that constitutes a notable portion of the U.S. adult population (at least 4%; Abdelnour et al., 2022; London & Landes, 2021), yet faces persistent stigma (Bisset et al., 2022; Mueller et al., 2012) and is frequently overlooked in employment contexts.

Our focus on conscientiousness is driven by its widespread use in employee selection. According to one survey, 22% of human resource professionals report using personality assessments—including measures of conscientiousness—as part of their hiring practices (Meinert, 2015). Conscientiousness is one of the five dimensions in the widely adopted five-factor model of personality, which also includes openness, extraversion, agreeableness, and neuroticism (Barrick & Mount, 1991; Mount & Barrick, 1998). Conscientiousness, commonly defined by traits such as being careful, diligent, responsible, organized, and planful (Barrick & Mount, 1991, p. 4), is especially relevant in selection due to its strong, consistent positive relationship with job performance across a wide variety of roles (Barrick & Mount, 1991; Mount & Barrick, 1998).
text:
Organizations are increasingly recognizing the importance of workplace neurodiversity, a term used to describe the broad spectrum of neurological and cognitive functioning across people (LeFevre-Levy et al., 2023). Roughly 15%−20% of the population has some form of neurodivergence, such as attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism, or dyslexia (Doyle, 2020), representing a significant yet understudied group in organizational research. Including neurodivergent people in the workplace has myriad benefits both for employees themselves and organizations (LeFevre-Levy et al., 2023; Silver et al., 2023). However, selection tools that were designed for neurotypical people, such as self-report personality measures, may weigh against selecting neurodiverse respondents if they capture traits that are job-irrelevant or are poorly suited to capture the talents and potential of neurodivergent job applicants (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). We address these concerns by examining group mean differences on and different interpretations of conscientiousness measures among those without and with ADHD, who represent a sizable (i.e., at least 4% of U.S. adults; Abdelnour et al., 2022; London & Landes, 2021) negatively stigmatized (Bisset et al., 2022; Mueller et al., 2012) and often overlooked group of workers.

We focus on conscientiousness assessments because they are a mainstay of employee selection procedures; one survey found that 22% of human resource managers use personality assessments, including conscientiousness assessments, as part of their hiring processes (Meinert, 2015). Conscientiousness is one trait represented by the five-factor model of personality, which measures the traits openness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism, and conscientiousness (Barrick & Mount, 1991; Mount & Barrick, 1998). Conscientiousness, defined by “being careful, thorough, responsible, organized, and planful” (Barrick & Mount, 1991, p. 4), is a particularly important predictor in selection given its positive association with job performance across many occupations (Barrick & Mount, 1991; Mount & Barrick, 1998).

Scholars have long been intrigued by the dynamics of categorization. Some have explored why certain emerging categories gain traction while others fail to do so (Durand & Khaire, 2017; Navis & Glynn, 2010). Others have examined what allows established categories to persist, or conversely, what causes them to fade from relevance (Kuilman & van Driel, 2013; Lockwood et al., 2023; Lounsbury & Rao, 2004). Still others have investigated how categories undergo cycles of entry and exit, or shifts in meaning and relevance over time (Cattani et al., 2008; Grodal, 2018; Pontikes & Barnett, 2015; Wry et al., 2011). Regardless of whether the focus is on the birth, decline, or transformation of categories, this body of research shares a core concern with understanding what makes categories viable.

Building on Lo et al. (2020), we define category viability as the degree to which actors and their audiences actively and collectively engage with a category to position and evaluate products. Put differently, a category’s viability lies in the willingness of these groups to associate its label with a set of offerings (Granqvist et al., 2012; Pontikes & Barnett, 2015). Yet categories are more than just labels. As social classification systems, they serve as rich repositories of cultural resources—comprising identities, vocabularies, practices, and symbolic meanings (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Soublière & Lockwood, 2022). For actors, categories are valuable because they offer tools to frame and distinguish their innovations (Lounsbury & Glynn, 2001, 2019; Zhao et al., 2017). For audiences, categories shape perception, focus attention, and structure evaluation (Navis & Glynn, 2011; Pontikes, 2012; Zuckerman, 1999). Ultimately, a category becomes and remains viable through consistent and meaningful use. But how does this viability evolve over time?
text:
Scholars have long been interested in category dynamics. Some scholars have focused on understanding why some nascent categories take off while others do not (Durand & Khaire, 2017; Navis & Glynn, 2010). Others have investigated why some established categories endure while others fall out of use (Kuilman & van Driel, 2013; Lockwood et al., 2023; Lounsbury & Rao, 2004). Still others have examined why categories experience waves of entries and exits, or shifts in usage over time (Cattani, Ferriani, Negro, & Perretti, 2008; Grodal, 2018; Pontikes & Barnett, 2015; Wry et al., 2011). Whether scholars focus on emergence, demise, or fluctuations in between, research on category dynamics is united by a common interest in understanding what makes categories viable.

Drawing on Lo and colleagues (2020), we define the viability of a category as the extent to which actors and their audiences collectively use a category to position and assess products. Put differently, a category’s viability is reflected in actors’ and audiences’ willingness to associate its label with certain products (Granqvist et al., 2012; Pontikes & Barnett, 2015). However, categories are more than labels. As a meaningful system of classification, categories additionally provide a rich reservoir of cultural resources—that is, identities, vocabularies, practices, and other symbolic elements (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Soublière & Lockwood, 2022). Thus, for actors, categories are useful because they furnish the means to situate and differentiate their new products (Lounsbury & Glynn, 2001, 2019; Zhao et al., 2017). For audiences, too, categories usefully guide their attention, and shape how they understand and assess new products (Navis & Glynn, 2011; Pontikes, 2012; Zuckerman, 1999). Simply put, a category is and remains viable when it is repeatedly and actively used. But how does a category’s viability change over time?

Silence is a widespread phenomenon in organizational life, yet it remains an elusive and multidimensional concept that has evolved over time and across disciplines (Morrison, 2014). In this study, we define silence on social injustices as the deliberate withholding of ideas, opinions, or concerns related to the fair and equitable treatment of social groups. This includes instances where managers choose not to speak out about injustices or unfair treatment occurring either within the boundaries of their organization or beyond (Pinder & Harlos, 2001; Van Dyne et al., 2003).

Building on prior empirical work, we conceptualize silence—the intentional withholding of input—not simply as the opposite of speaking up, which involves the purposeful expression of ideas (Sherf et al., 2021; Van Dyne et al., 2003). Importantly, unlike the mere absence of speech, silence is an active decision: individuals have something to say and are presented with a clear opportunity to say it, yet they consciously opt not to. Natural pauses in conversation or instances when individuals lack something to contribute do not constitute silence, as intentionality is absent. Similarly, individuals who are unaware of an issue cannot be expected to speak up about it, and thus their inaction does not qualify as silence. Nor does silence apply to situations where individuals have no opportunity or enabling context in which to express themselves. Our focus is on how bystanders or observers perceive silence—specifically, we examine circumstances in which observers are likely to believe that someone, such as a manager, is both aware of a social injustice and positioned to speak out but intentionally chooses to remain silent. In our experimental studies, we make this awareness and capability clear to ensure that the silence in question is perceived as deliberate by observers.
text:
Silence is pervasive in organizations, yet the concept is still quite elusive and varied across time and fields (Morrison, 2014). We define silence on social injustices as people’s withholding of ideas, opinions, or concerns related to the equitable and fair treatment of social groups. This can include situations in which managers refrain from speaking up about unfair treatment or injustices that occur within or outside the confines of their organization (Pinder & Harlos, 2001; Van Dyne et al., 2003).

Further, consistent with empirical research, we conceive of silence (the intentional withholding of ideas) as not merely the converse of speaking up (the intentional expression of ideas; Sherf et al., 2021; Van Dyne et al., 2003). Indeed, whereas not speaking up is a lack of a behavior, silence is an active behavior whereby individuals have something to say and an opportunity to speak up, but intentionally choose to remain quiet. Simply not having something to say or pausing naturally in conversations does not constitute silence, because the individual lacks intentionality. Moreover, being unaware of an issue and thus not speaking up does not equate to silence, as the absence of awareness renders one unable to contribute to the discussion in the first place. Additionally, if someone is not in a context that enables them to speak up, their lack of speaking up does not constitute silence. Given that we are interested in how observers (those who witness silence) perceive silence, we focus on situations in which observers likely presume someone has an opportunity to say something but chooses to be silent. That is, in our experimental studies, we make clear to observers that a manager is at least aware of an injustice and is able to speak up but has remained silent.

Li et al. (2019) further theorized that the behavioral expressions of schadenfreude would be directed toward the victim of the mistreatment. When initial feelings of schadenfreude are tempered by secondary appraisals, individuals may exhibit passive forms of antisocial behavior, such as avoidance or social exclusion. Conversely, when those initial feelings are intensified by secondary appraisals, individuals may engage in more active forms of antisocial behavior, including gossip or undermining. In both scenarios, these behaviors function to reinforce the potential gains in status or outcomes that elicited the pleasurable reaction in the first place (Li et al., 2019). Supporting this perspective, Xu et al. (2020) found that experiencing schadenfreude after witnessing coworkers suffer abusive supervision led to increased tendencies to undermine or act deviantly toward those coworkers.

Although Li et al. (2019) did not explicitly theorize the effects of schadenfreude on perceptions of the perpetrator, research suggests that schadenfreude tends to center more on the event than on the victim alone (Combs et al., 2009; van Dijk et al., 2006). In third-party evaluations of mistreatment, schadenfreude typically manifests as feelings of pleasure, satisfaction, happiness, or contentment derived from the mistreatment event itself (Blader et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). Since the perpetrator is the catalyst of the event, they are inherently part of the experience that elicits schadenfreude. Emotions such as pleasure and satisfaction are often linked to approach-oriented tendencies, motivating individuals to engage with others to share those positive affective states (Lazarus, 1991). Within the mistreatment context, one way to share such feelings is by directing prosocial engagement toward the perpetrator. Based on this rationale, we proposed the following hypothesis:  
Hypothesis 6: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment will have positive indirect effects on (a) prosocial behavior toward the perpetrator and (b) antisocial behavior toward the victim, mediated by schadenfreude elicited by the mistreatment event.
text:
Li et al. (2019) further theorized that the action tendencies of schadenfreude would be channeled toward the victim of the mistreatment. In cases where coarse feelings of schadenfreude are muted by secondary appraisals, behavioral reactions might involve passive forms of antisocial behavior like avoidance or ostracism. In cases where coarse feelings of schadenfreude are amplified by secondary appraisals, behavioral reactions might involve active forms of antisocial behavior like undermining and gossip. In either circumstance, the behaviors serve to cement the potential status and outcome gains that triggered the original pleasure (Li et al., 2019). In support of such arguments, Xu et al. (2020) showed that schadenfreude from observing coworkers receiving abusive supervision was associated with undermining and deviance toward those coworkers.

Although Li et al. (2019) did not theorize that schadenfreude would have implications for the perpetrator, the emotion of schadenfreude is typically more focused on the event than the victim (Combs et al., 2009; van Dijk et al., 2006). In the context of third-party perceptions of mistreatment, schadenfreude typically reflects pleasure, happiness, satisfaction, and contentment from observing the mistreatment event (Blader et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). That event involves the perpetrator—not just the victim—as the perpetrator was the agent who created the event. The action tendencies for pleasure, happiness, and satisfaction include approaching others to share in positive feelings (Lazarus, 1991). One way to do so in the context of a mistreatment event is by engaging in approach-oriented behaviors toward the perpetrator. We therefore predicted:
Hypothesis 6: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment have positive indirect effects on (a) prosocial behavior toward the perpetrator and (b) antisocial behavior toward the victim through schadenfreude from the event.

use: Furthermore, personality traits are structured hierarchically. While the Big Five dimensions offer an efficient summary of a significant portion of the personality trait space, they still leave considerable variance unexplained (Mõttus et al., 2020). For instance, recent work by Irwing et al. (2024) identifies at least 70 distinct personality facets, with the possibility of many more single-item nuances—stable, measurable, and meaningful traits that serve as the fundamental units of trait assessment (Mõttus et al., 2019)—existing both within and beyond these broader facets (Condon et al., 2020). These narrower traits, including both facets and nuances, often show greater variability across human populations, such as by age (Mõttus & Rozgonjuk, 2021), gender (Hofmann et al., 2023), and nationality (Achaa-Amankwaa et al., 2021), and they frequently predict life outcomes more effectively than the broad Big Five domains (e.g., Seeboth & Mõttus, 2018; Stewart et al., 2022). Accordingly, it is plausible that occupational differences may be more pronounced at the level of narrow traits, which could, in turn, offer more utility in aligning individuals with suitable jobs. Indeed, there is emerging evidence suggesting that nuances outperform the Big Five in distinguishing between higher and lower performing employees (Speer et al., 2022). Crucially, the issue is not whether one level of assessment offers the definitive representation of occupational traits, but rather that various levels can coexist and serve different purposes (Mõttus et al., 2020). In some contexts, such as narrative-based career counseling or coaching, domain-level insights may suffice. In others, particularly those involving algorithm-driven job-matching platforms, a fine-grained, nuance-level analysis could deliver superior value. Despite these possibilities, the exploration of occupational differences at the nuance level remains conspicuously absent—and has yet to be systematically compared to differences observed in the Big Five.
text: Furthermore, personality traits are organized hierarchically. Although the Big Five domains efficiently summarize a substantial proportion of the personality trait space, they leave plenty unaccounted for (Mõttus et al., 2020). For example, Irwing et al. (2024) showed that there are at least 70 distinct personality facets, and there may be many more single-item nuances—stable and measurable traits in their own right that currently constitute the fundamental units of trait assessment (Mõttus et al., 2019)—within/beyond them (Condon et al., 2020). The facets and nuances often vary more across human groups, such as age (Mõttus & Rozgonjuk, 2021), gender (Hofmann et al., 2023), and nationality (Achaa-Amankwaa et al., 2021), and typically account for more variance in various life outcomes than domains (e.g., Seeboth & Mõttus, 2018; Stewart et al., 2022). Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that occupational differences may also be more pronounced in certain narrower traits than in the Big Five domains and that these narrow traits may prove particularly useful for matching people and jobs, for example. There is already evidence that nuances help to differentiate higher and lower performing employees better than the Big Five domains (Speer et al., 2022). Importantly, the question is not about which level of assessment provides the one and only correct way to represent occupational differences. Instead, multiple levels of assessment can be used at the same time, providing parallel findings that can be used depending on the purpose at hand (Mõttus et al., 2020). Sometimes, simpler domains-based findings may be useful (e.g., in narrative job counseling or coaching), while often more detailed nuances-based findings may provide the most value (e.g., for algorithmic job-matching tools). So far, however, occupational differences in nuance-level personality traits have not been explored, never mind compared to those in the Big Five.

There has been limited research exploring the link between nonverbal behavior and cognitive ability, despite early observations such as Darwin’s (1872) claim that intense concentration is often accompanied by frowning. Building on this premise, individuals with lower cognitive ability may be more prone to frown during cognitively demanding tasks that require sustained concentration and self-regulation—such as employment interviews. For instance, a study involving computer science students found that facial action unit 17—the mentalis muscle, or chin raiser, which visually resembles a frown—is frequently activated during coding tasks (Tiam-Lee & Sumi, 2017). One interpretation is that students with lower cognitive ability may involuntarily engage this facial muscle under cognitive load. Moreover, the co-occurrence of the chin raiser (AU17) with action unit 15 (lip corner depressor) has been linked to expressions of doubt and uncertainty (Bitti et al., 2014)—states of mind that may be especially salient for individuals with lower cognitive ability during high-stakes situations like interviews, where maintaining focus and composure is key. Though facial expressions are just one form of nonverbal communication—which also includes behaviors like gesturing and self-touch—they serve as the focal point of our study because they are the most observable nonverbal cues in asynchronous video interview (AVI) monologues, where other behaviors may be limited or difficult to detect (Bonaccio et al., 2016).

To enhance our understanding of how cognitive ability is reflected in interviewee behavior, we employ machine learning (ML) techniques to evaluate the relative and incremental validity of verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal signals in predicting cognitive ability. Specifically, we develop, evaluate, and compare multiple ML models to determine how each modality—individually and in combination—contributes to the accurate assessment of cognitive ability based on ground truth measures. This approach offers a conceptual, methodological, and practical foundation for establishing the psychometric soundness of cognitive ability assessments in AVI contexts.  
Research Question 1: How do verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors contribute to the accuracy of AVI-based cognitive ability assessments, both independently and in combination with one another?
text:
There has not been substantial research linking nonverbal behavior to cognitive ability, although Darwin (1872) asserted that extended periods of concentration are often accompanied by frowns. Given this, individuals with lower cognitive ability may be more likely to frown during extended periods of concentration and self-regulation, such as during employment interviews. A study of computer science students found that facial action unit 17, the mentalis or chin raiser, which is similar visually to a frown, is often activated during coding sessions (Tiam-Lee & Sumi, 2017). One possibility is that students with lower levels of cognitive ability were involuntarily activating their mentalis muscle. Combinations of the chin raiser with facial action unit 15, the lip corner depressor, are present during expressions of doubt and uncertainty (Bitti et al., 2014), feelings that may be persistent for lower cognitive ability interviewees as they strive to maintain concentration and self-control during the interview. Facial expressions can be considered affect displays (Bonaccio et al., 2016), and although there are several other types of nonverbal behaviors (e.g., self-touch, gestures), we focus on facial expressions given that they are the primary nonverbal cues available in asynchronous video interview monologues.

To advance our knowledge regarding the relationship between cognitive ability and interviewee behavior, we use ML as a tool to examine the relative and incremental validity of measured verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors for assessing cognitive ability. Specifically, we train, test, and compare the validity of ML models that use verbal, paraverbal, and/or nonverbal behaviors to assess each ground truth cognitive ability measure. This serves as a conceptual, methodological, and practical foundation for examining the psychometric properties of AVI cognitive ability assessments.
Research Question 1: How do verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors contribute to AVI cognitive ability accuracy, both individually and incrementally over one another?

Firm–stakeholder dialogue, moreover, is characterized by its focus on relationships and a commitment to mutuality (Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012: 9). It entails “an orientation to other people that recognizes their inherent self-worth” (Taylor & Kent, 2014: 388), guided by a deliberate practice rooted in the recognition of relational interdependence. Such dialogue demands thoughtful preparation, earnest engagement, and a willingness to approach others with openness and empathy (Taylor & Kent, 2014). At its core, it relies on the firm’s capacity to be aware of and genuinely interested in its stakeholders—not only in relation to specific issues but also in recognizing broader concerns that may influence the relationship. These values are evidenced in a firm’s commitment to dialogue as an ongoing process, as well as in its decision to pursue direct, unmediated forms of engagement through meetings and other participatory formats that allow stakeholders to observe the firm's actions and enable managers to better understand stakeholder perspectives.

Engagement in stakeholder dialogue is thus a proactive choice—one that signals respect for stakeholders, a commitment to listening, and a dedication to mutual learning (Kaptein & Van Tulder, 2017: 210). Proactive involvement demonstrates that the firm acknowledges its interdependence with stakeholders (Jones et al., 2018), and that it is willing to explore and understand their preferences, perceptions, and frames of reference (Brønn & Brønn, 2003). It also communicates a willingness to respond to stakeholder concerns in meaningful ways. In contrast, firms that abstain from stakeholder dialogue tend to adopt a reactive posture, engaging only when prompted by triggering events such as crises, scandals, or direct criticism. Such responses are typically focused on damage control and impression management and suggest either a lack of empathy for stakeholders or a limited understanding of how deeply connected they are to the firm’s success.
text:
Furthermore, firm–stakeholder dialogue “focuses on relationships and strives for mutuality” (Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012: 9). It requires “an orientation to other people that recognizes their inherent self-worth” (Taylor & Kent, 2014: 388) through a deliberate act that begins with the recognition of relational interdependence and that requires preparation, due diligence, and an openness to and empathy for the other (Taylor & Kent, 2014). Most fundamentally, dialogue requires that an actor—in our case, a firm—has sufficient awareness of and regard for stakeholders to seek them out, as well as some concern for and attentiveness to issues beyond those focal to the relationship (Taylor & Kent, 2014). These dispositions are reflected in a firm’s commitment to the dialogue process and its decision to seek unmediated interactions with stakeholders through meetings and activities that offer opportunities for stakeholders to observe the firm and for managers to learn more about their stakeholders.

Participation in stakeholder dialogue is therefore the result of a choice to be proactive, to “express respect for stakeholders, to listen to their contribution and demonstrate the commitment to learn from each other” (Kaptein & Van Tulder, 2017: 210). Proactive involvement in stakeholder dialogue signals that the firm recognizes its interdependence with stakeholders (Jones et al., 2018); that it is open and willing to learn about stakeholders’ preferences, perceptions, and mental models (Brønn & Brønn, 2003); and that it is willing to learn about, and possibly address, stakeholders’ concerns. By contrast, in the absence of stakeholder dialogue, firms default to a reactive stance, opting not to act unless something (e.g., an accident, a scandal) happens or stakeholders target the firm with specific concerns. Reactive responses focus on remedial impression management and imply that the firm lacks positive regard for the stakeholder or has insufficient understanding of their interdependence.

In recent years, considerable attention has been directed toward a growing emphasis on social meaning and culture. This shift is by no means confined to the fields of organization and management; rather, it reflects a broader development in the social sciences, often referred to as the "discursive" or "linguistic turn" (e.g., Rorty, 1967). Yet, despite the emergence of a substantial body of research on discourse (for a comprehensive overview, see Phillips & Oswick, 2012), the visual mode of meaning-making remains notably underexplored within organization and management studies. While it is widely acknowledged in general discourse analysis that discourse includes both verbal and visual forms of representation (see, for instance, Fairclough, 1992; Wodak & Meyer, 2009), and although organizational discourse scholars have echoed this recognition (e.g., Cornelissen, Holt, & Zundel, 2011; Grant, Hardy, Oswick, & Putnam, 2004; Phillips, Lawrence, & Hardy, 2004), visual discourse continues to receive minimal attention. Even as early as the 1990s, scholars were calling for a stronger incorporation of visual data into organizational research (e.g., Meyer, 1991), yet this appeal has largely gone unheeded. As a result, the visual dimension remains what some have called an “absent present” within the scholarship of the field (Styhre, 2010b; see also Davison & Warren, 2009).

In contrast, other disciplines have been far more attuned to the pervasive role of visual artifacts in contemporary society—an evolution closely tied to the rise of new information and communication technologies. These fields have acknowledged not just the increasing volume of visual content but a transformation in the nature of visual communication itself, often described as a new form of "visual language" (e.g., Kress & Van Leeuwen, 1996; Mitchell, 1994). Reflecting this shift, a number of scholars across the cultural and social sciences have identified what they term an "iconic" (Boehm, 1994; Maar & Burda, 2004), "imagic" (Fellmann, 1995), or "pictorial turn" (Mitchell, 1994), signaling a paradigm in which images and visual representation are gaining analytical prominence alongside, and sometimes even over, verbal discourse.
text: Much has been written in recent times with regard to a shift toward social meaning and culture. Such emphasis is by no means limited to the study of organization and management, and has been described more broadly as a "discursive" or "linguistic turn" in social sciences (e.g., Rorty, 1967). Surprisingly enough, and despite a prominent line of research that addresses discourse (for an overview, see Phillips & Oswick, 2012), the visual mode of meaning construction has remained largely unexplored in organization and management research. While researchers have acknowledged that discourse encompasses verbal, as well as visual, representations (in discourse analysis in general, see, for instance, Fairclough, 1992; Wodak & Meyer, 2009; in discourse analysis in organization research, see, for instance, Cornelissen, Holt, & Zundel, 2011; Grant, Hardy, Oswick, & Putnam, 2004; Phillips, Lawrence, & Hardy, 2004), and early calls for a more thorough integration of visual data into organization research were already voiced more than 20 years ago (e.g., Meyer, 1991), the majority of research has almost exclusively focused on verbal text—”to an extent that commentary has described the visual dimension as an "absent present" in the scholarly endeavors of the field (Styhre, 2010b; see also Davison & Warren, 2009). However, other disciplines have more readily recognized the omnipresence of visual artifacts in modern societies that go hand in hand with new information and communication technologies, and have been, for quite some time, acknowledging the fact that there is not only an increasing quantity but also a novel quality to the usage of "visual language" (e.g., Kress & Van Leeuwen, 1996; Mitchell, 1994). Consequently, various scholars in cultural and social sciences have claimed to witness what they label an "iconic" (e.g., Boehm, 1994; Maar & Burda, 2004), "imagic" (e.g., Fellmann, 1995), or "pictorial" turn (e.g., Mitchell, 1994).

Footprints exhibit distinct patterns—comprising treads, lines, shapes, and crosshatches—that can be visually identified and described. Similarly, legacies possess identifiable content that can be analyzed either qualitatively or taxonomically. For instance, a qualitative study of elderly individuals found that legacies typically fall into two major categories: material-based and values-based (Hunter & Rowles, 2005). Material-based legacies involve the transfer of physical items or heirlooms, while values-based legacies reflect the transmission of significant beliefs and guiding principles. Echoing this distinction, Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017) argued that legacies may center on the allocation of tangible resources or the diffusion of normative value systems. Building on these insights, we propose that the lasting contributions attributed to managers, informal leaders, and high performers can likewise be categorized as either tangible or intangible (Bednar, 2013; Fox & Wade-Benzoni, 2017; Hunter & Rowles, 2005).

These contributions may take the form of enhancements to a unit’s resources—that is, the assets, capabilities, procedures, and information that collectively underpin organizational effectiveness (Barney, 1991). Such resource-oriented contributions could involve acquiring or developing new technologies, sourcing essential materials, expanding the expertise of colleagues, improving relational dynamics, mentoring others, or restructuring reporting lines. Alternatively, contributions might enhance the memory of the unit—the stored organizational knowledge accumulated from past experiences and used to inform present decisions and actions (Walsh & Ungson, 1991). In this domain, valuable contributions could include sharing strategic information, creating standard procedures, modeling role expectations, or archiving knowledge for future access. Finally, contributions may influence the culture of the unit—defined as the shared assumptions that manifest in values and artifacts and shape how members perceive, feel, and behave (Hatch, 1993; Schein, 2010). Cultural contributions might entail engaging in interpretive processes that translate assumptions into collective values or designing artifacts such as rituals, celebrations, stories, language, and symbolic objects.
text: Footprints have a particular pattern—”the tread, lines, shapes, and crosshatches that can be described while looking at them. In a similar way, legacies have a content that can be described qualitatively or taxonomically. A qualitative study of elderly people suggested that legacies can be materials-based or values-based (Hunter & Rowles, 2005); materials-based legacies involve the passing on of heirlooms and possessions, whereas values-based legacies involve the passing on of important beliefs and priorities. Similarly, Fox and Wade-Benzoni (2017) argued that legacies can be based around the distribution of tangible resources or the proliferation of value systems. We therefore argue that the contributions ascribed to managers, informal leaders, and stars can be more tangible or intangible (Bednar, 2013; Fox & Wade-Benzoni, 2017; Hunter & Rowles, 2005). In terms of the specific contributions themselves, they could represent improvements to the resources of a unit—”the assets, capabilities, processes, and information that enable the unit to be effective (Barney, 1991). The contributions could be based in procuring or creating technology, providing access to raw materials, growing the experience base of coworkers, improving relationships, training others, or altering reporting structures. Or the contributions could represent improvements to the memory of the unit—”the stored information from a unit's history that can be applied to current decisions and events (Walsh & Ungson, 1991). The contributions could be based in providing information to coworkers, creating standardized procedures, exemplifying how roles should be occupied, or recording information into archives. Or contributions could represent improvements to the culture of a unit—”the basic assumptions that become realized in values and artifacts and that guide perceptions, emotions, and actions (Hatch, 1993; Schein, 2010). The contributions could be based in participating in the interpretive processes that translate assumptions into values or creating artifacts like rituals, ceremonies, stories, language, or objects.

Organizational Citizenship Behavior (OCB) refers to discretionary actions that support the group but fall outside formal job responsibilities and are not directly rewarded (Motowidlo & Van Scotter, 1994). OCB is inherently other-oriented—it does not directly advance the individual’s own work and may even incur personal costs (Bergeron et al., 2013; Van Scotter et al., 2000). Research on group dynamics consistently shows that fair treatment fosters positive self-evaluations and a strong sense of interdependent identity, both of which promote cooperative behaviors like OCB. When individuals derive their self-worth from the group and identify with its members, particularly through experiences of justice, they develop a deep sense of shared fate. This sense of belonging leads them to internalize a responsibility to promote the group’s collective wellbeing (Mael & Ashforth, 1995), activating the psychological mechanism known as "reasons to" perform OCB (Parker et al., 2010). In such cases, individuals willingly place the group’s needs above their own, even at personal expense, and engage in more OCB as a result (Bolino & Grant, 2016).

However, as discussed earlier, social exchanges are often guided by the pursuit of personal benefit. When OCB is rooted in norms of social exchange, it is typically limited to reciprocating actions directly tied to the source of fairness and is motivated by the possibility of personal gain (Masterson et al., 2000; Rupp et al., 2014). In such cases, individuals may only engage in OCB to the extent necessary to maintain an equitable exchange balance (Flynn, 2003; Liao et al., 2019). Given that OCB frequently entails additional, unrewarded effort that exceeds obligations to direct exchange partners (Van Dyne et al., 2000), it may be less likely to occur when driven purely by instrumental motives—especially when such actions are personally costly (Bolino & Turnley, 2005; Koopman et al., 2016). However, when employees deeply internalize group goals and values, they are more likely to perform OCB at higher levels, fueled by intrinsic commitment to the group rather than by expectations of reciprocation (Greco et al., 2022; Matta et al., 2020).
text: OCB refers to discretionary actions that are helpful to the group but beyond the formal job description and are not explicitly rewarded (Motowidlo & Van Scotter, 1994). OCB is other-oriented and not directly beneficial for personal work and can even come at a cost for personal interests (Bergeron et al., 2013; Van Scotter et al., 2000). Group-oriented studies have widely documented that fair treatment enhances positive self-evaluations and interdependent identity, which motivate cooperative behavior such as OCB. Employees who attach self-worth to and identify with the group and members due to justice experience develop a strong sense of shared destiny with them and internalize the obligation to maximize their welfare (Mael & Ashforth, 1995). This spurs the psychological driver of "reasons to" for OCB (Parker et al., 2010). They are motivated to strive for the benefits and welfare of the group beyond personal interests or even at personal costs (Bolino & Grant, 2016). They would prioritize the needs of the group and members over those of their own by performing more OCB. However, as mentioned previously, exchange parties engage in reciprocal actions ultimately to pursue personal gains. To the extent that OCB is driven by social exchange, it will be confined to the accountable party for personal benefits (Masterson et al., 2000; Rupp et al., 2014) or will cease at a level that suffices to maintain an equitable balance (Flynn, 2003; Liao et al., 2019). OCB often requires extra efforts that go beyond direct exchange targets (Van Dyne et al., 2000) and the level of obligated reciprocation specified by fair treatment or even comes at personal costs (Bolino & Turnley, 2005; Koopman et al., 2016). When employees fully internalize group interests, they are motivated to perform such behavior to a greater degree (Greco et al., 2022; Matta et al., 2020).

Construal-level theory posits that psychological distance—defined as the “subjective experience that something is close or far away from the self, here, and now”—shapes how individuals evaluate people and make decisions (Trope & Liberman, 2010, p. 440). I apply this framework to explain how perceptions of racial versus political dissimilarity influence the mental representations individuals form about a coworker’s trustworthiness, either as psychologically proximal and concrete or psychologically distant and abstract. While prior research often lacks clarity regarding the specific domains in which trust is placed (see Schilke et al., 2021), I propose that individuals evaluate coworkers using domain-based mental representations. Specifically, role trustworthiness reflects a lower-level construal that involves concrete judgments about a coworker’s competence and reliability in their professional role. In contrast, person trustworthiness reflects a higher-level construal involving abstract, generalized assessments of a coworker’s character and integrity as a person. These two forms of trustworthiness parallel professional versus personal identities (Ashforth et al., 2000), as both differ in focus, can influence each other through spillover, and shape workplace behavior in distinct ways (Rothbard & Ramarajan, 2009).

Drawing on established ideological differences in construal-level preferences, system-justifying attitudes, and tendencies toward social dominance (Jost, 2017, 2021), I further theorize that liberals and conservatives process dissimilarity in meaningfully different ways when it comes to trust. Specifically, I argue that liberals are more inclined than conservatives to perceive politically dissimilar coworkers as globally untrustworthy—less trustworthy as people—and are thus less likely to share sensitive information with them. Conversely, I argue that conservatives are more likely than liberals to view racially dissimilar coworkers as professionally untrustworthy—less trustworthy within their job roles—and are therefore less inclined to rely on them at work. I developed, refined, and tested these hypotheses through a multi-method research design that includes a qualitative survey, a longitudinal dyadic field study, and three preregistered experimental studies.
text: Construal-level theory asserts that psychological distance, defined as the "subjective experience that something is close or far away from the self, here, and now," influences one's evaluations and behaviors (Trope & Liberman, 2010, p. 440). As such, I draw on it to explain how perceived racial versus political dissimilarity prompts one to adopt mental representations of a coworker's trustworthiness that are psychologically proximal/concrete versus psychologically distant/abstract. Research is often ambiguous when it comes to the domains in which trust is placed (see Schilke et al., 2021). However, I theorize that a coworker can be evaluated in terms of domain-based mental representations. Role trustworthiness is a lower-level construal that prompts more concrete perceptions of one's trustworthiness specifically with regard to their job. Person trustworthiness is a higher-level construal that prompts more abstract perceptions of one's trustworthiness generally as a person in the world. Role and person trustworthiness are akin to work and non-work (professional and personal) identities (Ashforth et al., 2000) in terms of their focal domains and in that they can have spillover but are distinct regarding how they are activated and their effects on workplace behavior (Rothbard & Ramarajan, 2009). Based on established ideological differences in construal-level tendencies, as well as system justification attitudes and social dominance orientations (Jost, 2017, 2021), I also theorize that liberals and conservatives differentially distrust dissimilar coworkers. Ultimately, I argue that liberals are more likely than conservatives to view politically dissimilar coworkers as less trustworthy people in the world and disclose less sensitive information to them. And I argue that conservatives are more likely than liberals to view racially dissimilar coworkers as less trustworthy in their jobs and rely on them less at work. I developed, refined, and tested my hypotheses across a qualitative survey, a longitudinal dyadic field survey, and three preregistered experiments.

In this article, we draw on the emotion expression and attribution literatures (e.g., Greenaway et al., 2018; Park et al., 2022) to examine the boundary conditions surrounding expressions of gratitude from the perspective of third-party observers. Unlike direct recipients of gratitude, third-party witnesses may be less inclined to attribute such expressions to genuinely prosocial motives and more critical in evaluating whether the gratitude was warranted (Bock & Thomas, 2023). These evaluative tendencies are likely to intensify when gratitude crosses status lines—for example, when employees express appreciation toward leaders—prompting witnesses to question whether the leader has truly earned the gratitude (Anicich et al., 2022). This concern aligns with prior findings suggesting that deservingness considerations are particularly salient in how employees perceive their leaders (Nater et al., 2023). More pointedly, research indicates that employees often intentionally withhold gratitude toward leaders when they believe it is unearned (Toegel et al., 2013).

Our proposed research model, illustrated in Figure 1, outlines these dynamics. We argue that expressions of gratitude directed at leaders influence how third-party observers perceive the expresser’s prosocial identity. Critically, however, we propose that this effect is moderated by perceptions of the leader’s deservingness. When a witness judges the leader to lack competence or warmth, the observer is likely to view the gratitude as undeserved and infer that the expresser is less genuinely prosocial. Furthermore, we contend that these inferential processes will shape the observer’s behavior toward the expresser, particularly in terms of willingness to offer help or seek feedback. In alignment with research on authenticity and motive inferences—and given evidence suggesting that gratitude used as a strategy for ingratiation or impression management may backfire (Algoe & Zhaoyang, 2016; Gulliford et al., 2019)—Studies 2 and 3 additionally investigate the role of self-enhancement motives in shaping these perceptions and responses (see Figure 1).
text:
In this article, we draw from the emotion expression and attributions literatures (e.g., Greenaway et al., 2018; Park et al., 2022) to focus on the boundary conditions of expressed gratitude among third-party witnesses. Compared to direct recipients of expressed gratitude, witnesses may be less motivated to attribute gratitude expressions to prosocial motives, and may be more critical of how deserved they are (Bock & Thomas, 2023). Such dynamics are likely to be particularly acute across status boundaries, where witnesses may ask if a leader has truly earned an employee’s gratitude (Anicich et al., 2022). Consistent with this idea, past research has shown concerns of deservingness to be front-of-mind when employees think about their leaders (Nater et al., 2023). More directly, research has shown that employees often withhold gratitude from their leaders out of a belief that the leader has not earned it (Toegel et al., 2013).

Our research model is presented in Figure 1. As shown in this model, we argue that leader-directed expressions of gratitude will have a direct effect on perceptions of the expresser’s prosocial identity. Crucially, however, we argue that this effect will hinge on perceptions of the leader’s deservingness. When a witness believes that a leader is less competent or warm, we theorize that the witness will perceive gratitude as undeserved, and attribute the expression to a less prosocial identity. Last, we argue that this pattern of effects will have implications for how the witness treats the expresser, influencing witnesses’ tendencies to both help and seek feedback from the expresser. In keeping with the notions of authenticity and motive attributions, and in light of research suggesting how gratitude perceived as an impression management tactic or ingratiation may elicit negative responses (Algoe & Zhaoyang, 2016; Gulliford et al., 2019), Studies 2 and 3 also explore self-enhancement motives as a mechanism (see Figure 1).

We argue further that the foundational principles of deonance theory align well with a broadened interpretation of the deontic response. At a specific level, this response has been characterized as an automatic surge of anger that motivates retributive action against the wrongdoer (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). More generally, however, the deontic response can be conceptualized as an automatic activation of moral emotions that drive behaviors aimed at upholding the moral order. One such moral emotion is empathy, which has been shown to elicit prosocial behaviors—partly as a way to diminish empathic distress (Batson et al., 2002; Haidt, 2003). Prosocial behaviors, defined as actions intended to benefit others (Bolino & Grant, 2016; Brief & Motowidlo, 1986), serve to reinforce the moral order, especially in instances where mistreatment is observed. In such cases, these behaviors represent what third parties feel they "ought to do" in response to another’s suffering.

While the existing literature has more extensively explored third-party anger and antisocial behaviors than empathy and prosocial responses, emerging evidence supports our extended view of the deontic response. For instance, Cheng et al. (2023) found that third parties who witnessed a coworker being treated uncivilly by a customer experienced empathy, which in turn led them to help the coworker—despite the study not being grounded in deonance theory (see also Chen et al., 2021; Feng & Savani, 2023). Conversely, Hu et al. (2022), in a study directly informed by deonance theory, did not identify empathy as a mediator between observed abusive supervision and helping behavior. These mixed findings underscore the need for further investigation. To that end, we conducted a meta-analytic synthesis to evaluate an extended prediction of deonance theory regarding behavioral reactions to mistreatment:
Hypothesis 4: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment have a positive indirect effect on prosocial behavior toward the victim through empathy toward the victim.
text:
We further argue that the spirit of deonance theory is consistent with an expanded conceptualization of the deontic response. At a specific level, that response is described as an automatic triggering of anger that fuels retribution against the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). At a general level, however, that response can be described as an automatic triggering of moral emotions that fuel behaviors that preserve the moral order. Empathy is a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003) that fuels prosocial behaviors, in part, as a means of reducing empathic arousal (Batson et al., 2002). Prosocial behaviors are defined as actions intended to benefit others (Bolino & Grant, 2016; Brief & Motowidlo, 1986). In the context of observing mistreatment, such behaviors should preserve the moral order because they represent what the third party “ought to do” in response to the suffering of a victim.

Although the volume of work on empathy and prosocial behavior in the third-party literature trails the volume of work on anger and antisocial behavior, some studies do support our expanded conceptualization of the deontic response. For example, in a study not rooted in deonance theory, Cheng et al. (2023) showed that third parties who observed a coworker being treated uncivilly by a customer felt empathy toward the coworker, with that empathy fueling helping of the coworker (see also Chen et al., 2021; Feng & Savani, 2023). In contrast, in a study that was rooted in deonance theory, Hu et al. (2022) failed to uncover a mediating role for empathy when connecting observations of coworker abusive supervision to helping of the coworker. We therefore sought to use our meta-analytic synthesis to test the following extension of deonance theory’s predictions regarding behavioral reactions to observing mistreatment:
Hypothesis 4: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment have a positive indirect effect on prosocial behavior toward the victim through empathy toward the victim.

use: In recent years, growing public concern over social justice has led corporate leaders around the globe to place greater emphasis on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) within their organizations. Surveys conducted in the months following the death of George Floyd revealed that DEI remained a top priority for C-suite executives, prompting a wave of pro-diversity and equity policy announcements across a variety of industries (Colletta, 2022; Fortune-Deloitte, 2020; Glazer & Francis, 2021; Ryan & Fiatte, 2022). While some of these efforts represented an intensification of existing practices—such as expanding diversity training—others introduced more innovative approaches, including the use of anonymized resumes (Agovino, 2020; Carlos, 2021). A critical question for management practice, one that demands a foundation in theory and empirical evidence, concerns which specific DEI initiatives organizations should adopt in order to generate meaningful and lasting change. Although scholarly efforts have addressed this issue for some time, determining the effectiveness of DEI interventions has proven difficult, with results often yielding mixed outcomes in two important respects. First, interventions that demonstrate positive effects under certain conditions frequently produce null or diminished results when applied in different contexts (Aboud et al., 2012; Hsieh et al., 2022; Rodon & Franco-Guillén, 2014). Compounding this challenge, only a small portion of the research on bias-reduction interventions directly examines work-related outcomes, with most studies focusing instead on broader, nonwork-related attitudes and behaviors (Paluck et al., 2021). This gap is notable, as workplace contexts may significantly shape how interventions perform. For instance, research has shown that a business-oriented framing of decision-making tasks can alter the way individuals process information (Rees et al., 2022). Moreover, several work-specific interventions—such as anonymized hiring practices or reminding employees of equal employment opportunity laws—can be uniquely assessed within organizational settings. As such, a comprehensive review of DEI interventions focused exclusively on workplace outcomes is both timely and necessary.
text: In recent years, increased public concern over social justice issues led corporate leaders worldwide to prioritize diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) policies in their organizations. Surveys conducted a few months to a year after George Floyd's death showed that DEI initiatives remained a top concern among C-suite executives, prompting several pro-diversity and equity policy announcements from organizations across industries (Colletta, 2022; Fortune-Deloitte, 2020; Glazer & Francis, 2021; Ryan & Fiatte, 2022). While some of these initiatives ramped up current standard organizational practices in this area, such as increasing diversity training, others introduced more novel strategies, such as implementing anonymized resumes (Agovino, 2020; Carlos, 2021). A key open question to management practice that should be informed by theory and grounded in empirical evidence is which initiatives companies should adopt based on their propensity to produce meaningful change in organizations. While much research has been conducted to address these issues, traditionally, this has been a challenging question to answer, as interventions have met with mixed success in this literature in two distinct ways. First, interventions that appear effective under certain conditions produce null or weaker effects in different contexts (Aboud et al., 2012; Hsieh et al., 2022; Rodon & Franco-Guillén, 2014). Despite this, only a small proportion of research examining interventions to decrease bias investigates work-related outcomes, with most interventional studies assessing more general, nonwork-related attitudes and behaviors (Paluck et al., 2021). This is notable since workplace settings could potentially influence the effectiveness of interventions in different ways. For example, research has shown that adopting a business framing to decision making can influence the cognitive processing of information (Rees et al., 2022). In addition, as certain work-specific interventions can be examined in a way that is distinct from other contexts (e.g., anonymizing resumes, reminding individuals about equal employment opportunity policies and laws), an evaluative review of interventions centered only on workplace outcomes is a pressing need.

Dhanani et al.’s (2021) review on workplace mistreatment revealed that 44% of employees had witnessed such behavior, underscoring the need for a deeper understanding of how third parties react to observing mistreatment. Deonance theory has emerged as the dominant framework for interpreting third-party reactions, as its core propositions are applicable to both directly experienced and vicariously observed mistreatment (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). However, despite this prominence, there remains a lack of scholarly consensus regarding the robustness of empirical support for the theory’s claims. More critically, the theory’s formal propositions fall short of capturing the diverse range of reactions that researchers have documented among third parties. With this gap in mind, we undertook a meta-analytic synthesis of the third-party literature, testing a comprehensive model that incorporates emotional, cognitive, and behavioral responses to observed mistreatment.

One of the most striking findings from our meta-analysis is that witnessing mistreatment constitutes a powerful psychological event. Although observers are not direct targets, the effect sizes identified in our analysis are on par with those found in studies involving firsthand experiences of abusive supervision, organizational injustice, incivility, and sexual harassment (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Mackey et al., 2017; Willness et al., 2007). The third-party viewpoint may offer a broader, more detached vantage point—a “bird’s-eye view”—but this perspective does not dull the emergence of strong emotional, cognitive, and behavioral reactions toward both perpetrators and victims. Another important and perhaps counterintuitive insight is that reactions toward victims can be unexpectedly negative. While responses to perpetrators aligned with patterns seen in 'first-party' studies, our results revealed that third-party observers often reported feelings of schadenfreude, more critical evaluations of victims, and even increased antisocial behavior directed at them. To better understand these findings, we explore their implications for the continued relevance and refinement of deonance theory in the third-party mistreatment literature.
text:
Dhanani et al.’s (2021) review of the prevalence of mistreatment showed that 44% of employees had observed mistreatment at work. That estimate illustrates the importance of better understanding third-party reactions to observing mistreatment. Deonance theory has emerged as the dominant lens for providing that understanding, as its core predictions are applicable to situations where mistreatment is either observed or experienced (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Despite that dominance, however, the literature lacks a consensus on how robust the support for deonance theory’s propositions has been. More importantly, the letter of those propositions fails to encompass the varied reactions that third-party scholars have examined in their work. With those issues in mind, we set out to perform a meta-analytic synthesis of the third-party literature, testing a model that integrates a variety of emotional, cognitive, and behavioral reactions to observe mistreatment.

One clear takeaway from our meta-analysis is that observing mistreatment is a surprisingly potent psychological event. Despite the fact that observers are not directly involved in the mistreatment, the effect sizes revealed in our synthesis are comparable in magnitude to “first-party” reviews of the abusive supervision, injustice, incivility, and sexual harassment literatures (Colquitt et al., 2013; Han et al., 2022; Mackey et al., 2017; Willness et al., 2007). If the third-party perspective affords a “bird’s-eye view,” the resulting altitude does not prevent a clear emergence of emotional, cognitive, and behavioral reactions toward the parties involved. Another clear takeaway is that the consequences of observing mistreatment can be counterintuitive—especially with respect to the victim. Although reactions to the perpetrator hewed closely to the patterns observed in “first-party” reviews, our results showed that observing mistreatment was associated with schadenfreude from the event, more negative evaluations of the victim, and more antisocial behaviors toward the victim. To unpack such results, we review the implications of our findings for deonance theory’s role in the third-party literature.

In many societies, individuals dedicate a significant portion of their adult lives to work, making a systematic understanding of how people’s traits align with their professional roles both practically and theoretically essential (Kristof-Brown et al., 2005). For instance, fields such as career planning, personnel selection, and executive coaching heavily rely on insights from personality research—yet the effectiveness of such practices depends on an accurate understanding of how personality traits vary across different occupations. The influence of personality is often highlighted by its predictive power in relation to major life outcomes (Roberts et al., 2007), with occupational choice being among the most prominent. While psychologists have extensively examined average personality differences within various demographic categories—including gender (e.g., Schmitt et al., 2008), age groups (e.g., Bleidorn et al., 2022), generational cohorts (e.g., Brandt et al., 2022; Smits et al., 2011), and geographic regions (e.g., Allik et al., 2017; Rentfrow & Jokela, 2019)—there remains a surprising lack of systematic research examining how personality traits differ across occupational categories.

A number of studies have profiled the characteristic personality traits of individuals working in specific occupations (Booth et al., 2016; Cerasa et al., 2016; Furnham, 2017; R. King et al., 2011; Lan et al., 2021; Lounsbury et al., 2012, 2016; Oh et al., 2018; Slišković et al., 2022), though these often rely on relatively small samples and limited occupational scope. While companies specializing in psychological assessments presumably maintain extensive databases reflecting personality variation by occupation, this proprietary information is typically withheld for commercial reasons. To date, only two large-scale studies have offered systematic overviews of personality traits across a broad set of occupations: one identified mean differences in the Big Five traits among 25 occupational groups (Törnroos et al., 2019), and the other presented Big Five mean scores for over 360 different occupations (Wolfram, 2023). However, for several reasons, these efforts represent only an initial step toward a comprehensive and methodologically rigorous map of personality trait variation across the occupational spectrum. In this paper, we aim to build on and significantly extend that foundation.
text:
In many societies, people tend to devote much of their adult life to work, so a systematic understanding of the match between people’s traits and their work is both practically and theoretically important (Kristof-Brown et al., 2005). For example, career planning, job applicant selection, and coaching are among the primary applications of personality research, while their success hinges on understanding how people’s traits actually vary with occupations. Likewise, the importance of personality traits is often illustrated by their ability to predict key life outcomes (Roberts et al., 2007), among which occupational choice ranks particularly high. However, while psychologists have already carefully mapped mean personality differences across many human categories, including gender (e.g., Schmitt et al., 2008) and age groups (e.g., Bleidorn et al., 2022), cohorts (e.g., Brandt et al., 2022; Smits et al., 2011), or geographical regions (e.g., Allik et al., 2017; Rentfrow & Jokela, 2019), surprisingly little systematic research has been dedicated to personality differences across occupations.
Several studies have described incumbents’ typical personality traits in one or a few occupations (Booth et al., 2016; Cerasa et al., 2016; Furnham, 2017; R. King et al., 2011; Lan et al., 2021; Lounsbury et al., 2012, 2016; Oh et al., 2018; Slišković et al., 2022), often with relatively small samples. While organizations that offer assessment services likely hold comprehensive databases about occupational variations in personality traits, they monetize this information and are therefore unlikely to publish it. We know of only two published, larger scale studies systematically mapping traits across a broader range of occupations. One reported mean differences in the Big Five personality domains across 25 occupational groups (Törnroos et al., 2019), while the other provided the Big Five mean scores for over 360 occupations (Wolfram, 2023). But for several reasons, these studies only constitute a start to the process of comprehensively and rigorously mapping occupational differences in personality traits. Here, we advance this research in multiple ways.

Extensive research has explored how organizations leverage human resource (HR) practices to acquire, develop, and retain strategically important human capital (Jackson et al., 2014). A central outcome of this body of work is the advancement of the configurational, or “internal fit,” perspective. This view suggests that organizational performance improves when individual HR practices are deliberately aligned and designed to reinforce one another (J. Delery & Gupta, 2016; Gerhart, 2007; Marler, 2012). Each HR practice serves a distinct function—for example, staffing aims to attract and hire qualified candidates, while compensation is intended to motivate employees through financial and non-financial rewards. High internal fit occurs when these practices are mutually supportive. For instance, combining selective staffing with performance-based compensation creates synergy by first identifying candidates with critical knowledge, skills, and abilities, and then motivating them to maximize their contributions.

Building on this foundation, scholars introduced the concept of a high-performance work system (HPWS), which refers to a cohesive set of internally aligned HR practices aimed at driving superior organizational outcomes (Appelbaum & Batt, 1994; Huselid, 1995; Kalleberg & Moody, 1994). Researchers typically draw on the Ability–Motivation–Opportunity (AMO) framework to guide the selection and integration of practices within an HPWS (Gerhart, 2007; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012; Lepak et al., 2006). According to this model, employee performance is influenced by their ability (skills and competencies), motivation (incentives and engagement), and opportunity (resources and involvement) to perform. Accordingly, effective HR systems include practices that identify and nurture talent based on job-relevant criteria, motivate employees using performance feedback and reward mechanisms, and empower them to participate meaningfully in organizational processes. HPWS built on AMO principles have been consistently linked to favorable outcomes, including enhanced firm performance (Han et al., 2019; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012).
text:
Considerable research attention has focused on understanding how organizations can use human resources (HR) practices to acquire, develop, and retain strategically valuable human capital resources (Jackson et al., 2014). A key outcome of this work has been the development of the configurational or “internal fit” perspective. This perspective proposes that organizations can achieve higher performance if they use systems of individual HR practices that are strategically configured to enhance each other (J. Delery & Gupta, 2016; Gerhart, 2007; Marler, 2012). Indeed, each HR practice has its own goals. For instance, the primary goal of staffing is to recruit and hire qualified workers, and the primary goal of compensation is to motivate workers through rewards. High internal fit occurs when firms adopt HR practices that complement and reinforce the effects of one another. For example, the combination of selective staffing and performance-based pay may create internal fit by selecting applicants with the most relevant knowledge, skills, and abilities and then by motivating them to fully utilize their capabilities.

Building upon this idea, scholars have introduced the concept of a high-performance work system (HPWS) defined as an internally fitting set of practices designed to achieve higher performance (Appelbaum & Batt, 1994; Huselid, 1995; Kalleberg & Moody, 1994). They have generally relied on the Ability–Motivation–Opportunity (AMO) model (Gerhart, 2007; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012; Lepak et al., 2006) to select and configure HR practices to form an HPWS. According to this model, employee performance is a function of their ability, motivation, and opportunity to perform. Thus, effective HR systems are thought to comprise practices that select and develop employees based on job- and organization-relevant criteria, motivate them through performance feedback and rewards, and involve and empower them by providing the opportunity to contribute. In turn, HPWS that focus on AMO tend to result in positive outcomes such as improved firm performance (Han et al., 2019; Jiang, Lepak, Hu, & Baer, 2012).
