We contend that intra-category coherence directly influences a category’s viability, but this impact is contingent upon the level of coherence between categories. Our argument unfolds in two stages. Building on prior foundational work (e.g., Koçak, Hannan, & Hsu, 2014; Wry et al., 2011; Zuckerman, 1999), we first posit that intra-category coherence is advantageous because it allows market participants to (a) group similar products into recognizable sets and (b) ascribe value to individual products within those sets. We conceptualize these underlying mechanisms as the “classification” and “valuation” benefits of intra-category coherence. Notably, these benefits may be in tension with one another. We then theorize that the coherence between categories moderates the relative strength of these two mechanisms, resulting in either a U-shaped or inverted U-shaped relationship between intra-category coherence and category viability. This framework offers a more refined explanation for the fluctuating trajectories that categories often exhibit over time.

To test our theory, we analyze 170 product categories over an 11-year span on Kickstarter, a leading crowdfunding platform. Our dataset includes over 425,000 detailed campaign narratives for new products. We employed a topic-modeling technique to assess the textual content of each campaign (Hannigan et al., 2019), allowing us to generate dynamic, time-varying measures of coherence both within individual categories (i.e., the degree of heterogeneity) and across related categories (i.e., the degree of distinctiveness). These measures help explain variations in category viability, which we operationalize as the number of campaigns launched in each category within a rolling 30-day window. Our findings reveal that when a category is relatively indistinct from others, there is an inverted U-shaped relationship between its internal heterogeneity and viability—suggesting that moderate heterogeneity is optimal. However, as a category becomes more distinctive, this relationship weakens and eventually reverses into a U-shaped curve—whereby both low and high levels of heterogeneity support greater viability.
text:
We argue that intra-category coherence has a direct effect on the viability of a category, but that this effect is contingent on inter-category coherence. We build our argument in two steps. Drawing on longstanding insights (e.g., Koçak, Hannan, & Hsu, 2014; Wry et al., 2011; Zuckerman, 1999), we first hold that intra-category coherence is beneficial to the extent that it enables actors and audiences to (a) group comparable products together, and (b) ascribe value to each product within this comparable set. We refer to these two latent mechanisms as “classification” and “valuation” benefits, which are at odds with one another. We then theorize how coherence across categories moderates the relative strength of the two benefits, yielding either a U-shaped or an inverted U-shaped relationship. Our theorization thus provides a nuanced explanation of the various fluctuations that categories may experience over their lifetime.

We test our ideas by examining 170 product categories over an 11-year period on Kickstarter, a major crowdfunding platform. We collected the detailed narratives of over 425,000 campaigns for new products, and used a topic-modeling approach to analyze the textual content of each campaign (Hannigan et al., 2019). We then developed time-varying measures of coherence within a category (i.e., a category’s degree of heterogeneity), and across related categories (i.e., a category’s degree of distinctiveness). We used these measures to explain fluctuations in the viability of each of these 170 categories, which we operationalized by counting the number of campaigns launched in a category over a moving 30-day period. We found that, when a category’s distinctiveness is low, there is an inverted U-shaped relationship between the category’s heterogeneity and its subsequent viability. In other words, viability increases when products within a category reach a balanced degree of heterogeneity. However, as distinctiveness grows, the inverted U-shaped relationship flattens, and eventually flips to a U-shaped relationship. That is, viability increases when products within a category reach either low or high degrees of heterogeneity.

Although various theoretical frameworks have been employed to study third-party perceptions of mistreatment, deonance theory has emerged as the dominant perspective (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Deonance theory conceptualizes fair treatment as a moral imperative—an "ought" that is intrinsically perceived as "the right thing to do" (Folger & Glerum, 2015). Accordingly, witnessing mistreatment is expected to elicit what the theory terms the “deontic response”: an automatic, emotion-driven reaction of anger that motivates retributive behavior toward the offender (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). This retributive impulse is seen as evolutionarily adaptive, serving to uphold moral order and social norms (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Empirical studies affirm this logic, showing that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are frequently linked to anger directed at the perpetrator and to antisocial behaviors aimed at them (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020).

Yet, despite strong empirical backing for deonance theory’s central propositions, three important questions remain regarding its capacity to fully explain third-party responses to mistreatment. The first of these concerns how observers respond to the victims. In its various formulations, deonance theory tends to emphasize perpetrators and the anger they provoke (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008), while references to victims are comparatively infrequent and often peripheral (Folger, 2001; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008; Folger & Stein, 2017). Nonetheless, several scholars have extended deonance theory to suggest that observing injustice may also elicit empathy for the victim and promote prosocial behavior toward them (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Hu et al., 2022; Liang & Park, 2022a). To what extent do these findings hold, and how well do they align with the “spirit”—if not the precise wording—of deonance theory?
text:
Although a number of theories have been applied in the literature on third-party perceptions of mistreatment, deonance theory has emerged as the dominant lens (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Deonance theory views appropriate treatment as an “ought”—as something inherently perceived as “the right thing to do” (Folger & Glerum, 2015). As a result, observing mistreatment should result in what the theory calls the “deontic response”: an automatic triggering of anger that fuels retribution against the perpetrator (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). Such retribution is adaptive in an evolutionary sense because it preserves the moral order (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). In support of such logic, studies have shown that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are associated with anger toward the perpetrator and antisocial behavior toward the perpetrator (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020).

Despite the robust support for deonance theory’s core prediction, three key questions remain about its utility for explaining third-party reactions to observing mistreatment. The first question centers on how observers might react to the victims of mistreatment. In the various formulations of deonance theory, the “letter” of its propositions is explicitly perpetrator-focused and anger-focused (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). References to the victim of the mistreatment are more rare and more tangential (Folger, 2001; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008; Folger & Stein, 2017). And yet, some scholars have drawn on deonance theory to argue that third-party perceptions of mistreatment should be associated with empathy toward the victim and prosocial behavior toward the victim (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Hu et al., 2022; Liang & Park, 2022a). How robust are such findings, and how consistent are they with the “spirit”—if not the letter—of deonance theory?

use:
In contrast to its strong focus on anger-driven responses toward perpetrators, deonance theory offers surprisingly limited guidance on how third parties should respond to victims of mistreatment. Its two most comprehensive articulations make no mention of victim-directed reactions (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Similarly, several more narrowly framed accounts omit any discussion of how observers engage with victims (Folger, 2012; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger & Shukla, 2019; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005). Where victims are mentioned, references are often brief and tangential. For instance, Folger (2001) noted that deonance could involve concern for others’ welfare, but this point was framed within a broader effort to preserve moral order through retributive means. Likewise, Folger and Skarlicki (2008) acknowledged—almost in passing—that third parties might offer emotional support to victims, yet they did not elaborate on how such behavior aligns with the core logic of deonance theory. In response to a critique suggesting that deonance might render observers “relatively indifferent to the other parties involved in the immediate situation” (Korsgaard et al., 2015, p. 388), Folger and Stein (2017) argued that the theory's emphasis on retribution does not inherently rule out more prosocial reactions. However, they stopped short of articulating how the framework might be extended to systematically encompass such empathic responses.

Nonetheless, other scholars within the literature on third-party perceptions of mistreatment have drawn on deonance theory as a conceptual foundation to explore how observers may respond to victims. In particular, recent work has proposed that witnessing mistreatment may evoke empathy for victims on the part of third parties (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Hu et al., 2022; Liang & Park, 2022a). Empathy is typically defined as an emotional response to the perceived suffering or well-being of another (Batson et al., 2002). Like anger, it is considered a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003). When understood as an emotion—as opposed to a trait or cognitive process—empathy manifests through feelings of sympathy, compassion, and tenderness toward those who are harmed (Batson et al., 1997).
text:
In contrast to its emphasis on anger-fueled behaviors against perpetrators, deonance theory says surprisingly little about how third parties should react to the victims of mistreatment. Its two most expansive formulations do not address reactions to victims at all (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Likewise, other more narrowly focused formulations omit discussion of victim-focused reactions (Folger, 2012; Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger & Shukla, 2019; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005). Other formulations include brief and tangential references to victims. For example, Folger (2001) noted that deonance could involve others’ welfare but that observation seemed bounded in the context of preserving the moral order through retribution. Folger and Skarlicki (2008) noted (parenthetically) that third parties could react to mistreatment by providing emotional support for victims, but they did not develop that suggestion using deonance theory’s logic. More recently, Folger and Stein (2017) reacted to a critique of deonance theory that argued that its logic could leave individuals “relatively indifferent to the other parties involved in the immediate situation” (Korsgaard et al., 2015, p. 388). Although Folger and Stein (2017) argued that the retributive emphasis of deonance theory need not preclude more prosocial reactions, they did not describe how the theory could be extended to include them.

Nevertheless, other scholars in the third-party perceptions of mistreatment literature have used deonance theory as a jumping-off point to explain how observers might react to the victims of mistreatment. In particular, scholars have suggested that third parties might react to observing mistreatment by feeling empathy toward the victims (Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Hu et al., 2022; Liang & Park, 2022a). Empathy can be defined as an emotion felt in response to the perceived welfare of another (Batson et al., 2002). Like anger, it can be classified as a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003). When operationalized as an emotion—rather than a personality trait or cognition—empathy is characterized by feelings of sympathy, compassion, and tenderness toward the person who is suffering (Batson et al., 1997).

Research on product categories and the processes of categorization has gained significant momentum, as evidenced by a rapidly expanding body of scholarship (for comprehensive reviews, see Cattani, Porac, & Thomas, 2017; Durand & Khaire, 2017; Durand & Thornton, 2018; Hannan et al., 2019). As a meaningful “conceptual system” that underpins market exchange (Rosa, Porac, Runser-Spanjol, & Saxon, 1999: 64), categories are valuable because they enable actors to position and differentiate their offerings (Navis & Glynn, 2010; Porac, Thomas, Wilson, Paton, & Kanfer, 1995; Zhao, Fisher, Lounsbury, & Miller, 2017), while allowing audiences to identify, evaluate, and lend legitimacy or support to those offerings (Navis & Glynn, 2011; Pontikes, 2012; Wry, Lounsbury, & Jennings, 2014). Crucially, products also become entangled with the trajectory of the category they inhabit—categories may fail to gain traction or ultimately dissolve (Aldrich & Fiol, 1994; Kennedy & Fiss, 2013). In essence, a product’s success is often contingent on the sustainability and visibility of its category—specifically, whether the category “remains in collective awareness and active use” (Lo, Fiss, Rhee, & Kennedy, 2020: 87).

A core focus of this literature is understanding how category viability evolves over time (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Lee, Struben, & Bingham, 2018; Wry, Lounsbury, & Glynn, 2011). Early studies often relied on a life cycle perspective, positing that categories emerge, grow, and stabilize (Kennedy, 2008; Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2010; Wry et al., 2011), before potentially declining or disappearing (Kuilman & van Driel, 2013). However, more recent research questions the assumption that category viability ever reaches a stable endpoint (Hsu & Grodal, 2015; Kennedy, Lo, & Lounsbury, 2010; Soublière & Gehman, 2020). Scholars have begun to explore how actors renew categories by introducing novel products that defy category norms (Rao, Monin, & Durand, 2003; Wry et al., 2014; Zhao, Ishihara, Jennings, & Lounsbury, 2018), or by renegotiating the meanings that constitute the category itself (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Grodal, 2018; Lockwood, Glynn, & Giorgi, 2023; Wry et al., 2011). In short, entrepreneurial activity within a category can continually and dynamically shape its viability.
text:
Research on product categories and categorization processes is on the rise, as is evident from the rapidly growing body of studies (for reviews, see Cattani, Porac, & Thomas, 2017; Durand & Khaire, 2017; Durand & Thornton, 2018; Hannan et al., 2019). As the meaningful “conceptual system” that facilitates market exchanges (Rosa, Porac, Runser-Spanjol, & Saxon, 1999: 64), categories are useful because they form the basis by which actors position and differentiate their products (Navis & Glynn, 2010; Porac, Thomas, Wilson, Paton, & Kanfer, 1995; Zhao, Fisher, Lounsbury, & Miller, 2017), and interested audiences identify, assess, and grant their support to products (Navis & Glynn, 2011; Pontikes, 2012; Wry, Lounsbury, & Jennings, 2014). Even more importantly, products also share a common fate with the category they enter, which may fail to emerge or eventually collapse (Aldrich & Fiol, 1994; Kennedy & Fiss, 2013). Indeed, the success of any product is tied to the viability of its category—that is, whether the category “remains in collective awareness and active use” (Lo, Fiss, Rhee, & Kennedy, 2020: 87).

Within this literature, a central concern has been to understand how the viability of categories changes over time (Glynn & Navis, 2013; Lee, Struben, & Bingham, 2018; Wry, Lounsbury, & Glynn, 2011). Early scholarship has often drawn on a life cycle model, whereby a category first emerges and then grows to become established (Kennedy, 2008; Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2010; Wry et al., 2011), but sometimes declines or disappears (Kuilman & van Driel, 2013). More recently, studies have begun to question whether the viability of a category ever really settles (Hsu & Grodal, 2015; Kennedy, Lo, & Lounsbury, 2010; Soublière & Gehman, 2020). For instance, scholars have investigated how actors infuse categories with new products that challenge conventions (Rao, Monin, & Durand, 2003; Wry et al., 2014; Zhao, Ishihara, Jennings, & Lounsbury, 2018), and renew categories by renegotiating their meanings (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Grodal, 2018; Lockwood, Glynn, & Giorgi, 2023; Wry et al., 2011). Simply put, entrepreneurial action within a category has the potential to dynamically alter its viability.

Our theoretical framework begins with the assumption that stakeholders recognize the heterogeneity of firms—that is, they understand that there are both high- and low-quality firms. However, in the absence of sufficient information to distinguish among them, stakeholders tend to err on the side of caution and assume the worst. This reflects the logic of Akerlof’s (1970) “lemons problem,” where buyers lacking reliable information cannot differentiate between high-quality goods and poor-quality ones (i.e., “lemons”), and consequently treat all goods as lemons to mitigate the risk of overpaying for a faulty product. In markets, quality signals help address this challenge; similarly, in organizational contexts, information enables stakeholders to discern between trustworthy and untrustworthy firms, instead of defaulting to a uniformly negative perception.

Stakeholders access information about firms either directly—through firm–stakeholder communication—or indirectly, via media reports. While each of these information channels helps reduce asymmetries, their combined influence has often been understudied. To better understand how direct dialogue interacts with the broader informational environment to influence stakeholder evaluations, we examine the effects of firm–stakeholder dialogue in relation to three key features of the media landscape: (a) the overall availability of media coverage about the firm, (b) the degree to which media content addresses contentious environmental, social, and governance (ESG) issues, and (c) the perceived reliability of media sources. We argue that firm–stakeholder dialogue exerts the strongest effect on stakeholder judgments under conditions of high information asymmetry—when public information is scarce, when it lacks ESG relevance, or when its credibility is undermined by censorship or misinformation. To assess these dynamics empirically, we draw on an original, hand-coded dataset capturing all media-reported interactions between 2,454 stakeholders and firms operating 26 mining sites globally, along with the stakeholders’ corresponding evaluations of each firm. We also supplement our analysis with a case illustration of a company’s attempts to develop a large open-pit gold mine at Rosia Montana in Romania, which exemplifies the interplay between dialogue and information availability in shaping stakeholder responses.
text:
Our theoretical framework begins from the premise that stakeholders are aware that firms are heterogenous (i.e., that there are good and bad firms) and that without enough information to differentiate between different types, stakeholders assume the worst about a firm. Akerlof (1970) described this as “the lemons problem”: buyers who lack information cannot distinguish good-quality products from bad-quality products (“lemons”), and thus converge on the assumption that all products on the market are lemons in order to avoid purchasing a lemon at the price of a good-quality product. Information about sellers and their products helps buyers differentiate between products and overcome the lemons problem. Similarly, information about firms helps stakeholders distinguish good from bad firms, instead of assuming that all firms are “lemons.”

Stakeholders obtain information about firms either directly from the firm, through firm–stakeholder dialogue, or indirectly, from the media. While both firm–stakeholder dialogue and media information help overcome information asymmetries, their interplay is often overlooked. To understand how firm–stakeholder dialogue and the informational environment within which this dialogue is embedded interact to shape stakeholder evaluations, we evaluate the impact of firm–stakeholder dialogue as a function of (a) the availability of media information, (b) the content of media information, and (c) the reliability of media information. We propose that firm–stakeholder dialogue has a greater impact on stakeholder evaluations when information asymmetries are most pronounced: when stakeholders can find little public information about a firm (i.e., when the availability of information is low); when available information is not focused on controversial environmental, social, and governance (ESG) issues (i.e., when ESG content is low); and when the reliability of available information is questionable because of media censorship or misinformation (i.e., when the reliability of information is low). We analyze these effects using original, manually coded stakeholder-level data that capture all media-reported firm–stakeholder interactions between 2,454 stakeholders and the firms operating 26 mines around the world, and these stakeholders’ evaluations about the mining firms. We also illustrate the effects we observe with the example of one company’s efforts to build a large open-pit gold mine at Rosia Montana in Romania.

As status perceptions are inherently relational (Hubbard, Pollock, Pfarrer, & Rindova, 2018; Podolny & Phillips, 1996), understanding how the media interprets and responds to a particular actor’s status necessitates considering the broader context, including other actors in the environment—such as previous transgressors—who themselves possess varying degrees of status (Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Han & Pollock, 2021). This relational nature is important because audiences’ evaluations of one actor are often shaped by how they perceive others (Boivie, Graffin, & Gentry, 2016), and the conduct of high-status actors frequently establishes a benchmark for assessing the behavior of others (Rao, Monin, & Durand, 2005). However, the influence of prior wrongdoers becomes more complex when examining conflicting perspectives on how widespread misconduct affects attention toward new cases. On one hand, as misconduct becomes more frequent, its novelty and perceived severity may diminish, prompting less media attention per incident and promoting a kind of “safety in numbers” (Ahmadjian & Robinson, 2001; Pollock, Rindova, & Maggitti, 2008; Zavyalova et al., 2012). On the other hand, when misconduct becomes widespread, audiences—including journalists (Shoemaker & Reese, 2013)—may detect troubling patterns of deviation, leading them to intensify their scrutiny of new transgressions (Desai, 2011; Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Han, Pollock, & Paruchuri, 2023; Naumovska, Zajac, & Lee, 2021; Rindova, Ferrier, & Wiltbank, 2010; Whitson & Galinsky, 2008). Despite appearing contradictory, both perspectives underscore the significant role of prior transgressors in shaping audience reactions.

To reconcile these competing views, we examine how the prevalence of misconduct by high-status transgressors—both within and outside the focal actor’s industry (hereafter referred to as high-status “insiders” and “outsiders,” respectively)—influences the scandalization of new misconduct events. The status of these previous offenders is critical because it shapes how media routines operate (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020). Journalists, while naturally drawn to novelty (Chandler, Polidoro, & Yang, 2020; Zavyalova et al., 2012), operate under constraints of limited attention and must assess whether an event merits coverage (Pollock et al., 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013). When high-status firms engage in repeated misconduct—each instance of which is likely to generate significant visibility (Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin et al., 2013)—these violations can amplify, rather than dilute, the salience of misconduct (Paruchuri, Han, & Prakash, 2021), reinforcing journalists' perceptions of newsworthiness (Pollock et al., 2008) and increasing the likelihood that similar new incidents receive heightened attention.
text:
As status perceptions are inherently relational (Hubbard, Pollock, Pfarrer, & Rindova, 2018; Podolny & Phillips, 1996), understanding how the media interprets and reacts to an actor’s status requires considering other actors in its environment—such as past transgressors—who, of course, have their own status (Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Han & Pollock, 2021). This is because audiences’ evaluations of one actor can influence how they evaluate another actor (Boivie, Graffin, & Gentry, 2016), and high-status actors’ behaviors often serve as the basis for evaluating others’ behaviors (Rao, Monin, & Durand, 2005). Yet, the influence of past transgressors is complicated when considering the divergent views on how misconduct prevalence affects the attention allocated to a firm’s current misconduct (Ahmadjian & Robinson, 2001; Zavyalova, Pfarrer, Reger, & Shapiro, 2012). While increasing prevalence can deprive misconduct of its novelty and salience, reducing the attention paid to each instance and creating “safety in numbers” (Pollock, Rindova, & Maggitti, 2008; Zavyalova et al., 2012), audiences—including journalists (Shoemaker & Reese, 2013)—can also sense a pattern in the misconduct’s proliferation (Rindova, Ferrier, & Wiltbank, 2010; Whitson & Galinsky, 2008), leading them to scrutinize new instances more (Desai, 2011; Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Han, Pollock, & Paruchuri, 2023; Naumovska, Zajac, & Lee, 2021). Although seemingly contradictory, both views nonetheless highlight the significant influence past transgressors have on audiences’ evaluations.

We disentangle these divergent views and offer a clearer understanding of when and how the prevalence of others’ misconduct leads to each dynamic by exploring how high-status past transgressors inside and outside the focal transgressor’s industry (hereafter, high-status “insiders” and “outsiders,” for brevity) each shape misconduct scandalization. Past transgressors’ status matters because it shapes media routines (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020). While journalists are biased toward novel cues (Chandler, Polidoro, & Yang, 2020; Zavyalova et al., 2012), they have limited attentional resources and thus seek assurances that a topic is newsworthy (Pollock et al., 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013). High-status firms’ repeated misconduct—each instance of which could potentially incur significant publicity (Adut, 2005, 2008; Graffin et al., 2013)—reinforces rather than reduces the misconduct’s salience (Paruchuri, Han, & Prakash, 2021), and assures journalists the new instance is newsworthy (Pollock et al., 2008).

This study offers several important contributions to the literature on conflict and multiteam systems (MTS). First, it helps reconcile mixed findings in the individual-level conflict literature by aligning both the theoretical lens and empirical focus at the intra-individual level. Specifically, we propose an intra-individual framework that uncovers the microfoundations of conflict within collectives. This approach affirms and extends the emerging shift in conflict research—from viewing conflict primarily as a shared team-level perception to recognizing diverse individual conflict experiences within teams (Park et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2021). It also builds on a growing consensus that advancing conflict research requires deeper engagement with its microfoundations, particularly given the substantial variance that exists at the intra-individual level (61.5% in Study 1 and 52.9% in Study 2). Put simply, a traditional inter-individual approach may obscure meaningful differences that emerge when studying conflict at the intra-individual level. Our cognitive process framework addresses this gap by explaining when and why certain conflict experiences enhance or hinder individual performance. We do so by focusing on self-regulatory processes related to attentional focus, which are influenced by key structural properties—namely, the direction and boundary—of conflict experiences. This framework encourages the conflict literature to shift beyond inter-individual comparisons and attend to variations within individuals themselves.

Second, our study advances the theoretical understanding of the cognitive mechanisms that connect conflict to performance. Prior research has produced inconsistent conclusions about whether conflict undermines team cognition (de Wit, Jehn, & Scheepers, 2013) or fosters collaborative problem-solving (Schulz-Hardt, Brodbeck, Mojzisch, Kerschreiter, & Frey, 2006). We build on this conversation by demonstrating how specific structural aspects of conflict experiences shape attentional focus at the individual level. First, the direction of conflict determines the trajectory of self-regulatory processes. For individuals initiating conflict (senders), articulating conflict enhances on-task attentional engagement by redirecting cognitive resources from the conflict issue back to the task—resulting in improved task focus. In contrast, for those on the receiving end, conflict often functions as an off-task cognitive distraction, leading to diminished task engagement. Second, the strength of these effects is influenced by the boundary of the conflict—whether it occurs within or between teams. Overall, our dual-path model of attentional focus provides a theoretical explanation for the intra-individual variation observed in conflict's impact on task performance.
text:
This study makes several contributions to the conflict and MTS literatures. First, our study helps to resolve the mixed findings in the individual conflict literature by aligning the level of theory and phenomena at the intra-individual level. More specifically, we advance an intra-individual framework to illuminate the microfoundations of conflict in collectives. This approach affirms and expands the recent shift from conceptualizing conflict as a shared perception at the team level to different conflict experiences among members (Park et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2021). It also builds on the emerging consensus that examining microfoundations is necessary to move the study of conflict forward to explore the substantial amount of variance at the intra-individual level (61.5% in Study 1 and 52.9% in Study 2). Stated differently, taking the traditional inter-individual focus approach masks the differing effects present when examining intra-individual conflict experiences. Our intra-individual cognitive process framework addresses the mixed effects by explaining when and why some conflict experiences help while others hurt individual performance via self-regulatory processes associated with attentional focus—as altered by the structural elements (i.e., directions and boundaries) of conflict experiences. This challenges the conflict literature to consider not only the potential for inter-individual differences but also for intra-individual differences.

Second, this study develops stronger theory on the cognitive mechanisms linking conflict and performance. The extant literature presents mixed findings regarding whether conflict is largely detrimental to cognitive processes in teams (de Wit, Jehn, & Scheepers, 2013) or may enhance collective problem-solving (Schulz-Hardt, Brodbeck, Mojzisch, Kerschreiter, & Frey, 2006). We extend this existing theory by showing how and why structural elements of conflict experiences impact individual attentional focus. First, the direction of conflict experiences governs the path of self-regulatory processes. For senders, expressing conflict enhanced their on-task attentional pull by shifting their cognitive engagement from the conflict issues back to their ongoing task—which positively impacts task engagement. Alternatively, for receivers, conflict issues become largely off-task cognitive demands—which negatively impacts task engagement. Next, the magnitudes of these paths are altered by the boundary of conflict experiences (within team vs. between team). In short, our dual-path attentional focus model offers a theoretical explanation for the intra-individual variances in conflict–task performance relationships.

Attribution theory posits that individuals have a natural tendency to seek explanations for ambiguous behavior. It underscores the influential role that inferred attributions play in shaping observers' responses and subsequent evaluations of the actor in question. One key way people interpret another’s behavior is by assessing whether it aligns with the individual’s past actions (Kelley, 1973). When behavior appears consistent over time, observers tend to attribute it to the person’s enduring traits or dispositions. Conversely, when behavior is inconsistent, they are more likely to interpret it as a reflection of the person’s specific attitudes or reactions to a given context.

To illustrate, consider a scenario in which your colleagues are discussing racial injustice, and Meredith, your White female manager, remains silent during the conversation. How might you, as an observer, interpret Meredith’s silence? According to attribution theory, if Meredith has previously remained silent about injustices—whether they involve her own group or another—then her current silence may be seen as consistent behavior, suggesting it stems from a stable personal trait, such as a tendency to remain quiet at work. In contrast, if Meredith has a history of speaking up about injustices in general but chooses not to speak on this occasion, her silence would be construed as inconsistent behavior. In this case, observers are more likely to conclude that her silence reflects a specific attitude toward this particular issue—perhaps discomfort with racial injustice or ambivalence toward the affected group.

In this way, attribution theory implies that when a manager exhibits what we call selective silence—the decision to remain silent in certain situations but not others—rather than total silence—remaining quiet across all situations—observers are more likely to infer that the silence stems from the manager’s views on a specific issue, rather than a general disposition to avoid speaking up. However, attribution theory does not specify the exact assumptions people might make about a manager’s intergroup attitudes. Here, social identity theory provides additional insight by suggesting how observers form judgments about whether a manager may hold intergroup bias or be supportive of social justice more broadly—and thus, how unsupportive they may appear toward an outgroup.
text:
Attribution theory suggests that observers possess an inherent tendency to seek the cause of ambiguous behavior. Attribution theory also highlights the importance that inferred attributions have in influencing reactions to and subsequent evaluations of an actor. One way people make sense of another’s behavior is by examining whether an individual is perceived to behave in a way consistent with their past behavior (Kelley, 1973). When people behave consistently, observers tend to attribute behavior to stable properties of an individual. In contrast, when people behave inconsistently, observers tend to attribute behavior to attitudes or reactions to a particular context.

For example, imagine your colleagues are discussing racial injustice and Meredith, your White female manager, remains silent on this outgroup injustice. To what extent will you, the observer, think that Meredith does not support the outgroup? Attribution theory suggests if Meredith has remained silent in the past (either on an ingroup or an outgroup injustice) and she is now silent on an outgroup injustice (racial injustice), she has engaged consistently and thus her silence would be attributed to her general tendencies or personality (e.g., Meredith is silent at work, even on issues that may directly affect her). However, if Meredith has spoken up in the past (either on an ingroup or outgroup injustice) and is now silent on an outgroup injustice (racial injustice), she has engaged inconsistently, and employees would attribute her silence to her attitudes toward the issue in question (e.g., there is something about racial injustice that makes Meredith silent).

Thus, attribution theory suggests that when a manager engages in what we term selective silence (withholding of speaking up under certain situations but not others) compared to total silence (withholding of speaking up under all circumstances), observers will more likely attribute the silence to the manager’s underlying attitudes toward the particular issue (compared to the manager’s general tendencies of simply remaining silent at work). However, attribution theory does not outline the specific assumptions people will make about a manager’s underlying intergroup attitudes. Social identity theory helps explain whether observers will presume that a manager harbors intergroup bias or is generally supportive of social justice, and thus, the extent to which a manager is unsupportive of an outgroup.

use: Building on the recategorization theory outlined above, we propose that incumbents are likely to experience cognitive dissonance when they observe that a demographically different director also shares salient demographic similarities with them. The combination of perceived similarities and differences is likely to cause discomfort among incumbents, compelling them to either classify the director as an out-group member or to recategorize them as part of a more inclusive in-group. When a director differs from incumbents along certain dissimilar demographic dimensions, the likelihood that incumbents will recategorize them as an in-group member increases if the director is also similar to them along other meaningful dimensions. For example, when Dina Dublon—a young, female executive—was appointed to the board of Hartford Financial Services Group in 1999, she shared important characteristics with incumbent directors, such as a top executive background and expertise in finance. This alignment was publicly acknowledged by the CEO and chairman, who emphasized that “Dina Dublon's expertise in strategy and finance makes her an excellent addition to The Hartford’s board” (PR Newswire, 1999). Conversely, when controlling for a director’s shared demographic similarities, incumbents will find recategorization less feasible when that director is markedly different along other dissimilar dimensions. For instance, a Hispanic director is less likely to be recategorized as an in-group member by an all-white board than by a board that includes multiple Hispanic members, such as the board of Alcoa, Inc. These examples suggest that a demographically different director is more likely to be perceived as an in-group member by incumbents when they also exhibit greater similarity to them in other shared demographic respects. Because incumbents tend to prefer new directors they can more easily recategorize as in-group members, we propose the following: Hypothesis 1: The greater a new director’s similarity to incumbents along shared demographic dimensions, the greater that director’s difference from them along dissimilar dimensions.
text: Following the recategorization theory above, we suggest that incumbents are likely to experience cognitive dissonance when they recognize that a demographically different director is similar to them along other salient demographic dimensions. The simultaneously perceived similarities and differences of the director are likely to make incumbents feel uncomfortable, prompting them either to view the director as an out-group member or recategorize the director as an in-group member. Holding a director's difference from incumbents along dissimilar dimensions constant, incumbents should be more able and likely to recategorize the director as a member of a more inclusive in-group when the director is more similar to them along other shared dimensions. For instance, when Dina Dublon, a young female executive, was appointed to the board of Hartford Financial Services Group in 1999, she was similar to the majority of incumbent directors in top executive background and functional area of expertise (finance). The CEO and chairman of Hartford publicly highlighted that incumbents' favorable views toward her were in part based on their observation that "Dina Dublon's expertise in strategy and finance makes her an excellent addition to The Hartford's board" (PR Newswire, 1999). Holding a director's similarity to incumbents along shared dimensions constant, incumbents are less able and likely to recategorize a new director as a member of a more inclusive in-group when the director is more different from them along dissimilar dimensions. For instance, a Hispanic director is less likely to be recategorized as an in-group member by a board composed entirely of white directors than by a board with several Hispanic directors, e.g., the board of Alcoa, Inc. These arguments suggest that a director who differs more from incumbents along dissimilar dimensions will be recategorized as an in-group member to the extent that the director is more similar to incumbents along other shared demographic dimensions. Because incumbents tend to select a new director who can be recategorized as a more favorable in-group member, we expect that a new director's demographic difference from incumbents along dissimilar dimensions will be positively predicted by his or her similarities to them along shared dimensions: Hypothesis 1: The greater a new director's similarity to incumbents along shared demographic dimensions, the greater the new director's difference from them along dissimilar dimensions.

The significance of stakeholders’ evaluations is well-established across various strands of management research, including work on corporate reputation (Fombrun & Shanley, 1990; Rindova et al., 2005; Zavyalova, Pfarrer, Reger, & Hubbard, 2016), impression management (Bansal & Clelland, 2004; Elsbach & Sutton, 1992; Graffin et al., 2016), and stakeholder management (Freeman, 1984; Freeman, Harrison, Wicks, Parmar, & de Colle, 2010). Positive stakeholder assessments foster greater cooperation, enhancing firms’ competitive advantage (Barney, 2018; Harrison et al., 2010; Jones et al., 2018) and improving organizational performance (Godfrey, Merrill, & Hansen, 2009; Henisz et al., 2014). Conversely, negative evaluations hinder firms’ access to critical resources and partnerships (Park & Rogan, 2019; Yu, Sengul, & Lester, 2008).

A core feature of the firm–stakeholder relationship is the presence of information asymmetry (Crilly, Zollo, & Hansen, 2012; Hill & Jones, 1992). Across all types of exchange, stakeholders face uncertainty about a firm’s quality or capability (Akerlof, 1970; Mishina, Block, & Mannor, 2012; Rindova et al., 2005), as well as about its character or likelihood to act opportunistically (Arrow, 1969; Williamson, 1975). Akerlof (1970) famously argued that such asymmetries lead to adverse selection, where markets deteriorate because buyers, unable to reliably distinguish between high- and low-quality products ("lemons"), are only willing to pay the price of the inferior goods.

This logic extends beyond markets for physical goods. Similar to Akerlof’s buyers, stakeholders lacking reliable information may default to assuming the worst—believing the firm is of low quality or untrustworthy—and thus choose not to engage. For a firm to secure the cooperation of stakeholders and gain access to vital resources, it must actively reduce this information asymmetry (Jones, 1995). Overcoming the "lemons problem" involves offering credible signals of both its capabilities and integrity. Firms may do this by issuing required or voluntary disclosures (Healy & Palepu, 2001; Kim & Lyon, 2014; Marquis, Toffel, & Zhou, 2016; Reid & Toffel, 2009), obtaining third-party certifications and ratings (Christmann & Taylor, 2006; King, Lenox, & Terlaak, 2005; Terlaak & King, 2006), and disseminating such information through media channels. Crucially, firms can further reduce information gaps through direct engagement and dialogue with stakeholders, fostering mutual understanding and trust.
text:
The importance of stakeholders’ evaluations has been stressed by different management literatures, including research on corporate reputation (Fombrun & Shanley, 1990; Rindova et al., 2005; Zavyalova, Pfarrer, Reger, & Hubbard, 2016), impression management (Bansal & Clelland, 2004; Elsbach & Sutton, 1992; Graffin et al., 2016), and stakeholder management (Freeman, 1984; Freeman, Harrison, Wicks, Parmar, & de Colle, 2010). Positive evaluations increase stakeholders’ cooperation and, thus, firms’ competitive advantage (Barney, 2018; Harrison et al., 2010; Jones et al., 2018) and performance (Godfrey, Merrill, & Hansen, 2009; Henisz et al., 2014), while the deterioration of stakeholder evaluations impedes firms’ access to resources and collaborations (Park & Rogan, 2019; Yu, Sengul, & Lester, 2008).

A defining aspect of the relationship between a firm and a stakeholder is the asymmetry of information between them (Crilly, Zollo, & Hansen, 2012; Hill & Jones, 1992). In all exchange relationships, participants face some degree of uncertainty about their partners’ quality or capability (Akerlof, 1970; Mishina, Block, & Mannor, 2012; Rindova et al., 2005) and their character or propensity to behave opportunistically (Arrow, 1969; Williamson, 1975). Akerlof (1970) argued that information asymmetries lead to quality deterioration in markets because buyers who cannot distinguish between high-quality products and low-quality products (lemons) are only willing to pay the price of the low-quality products.

Akerlof’s argument applies broadly. Just like the buyers in Akerlof’s model, stakeholders with limited information about a firm are likely to assume the worse—that is, that the firm is a bad firm or lemon—and to refrain from participating in exchanges with it. Thus, a firm seeking access to resources that can only be obtained through the cooperation of stakeholders needs to invest in reducing the asymmetry of information between itself and its stakeholders (Jones, 1995). It needs to overcome the lemons problem by demonstrating that its capabilities and character can be trusted. The firm can accomplish this through mandated or voluntary information disclosures (Healy & Palepu, 2001; Kim & Lyon, 2014; Marquis, Toffel, & Zhou, 2016; Reid & Toffel, 2009), including third-party certification and ratings (Christmann & Taylor, 2006; King, Lenox, & Terlaak, 2005; Terlaak & King, 2006), that are disseminated through media channels. Importantly, a firm can also reduce information asymmetries through direct interactions that establish dialogue between the firm and its stakeholders.

Although, on average, only about two incumbents per board share prior board appointments with a demographically different incoming director, these incumbents can play a pivotal role in influencing how other board members perceive and categorize the newcomer. Existing research on director appointments indicates that directors with prior ties to a candidate are particularly likely to be consulted during the selection process (Lorsch & MacIver, 1989; Westphal & Stern, 2006, 2007). Building on this, we argue that incumbents who have served alongside demographically different directors are more likely to stress the candidate's similarities to other directors and downplay their differences. Such incumbents may begin to emphasize shared qualities even before the formal selection process begins, thereby increasing the perceived alignment between the new director and the rest of the board. Moreover, by drawing on their previous interactions, these incumbents can reassure others that any demographic differences are inconsequential. These informal endorsements are likely to enhance fellow directors’ willingness to reclassify the new director as a socially favorable in-group member. As the number of incumbents with prior board experience alongside the diverse candidate increases, so too does the likelihood that the candidate’s similarities to incumbents are foregrounded, and their dissimilarities minimized.

This perspective suggests that a new director’s perceived similarity to incumbent directors along certain demographic dimensions enables them to differ more substantially along other dimensions, particularly when more incumbents have shared prior board experience with the director. While we have proposed that a director’s surface-level similarity to incumbents encourages acceptance despite deeper-level differences, we further argue that such acceptance is enhanced by shared board history. Therefore, we predict a positive interaction between prior common board appointments and demographic similarity: Hypothesis 2 – The more prior common board appointments a new director has with incumbents, the stronger the positive relationship between the new director’s similarity to incumbents along shared demographic dimensions and their difference from incumbents along dissimilar demographic dimensions.
text: Although on average only about two incumbents per board have prior common board appointments with a demographically different new director, they can significantly influence other incumbents' tendency to recategorize the director as a more inclusive in-group member. Research on director appointments suggests that directors who have prior ties to a director candidate are especially likely to be consulted by fellow directors during the director selection process (Lorsch and MacIver, 1989; Westphal and Stern, 2006, 2007). Our argument above suggests that incumbents who have prior common board appointments with a demographically different director are likely to emphasize the director's similarities to fellow directors while trivializing his or her dissimilarities. They may highlight the director's similarity to other incumbents, possibly even before the selection process starts, thus increasing the salience of the director's similarity to other incumbents and facilitating their recategorization of the director. In addition, they can assure other directors that the minority director's demographic differences are of trivial concern based on their interactions with him or her on other boards. Such conversations should increase fellow directors' tendency to recategorize the director as a more favorable in-group member. The more that incumbents have prior common board appointments with a demographically different director, the more likely the director's similarity to incumbents will be highlighted and the less likely his or her dissimilarity will become a major concern for other incumbents. These arguments suggest that a new director's degree of similarity to incumbents along shared demographic dimensions will allow him or her to be more different from them along other dissimilar dimensions and still be recategorized as an in-group member when the new director has more prior common board appointments with incumbents. Thus, although we have suggested that a new director's similarity to incumbents along shared dimensions positively predicts the director's difference from them along dissimilar dimensions, a new director's similarity to incumbents along shared dimensions will be positively related to a higher degree of difference along dissimilar dimensions when the new director has more prior common board appointments with incumbents: Hypothesis 2: The more a new director has prior common board appointments with incumbents, the more positive the relationship between the director's similarity to incumbents along shared demographic dimensions and his or her difference from them along dissimilar dimensions.

Notably, our integrative model incorporates moderators intended to capture the conditions under which the predicted relationships between third-party perceptions of mistreatment and emotional responses become stronger or weaker. While deonance theory typically does not emphasize moderating variables, Folger and Glerum (2015) have suggested that contextual factors can function as “switching variables”—setting the boundary conditions for the theory’s main propositions. One particularly salient contextual factor in third-party research is the relative power of the individuals involved (O’Reilly & Aquino, 2011). For instance, third parties may have less power than the perpetrator—such as when observing a manager engaging in abusive supervision—equal power—such as witnessing a peer's incivility—or more power—such as watching a customer harass an employee. Similarly, the third party’s power relative to the victim can vary: they might have more power (e.g., observing a customer being mistreated), equal power (e.g., witnessing a peer being abused by a supervisor), or less power (e.g., seeing a manager mistreated by a subordinate). Accordingly, Figure 1 illustrates relative power as a potential “switching variable,” including the third party's power in relation to both the perpetrator and the victim as moderating factors.

This work offers significant theoretical and empirical contributions to the literature on third-party perceptions of mistreatment. From a theoretical standpoint, using deonance theory as the prevailing lens for explaining third-party reactions invites three avenues for critical reflection or “problematization” (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011). First, the theory prioritizes anger toward the perpetrator as the core deontic emotion (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008), leaving less theoretical space for other emotional responses, such as empathy for the victim, to be considered equally central. Second, the theory posits that emotional reactions to witnessing mistreatment are evolutionarily adaptive because they encourage behavior that helps restore a moral balance (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Emotions like schadenfreude, which may lead to support for the perpetrator or harm to the victim, challenge this assumption of moral reinforcement. Third, the theory places strong emphasis on emotion as the direct, automatic driver of resulting behavior—casting such actions as impulsive and unreflective (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). This focus limits the consideration of more deliberative processes, in which emotions might influence behavior indirectly through cognitive evaluations of the involved parties.
text:
Notably, our integrative model includes moderators that attempt to capture when the predicted relationships between third-party perceptions of mistreatment and emotions should be stronger or weaker. Although deonance theory does not generally emphasize moderators, Folger and Glerum (2015) noted that contextual characteristics could act as “switching variables” that serve as boundary conditions for the theory’s propositions. A salient contextual characteristic in third-party research is the relative power of the parties involved (O’Reilly & Aquino, 2011). Third parties may have less power than perpetrators (e.g., observing a manager engaging in abusive supervision), the same power as perpetrators (e.g., observing a coworker engaging in incivility), or more power than perpetrators (e.g., observing a customer engaging in sexual harassment). Likewise, third parties may have more power than victims (e.g., observing a customer being treated unfairly), the same power as victims (e.g., observing a coworker receiving abusive supervision), or less power than victims (e.g., observing a manager being treated uncivilly). Figure 1 therefore explores relative power as a potential “switching variable” by including the third party’s relative power to the perpetrator and victim as moderators.

Our work makes important theoretical and empirical contributions to the literature on third-party perceptions of mistreatment. Theoretically speaking, the reliance on deonance theory as the dominant lens for explaining third-party reactions can be potentially “problematized” (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011) on three grounds. First, the theory’s formulations elevate anger toward the perpetrator as the signature element of the deontic response (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). That elevation leaves fewer reasons to expect empathy toward the victim to emerge as well. Second, the theory argues that emotional reactions to observing mistreatment should be evolutionarily adaptive by encouraging behaviors that reaffirm the moral order (Folger & Cropanzano, 2010; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). A response like schadenfreude is less adaptive in that regard, particularly if it is associated with prosocial behavior toward the perpetrator or antisocial behavior toward the victim. Third, the theory’s formulations emphasize emotion as the driver of behavior—describing resulting actions as impulsive, automatic, and not thoroughly considered (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Skarlicki, 2005, 2008). That emphasis leaves fewer reasons to expect emotions to influence behaviors indirectly through evaluations of the parties involved.

Although pay-for-performance (PFP) is widely employed by organizations to motivate employee effort, enhance performance, and attract and retain talent (Gerhart & Newman, 2020), its impact on creative performance remains highly contested (e.g., Byron & Khazanchi, 2012; deCharms, 1968; Deci et al., 2017; Eisenberger et al., 1999). Classical motivation theories—such as reinforcement theory (Skinner, 1953), equity theory (Adams, 1963), and expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964)—propose that PFP can positively influence performance, including creative tasks. These models suggest that intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives can have additive effects, thereby enhancing outcomes (Gupta & Shaw, 1998; Jenkins et al., 1998; Kim et al., 2022). In contrast, traditional creativity theory, rooted in cognitive evaluation theory (CET) and later self-determination theory (SDT), argues that PFP can suppress creativity (Amabile, 1983a, 1983b, 1998; Hennessey, 1989; Lepper et al., 1973). These perspectives contend that extrinsic rewards often diminish intrinsic motivation by being perceived as controlling, thereby undermining self-determination, feelings of competence, and autonomy (Deci et al., 1999; Ryan & Deci, 2017). Thus, from this viewpoint, PFP may hinder creative performance by weakening the intrinsic drive considered essential to creativity.

Nevertheless, Amabile and colleagues later revised this stance, introducing a "radically modified" creativity theory suggesting that extrinsic rewards can in fact enhance both intrinsic motivation and creativity—provided they are delivered in a way that supports autonomy and conveys meaningful information, and especially when intrinsic motivation is already high (Amabile & Pratt, 2016, p. 164). Despite this evolution in theory, scholarly disagreement continues regarding the appropriateness of PFP in roles requiring creativity (Deci et al., 2017; Howard et al., 2017; Ryan & Deci, 2017). This enduring theoretical divide is particularly consequential given that creativity is widely recognized as essential to organizational innovation, adaptability, and long-term competitiveness (George, 2007; Mumford & Gustafson, 1988; Shalley et al., 2004; Zhou & Hoever, 2014). The debate has also prominently entered popular discourse, with well-known books and TED Talks arguing that PFP adversely affects creativity—claiming, for instance, that “pay-for-performance schemes … usually don’t work and often do harm” (Pink, 2011), “to increase creativity, slash your budget in half” (Schaefer, 2018), or “how money kills creativity” (Meyer, 2019). Such viewpoints underscore the urgent need for more rigorous, evidence-based insights to help organizations make informed decisions regarding the use of PFP in creative work contexts (T.-Y. Park et al., 2022).
text:
Although most organizations use pay-for-performance (PFP) to incentivize employee effort and performance and attract and retain desired types of employees (Gerhart & Newman, 2020), there have been competing arguments concerning PFP’s effects on creative performance (e.g., Byron & Khazanchi, 2012; deCharms, 1968; Deci et al., 2017; Eisenberger et al., 1999). Traditional motivation theories (e.g., reinforcement theory, Skinner, 1953; equity theory, Adams, 1963; expectancy theory, Vroom, 1964) suggest a positive effect of PFP on performance, including creative performance, because motivation from intrinsic interest and extrinsic incentives combine additively (Gupta & Shaw, 1998; Jenkins et al., 1998; Kim et al., 2022). By contrast, traditional creativity theory, which owes an intellectual debt to cognitive evaluation theory (CET), and, later, self-determination theory (SDT), suggest that PFP undermines creativity (Amabile, 1983a, 1983b, 1998; Hennessey, 1989; Lepper et al., 1973). These theories posit that PFP is detrimental to intrinsic motivation because extrinsic rewards are perceived as controlling, thus undermining self-determination, self-competence, and autonomy (Deci et al., 1999; Ryan & Deci, 2017). Therefore, traditional creativity theory suggests that PFP will also undermine creativity because of the theorized centrality of intrinsic motivation to creativity.
However, Amabile and colleagues later proposed a “radically modified” creativity theory, suggesting that extrinsic rewards may “bolster” intrinsic motivation and creativity if they are implemented in an autonomy- and information-supportive manner and/or if initial intrinsic motivation is already high (Amabile & Pratt, 2016, p. 164). Even with these evolving perspectives, theoretical disagreement persists over whether PFP should be implemented in work requiring creativity (Deci et al., 2017; Howard et al., 2017; Ryan & Deci, 2017). This long-standing theoretical debate over the nature of the PFP–creative performance relationship is especially important because creativity is considered a critical determinant of organizational success, as it is seen as fundamental to organizations being more innovative, competitive, and responsive to change (George, 2007; Mumford & Gustafson, 1988; Shalley et al., 2004; Zhou & Hoever, 2014). In best-selling books and TED Talks, some practitioners continue to argue that PFP undermines employee creativity (e.g., “Pay-for-performance schemes … usually don’t work and often do harm,” Pink, 2011; “To increase creativity, slash your budget in half,” Schaefer, 2018; “How money kills creativity,” Meyer, 2019). This highlights the need for better evidence-based guidance to be brought to bear on this debate (T.-Y. Park et al., 2022).

Our objective is to theorize how both intra-category and inter-category coherence, at a given moment, influence the ongoing viability of a focal category. Put differently, we seek to explain how a category’s trajectory is continuously shaped by entrepreneurial activity. As actors introduce new products and engage with audiences, they actively construct the coherence observed within and across categories. This coherence, in turn, redefines the focal category’s viability—for both producers who consider entering it and audiences who navigate it to discover relevant offerings.

To preview our core argument, we propose that a focal category’s viability is primarily influenced by the degree of heterogeneity among the products it includes—that is, intra-category coherence. However, we argue that the precise shape of the relationship between heterogeneity and viability is not fixed but varies depending on how distinct the focal category is relative to a set of closely related categories—that is, inter-category coherence. Depending on the degree of distinctiveness, the heterogeneity–viability curve may exhibit an inverted U-shape, or, alternatively, flatten or reverse into a U-shape—steering the category’s evolution along potentially divergent trajectories.

To clarify this relationship and how the shape of this curve may shift, we draw on Haans, Pieters, and He (2016: 1178) by examining “the latent theoretical mechanisms underlying the (inverted) U-shaped relationship.” Prior research has shown that intra-category coherence is critical because it helps actors and audiences group, interpret, and evaluate products (Porac et al., 1995; Zuckerman, 1999). Based on this, we argue that heterogeneity enhances category viability when it produces two key advantages: (a) classification benefits, which arise as actors and audiences categorize and compare similar products (Hsu & Hannan, 2005; Wry et al., 2011), and (b) valuation benefits, which stem from the ability to communicate and assess a product’s worth (Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2011). We accordingly conceptualize viability as a function of the combined effects of these classification and valuation benefits.

In the following section, we first elaborate how achieving both benefits entails a tension between them, which manifests in either U-shaped or inverted U-shaped viability curves. We then describe how the exact curvature depends on the relative strength of classification versus valuation advantages—dynamics that are, in turn, moderated by the focal category’s distinctiveness in relation to neighboring categories. Table 3 provides a summary of this framework, which we elaborate in detail below.
text:
Our goal is to theorize how both intra- and inter-category coherence, at a given point in time, affect the subsequent viability of a focal category. Put differently, we explain how the path of a focal category is continuously made by entrepreneurial action. As actors position their new products and communicate with audiences, they shape the coherence that products exhibit within and across categories. In turn, this coherence alters the viability of a focal category for actors and their audiences, who respectively enter the category and search for products within it.

To preview our argument, we first claim that the viability of a focal category is primarily driven by the degree of heterogeneity reached by the products within it (i.e., intra-category coherence). However, we argue that the shape of the heterogeneity–viability relationship cannot be determined beforehand but is contingent on the category’s distinctiveness compared to a set of related categories (i.e., inter-category coherence). Depending on the degree of distinctiveness that a focal category exhibits, the heterogeneity–viability may either take an inverted U-shape, or flatten and even flip to a U-shape, thereby steering the category in potentially opposite paths.

To explicate this relationship and how its shape changes, we follow Haans, Pieters, and He (2016: 1178) and consider “the latent theoretical mechanisms underlying the (inverted) U-shaped relationship.” It is well-established that intra-category coherence is useful to the extent that it enables actors and audiences to group and evaluate products (Porac et al., 1995; Zuckerman, 1999). Thus, we contend that heterogeneity contributes to a category’s viability when it confers: (a) classification benefits, by enabling actors and audiences to cluster comparable products (Hsu & Hannan, 2005; Wry et al., 2011); and (b) valuation benefits, by enabling them to convey and assess the value of each product (Khaire & Wadhwani, 2010; Navis & Glynn, 2011). We understand the viability of a category as a function of the additive effects of classification and valuation benefits.

In the following section, we first explain how achieving both benefits involves a trade-off, leading either to a U-shaped or an inverted U-shaped relationship. We then explain how the shape of this relationship depends on the relative strength of classification and valuations benefits, which are moderated by the focal category’s distinctiveness compared to other related categories. Table 3 summarizes the logic behind our argument, which we unpack below.

use: Given that gender is a readily observable personal characteristic and a powerful basis for social categorization—and that corporate boards are typically male-dominated—researchers have drawn on social psychological theories of gender stereotypes and tokenism to explain the effects of gender diversity. Beginning with Schein’s foundational work (1973, 1975), a substantial body of research in social psychology has demonstrated that women frequently encounter gender-based stereotypes and implicit biases in organizational settings (Cuddy, Glick, & Beninger, 2011; Eagly & Mladinic, 1989; Eagly & Karau, 2002; Heilman, 2012; Rudman & Phelan, 2008). These studies highlight that differential treatment towards men and women arises not only from women’s underrepresentation in many professional contexts, but also from widespread assumptions—held by both men and women—regarding gender traits and leadership capabilities. Such biases contribute to entrenched and enduring generalizations about men’s and women’s attributes (Heilman, 2012: 114). Evidence from a variety of studies, employing diverse methods and conducted across different cultural and contextual settings, has shown that these gender stereotypes are remarkably persistent over time (Heilman, 2012). Research consistently finds that men are predominantly associated with agency—characterized by achievement orientation, assertiveness, autonomy, and rationality (Heilman, 2012: 115). In contrast, women are more closely linked to communality, defined by interpersonal warmth, cooperation, deference, and emotional sensitivity (Heilman, 2012: 115). Further research reveals a perceived trade-off between warmth and competence, whereby women seen as warm are often judged as less competent (e.g., Cuddy et al., 2011). Yet women seeking senior leadership roles are expected to display agentic behaviors such as assertiveness, independence, and decisiveness—traits that stereotypically contradict prescriptive norms of femininity (Heilman, 2012: 123). When women violate these gender norms, they may face “backlash effects”—negative social and professional reactions that can occur throughout the employment spectrum, from hiring and salary negotiations to promotion and leadership assessments (Rudman & Phelan, 2008: 65). Consequently, women often strategically modify their behavior to conform to gender expectations, in an effort to avoid social penalties and mitigate rejection (Heilman, 2012: 125).
text: Given that gender is a visible individual characteristic that provides a strong basis for categorizing people and that corporate boards are generally dominated by men, researchers have also used social psychology theories on gender stereotypes and tokenism to explain the impact of gender diversity. Starting with Schein's work (1973, 1975), a large body of social psychology literature has established that women may face gender stereotypes and underlying bias in the workplace (Cuddy, Glick, & Beninger, 2011; Eagly & Mladinic, 1989; Eagly & Karau, 2002; Heilman, 2012; Rudman & Phelan, 2008). This research has shown that there are differences in the treatment of men and women that arise from women being in the minority in the workplace, but also from underlying biases that both men and women tend to have about gender characteristics and leadership qualities. These underlying biases cause both men and women to make stable, fundamental "generalizations about the attributes of men and women" (Heilman, 2012: 114). A range of studies with varying methods have shown gender stereotypes to be consistent over time, and across cultures and contexts (Heilman, 2012). Studies on gender stereotypes have found that agency is the defining characteristic of men, one that "denotes achievement, inclination to take charge, autonomy, and rationality" (Heilman, 2012: 115). Meanwhile, communality is the defining characteristic of women, as represented by "concern for others, affiliative tendencies, deference, and emotional sensitivity" (Heilman, 2012: 115). Furthermore, research has shown that there exists a tradeoff between warmth and competence, where women are perceived to be warm but then deemed less competent (e.g., Cuddy et al., 2011). Yet, to be successful in high-level professional positions, "women have to violate gender stereotypical prescriptions. They have to be able to compete aggressively for positions, to act independently and decisively, and to take charge when the situation requires it" (Heilman, 2012: 123). Not behaving in line with stereotypical expectations, however, can result in negative reactions known as "backlash effects," which have been shown to occur "at every stage of employment, from hiring and salary negotiations to promotion and leadership evaluations" (Rudman & Phelan, 2008: 65) and that may result in less favorable outcomes for women if they show agentic behavior. As a result, women often behave in ways that align with stereotypical prescriptions and are "strategically tailored to minimize social rejection" (Heilman, 2012: 125).

As previously discussed, deonance theory has become the prevailing framework for understanding third-party responses to observed mistreatment (e.g., Dunford et al., 2015; Falk & Fox, 2014; Her & Sin, 2024; Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Jiang et al., 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Priesemuth & Schminke, 2019; Reich & Hershcovis, 2015; Spencer & Rupp, 2009; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). The term "deonance" is derived from the Greek word deon, meaning duty or obligation (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Stein, 2017). Though rooted in the justice literature, Folger and Glerum (2015) emphasized that the theory’s relevance extends beyond this original domain.

Deonance theory posits that proper treatment of others is not merely instrumental but a fundamental duty—an ethical obligation valued intrinsically, independent of personal gain (Folger & Glerum, 2015). This downplaying of self-interest renders the theory especially appropriate for analyzing third-party reactions, as it centers on people’s moral responses to what they witness happening to others, rather than how events affect themselves directly (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Within this framework, appropriate conduct is viewed as a transcendent moral imperative—a guiding standard by which individuals ought to live (Folger, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). The notion that morality holds intrinsic worth likely emerged as an adaptive response to the social complexities of human life (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).

The theory characterizes deonance as a psychological state activated by perceived violations of moral principles. For instance, Folger (2001) defined this state as “an experience aroused when motivated by moral considerations” (p. 7), while Folger and Skarlicki (2008) described it as a “psychological state yielding emotionally charged reactions to events seen as violating or infringing upon moral norms about social conduct” (p. 32). Similarly, Folger (2012) emphasized that deonance arises when a situation evokes beliefs about the relevance of moral norms, even if at an unconscious level (p. 126). Therefore, when a third party witnesses someone being mistreated, that observation can trigger the morally grounded emotional arousal that defines deonance—an arousal that drives the emotional responses central to the theory’s predictions.
text:
As noted previously, deonance theory has emerged as the dominant conceptual lens for understanding third-party reactions to observing mistreatment (e.g., Dunford et al., 2015; Falk & Fox, 2014; Her & Sin, 2024; Hericher & Bridoux, 2023; Jiang et al., 2023; Kay et al., 2023; Lin & Loi, 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2015; O’Reilly et al., 2016; Priesemuth & Schminke, 2019; Reich & Hershcovis, 2015; Spencer & Rupp, 2009; Umphress et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). The moniker of the theory is taken from the Greek deon, which reflects obligation or duty (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Folger & Stein, 2017). Although deonance theory originated in the justice literature, Folger and Glerum (2015) noted that its applicability transcends that boundary.

Deonance theory views appropriate treatment as something people owe one another—as a duty that matters as an end in itself, regardless of self-interested concerns (Folger & Glerum, 2015). The theory’s minimizing of self-interest made it uniquely relevant to the third-party context, with its descriptions emphasizing observations of others’ treatment more than other lenses of the time (Folger, 2001, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). From the perspective of deonance theory, appropriate treatment is a transcendent value—a moral norm by which humans should live their lives (Folger, 2012; Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Appropriateness as a transcendent value or moral norm has likely evolved in humans as an adaptive response to the complex problems encountered in everyday life (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Skarlicki, 2008).

The theory describes deonance as a psychological state that is aroused when moral norms have been violated. For example, Folger (2001) defined deonance as “an experience aroused when motivated by moral considerations” (p. 7). Folger and Skarlicki (2008) conceptualized it as a “psychological state yielding emotionally charged reactions to events seen as violating or infringing upon moral norms about social conduct” (p. 32). Similarly, Folger (2012) described it as a state “whose arousal occurs when a situation brings to bear beliefs about the relevance of moral directives (even if not a perception at the conscious level)” (p. 126). Thus, when a third party observes a perpetrator mistreating a victim, that observation should trigger the heightened arousal that characterizes deonance. And it is that arousal that can give rise to the emotional consequences predicted by the theory.
