According to construal level theory, construals refer to “the mental representations people apply to targets” (Trope & Liberman, 2003, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017, p. 368). These representations vary along a continuum of abstraction, ranging from concrete and situated (i.e., “low-level” construals) to abstract and decontextualized (i.e., “high-level” construals; Trope & Liberman, 2010). Individuals adopt concrete construals when their focus includes contextual elements and incidental details—what Liberman and Trope (2008, p. 1201) describe as “contextualized representations that include subordinate and incidental features.” In contrast, abstract construals emerge when individuals extract central meanings, focusing on “decontextualized representations that extract the gist from the available information” (Liberman & Trope, 2008, p. 1202). In essence, as the level of construal increases, representations become more abstract and less concrete (Trope & Liberman, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017).

Within organizational and applied psychology, construal level theory has been widely applied to explore how individuals mentally represent diverse targets. Researchers have examined construals of a wide array of organizationally relevant elements, including objects, activities, events, people, and situations (Trope & Liberman, 2003, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017). For instance, studies have shown that employees, managers, and leaders construe organizational goals and visions (Berson et al., 2015; Carton et al., 2014, 2023), work challenges (Zhong et al., 2021), job roles (Reyt & Wiesenfeld, 2015), and organizational change efforts (Berson et al., 2021) at different levels of abstraction.

Building on these insights and aligned with our research phenomenon, we examine how individuals construe the work context as a way to understand their perceptions of the workplace environment. We specifically focus on the construal of a particular work context because (a) broadly defined, context encompasses the environment in which work is performed, decisions are made, and adverse events may unfold (e.g., D. A. Hofmann & Stetzer, 1996; Johns, 2018); and (b) context meaningfully shapes employee perceptions and behaviors (e.g., Johns, 2006). More precisely, the work context is commonly defined as “stimuli and phenomena that surround and thus exist in the environment external to the individual” (Mowday & Sutton, 1993, p. 198). Work context includes several key dimensions—such as physical elements (e.g., the appearance of the workspace), task-related factors (e.g., role demands or task expectations), and social features (e.g., the presence or influence of coworkers; Johns, 2006). Scholars have widely emphasized the importance of theorizing about context to better understand employee behavior within organizations (e.g., Bamberger & Pratt, 2010; Heath & Sitkin, 2001; Johns, 2001, 2006; Pfeffer, 1997; Rousseau & Fried, 2001).
text:
According to construal level theory, construals are “the mental representations people apply to targets” (Trope & Liberman, 2003, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017, p. 368). The construal of a given target varies along a continuum of “abstraction”—thus ranging from more concrete and contextualized (i.e., “low-level” construal) to more abstract and decontextualized (i.e., “high-level” construal; Trope & Liberman, 2010). Concrete construals are adopted when individuals focus on “contextualized representations that include subordinate and incidental features” (Liberman & Trope, 2008, p. 1201), versus more abstractly when they focus on “decontextualized representations that extract the gist from the available information” (Liberman & Trope, 2008, p. 1202). Thus, as the construal level of a given target shifts higher, it increases in abstraction (and is therefore less concrete; Trope & Liberman, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017).

Research on organizational and applied psychology has used construal level theory to investigate construals toward a myriad of targets. In particular, scholars have considered construals of objects, activities, people, situations, events, and more (see Trope & Liberman, 2003, 2010; Wiesenfeld et al., 2017, for reviews)—many of which are pertinent to organizations. For example, employees, managers, and company leaders can construe goals or visions (Berson et al., 2015; Carton et al., 2014, 2023), work problems (Zhong et al., 2021), work roles (Reyt & Wiesenfeld, 2015), and change initiatives (Berson et al., 2021) at varying degrees of abstraction.

Building on the above, and given our phenomenon of interest, we focus on the construal of a given work context to capture the work environment. We specifically focus on a given work context because (a) broadly speaking, context captures the environment within which work is done, decisions are made, and accidents could occur (e.g., D. A. Hofmann & Stetzer, 1996; Johns, 2018) and (b) context drives employee perceptions and behavior (e.g., Johns, 2006). More specifically, the work context has been defined as “stimuli and phenomena that surround and thus exist in the environment external to the individual” (Mowday & Sutton, 1993, p. 198). Work contexts span a myriad of features, including physical (e.g., what the workplace looks like), task (e.g., demands or expectations of roles and tasks), and social (e.g., aspects of people or their salience; Johns, 2006). Many have argued that theorizing about context is critical to understanding employee behaviors in organizations (e.g., Bamberger & Pratt, 2010; Heath & Sitkin, 2001; Johns, 2001, 2006; Pfeffer, 1997; Rousseau & Fried, 2001).

Although scholars have adopted slightly varying definitions of scandal (e.g., Adut, 2005; Esser & Hartung, 2004; Nyhan, 2014; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018; Thompson, 2000), they consistently emphasize actions perceived by the broader public as objectionable and typically include the three core elements identified by Thompson (2000): (a) some level of public awareness of the action or event, (b) the presence of an audience of nonparticipating observers, and (c) a mechanism by which the conduct becomes visible to others. As Thompson (2000: 19) stated, “activities that remain invisible to non-participants cannot, ipso facto, be scandalous.” Scandals, therefore, emerge when individuals engage in norm-violating behaviors that are disruptive to societal values, and these behaviors are brought into the public eye (Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018)—most often through media exposure (Graffin et al., 2013; Nyhan, 2014, 2017; Thompson, 2000).

The media plays a central role in this process. As Nyhan (2014: 436) observed, while scandals are generally framed as the revelation of wrongdoing or norm violation, “whether any specific case meets such a standard is often unclear or contested,” with contextual factors shaping public interpretation. Thompson (2000: 16) similarly emphasized that “scandals are often rather messy affairs, involving the alleged transgression of values and norms which are themselves subject to contestation.” As such, the media must make discretionary judgments about whether to report—and potentially elevate—a transgression into a scandal. Adut’s (2005) analysis of Oscar Wilde’s scandalization in Victorian England suggests that turning a transgression into a scandal requires calculating whether the benefits of publicity outweigh the costs associated with mobilizing public disapproval. Nyhan (2014, 2017) echoed this view in the political domain, showing that the likelihood of a presidential or gubernatorial transgression becoming a scandal often hinges on supportive context, such as low approval ratings or the absence of competing news events.

Because scandalizing an act of misconduct involves carefully weighing potential costs against perceived benefits, it follows that not all norm violations result in scandal (Adut, 2005; Graffin et al., 2013; Nyhan, 2014, 2017). To better understand how the media makes these evaluations, it is crucial to examine how perceptions of newsworthiness and established journalistic routines influence media decision-making (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020; Oliver, Campbell, Graffin, & Bundy, 2023; Pollock et al., 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013).
text:
Although scholars have offered slightly different definitions of scandal (e.g., Adut, 2005; Esser & Hartung, 2004; Nyhan, 2014; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018; Thompson, 2000), they have all focused on actions that the broad public is likely to find objectionable, and incorporated the three elements Thompson (2000) outlined: (a) some degree of public knowledge about the action or event, (b) the fact that the “public” includes nonparticipating observers, and (c) a process for making the actions or events visible to others. Thompson (2000: 19) went on to note, “activities that remain invisible to non-participants cannot, ipso facto, be scandalous.” Thus, scandals arise when actors engage in behaviors that transgress social norms and values in disruptive ways, and their behaviors are brought into the public forum (Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013; Piazza & Jourdan, 2018), typically by the media (Graffin et al., 2013; Nyhan, 2014, 2017; Thompson, 2000).

The media’s role is critical because, as Nyhan (2014: 436) noted, scandals are typically defined “as the result of the disclosure of some act of wrongdoing or norm violation” but “whether any specific case meets such a standard is often unclear or contested,” and context influences perceptions of whether a scandal has occurred (Nyhan, 2017). Thompson (2000: 16) similarly noted, “scandals are often rather messy affairs, involving the alleged transgression of values and norms which are themselves subject to contestation.” Thus, the media must assess whether to publicize, and potentially scandalize, a transgression. In his study of how Oscar Wilde’s homosexuality was scandalized during the Victorian Era, Adut (2005) theorized that for a transgression to become a scandal, the benefits of publicizing the transgression must outweigh the costs of attracting and coordinating the public’s disapproval. Nyhan (2014, 2017) similarly noted that for political scandals context is important, and whether a president’s or governor’s transgression became a scandal depended on their approval ratings and the extent to which there were competing news stories.

The need to balance the costs and benefits of scandalizing misconduct is therefore the reason why not all transgressions are scandalized (Adut, 2005; Graffin et al., 2013; Nyhan, 2014, 2017). To understand how the media assesses the costs and benefits of publicizing—and potentially scandalizing—misconduct, it is important to first understand how perceptions of a story’s newsworthiness and the media’s routines shape its coverage decisions (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020; Oliver, Campbell, Graffin, & Bundy, 2023; Pollock et al., 2008; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013).

The present study makes several important contributions to the literature on asynchronous video interviews (AVIs). First, it offers a comprehensive approach to examining various forms of cognitive ability within the AVI context. While general mental ability (GMA or g) has long been recognized as a robust predictor of job performance across a wide range of occupations—particularly in cognitively demanding roles (Hunter et al., 2006; cf. Sackett et al., 2022)—it is equally important to account for other, more specific cognitive abilities in interview settings (Schneider & Newman, 2015). Verbal ability, for instance, is especially vital, as it has been shown to strongly influence both interview performance and subsequent job success (König et al., 2007; Lang et al., 2010; Melchers et al., 2009). Furthermore, interviewers often form impressions of a candidate’s cognitive ability during the interview process (Huffcutt et al., 2001), underscoring the relevance of perceived intellect. To address these considerations, this study develops and rigorously evaluates AVI assessment methods using three ground-truth indicators of distinct yet relevant cognitive ability constructs: GMA test scores, verbal ability test scores, and observer ratings of interviewee intellect.

Second, this research investigates the incremental value of different behavioral modalities for automatically assessing cognitive ability in interviews. According to the model of interviewee performance (Huffcutt et al., 2011), cognitive ability influences verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors—implying that all three behavior types may serve as valid indicators for assessing mental ability. More broadly, communication research emphasizes the critical role of paraverbal and nonverbal cues in social interactions, including job interviews (Bonaccio et al., 2016). Despite this, there remains a surprising lack of empirical work examining how these behavioral modes relate to cognitive ability—even though a substantial body of research has explored how Big Five personality traits correspond with these behaviors (Bourdage et al., 2018; Peeters & Lievens, 2006; Van Iddekinge et al., 2007). Given the central role of cognitive ability in interview-based hiring decisions (Roth & Huffcutt, 2013), this gap is striking. To address it, we train machine learning (ML) models using verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal data and compare their validity in predicting cognitive ability. These findings offer guidance on which behavioral modes are most informative for ML-based assessments—an essential consideration in light of ongoing ethical concerns about the bias and fairness of nonverbal cues, such as facial expressions, in AVI evaluations (e.g., Harris et al., 2018).
text:
The present study contributes to the literature on AVIs in several ways. First, it presents an integrative approach to examining different types of cognitive ability AVIs. General mental ability (GMA or g) has long been recognized as a key predictor of job performance across occupations, especially in complex jobs (Hunter et al., 2006; cf. Sackett et al., 2022). For example, GMA predicts task and overall job performance better than the combined Big Five personality traits (Gonzalez-Mulé et al., 2014). Within an interview context, it is also important to consider other specific cognitive abilities (Schneider & Newman, 2015). Verbal ability is a specific ability that is particularly relevant because it strongly predicts interview and later job performance (König et al., 2007; Lang et al., 2010; Melchers et al., 2009). Additionally, because the cognitive ability of interviewees is often evaluated by interviewers (Huffcutt et al., 2001), it is crucial to consider perceptions of interviewee cognitive ability. Therefore, the present study develops and critically evaluates AVIs using three ground truth measures of distinct cognitive ability constructs that are highly relevant to the employment interview context: GMA test scores, verbal ability test scores, and observer ratings of interviewee intellect.

Second, our investigation examines the incremental validity of different types of behavior for automatically assessing interviewee cognitive ability. The model of interviewee performance (Huffcutt et al., 2011) claims that cognitive ability affects the verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal modes of behavior, and thus, all three types of behavior may be useful for assessing cognitive ability. More generally, researchers have pointed to the importance of paraverbal and nonverbal behaviors for communication, including interviews (Bonaccio et al., 2016). However, scant evidence is available regarding the relationships between cognitive ability and different modes of behavior, even though there is an extensive body of research on how the Big Five personality traits relate to the three modes of interviewee behavior (Bourdage et al., 2018; Peeters & Lievens, 2006; Van Iddekinge et al., 2007). The lack of corresponding studies on cognitive ability is surprising, considering that cognitive ability plays a pervasive role in interview outcomes (Roth & Huffcutt, 2013). Thus, we train and compare the validity of ML models that use verbal, paraverbal, and/or nonverbal behaviors to assess interviewee cognitive ability. This informs about the modes of behavior one should use in ML models, which is essential given concerns about the bias and fairness of nonverbal behaviors (e.g., facial expressions) in AVIs (e.g., Harris et al., 2018).

Thus, while a firm takes a proactive role by engaging in stakeholder dialogue, it adopts a reactive posture when resorting to impression management to repair its reputation following a crisis. These two approaches—engaging in dialogue and relying on image remediation—represent opposite ends of a proactive–reactive continuum that reflects the extent to which a firm values its stakeholders and acknowledges the interdependence of their relationship. A firm involved in stakeholder dialogue demonstrates deliberate and sustained efforts to build and maintain relationships with stakeholders. In contrast, a firm that primarily relies on impression management in crisis situations often downplays the importance of stakeholder input and the potential for mutual learning. Furthermore, the nature of the information shared differs across these approaches: stakeholder dialogue typically involves richer and more diverse information exchange, while reactive communication tends to be narrowly focused on immediate concerns. In crises, control of the narrative often shifts to others, and the firm finds itself in a responsive rather than directive role.

Stakeholders, in turn, observe and interpret these differing organizational postures when forming evaluations of firms. A firm that proactively engages in dialogue signals recognition of the stakeholder and demonstrates a willingness to address both the benefits and drawbacks of its activities. This openness serves as a meaningful signal: a stakeholder is more likely to perceive the firm as trustworthy or ethical—a “good firm”—after speaking with a representative who listens attentively and answers questions with transparency and respect. Conversely, when firms avoid open communication and respond only in the wake of a crisis or under pressure, stakeholders may perceive the firm as deceptive or unreliable—a “bad firm” or a "lemon". Based on these observations, we propose that the posture a firm adopts toward its stakeholders—from proactive engagement to reactive image repair—shapes how stakeholders assess and evaluate the firm.

However, we contend that stakeholder dialogue does not produce uniform effects on stakeholder evaluations. Specifically, our foundational assumption is that proactive dialogue on its own does not necessarily change how stakeholders evaluate a firm. Rather, we argue that the impact of stakeholder dialogue is heavily dependent on the informational context in which the firm–stakeholder relationship—and the dialogue itself—takes place. In particular, stakeholder evaluations can improve or worsen depending on the availability, reliability, and content of broader media information about the firm. These contextual factors shape how stakeholders interpret the firm's behavior and determine whether dialogue is seen as meaningful or merely performative.
text:
Thus, whereas the firm acts proactively when participating in stakeholder dialogue, it is reactive when it relies on impression management to remediate its image in the aftermath of a crisis. Seeking firm–stakeholder dialogue and relying on impression-management responses to crises represent the two ends of a proactive-to-reactive continuum that indicates the degree to which a firm recognizes the value of its stakeholders and of their interdependent relationship. A firm engaged in stakeholder dialogue acknowledges stakeholders through an ongoing, deliberate process meant to solidify the firm’s relationship with them; a firm relying extensively on remedial impression management downplays the importance of stakeholder contributions or the value of learning from them. The information exchanged through stakeholder dialogue and through remedial impression management may be different, too: stakeholder dialogue involves the exchange of more and diverse information, whereas reactive responses focus on information pertinent to the situation. In crises, others control the conversation and the firm reacts to it.

On their end, stakeholders observe and interpret these different stances in their evaluations of firms. A firm that participates proactively in dialogue with a stakeholder is recognizing the stakeholder and signaling its openness to exchanging information about its projects’ positive and negative impacts. Openness to discussing these issues is an important signal about the firm. A stakeholder is more likely to believe they are interacting with a “good firm” following a meeting with a manager who listens to concerns and seeks to answer questions openly and respectfully. By contrast, a stakeholder is more likely to believe the firm is a “bad firm” (a lemon) if managers avoid dialogue and react only after the onset of a crisis or after stakeholders insistently demand information. Building on these arguments, we suggest that a firm’s stance toward its stakeholders—ranging from proactive stakeholder dialogue to reactive remedial impression management—influences how a stakeholder evaluates the firm.

We argue, however, that stakeholder dialogue does not have a uniform effect on stakeholders’ evaluations. That is, our baseline expectation is that, by itself, proactivity through stakeholder dialogue does not have a direct effect on changes in stakeholder evaluations of firms. Instead, we argue that the value of stakeholder dialogue is highly contingent on the informational environment in which the relationship between the firm and the stakeholder—and the firm–stakeholder dialogue itself—is embedded, and can therefore contribute to the improvement or deterioration of stakeholder evaluations depending on the quantity (or availability), the quality (or reliability), and the content of media information available about the firm.

When managers speak out against social injustices, they are often perceived as supportive of the marginalized groups they advocate for (for exceptions, see General Discussion; Chu & Ashburn-Nardo, 2022; Czopp & Monteith, 2003; Dickter et al., 2012; Thoroughgood et al., 2021). However, many managers opt to stay silent in the face of injustice (De Souza & Schmader, 2022; Dickter & Newton, 2013). This silence may be partially driven by concerns over negative judgments. For instance, managers may fear misspeaking or coming across as insincere, which could lead observers to view them as less supportive of a social group than if they had chosen not to speak at all (Berkowitz, 2021; Dowell & Jackson, 2020; Martinez et al., 2017; Morris, 2020; Wagner et al., 2009; Warren, 2022). As a result, these fears can reinforce a cycle of managerial silence in the face of social injustice (Apfelbaum et al., 2008).

Although managers may be preoccupied with how speaking up will affect others’ perceptions of them, they may overlook the reputational consequences of remaining silent. For years, social justice advocates have emphasized that silence can be profoundly expressive—captured in phrases such as “silence speaks volumes” or “silence is deafening” (Capatides, 2020; Obasi, 2020; Salon, 2020; Walker-Smith, 2022). Such expressions underscore that silence is not a neutral stance; rather, it can be interpreted critically when there is widespread recognition of an injustice and a clear opportunity to address it. These critiques of inaction are especially salient following “megathreats”—high-profile, identity-relevant negative events that attract considerable public and media attention (Leigh & Melwani, 2019).

We argue that observers do in fact take notice of managerial silence and form judgments about a manager’s intergroup attitudes and support for marginalized communities not only based on the causes they vocalize, but also on those they choose to overlook. To explain how a manager’s silence in the face of injustice may be interpreted, we draw on two established theoretical frameworks in psychology and management. Attribution theory (Kelley, 1973) suggests that people will evaluate a manager’s current silence in light of their behavioral history—whether they have previously spoken up or remained silent on a related reference issue. This behavioral context shapes individuals’ attributions about the manager’s underlying intergroup attitudes and, ultimately, their perceived support for the outgroup in question. Social identity theory adds further nuance by suggesting that whether the prior reference issue was connected to the manager’s ingroup or an outgroup will influence whether they are perceived as harboring intergroup bias or as genuinely committed to social justice.
text:
When managers speak up on social injustices, observers often perceive that they are supportive of the groups they are advocating for (for a discussion on exceptions, see General Discussion; Chu & Ashburn-Nardo, 2022; Czopp & Monteith, 2003; Dickter et al., 2012; Thoroughgood et al., 2021). Yet, many managers choose to remain silent on injustices (De Souza & Schmader, 2022; Dickter & Newton, 2013). This may be due in part because they worry that negative perceptions will arise. For example, managers may fear saying the wrong thing or appearing disingenuous, leading observers to judge them as actually less supportive of a social group than if they had remained silent (Berkowitz, 2021; Dowell & Jackson, 2020; Martinez et al., 2017; Morris, 2020; Wagner et al., 2009; Warren, 2022). This fear can ultimately perpetuate managerial silence on social injustices (Apfelbaum et al., 2008).

While managers may have concerns about how they will be perceived if they speak up, they may fail to realize the perceptual consequences of remaining silent. For decades, social justice activists have warned that silence on injustices “speaks volumes” or “is deafening” (Capatides, 2020; Obasi, 2020; Salon, 2020; Walker-Smith, 2022). These phrases imply that observers notice the behavior of silence and can be critical of those who remain silent when there is collective awareness of an injustice and a clear opportunity to speak up. Indeed, these critiques of silence typically occur after some sort of “megathreat”—a negative, identity-relevant event that receives significant media attention (Leigh & Melwani, 2019).

We argue that observers do sometimes notice silence and not only evaluate a manager’s intergroup attitudes and support for an outgroup based on the issues that they speak up about, but also on the issues that they remain silent on. To specify how a manager’s silence on an outgroup injustice will be perceived, we draw from two prominent theories in psychology and management. Attribution theory (Kelley, 1973) suggests that perceptions of a manager’s silence will be based, in part, on a manager’s past behavior (i.e., whether they have spoken up or remained silent in the past on a reference issue). This past behavior will influence people’s attributions about a manager’s underlying intergroup attitudes and, in turn, their perceived support for an outgroup. Social identity theory elucidates that whether the past reference issue is relevant to the manager’s ingroup or outgroup will shape the particular attributions people will make about a manager—whether they are seen as harboring intergroup bias or as generally supportive of social justice.

Our findings yield several important theoretical implications. First, this research informs theories concerning the alignment between the personality traits of an “ideal incumbent” and the characteristics of employees. According to personality demands–fit theory (Denissen et al., 2018), job performance is not uniformly influenced by global levels of conscientiousness or its individual facets. In other words, lower scores on a particular facet of conscientiousness may not undermine performance in every job context, suggesting that a blanket, top-down selection based on overall conscientiousness may be inappropriate. Notably, in both student and employed adult samples, the smallest differences between individuals with and without ADHD emerged on items related to achievement-striving and dutifulness, both in general and work-specific measures. Meta-analytic findings identify these two facets as especially predictive of job performance outcomes—such as task effectiveness and avoidance of counterproductive behaviors (Dudley et al., 2006; Ones et al., 2007). Together, these findings suggest that a stronger focus on achievement-striving and dutifulness, rather than other conscientiousness facets, could help reduce mean group disparities by ADHD status while simultaneously enhancing predictive validity.

Second, our study offers a novel application of established theories on situational strength and contextual contingencies (Tett & Burnett, 2003; Wright & Mischel, 1987) by linking them to frame-of-reference (FOR) manipulations. We explore item contextualization as a strategy for mitigating differences in conscientiousness scores by ADHD status. While past research has shown that work-specific measures of conscientiousness can enhance reliability and validity (Lievens et al., 2008; Schmit et al., 1995), little attention has been given to contextualization as a method for addressing mean differences across demographic or neurodiverse groups. Our findings point to the value of this underexplored approach in promoting fairness in personnel selection beyond the specific context of ADHD. Though our method—adding “at work” tags—shows early promise, there is reason to believe that deeper contextualization strategies (e.g., realistic job-related scenarios) could yield even more robust effects (Lievens & Sackett, 2017), providing direction for future work.

Third, our findings call into question the appropriateness of interpreting mean differences in conscientiousness traits by ADHD status as purely reflective of differences in the underlying latent construct. Measurement invariance (MI) analyses revealed intercept non-invariance across several items in both general and work-specific conscientiousness measures. This suggests that observed group differences—particularly on items concerning task initiation, switching, and persistence—might stem from differential item interpretation rather than true differences in conscientiousness (Putnick & Bornstein, 2016). Future research is needed to explore why these interpretive differences arise and how they impact the psychometric functioning of such measures (MacCallum et al., 1992). Nonetheless, our results highlight that individuals with and without ADHD may not respond to conscientiousness items in equivalent ways, raising important considerations for fair assessment practices.
text:
Our findings have several theoretical implications. First, the present research has implications for theory on how the personality attributes of an “ideal incumbent” fit with employee characteristics. According to personality demands-fit theory (Denissen et al., 2018), not all jobs require the same levels of overall conscientiousness or its facets. Lower scores on one conscientiousness facet may not compromise performance in all job roles equally, meaning that top–down selection on global conscientiousness may not be desirable. Regarding facets of conscientiousness, across both student and employed adult samples, the magnitude of the difference between those with and without ADHD on general and work-specific conscientiousness items was the smallest for achievement-striving and dutifulness. Meta-analyses suggest that these two facets are among the more predictive facets of conscientiousness for overall performance, task performance, and avoidance of counterproductive work behavior (Dudley et al., 2006; Ones et al., 2007). Putting these two findings together, focusing on achievement-striving and dutifulness facets (vs. other facets) could reduce mean differences by ADHD status while potentially improving validity for predicting performance.

Second, our research innovatively connects well-known theory on situational contingencies (Tett & Burnett, 2003; Wright & Mischel, 1987) to FOR manipulations to explore item contextualization as a method for reducing ADHD-based conscientiousness differences. Although research suggests work-specific (vs. general) conscientiousness measures improve reliability and criterion-related validity (Lievens et al., 2008; Schmit et al., 1995), previous research has not examined item contextualization as a way to address group mean differences on predictors. This novel approach opens the possibility for future research aimed at assessing fairness in selection assessments that extend beyond those with ADHD. There is some promise of our contextualization method (i.e., adding “at work” tags to conscientiousness items), but other, higher fidelity contextualization methods may be even more effective (see Lievens & Sackett, 2017).

Third, it might not be appropriate to compare group means on conscientiousness scores by ADHD status and assume the differences are due to the latent trait. Our MI analysis revealed that the intercepts of several items were not equal across groups in both the work-specific and general conscientiousness samples, suggesting that group differences on some items (e.g., related to task initiation, switching, and persistence) may be due to differences in item interpretation rather than true differences in the underlying latent constructs the items intend to measure (Putnick & Bornstein, 2016). Potential reasons for and validation of these differences warrant future research (MacCallum et al., 1992), but the results indicate that those with and without ADHD interpret some conscientiousness items differently.

In the example above, if Meredith previously spoke out against sexism—an injustice targeting her ingroup—and later remains silent in the face of racial injustice—an issue affecting an outgroup—she appears to confirm the expectation that individuals are more inclined to advocate for their own group than for others. By having spoken up in the past, Meredith has already demonstrated her willingness, confidence, and knowledge to address issues of injustice, signaling that she possesses both the capacity and perceived safety to speak out in her organizational context. Her subsequent silence on an outgroup issue, then, may not be interpreted as circumstantial or constrained, but rather as a deliberate omission. This disparity in action implies to observers that Meredith may possess underlying intergroup bias, thereby associating her silence with a lack of support for the outgroup.

In contrast, when a manager shows consistent silence—first on an ingroup injustice and then again on an outgroup injustice—they do not reinforce the pattern of selectively supporting their own group while neglecting others. Instead, their uniform silence across both contexts suggests a more neutral or habitual disposition to remain silent at work, regardless of the issue. According to attribution theory, observers are likely to interpret this consistent silence not as a reflection of specific intergroup attitudes, but as stemming from more general tendencies—such as risk aversion, disengagement, or workplace norms. Therefore, we propose that when a manager has previously spoken up (in contrast to remaining silent) on an ingroup issue and then remains silent on an outgroup issue, they will be perceived as more intergroup-biased and as less supportive of the outgroup. More specifically, we theorize:

Hypothesis 1: When a manager has previously spoken up (versus remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is later silent on a focal outgroup issue, observers will perceive them to possess greater intergroup bias.  
Hypothesis 2: When a manager has previously spoken up (versus remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is later silent on a focal outgroup issue, observers will perceive them to be less supportive of the outgroup.  
Hypothesis 3: When a manager has previously spoken up (versus remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is then silent on a focal outgroup issue, perceived intergroup bias will mediate the relationship, such that observers will perceive the manager as less supportive of the outgroup.
text:
In the above example, if Meredith spoke up in the past on sexism (an ingroup injustice) and is then silent on racial injustice (an outgroup injustice), Meredith is confirming the expectation that she selectively supports her ingroup and not an outgroup. Additionally, by speaking out in the past, she has demonstrated a willingness, comfort, and sufficient knowledge to speak up on injustices, something she appears to be reluctant to do for an outgroup. Finally, given that Meredith has spoken up in the past, she has demonstrated that she is in a context in which there is an opportunity to speak up, but appears to have chosen not to do so on behalf of an outgroup. Thus, when Meredith speaks up on sexism and is then silent on racial injustice, observers will likely intuit that Meredith harbors intergroup bias, and therefore her silence indicates that she does not support the outgroup.

In contrast, when a manager engages in total silence by previously remaining silent on an ingroup injustice and is then also silent on an outgroup injustice, the manager does not confirm the expectation that people behave in ways to support their ingroup and not an outgroup. Indeed, they are behaving similarly for both the ingroup and the outgroup. As explained above, according to attribution theory, observers will likely assume that silence is not due to Meredith’s attitudes on the particular issue, but rather perhaps because she simply remains silent at work, for whatever reason. In sum, we theorize that when a manager has previously spoken up (vs. remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is then silent on an outgroup focal issue, observers will perceive that a manager harbors more intergroup bias and, in turn, is less supportive of the outgroup. More formally, we theorize:
Hypothesis 1: When a manager has previously spoken up (vs. remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is then silent on a focal outgroup issue, observers will perceive that they harbor more intergroup bias.
Hypothesis 2: When a manager has previously spoken up (vs. remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is then silent on a focal outgroup issue, observers will perceive that they are less supportive of the focal outgroup.
Hypothesis 3: When a manager has previously spoken up (vs. remained silent) on an ingroup reference issue and is then silent on a focal outgroup issue, observers will perceive that they harbor more intergroup bias, which in turn will lead to the perception that they are less supportive of the focal outgroup.

Existing research raises important concerns about the conventional use of conscientiousness measures in evaluating individuals with ADHD. Specifically, lower conscientiousness scores among this population may reflect not inherent deficits, but instead a combination of factors: (a) insufficient testing accommodations offered by organizations, (b) the inclusion of traits that are irrelevant to job performance, and/or (c) the assessment of personality in general life contexts rather than in work-specific settings. As the opening quote of this article implies, individuals with ADHD may, in fact, demonstrate conscientious behaviors in professional environments—particularly when those environments provide meaningful motivation—despite facing challenges related to conscientiousness in other, lower-stakes areas of life. Qualitative (Lasky et al., 2016) and experimental (Fabiano et al., 2018) studies support this notion, showing that individuals with ADHD can perform on par with their non-ADHD peers in engaging, hands-on work settings that promote attentional control. This evidence challenges the assumption that people with ADHD are intrinsically underperformers compared to those without ADHD. Legally, under the Americans with Disabilities Act (1990), a disability is defined as a condition that substantially limits one or more major life activities. In the employment context, this means that discrimination is only legally justifiable if the individual cannot perform essential job functions, with or without reasonable accommodations. While accommodations may facilitate conscientious behavior, we are not suggesting that self-report conscientiousness measures violate legal standards. Rather, we argue that these measures could be meaningfully improved to better serve applicants with ADHD within hiring processes.

Despite growing attention to workplace experiences of individuals with ADHD, scant research has examined issues during the preemployment phase of the job cycle. Most existing studies have instead concentrated on on-the-job factors that impact performance and success (Gordon & Fabiano, 2019; Lasky et al., 2016). Scholars have proposed that contextualizing personality assessments using a work frame of reference (FOR)—for example, by prefacing conscientiousness items and instructions with the phrase “at work”—may create a more equitable assessment for neurodivergent candidates (Schmit et al., 1995; Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). This approach may be particularly inclusive for two reasons. First, items referencing behavior in professional settings are more likely to demonstrate legal defensibility, as they are closely tied to job performance. Second, conscientious behavior is often more pronounced in work contexts than in other life domains, revealing a situational effect (Wright & Mischel, 1987). In other words, the workplace can activate specific expressions of conscientiousness (Tett & Burnett, 2003). Importantly, emerging research suggests that this context-sensitivity in conscientiousness expression may be especially marked among individuals with ADHD (Delisle & Braun, 2011; Fabiano et al., 2018; Lasky et al., 2016).
text:
Existing research raises questions about the status quo use of conscientiousness measures in populations with ADHD to the extent that lower conscientiousness scores for people with ADHD reflect some combination of (a) inadequate testing accommodations provided by organizations, (b) measuring job-irrelevant characteristics, and/or (c) measuring personality as habits of thoughts, feelings, and behaviors in general but not necessarily habits on the job. As the opening quote to this article suggests, work contexts may motivate conscientious behavior in work settings among those with ADHD, despite struggling with conscientiousness in lower stakes life domains. This idea has received some support from qualitative (Lasky et al., 2016) and experimental (Fabiano et al., 2018) research finding that people with ADHD can perform similarly to those without ADHD in stimulating, hands-on work environments that motivate attentional control. Thus, it is not the case that people with ADHD are destined to be poorer performers than people without ADHD. Situating these issues in the legal landscape, a disability is any impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities (Americans with Disabilities Act, 1990). Under U.S. law (Americans with Disabilities Act, 1990), employers can discriminate against a person with a disability only if they cannot perform essential work duties with or without accommodations. Although accommodations could support conscientious behavior at work, we are not suggesting that conscientiousness self-report measures violate the law; rather, we present evidence suggesting that these measures can be improved upon in selection for applicants with ADHD.

Very little research has explored potential issues at the preemployment stage of the employment cycle for those with ADHD, with much of the literature focusing on work-related and personal factors that influence success on the job (Gordon & Fabiano, 2019; Lasky et al., 2016). Scholars have suggested that contextualizing personality measures within a work frame of reference (FOR; i.e., by adding “at work” tags to conscientiousness items and form instructions; Schmit et al., 1995) could be more inclusive of neurodivergent job applicants for two reasons (Melson-Silimon et al., 2019). First, items that ask specifically about work behavior are likely more legally defensible due to their clearer job relevance. Second, conscientiousness at work may be higher than outside of work (a situational effect; Wright & Mischel, 1987). In other words, work contexts may be more likely to activate context-specific conscientious behaviors (Tett & Burnett, 2003). Moreover, research suggests that the expression of conscientiousness may be more context-sensitive for those with ADHD than for others (Delisle & Braun, 2011; Fabiano et al., 2018; Lasky et al., 2016).

Adopting a more fluid view of categories, another stream of research has explored the dynamics that emerge among actors who hold differing interpretations of a category (Lounsbury & Rao, 2004; Rao et al., 2003). These studies have primarily highlighted the challenges posed by this multiplicity of understandings, as actors diverge from or converge on shared meanings (Jones, Maoret, Massa, & Svejenova, 2011; Santos & Eisenhardt, 2009), shaping the opportunities available to new entrants or restricting access to categorical space (Grodal, 2018; Wry et al., 2011). However, the meaning of established categories is not necessarily fixed or ossified. Instead, it often serves as a platform for continued negotiation and reinterpretation, especially as actors innovate and audience expectations shift (Lockwood et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2018). This body of work suggests that categories do not always follow a predictable, linear developmental path—as implied by the life cycle model—but may experience fluctuating relevance and utility over time, cycling through phases of increased or diminished viability.

Both streams of research emphasize that the viability of a product category is the result of deliberate and ongoing efforts, wherein actors and audiences work collectively to define and affirm its core meaning and purpose (Lounsbury & Glynn, 2019; Wry et al., 2011). While both perspectives acknowledge that categories do not evolve in a vacuum (Durand & Thornton, 2018; Glynn & Navis, 2013), the theoretical implications of their mutual interdependence have largely been overlooked (Lo et al., 2020). The first stream contributes important insights into the evolutionary trajectories of categories (Cattani et al., 2017; Navis & Glynn, 2010; Zuckerman, 1999), yet it typically assumes a uniform path of development across categories. The second stream, while recognizing that categories can evolve in diverse ways (Lee et al., 2018; Wry et al., 2011), often focuses narrowly on isolated categories, offering limited attention to the ways in which categories interact or influence one another (with notable exceptions such as Haans, 2019; Hsu & Grodal, 2021).

Put differently, although the literature has acknowledged the coexistence of multiple categories, it has paid insufficient attention to the question of how categories coevolve. This represents a surprising oversight, considering the extensive literature on how actors span, straddle, or hybridize across categorical boundaries (Goldberg, Hannan, & Kovács, 2016; Hsu, 2006; Paolella & Durand, 2016). We know, for example, that actors can face penalties when they mix elements from different categories (Hsu, Koçak, & Hannan, 2009; Negro, Hannan, & Rao, 2010; Zuckerman, 1999), but that such strategies can also confer strategic advantages under certain conditions (Paolella & Durand, 2016; Pontikes, 2012; Wry et al., 2014). Yet, little is known about how these boundary-spanning efforts impact not only the focal category in which actors are embedded but also the related categories from which they draw. Building on and extending these insights, this paper theorizes how both intra- and inter-category dynamics influence the ongoing viability of a category.
text:
Espousing a more fluid understanding of categories, another research stream has examined the dynamics between actors holding different understandings of a category (Lounsbury & Rao, 2004; Rao et al., 2003). These studies have often emphasized the challenges associated with a multiplicity of views, as actors diverge or converge around the underlying meaning of a category (Jones, Maoret, Massa, & Svejenova, 2011; Santos & Eisenhardt, 2009), and create or restrict space for new entrants (Grodal, 2018; Wry et al., 2011). Yet, the meaning of established categories does not necessarily ossify, but forms the basis for further negotiations as actors innovate and audiences’ expectations evolve (Lockwood et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2018). This stream has suggested that categories do not always follow a linear development, as the life cycle model suggests, but may go through cycles of ups and downs, making them more, or less, viable at different points in time.

Both research streams have viewed the viability of a product category as an effortful accomplishment, whereby actors and audiences collectively agree on its underlying meaning and purpose (Lounsbury & Glynn, 2019; Wry et al., 2011). However, while each stream has acknowledged that no category evolves in isolation (Durand & Thornton, 2018; Glynn & Navis, 2013), the theoretical implications of this assertion have rarely been examined (Lo et al., 2020). The first stream has offered valuable insights into the evolutionary trajectory of categories (Cattani et al., 2017; Navis & Glynn, 2010; Zuckerman, 1999), but the life cycle approach implicitly assumes a similar trajectory for all categories. The second stream has recognized that categories take different trajectories (Lee et al., 2018; Wry et al., 2011), but these studies have focused on one category at a time and paid little attention to how categories influence each other (but see Haans, 2019; Hsu & Grodal, 2021).

In other words, although past research has recognized that multiple categories coexist, how categories coevolve remains undertheorized. This is surprising, given the wellspring of studies on how actors span, straddle, or hybridize categories (Goldberg, Hannan, & Kovács, 2016; Hsu, 2006; Paolella & Durand, 2016). For instance, we know that actors are often penalized when they borrow elements from different categories (Hsu, Koçak, & Hannan, 2009; Negro, Hannan, & Rao, 2010; Zuckerman, 1999), yet at times benefit from doing so (Paolella & Durand, 2016; Pontikes, 2012; Wry et al., 2014). However, little is known about how such efforts affect the viability of the category to which actors belong, or the other related categories from which they borrow. Extending prior insights, we theorize how intra- and inter-category dynamics shape the viability of a category.

Incentive Effects of Varying PFP Intensity on Creative Performance

A natural extension of the fixed pay versus pay-for-performance (PFP) dichotomy is to explore the effects of different levels of PFP intensity—that is, the degree to which compensation depends on performance (Gerhart, 2017; Kim et al., 2022). PFP intensity refers to the ratio of performance-contingent variable pay (i.e., incentives) to guaranteed pay (i.e., base salary). At high levels of PFP intensity, individuals receive a lower base salary but stand to gain significantly more through performance-based incentives (Balkin et al., 2015; Milgrom & Roberts, 1992). Conversely, low PFP intensity offers a higher base salary with lower variable components. In this spectrum, fixed pay—which guarantees all compensation regardless of performance—represents the lowest point of PFP intensity. At the opposite end is a compensation scheme entirely dependent on performance, with no guaranteed base pay, representing maximum PFP intensity. For many organizations in the private sector, the key question is not whether to implement PFP, but rather how much PFP intensity is optimal (Gerhart & Milkovich, 1990).

Gerhart and Fang (2014) articulated two opposing concerns regarding the appropriate level of PFP intensity. On one hand, if PFP intensity is too low, it may fail to motivate employees or influence performance, implying that a certain threshold of intensity is necessary for PFP systems to be effective. This aligns with research by Mitra and colleagues (1997, 2016), who emphasized the importance of crossing a cognitive threshold—known as the just noticeable difference or the smallest meaningful pay increase—for incentives to influence behavior and performance. On the other hand, drawing on agency theory, Gerhart and Fang cautioned that excessively high PFP intensity may result in increased agency costs due to misaligned interests between principals (employers) and agents (employees). Because employees tend to be risk-averse and favor guaranteed income (i.e., fixed pay) over uncertain earnings (i.e., variable pay), employers adopting high PFP intensity must often provide higher compensation differentials as risk premiums (Milgrom & Roberts, 1992; Prendergast, 1999). Furthermore, when employees are strongly incentivized to achieve high performance, there may be greater temptation to engage in opportunistic behaviors. Employers must therefore invest more in selecting trustworthy employees, monitoring behaviors, and designing robust performance evaluations (Milgrom & Roberts, 1992). In summary, agency theory suggests that beyond a certain point, the benefits of heightened incentive structures may be outweighed by the increased risks and costs associated with employee risk aversion and opportunism.
text:
Incentive Effects of Different PFP Intensities on Creative Performance
A logical extension of the fixed pay versus PFP dichotomy would be to examine varying strengths of PFP—that is, PFP intensity (Gerhart, 2017; Kim et al., 2022). PFP intensity indicates the degree to which payouts (i.e., base pay and incentives) are linked to performance, determining the ratio of variable pay contingent on performance (i.e., incentives) to guaranteed pay (i.e., base pay). In this regard, high PFP intensity provides lower base pay but higher incentives for higher performance (Balkin et al., 2015; Milgrom & Roberts, 1992). Low PFP intensity, meanwhile, guarantees a higher base pay with lower incentives. Hence, fixed pay, where payouts are fully guaranteed without any risks (in the form of incentives), conceptually represents the lowest PFP intensity. At the other extreme, the case in which payouts depend entirely on one’s performance without any guaranteed base pay indicates the highest PFP intensity. For many private sector firms, the question is not whether to use PFP but what the appropriate level of PFP intensity should be (Gerhart & Milkovich, 1990).
Gerhart and Fang (2014) raised two contrasting concerns regarding PFP intensity. On the one hand, if PFP intensity is too low, PFP will not influence one’s motivation and performance, meaning that, for a PFP scheme to be effective, PFP intensity should be sufficiently high. This is consistent with the work by Mitra et al. (1997, 2016) on just noticeable difference/smallest meaningful pay increase, which focused on the need for PFP to be sufficiently large to pass a cognitive threshold and influence performance. On the other hand, grounded in agency theory, Gerhart and Fang (2014) proposed that high PFP intensity raises agency costs prompted by incongruent motivation and preferences between a principal (i.e., employer) and an agent (i.e., employee). Specifically, given that employees are risk-averse and tend to prefer certain payments (i.e., fixed pay) to uncertain payments (i.e., variable pay, incentives), while higher PFP intensity schemes are typically designed to involve greater pay at risk for employees, employers need to offer higher compensation differentials as risk premiums under such schemes (Milgrom & Roberts, 1992; Prendergast, 1999). Additionally, to the degree that employees try to achieve higher performance in opportunistic ways to earn bigger rewards attached to higher PFP intensity schemes, greater costs will be imposed on employers to select conscientious employees, monitor their behavior, and design and implement appropriate performance evaluations (Milgrom & Roberts, 1992). In summary, agency theory suggests that when PFP intensity goes above a certain level, the advantages of higher incentives might be offset by the costs associated with offering risk premiums for employees’ risk-bearing behaviors and managing their opportunistic behaviors.

Observers' concern with the morality of leniency is understandable, as punishment is widely regarded as a foundational component of justice and ethical conduct (van Prooijen, 2018). According to deontological ethics, adherence to established rules, norms, and standards is essential for preserving social order and safeguarding employee well-being. Punishment thus serves a critical moral function by deterring misconduct and preventing harm (Kant, 1785/2012). From this perspective, leniency may be seen as a moral transgression, violating key justice principles such as equity, reciprocity, and procedural consistency (Graham et al., 2011). Given that organizational leaders have the authority to enforce punishment (Podsakoff et al., 2010), they are viewed as responsible for maintaining ethical and just standards (Kant, 1785/2012). Consequently, when leaders exhibit leniency, it is often perceived as morally inappropriate and unjust.

Although prior research demonstrates that employees often respond negatively to leaders’ moral misconduct (Skarlicki & Kulik, 2005), findings about how observers react to leniency remain mixed. For example, Dang et al. (2017a) found that observers perceived leniency as unethical and responded punitively, whereas Zhu et al. (2021) found that perceptions of injustice associated with leniency could elicit prosocial behavior. Despite these divergent outcomes, both studies suggest that observers evaluate leniency through a moral lens. As they attempt to interpret lenient behavior, observers likely consider a range of moral factors, including judgments about the leader and their own moral intuitions. We argue that observers' sensemaking of leniency is a nuanced, socially constructed process (Bies et al., 2021), influenced by moral considerations not just about the leader’s decision, but also about the offender (Brubacher, 2019; Potter, 2009). In trying to make sense of leniency, observers may consider situational factors surrounding the offender that could render leniency more understandable. Deontological ethics suggest that while justice necessitates punishment, it also allows for context-sensitive interpretations of whether the leader’s response appropriately fits the offense (Kant, 1785/2012; Potter, 2009).

Drawing from research on both punishment (Kant, 1785/2012; van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2012) and compassion (Atkins & Parker, 2012; Dutton et al., 2014; Goetz et al., 2010), we explore how moral considerations for the offender shape observers’ reactions to leader leniency. Punishment research has shown that employees ascribe significance to the way leaders handle misconduct; when leaders deviate from normative punishment expectations, perceived unfairness often leads to negative reactions (Maitlis et al., 2013). Leniency, in this sense, may be viewed as unfair—reflecting broad judgments about the inappropriateness or wrongfulness of a leader’s decision (Ambrose et al., 2015). This perceived unfairness is likely to elicit punitive responses, such as diminished support for lenient leaders—defined here as the withholding of psychological or instrumental support that might otherwise be provided (Bavik et al., 2020; Jolly et al., 2021).
text:
Observers’ consideration of the morality of leniency is understandable because punishment is considered a cornerstone of justice and ethics (van Prooijen, 2018). The deontology philosophy view articulates that adherence to rules, regulations, and standards maintains social stability and protects employees’ well-being. Punishment plays a vital role in preserving ethics by controlling misconduct and avoiding harm (Kant, 1785/2012). Thus, leniency violates moral principles associated with justice (a concern for equity, reciprocity, and consistency in treatment and with procedures; Graham et al., 2011). Given leaders’ authority to dispense punishment (Podsakoff et al., 2010), they are responsible for upholding ethics and justice standards (Kant, 1785/2012), and therefore, their leniency would typically be interpreted as morally inappropriate and unjust.

Although research has shown that employees respond negatively to leaders’ moral violations (see Skarlicki & Kulik, 2005), evidence has not painted a clear picture of observers’ reactions to leniency. For instance, Dang et al. (2017a) found that the unethical nature of leader leniency motivated punitive observer responses, while Zhu et al. (2021) found that the injustice of leniency prompted prosocial acts. Despite these different reactions, a commonality in these studies is that observers’ reactions are based on the moral implications of leniency. As observers try to make sense of leniency, they likely consider various moral aspects of the situation, such as moral considerations about the leader and of observers themselves. We contend that observers’ sensemaking of leniency is a complex social construction (Bies et al., 2021) that weighs moral consideration for the offender (Brubacher, 2019; Potter, 2009) as observers try to understand the leader’s choice to be lenient (Maitlis et al., 2013; Sandberg & Tsoukas, 2015). Observers likely consider factors about the offender, which may reasonably explain the leniency. Along these lines, principles of deontology articulate that while justice is served by punishing offenders, sensemaking also accounts for whether the reaction is appropriate for the offender (Kant, 1785/2012; Potter, 2009).

Integrating research on punishment (Kant, 1785/2012; van Prooijen, 2018; Zhu et al., 2012) and compassion (Atkins & Parker, 2012; Dutton et al., 2014; Goetz et al., 2010), we explain how observers’ moral consideration for offenders influences their reactions to leaders’ leniency. Scholarship on punishment has emphasized that employees derive meaning from leader responses to misconduct; when leaders violate punishment norms, the unfairness of this promotes negative reactions (Maitlis et al., 2013). Leniency, then, is likely to be judged as unfair (global perceptions of the inappropriateness or wrongfulness of a leader’s actions and decisions; Ambrose et al., 2015), motivating punitive reactions. Therefore, the unfairness of leader leniency should motivate observers to withhold support to lenient leaders (of them providing psychological or instrumental resources given to leaders; Bavik et al., 2020; Jolly et al., 2021).

In line with the view that human agency stems from the interplay between person and situation (Bandura, 2006), focusing solely on self-change represents only the personal dimension of agentic change, neglecting the situational aspect. Within organizations, employees' situations are fundamentally shaped by their job designs—that is, the constellation of tasks and relationships for which they are responsible (Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991). While prior research largely emphasizes mindsets about the self, we argue that employees can also develop fixed or growth mindsets about their jobs. Just as individuals may mistakenly view personal abilities as fixed when they are actually malleable (Chiu et al., 1997; Heslin et al., 2005, 2006), employees may similarly perceive their job designs as more rigid than they are in practice (Berg et al., 2010; Gordon et al., 2018; Kooij et al., 2017). As Ilgen and Hollenbeck (1991, p. 197) describe, jobs are often designed to be “bureaucratic” and “quasistatic," meaning that their elements operate independently of the jobholder and remain stable over time. During onboarding and training, employees are socialized to perform their job as a series of routines (Nelson & Winter, 1982; Van Maanen & Schein, 1979). Over time, the repeated enactment of these routines renders them automatic, often leading employees to stop questioning their adaptability (Ashforth & Fried, 1988; Bargh & Chartrand, 1999; Dane, 2011; Langer, 1989; Parker et al., 1997, 2019). Consequently, even those in high-autonomy or high-ranking roles may come to view their jobs as relatively immutable (Berg et al., 2010).

To articulate the idea that employees may hold beliefs about the flexibility of their job designs, we introduce the concept of a job-growth mindset—defined as the belief in the malleability of the tasks and relationships that constitute one’s job. This concept complements the well-established notion of a self-growth mindset. Specifically, we propose that promoting both mindsets together—an integrated orientation we call a dual-growth mindset—may be especially powerful in fostering agentic change at work. Notably, cultivating a dual-growth mindset may not only increase employees’ capacity for meaningful change, but also enhance their personal well-being—an outcome that is known to be difficult to improve in lasting ways (Fisher, 2010). Research on the hedonic treadmill suggests that happiness often returns to a stable baseline after positive events (Brickman & Campbell, 1971; Diener & Oishi, 2005; Kahneman, 1999). However, studies have also shown that intentional, agentic efforts can lead to durable improvements in well-being (Lyubomirsky et al., 2005). We suggest that cultivating a dual-growth mindset may be key to enabling such sustained agentic change, resulting in more enduring increases in happiness. In contrast, fostering only self-growth or job-growth mindset in isolation may fall short of driving the magnitude of change needed to produce lasting improvements in well-being.
text:
following the view that both the person and situation must be considered for a full understanding of human agency (Bandura, 2006), making changes to oneself represent only the person side of agentic change, overlooking the situation side. In organizations, employees’ situations are shaped by their job designs, or the bundle of tasks and relationships for which they are responsible (Ilgen & Hollenbeck, 1991). Whereas prior research has focused on mindsets about the self, we propose that employees can also hold fixed or growth mindsets about their jobs. Just as people may mistakenly view abilities as fixed that are in fact malleable (Chiu et al., 1997; Heslin et al., 2005, 2006), employees may view their job designs as more fixed than they are in reality (Berg et al., 2010; Gordon et al., 2018; Kooij et al., 2017). According to Ilgen and Hollenbeck (1991, p. 197), managers often design jobs to be “bureaucratic” and “quasistatic,” so that the “elements comprising the jobs exist independently of job incumbents,” and “do not change on a day-to-day basis, but rather are relatively constant over time.” During socialization and training processes, employees learn their jobs as a set of routines (Nelson & Winter, 1982; Van Maanen & Schein, 1979). Research has long shown that repeated enactment of routines makes them automatic, to the point that employees often do not reflect on whether these routines can be changed (Ashforth & Fried, 1988; Bargh & Chartrand, 1999; Dane, 2011; Langer, 1989; Parker et al., 1997, 2019). Thus, even employees in high-ranking, autonomous positions may come to view their jobs as relatively fixed (Berg et al., 2010).

To capture the idea that employees may hold mindsets about the flexibility of their job designs, we introduce the concept of job-growth mindset, or beliefs about the malleability of the bundle of tasks and relationships that comprise one’s job. Job-growth mindset complements the notion of self-growth mindset from past research. In particular, we propose that fostering both mindsets at the same time, which we call dual-growth mindset, may be most conducive to agentic change at work. Importantly, fostering dual-growth mindset may enable employees to make agentic changes that boost their own happiness, a valued outcome that is notoriously difficult to durably change (Fisher, 2010). According to research on the hedonic treadmill (Brickman & Campbell, 1971; Diener & Oishi, 2005; Kahneman, 1999), positive events may spark temporary increases in happiness, but individuals often quickly return to their baseline level of happiness. At the same time, evidence suggests that individuals can sustainably boost their happiness through intentional, agentic change (Lyubomirsky et al., 2005). We propose that fostering dual-growth mindset may enable such agentic change, leading to relatively durable gains in happiness. In contrast, fostering either self- or job-growth mindset alone may fail to produce agentic changes that are substantial enough to sustainably enhance happiness.

The current research introduces the concept of a dual-growth mindset as a promising yet previously underexplored driver of employee happiness in organizational settings. Growing scholarly attention has shifted toward bottom-up, employee-initiated efforts to cultivate workplace happiness, moving beyond top-down, manager-led interventions (Grant & Ashford, 2008; Parker et al., 2010). Despite this shift, much of the literature on proactive and agentic behavior has largely neglected the role of mindsets as a foundational catalyst for such change. Our work contributes to this gap by distinguishing between growth mindsets about the self and about the job—arguing that both are critical. We find that cultivating a dual-growth mindset—endorsing growth potential in both the individual and the work environment—leads to sustained increases in happiness after six months. In contrast, fostering either mindset in isolation fails to produce lasting gains. Supplementary analyses indicate that job crafting intentions mediate these effects. Additionally, increases in dual-growth mindset appear more enduring than those observed for either self- or job-specific growth mindsets alone. Altogether, these findings offer theoretical insight into dual-growth mindset as a powerful, bottom-up mechanism for achieving greater well-being in organizations.

Our research provides a plausible explanation for the modest and inconsistent effects associated with self-growth mindsets as reported in recent studies and meta-analyses (Burgoyne et al., 2020; Sisk et al., 2018). For example, two meta-analyses revealed that the benefits of self-growth mindsets were generally weak and varied considerably based on sample and context (Sisk et al., 2018). Our findings suggest that lasting changes in happiness may require individuals to believe not only in their own capacity for growth but also in the potential to reshape the environments in which they operate. Furthermore, our research invites a new perspective on the well-established construct of person–environment fit (Kristof-Brown et al., 2005), which is often conceptualized with static definitions of person and environment. By introducing the idea that both entities can be viewed as modifiable, our work opens the door to a more dynamic understanding of how employees can proactively create better fits between themselves and their evolving work contexts.

In addition, our findings offer a fresh lens through which to understand the frequent shortcomings of job redesign interventions in delivering long-term benefits (e.g., Frank & Hackman, 1975; Griffin, 1991). Traditional job enrichment efforts often involve assigning new, structured tasks and training employees to perform them (Campion & McClelland, 1993). However, when such redesigns are paired with a dual-growth mindset, employees may be better positioned to personalize their enriched roles, resulting in more meaningful and sustainable improvements. Similarly, dual-growth mindsets may enhance the effectiveness of custom work arrangements, which are becoming increasingly common in modern organizations (Liao et al., 2016; Rousseau et al., 2006). By recognizing and acting on opportunities to tailor their work, employees with dual-growth mindsets may craft more optimal arrangements. Nonetheless, future research is needed to explore the boundaries of this benefit, particularly regarding the timing of mindset cultivation—prematurely fostering dual-growth mindsets could unintentionally lead employees to overextend themselves by accepting more demands than they can effectively manage.
text:
The present research introduces dual-growth mindset as a potentially powerful—but previously overlooked—driver of employee happiness in organizations. Scholars have increasingly documented a shift in focus from top-down, manager-driven changes to the bottom-up initiative that employees can take to shape their own happiness at work (Grant & Ashford, 2008; Parker et al., 2010). However, this body of research on agentic and proactive behaviors has largely overlooked mindsets as a central engine of change. Our research introduces mindsets about the job as an important complement to mindsets about the self. We find that fostering dual-growth mindset yielded gains in happiness 6 months later, while fostering either self- or job-growth mindset alone did not yield lasting gains in happiness—supplementary analyses suggest that these effects are mediated by job crafting intentions. Furthermore, we find that increases in dual-growth mindset are more sustainable than increases in either self- or job-growth mindset in isolation. These results uncover valuable theoretical insights on dual-growth mindset as a bottom-up vehicle for change—and ultimately greater happiness—in organizations.

First, our research may help to explain the limited effects of self-growth mindsets that have been found in recent research and meta-analyses (Burgoyne et al., 2020; Sisk et al., 2018). In two meta-analyses, self-growth mindset had weak effects overall, which varied by sample and context (Sisk et al., 2018). Our work suggests that to achieve sustainable gains, it may be important for individuals to believe they can change their environments as well as themselves. Second, our work opens up new possibilities for research on person–environment fit (Kristof-Brown et al., 2005), which tends to treat the person and environment as fixed entities. Our studies hint that seeing both entities as flexible may help employees create a more optimal fit. Bringing the concept of dual-growth mindset to research on person–environment fit may offer a more dynamic perspective on how person–environment fit evolves (or not) over time.

Third, our research offers a new explanation for why job redesign interventions often fail to achieve longer term gains (e.g., Frank & Hackman, 1975; Griffin, 1991). Typically, when jobs are enriched, employees are given a new set of fixed tasks and trained in the skills necessary to accomplish those tasks (Campion & McClelland, 1993). If job redesign is complemented with dual-growth mindsets, employees may be better equipped to customize their redesigned jobs and accrue greater benefits over time. Similarly, dual-growth mindsets may help advance research on how employees and organizations can leverage the benefits of customized work arrangements, which are increasingly prevalent and important (Liao et al., 2016; Rousseau et al., 2006). Fostering dual-growth mindsets may help employees recognize opportunities for more optimal customized work arrangements. However, more research is needed to understand whether and when this is the case, as fostering dual-growth mindsets before employees customize their work arrangements could backfire by compelling them to take on more demands than they can handle.
