Firm–stakeholder dialogue is a continuous “structured interactive and proactive process” (Kaptein & Van Tulder, 2017: 22) that enables direct, two-way communication between a firm and its stakeholders. This unmediated exchange of information helps to surface relevant insights (Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012), thereby reducing information asymmetry between the parties. Dialogue allows stakeholders to hear how the firm frames and interprets a given issue, while the firm gains insight into the stakeholder’s perspective and understanding of that same issue (Brønn & Brønn, 2003).

As a direct communication channel, stakeholder dialogue allows firms to share information that is more detailed (i.e., greater in volume), more accurate and specific (i.e., higher in quality), and more nuanced (i.e., more diverse in content) than information typically disseminated through mediated channels such as the media. For instance, managers can deliver in-depth explanations of a project’s benefits and consequences, thereby increasing information quantity. They can also avoid distortion by intermediaries, improving the precision and quality of their message. Additionally, managers can share varied information about how value will be generated and distributed (Asher, Mahoney, & Mahoney, 2005; Garcia‐Castro & Aguilera, 2015), as well as clarify their orientation toward stakeholders (Berman, Wicks, Kotha, & Jones, 1999; Crilly & Sloan, 2012; Jones, 1995). Through these channels, dialogue plays a key role in reducing information asymmetry and enabling stakeholders to better assess the firm.

However, firm–stakeholder dialogue is not without risks. Firms may attempt to manipulate perceptions by strategically misrepresenting or selectively disclosing information. Research on anticipatory impression management finds that organizations often use diversionary tactics to guide stakeholders’ attention away from contentious issues and shape how those issues are understood, thereby minimizing the likelihood or intensity of conflict (Elsbach et al., 1998). Compared to blatant misconduct, misleading communication regarding firm impacts or performance—sometimes framed as “puffery” (Hoffman, 2005)—may not be viewed by managers as unethical, but rather as a pragmatic approach to minimizing stakeholder pushback. Hence, while dialogue offers a platform for more robust information sharing, it does not ensure transparency or the integrity of the information conveyed.

Emerging research has emphasized that firm–stakeholder dialogue can extend beyond the simple exchange of information (Lane, 2020; Nartey et al., 2022; Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012). Dialogue becomes a collaborative space wherein both firm and stakeholder engage in an intentional process “to increase understanding and shared meaning, raise awareness of taken-for-granted assumptions and beliefs, enhance learning, encourage collaboration and enhance the quality of decisions and actions” (Roper, Zorn, & Weaver, 2004: 11). Such dialogic engagement fosters openness to learning and shifts in perspective (Brønn & Brønn, 2003), ultimately leading to “the co-creation of shared understandings by company and stakeholder” (Johnson-Cramer, Berman, & Post, 2003: 148).
text:
Firm–stakeholder dialogue is an ongoing “structured interactive and proactive process” (Kaptein & Van Tulder, 2017: 22) that facilitates the exchange of information between firms and their stakeholders through direct (unmediated) two-way communication. Firm–stakeholder dialogue surfaces relevant information (Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012), thus reducing the asymmetry of information between the two parties. Through dialogue, the stakeholder hears the firm’s definition and interpretation of an issue, and the firm learns about the stakeholder’s definition and interpretation of that issue (Brønn & Brønn, 2003).

As a venue of unmediated information exchange, stakeholder dialogue affords a firm the opportunity to provide directly to the stakeholder information that is more detailed (i.e., in higher quantity), more precise (i.e., of higher quality), and more nuanced (i.e., more varied) than media information. Managers can provide detailed information about the benefits and impacts of a project, increasing the quantity of information available to the stakeholder. They can provide more precise information, avoiding distortions by infomediaries (e.g., the media); thus, the quality of information is also higher. Further, they can provide different types of information about how value will be created and allocated (Asher, Mahoney, & Mahoney, 2005; Garcia‐Castro & Aguilera, 2015) and about their stakeholder orientation (Berman, Wicks, Kotha, & Jones, 1999; Crilly & Sloan, 2012; Jones, 1995). In all these ways, stakeholder dialogue reduces information asymmetries between the firm and its stakeholder, enabling the stakeholder to better evaluate the firm.

Firms engaging in dialogue with stakeholders may also try to mislead their stakeholders by manipulating or misrepresenting the information they provide. Research on anticipatory impression management has shown that organizations often engage in tactics to divert stakeholders’ attention from critical issues and to frame the interpretation of those issues in ways that diminish the onset and escalation of conflict (Elsbach et al., 1998). Compared with severe acts of organizational misconduct, misleading stakeholders on the benefits and impacts of a firm’s operations—and other actions that can be defended as “puffery” (Hoffman, 2005)—may be perceived by managers not as wrongdoing but as a necessary effort to manage the message and potential conflict with stakeholders. Thus, while firm–stakeholder dialogue is an opportunity for the firm to provide more information, the dialogue does not guarantee that the information provided to stakeholders is accurate.

Recent scholarship has shown that firm–stakeholder dialogue can go much further than a two-way exchange of information (Lane, 2020; Nartey et al., 2022; Theunissen & Wan Noordin, 2012). Dialogue brings together the firm and the stakeholder in a purposeful effort “to increase understanding and shared meaning, raise awareness of taken for granted assumptions and beliefs, enhance learning, encourage collaboration and enhance the quality of decisions and actions” (Roper, Zorn, & Weaver, 2004: 11). As such, it increases participants’ willingness to learn and to adapt one’s perspective (Brønn & Brønn, 2003), leading to “the co-creation of shared understandings by company and stakeholder” (Johnson-Cramer, Berman, & Post, 2003: 148).

Limitations of Existing Work  
Given the wide range of occupations people can hold, studies examining occupational differences require large and diverse samples to adequately represent various job roles. However, such large-scale studies often rely on abbreviated personality assessments due to practical constraints. For instance, Törnroos et al. (2019) and Wolfram (2023) utilized only three items to measure each of the broad and complex Big Five personality domains, limiting both the reliability and the generalizability of their results (McCrae, 2015). Importantly, the Big Five are not singular traits but expansive domains that encompass numerous distinct traits (Bainbridge et al., 2022; Goldberg, 1995), making it impossible to capture their full scope accurately or equitably with such minimal measures (McCrae & Mõttus, 2019). For example, Wolfram (2023) assessed only anxiety within neuroticism and sociability within extraversion (Lang et al., 2011), omitting many other essential facets like depression, dependence, social sensitivity, and impulsivity for neuroticism, or warmth, assertiveness, and adventurousness for extraversion (e.g., Irwing et al., 2024). This narrow measurement approach risks conflating the broader domains with their limited subcomponents, potentially leading to misleading interpretations.

Additionally, both studies were conducted solely within a British sociocultural context, raising questions about the global applicability of their findings. Even if these studies were otherwise methodologically flawless, it would be premature to generalize their results to other regions such as North America, Argentina, or Estonia, or to use them for practical decisions like hiring, counseling, or coaching in those contexts. Cultural norms and societal structures influence how personality traits align with occupational choices. For instance, in some cultures, individuals may select careers based on familial expectations rather than personal predispositions, potentially muting trait-based occupational differences. Conversely, in countries with more fluid labor markets or robust career counseling systems, occupational personality differences may be more pronounced. The extent to which these findings replicate across diverse cultural contexts will ultimately determine their broader validity and utility.

A further limitation of previous research is the exclusive reliance on a single-method approach to personality assessment—namely, self-reports. While widely used, self-reports may not fully capture the complexity of personality, especially in occupational settings. Informant reports—evaluations by others who know the individual well—can provide complementary and sometimes more accurate insights, as supported by findings in organizational psychology (Connelly et al., 2022; Connelly & McAbee, 2024; Connelly & Ones, 2010) and theories such as the trait–reputation–identity model (McAbee & Connelly, 2016) and socioanalytic theory (Hogan & Blickle, 2013). Method-specific variance accounts for a substantial portion of the variability in personality scores (McCrae & Mõttus, 2019), which can distort perceived differences between occupational groups. For decades, scholars (e.g., Connelly & Ones, 2010; Hofstee, 1994; McCrae et al., 1998; Schmidt & Hunter, 1996; Vazire, 2006) have cautioned against relying solely on self-reports for evaluating latent constructs like personality, as doing so may lead to biased or incomplete conclusions. Nonetheless, personality psychology—particularly in the context of occupational differences—rarely employs multi-method assessments, likely due to the logistical challenges involved in collecting such data.
text:
Limitations of Existing Work
Due to the variety of jobs that people can hold, studies on occupational differences require large samples to sample these jobs, and such studies can usually only accommodate minimalist personality assessments. Accordingly, Törnroos et al. (2019) and Wolfram (2023) used only three items to assess each broad and multifaceted Big Five domain, limiting the findings’ reliability and generalizability (McCrae, 2015). The Big Five are not traits per se but broad and multifaceted domains that summarize many traits (Bainbridge et al., 2022; Goldberg, 1995), so it is impossible to achieve a full and balanced representation of them with only three items (McCrae & Mõttus, 2019). In Wolfram (2023), for example, only the anxiety aspect of neuroticism and the sociability aspect of extraversion were assessed (Lang et al., 2011), whereas both domains encompass numerous other traits such as depression, need for acceptance, dependence, social sensitivity, lack of self-efficacy and impulsiveness for neuroticism, and warmth, vigor, positivity, assertiveness, and adventurousness for extraversion (e.g., Irwing et al., 2024). Using such limited measures risks confusing broad constructs with their narrow assessments.
Moreover, both of these large studies explored occupational differences in the same sociocultural context, Britain, so their findings’ generalizability to other populations requires further assessment. So, even if Wolfram (2023) and Törnroos et al. (2019) were methodologically perfect studies, it would be premature to assume that their findings accurately describe how people vary with occupations elsewhere in the world, say, Northern America, Argentina, or Estonia, and that these findings could be used for candidate selection, job counseling, or coaching in these regions. For example, in some societies, people may be comparatively more likely to choose occupations based on family traditions rather than their personal traits, reducing occupational personality differences; in contrast, in societies with particularly dynamic labor markets and/or extensive career counseling, occupational personality differences may be larger. Moreover, the traits valued at particular jobs may vary with societies. However, to the extent that occupational personality differences are replicated in cultures beyond Britain, their further generalizability becomes more plausible.
Another major limitation of the past studies is their reliance on a single trait assessment method. In addition to self-reported traits, informant reports can provide unique insights, particularly in the context of organizational psychology (Connelly et al., 2022; Connelly & McAbee, 2024; Connelly & Ones, 2010) and consistently with the trait–reputation–identity model (McAbee & Connelly, 2016) and socioanalytic theory (Hogan & Blickle, 2013). Method-specific variance constitutes a large fraction of the assessed trait score variance (McCrae & Mõttus, 2019) and could influence observed trait differences between occupations. For decades, many researchers (e.g., Connelly & Ones, 2010; Hofstee, 1994; McCrae et al., 1998; Schmidt & Hunter, 1996; Vazire, 2006) have emphasized that assessing latent constructs like personality traits with a single method, self-reports, can result in misleading conclusions. Yet, this is rarely done in personality psychology generally and occupational personality research specifically, likely because collecting multimethod data is considered difficult.

Moreover, research in ethics suggests that individuals’ perceptions of harm significantly shape the extent to which they ethically frame behaviors aimed at mitigating risk or preventing harm to others. Ethical framing refers to the degree to which a person interprets a behavior or situation as being imbued with moral or ethical significance (Tenbrunsel & Messick, 1999, p. 688). We propose that the more an individual perceives their actions as having the potential to harm others, the more inclined they will be to view those actions through an ethical lens.

The role of harm in ethical decision making is well-established across various strands of moral psychology and ethical theory (Kohlberg, 1969; Piaget, 1932; Turiel, 1983), with harm often considered the most central, frequent, and universally salient ethical concern (Graham et al., 2011; Haidt, 2007; W. Hofmann et al., 2014; Schein & Gray, 2018). The theory of dyadic morality, for instance, posits that “harm is the fundamental basis by which moral judgments are made and maintained” (Schein & Gray, 2018, p. 32). Additionally, research shows that actively causing harm is judged as more morally egregious than failing to prevent it, emphasizing the ethical salience of actions that create risk (Cushman et al., 2006, p. 1083). Such findings suggest that behaviors perceived to directly endanger others are particularly likely to be framed as ethical issues.

This principle—that perceived harm functions as a precursor to ethical perception—extends into legal theory and moral philosophy. The “harm principle” maintains that individuals and organizations have an ethical duty to prevent harm to others, and that preventing harm is widely recognized as a legitimate moral basis for legal prohibitions (Feinberg, 1984, 1986; Hart, 1961; Holtug, 2002, p. 24). In line with this view, behavior that is not associated with potential harm is less likely to be interpreted as ethically relevant (Feinberg, 1987). Therefore, we propose the following:
Hypothesis 2: Perceived harm is positively associated with the ethical framing of risk-mitigating behavior.

Bringing these arguments together, we contend that high-level construals reduce perceived harm and, in doing so, diminish the likelihood of ethical framing for actions intended to protect others. For example, consider an employee remotely monitoring offshore drilling operations from a command center located hundreds of miles away. This spatial distance fosters high-level (i.e., more abstract) construals of the rig’s work environment. Such high-level construals involve overlooking concrete risk details (e.g., deeming safety-relevant rig specifics as incidental, downplaying the probability of harm, or focusing on general goals—“why”—rather than specific prevention strategies—“how”). As a result, the employee is less likely to perceive a strong ethical imperative to engage in protective behaviors, whether aimed at safeguarding rig personnel, infrastructure, or the surrounding environment. Put differently:
Hypothesis 3: Work context construal has an indirect effect on the ethical framing of risk-mitigating behavior through perceived harm. Specifically, as a work context is construed at a higher (more abstract) level, perceived harm decreases, thereby reducing the likelihood of ethically framing risk-mitigating behaviors.
text:
Furthermore, research on ethics suggests that perceived harm likely influences the extent to which individuals ethically frame risk-mitigating behaviors or actions intended to decrease the potential for harm to befall others. Ethical framing refers to the degree to which an individual perceives a behavior or situation as “infused with ethical or moral considerations” (Tenbrunsel & Messick, 1999, p. 688). We argue that the more an individual perceives the potential for their behavior to harm others, the more likely they will frame this behavior with an ethical lens.

Research on ethics and morality has established that harm plays a central role in ethical decision making (Kohlberg, 1969; Piaget, 1932; Turiel, 1983), and harm is likely the most important, frequent, and universal ethical consideration (Graham et al., 2011; Haidt, 2007; W. Hofmann et al., 2014; Schein & Gray, 2018). For example, the theory of dyadic morality argues that “harm is the fundamental basis by which moral judgments are made and maintained” (Schein & Gray, 2018, p. 32). Relatedly, harming someone through one’s actions is “morally worse” than one’s inaction (Cushman et al., 2006, p. 1083)—meaning that behaving in ways that actively put others at risk is especially likely to be framed as an ethical issue.

This logic—that harm is the precursor to perceiving something as an ethical obligation—also extends to research on law and philosophy. For example, the “harm principle” suggests that individuals or organizations are ethically obligated to prevent harm to others, and “everyone agrees that prevention of harm to others is at least one of the morally acceptable reasons for criminal prohibitions” (Feinberg, 1984, 1986; Hart, 1961; Holtug, 2002, p. 24). Thus, individuals are unlikely to perceive a situation or behavior as having ethical implications if they do not associate it with harm (e.g., Feinberg, 1987). Therefore:
Hypothesis 2: Perceived harm is positively related to ethical framing of risk-mitigating behavior.

Tying these arguments together, we argue that high-level construals reduce the perception of harm, which correspondingly decreases the ethical framing of behaviors intended to reduce the risk to others. For example, an employee monitoring offshore drilling operations while working from an onshore command center (e.g., hundreds of miles away) will construe the rig work context at a higher (more abstract) level. High-level work context construals involve overlooking the potential for harm to occur on the rig (e.g., deeming safety-relevant details of the rig as incidental; downplaying likelihood of harmful events on the rig; focusing on “why” vs. “how” to prevent harm on the rig). This will in turn reduce the perceived ethical obligation to engage in behaviors intended to protect the people on the rig, the rig itself, or the surrounding environment (i.e., ethical framing of risk-mitigating behaviors). Therefore, we hypothesize:
Hypothesis 3: There is a significant indirect effect of work context construal on ethical framing of risk-mitigating behavior via perceived harm. As a work context is construed at a higher (more abstract) level, risk-mitigating behaviors will be less likely to be ethically framed, due to decreased perceived harm.

When employees develop a stronger self-growth mindset without a corresponding increase in job-growth mindset, they may feel motivated to enhance their skills and personal attributes but perceive limited opportunities to apply these changes within the confines of their existing job structures. For example, a salesperson might work to improve their public speaking abilities but find they are unable to incorporate more public speaking into their role, ultimately limiting the change they experience at work. Conversely, when employees increase in job-growth mindset but not in self-growth mindset, they may recognize opportunities to reshape aspects of their job but feel constrained by their perceived lack of relevant skills or traits. For instance, a salesperson who excels in one-on-one communication but lacks confidence in public speaking may choose to schedule more individual meetings at trade shows rather than rise to the challenge of speaking on stage. In both scenarios, employees may quickly adapt to the modest changes they are able to achieve by adjusting only either the self or the job, limiting longer-term impact on happiness. Moreover, the benefits of fostering only one type of growth mindset may diminish over time if those changes do not translate into lasting workplace developments. Without fresh, meaningful avenues for change, employees may begin reverting to a more fixed mindset. In contrast, cultivating a dual-growth mindset—one that encompasses both personal development and changes to the job—may offer more durable and impactful opportunities for transforming one’s work life.

In summary, promoting either a self- or job-growth mindset in isolation may lead to incremental improvements that are quickly offset by the hedonic treadmill effect, whereby initial gains in well-being taper off. In contrast, fostering a dual-growth mindset may support more substantial and intentional changes that are less prone to this effect. However, these deeper changes may require time to yield improvements in happiness, as individuals navigate learning curves, develop new competencies, and gain confidence in newly undertaken challenges (Campion & McClelland, 1993; Reis et al., 2000). Based on this reasoning, we hypothesize that increases in dual-growth mindset will lead to meaningful gains in happiness over time, whereas increases in either self- or job-growth mindset alone will not produce enduring improvements in well-being.

To investigate this hypothesis, we conducted two experimental studies: a quasi-experiment in a Fortune 500 technology firm (Study 1) and a more tightly controlled laboratory-style experiment involving employees from a diverse range of organizations and roles (Study 2). In both studies, participants were randomly assigned to one of three mindset interventions—self-growth, job-growth, or dual-growth—and their happiness was assessed prior to the intervention, as well as at short-term and long-term follow-ups. In Study 1, we used peer and manager ratings to evaluate changes in happiness, allowing us to assess whether these interventions induced effects that were visible to others—thereby reducing potential demand effects and social desirability bias. Recognizing the importance of subjective well-being, Study 2 complemented these findings by measuring self-reported happiness along with direct assessments of self- and job-growth mindsets. In both studies, we additionally gathered data on participants’ job crafting intentions (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001), which we used to examine whether agentic change plausibly mediates the relationship between dual-growth mindset and lasting improvements in happiness.

when employees increase in self-growth mindset but not job-growth mindset, they may be motivated to change their abilities and traits but see limited opportunity to leverage these changes within their (fixed) job designs. Continuing with the earlier example, salespeople may want to improve their public speaking skills but fail to increase the amount of public speaking in their job, limiting the change they ultimately experience in their work. Conversely, when employees increase in job-growth mindset but not self-growth mindset, they may see relatively little opportunity for change given their (fixed) abilities and traits. For instance, salespeople who are skilled at pitching one-on-one but not on stage may add more individual meetings at trade shows but not stretch to the task of pitching publicly. Employees may quickly habituate to the small changes they are able to make from adjusting only the self or job, resulting in little impact on enduring happiness. Moreover, gains in self- or job-growth mindset may only persist as long as employees can achieve corresponding changes on the job. As employees exhaust the small changes that can be made by fostering either self- or job-growth mindset alone, their mindsets may become more fixed, as they no longer see fresh opportunities for change. Increases in dual-growth mindset should be more sustainable, as employees should have more long-term opportunities to change their work lives in relatively substantial ways.

In sum, whereas fostering either self- or job-growth mindset alone may lead to smaller changes that are soon vanquished by the hedonic treadmill, fostering dual-growth mindset may spur relatively substantial agentic changes that are less susceptible to the hedonic treadmill. However, these relatively substantial changes may take time to translate into gains in happiness, as employees work their way through the learning curve to build skills and feel competent in their new challenges (Campion & McClelland, 1993; Reis et al., 2000). As such, we hypothesize that increases in dual-growth mindset yield gains in happiness that take time to develop, while increases in either self- or job-growth mindset alone do not yield lasting gains in happiness.

To test this hypothesis, we conducted two experimental studies: a quasi-experiment in a Fortune 500 technology company (Study 1), and a more controlled experiment with employees in a variety of organizations and jobs (Study 2). In both experiments, we assigned participants to one of three mindset interventions (self, job, or dual) and measured happiness before the intervention and at short- and long-term posttests. In Study 1, we measured happiness using ratings from participants’ managers and peers, testing whether the mindset interventions produced gains in happiness that were observable by others. Although this helped to mitigate demand characteristics and social desirability biases, the subjective experience of happiness is also important. Thus, in Study 2, we measured self-reported happiness, as well as self- and job-growth mindset directly. In both experiments, we collected supplementary data on participants’ job crafting intentions (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001), which we used to test the plausibility of our theorizing that agentic change mediates the impact of dual-growth mindset on happiness.

Scholars across psychology, sociology, and organizational science have long examined the role of emotions (Menges & Kilduff, 2015). Emotions are affective states that arise in response to perceived challenges or opportunities, fundamentally shaping how individuals interpret and respond to experiences (Ashforth & Humphrey, 1995; Keltner & Gross, 1999). These affective responses are typically triggered by identifiable stimuli—events or objects encountered in one's environment (Frijda, 2007; Izard, 2009). Among the most emotionally impactful stimuli are those occurring in social interactions, especially with organizational leaders (Basch & Fisher, 2000; Dasborough, 2006; Gaddis, Connelly & Mumford, 2004; Mignonac & Herrbach, 2004).

Emotional expressions function as potent tools of social influence (Ekman, 1972; Keltner & Haidt, 1999), conveying social cues that shape others’ emotional states, thoughts, and behaviors (Hareli & Rafaeli, 2008). To investigate these dynamics, we draw on the Emotions as Social Information (EASI) model (Van Kleef, De Dreu & Manstead, 2010), which provides a comprehensive framework for understanding how emotional expressions influence observers. The EASI model proposes that beyond eliciting affective reactions, emotional expressions initiate inferential processes through which observers form judgments about the emoting individual. Observers interpret these expressions by inferring the other’s mental state and intentions (De Melo, Carnevale, Read & Gratch, 2013). For instance, happiness may signal progress toward goals, while anger suggests frustration or obstruction by others—both shaping observers’ understanding and their own behavioral responses (Van Kleef et al., 2010). Thus, emotional expressions not only affect how others feel but also influence how they think and act by signaling underlying appraisals and intentions—even when the observers’ own emotions remain unchanged (Van Kleef & Côté, 2022).

While prior research has thoroughly explored the general influence of emotional displays, scholars have increasingly recognized variations in emotional impact depending on intensity and timing. Specifically, studies highlight that the peak—or maximum intensity—of emotional expression during an interaction holds disproportionate weight in shaping outcomes. According to EASI theory, stronger emotional expressions enhance inferential effects on observers (Van Kleef & Côté, 2022). This aligns with theories of judgment and memory, which suggest that individuals rely on salient features—rather than the full scope—of an experience when forming evaluations (Ariely & Carmon, 2000). Notably, individuals tend to anchor their evaluation of an event to its most intense emotional moment, rather than averaging across all experiences within it (Ariely & Carmon, 2000; Geng, Chen, Lam & Zheng, 2013). For our purposes, each “event” refers to all comments a board member makes regarding a particular agenda item. Given cognitive processing limitations, individuals highlight the peak intensity of emotion expressed—here, the most intense emotional moment in a director’s comments—as most influential. This peak emotional expression plays a critical role in shaping others’ overarching impressions of an interaction, surpassing the effects of average or generalized emotional tone (Fredrickson & Kahneman, 1993; Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber & Redelmeier, 1993).
text:
Scholars have long studied emotions in psychology, sociology, and organization science (Menges & Kilduff, 2015). In reaction to challenges and opportunities, emotions are the states of feeling that accompany one’s experience (Ashforth & Humphrey, 1995; Keltnert & Gross, 1999). Emotions arise in response to a discernible stimulus, such as an event or object (Frijda, 2007; Izard, 2009). Stimuli with the greatest emotional impact are those that occur in social interaction, particularly with leaders in the organization (Basch & Fisher, 2000; Dasborough, 2006; Gaddis, Connelly & Mumford, 2004; Mignonac & Herrbach, 2004).

Emotional expressions are one of the most powerful forms of social influence (Ekman, 1972; Keltner & Haidt, 1999), affecting others by communicating social information and potentially shaping their emotions, thoughts, and behaviors (Hareli & Rafaeli, 2008). From this rich literature on emotions, we adopt the emotions as social information (EASI) model (Van Kleef, De Dreu & Manstead, 2010) as our guiding framework. Building on previous work, this perspective recognizes that individuals are influenced by emotional expressions in their own affective reactions, but, further, that observing those expressions also triggers inferential processes that shape observer cognition and behavior. In these inferential processes, observers appraise the mental state and infer the intentions of the emoting individual (De Melo, Carnevale, Read & Gratch, 2013). The emotional expression itself informs others’ attributions. For example, observers appraise displays of happiness as indicative of the emoter’s progress toward goals, while anger displays convey a message of goal obstruction caused by another party; both expressions then shape observers’ own behavioral responses (Van Kleef et al., 2010). Thus, emotional displays can lead observers to certain inferences about the emoter and the situation, and shape their behavior—independent of emotions experienced by observers themselves2 (Van Kleef & Côté, 2022).

While much research has examined generalized effects of displayed emotions, scholars have also identified the varying impact of emotions at different levels. Studies have shown that it is the peak, or maximum level, of emotions displayed in conversations that are influential to outcomes. EASI theory suggests that the effects of emotional expression on observers’ inferential processes are strongest when emoters’ displays are also stronger (Van Kleef & Côté, 2022). When individuals reflect on an event, they do not consider the exchange in its totality but rely on certain features of their experience (Ariely & Carmon, 2000). Prior work suggests that it is the peak level of a person’s experience that determines their evaluation of the whole event (Ariely & Carmon, 2000; Geng, Chen, Lam & Zheng, 2013). In our case, the event constitutes all comments made by a board member in discussion of an agenda item. Based on cognitive limitations, individuals do not continually average detailed characteristics of stimuli but instead process their peak (Ariely & Carmon, 2000)—here, the strongest display of emotional expressions within a director’s comments on a given agenda item. Peak displayed emotions are the most memorable and influential on others’ summary assessment of an interaction, above and beyond the average or general state of all stimuli (Fredrickson & Kahneman, 1993; Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber & Redelmeier, 1993).3

Although empathic responses can be accommodated within an expanded framework of deonance theory, the same may not hold true for the third emotion included in our synthesis. Schadenfreude—defined as deriving pleasure from the misfortune of others (Leach et al., 2003; Leon & Halbesleben, 2015; Li et al., 2019)—comes from the German words for “harm” (schaden) and “pleasure” (freude). A growing body of recent research has demonstrated that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are often linked to feelings of schadenfreude triggered by such events (e.g., Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). These contemporary insights extend earlier, though more sporadic, explorations of schadenfreude within third-party literature (Blader et al., 2013; Mitchell et al., 2015).

In contrast to anger and empathy, schadenfreude is less readily categorized as a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003). Its elicitors are relatively more self-interested, and its action tendencies tend to lack the prosocial orientation seen in other moral emotions (Haidt, 2003). Nonetheless, Mitchell et al. (2015) integrated deonance theory with the concept of scope of justice (Opotow, 1990) to explain circumstances under which third parties might feel satisfaction when witnessing mistreatment. Their findings revealed that third-party observers of abusive supervision experienced contentment when the targeted coworkers were perceived as being outside their moral circle. Similar conclusions were reached in subsequent research (e.g., Xu et al., 2020). Liu et al. (2021) also invoked deonance theory in their analysis of anger and schadenfreude prompted by observed incivility, though they primarily engaged deonance theory in relation to anger. While they hypothesized that schadenfreude would arise under certain combinations of observer- and victim-related moderators—such as dark triad traits or interdependence—their findings supported a general association between observed incivility and schadenfreude regardless of these specific factors.

Attempting to broaden the scope of deonance theory to encompass schadenfreude challenges its foundational assumptions. Not only does schadenfreude lack the moral grounding typical of emotions associated with deonance (Haidt, 2003), but its evolutionary role is thought to concern dominance regulation rather than the maintenance of social order (Lange & Boecker, 2019). In a conceptual contribution, Li et al. (2019) proposed an alternative framework rooted in appraisal theories of emotion (Lazarus, 1991) to explain schadenfreude in third-party contexts. They argued that in competitive workplace environments—characterized by performance pressures and reward systems—witnessing a colleague’s misfortune can trigger raw, unfiltered feelings of schadenfreude. These arise through a primary appraisal process, in which the observer perceives the victim’s loss as a relative gain in status or resources. Subsequent secondary appraisal processes—whether implicit or explicit—may then evaluate situational factors such as the fairness of the event or the cause of the victim's misfortune, either attenuating or intensifying the initial emotional response (Li et al., 2019; see also Leon & Halbesleben, 2015). Building on Li et al.’s (2019) framework, we propose the following:

Hypothesis 5: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively associated with schadenfreude experienced from the event.
text:
Although relationships with empathy can be accommodated by an expanded conceptualization of deonance theory, that may not be the case for the third emotion included in our synthesis. Schadenfreude is defined as pleasure from the misfortune of others (Leach et al., 2003; Leon & Halbesleben, 2015; Li et al., 2019), with the term taken from the German words for harm (schaden) and pleasure (freude). An increasing number of recent studies have shown that third-party perceptions of mistreatment are associated with schadenfreude from the event (e.g., Cheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021; Qiao et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020). Such studies have supplemented earlier, more occasional attention to schadenfreude in the third-party literature (Blader et al., 2013; Mitchell et al., 2015).

Unlike anger and empathy, schadenfreude is less classifiable as a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003). Its triggers are somewhat more self-interested than those other two emotions, with its action tendencies less prosocial (Haidt, 2003). Nevertheless, Mitchell et al. (2015) combined deonance theory with the concept of scope of justice (Opotow, 1990) to describe when third parties might feel contentment when observing a mistreatment event. Their results showed that third parties who observed coworkers receiving abusive supervision felt contentment when those coworkers were deemed to be outside their scope of justice (see also Xu et al., 2020). Liu et al. (2021) drew on deonance theory in their examination of observed incivility as a trigger of anger and schadenfreude but limited their discussion of deonance arguments to their anger predictions. Although they predicted that observed incivility would trigger schadenfreude at particular levels of observer- and victim-based moderators (i.e., dark triad and interdependence), their results supported that linkage when collapsing across such variables (Liu et al., 2021).

Expanding the psychological state of deonance so that it can also manifest as schadenfreude places strain on deonance theory’s core assumptions. Not only is schadenfreude not a moral emotion (Haidt, 2003) but its evolutionary adaptiveness centers on regulating dominance rather than preserving order (Lange & Boecker, 2019). In a conceptual article, Li et al. (2019) drew on appraisal theories of emotion (Lazarus, 1991) rather than deonance theory to explain why schadenfreude might emerge in third-party contexts. Specifically, they argued that observing mistreatment would result in coarse feelings of schadenfreude when the work context has reward systems and performance pressures that encourage competition—something common in work organizations (Li et al., 2019). Here, the misfortune of others signals potential gains to the observer’s relative status and outcomes. Those coarse feelings of schadenfreude should be experienced rapidly and automatically, as a result of a primary appraisal process. Once initially felt, those coarse feelings may be more thoroughly considered—either implicitly or explicitly—in a secondary appraisal process. That process could, for example, consider observers’ assumptions about the way the world works or attributions for the victim’s misfortune (see also Leon & Halbesleben, 2015). That secondary appraisal process would then either dampen or amplify the coarse feelings of schadenfreude (Li et al., 2019). Drawing on Li et al.’s (2019) theorizing, we predicted:
Hypothesis 5: Third-party perceptions of mistreatment are positively related to schadenfreude from the event.

As previously discussed, Folger and Glerum (2015) suggested that contextual variables can act as "switching variables," moderating the predictions derived from deonance theory. In another line of work, Folger and Cropanzano (2010) highlighted the role of hierarchical structures in shaping emotional responses to violations of moral norms. Within the context of third-party evaluations, such hierarchies influence whether the observer holds more, equal, or less power relative to either the perpetrator or the victim. Given that deonance theory centers on emotional reactions, we focus here on how an observer’s relative power may shape their emotional responses to witnessed mistreatment.

Power is often conceptualized as the capacity to influence or alter the states of others—an ability frequently rooted in formalized hierarchies (Keltner et al., 2003). According to approach–inhibition theory, higher relative power triggers the activation of the brain’s behavioral approach system (Keltner et al., 2003). This system is thought to regulate behaviors related to aggression, affiliation, achievement, sex, and nourishment. Its core emotional driver is desire, while its fundamental action tendency is oriented toward forward movement or engagement (DePue, 1995). Evolutionary accounts suggest these neurological systems evolved because they conferred survival advantages—making such patterns deeply embedded and persistent over time. In this view, the behavioral approach system facilitates critical survival behaviors such as hunting, social bonding, and reproduction (DePue, 1995).

Within this framework, emotions like anger, empathy, and schadenfreude are all relevant due to their association with approach-related tendencies. Anger is intrinsically social and aggressive, arising from perceived offenses against oneself or one's affiliates (Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). Its behavioral impulse is also forward-leaning, typically manifesting as retributive action (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015). Empathy, too, is socially and communally oriented, emerging from the emotional resonance with another’s suffering (Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). It promotes support and comforting behaviors—also marked by approach-oriented engagement. Schadenfreude, the experience of pleasure at another’s misfortune, carries elements of aggression and competitiveness, grounded in social comparison (Feather & Sherman, 2002; van Dijk et al., 2006). Its action tendencies align with those of related emotions—joy (expansive behavior), envy (attainment driven), and resentment (aggressive response; Feather & Sherman, 2002; Lazarus, 1991; van Dijk et al., 2006)—each marked by a proactive posture.

We propose that an observer's relative power functions as a "switching variable" that intensifies the link between perceived mistreatment and emotional responses such as anger, empathy, and schadenfreude. Consistent with the idea that power activates the behavioral approach system (Keltner et al., 2003), greater power should make third parties more receptive to emotions that involve engagement and proactive behavior. Specifically, relative power over the perpetrator is likely to most strongly moderate the relationship between observing mistreatment and experiencing anger directed at that perpetrator, as both share a focal target. In turn, relative power over the victim should be most predictive of empathy, as it speaks to a capacity for protective or supportive action. Schadenfreude, which involves affective responses to both perpetrator and victim, is likely to be moderated by an observer’s relative power to both parties, given the dual referential nature of that emotion.
text:
As noted previously, Folger and Glerum (2015) argued that contextual characteristics could serve as “switching variables” that moderate deonance theory’s predictions. Elsewhere, Folger and Cropanzano (2010) emphasized the salience of hierarchical structures in shaping how humans react emotionally to violations of moral norms. In a third-party context, hierarchical structures determine whether third parties have more power than perpetrators or victims, the same power as perpetrators or victims, or less power than perpetrators or victims. Given deonance theory’s emphasis on emotions, our focus is on how relative power might moderate third-party emotional reactions to mistreatment.

Power can be defined as a person’s capacity to modify others’ states—with such capacity often flowing out of formalized hierarchical structures (Keltner et al., 2003). Approach–inhibition perspectives on power argue that high levels of relative power activate the brain’s behavioral approach system (Keltner et al., 2003). That system is believed to govern reactions relevant to aggression, social attachment, achievement, sex, and food. Its central emotional feature is one of desire, with its central action tendency one of forward locomotion (DePue, 1995). Such neurological systems are believed to have evolutionary underpinnings—providing survival benefits that cause them to become hardwired over time. In the case of the behavioral approach system, it facilitates survival-critical behaviors ranging from hunting to community building to mating (DePue, 1995).

The emotions of anger, empathy, and schadenfreude all possess qualities that make them relevant to the behavioral approach system. Anger is inherently aggressive and social, resulting from some offense against “me or mine” (Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). Its action tendency is also one of forward locomotion, in the form of retribution (Folger et al., 2005; Folger & Glerum, 2015; Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). Empathy is inherently social and communal, resulting from being moved by the suffering of another (Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). Its action tendency is also forward-moving, in the form of support and comfort (Haidt, 2003; Lazarus, 1991). For its part, schadenfreude has elements of aggression and achievement given its social comparative emphasis (Feather & Sherman, 2002; van Dijk et al., 2006). Its action tendency should also be forward-moving, based on the tendencies associated with related emotions like joy (expansiveness), envy (possession seeking), and resentment (aggression; Feather & Sherman, 2002; Lazarus, 1991; van Dijk et al., 2006).

We theorize that relative power can serve as a “switching variable” that amplifies the relationships between third-party perceptions of mistreatment and anger, empathy, and schadenfreude. To the extent that more power activates the behavioral approach system (Keltner et al., 2003), it should put third parties in a frame of mind that is more amenable to the triggering of approach-centric emotions. Relative power to the perpetrator should be most relevant to the relationship between observing mistreatment and anger toward the perpetrator, given the shared referent. Likewise, relative power to the victim should be most relevant to relationships between observing mistreatment and empathy toward the victim. Schadenfreude from the event has elements of both the perpetrator and the victim, as it is the perpetrator who creates the event and the victim who fuels the pleasure that may result. We therefore predict that relative power to both parties will moderate relationships with that emotion.

Summarizing insights from media research (e.g., Lee, 2009; Shoemaker, Danielian, & Brendlinger, 1991), Oliver and colleagues (2023) identify two key factors that shape a story’s potential newsworthiness: deviance and social significance. Deviance captures the extent to which an actor stands out from peers within a community, region, or broader reference group (Oliver et al., 2023; Shoemaker et al., 1991), making the actor more salient and attention-worthy (Lippmann, 1922). Deviance may manifest as violations of social norms, statistical outliers, or other departures from expectations, but it must hold public interest. Particularly, negative forms of deviance are more likely to be newsworthy (Rozin & Royzman, 2001; Shoemaker, 1996), as humans are evolutionarily attuned to detect threats (Shoemaker, 1996), and negative information tends to carry more weight in impression formation and competence evaluations (Skowronski & Carlston, 1989).

Social significance refers to the relevance or influence an actor holds for a particular audience or community, often rooted in the actor’s cultural, economic, or institutional prominence (Oliver et al., 2023; Shoemaker et al., 1991). Actors with higher visibility or influence—such as large, powerful organizations—tend to be seen as more socially significant. For instance, “large and highly visible firms that are known for significantly impacting many stakeholders are likely to be perceived as more socially significant compared to smaller, less visible firms with a smaller perceived political, economic, or cultural impact” (Oliver et al., 2023: 1218). Because of their elevated status, such actors tend to attract consistent media attention—even when their actions do not deviate from the norm—due solely to their high social significance.

We propose that data breaches involving high-status firms are especially likely to be framed as newsworthy. This is because data breaches represent a form of normative deviance—violating the implicit social contract between a firm and its customers to keep their personal information safe. Breaches can result in substantial harm, especially if exposed data are exploited for identity theft or other illicit uses (Culnan & Armstrong, 1999; Gwebu, Wang, & Wang, 2018; Martin et al., 2017). Furthermore, high-status actors are expected to uphold elevated ethical and security standards (Han & Pollock, 2021; Pollock et al., 2019), and when those expectations are breached, the behavior may be perceived as more deviant than equivalent actions by lower-status counterparts (Graffin et al., 2013). At the same time, high-status firms carry high social significance due to their elite positions in social hierarchies and industries, often shaping sectoral or even macroeconomic conditions (Hoffman & Ocasio, 2001; Rao et al., 2005; Sauder et al., 2012). This enhances their visibility and makes their misconduct more likely to be scrutinized.

Previous research supports the notion that misconduct by high-status actors tends to receive more media attention and is more likely to escalate into scandal (Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013). For example, Graffin and colleagues (2013) found that during the British parliamentary expense scandal, high-status Members of Parliament (MPs) were more likely to face public scandal and lose their seats compared to lower-status MPs. Similarly, Dewan and Jensen (2020) documented that high-status firms were disproportionately targeted by the Securities and Exchange Commission when part of broader, multi-firm fraud cases. These findings anchor our argument and lead us to formulate the following baseline hypothesis:

Hypothesis 1. High-status firms are more likely than non-high-status firms to have their misconduct scandalized by the media.
text:
Summarizing the media literature (e.g., Lee, 2009; Shoemaker, Danielian, & Brendlinger, 1991), Oliver and colleagues (2023) identified two factors that influence a story’s potential newsworthiness: deviance and social significance. Deviance refers to characteristics that set actors apart from others in their community, region, or other categories. (Oliver et al., 2023; Shoemaker et al., 1991), making them stand out relative to others (Lippmann, 1922). Deviance can take a variety of forms, from violating social norms to statistical oddities, but it should be interesting to the broader public. Negative forms of deviance are particularly noteworthy (Rozin & Royzman, 2001; Shoemaker, 1996), since humans are hard-wired to be on the lookout for threats (Shoemaker, 1996), and negative information shapes impression formation and ability assessments (Skowronski & Carlston, 1989).

Social significance refers to the actor’s significance to some group or audience based on their cultural, economic, or institutional influence (Oliver, et al., 2023; Shoemaker et al.,1991). The more prominent or influential the actor, the greater their social significance. For example, “large and highly visible firms that are known for significantly impacting many stakeholders are likely to be perceived as more socially significant compared to smaller, less visible firms with a smaller perceived political, economic, or cultural impact” (Oliver et al., 2023: 1218). Socially significant actors, such as high-status firms, are more likely to receive ongoing attention, even for behaviors that are not deviant, because of their social significance.

We argue that data breaches by high-status firms are, all else equal, likely to be newsworthy events because data breaches are a form of “normative” deviance that breaks the firm’s social contract with its customers that it will keep their data safe, and can result in significant financial harm if the breached data are used for identity theft or other illegal purposes (Culnan & Armstrong, 1999; Gwebu, Wang, & Wang, 2018; Martin et al., 2017). Moreover, audiences have high expectations about the values high-status actors will uphold (Han & Pollock 2021; Pollock et al., 2019), and violating these expectations can lead audiences to perceive high-status actors’ misconduct as more deviant than similar misconduct by lower-status actors (Graffin et al., 2013). Further, high-status firms are socially significant, and thus garner substantial attention, since they occupy the top ranks of a social hierarchy and are typically among the most influential firms in their industries, and sometimes the economy (Hoffman & Ocasio, 2001; Rao et al., 2005; Sauder et al., 2012).

Research has shown that high-status actors’ misconduct is more likely to receive wide attention and become scandalized (e.g., Dewan & Jensen, 2020; Graffin et al., 2013). For example, Graffin and colleagues (2013) found that when a scandal involving the expense accounts of British Members of Parliament (MPs) erupted, high-status MPs were more likely to have their behavior scandalized by the media and to lose their seats than were lower-status MPs. Dewan and Jensen (2020) similarly found that high-status firms were more likely to be the target of the Securities and Exchange Commission’s enforcement for securities fraud when they were part of multi-actor scandals. Thus, although established, we treat this as our baseline relationship and hypothesize:

Hypothesis 1. High-status firms are more likely than non-high-status firms to have their misconduct scandalized by the media.

Job crafting marks a profound shift from traditional notions of work redesign, which place the responsibility for altering job roles solely in the hands of management (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). In contrast, job crafting refers to the informal and proactive changes employees make to align their jobs more closely with their personal preferences, motivations, and passions (Tims, Bakker, & Derks, 2012; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001:179) originally defined job crafting as “the physical and cognitive changes individuals make in the task and relational boundaries of their work.” These changes can occur through task crafting (modifying job tasks), relational crafting (adjusting the frequency, nature, or intensity of workplace interactions), or cognitive crafting (changing one’s perception of the job), all aimed at enhancing the personal meaning of work and aligning it with one’s identity and values.

To better suit their personal needs, aspirations, or life circumstances, employees may adapt their tasks, develop new relationships, or reframe the way they perceive their roles (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001), thereby cultivating a deeper sense of meaning in their work. A growing body of research now examines how individuals shape meaningful work through job crafting (e.g., Bindl et al., 2019; Bruning & Campion, 2018), including its interdependent nature across various life domains. For instance, activities at home may deplete resources needed for job crafting in the workplace, highlighting potential trade-offs across life contexts (De Bloom et al., 2020).

Scholars studying employees in lower-status or routine roles argue that job crafting is accessible even in the most constrained job settings (Berg et al., 2010), reinforcing the notion that all workers possess the capability to engage in job crafting (Tims et al., 2012). Supporting this, Leana et al. (2009) found that childcare center teachers exercised some autonomy in going beyond their formal job descriptions and taking initiative. These employees were observed to construct their own work roles and identities, especially through collaborative job crafting — that is, collectively making physical or cognitive modifications to job tasks or relational boundaries with colleagues. Yet, Leana et al.'s study did not find empirical support for a link between individual job crafting and improved job performance, measured by classroom care quality.

Why, then, do employees engage in job crafting? Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) framed job crafting as a means to enhance both the meaning and the identity associated with work. They particularly emphasized the interpersonal dimension, suggesting that relational job crafting — modifying the number or quality of social interactions at work — can amplify the meaningfulness of one’s job. For example, they describe nurses (initiating or deepening interactions with patients and families) and hairdressers (engaging clients in personal conversations) as illustrating how relational dynamics can enrich job meaning. More recent studies have confirmed that employees often engage in relational job crafting with coworkers (Bindl et al., 2019; Bruning & Campion, 2018; Rofcanin et al., 2019).

Relational job crafting appears especially relevant in the context of fieldworkers such as community health workers, community-based teachers, and microfinance agents, whose work is embedded in local communities. Unlike cleaners, janitors, or nurses who generally operate under close daily supervision in centralized workplaces and with limited formal autonomy (Berg et al., 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001), these field-based workers typically enjoy relatively high levels of autonomy while interacting directly with beneficiaries (Mair, Wolf, & Seelos, 2016). Autonomy has long been linked to greater opportunities for and experiences of meaningful work (Deci & Ryan, 2000; Ryan & Deci, 2000). This raises a compelling question: Do fieldworkers in social enterprises working in poverty-affected regions—such as those in South Asia (Mair et al., 2012)—engage in job crafting? If so, what factors enable or constrain their ability to do so?
text:
Job crafting represents a significant departure from work redesign, which allocates the responsibility of changing employees’ jobs firmly to the domain of management (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). It refers to the informal and proactive changes employees make to better align their jobs with their own preferences, motives, and passions (Tims, Bakker, & Derks, 2012; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001: 179) originally defined job crafting as “the physical and cognitive changes individuals make in the task and relational boundaries of their work.” Employees may enhance the meaning of their work through task crafting (adjusting tasks at work), relational crafting (changing the frequency, quality, or intensity of social interactions with others at work), or cognitive crafting (altering how they see their jobs) in line with their preferences and identity.

In pursuit of aligning their needs, aspirations, and circumstances to their jobs, employees may alter their tasks, interact more with others or build new relationships, and reframe how they view their jobs (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001) to increase the meaning of their work. There is now an expanding stream of research exploring how employees shape or craft meaningful work (e.g., Bindl et al., 2019; Bruning & Campion, 2018) and the interdependent nature of crafting across life domains, such that activities at home may reduce an individual’s resources to job craft at work (De Bloom et al., 2020).

Researchers focusing on lower-ranked workers have argued that even the most routine and lowest-level jobs present some latitude to employees to craft their jobs (Berg et al., 2010), consistent with the idea that all employees are capable of engaging in job crafting (Tims et al., 2012). In support of this argument, Leana et al. (2009) found that teachers at childcare centers have some autonomy in going beyond prescribed job requirements to take initiative. These workers shape their own identities and work roles through the personal construction of their work, as evidenced by the influence of collaborative job crafting (i.e., jointly, together with colleagues, creating physical or cognitive changes in the tasks or the relational boundaries of their work). However, Leana et al. (2009) did not find empirical support for the hypothesis that individual job crafting increased performance, as measured by the quality of care in the classroom.

If employees craft their jobs, why do they do so? Wrzesniewski and Dutton (2001) considered job crafting as a way to improve meaning and identity at work. The authors theorized, for instance, about the interpersonal aspect of job crafting, such that changing the number or extent of interactions with others at work (i.e., engaging in relational job crafting) may increase the meaning they attach to work. They illustrated this with examples of nurses (interacting with staff and beneficiaries, such as patients’ families) and hairdressers (initiating personal conversations with clients), who, the authors argued, imbue their work with meaning. Various studies have also found that employees engage in relational job crafting with colleagues at work (Bindl et al., 2019; Bruning & Campion, 2018; Rofcanin et al., 2019).

Relational job crafting would seem to be particularly germane in our context of fieldworkers, such as community health workers, teachers in local communities, and microfinance agents, operating in local communities. In contrast to cleaners, nurses, or janitors working in an office or a hospital with limited formal power and autonomy and often under direct daily supervision in the workplace (Berg et al., 2010; Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001), these fieldworkers typically work relatively autonomously with beneficiaries in local communities (Mair, Wolf, & Seelos, 2016). Autonomy has been associated with scope for and higher levels of meaningful work (Deci & Ryan, 2000; Ryan & Deci, 2000). Given this, do fieldworkers in social organizations operating in poverty settings, such as in South Asia (Mair et al., 2012), engage in job crafting? If so, what constrains or enables them to do so?

Incentive Effects of PFP on Creative Performance  
Although Kim et al. (2022) made strides in resolving the debate surrounding the relationship between pay-for-performance (PFP) and performance on interesting tasks, they also brought attention to a critical, unresolved issue: How does PFP specifically affect creative performance? Because different types of tasks are defined and structured around distinct conceptual foundations, conclusions drawn from research on noncreative tasks may not be applicable when predicting the direction or strength of the PFP–performance relationship in creative contexts (Schwab, 1980). Creative tasks, by definition, demand outputs that are both novel and useful (Amabile, 1998; Shalley et al., 2004; Zhou, 1998). This sets them apart from merely interesting tasks—such as reproducing SOMA block patterns (Brockner & Vasta, 1981), solving puzzles (Deci, 1972), or assembling erector toys (Farr, 1976)—which do not necessarily necessitate novelty, nor do they always meet the criterion of utility. While creative tasks may often be engaging, engaging tasks are not inherently creative. Moreover, because different performance outcomes are driven by different mechanisms, inferring the impact of PFP on creativity from prior studies that did not measure creative outputs can be misleading. Meta-analyses (Jenkins et al., 1998; Kim et al., 2022) have demonstrated that the strength of the PFP–performance relationship varies considerably depending on the type of performance measure used (see Tables 2 and 5 in Kim et al., 2022). The componential theory of creativity furthers this distinction by identifying a specific set of elements that uniquely shape creative performance, distinguishing it from other forms of performance evaluation (Amabile & Pratt, 2016).

Initial theoretical perspectives on creativity were aligned with Cognitive Evaluation Theory (CET) and Self-Determination Theory (SDT). Rooted in the intrinsic motivation hypothesis, early creativity research suggested that extrinsic incentives like PFP could undermine creativity, as they may introduce perceived external constraints that diminish intrinsic engagement with the task (Amabile, 1983a). In contrast, intrinsic motivation—stemming directly from task involvement—was consistently linked with heightened creativity. However, Kim et al. (2022) reported a positive—not negative—effect of PFP on performance in intrinsically interesting tasks, thereby challenging the traditional theoretical assumption that PFP undermines creativity. Their findings call into question the CET/SDT-based rationale commonly used to argue that PFP is harmful to creative performance.

Furthermore, evolving theoretical frameworks now recognize that extrinsic incentive systems can, under certain conditions, enhance creativity. Amabile and colleagues (Amabile & Pratt, 2016; Hennessey & Amabile, 2010) have revised traditional creativity theory, proposing that extrinsic motivators may exert "boosting effects" when implemented in environments that support autonomy. This view aligns more closely with traditional motivation theories (e.g., Latham, 2007; Vroom, 1964), which posit that intrinsic task interest and extrinsic incentives can interact additively to enhance performance. The revised creativity framework suggests that intrinsic and extrinsic motivation may be synergistic—i.e., their combined effect may be greater than the sum of their individual impacts. This stands in contrast to older models stemming from CET/SDT, which argue for nonadditivity and even potential interference between the two forms of motivation. Empirical evidence from Byron and Khazanchi’s (2012) meta-analysis supports this revised view, finding that creative performance-contingent rewards were positively associated with creativity and did not suppress it. Nonetheless, concerns remain regarding the ecological validity of prior experimental studies, which often failed to mirror real-world organizational contexts. As such, many questions about employee performance on creative tasks under PFP conditions remain unanswered.

In sum, the literature has made meaningful progress in exploring the connection between PFP and creative performance. Yet, despite the growing significance of creativity in modern organizations and a shift in theoretical perspectives—from rigidity to conditional interaction—the empirical consensus remains limited (Gerhart & Fang, 2015; Kim et al., 2022; T.-Y. Park et al., 2022). To deepen our understanding of this debate, I examine the effects of implementing PFP versus not offering it at all. While CET/SDT and early creativity theory argue that PFP hampers creativity, traditional motivation frameworks and revised models of creativity together suggest that offering PFP may actually enhance creative performance relative to fixed-pay alternatives.
text:
Incentive Effects of PFP on Creative Performance
Although Kim et al. (2022) seemed to resolve the controversy over the relationship between PFP and performance in interesting tasks, they identified an important yet-to-be-answered question: How does PFP influence creative performance specifically? Given that different task types consist of different elements grounded in distinct construct definitions, conclusions regarding the PFP–performance relationship in noncreative tasks cannot be generalized to predict the direction and effect size of the relationship in creative tasks (Schwab, 1980). Creative tasks, by definition, require both novelty and usefulness in the generated outputs (Amabile, 1998; Shalley et al., 2004; Zhou, 1998). This distinguishes them from interesting tasks—such as reproducing SOMA blocks (Brockner & Vasta, 1981), solving puzzles (Deci, 1972), or assembling erector toys (Farr, 1976)—that do not necessarily require a novel solution and might not meet the usefulness standard. Hence, although creative tasks can often be interesting, interesting tasks are not necessarily creative tasks. Moreover, because different performance measures have different determinants and thus consequences, it might not be accurate to infer the effect of PFP on creative performance based on previous PFP research, which did not include performance measures of creativity. For instance, meta-analytic evidence (Jenkins et al., 1998; Kim et al., 2022) tells us that the magnitude of the PFP–performance relationship varies according to the type of performance measures (for details, see Tables 2 and 5 in Kim et al., 2022). Likewise, the componential model of creativity proposes the unique combinations of components that determine creative performance, distinguishing creative performance from other measures of performance (Amabile & Pratt, 2016).
Early theoretical work on creativity was consistent with the CET/SDT perspective. Specifically, grounded in the intrinsic motivation hypothesis of creativity, traditional creativity theory suggests that extrinsic motivators (e.g., PFP) are detrimental to creativity because people feel they are controlled by external pressures, whereas an intrinsically motivated state rooted in the task itself is positively associated with higher creativity (Amabile, 1983a). However, as noted, Kim et al. (2022) documented a positive, not negative, effect of PFP on performance in intrinsically interesting tasks, thus questioning the theoretical basis of traditional creativity theory. Hence, to the extent that traditional creativity theory relies on CET/SDT pertaining to intrinsic motivation to explain why PFP harms creative performance, such a theoretical basis is not supported by the empirical evidence in Kim et al. (2022).
Moreover, the evolving theoretical perspectives on creativity that recognize the possibility that extrinsic motivators can enhance creativity call for examining PFP’s effects on creative performance. Amabile and colleagues (Amabile & Pratt, 2016; Hennessey & Amabile, 2010) revised creativity theory, proposing that “boosting effects” between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation might exist when extrinsic motivators are implemented in autonomy-supportive work settings. This argument is, at the very least, logically more in line with traditional motivation theories (vs. CET/SDT and traditional creativity theory), in which task interest and extrinsic incentives combine additively to determine performance (Latham, 2007; Vroom, 1964). That is, the revised creativity theory suggests that because intrinsic and extrinsic motivation might combine synergistically, the total motivation can be greater than the sum of the two. This contrasts with traditional creativity theory grounded in the CET/SDT argument regarding the nonadditivity of intrinsic and extrinsic motivation, in which the total motivation can be less than the sum of the two. The findings of Byron and Khazanchi’s (2012) meta-analysis are consistent with the revised creativity theory argument insofar as creative performance-contingent rewards did not undermine creative performance but were associated with higher creative performance. However, due to the aforementioned limitations regarding experimental designs being less representative of actual work settings in the primary studies in their meta-analysis, questions about how workers perform creative tasks under PFP remain unanswered.
Overall, the literature has taken important steps toward understanding the PFP–creative performance relationship. However, despite the importance of creativity in organizations and evolving theoretical arguments about whether PFP helps or harms creative performance (Amabile & Pratt, 2016; Hennessey & Amabile, 2010; Zhou & Hoever, 2014), the existing scientific evidence provides limited guidance (Gerhart & Fang, 2015; Kim et al., 2022; T.-Y. Park et al., 2022). To advance our understanding of this issue, I first examine the effects of the absence versus the presence of PFP. Although CET/SDT and traditional creativity theory suggest that PFP is detrimental to creative performance, traditional motivation theories and revised creativity theory converge to suggest that creative performance is higher under PFP than under fixed pay.
